1.37 Here we look at some variations of Example 1.3.4.

(a) In the warden's calculation of Example 1.3.4 it was assumed that if $A$ were to be
pardoned, then with equal probability the warden would tell A that either $\mathrm { B }$ or $\mathrm { C }$
would die. However, this need not be the case. The warden can assign probabilities
$\gamma$ and $1 - \gamma$ to these events, as shown here:


<table>
<tr>
<th>Prisoner pardoned</th>
<th colspan="2">Warden tells A</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { A }$</td>
<td>B dies</td>
<td>with probability $\gamma$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { A }$</td>
<td>C dies</td>
<td>with probability $1 - \gamma$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>B</td>
<td>C dies</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { C }$</td>
<td>B dies</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>


Calculate $P \left( A | \mathcal{W} \right)$ as a function $\gamma .$ For what values of $\gamma$ is $P \left( A | \mathcal{W} \right)$ less than,
equal $t o ,$ or greater than $\frac { 1 } { 3 }$

(b) Suppose again that $\gamma = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ as in the example. After the warden tells $A$ that $\mathrm { R }$
will die, A thinks for a while and realizes that his original calculation was false.
However, $A$ then gets a bright idea. A asks the warden if he can swap fates with C.
The warden, thinking that no information has been passed, agrees to this. Prove
that A's reasoning is now correct and that his probability of survival has jumped
to $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$

A similar, but somewhat more complicated, problem, the "Monte Hall problem" is
discussed by Selvin (1975). The problem in this guise gained a fair amount of noto-
riety when it appeared in a Sunday magazine (vos Savant 1990) along with a correct
answer but with questionable explanation. The ensuing debate was even reported on
the front page of the Sunday New York Times (Tierney 1991). A complete and some-
what amusing treatment is given by Morgan et al. (1991) [see also the response by vos
Savant 1991]. Chun (1999) pretty much exhausts the problem with a very thorough
analysis.

1.38 Prove each of the following statements. (Assume that any conditioning event has pos-
itive probability.)

(a) If $P \left( B \right) = 1 ,$ then $P \left( A | B \right) = P \left( A \right)$ for any $A .$

(b) If $A \subset B ,$ then $P \left( B | A \right) = 1$ and $P \left( A | B \right) = P \left( A \right) / P \left( B \right) .$

(c) If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, then

$$P \left( A | A \cup B \right) = \frac { P \left( A \right) } { P \left( A \right) + P \left( B \right) }$$

(d) $P \left( A \cap B \cap C \right) = P \left( A | B \cap C \right) P \left( B | C \right) P \left( C \right) .$

1.39 A pair of events $A$ and $B$ cannot be simultaneously mutually exclusive and independent.
Prove that if $P \left( A \right) > 0$ and $P \left( B \right) > 0 ,$ then:

(a) If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, they cannot be independent.

(b) If $A$ and $B$ are independent, they cannot be mutually exclusive.

1.40 Finish the proof of Theorem 1.3.9 by proving parts (b) and $\left( c \right) .$

1.41 As in Example 1.3.6, consider telegraph signals "dot" and "dash" sent in the proportion
3:4, where erratic transmissions cause a dot to become a dash with probability $\frac { 1 } { 4 }$ and
a dash to become a dot with probability $\frac { 1 } { 3 } .$

(a) $I f \quad a \quad d a s h$ is received, what is the probability that a dash has been sent?

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="43" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 1.7" -->

(b) Assuming independence between signals, if the message dot-dot was received,
what is the probability distribution of the four possible messages that could have
been sent?

1.42 The inclusion-exclusion identity of Miscellanea 1.8.1 gets it name from the fact that
it is proved by the method of inclusion and exclusion (Feller 1968, Section IV.1). Here
we go into the details. The probability $P \left( \cup _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } \right)$ is the sum of the probabilities of
all the sample points that are contained in at least one of the $A _ { i } s .$ The method of
inclusion and exclusion is a recipe for counting these points.

(a) Let $E _ { k }$ denote the set of all sample points that are contained in exactly $k$ of the
events $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { n } .$ Show that $P \left( \cup _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } P \left( E _ { i } \right) .$

(b) Without loss of generality, assume that $E _ { k }$ is contained in $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { k } .$ Show
that $P \left( E _ { k } \right)$ appears $k$ times in the sum $P _ { 1 } ,$ $\binom { k } { 2 }$ times in the sum $P _ { 2 } ,$ $\binom { k } { 3 }$ times in
the sum $P _ { 3 } ,$ etc.

(c) Show that

$$k - \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) + \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right) - \cdots + \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) = 1$$

(See Exercise 1.27.)

(d) Show that parts $\left( a \right) - \left( c \right)$ imply $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } P \left( E _ { i } \right) = P _ { 1 } - P _ { 2 } = \cdots \pm P _ { n } ,$ establishing
the inclusion-exclusion identity.

1.43 For the inclusion-exclusion identity of Miscellanea 1.8.1:

(a) Derive both Boole's and Bonferroni's Inequality from the inclusion-exclusion iden-
tity.

(b) Show that the $P _ { i }$ satisfy $P _ { i } \geq P _ { j }$ if $i \leq j$ and that the sequence of bounds in
Miscellanea 1.8.1 improves as the number of terms increases.

(c) Typically as the number of terms in the bound increases, the bound becomes more
useful. However, Schwager (1984) cautions that there are some cases where there
is not much improvement, in particular if the $A _ { i } s$ are highly correlated. Examine
what happens to the sequence of bounds in the extreme case when $A _ { i } = A$ for
every $i .$ (See Worsley 1982 and the correspondence of Worsley 1985 and Schwager
1985.)

1.44 Standardized tests provide an interesting application of probability theory. Suppose
first that a test consists of 20 multiple-choice questions, each with 4 possible answers.
If the student guesses on each question, then the taking of the exam can be modeled
as a sequence of 20 independent events. Find the probability that the student gets at
least 10 questions correct, given that he is guessing.

1.45 Show that the induced probability function defined in (1.4.1) defines a legitimate
probability function in that it satisfies the Kolmogorov Axioms.

1.46 Seven balls are distributed randomly into seven cells. Let $X _ { i } = \text { the }$ number of cells
containing exactly $i$ balls. What is the probability distribution of $X _ { 3 }$ (That $\mathrm { i s } ,$ find
$P \left( X _ { 3 } = x \right)$ for every possible x.)

1.47 Prove that the following functions are cdfs.

(a) $\frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi } \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( x \right) ,$ $x \in \left( - \infty , \infty \right)$

(b) $\left( 1 + e ^ { - x } \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$ $x \in \left( - \infty , \infty \right)$

(c) $e ^ { - e ^ { - x } } ,$ $x \in \left( - \infty , \infty \right)$

(d) $1 - e ^ { - x } ,$ $x \in \left( 0 , \infty \right)$

(e) the function defined in (1.5.6)

1.48 Prove the necessity part of Theorem 1.5.3.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="44" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROBABILITY THEORY" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 1.8" -->

1.49 A cdf $F _ { X }$ is stochastically greater than a cdf $F _ { Y }$ if $F _ { X } \left( t \right) \leq F _ { Y } \left( t \right)$ for all $t$ and $F _ { X } \left( t \right) <$
$F _ { Y } \left( t \right)$ for some t. Prove that if $X \sim F _ { X }$ and $Y \sim F _ { Y } ,$ then

$$P \left( X > t \right) \geq P \left( Y > t \right) \quad \text { for every } t$$

and

$$P \left( X > t \right) > P \left( Y > t \right) f o r \quad s o m e \quad t ,$$

that is, $X$ tends to be bigger than $Y .$

1.50 Verify formula (1.5.4), the formula for the partial sum of the geometric series.

1.51 An appliance store receives a shipment of 30 microwave ovens, 5 of which are (unknown
to the manager) defective. The store manager selects 4 ovens at random, without
replacement, and tests to see if they are defective. Let $X = \mathrm { n u m b e r }$ of defectives
found. Calculate the pmf and cdf of $X$ and plot the $\mathrm { c d f } .$

1.52 Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with pdf $f \left( x \right)$ and cdf $F \left( x \right) .$ For a fixed number
$x _ { 0 } ,$ define the function

$$g \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { f \left( x \right) / \left[ 1 - F \left( x _ { 0 } \right) \right] } & { x \geq x _ { 0 } } \\ { 0 } & { x < x _ { 0 } } \end{array} \right.$$

Prove that $g \left( x \right)$ is a pdf. (Assume that $F \left( x _ { 0 } \right) < 1$ 1.)

1.53 $A$ certain river floods every year. Suppose that the low-water mark is set at 1 and the
high-water mark $Y$ has distribution function

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = P \left( Y \leq y \right) = 1 - \frac { 1 } { y ^ { 2 } } , \quad 1 \leq y < \infty .$$

(a) Verify that $F _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ is a cdf.

(b) Find $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$ the pdf of $Y$ .

(c) If the low-water mark is reset at 0 and we use a unit of measurement that is $\frac { 1 } { 1 0 }$ of
that given previously, the high-water mark becomes $Z = 1 0 \left( Y \quad - \quad 1 \right) .$ Find $F _ { Z } \left( z \right) .$

1.54 For each of the following, determine the value of $c$ that makes $f \left( x \right)$ a pdf.

(a) $f \left( x \right) = c \sin x ,$ $0 < x < \pi / 2$

(b) $f \left( x \right) = c e ^ { - | x | } ,$ $- \infty < x < \infty$

1.55 An clectronic device has lifetime denoted by $T .$ The device has value $V = 5$ if it fails
before time $t = 3 ;$ otherwise, it has value $V = 2 T .$ Find the cdf of $V ,$ if $T$ has pdf

$$f _ { T } \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 . 5 } e ^ { - t / \left( 1 . 5 \right) } , \quad t > 0 .$$


## 1.8 Miscellanea


### 1.8.1 Bonferroni and Beyond

The Bonferroni bound of (1.2.10), or Boole's Inequality (Theorem 1.2.11), provides
simple bounds on the probability of an intersection or union. These bounds can be
made more and more precise with the following expansion.

For sets $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots A _ { n } ,$ we create a new set of nested intersections as follows. Let

$$P _ { 1 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } P \left( A _ { i } \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="45" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 1.8" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->

$$P _ { 2 } = \sum _ { 1 \leq i < j \leq n } ^ { n } P \left( A _ { i } \cap A _ { j } \right)$$
$$P _ { 3 } = \sum _ { 1 \leq i < j < k \leq n } P \left( A _ { i } \cap A _ { j } \cap A _ { k } \right)$$
$$P _ { n } = P \left( A _ { 1 } \cap A _ { 2 } \cap \cdots \cap A _ { n } \right) .$$
:

Then the inclusion-exclusion identity says that

$$P \left( A _ { 1 } \cup A _ { 2 } \cup \cdots \cup A _ { n } \right) = P _ { 1 } - P _ { 2 } + P _ { 3 } - P _ { 4 } + \cdots \pm P _ { n } .$$

Moreover, the $P _ { i }$ are ordered in that $P _ { i } \geq P _ { j }$ if $i \leq j ,$ and we have the sequence of
upper and lower bounds

$$P _ { 1 } \geq P \left( \cup _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } \right) \geq P _ { 1 } - P _ { 2 }$$
$$P _ { 1 } - P _ { 2 } + P _ { 3 } \geq P \left( \cup _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } \right) \geq P _ { 1 } - P _ { 2 } + P _ { 3 } - P _ { 4 }$$

:

See Exercises 1.42 and 1.43 for details.

These bounds become increasingly tighter as the number of terms increases, and
they provide a refinement of the original Bonferroni bounds. Applications of these
bounds include approximating probabilities of runs (Karlin and Ost 1988) and
multiple comparisons procedures (Naiman and Wynn 1992).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Chapter 2 Transformations and Expectations

"We want something more than mere theory and preaching $n o w , t h o u g h .$

Sherlock Holmes
A Study in Scarlet

Often, if we are able to model $a$ phenomenon in terms of a random variable $X$
with cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ we will also be concerned with the behavior of functions of $X .$ In
☒
this chapter we study techniques that allow us to gain information about functions
of $X$ that may be of interest, information that can range from very complete (the
☒
distributions of these functions) to more vague (the average behavior).


## 2.1 Distributions of Functions of a Random Variable

If $X$ is a random variable with cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ then any function of $X ,$ say $g \left( X \right) ,$ is
☒
also a random variable. Often $g \left( X \right)$ is of interest itself and we write $Y = g \left( X \right)$ to
☒ ☒
denote the new random variable $g \left( X \right) .$ Since $Y$ is a function of $X ,$ we can describe
the probabilistic behavior of $Y$ in terms of that of $X .$ That is, for any set $A ,$
☒ ☒ ☒

$$P \left( Y \in A \right) = P \left( g \left( X \right) \in A \right) ,$$ ☒

showing that the distribution of $Y$ depends on the functions $F _ { X }$ and $g .$ Depending
on the choice of $g ,$ it is sometimes possible to obtain a tractable expression for this
probability.

Formally, if we write $y = g \left( x \right) ,$ the function $g \left( x \right)$ defines a mapping from the original
sample space of $X ,$ $\mathcal{X} ,$ to a new sample space, $\mathcal{Y} ,$ the sample space of the random

☒
variable $Y .$ That is,

$$g \left( x \right) : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} .$$

We associate with $g$ an inverse mapping, denoted by $g ^ { - 1 } ,$ which is a mapping from
subsets of $\mathcal{Y}$ to subsets of $\mathcal{X} ,$ and is defined by

(2.1.1)
$$g ^ { - 1 } \left( A \right) = \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \in A \right\} .$$

Note that the mapping $g ^ { - 1 }$ takes sets into sets; that is, $g ^ { - 1 } \left( A \right)$ is the set of points
in $\mathcal{X}$ that $g \left( x \right)$ takes into the set $A .$ It is possible for $A$ to be a point set, say $A = y .$
Then

$$g ^ { - 1 } \left( \left\{ y \right\} \right) = \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) = y \right\} .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="48" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="· Section 2.1" -->

In this case we often write $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ instead of $g ^ { - 1 } \left( \left\{ y \right\} \right) .$ The quantity $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ can still
be a set, however, if there is more than one $x$ for which $g \left( x \right) = y .$ If there is only one $x$
for which $g \left( x \right) = y ,$ then $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ is the point set $x ,$ and we will write $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = x .$ If
the random variable $Y$ is now defined by $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ we can write for any set $A \subset \mathcal{Y} ,$
☒
(2.1.2)

$$P \left( Y \in A \right) = P \left( g \left( X \right) \in A \right)$$
$$= P \left( \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \in A \right\} \right)$$
$$= P \left( X \in g ^ { - 1 } \left( A \right) \right) .$$

This defines the probability distribution of $Y .$ It is straightforward to show that this
probability distribution satisfies the Kolmogorov Axioms.

If $X$ is a discrete random variable, then $\mathcal{X}$ is countable. The sample space for
☒
$Y = g \left( X \right)$ is ) = $y : y = g \left( x \right) , x$ $x \in \mathcal{X} ,$ which is also a countable sct. Thus, $Y$ is also
☒
a discrete random variable. From $\left( 2 . 1 . 2 \right) ,$ the pmf for $Y$ is

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = P \left( Y = y \right) = \sum _ { x \in g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) } P \left( X = x \right) = \sum _ { x \in g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) } f _ { X } \left( x \right) , \quad \mathrm { f o r } y \in \mathcal{Y} ,$$

and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = 0$ for $y \notin \mathcal{Y} .$ In this case, finding the pmf of $Y$ involves simply identifying
$g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) ,$ for each $y \in \mathcal{Y} ,$ and summing the appropriate probabilities.

Example 2.1.1 (Binomial transformation) A discrete random variable $X$ has
☒
a binomial distribution if its pmf is of the form

(2.1.3)
$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = P \left( X = x \right) = \binom { n } { x } p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where $n$ is a positive integer and $0 \leq p \leq 1 .$ Values such as $n$ and $p$ that can
be set to different values, producing different probability distributions, are called
parameters. Consider the random variable $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ where $g \left( x \right) = n \quad - \quad x ;$ that is,
☒
$Y = n \quad - \quad X .$ Here $\mathcal{X} = \left\{ 0 , 1 , \ldots , n \right\}$ and $V = y : y = g \left( x \right) ,$ $\left. x \in \mathcal{X} \right\} = \left\{ 0 , 1 , \ldots , n \right\} .$
For any $y \in \mathcal{Y} ,$ $n - \quad x = g \left( x \right) = y$ if and only if $x = n \quad - y .$ Thus, $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ is the single
point $x = n \quad - y ,$ and

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \sum _ { x \in g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) } f _ { X } \left( x \right)$$
$$= f _ { X } \left( n - y \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { n - y } \end{array} \right) p ^ { n - y } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - \left( n - y \right) }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { y } \end{array} \right) \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { y } p ^ { n - y } . \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \text { Definition } 1 . 2 . 1 7 \\ \text { implies } \binom { n } { y } = \binom { n } { n - y } \end{array} \right) .$$

Thus, we see that $Y$ also has a binomial distribution, but with parameters $n$ and
$1 \quad - \quad p .$

If $X$ and $Y$ are continuous random variables, then in some cases it is possible to
find simple formulas for the cdf and pdf of $Y$ in terms of the cdf and pdf of $X$ and
the function $g .$ In the remainder of this section, we consider some of these cases.

II

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="49" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.1 DISTRIBUTIONS OF FUNCTIONS OF $A$ RANDOM VARIABLE" -->

</figure>


The cdf of $Y = g \left( X \right)$ is

(2.1.4)

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = P \left( Y \leq y \right)$$
$$= P \left( g \left( X \right) \leq y \right)$$
$$= P \left( \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \leq y \right\} \right)$$
$$= \int _ { \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \leq y \right\} } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$

Sometimes there may be difficulty in identifying $\left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \leq y \right\}$ and carrying out
the integration of $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ over this region, as the next example shows.

Example 2.1.2 (Uniform transformation) Suppose $X$ has a uniform distribu-
tion on the interval $\left( 0 , 2 \pi \right) ,$ that is,

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 / \left( 2 \pi \right) } & { 0 < x < 2 \pi } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Consider $Y = \sin ^ { 2 } \left( X \right) .$ Then (see Figure 2.1.1)

(2.1.5)
$$P \left( Y \leq y \right) = P \left( X \leq x _ { 1 } \right) + P \left( x _ { 2 } \leq X \leq x _ { 3 } \right) + P \left( X \geq x _ { 4 } \right) .$$

From the symmetry of the function $\sin ^ { 2 } \left( x \right)$ and the fact that $X$ has a uniform distri-
bution, we have

and $P \left( x _ { 2 } \leq X \leq x _ { 3 } \right) = 2 P \left( x _ { 2 } \leq X \leq \pi \right) ,$
$$P \left( X \leq x _ { 1 } \right) = P \left( X \geq x _ { 4 } \right)$$

SO

(2.1.6)
$$P \left( Y \leq y \right) = 2 P \left( X \leq x _ { 1 } \right) + 2 P \left( x _ { 2 } \leq X \leq \pi \right) ,$$

where $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ are the two solutions to

$$\sin ^ { 2 } \left( x \right) = y , \quad 0 < x < \pi ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="50" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.1" -->

Thus, even though this example dealt with a seemingly simple situation, the resulting
expression for the cdf of $Y$ was not simple.

When transformations are made, it is important to keep track of the sample spaces
of the random variables; otherwise, much confusion can arise. When the transforma-
tion is from $X$ to $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ it is most convenient to use

(2.1.7) $\mathcal{X} = \left\{ x : f _ { X } \left( x \right) > 0 \right\}$
$$y = \left\{ y : y = g \left( x \right) \text { for somc } x \in \mathcal{X} \right\} .$$

The pdf of the random variable $X$ is positive only on the set $\mathcal{X}$ and is 0 elsewhere.
Such a set is called the support set of a distribution or, more informally, the support
of a distribution. This terminology can also apply to a pmf or, in general, to any
nonnegative function.

It is easiest to deal with functions $g \left( x \right)$ that are monotone, that is, those that satisfy
either

$$u > v \Rightarrow g \left( u \right) > g \left( v \right) \quad \left( \text { incrcasing } \right) \quad \text { or } \quad u < v \Rightarrow g \left( u \right) > g \left( v \right) \quad \left( \text { docreasing } \right) .$$

If the transformation $x \rightarrow g \left( x \right)$ is monotone, then it is one-to-one and onto from
$\mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} .$ That is, each $x$ goes to only one $y$ and cach $y$ comes from at most one $x$
(one-to-one). Also, for $\mathcal{Y}$ defined as in (2.1.7), for each $y \in \mathcal{Y}$ there is an $x \in \mathcal{X}$ such
that $g \left( x \right) = y$ (onto). Thus, the transformation $g$ uniquely pairs $x s$ and $y \mathrm { s } .$ If $g$ is
monotone, then $g ^ { - 1 }$ is single-valued; that is, $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = x$ if and only if $y = g \left( x \right) .$ If $g$
is increasing, this implies that

$$\left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \leq y \right\} = \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g ^ { - 1 } \left( g \left( x \right) \right) \leq g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right\}$$
$$= \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : x \leq g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right\} .$$
(2.1.8)

If $g \quad i s \quad d e c r e a s i n g ,$ this implies that

$$\left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g \left( x \right) \leq y \right\} = \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : g ^ { - 1 } \left( g \left( x \right) \right) \geq g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right\}$$

(2.1.9)
$$= \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : x \geq g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right\} .$$

(A graph will illustrate why the inequality reverses in the decreasing case.) If $g \left( x \right)$ is
an increasing function, then using (2.1.4), we can write

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \int _ { \left\{ x \in \mathcal{X} : x \leq g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right\} } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x = F _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) .$$

If $g \left( x \right)$ is decreasing, we have

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \int _ { g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) } ^ { \infty } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x = 1 - F _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) .$$

The continuity of $X$ is used to obtain the second equality. We summarize these results
in the following theorem.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="51" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.1 DISTRIBUTIONS OF FUNCTIONS OF A RANDOM VARIABLE" -->

Theorem 2.1.3 Let $X$ have cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ let $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ and let $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ be defined
as in $\left( 2 . 1 . 7 \right) .$

a. If g is an increasing function on $\mathcal{X} ,$ $F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = F _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right)$ for $y \in \mathcal{Y} .$

b. If $g$ is a decreasing function on $\mathcal{X}$ and $X$ is a continuous random variable, $F _ { Y } \left( y \right) =$
$1 - F _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right)$ for $y \in \mathcal{Y} .$

Example 2.1.4 (Uniform-exponential relationship-I) Suppose $X \sim f _ { X } \left( x \right) =$
1 if $0 < x < 1$ and 0 otherwise, the $u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ distribution. It is straightforward
to check that $F _ { X } \left( x \right) = x ,$ $0 < x < 1 .$ We now make the transformation $Y = g \left( X \right) =$
$- \log X .$ Since

$$\frac { d } { d x } g \left( x \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \log x \right) = \frac { - 1 } { x } < 0 , \quad \text { for } \quad 0 < x < 1 ,$$

$g \left( x \right)$ is a decreasing function. As $X$ ranges between 0 and 1, $- \log x$ ranges between 0
and $\infty ,$ that is, $y = \left( 0 , \infty \right) .$ For $y > 0 ,$ $y = - \log x$ implies $x = e ^ { - y } ,$ so $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = e ^ { - y } .$
Therefore, for $y > 0 ,$

$\left( F _ { X } \left( x \right) = x \right)$
$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = 1 - F _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) = 1 - F _ { X } \left( e ^ { - y } \right) = 1 - e ^ { - y } . \quad \left( F _ { X } \left( \right. \right.$$

Of course, $F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = 0$ for $y \leq 0 .$ Note that it was necessary only to verify that
$g \left( x \right) = - \log x$ is monotone on $\left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ the support of $X .$
$|$

If the pdf of $Y$ is continuous, it can be obtained by differentiating the cdf. The
resulting expression is given in the following theorem.

Theorem 2.1.5 Let $X$ have pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and let $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ where g is a monotone
function. Let $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ be defined by (2.1.7). Suppose that $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ is continuous on $\mathcal{X}$
and that $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ has a continuous derivative on $\mathcal{Y} .$ Then the pdf of $Y$ is given by

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { f _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) | \frac { d } { d y } g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) | } & { y \in \mathcal{Y} } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise } } \end{array} \right.$$
(2.1.10)

Proof: From Theorem 2.1.3 we have, by the chain rule,

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \frac { d } { d y } F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} f _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) \frac { d } { d y } g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) & \text { if } g \text { is increasing } \\ & \\ - f _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) \frac { d } { d y } g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) & \text { if } g \text { is decreasing } , \end{array} \right.$$

which can be expressed concisely as (2.1.10).

Example 2.1.6 (Inverted gamma pdf) Let $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ be the gamma pdf

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( n - 1 \right) ! \beta ^ { n } } x ^ { n - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } , \quad 0 < x < \infty ,$$

☐

where $\beta$ is a positive constant and $n$ is a positive integer. Suppose we want to find $\mathrm { t h e }$
pdf of $g \left( X \right) = 1 / X .$ Note that here the support sets $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ are both the interval

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="52" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.1" -->

$\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$ If we let $y = g \left( x \right) ,$ then $g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = 1 / y$ and $\frac { d } { d y } g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = - 1 / y ^ { 2 } .$ Applying the
above theorem, for $y \in \left( 0 , \infty \right) ,$ we get

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = f _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) | \frac { d } { d y } g ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( n - 1 \right) ! \beta ^ { n } } \left( \frac { 1 } { y } \right) ^ { n - 1 } e ^ { - 1 / \left( \beta y \right) } \frac { 1 } { y ^ { 2 } }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( n - 1 \right) ! \beta ^ { n } } \left( \frac { 1 } { y } \right) ^ { n + 1 } e ^ { - 1 / \left( \beta y \right) }$$

a special case of a pdf known as the inverted gamma $p d f .$
II

In many applications, the function $g$ may be neither increasing nor decreasing;
hence the above results will not apply. However, it is often the case that $g$ will be
monotone over certain intervals and that allows us to get an expression for $Y = g \left( X \right) .$
(If $g$ is not monotone over certain intervals, then we are in $d e e p$ trouble.)

Example 2.1.7 (Square transformation) Suppose $X$ is a continuous random
variable. For $y > 0 ,$ the cdf of $Y = X ^ { 2 }$ is

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = P \left( Y \leq y \right) = P \left( X ^ { 2 } \leq y \right) = P \left( - \sqrt { y } \leq X \leq \sqrt { y } \right) .$$

Because $x$ is continuous, we can drop the equality from the left endpoint and obtain

$$F _ { Y } \left( y \right) = P \left( - \sqrt { y } < X \leq \sqrt { y } \right)$$
$$= P \left( X \leq \sqrt { y } \right) - P \left( X \leq - \sqrt { y } \right) = F _ { X } \left( \sqrt { y } \right) - F _ { X } \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) .$$

The pdf of $Y$ can now be obtained from the cdf by differentiation:

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \frac { d } { d y } F _ { Y } \left( y \right)$$
$$= \frac { d } { d y } \left[ F _ { X } \left( \sqrt { y } \right) - F _ { X } \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { y } } f _ { X } \left( \sqrt { y } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { y } } f _ { X } \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) ,$$

where we use the chain rule to differentiate $F _ { X } \left( \sqrt { y } \right)$ and $F _ { X } \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) .$ Therefore, the
pdf is

(2.1.11)
$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { y } } \left( f _ { X } \left( \sqrt { y } \right) + f _ { X } \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) \right) .$$

Notice that the pdf of $Y$ in (2.1.11) is expressed as the sum of two pieces, pieces
that represent the intervals where $g \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 }$ is monotone. In general, this will be the
case.

<!-- PageNumber="11" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="53" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.1 DISTRIBUTIONS OF FUNCTIONS OF A RANDOM VARIABLE" -->

Theorem 2.1.8 Let $X$ have pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ let $Y = g \left( X \right) ,$ and define the sample
space $\mathcal{X}$ as in (2.1.7). Suppose there exists a partition, $A _ { 0 } , A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { k } ,$ $o f \mathcal{X}$ such that
$P \left( X \in A _ { 0 } \right) = 0$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ is continuous on each $A _ { i } .$ Further, suppose there exist
functions $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , \ldots , g _ { k } \left( x \right) ,$ defined on $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { k } ,$ respectively, satisfying

i. $g \left( x \right) = g _ { i } \left( x \right) ,$ for $x \in A _ { i } ,$

ii. $g _ { i } \left( x \right)$ is monotone on $A _ { i } ,$

iii. the set $y = \left\{ y : y = g _ { i } \left( x \right) \right.$ for some $\left. x \in A _ { i } \right\}$ is the same for each $i = 1 , \ldots , k ,$
and

iv. $g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ has a continuous derivative on $\mathcal{Y} ,$ for each $i = 1 , \ldots , k .$

Then

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } f _ { X } \left( g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) | \frac { d } { d y } g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) | & y \in \mathcal{Y} \\ 0 & \end{array} . \right.$$
otherwise.

The important point in Theorem 2.1.8 is that $\mathcal{X}$ can be divided into sets $A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { k }$
such that $g \left( x \right)$ is monotone on each $A _ { i } .$ We can ignore the "exceptional set" $A _ { 0 }$ since
$P \left( X \in A _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$ It is a technical device that is used, for example, to handle endpoints
of intervals. It is important to note that each $g _ { i } \left( x \right)$ is a one-to-one transformation
from $A _ { i }$ onto $\mathcal{Y}$ . Furthermore, $g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ is a one-to-one function from $\mathcal{Y}$ onto $A _ { i }$ such
that, for $y \in \mathcal{Y} ,$ $g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ gives the unique $x = g _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \in A _ { i }$ for which $g _ { i } \left( x \right) = y .$ (See
Exercise 2.7 for an extension.)

Example 2.1.9 (Normal-chi squared relationship) Let $X$ have the standard
normal distribution,

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty .$$

Consider $Y = X ^ { 2 } .$ The function $g \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 }$ is monotone on $\left( - \infty , 0 \right)$ and on $\left( 0 , \infty \right) .$
The set $y = \left( 0 , \infty \right) .$ Applying Theorem 2.1.8, we take

$$A _ { 0 } = \left\{ 0 \right\}$$
$$A _ { 1 } = \left( - \infty , 0 \right) , \quad g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 } , \quad g _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = - \sqrt { y }$$
$$A _ { 2 } = \left( 0 , \infty \right) , \quad g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 } , \quad g _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = \sqrt { y } .$$

The pdf of $Y$ is

$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - \left( - \sqrt { y } \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { y } } | + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - \left( \sqrt { y } \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { y } } |$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { y } } e ^ { - y / 2 } , \quad 0 < y < \infty .$$

The pdf of $Y$ is one that we will often encounter, that of a chi squared random ariable
with 1 degree of freedom.

We close this section with a special and very useful transformation.

Theorem 2.1.10 (Probability integral transformation) Let $X$ have continuous
cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and define the random variable $Y$ as $Y = F _ { X } \left( X \right) .$ Then $Y$ is uniformly
distributed on $\left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ that is, $P \left( Y \leq y \right) = y , 0 < y < 1 .$

Before we prove this theorem, we will digress for a moment and look at $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } ,$ the
inverse of the cdf $F _ { X } ,$ in some detail. If $F _ { X }$ is strictly increasing, then $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 }$ is well
defined by

(2.1.12)
$$F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = x \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad F _ { X } \left( x \right) = y .$$

However, if $F _ { X }$ is constant on some interval, then $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 }$ is not well defined by (2.1.12),
as Figure 2.1.2 illustrates. Any $x$ satisfying $x _ { 1 } \leq x \leq x _ { 2 }$ satisfies $F _ { X } \left( x \right) = y .$

This problem is avoided by defining $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ for $0 < y < 1$ by

(2.1.13)
$$F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = \inf \left\{ x : F _ { X } \left( x \right) \geq y \right\} ,$$

a definition that agrees with (2.1.12) when $F _ { X }$ is nonconstant and provides an $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 }$
that is single-valued even when $F _ { X }$ is not strictly increasing. Using this definition, in
Figure 2.1.2b, we have $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) = x _ { 1 } .$ At the endpoints of the range of $y ,$ $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right)$ can
also be defined. $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( 1 \right) = \infty$ if $F _ { X } \left( x \right) < 1$ for $a l l \quad x$ and, for any $F _ { X } ,$ $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( 0 \right) = - \infty .$

Proof of Theorem 2.1.10: For $Y = F _ { X } \left( X \right)$ we have, for $0 < y < 1 ,$

$$P \left( Y \leq y \right) = P \left( F _ { X } \left( X \right) \leq y \right)$$
$$P \left( F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left[ F _ { X } \left( X \right) \right] \leq F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) \quad \left( F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \text { is increasing } \right)$$
$$= P \left( X \leq F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right)$$
$$= F _ { X } \left( F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right)$$
$$= y .$$
\=
(see paragraph below)
$$\left( \mathrm { d e f i n i t i o n } \mathrm { o f } F _ { X } \right)$$
(continuity of $F _ { X }$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="55" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXPECTED VALUES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.2" -->

At the endpoints we have $P \left( Y \leq y \right) = 1$ for $y \geq 1$ and $P \left( Y \leq y \right) = 0$ for $y \leq 0 ,$
showing that $Y$ has a uniform distribution.

The reasoning behind the equality

$$P \left( F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( F _ { X } \left( X \right) \right) \leq F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right) = P \left( X \leq F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( y \right) \right)$$

is somewhat subtle and deserves additional attention. If $F _ { X }$ is strictly increasing, then
it is true that $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right) = x .$ (Refer to Figure 2.1.2a.) However, if $F _ { X }$ is flat, it
may be that $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right) \neq x .$ Suppose $F _ { X }$ is as in Figure 2.1.2b and let $x \in \left[ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right] .$
Then $F _ { X } ^ { - 1 } \left( F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right) = x _ { 1 }$ for any $x$ in this interval. Even in this case, though, the
probability equality holds, since $P \left( X \leq x \right) = P \left( X \leq x _ { 1 } \right)$ for any $x \in \left[ x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right] .$ The
flat cdf denotes a region of 0 probability $\left( P \left( x _ { 1 } < X \leq x \right) = F _ { X } \left( x \right) - F _ { X } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) = 0 \right) .$
☐

One application of Theorem 2.1.10 is in the generation of random samples from a
particular distribution. If it is required to generate an observation $X$ from a population
with cdf $F _ { X } ,$ we need only generate a uniform random number $U ,$ between 0 and 1,
and solve for $\dot { x }$ in the equation $F _ { X } \left( x \right) = u .$ (For many distributions there are other
methods of generating observations that take less computer time, but this method is
still useful because of its general applicability.)


## 2.2 Expected Values

The expected value, or expectation, of a random variable is merely its average value,
where we speak of "average" value as one that is weighted according to the probability
distribution. The expected value of a distribution can be thought of as a measure of
center, as we think of averages as being middle values. By weighting the values of
the random variable according to the probability distribution, we hope to obtain a
number that summarizes a typical or expected value of an observation of the random
variable.

Definition 2.2.1 The expected value or mean of a random variable $g \left( X \right) ,$ denoted
by $\mathrm { E } g \left( X \right) ,$ is

$$E g \left( X \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x } & { \text { if } X \text { is continuc } } \\ { \sum _ { x \in \mathcal{X} } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \sum _ { x \in \mathcal{X} } g \left( x \right) P \left( X = x \right) } & { \text { if } X \text { is discrete } } \end{array} \right. ,$$

provided that the integral or sum exists. If $E | g \left( X \right) | = \infty ,$ we say that $\mathrm { E } g \left( X \right)$ does
not exist. (Ross 1988 refers to this as the "law of the unconscious statistician." We
do not find this amusing.)

Example 2.2.2 (Exponential mean) Suppose $X$ has an exponential $\left( \lambda \right)$ distri-
bution, that is, it has pdf given by

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - x / \lambda } , \quad 0 \leq x < \infty , \quad \lambda > 0 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="56" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.2" -->

Then $\mathrm { E } X$ is given by

$$E X = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \lambda } x e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x$$
$$= - x e ^ { - x / \lambda } | _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x \quad \text { \left(integration by parts\right) }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x = \lambda .$$
||

Example 2.2.3 (Binomial mean) If $X$ has a binomial distribution, its pmf is
given by

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \binom { n } { x } p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where $n$ is a positive integer, $0 \leq p \leq 1 ,$ and for every fixed pair $n$ and $p$ the pmf
sums to 1. The expected value of a binomial random variable is given by

$$E X = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } x \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } = \sum _ { x = 1 } ^ { n } x \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x }$$

(the $x = 0$ term is 0). Using the identity $x \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) = n \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { x - 1 } \end{array} \right) ,$ we have

$$E X = \sum _ { x = 1 } ^ { n } n \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { x - 1 } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x }$$
$$= \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } n \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { y } \end{array} \right) p ^ { y + 1 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - \left( y + 1 \right) }$$
$$= n p \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { y } \end{array} \right) p ^ { y } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - 1 - y }$$
$$= n p ,$$

(substitute $\left. y = x \quad - \quad 1 \right)$

since the last summation must be 1, being the sum over all possible values of $a$
binomial(n $\left. - \quad 1 , p \right)$ pmf.

Example 2.2.4 (Cauchy mean) A classic example of a random variable whose
expected value does not exist is a Cauchy random variable, that is, one with pdf

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty$$

It is straightforward to check that $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x = 1 ,$ but $E | X | = \infty .$ Write

$$E | X | = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { | x | } { \pi } \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x .$$

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="57" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.2 EXPECTED VALUES" -->

For any positive number $M ,$

$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { M } \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { \log \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 2 } | _ { 0 } ^ { M } = \frac { \log \left( 1 + M ^ { 2 } \right) } { 2 }$$

Thus,

$$E | X | = \lim _ { M \rightarrow \infty } \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { M } \frac { x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \lim _ { M \rightarrow \infty } \log \left( 1 + M ^ { 2 } \right) = \infty$$

and $\mathrm { E } X$ does not exist.

Il

The process of taking expectations is a linear operation, which means that the
expectation of a linear function of $X$ can be easily evaluated by noting that for any
☒

constants $a$ and b,

(2.2.1)
$$E \left( a X + b \right) = a E X + b .$$

For example, if $X$ is binomial(n, $\left. p \right) ,$ so that $E X = n p ,$ then
☒

$$E \left( X \quad - \quad n p \right) = E X \quad - \quad n p = n p - n p = 0 .$$

The expectation operator, in fact, has many properties that can help ease calcu-
lational effort. Most of these properties follow from the properties of the integral or
sum, and are summarized in the following theorem.

Theorem 2.2.5 Let $X$ be a random variable and let $a ,$ $b ,$ and $c$ be constants. Then
for any functions $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right)$ and $g _ { 2 } \left( x \right)$ whose expectations exist,

a. $\mathrm { E } \left( a g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) + b g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) + c \right) = a \mathrm { E } g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) + b \mathrm { E } g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) + c .$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

b. If $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \geq 0$ for all x, then $\mathrm { E } g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) \geq 0 .$
☒

c. If $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \geq g _ { 2 } \left( x \right)$ for $a l l \quad x ,$ then $\mathrm { E } g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) \geq \mathrm { E } g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒

d. If $a \leq g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \leq b$ for all x, then $a \leq E g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) \leq b .$
☒ ☒

Proof: We will give details for only the continuous case, the discrete case being
similar. By definition,

$$E \left( a g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) + b g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) + c \right)$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( a g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) + b g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) + c \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } a g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x + \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } b g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x + \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } c f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$ ☒ ☒

by the additivity of the integral. Since a, $b ,$ and $c$ are constants, they factor out of
their respective integrals and we have

$$E \left( a g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) + b g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) + c \right)$$
$$= a \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x + b \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x + c \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= a E g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) + b E g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) + c ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

establishing (a). The other three properties are proved in a similar manner.
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="58" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.2" -->

Example 2.2.6 (Minimizing distance) The expected value of a random variable
has another property, one that we can think of as relating to the interpretation of
$\mathrm { E } X$ as a good guess at a value of $X .$

Suppose we measure the distance between a random variable $X$ and a constant $b$ by
$\left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 } .$ The closer $b$ is to $X ,$ the smaller this quantity is. We can now determine the
value of $b$ that minimizes $E \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 }$ and, hence, will provide us with a good predictor
of $X .$ (Note that it does no good to look for a value of $b$ that minimizes $\left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 } ,$
since the answer would depend on $X ,$ making it a useless predictor of $\left. X . \right)$

We could proceed with the minimization of $E \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 }$ by using calculus, but there
is a simpler method. (See Exercise 2.19 for a calculus-based proof.) Using the belief
that there is something special about $E X ,$ we write

$$E \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 } = E \left( X - E X + E X - b \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E \left( \left( X - E X \right) + \left( E X - b \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \quad \mathrm { E } \left( X - \mathrm { E } X \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \mathrm { E } X - b \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 \mathrm { E } \left( \left( X - \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } X - b \right) \right) ,$$
$$\left( \mathrm { a d d } \pm \mathrm { E } X , \mathrm { w h i c h } \right)$$
$$\left( g r o u p \quad t e r m s \right)$$

where we have expanded the square. Now, note that

$$\mathrm { E } \left( \left( X - \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } X - b \right) \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } X - b \right) \mathrm { E } \left( X - \mathrm { E } X \right) = 0 ,$$

since $\left( E X \quad - \quad b \right)$ is constant and comes out of the expectation, and $E \left( X \quad - \quad E X \right) =$
$E X - \quad E X = 0 .$ This means that

(2.2.2)
$$E \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 } = E \left( X - E X \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( E X - b \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

We have no control over the first term on the right-hand side of (2.2.2), and the
second term, which is always greater than or equal to 0, can be made equal to 0 by
choosing $b = \mathrm { E } X .$ Hence,

(2.2.3)
$$\min _ { b } \mathrm { E } \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { E } \left( X - \mathrm { E } X \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Sce Exercise 2.18 for a similar result about the median.
!!

When evaluating expectations of nonlinear functions of $X ,$ we can proceed in one
of two ways. From the definition of $\mathrm { E } g \left( X \right) ,$ we could directly calculate

(2.2.4)
$$E g \left( X \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x .$$

But we could also find the pdf $f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ of $Y = g \left( X \right)$ and we would have

(2.2.5)
$$E g \left( X \right) = E Y = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } y f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y .$$

Example 2.2.7 (Uniform-exponential relationship-II) Let $X$ have a uniform(0,1)
distribution, that is, the pdf of $X \quad i s \quad g i v e n$ by

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right.$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="59" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MOMENTS AND MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

and define a new random variable $g \left( X \right) = - \log X .$ Then

$$E g \left( X \right) = E \left( - \log X \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \log x d x = x - x \log x | _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } = 1 .$$

But we also saw in Example 2.1.4 that $Y = - \log X$ has cdf $1 - e ^ { - y }$ and, hence, pdf
$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \frac { d } { d y } \left( 1 - e ^ { - y } \right) = e ^ { - y } ,$ $0 < y < \infty ,$ which is a special case of the exponential
pdf with $\lambda = 1$ 1. Thus, by Example 2.2.2, $\mathrm { E } Y = 1$ 1.
Il


## 2.3 Moments and Moment Generating Functions

The various moments of a distribution are an important class of expectations.

Definition 2.3.1 For each integer n, the nth moment of $X$ (or $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ $\mu _ { n } ^ { \prime } ,$ is

$$\mu _ { n } ^ { \prime } = E X ^ { n } .$$

The nth central moment of $X ,$ $\mu _ { n } ,$ is

$$\mu _ { n } = E \left( X - \mu \right) ^ { n } ,$$

where $\mu = \mu _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = E X .$

Aside from the mean, $\mathrm { E } X ,$ of a random variable, perhaps the most important
moment is the second central moment, more commonly known as the variance.

Definition 2.3.2 The variance of a random variable $X$ is its second central moment,
Var $X = E \left( X - E X \right) ^ { 2 } .$ The positive square root of Var $X$ is the standard deviation
of $X .$

The variance gives a measure of the degree of spread of a distribution around its
mean. We saw earlier in Example 2.2.6 that the quantity $E \left( X - b \right) ^ { 2 }$ is minimized by
choosing $b = E X .$ Now we consider the absolute size of this minimum. The inter-
pretation attached to the variance is that larger values mean $X$ is more variable. At
the extreme, if Var $X = E \left( X - E X \right) ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$ then $X$ is equal to $\mathrm { E } X$ with probability
☒
1, and there is no variation in $X .$ The standard deviation has the same qualitative
interpretation: Small values mean $X$ is very likely to be close to $\mathrm { E } X ,$ and large val-
ues mean $X$ is very variable. The standard deviation is easier to interpret in that
the measurement unit on the standard deviation is the same as that for the original
variable $X .$ The measurement unit on the variance is the square of the original unit.

Example 2.3.3 (Exponential variance) Let $X$ have the exponential(A) distri-
bution, defined in Example 2.2.2. There we calculated $\mathrm { E } X = \lambda$ ), and we can now
calculate the variance by

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = E \left( X - \lambda \right) ^ { 2 } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( x - \lambda \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \lambda + \lambda ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="60" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

$x$

</figure>


To complete the integration, we can integrate cach of the terms separately, using
integration by parts on the terms involving $x$ and $x ^ { 2 } .$ Upon doing this, we find that
$\mathrm { V a r } X = \lambda ^ { 2 } .$

Il

We see that the variance of an exponential distribution is directly related to the
parameter $\lambda .$ Figure 2.3.1 shows several exponential distributions corresponding to
different values of $\lambda .$ Notice how the distribution is more concentrated about its mean
for smaller values of $\lambda .$ The behavior of the variance of an exponential, as a function
of $\lambda ,$ is a special case of the variance behavior summarized in the following theorem.

Theorem 2.3.4 If $X$ is a random variable with finite variance, then for any con-
stants $a$ and $b ,$

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( a X + b \right) = a ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } X .$$

Proof: From the definition, we have

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( a X + b \right) = E \left( \left( a X + b \right) - E \left( a X + b \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E \left( a X - a E X \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= a ^ { 2 } E \left( X - E X \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= a ^ { 2 } \text { Var } X .$$

$\left( E \left( a X + b \right) = a E X + b \right)$
☐

$I t$ is sometimes casier to use an alternative formula for the variance, given by

(2.3.1)
$$\mathrm { V a r } X = E X ^ { 2 } - \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

which is casily established by noting

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="61" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MOMENTS AND MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTIONS" -->

Var $X = E \left( X - E X \right) ^ { 2 } = E \left[ X ^ { 2 } - 2 X E X + \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } \right]$

$$= E X ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E X ^ { 2 } - \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

where we use the fact that $E \left( X E X \right) = \left( E X \right) \left( E X \right) = \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ since $\mathrm { E } X$ is a constant.
We now illustrate some moment calculations with a discrete distribution.

Example 2.3.5 (Binomial variance) Let $X \sim \mathrm { b i n o m i a l } \left( n , p \right) ,$ that is,

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , n$$

We have previously seen that $E X = n p .$ To calculate Var $X$ we first calculate $\mathrm { E } X ^ { 2 } .$
We have

(2.3.2)
$$E X ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } x ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } .$$

In order $t o$ sum this series, we must first manipulate the binomial coefficient in a
manner similar to that used for $\mathrm { E } X$ (Example 2.2.3). We write

(2.3.3)
$$x ^ { 2 } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) = x \frac { n ! } { \left( x - 1 \right) ! \left( n - x \right) ! } = x n \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { x - 1 } \end{array} \right)$$

The summand in (2.3.2) corresponding to $x = 0$ is 0, and using (2.3.3), we have

$$E X ^ { 2 } = n \sum _ { x = 1 } ^ { n } x \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { x - 1 } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x }$$
$$= n \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( y + 1 \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n - 1 } \\ { y } \end{array} \right) p ^ { y + 1 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - 1 - y }$$
$$= n p \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } y \left( \begin{array}{} n - 1 \\ y \end{array} \right) p ^ { y } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - 1 - y } + n p \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} n - 1 \\ y \end{array} \right) p ^ { y } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - 1 - y } .$$
$$\left( \sin g y = x - 1 \right)$$

Now it is easy to see that the first sum is equal to $\left( n - \quad 1 \right) p$ (since it is the mean of a
binomial(n $\left. \left. - \quad 1 , p \right) \right) ,$ while the second sum is equal to 1. Hence,

(2.3.4)
$$E X ^ { 2 } = n \left( n - 1 \right) p ^ { 2 } + n p .$$

Using (2.3.1), we have

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = n \left( n - 1 \right) p ^ { 2 } + n p - \left( n p \right) ^ { 2 } = - n p ^ { 2 } + n p = n p \left( 1 - p \right) .$$

Calculation of higher moments proceeds in an analogous manner, but usually the
mathematical manipulations become quite involved. In applications, moments of or-
der 3 or 4 are sometimes of interest, but there is usually little statistical reason for
examining higher moments than these.

II

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="62" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

We now introduce a new function that is associated with a probability distribution,
the moment generating function (mgf). As its name suggests, the mgf can be used
to generate moments. In practice, it is easier in many cases to calculate moments
directly than to use the mgf. However, the main use of the mgf is not to generate
moments, but to help in characterizing a distribution. This property can lead to some
extremely powerful results when used properly.

Definition 2.3.6 Let $X$ be a random variable with cdf $F _ { X } .$ The moment generating
function (mgf ) of $X$ (or $F _ { X } ,$ denoted by $M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ is

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = E e ^ { t X } ,$$

provided that the expectation exists for $t$ in some neighborhood of 0. That is, there
is an $h > 0$ such that, for all $t$ in $- h < t <$ h, $\mathrm { E } e ^ { t X }$ exists. If the expectation does
not exist in a neighborhood of 0, we say that the moment generating function does
not exist.

More explicitly, we can write the mgf of $X$ as

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } e ^ { t x } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x \quad \text { if } X \text { is continuous } ,$$

or

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \sum _ { x } e ^ { t x } P \left( X = x \right) \quad \text { if } X \text { is discrete } .$$

It is very easy to see how the mgf generates moments. We summarize the result in
the following theorem.

Theorem 2.3.7 If $X$ has mgf $M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ then

$$E X ^ { n } = M _ { X } ^ { \left( n \right) } \left( 0 \right) ,$$

where we define

$$M _ { X } ^ { \left( n \right) } \left( 0 \right) = \frac { d ^ { n } } { d t ^ { n } } M _ { X } \left( t \right) | _ { t = 0 } .$$

That is, the nth moment is equal $t o$ the nth derivative $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$ evaluated at $t = 0 .$

Proof: Assuming that we can differentiate under the integral sign (see the next
section), we have

$$\frac { d } { d t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \frac { d } { d t } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } e ^ { t x } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( \frac { d } { d t } e ^ { t x } \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( x e ^ { t x } \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= E X e ^ { t X } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="63" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MOMENTS AND MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

Thus,

$$\frac { d } { d t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) | _ { t = 0 } = E X e ^ { t X } | _ { t = 0 } = E X$$

Proceeding in an analogous manner, we can establish that

$$\frac { d ^ { n } } { d t ^ { n } } M _ { X } \left( t \right) | _ { t = 0 } = E X ^ { n } e ^ { t X } | _ { t = 0 } = E X ^ { n }$$

Example 2.3.8 (Gamma mgf) In Example 2.1.6 we encountered a special case
of the gamma $\mathrm { p d f } ,$

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } , \quad 0 < x < \infty , \quad \alpha > 0 , \quad \beta > 0 ,$$

where $\Gamma \left( \alpha \right)$ denotes the gamma function, some of whose properties are given in Section
3.3. The mgf is given by

(2.3.5)

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { t x } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - \left( \frac { 1 } { \beta } - t \right) x } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x / \left( \frac { \beta } { 1 - \beta t } \right) } d x .$$

We now recognize the integrand in (2.3.5) as the kernel of another gamma pdf.
(The kernel of a function is the main part of the function, the part that remains
when constants are disregarded.) Using the fact that, for any positive constants a
and $b ,$

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( a \right) b ^ { a } } x ^ { a - 1 } e ^ { - x / b }$$

is a pdf, we have that

$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( a \right) b ^ { a } } x ^ { a - 1 } e ^ { - x / b } d x = 1$$

and, hence,

(2.3.6)
$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { a - 1 } e ^ { - x / b } d x = \Gamma \left( a \right) b ^ { a } .$$

Applying (2.3.6) to (2.3.5), we have

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \left( \frac { \beta } { 1 - \beta t } \right) ^ { \alpha } = \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 - \beta t } \right) ^ { \alpha } \quad \mathrm { i f } t < \frac { 1 } { \beta } .$$

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="64" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

If $t \geq 1 / \beta ,$ then the quantity $\left( 1 / \beta \right) - t ,$ in the integrand of (2.3.5), is nonpositive and
the integral in (2.3.6) is infinite. Thus, the mgf of the gamma distribution exists only
if $t < 1 / \beta .$ (In Section 3.3 we will explore the gamma function in more detail.)

The mean of the gamma distribution is given by

$$E X = \frac { d } { d t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) | _ { t = 0 } = \frac { \alpha \beta } { \left( 1 - \beta t \right) ^ { \alpha + 1 } } | _ { t = 0 } = \alpha \beta$$

Other moments can be calculated in a similar manner.

II

Example 2.3.9 (Binomial mgf) For a second illustration of calculating a moment
generating function, we consider a discrete distribution, the binomial distribution. The
$b i n o m i a l \left( n , p \right)$ pmf is given in (2.1.3). So

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } e ^ { t x } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) \left( p e ^ { t } \right) ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x } .$$

The binomial formula (see Theorem 3.2.2) gives

(2.3.7)
$$\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) u ^ { x } v ^ { n - x } = \left( u + v \right) ^ { n } .$$

Hence, letting $u = p e ^ { t }$ and $v = 1 \quad - \quad p ,$ we have

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \left[ p e ^ { t } + \left( 1 - p \right) \right] ^ { n } .$$

II

As previously mentioned, the major usefulness of the moment generating function is
not in its ability to generate moments. Rather, its usefulness stems from the fact that,
in many cases, the moment generating function can characterize a distribution. There
are, however, some technical difficulties associated with using moments to characterize
a distribution, which we will now investigate.

If the mgf exists, it characterizes an infinite set of moments. The natural question is
whether characterizing the infinite set of moments uniquely determines a distribution
function. The answer to this question, unfortunately, is no. Characterizing the set of
moments is not enough to determine a distribution uniquely because there may be
two distinct random variables having the same moments.

Example 2.3.10 (Nonunique moments) Consider the two pdfs given by

$$f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } x } e ^ { - \left( \log x \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad 0 \leq x < \infty ,$$
$$f _ { 2 } \left( x \right) = f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \left[ 1 + \sin \left( 2 \pi \log x \right) \right] , \quad 0 \leq x < \infty .$$

(The pdf $f _ { 1 }$ is a special case of a lognormal pdf.)

It can be shown that if $X _ { 1 } \sim f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) ,$ then

$$E X _ { 1 } ^ { r } = e ^ { r ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad r = 0 , 1 , \ldots ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="65" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MOMENTS AND MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTIONS" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 2.3.2. Two pdfs with the same moments: $f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } x } e ^ { - \left( \log x \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 }$ and $f _ { 2 } \left( x \right) =$ ☒ $f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \left[ 1 + \sin \left( 2 \pi \log x \right) \right]$</figcaption>

.2

2

4

$x$

</figure>


so $X _ { 1 }$ has all of its moments. Now suppose that $X _ { 2 } \sim f _ { 2 } \left( x \right) .$ We have
☒

$$X X _ { 2 } ^ { r } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { r } f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \left[ 1 + \sin \left( 2 \pi \log x \right) \right] d x$$
$$= E X _ { 1 } ^ { r } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { r } f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \sin \left( 2 \pi \log x \right) d x .$$

However, the transformation $y = \log x - r$ shows that this last integral is that of
an odd function over $\left( - \infty , \infty \right)$ and hence is equal to 0 for $r = 0 , 1 , \ldots .$ Thus, even
though $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ have distinct pdfs, they have the same moments for all $r$ r. The two
pdfs are pictured in Figure 2.3.2.

See Exercise 2.35 for details and also Exercises 2.34, 2.36, and 2.37 for more about
mgfs and distributions.
11

The problem of uniqueness of moments does not occur if the random variables
have bounded support. If that is the case, then the infinite sequence of moments
does uniquely determine the distribution (see, for example, Billingsley 1995, Section
30). Furthermore, if the mgf exists in a neighborhood of 0, then the distribution $i s$
uniquely determined, no matter what its support. Thus, existence $\mathrm { o f }$ all moments is
not equivalent to existence of the moment generating function. The following theorem
shows how a distribution can be characterized.

Theorem 2.3.11 Let $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $F _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ be two cdfs all of whose moments exist.

a. If $X$ and $Y$ have bounded support, then $F _ { X } \left( u \right) = F _ { Y } \left( u \right)$ for all $u$ $i f$ and only if
$\mathrm { E } X ^ { r } = \mathrm { E } Y ^ { r }$ for all integers $r = 0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots .$

b. If the moment generating functions exist and $M _ { X } \left( t \right) = M _ { Y } \left( t \right)$ for all $t$ in some
neighborhood of $0 ,$ then $F _ { X } \left( u \right) = F _ { Y } \left( u \right)$ for all u.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="66" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

In the next theorem, which deals with a sequence of mgfs that converges, we do
not treat the bounded support case separately. Note that the uniqueness assump-
tion is automatically satisfied if the limiting mgf exists in a neighborhood of 0 (see
Miscellanea 2.6.1).

Theorem 2.3.12 (Convergence of mgfs)
Suppose $\left\{ X _ { i } , i = 1 , 2 , \ldots \right\}$ is a se-
quence of random variables, each with mgf $M _ { X _ { i } } \left( t \right) .$ Furthermore, suppose that

$$\lim _ { i \rightarrow \infty } M _ { X _ { i } } \left( t \right) = M _ { X } \left( t \right) , \quad \text { for all } t \text { in a neighborhood of } 0 ,$$

and $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$ is an mgf. Then there is a unique cdf $F _ { X }$ whose moments are determined
by $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$ and, for all $x$ where $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ is continuous, we have

$$\lim _ { i \rightarrow \infty } F _ { X _ { i } } \left( x \right) = F _ { X } \left( x \right) .$$

That $i s ,$ convergence, for $| t | < h ,$ of mgfs to an mgf implies convergence of cdfs.

The proofs of Theorems 2.3.11 and 2.3.12 rely on the theory of Laplace transforms.
(The classic reference is Widder 1946, but Laplace transforms also get a comprehen-
sive treatment by Feller 1971.) The defining equation for $M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ that is,

(2.3.8)
$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } e ^ { t x } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x ,$$

defines a Laplace transform $\left( M _ { X } \left( t \right) \right.$ is the Laplace transform of $f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ A key fact
about Laplace transforms is their uniqueness. If (2.3.8) is valid for all $t$ such that
$I t | < h ,$ where $h$ is some positive number, then given $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$ there is only one function
$f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ that satisfies (2.3.8). Given this fact, the two previous theorems are quite
reasonable. While rigorous proofs of these theorems are not beyond the scope of this
book, the proofs are technical in nature and provide no real understanding. We omit
them.

The possible nonuniqueness of the moment sequence is an annoyance. If we show
that a sequence of moments converges, we will not be able to conclude formally that
the random variables converge. To do so, we would have to verify the uniqueness of
the moment sequence, a generally horrible $\mathrm { j o b }$ (see Miscellanea 2.6.1). However, if
the sequence of mgfs converges in a neighborhood of 0, then the random variables
converge. Thus, we can consider the convergence of mgfs as a sufficient, but not
necessary, condition for the sequence of random variables to converge.

Example 2.3.13 (Poisson approximation) One approximation that is usually
taught in elementary statistics courses is that binomial probabilities (see Example
2.3.5) can be approximated by Poisson probabilities, which are generally easier to
calculate. The binomial distribution is characterized by two quantities, denoted by $n$
and $p .$ It is taught that the Poisson approximation is valid "when $n$ is large and $n p$
is small," and rules of thumb are sometimes given.

The $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ pmf is given by

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { x } } { x ! } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="67" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MOMENTS AND MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.3" -->

where $\lambda$ is a positive constant. The approximation states that if $X \sim \mathrm { b i n o m i a l } \left( n , p \right)$
and $Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ with $\lambda = n p ,$ then

(2.3.9)
$$P \left( X = x \right) \approx P \left( Y = x \right)$$

for large $n$ and small $n p .$ We now show that the mgfs converge, lending credence to
this approximation. Recall that

(2.3.10)
$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \left[ p e ^ { t } + \left( 1 - p \right) \right] ^ { n } .$$

For the $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ distribution, we can calculate (see Exercise 2.33)

$$M _ { Y } \left( t \right) = e ^ { \lambda \left( e ^ { t } - 1 \right) }$$

and if we define $p = \lambda / n ,$ then $M _ { X } \left( t \right) \rightarrow M _ { Y } \left( t \right)$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$ co. The validity of the
approximation in $\left( 2 . 3 . 9 \right)$ will then follow from Theorem 2.3.12.

We first must digress a bit and mention an important limit result, one that has wide
applicability in statistics. The proof of this lemma may be found in many standard
calculus texts.

Lemma 2.3.14 Let $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be a sequence of numbers converging to $a$ a, that is,
$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } a _ { n } = a .$ Then

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 + \frac { a _ { n } } { n } \right) ^ { n } = e ^ { a } .$$

Returning to the example, we have

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \left[ p e ^ { t } + \left( 1 - p \right) \right] ^ { n } = \left[ 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } \left( e ^ { t } - 1 \right) \left( n p \right) \right] ^ { n } = \left[ 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } \left( e ^ { t } - 1 \right) \lambda \right] ^ { n } ,$$

because $\lambda = n p .$ Now set $a _ { n } = a = \left( e ^ { t } - 1 \right) \lambda ,$ and apply Lemma 2.3.14 to get

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } M _ { X } \left( t \right) = e ^ { \lambda \left( e ^ { t } - 1 \right) } = M _ { Y } \left( t \right) ,$$

the moment generating function of the Poisson.

The Poisson approximation can be quite good even for moderate $p$ and n. In Figure
2.3.3 we show a binomial mass function along with its Poisson approximation, with
$\lambda = n p .$ The approximation appears to be satisfactory.
|

We close this section with a useful result concerning mgfs.

Theorem 2.3.15 For any constants a and $b ,$ the mgf of the random variable $a X + b$
is given by

$$M _ { a X + b } \left( t \right) = e ^ { b t } M _ { X } \left( a t \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="68" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 2.3.3. Poisson (dotted line) approximation to the binomial (solid line), $n = 1 5 ,$ $p = . 3$</figcaption>

3

.2

1

İ

0

!

0
