14

$x$

</figure>


Proof: By definition,

$$M _ { a X + b } \left( t \right) = E \left( e ^ { \left( a X + b \right) t } \right)$$
$$= E \left( e ^ { \left( a X \right) t } e ^ { b t } \right)$$
$$= e ^ { b t } E \left( e ^ { \left( a t \right) X } \right)$$
$$= e ^ { b t } M _ { X } \left( a t \right) ,$$

(properties of exponentials)

$\left( e ^ { b t } \text { is constant } \right)$

$$\left( \text { definition of } \mathrm { m g f } \right)$$

proving the theorem.

☐


## 2.4 Differentiating Under an Integral Sign

In the previous section we encountered an instance in which we desired to interchange
the order of integration and differentiation. This situation is encountered frequently in
theoretical statistics. The purpose of this section is to characterize conditions under
which this operation is legitimate. We will also discuss interchanging the order $\mathrm { o f }$
differentiation and summation.

Many of these conditions can be established using standard theorems from calculus
and detailed proofs can be found in most calculus textbooks. Thus, detailed proofs
will not be presented here.

We first want to establish the method of calculating

(2.4.1)
$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { a \left( \theta \right) } ^ { b \left( \theta \right) } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x ,$$

where $- \infty < a \left( \theta \right) ,$ $b \left( \theta \right) < \infty$ for all 0. The rule for differentiating (2.4.1) is called
Leibnitz's Rule and is an application of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and
the chain rule.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="69" -->
<!-- PageHeader="DIFFERENTIATING UNDER $A N$ INTEGRAL SIGN" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->

Theorem 2.4.1 (Leibnitz's Rule) If $f \left( x , \theta \right) ,$ $a \left( \theta \right) ,$ and $b \left( \theta \right)$ are differentiable with
respect to $\theta ,$ then

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { a \left( \theta \right) } ^ { b \left( \theta \right) } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x = f \left( b \left( \theta \right) , \theta \right) \frac { d } { d \theta } b \left( \theta \right) - f \left( a \left( \theta \right) , \theta \right) \frac { d } { d \theta } a \left( \theta \right) + \int _ { a \left( \theta \right) } ^ { b \left( \theta \right) } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x .$$

Notice that if $a \left( \theta \right)$ and $b \left( \theta \right)$ are constant, we have a special case of Leibnitz's Rule:

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x .$$

Thus, in general, if we have the integral of a differentiable function over a finite range,
differentiation of the integral poses no problem. If the range of integration is infinite,
however, problems can arise.

Note that the interchange of derivative and integral in the above equation equates
a partial derivative with an ordinary derivative. Formally, this must be the case since
the left-hand side is a function of only $\theta ,$ while the integrand on the right-hand side
is a function of both $\theta$ and x.

The question of whether interchanging the order of differentiation and integration
is justified is really a question of whether limits and integration can be interchanged,
since a derivative is a special kind of limit. Recall that if $f \left( x , \theta \right)$ is differentiable, then

$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) = \lim _ { \delta \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f \left( x , \theta + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta \right) } { \delta } ,$$

so we $h a v e$

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \lim _ { \delta \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { f \left( x , \theta + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta \right) } { \delta } \right] d x ,$$

while

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x = \lim _ { \delta \rightarrow 0 } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left[ \frac { f \left( x , \theta + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta \right) } { \delta } \right] d x .$$

Therefore, if we can justify the interchanging of the order of limits and integration,
differentiation under the integral sign will be justified. Treatment of this problem
in full generality will, unfortunately, necessitate the use of measure theory, a topic
that will not be covered in this book. However, the statements and conclusions of
some important results can be given. The following theorems are all corollaries of
Lebesgue's Dominated Convergence Theorem (see, for example, Rudin 1976).

Theorem 2.4.2 Suppose the function $h \left( x , y \right)$ is continuous at $y _ { 0 }$ for each $x ,$ and
there exists a function $g \left( x \right)$ satisfying

i. $| h \left( x , y \right) | \leq g \left( x \right)$ for $a l l \quad x$ and $y ,$

ii. $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) d x < \infty .$

Then

$$\lim _ { y \rightarrow y _ { 0 } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( x , y \right) d x = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \lim _ { y \rightarrow y _ { 0 } } h \left( x , y \right) d x .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="70" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->

The key condition in this theorem is the existence of a dominating function $g \left( x \right) ,$
with a finite integral, which ensures that the integrals cannot be too badly behaved.
We can now apply this theorem to the case we are considering by identifying $h \left( x , y \right)$
with the difference $\left( f \left( x , \theta + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta \right) \right) / \delta .$

Theorem 2.4.3 Suppose $f \left( x , \theta \right)$ is differentiable at $\theta = \theta _ { 0 } ,$ that is,

$$\lim _ { \delta \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right) } { \delta } = \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) | _ { \theta = \theta _ { 0 } }$$

exists for $e v e r y$ x, and there exists a function $g \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ and a constant $\delta _ { 0 } > 0$ such that

i.
$| \frac { f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right) } { \delta } | \leq g \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right)$
for all $x$ and $| \delta | \leq \delta _ { 0 } ,$

ii. $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right) d x < \infty .$

Then

(2.4.2)
$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x | _ { \theta = \theta _ { 0 } } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left[ \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) | _ { \theta = \theta _ { 0 } } \right] d x$$

Condition (i) is similar to what is known as a Lipschitz condition, a condition
that imposes smoothness on a function. Here, condition (i) is effectively bounding
the variability in the first derivative; other smoothness constraints might bound this
variability by a constant (instead of a function $\left. g \right) ,$ or place a bound on the variability
of the second derivative of $f .$

The conclusion of Theorem 2.4.3 is a little cumbersome, but it is important to realize
that although we seem to be treating $\theta$ as a variable, the statement of the theorem
is for one value of $\theta .$ That is, for each value $\theta _ { 0 }$ for which $f \left( x , \theta \right)$ is differentiable at
$\theta _ { 0 }$ and satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), the order of integration and differentiation can
be interchanged. Often the distinction between $\theta$ and $\theta _ { 0 }$ is not stressed and (2.4.2) is
written

(2.4.3)
$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) d x .$$

Typically, $f \left( x , \theta \right)$ is differentiable at all $\theta ,$ not at $\mathrm { i u s t }$ one value $\theta _ { 0 } .$ In this case,
condition (i) of Theorem 2.4.3 can be replaced by another condition that often proves
easier to verify. By an application of the mean value theorem, it follows that, for fixed
$x$ and $\theta _ { 0 } ,$ and $| \delta | \leq \delta _ { 0 } ,$

$$\frac { f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } + \delta \right) - f \left( x , \theta _ { 0 } \right) } { \delta } = \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) | _ { \theta = \theta _ { 0 } + \delta ^ { * } \left( x \right) }$$

for some number $\delta ^ { * } \left( x \right) ,$ where $| \delta ^ { * } \left( x \right) | \leq \delta _ { 0 } .$ Therefore, condition (i) will be satisfied
if we find a $g \left( x , \theta \right)$ that satisfies condition (ii) and

(2.4.4)
$$| \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x , \theta \right) | _ { \theta = \theta ^ { \prime } } | \leq g \left( x , \theta \right) \quad \text { for all } \theta ^ { \prime } \text { such that } | \theta ^ { \prime } - \theta | \leq \delta _ { 0 } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="71" -->
<!-- PageHeader="DIFFERENTIATING UNDER AN INTEGRAL SIGN" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->

Note that in (2.4.4) $\delta _ { 0 }$ is implicitly a function of $\theta ,$ as is the case in Theorem 2.4.3.
This is permitted since the theorem is applied to each value of $\theta$ individually. From
(2.4.4) we get the following corollary.

Corollary 2.4.4 Suppose $f \left( x , \theta \right)$ is differentiable in $\theta$ and there exists a function
$g \left( x , \theta \right)$ such that $\left( 2 . 4 \cdot 4 \right)$ is satisfied and $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x , \theta \right) d x < \infty$ . Then $\left( 2 . 4 . 3 \right)$ holds.

Notice that both condition (i) of Theorem 2.4.3 and $\left( 2 . 4 . 4 \right)$ impose a uniformity
requirement on the functions to be bounded; some type of uniformity is generally
needed before derivatives and integrals can be interchanged.

Example 2.4.5 (Interchanging integration and differentiation-I) Let $X$
have the exponential() pdf given by $f \left( x \right) = \left( 1 / \lambda \right) e ^ { - x / \lambda } ,$ $0 < x < \infty ,$ and suppose
we want to calculate

(2.4.5)
$$\frac { d } { d \lambda } E X ^ { n } = \frac { d } { d \lambda } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \right) e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x$$

for integer $n > 0 .$ If we could move the differentiation inside the integral, we would
have

$$\frac { d } { d \lambda } E X ^ { n } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda } x ^ { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \right) e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { x ^ { n } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { x } { \lambda } - 1 \right) e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } \mathbb{E} X ^ { n + 1 } - \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \mathrm { E } X ^ { n } .$$

(2.4.6)

To justify the interchange of integration and differentiation, we bound the derivative
of $x ^ { n } \left( 1 / \lambda \right) e ^ { - x / \lambda } .$ Now

$$| \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda } \left( \frac { x ^ { n } e ^ { - x \lambda } } { \lambda } \right) | = \frac { x ^ { n } e ^ { - x / \lambda } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } | \frac { x } { \lambda } - 1 | \leq \frac { x ^ { n } e ^ { - x / \lambda } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { x } { \lambda } + 1 \right) . \quad \left( \mathrm { s i n c e } \frac { x } { \lambda } > 0 \right)$$

For some constant $\delta _ { 0 }$ satisfying $0 < \delta _ { 0 } < \lambda ,$ take

$$g \left( x , \lambda \right) = \frac { x ^ { n } e ^ { - x / \left( \lambda + \delta _ { 0 } \right) } } { \left( \lambda - \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { x } { \lambda - \delta _ { 0 } } + 1 \right) .$$

We then have

$$| \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda } \left( \frac { x ^ { n } e ^ { - x / \lambda } } { \lambda } \right) | _ { \lambda = \lambda ^ { \prime } } | \leq g \left( x , \lambda \right) \quad \text { for all } \lambda ^ { \prime } \text { such that } | \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda | \leq \delta _ { 0 } .$$

Since the exponential distribution has all of its moments, $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x , \lambda \right) d x < \infty$ as long
as $\lambda - \delta _ { 0 } > 0 ,$ so the interchange of integration and differentiation is justified.
||

The property illustrated for the exponential distribution holds for a large class of
densities, which will be dealt with in Section 3.4.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="72" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS Section 2.4" -->

Notice that (2.4.6) gives us a recursion relation for the moments of the exponential
distribution,

(2.4.7)
$$E X ^ { n + 1 } = \lambda E X ^ { n } + \lambda ^ { 2 } \frac { d } { d \lambda } E X ^ { n } ,$$

making the calculation of the $\left( n + 1 \right) s t$ moment relatively easy. This type of relation-
ship exists for other distributions. In particular, if $X$ has a normal distribution with
mean $\mu$ and variance 1, so it has pdf $f \left( x \right) = \left( 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } \right) e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } ,$ then

$$E X ^ { n + 1 } = \mu E X ^ { n } - \frac { d } { d \mu } E X ^ { n } .$$

We illustrate one more interchange of differentiation and integration, one involving
the moment generating function.

Example 2.4.6 (Interchanging integration and differentiation-II) Again
let $X$ have a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance 1, and consider the mgf
of $X ,$

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = E e ^ { t X } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } e ^ { t x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } d x .$$

In Section 2.3 it was stated that we can calculate moments by differentiation of $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$
and differentiation under the integral sign was justified:

(2.4.8)
$$\frac { d } { d t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \frac { d } { d t } E e ^ { t X } = E \frac { \partial } { \partial t } e ^ { t X } = E \left( X e ^ { t X } \right) .$$

We can apply the results of this section to justify the operations in (2.4.8). Notice
that when applying either Theorem 2.4.3 or Corollary 2.4.4 here, we identify $t$ with
the variable $\theta$ in Theorem 2.4.3. The parameter $\mu$ is treated as a constant.

From Corollary 2.4.4, we must find a function $g \left( x , t \right) ,$ with finite integral, that
satisfies

(2.4.9)
$$\frac { \partial } { \partial t } e ^ { t x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | _ { t = t ^ { \prime } } \leq g \left( x , t \right) \quad \text { for all } t ^ { \prime } \text { such that } | t ^ { \prime } - t | \leq \delta _ { 0 } .$$

Doing the obvious, we have

$$| \frac { \partial } { \partial t } e ^ { t x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | = | x e ^ { t x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | \leq | x | e ^ { t x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } .$$

It is easiest to define our function $g \left( x , t \right)$ separately for $x \geq 0$ and $x < 0 .$ We take

$$g \left( x , t \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { | x | e ^ { \left( t - \delta _ { 0 } \right) x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { | x | e ^ { \left( t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) x } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.$$

$I t$ is clear that this function satisfies (2.4.9); it remains to check that its integral is
finite.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="73" -->
<!-- PageHeader="DIFFERENTIATING UNDER AN INTEGRAL SIGN" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->

For $x \geq 0$ we have

$$g \left( x , t \right) = x e ^ { - \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) + \mu ^ { 2 } \right) / 2 }$$

We now complete the square in the exponent; that is, we write

$$x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) + \mu ^ { 2 }$$
$$= x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) + \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \mu ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \left( x - \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } + \mu ^ { 2 } - \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

and $s o ,$ for $x \geq 0 ,$

$$g \left( x , t \right) = x e ^ { - \left[ x - \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - \left[ \mu ^ { 2 } - \left( \mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] / 2 } .$$

Since the last exponential factor in this expression does not depend on x, $\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } g \left( x , t \right) d x$
is essentially calculating the mean of a normal distribution with mean $\mu + t + \delta _ { 0 } ,$ except
that the integration is only over $\left[ 0 , \infty \right) .$ However, it follows that the integral is finite
because the normal distribution has a finite mean (to be shown in Chapter 3). A
similar development for $x < 0$ shows that

$$g \left( x , t \right) = | x | e ^ { - \left[ x - \left( \mu + t - \delta _ { 0 } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - \left[ \mu ^ { 2 } - \left( \mu + t - \delta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] / 2 }$$

and so $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { 0 } g \left( x , t \right) d x < \infty .$ Therefore, we have found an integrable function satisfying
(2.4.9) and the operation in (2.4.8) is justified.
|

We now turn to the question of when it is possible to interchange differentiation
and summation, an operation that plays an important role in discrete distributions.
Of course, we are concerned only with infinite sums, since a derivative can always be
taken inside a finite sum.

Example 2.4.7 (Interchanging summation and differentiation) Let $X$ be a
discrete random variable with the geometric distribution

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , \quad 0 < \theta < 1 .$$

We have that $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } = 1$ and, provided that the operations are justified,

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { d } { d \theta } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x }$$
$$= \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \left[ \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } - \theta x \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x - 1 } \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } x \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } .$$

Since $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } = 1$ for all $0 < \theta < 1 ,$ its derivative is 0. So we have

(2.4.10)
$$\frac { 1 } { \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } x \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } = 0 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="74" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS Section 2.4" -->

Now the first sum in (2.4.10) is equal to 1 and the second sum is $\mathrm { E } X$ hence (2.4.10)
becomes

$$\frac { 1 } { \theta } - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \theta } E X = 0$$

or

$$E X = \frac { 1 - \theta } { \theta } .$$

We have, in essence, summed the series $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } x \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x }$ by differentiating.
!!

Justification for taking the derivative inside the summation is more straightforward
than the integration case. The following theorem provides the details.

Theorem 2.4.8 Suppose that the series $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } h \left( \theta , x \right)$ converges for all $\theta$ in an
interval $\left( a , b \right)$ of real numbers and

i. $\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } h \left( \theta , x \right)$ is continuous in $\theta$ for each x,

ii. $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } h \left( \theta , x \right)$ converges uniformly on every closed bounded subinterval of $\left( a , b \right) .$
Then

(2.4.11)
$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } h \left( \theta , x \right) = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } h \left( \theta , x \right)$$

The condition of uniform convergence is the key one to verify in order to establish
that the differentiation can be taken inside the summation. Recall that a series con-
verges uniformly if its sequence of partial sums converges uniformly, a fact that we
use in the following example.

Example 2.4.9 (Continuation of Example 2.4.7) To apply Theorem 2.4.8 we
identify

$$h \left( \theta , x \right) = \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x }$$

and

$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } h \left( \theta , x \right) = \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } - \theta x \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x - 1 } ,$$

and verify that $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } h \left( \theta , x \right)$ converges uniformly. Define $S _ { n } \left( \theta \right)$ by

$$S _ { n } \left( \theta \right) = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \left[ \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } - \theta x \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x - 1 } \right] .$$

The convergence will be uniform on $\left[ c , d \right] \subset \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ if, given $\epsilon > 0 ,$ we can find an $N$
such that

$$n > N \Rightarrow | S _ { n } \left( \theta \right) - S _ { \infty } \left( \theta \right) | < \epsilon \quad \text { for all } \theta \in \left[ c , d \right] .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="75" -->
<!-- PageHeader="DIFFERENTIATING UNDER AN INTEGRAL SIGN" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.4" -->

Recall the partial sum of the geometric series (1.5.3). If $y \neq 1 ,$ then we can write

$$\sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { n } y ^ { k } = \frac { 1 - y ^ { n + 1 } } { 1 - y }$$

Applying this, we have

$$\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x } = \frac { 1 - \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n + 1 } } { \theta }$$
$$\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \theta x \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x - 1 } = \theta \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } - \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x }$$
$$= - \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x }$$
$$= - \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left[ \frac { 1 - \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n + 1 } } { \theta } \right]$$

Here we (justifiably) pull the derivative through the finite sum. Calculating this
derivative gives

$$\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { n } \theta x \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { x - 1 } = \frac { \left( 1 - \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n + 1 } \right) - \left( n + 1 \right) \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n } } { \theta }$$

and, hence,

$$S _ { n } \left( \theta \right) = \frac { 1 - \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n + 1 } } { \theta } - \frac { \left( 1 - \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n + 1 } \right) - \left( n + 1 \right) \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n } } { \theta }$$
$$= \left( n + 1 \right) \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n } .$$

It is clear that, for $0 < \theta < 1$ 1, $S _ { \infty } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } S _ { n } \left( \theta \right) = 0 .$ Since $S _ { n } \left( \theta \right)$ is continuous,
the convergence is uniform on any closed bounded interval. Therefore, the series of
derivatives converges uniformly and the interchange of differentiation and summation
is justified.
Il

We close this section with a theorem that is similar to Theorem 2.4.8, but treats
the case of interchanging the order of summation and integration.

Theorem 2.4.10 Suppose the series $\sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } h \left( \theta , x \right)$ converges uniformly on $\left[ a , b \right]$ and
that, for each $x ,$ $h \left( \theta , x \right)$ is a continuous function $\theta .$ Then

$$\int _ { a } ^ { b } \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } h \left( \theta , x \right) d \theta = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \int _ { a } ^ { b } h \left( \theta , x \right) d \theta$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="76" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.5" -->


## 2.5 Exercises

2.1 In each of the following find the pdf of $Y .$ Show that the pdf integrates to 1.

(a) $Y = X ^ { 3 }$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 4 2 x ^ { 5 } \left( 1 - x \right) ,$ $0 < x < 1$

(b) $Y = 4 X + 3$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 7 e ^ { - 7 x } ,$ $0 < x < \infty$

(c) $Y = X ^ { 2 }$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 3 0 x ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - x \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ $0 < x < 1$

(See Example A.0.2 in Appendix $\left. A . \right)$

2.2 In each of the following find the pdf of $Y$ .

(a) $Y = X ^ { 2 }$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 1$ $0 < x < 1$

(b) $Y = - \log X$ and $X$ has pdf

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \frac { \left( n + m + 1 \right) ! } { n ! m ! } x ^ { n } \left( 1 - x \right) ^ { m } , \quad 0 < x < 1 , \quad m , n \text { positive }$$

probability distribution of $Y = X / \left( X + 1 \right) .$ Note that here both $X$ and $Y$ are discrete

$$P \left( Y > y \right) \geq P \left( U > y \right) = 1 - y , \quad \text { for all } y , 0 < y < 1 ,$$

the number a.

(c) Show that if $X \sim f \left( x \right) ,$ symmetric, and $\mathrm { F } X$ exists, then $\mathrm { E } X = a .$

2.27 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be a pdf, and let $a$ be a number such that if $a \geq x \geq y ,$ then $f \left( a \right) \geq f \left( x \right) \geq$
$f \left( y \right) ,$ and if $a \leq x \leq y ,$ then $f \left( a \right) \geq f \left( x \right) \geq f \left( y \right) .$ Such a pdf is called unimodal with
a mode equal to a.

(a) Give an example of a unimodal pdf for which the mode is unique.

(b) Give an example of a unimodal pdf for which the mode is not unique.

point of symmetry is a mode.

(d) Consider the pdf $f \left( x \right) = e ^ { - x } ,$ $x \geq 0 .$ Show that this pdf is unimodal. What is its
mode?

2.28 Let $\mu _ { n }$ denote the nth central moment of a random variable $X .$ Two quantities of
interest, in addition to the mean and variance, are

$$\alpha _ { 3 } = \frac { \mu _ { 3 } } { \left( \mu _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \quad \text { and } \quad \alpha _ { 4 } = \frac { \mu _ { 4 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } }$$

The value $\alpha _ { 3 }$ is called the skewness and $\alpha _ { 1 }$ is called the kurtosis. The skewness measures
the lack of symmetry in the pdf (see Exercise 2.26). The kurtosis, although harder to
interpret, measures the peakedness or flatness of the pdf.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="80" -->
<!-- PageHeader="TRANSFORMATIONS AND EXPECTATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 2.5" -->

(a) Show that if a pdf is symmetric about a point a, then $\alpha _ { 3 } = 0 .$

(b) Calculate $\alpha _ { 3 }$ for $f \left( x \right) = e ^ { - x } ,$ $x \geq 0 ,$ a pdf that is skewed to the right.

(c) Calculate $\alpha _ { 4 }$ for each of the following pdfs and comment on the peakedness of
each.

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty$$
$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \quad - 1 < x < 1$$
$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - | x | } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty$$

Ruppert (1987) uses influence functions (see Miscellanea 10.6.4) to explore further the
meaning of kurtosis, and Groeneveld (1991) uses them to explore skewness; see also
Balanda and MacGillivray (1988) for more on the interpretation of $\alpha _ { 4 } .$

$$P \left( y = y \right) = a \left( \frac { 1 } { y + a } \right) \frac { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { y } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { a + b - 1 } \\ { a } \end{array} \right) } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n + a + b - 1 } \\ { y + a } \end{array} \right) }$$

where $n ,$ $a ,$ and $b$ are integers, and $y = 0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .$ Use factorial moments to
calculate the variance of the beta-binomial. (See Exercise 4.34 for another approach
to this calculation.)

2.34 A distribution cannot be uniquely determined by a finite collection of moments, as this
example from Romano and Siegel (1986) shows. Let $X$ have the normal distribution,
that is, $X$ has pdf

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty .$$

Define a discrete random variable $Y$ by

$$P \left( Y = \sqrt { 3 } \right) = P \left( Y = - \sqrt { 3 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } , \quad P \left( Y = 0 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } .$$

Show that

$$E X ^ { r } = E Y ^ { r } \quad \text { for } r = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 .$$

(Romano and Siegel point out that for any finite $n$ there exists a discrete, and hence
nonnormal, random variable whose first $n$ moments are equal to those of X.)

$$E X _ { 1 } ^ { r } = e ^ { r ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad r = 0 , 1 , \ldots .$$

So $f _ { 1 } \left( x \right)$ has all of its moments, and all of the moments are finite.

(b) Now show that

$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { r } f _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \sin \left( 2 \pi \log x \right) d x = 0$$

for all positive integers r, so $\mathrm { E } X _ { 1 } ^ { r } = \mathrm { E } X _ { 2 } ^ { r }$ for all r. (Romano and Siegel 1986
discuss an extreme version of this example, where an entire class of distinct pdfs
have the same moments. Also, Berg 1988 has shown that this moment behavior
can arise with simpler transforms of the normal distribution such as $X ^ { 3 }$
