</figure>


The double exponential provides a symmetric distribution with "fat" tails (much
fatter than the normal) but still retains all of its moments. It is straightforward to
calculate

$$E X = \mu$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } X = 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$
and

The double exponential distribution is not bell-shaped. In fact, it has a peak (or
more formally, a point of nondifferentiability) at $x = \mu .$ When we deal with this
distribution analytically, it is important to remember this point. The absolute value
signs can also be troublesome when performing integrations, and it is best to divide
the integral into regions around $x = \mu :$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="111" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.4" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXPONENTIAL FAMILIES" -->

(3.3.23)

$$E X = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { x } { 2 \sigma } e ^ { - | x - \mu | / \sigma } d x$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \mu } \frac { x } { 2 \sigma } e ^ { \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma } d x + \int _ { \mu } ^ { \infty } \frac { x } { 2 \sigma } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma } d x .$$

Notice that we can remove the absolute value signs over the two regions of integration.
(This strategy is useful, in general, in dealing with integrals containing absolute values;
divide up the region of integration so the absolute value signs can be removed.)
Evaluation of (3.3.23) can be completed by performing integration by parts on each
integral.

There are many other continuous distributions that have uses in different statis-
tical applications, many of which will appear throughout the rest of the book. The
comprehensive work by Johnson and co-authors, mentioned at the beginning of this
chapter, is a valuable reference for most useful statistical distributions.


## 3.4 Exponential Families

$A$ family of pdfs or pmfs is called an exponential family if it can be expressed as

(3.4.1)
$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Here $h \left( x \right) \geq 0$ and $t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , \ldots , t _ { k } \left( x \right)$ are real-valued functions of the observation $x$ (they
cannot depend on $\theta ,$ and $c \left( \theta \right) \geq 0$ and $w _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) , \ldots , w _ { k } \left( \theta \right)$ are real-valued functions of
the possibly vector-valued parameter $\theta$ (they cannot depend on x). Many common
families introduced in the previous section are exponential families. These include the
continuous families-normal, gamma, and beta, and the discrete families-binomial,
Poisson, and negative binomial.

To verify that a family of pdfs or pmfs is an exponential family, we must identify the
functions $h \left( x \right) ,$ $c \left( \theta \right) ,$ $w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) ,$ and $t _ { i } \left( x \right)$ and show that the family has the form (3.4.1).
The next example illustrates this.

Example 3.4.1 (Binomial exponential family) Let $n$ be a positive integer and
consider the binomial(n, $\left. p \right)$ family with $0 < p <$ 1. Then the pmf for this family, for
$x = 0 , \ldots , n$ and $0 < p < 1 ,$ is

(3.4.2)

$$f \left( x | p \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - x }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n } \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) ^ { x }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n } \exp \left( \log \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) x \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="112" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.4" -->

Define

$$h \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) \quad x = 0 , \ldots , n } \quad { } _ { c \left( p \right) = \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n } , \quad 0 < p < 1 } \right. ,$$
$$w _ { 1 } \left( p \right) = \log \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) , \quad 0 < p < 1 , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = x .$$

Then we have

(3.4.3)
$$f \left( x | p \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( p \right) \exp \left[ w _ { 1 } \left( p \right) t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \right] ,$$

which is of the form $\left( 3 . 4 . 1 \right)$ with $k = 1 .$ In particular, note that $h \left( x \right) > 0$ only if
$x = 0 , \ldots , n$ and $c \left( p \right)$ is defined only if $0 < p <$ 1. This is important, as $\left( 3 . 4 . 3 \right)$ must
match $\left( 3 . 4 . 2 \right)$ for all values of $x$ and is an exponential family only if $0 < p < 1$ (so the
functions of the parameter are only defined here). Also, the parameter values $p = 0$
and 1 are sometimes included in the binomial model, but we have not included them
here because the set of $x$ values for which $. f \left( x | p \right) > 0$ is different for $p = 0$ and 1 than
for other $p$ values.
|

The specific form of (3.4.1) results in exponential families having many nice math-
ematical properties. But more important for a statistical model, the form of (3.4.1)
results in many nice statistical properties. We next illustrate a calculational shortcut
for moments of an exponential family.

Theorem 3.4.2
If $X$ is a random variable with $p d f$ or pmf of the form (3.4.1),
then

$$E \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta _ { j } } t _ { i } \left( X \right) \right) = - \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta _ { j } } \log c \left( \theta \right)$$
$$\left( 3 . 4 . 5 \right) \quad \mathrm { V a r } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta _ { j } } t _ { i } \left( X \right) \right) = - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta _ { j } ^ { 2 } } \log c \left( \theta \right) - \mathrm { E } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) } { \partial \theta _ { j } ^ { 2 } } t _ { i } \left( X \right) \right) .$$
(3.4.4)

Although these equations may look formidable, when applied to specific cases they
can work out quite nicely. Their advantage is that we can replace integration or
summation by differentiation, which is often more straightforward.

Example 3.4.3 (Binomial mean and variance) From Example 3.4.1 we have

$$\frac { d } { d p } w _ { 1 } \left( p \right) = \frac { d } { d p } \log \frac { p } { 1 - p } = \frac { 1 } { p \left( 1 - p \right) }$$
$$\frac { d } { d p } \log c \left( p \right) = \frac { d } { d p } n \log \left( 1 - p \right) = \frac { - n } { 1 - p }$$

and thus from Theorem 3.4.2 we have

$$E \left( \frac { 1 } { p \left( 1 - p \right) } X \right) = \frac { n } { 1 - p }$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="113" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXPONENTIAL FAMILIES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.4" -->

and a bit of rearrangement yields $E \left( X \right) = n p .$ The variance identity works in a similar
manner.

11

The proof of Theorem 3.4.2 is a calculus excursion and is relegated to Exercise 3.31.
See also Exercise 3.32 for a special case.

We now look at another example and some other features of exponential families.

Example 3.4.4 (Normal exponential family) Let $f \left( x | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ be the $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$
family of pdfs, where $\theta = \left( \mu , \sigma \right) ,$ $, - \infty < \mu < \infty$ , $\sigma > 0$ 0. Then

$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \exp \left( - \frac { \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \exp \left( - \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right) \exp \left( - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu x } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right) .$$
(3.4.6)

Define

$$h \left( x \right) = 1 \quad f o r \quad a l l \quad x ;$$
$$c \left( \theta \right) = c \left( \mu , \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \exp \left( \frac { - \mu ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right) , \quad - \infty < \mu < \infty , \sigma > 0 ;$$
$$w _ { 1 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } , \quad \sigma > 0 ; \quad w _ { 2 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) = \frac { \mu } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } , \sigma > 0 ;$$
$$t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = - x ^ { 2 } / 2 ; \quad \text { and } \quad t _ { 2 } \left( x \right) = x .$$

Then

$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \mu , \sigma \right) \exp \left[ w _ { 1 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) + w _ { 2 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) t _ { 2 } \left( x \right) \right] ,$$

which $i s$ the form (3.4.1) with $k = 2 .$ Note again that the parameter functions are
defined only over the range of the parameter.
Il

In general, the $\mathrm { s e t }$ of $x$ values for which $f \left( x | \theta \right) > 0$ cannot depend on $\theta$ in an
exponential family. The entire definition of the pdf or pmf must be incorporated into
the form $\left( 3 . 4 . 1 \right) .$ This is most easily accomplished by incorporating the range of $x$
into the expression for $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ through the use of an indicator function.

Definition 3.4.5 The indicator function of a set $A ,$ most often denoted by $I _ { A } \left( x \right) ,$
is the function

$$I _ { A } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { x \in A } \\ { 0 } & { x \notin A } \end{array} \right.$$

An alternative notation is $I \left( x \in A \right) .$

Thus, the normal pdf of Example 3.4.4 would be written

$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \mu , \sigma \right) \exp \left[ w _ { 1 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) t _ { 1 } \left( x \right) + w _ { 2 } \left( \mu , \sigma \right) t _ { 2 } \left( x \right) \right] I _ { \left( - \infty , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="114" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.4" -->

Since the indicator function is a function of only x, it can be incorporated into the
function $h \left( x \right) ,$ showing that this pdf is of the form (3.4.1).

From (3.4.1), since the factor $\exp \left( \cdot \right)$ is always positive, it can be seen that for
any $\theta \in \Theta ,$ that is, for any $\theta$ for which $c \left( \theta \right) > 0 ,$ $\left\{ x : f \left( x | \theta \right) > 0 \right\} = \left\{ x : h \left( x \right) > \right.$
$0$ and this set does not depend on $\theta .$ So, for example, the set of pdfs given by
$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta ^ { - 1 } \exp \left( 1 - \left( x / \theta \right) \right) ,$ 0 < A > x < o, is not an exponential family even
though we can write $\theta ^ { - 1 } \exp \left( 1 - \left( x / \theta \right) \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( w \left( \theta \right) t \left( x \right) \right) ,$ where $h \left( x \right) = e ^ { 1 } ,$
$c \left( \theta \right) = \theta ^ { - 1 } ,$ $w \left( \theta \right) = \theta ^ { - 1 } ,$ and $t \left( x \right) = - \quad x .$ Writing the pdf with indicator functions
makes this very clear. We have

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta ^ { - 1 } \exp \left( 1 - \left( \frac { x } { \theta } \right) \right) I _ { \left[ \theta , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) .$$

The indicator function cannot be incorporated into any of the functions of (3.4.1) since
it is not a function of $x$ alone, not a function of $\theta$ alone, and cannot be expressed as
an exponential. Thus, this is not an exponential family.

An exponential family is sometimes reparameterized as

(3.4.7)
$$f \left( x | \eta \right) = h \left( x \right) c ^ { * } \left( \eta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \eta _ { i } t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Here the $h \left( x \right)$ and $t _ { i } \left( x \right)$ functions are the same as in the original parameteriza-
tion (3.4.1). The set $\mathcal{H} = \left\{ \eta = \left( \eta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \eta _ { k } \right) : \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( x \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \eta _ { i } t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) d x < \infty \right.$
is called the natural parameter space for the family. (The integral is replaced by a
sum over the values of $x$ for which $h \left( x \right) > 0$ if $X$ is discrete.) For the values of
$\eta \in \mathcal{H} ,$ we must have $c ^ { * } \left( \eta \right) = \left[ \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( x \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \eta _ { i } t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) d x \right] ^ { - 1 }$ to ensure that
the pdf integrates to 1. Since the original $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ in $\left( 3 . 4 . 1 \right)$ is a pdf or pmf, the set
$\left\{ \eta = \left( w _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) , \ldots , w _ { k } \left( \theta \right) \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ must be a subset of the natural parameter space.
But there may be other values of $\eta \in \mathcal{H}$ also. The natural parameterization and the
natural parameter space have many useful mathematical properties. For example, $\mathcal{H}$
is convex.

Example 3.4.6 (Continuation of Example 3.4.4) To determine the natural
parameter space for the normal family of distributions, replace $w _ { i } \left( \mu , \sigma \right)$ with $\eta _ { i }$ in
(3.4.6) to obtain

(3.4.8)
$$f \left( x | \eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { \sqrt { \eta _ { 1 } } } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \exp \left( - \frac { \eta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \eta _ { 1 } } \right) \exp \left( - \frac { \eta _ { 1 } x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \eta _ { 2 } x \right) .$$

The integral will be finite if and only if the coefficient on $x ^ { 2 }$ is negative. This means
$\eta _ { 1 }$ must be positive. If $\eta _ { 1 } > 0 ,$ the integral will be finite regardless of the value
of $n 2 .$ Thus the natural parameter space is $\left\{ \left( \eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } \right) : \eta _ { 1 } > 0 , - \infty < \eta _ { 2 } < \infty \right\} .$
Identifying (3.4.8) with (3.4.6), we see that $\eta _ { 2 } = \mu / \sigma ^ { 2 }$ and $\eta _ { 1 } = 1 / \sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Although
natural parameters provide a convenient mathematical formulation, they sometimes
lack simple interpretations like the mean and variance.

<!-- PageNumber="11" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="115" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXPONENTIAL FAMILIES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.4" -->

In the representation (3.4.1) it is often the case that the dimension of the vector
$\theta$ is equal to $k ,$ the number of terms in the sum in the exponent. This need not be
so, and it is possible for the dimension of the vector $\theta$ to be equal to $d < k .$ Such an
exponential family is called a curved exponential family.

Definition 3.4.7 A curved exponential family is a family of densities of the form
(3.4.1) for which the dimension of the vector $\theta$ is equal to $d < k .$ If $d = k ,$ the family
is a full exponential family. (See also Miscellanea 3.8.3.)

Example 3.4.8 (A curved exponential family) The normal family of Example
3.4.4 is a full exponential family. However, if we assume that $\sigma ^ { 2 } = \mu ^ { 2 } ,$ the family
becomes curved. (Such a model might be used in the analysis of variance; see Exercises
11.1 and 11.2.) We then have

$$f \left( x | \mu \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi \mu ^ { 2 } } } \exp \left( - \frac { \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mu ^ { 2 } } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi \mu ^ { 2 } } } \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \exp \left( - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mu ^ { 2 } } + \frac { x } { \mu } \right) .$$
(3.4.9)

For the normal family the full exponential family would have parameter space
$\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathfrak{R} \times \left( 0 , \infty \right) ,$ while the parameter space of the curved family $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \left( \mu , \mu ^ { 2 } \right)$
is a parabola.
Il

Curved exponential families are useful in many ways. The next example illustrates
a simple use.

Example 3.4.9 (Normal approximations) In Chapter 5 we will see that if
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a sample from a $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ population, then the distribution of $\bar { X } =$
$\sum _ { i } X _ { i } / n$ is approximately

$$\bar { X } \sim n \left( \lambda , \lambda / n \right) ,$$

a curved exponential family.

The $n \left( \lambda , \lambda / n \right)$ approximation is justified by the Central Limit Theorem (Theorem
5.5.14). In fact, we might realize that most such CLT approximations will result in
a curved normal family. We have seen the normal binomial approximation (Example
3.3.2): If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $\mathrm { B e r n o u l l i } \left( p \right) ,$ then

$$\bar { X } \sim n \left( p , p \left( 1 - p \right) / n \right) ,$$

approximately. For another illustration, see Example 5.5.16.
Il

Although the fact that the parameter space is a lower-dimensional space has some
influence on the properties of the family, we will see that curved families still enjoy
many of the properties of full families. In particular, Theorem 3.4.2 applies to curved
exponential families. Moreover, full and curved exponential families have other sta-
tistical properties, which will be discussed throughout the remainder of the text. For

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="116" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.5" -->

example, suppose we have a large number of data values from a population that has
a pdf or pmf of the form (3.4.1). Then only $k$ numbers (k = number of terms in the
sum in (3.4.1)) that can be calculated from the data summarize all the information
about $\theta$ that is in the data. This "data reduction" property is treated in more detail
in Chapter 6 (Theorem 6.2.10), where we discuss sufficient statistics.

For more of an introduction to exponential families, see Lehmann (1986, Section 2.7)
or Lehmann and Casella (1998, Section 1.5 and Note 1.10.6). A thorough introduction,
at a somewhat more advanced level, is given in the classic monograph by Brown
(1986).


## 3.5 Location and Scale Families

In Sections 3.3 and 3.4, we discussed several common families of continuous distribu-
tions. In this section we discuss three techniques for constructing families of distri-
butions. The resulting families have ready physical interpretations that make them
useful for modeling as well as convenient mathematical properties.

The three types of families are called location families, scale families, and location-
scale families. Each of the families is constructed by specifying a single pdf, say $f \left( x \right) ,$
called the standard $p d f$ for the family. Then all other pdfs in the family are generated
by transforming the standard pdf in a prescribed way. We start with a simple theorem
about pdfs.

Theorem 3.5.1 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be any $p d f$ and let $\mu$ and $\sigma > 0$ be any given constants.
Then the function

$$g \left( x | u , \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left( \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right)$$

is a pdf.

Proof: To verify that the transformation has produced a legitimate $\mathrm { p d f } ,$ we need $t o$
check that $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right) ,$ as a function of x, is a pdf for every value of $\mu$ and $\sigma$
we might substitute into the formula. That is, we must check that $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right)$
is nonnegative and integrates to 1. Since $f \left( x \right)$ is a pdf, $f \left( x \right) \geq 0$ for all values of x.
So, $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right) \geq 0$ for all values of $x ,$ $\mu ,$ and $\sigma .$ Next we note that

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left( \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right) d x = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( y \right) d y$$
(substitute y = 2 -")

=1,

$$\left( s i n c e \quad f \left( y \right) i s \quad a \quad p d f \right)$$

as was to be verified.

☐

We now turn to the first of our constructions, that of location families.

Definition 3.5.2 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be any pdf. Then the family of pdfs $f \left( x - \mu \right) ,$ indexed
by the parameter $\mu , - \infty < \mu < \infty ,$ is called the location family with standard $p d f$
$f \left( x \right)$ and $\mu$ is called the location parameter for the family.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="117" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.5" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOCATION AND SCALE FAMILIES" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 3.5.1. Two members of the same location family: means at $0$ and 2</figcaption>

-3 -2 -1 0 1 2 3 4 5x

1

</figure>


To see the effect of introducing the location parameter $\mu ,$ consider Figure 3.5.1. At
$x = \mu ,$ $f \left( x - \mu \right) = f \left( 0 \right)$ at $x = \mu + 1 ,$ $f \left( x - \mu \right) = f \left( 1 \right)$ and, in general, at $x = \mu + a ,$
$f \left( x - \mu \right) = f \left( a \right) .$ Of course, $f \left( x - \mu \right)$ for $\mu = 0$ is just $f \left( x \right) .$ Thus the location
parameter $\mu \mathrm { s i m p l y }$ shifts the pdf $f \left( x \right)$ so that the shape of the graph is unchanged
but the point on the graph that was above $x = 0$ for $f \left( x \right)$ is above $x = \mu$ for $f \left( x - \mu \right) .$
It is clear from Figure 3.5.1 that the area under the graph of $f \left( x \right)$ between $x = - \quad 1$
and $x = 2$ is the same as the area under the graph of $f \left( x - \mu \right)$ between $x = - \quad 1$ and
$x = \mu + 2 .$ Thus if $X$ is a random variable with pdf $f \left( x - \mu \right) ,$ we can write

$$P \left( - 1 \leq X \leq 2 | 0 \right) = P \left( \mu - 1 \leq X \leq \mu + 2 | \mu \right) ,$$

where the random variable $X$ has pdf $f \left( x \quad - 0 \right) = f \left( x \right)$ on the left of the equality and
pdf $f \left( x - \mu \right)$ on the right.

Several of the families introduced in Section 3.3 are, or have as subfamilies, location
families. For example, if $\sigma > 0$ is a specified, known number and we define

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty ,$$

then the location family with standard pdf $f \left( x \right)$ is the set of normal distributions
with unknown mean $\mu$ and known variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ To see this, check that replacing $x$
by $x - \mu$ in the above formula yields pdfs of the form defined in (3.3.13). Similarly,
the Cauchy family and the double exponential family, with $\sigma$ a specified value and
$\mu$ a parameter, are examples of location families. But the point of Definition 3.5.2 is
that we can start with any pdf $f \left( x \right)$ and generate a family of pdfs by introducing a
location parameter.

If $X$ is a random variable with pdf $f \left( x - \mu \right) ,$ then $X$ may be represented as $X = Z +$
$\mu ,$ where $Z$ is a random variable with pdf $f \left( z \right) .$ This representation is a consequence
of Theorem 3.5.6 (with $\sigma = 1$ 1), which will be proved later. Consideration of this
representation indicates when a location family might be an appropriate model for
an observed variable $X .$ We will describe two such situations.

First, suppose an experiment is designed to measure some physical constant $\mu ,$ say
the temperature of a solution. But there is some measurement error involved in the
observation. So the actual observed value $X$ is $Z + \mu ,$ where Z is the measurement

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="118" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.5" -->

error. $X$ will be greater than $\mu$ if $Z > 0$ for this observation and less than $\mu$ if
$Z < 0 .$ The distribution of the random measurement error might be well known from
previous experience in using this measuring device to measure other solutions. If this
distribution has pdf $f \left( z \right) ,$ then the pdf of the observed value $X$ is $f \left( x - \mu \right) .$

As another example, suppose the distribution of reaction times of drivers on a
coordination test is known from previous experimentation. Denote the reaction time
for a randomly chosen driver by the random variable $Z .$ Let the pdf of $Z$ describing the
known distribution be $f \left( z \right) .$ Now, consider "applying a treatment" to the population.
For example, consider what would happen if everyone drank three glasses of beer. We
might assume that everyone's reaction time would change by some unknown amount
$\mu .$ (This very simple model, in which everyone's reaction time changes by the same
amount $\mu ,$ is probably not the best model. For example, it is known that the effect
of alcohol is weight-dependent, so heavier people are likely to be less affected by the
beers.) Being open-minded scientists, we might even allow the possibility that $\mu < 0 ,$
that is, that the reaction times decrease. Then, if we observe the reaction time of a
randomly selected driver after "treatment," the reaction time would be $X = Z + \mu$
and the family of possible distributions for $X$ would be given by $f \left( x - \mu \right) .$

If the set of $x$ for which $f \left( x \right) > 0$ is not the whole real line, then the set of $x$ for
which $f \left( x - \mu \right) > 0$ will depend on $\mu .$ Example 3.5.3 illustrates this.

Example 3.5.3 (Exponential location family) Let $f \left( x \right) = e ^ { - x } ,$ $x \geq 0 ,$ and
$f \left( x \right) = 0 ,$ $x < 0 .$ To form a location family we replace $x$ with $x - \mu$ to obtain

$$f \left( x | \mu \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) } } & { x - \mu \geq 0 } \\ { 0 } & { x - \mu < 0 } \end{array} \right.$$
$$= \left\{ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) } } & { x \geq \mu } \\ { 0 } & { x < \mu } \end{array} \right.$$

Graphs of $f \left( x | \mu \right)$ for various values of $\mu$ are shown in Figure 3.5.2. As in Figure 3.5.1,
the graph has been shifted. Now the positive part of the graph starts at $\mu$ rather than
at 0. If $X$ measures time, then $\mu$ might be restricted $t o$ be nonnegative so that $X$
will be positive with probability 1 for every value of $\mu .$ In this type of model, where
$\mu$ denotes a bound on the range of $X ,$ $\mu$ is sometimes called a threshold parameter. |

<figcaption>Figure 3.5.2. Exponential location densities</figcaption>

</figure>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.5" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOCATION AND SCALE FAMILIES" -->
<!-- PageNumber="119" -->

<figcaption>Figure 3.5.3. Members of the same scale family</figcaption>

The other two types of families to be discussed in this section are scale families and
location-scale families.

Definition 3.5.4 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be any pdf. Then for any $\sigma > 0 ,$ the family of pdfs
$\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( x / \sigma \right) ,$ indexed by the parameter o, is called the scale family with standard pdf
$f \left( x \right)$ and $\sigma$ is called the scale parameter of the family.

The effect of introducing the scale parameter $\sigma$ is either to stretch $\left( \sigma > 1 \right)$ or to
contract $\left( \sigma < 1 \right)$ the graph of $f \left( x \right)$ while still maintaining the same basic shape of
the graph. This is illustrated in Figure 3.5.3. Most often when scale parameters are
used, $f \left( x \right)$ is either symmetric about 0 or positive only for $x > 0 .$ In these cases the
stretching is either symmetric about 0 or only in the positive direction. But, in the
definition, any pdf may be used as the standard.

Several of the families introduced in Section 3.3 either are scale families or have
scale families as subfamilies. These are the gamma family if $\alpha$ is a fixed value and
$\beta$ is the scale parameter, the normal family if $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma$ is the scale parameter,
the exponential family, and the double exponential family if $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma$ is the scale
parameter. In each case the standard pdf is the pdf obtained by setting the scale
parameter equal to 1. Then all other members of the family can be shown $\mathrm { t o }$ be of
the form in Definition 3.5.4.

Definition 3.5.5 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be any pdf. Then for any $\mu ,$ $- \infty < \mu < \infty ,$ and any
$\sigma > 0 ,$ the family of pdfs $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right) ,$ indexed by the parameter $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) ,$ is called
the location-scale family with standard pdf $f \left( x \right) ;$ $\mu$ is called the location parameter
and $\sigma$ is called the scale parameter.

The effect of introducing both the location and scale parameters is to stretch $\left( \sigma > 1 \right)$
or contract $\left( \sigma < 1 \right)$ the graph with the scale parameter and then shift the graph so that
the point that was above 0 is now above $\mu .$ Figure 3.5.4 illustrates this transformation
of $f \left( x \right) .$ The normal and double exponential families are examples of location-scale
families. Exercise 3.39 presents the Cauchy as a location-scale family.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="120" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.5" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 3.5.4. Members of the same location-scale family</figcaption>
