$x$

</figure>


The following theorem relates the transformation of the pdf $f \left( x \right)$ that defines a
location-scale family to the transformation of a random variable $Z$ with pdf $f \left( z \right) .$ As
mentioned earlier in the discussion of location families, the representation in terms
of $Z$ is a useful mathematical tool and can help us understand when a location-scale
family might be appropriate in a modeling context. Setting $\sigma = 1$ in Theorem 3.5.6
yields a result for location (only) families, and setting $\mu = 0$ yields a result for scale
$\left( \mathrm { o n l y } \right)$ families.

Theorem 3.5.6 Let $f \left( \cdot \right)$ be any pdf. Let $\mu$ be any real number, and let $\sigma$ be any
positive real number. Then $X$ is $a$ random variable with pdf $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right)$ if and
only if there exists a random variable $Z$ with pdf $f \left( z \right)$ and $X = \sigma Z + \mu .$

Proof: To prove the "if" part, define $g \left( z \right) = \sigma z + \mu .$ Then $X = g \left( Z \right) ,$ $g$ is a monotone
function, $g ^ { - 1 } \left( x \right) = \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma ,$ and $| \left( d / d x \right) g ^ { - 1 } \left( x \right) | = 1 / \sigma .$ Thus by Theorem 2.1.5,
the pdf of $X$ is

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = f _ { Z } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( x \right) \right) | \frac { d } { d x } g ^ { - 1 } \left( x \right) | = f \left( \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right) \frac { 1 } { \sigma } .$$

To prove the "only if" part, define $g \left( x \right) = \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma$ and let $Z = g \left( X \right) .$ Theorem
☒
2.1.5 again applies: $g ^ { - 1 } \left( z \right) = \sigma z + \mu ,$ $| \left( d / d z \right) g ^ { - 1 } \left( z \right) | = \sigma ,$ and the pdf of $Z$ is

$$f _ { Z } \left( z \right) = f _ { X } \left( g ^ { - 1 } \left( z \right) \right) | \frac { d } { d z } g ^ { - 1 } \left( z \right) | = \frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left( \frac { \left( \sigma z + \mu \right) - \mu } { \sigma } \right) \sigma = f \left( z \right) .$$

Also,

$$\sigma Z + \mu = \sigma g \left( X \right) + \mu = \sigma \left( \frac { X - \mu } { \sigma } \right) + \mu = X .$$ ☒

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="121" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INEQUALITIES AND IDENTITIES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

An important fact to extract from Theorem 3.5.6 is that the random variable
$Z = \left( X - \mu \right) / \sigma$ has pdf

$$f _ { Z } \left( z \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 } f \left( \frac { z - 0 } { 1 } \right) = f \left( z \right) .$$

That is, the distribution of $Z$ is that member of the location-scale family correspond-
ing to $\mu = 0 ,$ $\sigma = 1 .$ This was already proved for the special case of the normal family
in Section 3.3.

Often, calculations can be carried out for the "standard" random variable Z with
pdf $f \left( z \right)$ and then the corresponding result for the random variable $X$ with pdf
$\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right)$ can be easily derived. An example is given in the following, which
is a generalization of a computation done in Section 3.3 for the normal family.

Theorem 3.5.7 Let $Z$ be a random variable with pdf $f \left( z \right) .$ Suppose $E Z$ and $\mathrm { V a r } Z$
exist. If $X$ is a random variable with pdf $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right) ,$ then

$$E X = \sigma E Z + \mu$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } Z .$$
and

In particular, if $E Z = 0$ and Var $Z = 1 ,$ then $\mathrm { E } X = \mu$ and Var $X = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$

Proof: By Theorem 3.5.6, there is a random variable $Z ^ { * }$ with pdf $f \left( z \right)$ and $X =$
$\sigma Z ^ { * } + \mu .$ So $E X = \sigma E Z ^ { * } + \mu = \sigma E Z + \mu$ and $\mathrm { V a r } X = \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } Z ^ { * } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } Z .$
☐

For any location-scale family with a finite mean and variance, the standard pdf $f \left( z \right)$
can be chosen in such a way that $E Z = 0$ and Var $Z = 1 .$ (The proof that this choice
can be made is left as Exercise 3.40.) This results in the convenient interpretation
of $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ as the mean and variance of $X ,$ respectively. This is the case for the
usual definition of the normal family as given in Section 3.3. However, this is not the
choice for the usual definition of the double exponential family as given in Section
3.3. There, $\mathrm { V a r } Z = 2 .$

Probabilities for any member of a location-scale family may be computed in terms
of the standard variable $Z$ because

$$P \left( X \leq x \right) = P \left( \frac { X - \mu } { \sigma } \leq \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right) = P \left( Z \leq \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right) .$$

Thus, if $P \left( Z \leq z \right)$ is tabulated or easily calculable for the standard variable $Z ,$ then
probabilities for $X$ may be obtained. Calculations of normal probabilities using the
standard normal table are examples of this.


## 3.6 Inequalities and Identities

Statistical theory is literally brimming with inequalities and identities-so many that
entire books are devoted to the topic. The major work by Marshall and Olkin (1979)
contains many inequalities using the concept of majorization. The older work by
Hardy, Littlewood, and Polya (1952) is a compendium of classic inequalities. In this
section and in Section 4.7 we will mix some old and some new, giving some idea of the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="122" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

types of results that exist. This section is devoted to those identities and inequalities
that arise from probabilistic concerns, while those in Section 4.7 rely more on basic
properties of numbers and functions.


### 3.6.1 Probability Inequalities

The most famous, and perhaps most useful, probability inequality is Chebychev's
Inequality. Its usefulness comes from its wide applicability. As with many important
results, its proof is almost trivial.

Theorem 3.6.1 (Chebychev's Inequality) Let $X$ be a random variable and let
$g \left( x \right)$ be a nonnegative function. Then, for any $r > 0 ,$

$$P \left( g \left( X \right) \geq r \right) \leq \frac { E g \left( X \right) } { r }$$

Proof:

$$E g \left( X \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$\geq \int _ { \left\{ x : g \left( x \right) \geq r \right\} } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$

(g is nonnegative)

$$\geq r \int _ { \left\{ x : g \left( x \right) \geq r \right\} } f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x$$
$$= r P \left( g \left( X \right) \geq r \right) .$$

Rearranging now produces the desired inequality.

(definition)
☐

Example 3.6.2 (Illustrating Chebychev) The most widespread use of Cheby-
chev's Inequality involves means and variances. Let $g \left( x \right) = \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\mu = E X$
and $\sigma ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } X .$ For convenience write $r = t ^ { 2 } .$ Then

$$P \left( \frac { \left( X - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \geq t ^ { 2 } \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } E \frac { \left( X - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } }$$

Doing some obvious algebra, we get the inequality

$$P \left( | X - \mu | \geq t \sigma \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } }$$

and its companion

$$P \left( | X - \mu | < t \sigma \right) \geq 1 - \frac { 1 } { t ^ { 2 } } ,$$

which gives a universal bound on the deviation $| X - \mu |$ in terms of o. For example,
taking $t = 2 ,$ we get

$$P \left( | X - \mu | \geq 2 \sigma \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ { 2 } } = . 2 5 ,$$

so there is at least a 75% chance that a random variable will be within $2 \sigma$ of its mean
(no matter what the distribution of $\left. X \right) .$

II

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="123" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INEQUALITIES AND IDENTITIES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

While Chebychev's Inequality is widely applicable, it is necessarily conservative.
(See, for example, Exercise 3.46 and Miscellanea 3.8.2.) In particular, we can often
get tighter bounds for some specific distributions.

Example 3.6.3 (A normal probability inequality)
If $Z$ is standard normal,
then

(3.6.1)
$$P \left( | Z | \geq t \right) \leq \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { \pi } } \frac { e ^ { - t ^ { 2 } / 2 } } { t } , \quad \text { for all } t > 0 .$$

Compare this with Chebychev's Inequality. For $t = 2 ,$ Chebychev gives $P \left( | Z | \geq t \right) \leq$
.25 but $\sqrt { \left( 2 / \pi \right) } e ^ { - 2 } / 2 = . 0 5 4 ,$ a vast improvement.
☒

To prove (3.6.1), write

$$P \left( Z \geq t \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } d x$$
$$\leq \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \int _ { t } ^ { \infty } \frac { x } { t } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \frac { e ^ { - t ^ { 2 } / 2 } } { t }$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { since } x / t > 1 } \\ { \text { for } x > t } \end{array} \right)$

and use the fact that $P \left( | Z | \geq t \right) = 2 P \left( Z \geq t \right) .$ A lower bound on $P \left( | Z | \geq t \right)$ can be
established in a similar way (see Exercise 3.47).

Il

Many other probability inequalities exist, and almost all of them are similar in
spirit to Chebychev's. For example, we will see (Exercise 3.45) that

$$P \left( X \geq a \right) \leq e ^ { - a t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$$

but, of course, this inequality requires the existence of the mgf. Other inequalities,
tighter than Chebychev but requiring more assumptions, exist (as detailed in Miscel-
lanea 3.8.2).


### 3.6.2 Identities

In this section we present a sampling of various identities that can be useful not only
in establishing theorems but also in easing numerical calculations. An entire class of
identities can be thought of as "recursion relations," a few of which we have already
seen. Recall that if $X$ is $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ then

(3.6.2)
$$P \left( X = x + 1 \right) = \frac { \lambda } { x + 1 } P \left( X = x \right) ,$$

allowing us to calculate Poisson probabilities recursively starting from $P \left( X = 0 \right) =$
$e ^ { - \lambda } .$ Relations like (3.6.2) exist for almost all discrete distributions (see Exercise 3.48).
Sometimes they exist in a slightly different form for continuous distributions.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="124" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

Theorem 3.6.4 Let $X _ { \alpha , \beta }$ denote a $g a m m a \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ random variable with pdf $f \left( x | \alpha , \beta \right) ,$
where $\alpha > 1 .$ Then for any constants $a$ and $b ,$

(3.6.3)
$$P \left( a < X _ { \alpha , \beta } < b \right) = \beta \left( f \left( a | \alpha , \beta \right) - f \left( b | \alpha , \beta \right) \right) + P \left( a < X _ { \alpha - 1 , \beta } < b \right) .$$

Proof: By definition,

$$P \left( a < X _ { \alpha , \beta } < b \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \int _ { a } ^ { b } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \left[ - x ^ { \alpha - 1 } \beta e ^ { - x / \beta } | _ { a } ^ { b } + \int _ { a } ^ { b } \left( \alpha - 1 \right) x ^ { \alpha - 2 } \beta e ^ { - x / \beta } d x \right] ,$$

where we have done an integration by parts with $u = x ^ { \alpha - 1 }$ and $d v = e ^ { - x / \beta } d x .$
Continuing, we have

$$P \left( a < X _ { \alpha , \beta } < b \right) = \beta \left( f \left( a | \alpha , \beta \right) - \dot { f } \left( b | \alpha , \beta \right) \right) + \frac { \left( \alpha - 1 \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha - 1 } } \int _ { a } ^ { b } x ^ { \alpha - 2 } e ^ { - x / \beta } d x .$$

Using the fact that $\Gamma \left( \alpha \right) = \left( \alpha - 1 \right) \Gamma \left( \alpha - 1 \right) ,$ we see that the last term is $P \left( a < \right.$
$\left. X _ { \alpha - 1 , \beta } < b \right) .$
☐

If $\alpha$ is an integer, repeated use of (3.6.3) will eventually lead to an integral that
can be evaluated analytically (when $\alpha = 1 ,$ the exponential distribution). Thus, we
can easily compute these gamma probabilities.

There is an entire class of identities that rely on integration by parts. The first
of these is attributed to Charles Stein, who used it in his work on estimation of
multivariate normal means (Stein 1973, 1981).

Lemma 3.6.5 (Stein's Lemma) Let $X \sim n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and let $g$ be a differentiable
function satisfying $E | g ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) | < \infty .$ Then

$$E \left[ g \left( X \right) \left( X - \theta \right) \right] = \sigma ^ { 2 } E g ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) .$$

Proof: The left-hand side $\mathrm { i s }$

$$E \left[ g \left( X \right) \left( X - \theta \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) \left( x - \theta \right) e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } d x .$$

Use integration by parts with $u = g \left( x \right)$ and $d v = \left( x - \theta \right) e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } d x$ to get

$$E \left[ g \left( X \right) \left( X - \theta \right) \right] = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \left[ - \sigma ^ { 2 } g \left( x \right) e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } | _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } + \sigma ^ { 2 } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } d x \right]$$

The condition on $g ^ { \prime }$ is enough to ensure that the first term is 0 and what remains on
the right-hand side is $\sigma ^ { 2 } E g ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) .$
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="125" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INEQUALITIES AND IDENTITIES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

Example 3.6.6 (Higher-order normal moments) Stein's Lemma makes calcu-
lation of higher-order moments quite easy. For example, if $X \sim n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ then

$$E X ^ { 3 } = E X ^ { 2 } \left( X - \theta + \theta \right)$$
$$= E X ^ { 2 } \left( X - \theta \right) + \theta E X ^ { 2 }$$
$$= 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } E X + \theta E X ^ { 2 }$$
$$= 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \theta + \theta \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } + \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= 3 \theta \sigma ^ { 2 } + \theta ^ { 3 } .$$

(g(x) = x2, g'(x) =2x)

I!

Similar integration-by-parts identities exist for many distributions (see Exercise
3.49 and Hudson 1978). One can also get useful identities by exploiting properties of
a particular distribution, as the next theorem shows.

Theorem 3.6.7 Let $x _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ denote a $c h i$ squared random variable with $p$ degrees of
freedom. For any function $h \left( x \right) ,$

(3.6.4)
$$\mathrm { E h } \left( \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } \right) = p E \left( \frac { h \left( \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) } { \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right)$$

provided the expectations exist.

Proof: The phrase "provided the expectations exist" is a lazy way of avoiding spec-
ification of conditions on $h .$ In general, reasonable functions will satisfy (3.6.4). We
have

$$E h \left( \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( p / 2 \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } h \left( x \right) x ^ { \left( p / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } d x$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( p / 2 \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( \frac { h \left( x \right) } { x } \right) x ^ { \left( \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } d x ,$$

where we have multiplied the integrand by $x / x .$ Now write

$$\Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } = \frac { \Gamma \left( \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 \right) 2 ^ { \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 } } { p }$$

so we have

$$\mathrm { E } h \left( \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { p } { \Gamma \left( \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 \right) 2 ^ { \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( \frac { h \left( x \right) } { x } \right) x ^ { \left( \left( p + 2 \right) / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } d x$$
$$= p E \left( \frac { h \left( \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) } { \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) .$$

☐

Some moment calculations are very easy with (3.6.4). For example, the mean of a
$x _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ is

$$E \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } = p E \left( \frac { \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) = p E \left( 1 \right) = p ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="126" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.6" -->

and the second moment is

$$E \left( \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = p E \left( \frac { \left( \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } = p E \left( \chi _ { p + 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) = p \left( p + 2 \right) .$$

So $\mathrm { V a r } \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } = p \left( p + 2 \right) - p ^ { 2 } = 2 p .$

We close our section on identities with some discrete analogs of the previous identi-
ties. A general version of the two identities in Theorem 3.6.8 is due to Hwang (1982).

Theorem 3.6.8 (Hwang)
Let $g \left( x \right)$ be a function with $- \infty < \mathrm { E g } \left( X \right) < \infty$ and
$- \infty < g \left( - 1 \right) < \infty$ . Then:

a. If $X \sim P o i s s o n \left( \lambda \right) ,$

(3.6.5)
$$E \left( \lambda g \left( X \right) \right) = E \left( X g \left( X - 1 \right) \right) .$$

b. If $X \sim n e g a t i v e \quad b i n o m i a l \left( r , p \right) ,$

(3.6.6)
$$E \left( \left( 1 - p \right) g \left( X \right) \right) = E \left( \frac { X } { r + X - 1 } g \left( X - 1 \right) \right) .$$

Proof: We will prove part (a), saving part (b) for Exercise 3.50. We have

$$E \left( \lambda g \left( X \right) \right) = \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \lambda g \left( x \right) \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { x } } { x ! }$$
$$= \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { x + 1 } } { x ! } \frac { \left( x + 1 \right) } { \left( x + 1 \right) }$$
$$= \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { \infty } \left( x + 1 \right) g \left( x \right) \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { x + 1 } } { \left( x + 1 \right) ! }$$

Now transform the summation index, writing $y = x +$ 1. As $x$ goes from 0 to $\infty ,$ $y$
goes from 1 to $\infty .$ Thus

$$E \left( \lambda g \left( X \right) \right) = \sum _ { y = 1 } ^ { \infty } y g \left( y - 1 \right) \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { y } } { y ! }$$
$$= \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { \infty } y g \left( y - 1 \right) \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { y } } { y ! }$$
$$= E \left( X g \left( X - 1 \right) \right) ,$$

(added term is 0)

since this last sum is a Poisson(X) expectation.
☐

Hwang (1982) used his identity in a manner similar to Stein, proving results about
multivariate estimators. The identity has other applications, in particular in moment
calculations.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="127" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

Example 3.6.9 (Higher-order Poisson moments) For $X \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ take
$g \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 }$ and use (3.6.5):

$$E \left( \lambda X ^ { 2 } \right) = E \left( X \left( X - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = E \left( X ^ { 3 } - 2 X ^ { 2 } + X \right) .$$

Therefore, the third moment of a $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ is

$$E X ^ { 3 } = \lambda E X ^ { 2 } + 2 E X ^ { 2 } - E X$$
$$= \lambda \left( \lambda + \lambda ^ { 2 } \right) + 2 \left( \lambda + \lambda ^ { 2 } \right) - \lambda$$
$$= \lambda ^ { 3 } + 3 \lambda ^ { 2 } + \lambda .$$

For the negative binomial, the mean can be calculated by taking $g \left( x \right) = r + x$ in

(3.6.6):
$$E \left( \left( 1 - p \right) \left( r + X \right) \right) = E \left( \frac { X } { r + X - 1 } \left( r + X - 1 \right) \right) = E X$$

so, rearranging, we get

$$\left. \left( E X \right) \left( 1 \quad - p \right) - 1 \right) = - \quad r \left( 1 \quad - \quad p \right)$$

or

$$E X = \frac { r \left( 1 - p \right) } { p }$$

Other moments can be calculated similarly.

11


## 3.7 Exercises

3.1 Find expressions for $\mathrm { E } X$ and Var $X$ if $X$ is a random variable with the general discrete
$\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( N _ { 0 } , N _ { 1 } \right)$ distribution that puts equal probability on each of the values $N _ { 0 } ,$ $N _ { 0 } +$
$1 , \ldots , N _ { 1 } .$ Here $N _ { 0 } \leq N _ { 1 }$ and both are integers.

3.2 A manufacturer receives a lot of 100 parts from a vendor. The lot will be unacceptable if
more than five of the parts are defective. The manufacturer is going to select randomly
$K$ parts from the lot for inspection and the lot will be accepted if no defective parts
are found in the sample.

(a) How large does $K$ have to be to ensure that the probability that the manufacturer
accepts an unacceptable lot is less than .10?

(b) Suppose the manufacturer decides to accept the lot if there is at most one defective
in the sample. How large does $K$ have to be to ensure that the probability that
the manufacturer accepts an unacceptable lot is less than .10?

3.3 The flow of traffic at certain street corners can sometimes be modeled as a sequence
of Bernoulli trials by assuming that the probability of a car passing during any given
second is a constant $p$ and that there is no interaction between the passing of cars at
different seconds. If we treat seconds as indivisible time units (trials), the Bernoulli
model applies. Suppose a pedestrian can cross the street only if no car is to pass during
the next 3 seconds. Find the probability that the pedestrian has to wait for exactly 4
seconds before starting to cross.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="128" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

3.4 A man with $n$ keys wants to open his door and tries the keys at random. Exactly one
key will open the door. Find the mean number of trials if

(a) unsuccessful keys are not eliminated from further selections.

(b) unsuccessful keys are eliminated.

3.5 A standard drug is known to be effective in 80% of the cases in which it is used. A new
drug is tested on 100 patients and found to be effective in 85 cases. Is the new drug
superior? (Hint: Evaluate the probability of observing 85 or more successes assuming
that the new and old drugs are equally effective.)

3.6 A large number of insects are expected to be attracted to a certain variety of rose plant.
A commercial insecticide is advertised as being 99% effective. Suppose 2,000 insects
infest a rose garden where the insecticide has been applied, and let $X = n u m b e r \quad o f$
surviving insects.

(a) What probability distribution might provide a reasonable model for this experi-
ment?

(b) Write down, but do not evaluate, an expression for the probability that fewer than
100 insects survive, using the model in part (a).

(c) Evaluate an approximation to the probability in part (b).

3.7 Let the number of chocolate chips in a certain type of cookie have a Poisson distribu-
tion. We want the probability that a randomly chosen cookie has at least two chocolate
chips to be greater than .99. Find the smallest value of the mean of the distribution
that ensures this probability.

3.8 Two movie theaters compete for the business of 1,000 customers. Assume that each
customer chooses between the movie theaters independently and with "indifference."
Let N denote the number of seats in each theater.

(a) Using a binomial model, find an expression for $N$ that will guarantee that the
probability of turning away a customer (because of a full house) is less than 1%.

(b) Use the normal approximation to get a numerical value for $N .$

3.9 Often, news stories that are reported as startling "one-in-a-million" coincidences are
actually, upon closer examination, not rare events and can even be expected to occur.
A few years ago an elementary school in New York state reported that its incoming
kindergarten class contained five sets of twins. This, of course, was reported throughout
the state, with a quote from the principal that this was a "statistical impossibility".
Was it? Or was it an instance of what Diaconis and Mosteller (1989) call the "law of
truly large numbers"? Let's do some calculations.

(a) The probability of a twin birth is approximately $1 / 9 0 ,$ and we can assume that
an elementary school will have approximately 60 children entering kindergarten
(three classes of 20 each). Explain how our "statistically impossible" event can be
thought of as the probability of 5 or more successes from a binomial(60, 1/90). Is
this even rare enough to be newsworthy?

(b) Even if the probability in part (a) is rare enough to be newsworthy, consider that
this could have happened in any school in the county, and in any county in the
state, and it still would have been reported exactly the same. (The "law of truly
large numbers" is starting to come into play.) New York state has 62 counties, and
it is reasonable to assume that each county has five elementary schools. Does the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="129" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

event still qualify as a "statistical impossibility", or is it becoming something that
could be expected to occur?

(c) If the probability in part (b) still seems small, consider further that this event
could have happened in any one of the 50 states, during any of the last 10 years,
and still would have received the same news coverage.

In addition to Diaconis and Mosteller (1989), see Hanley (1992) for more on coinci-
dences.

3.10 Shuster (1991) describes a number of probability calculations that he did for a court
case involving the sale of cocaine. A Florida police department seized 496 suspected
packets of cocaine, of which four were randomly selected and tested and found to
actually be cocaine. The police then chose two more packets at random and, posing
as drug dealers, sold the packets to the defendant. These last two packets were lost
before they could be tested to verify that they were, indeed, cocaine.

(a) If the original 496 packets were composed of $N$ packets of cocaine and $M = 4 9 6 - N$
noncocaine, show that the probability of selecting 4 cocaine packets and then 2
noncocaine packets, which is the probability that the defendant is innocent of
buying cocaine, is

$$\frac { \left( \begin{array}{} { N } \\ { 4 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { M } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) } { \left( \begin{array}{} { N + M } \\ { 4 } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { N + M - 4 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) }$$

(b) Maximizing (in $M$ and $\left. N \right)$ the probability in part (a) maximizes the defendant's
"innocence probability". Show that this probability is .022, attained at $M = 1 6 5$
and $N = 3 3 1 .$

3.11 $\mathrm { T h e }$ hypergeometric distribution can be approximated by either the binomial or the
Poisson distribution. (Of course, it can be approximated by other distributions, but in
this exercise we will concentrate on only these two.) Let $X$ have the hypergeometric
distribution

$$P \left( X = x | N , M , K \right) = \frac { \binom { M } { x } \binom { N - M } { K - x } } { \binom { N } { K } } , x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , K .$$

(a) Show that as $N \rightarrow \infty ,$ $M \rightarrow \infty ,$ and $M / N \rightarrow p ,$

$$P \left( X = x | N , M , K \right) \rightarrow \left( \begin{array}{} K \\ x \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { K - x } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots , K .$$

(Stirling's Formula from Exercise 1.23 may be helpful.)

(b) Use the fact that the binomial can be approximated by the Poisson to show that
if $N \rightarrow \infty ,$ $M \rightarrow \infty ,$ K -> co, $M / N \rightarrow 0 ,$ and $K M / N \rightarrow \lambda ,$ then

$$P \left( X = x | N , M , K \right) \rightarrow \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { x } } { x ! } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , \ldots .$$

(c) Verify the approximation in part (b) directly, without using the Poisson approxi-
mation to the binomial. (Lemma 2.3.14 is helpful.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="130" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

3.12 Suppose $X$ has a binomial(n, $\left. p \right)$ distribution and let $Y$ have a negative binomial(r, $\left. p \right)$
distribution. Show that $F _ { X } \left( r - 1 \right) = 1 - F _ { Y } \left( n - r \right) .$

3.13 A truncated discrete distribution is one in which a particular class cannot be observed
and is eliminated from the sample space. In particular, if $X$ has range $0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots .$ and
the 0 class cannot be observed (as is usually the case), the 0-truncated random variable
$X _ { T }$ has pmf

$$P \left( X _ { T } = x \right) = \frac { P \left( X = x \right) } { P \left( X > 0 \right) } , \quad x = 1 , 2 , \ldots$$

Find the pmf, mean, and variance of the 0-truncated random variable starting from

(a) $X \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) .$

(b) X ~ negative binomial(r, $\left. p \right) ,$ as in (3.2.10).

3.14 Starting from the $0$ 0-truncated negative binomial (refer to Exercise 3.13), if we let $r \rightarrow$
0, we get an interesting distribution, the logarithmic series distribution. $A$ random
variable $X$ has a logarithmic series distribution with parameter $p$ if

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \frac { - \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { x } } { x \log p } , \quad x = 1 , 2 , \ldots , \quad 0 < p < 1 .$$

(a) Verify that this defines a legitimate probability function.

(b) Find the mean and variance of $X .$ (The logarithmic series distribution has proved
useful in modeling species abundance. See Stuart and Ord 1987 for a more detailed
discussion of this distribution.)

3.15 In Section 3.2 it was claimed that the $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ distribution is the limit of the negative
binomial(r, $\left. p \right)$ distribution as $r \rightarrow \infty ,$ $p \rightarrow 1 ,$ and $r \left( 1 - p \right) \rightarrow \lambda .$ Show that under these
conditions the mgf of the negative binomial converges to that of the Poisson.

3.16 Verify these two identities regarding the gamma function that were given in the text:

(a) $\Gamma \left( \alpha + 1 \right) = \alpha \Gamma \left( \alpha \right)$

(b) $\Gamma \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) = \sqrt { \pi }$

3.17 Establish a formula similar to (3.3.18) for the gamma distribution. $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$
then for any positive constant $\nu$ v,

$$E X ^ { \nu } = \frac { \beta ^ { \nu } \Gamma \left( \nu + \alpha \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) } .$$

3.18 There is an interesting relationship between negative binomial and gamma random
variables, which may sometimes provide a useful approximation. Let $Y$ be a negative
binomial random variable with parameters $r$ and $p ,$ where $p$ is the success probability.
Show that as $p \rightarrow 0 ,$ the mgf of the random variable $p Y$ converges to that of a gamma
distribution with parameters $r$ and 1.

3.19 Show that

$$\int _ { x } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) } z ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - z } d z = \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { \alpha - 1 } \frac { x ^ { y } e ^ { - x } } { y ! } , \quad \alpha = 1 , 2 , 3 , \ldots .$$

(Hint: Use integration by parts.) Express this formula as a probabilistic relationship
between Poisson and gamma random variables.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="131" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

3.20 Let the random variable $X$ have the pdf

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad 0 < x < \infty .$$

(a) Find the mean and variance of $X .$ (This distribution is sometimes called a folded
normal.)

(b) If $X$ has the folded normal distribution, find the transformation $g \left( X \right) = Y$ and
values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ so that $Y \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) .$

3.21 Write the integral that would define the mgf of the pdf

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }$$

Is the integral finite? (Do you expect it to be?)

3.22 For each of the following distributions, verify the formulas for $E X$ and Var $X$ given in
the text.

(a) Verify $\mathrm { V a r } X$ if $X$ has $\text { a Poisson } \left( \lambda \right)$ distribution. (Hint: Compute $E X \left( X \quad - \quad 1 \right) =$
$E X ^ { 2 } - E X$

(b) Verify Var $X$ if $X$ has a negative binomial(r, $\mathrm { l } \left( r , p \right)$ distribution.

(c) Verify $\mathrm { V a r } X$ if $X$ has a gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution.

(d) Verify $\mathrm { E } X$ and Var $X$ if $X$ has a $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution.
☒

(e) Verify $\mathrm { E } X$ and Var $X$ if $X$ has a double exponential(u, $\left( \mu , \sigma \right)$ distribution.
☒ ☒

3.23 The Pareto distribution, with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$ has pdf

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { \beta \alpha ^ { \beta } } { x ^ { \beta + 1 } } , \quad \alpha < x < \infty , \quad \alpha > 0 , \quad \beta > 0 .$$

(a) Verify that $f \left( x \right)$ is a pdf.

(b) Derive the mean and variance of this distribution.

(c) Prove that the variance does not exist if $\beta \leq 2 .$

3.24 Many "named" distributions are special cases of the more common distributions al-
ready discussed. For each of the following named distributions derive the form of the
pdf, verify that it is a pdf, and calculate the mean and variance.

(a) If $X \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \beta \right) ,$ then $Y = X ^ { 1 / \gamma }$ has the Weibull(y, $l \left( \gamma , \beta \right)$ distribution, where
$\gamma > 0$ is a constant.

(b) If $X \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \beta \right) ,$ then $Y = \left( 2 X / \beta \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ has the Rayleigh distribution.

(c) If $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( a , b \right) ,$ then $Y = 1 / X$ has the inverted gamma $I G \left( a , b \right)$ distribution.
(This distribution is useful in Bayesian estimation of variances; see Exercise 7.23.)

(d) If $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } , \beta \right) ,$ then $Y = \left( X / \beta \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ has the Maxwell distribution.

(e) If $X \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( 1 \right) ,$ then $Y = \alpha - \gamma \log X$ has the $G u m b e l \left( \alpha , \gamma \right)$ distribution,
☒ ☒
where $- \infty < \alpha < \infty$ and $\gamma > 0 .$ (The Gumbel distribution is also known as the
extreme value distribution.)

3.25 Suppose the random variable $T$ is the length of life of an object (possibly the lifetime
of an electrical component or of a subject given a particular treatment). The hazard
function $h _ { T } \left( t \right)$ associated with the random variable $T$ is defined by

$$h _ { T } \left( t \right) = \lim _ { \delta \rightarrow 0 } \frac { P \left( t \leq T < t + \delta | T \geq t \right) } { \delta } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="132" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

Thus, we can interpret $h _ { T } \left( t \right)$ as the rate of change of the probability that the object
survives a little past time $t ,$ given that the object survives to time $t .$ Show that if $T$ is
a continuous random variable, then

$$h _ { T } \left( t \right) = \frac { f _ { T } \left( t \right) } { 1 - F _ { T } \left( t \right) } = - \frac { d } { d t } \log \left( 1 - F _ { T } \left( t \right) \right) .$$

3.26 Verify that the following pdfs have the indicated hazard functions (see Exercise 3.25).

(a) $I f \quad T \sim e x p o n e n t i a l \left( 3 \right) ,$ then $h _ { T } \left( t \right) = 1 / \beta .$

(b) If $T \sim \mathrm { W e i b u l l } \left( \gamma , \beta \right) ,$ then $h _ { T } \left( t \right) = \left( \gamma / \beta \right) t ^ { \gamma - 1 } .$

(c) If $T \sim \mathrm { l o g i s t i c } \left( \mu , \beta \right) ,$ that is,

$$F _ { T } \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + e ^ { - \left( t - \mu \right) / \beta } }$$

then $h _ { T } \left( t \right) = \left( 1 / \beta \right) F _ { T } \left( t \right) .$

3.27 For each of the following families, show whether all the pdfs in the family are unimodal
(see Exercise 2.27).

(a) uniform(a, $\left. b \right)$

(b) $\mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$

(c) $\mathrm { n } \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$

(d) $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$

3.28 Show that each of the following families is an exponential family.

(a) normal family with either parameter $\mu$ or $\sigma$ known

(b) gamma family with either parameter $\alpha$ or $\beta$ known or both unknown

(c) beta family with either parameter $\alpha$ or $\beta$ known or both unknown

(d) Poisson family

(e) negative binomial family with $r$ known, $0 < p < 1$

3.29 For each family in Exercise 3.28, describe the natural parameter space.
3.30 Use the identities of Theorem 3.4.2 to

(a) calculate the variance of a binomial random variable.

(b) calculate the mean and variance of a $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ random variable.
☒

3.31 In this exercise we will prove Theorem 3.4.2.

(a) Start from the equality

$$\int h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) d x = 1 ,$$

differentiate both sides, and then rearrange terms to establish (3.4.4). (The fact
that $\frac { d } { d x } \log g \left( x \right) = g ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) / g \left( x \right)$ will be helpful.)

(b) Differentiate the above equality a second time; then rearrange to establish (3.4.5).
(The fact that
$\frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d x ^ { 2 } } \log g \left( x \right) = \left( g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x \right) / g \left( x \right) \right) - \left( g ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) / g \left( x \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$ will be helpful.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="133" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

3.32 (a) If an exponential family can be written in the form (3.4.7), show that the identities
of Theorem 3.4.2 simplify to

$$E \left( t _ { j } \left( X \right) \right) = - \frac { \partial } { \partial \eta _ { j } } \log c ^ { * } \left( \eta \right) ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( t _ { j } \left( X \right) \right) = - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \eta _ { j } ^ { 2 } } \log c ^ { * } \left( \eta \right) .$$

(b) Use this identity to calculate the mean and variance of a gamma(a, $\left. b \right)$ random
variable.

3.33 For each of the following families:

(i) Verify that it is an exponential family.

(ii) Describe the curve on which the $\theta$ parameter vector lies.

(iii) Sketch a graph of the curved parameter space.

(a) $n \left( \theta , \theta \right)$

(b) $n \left( \theta , a \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ a known

(c) $\mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , 1 / \alpha \right)$

(d) $f \left( x | \theta \right) = C \exp \left( - \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 4 } \right) ,$ $C$ a normalizing constant

3.34 In Example 3.4.9 we saw that normal approximations can result in curved exponential
families. For each of the following normal approximations:

(i) Describe the curve on which the $\theta$ parameter vector lies.

(ii) Sketch a graph of the curved parameter space.

(a) Poisson approximation: $\bar { X } \sim n \left( \lambda , \lambda / n \right)$

(b) binomial approximation: $\bar { X } \sim n \left( p , p \left( 1 - p \right) / n \right)$

(c) negative binomial approximation: $\bar { X } \sim n \left( r \left( 1 - p \right) / p , r \left( 1 - p \right) / n p ^ { 2 } \right)$
☒

3.35 (a) The normal family that approximates a Poisson can also be parameterized as
$n \left( e ^ { \theta } , e ^ { \theta } \right) ,$ where $- \infty < \theta < \infty .$ Sketch a graph of the parameter space, and
compare with the approximation in Exercise 3.34(a).

(b) Suppose that $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ and we assume that $E X = \mu .$ Sketch a graph of
the parameter space.

(c) Suppose that $X _ { i } \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n ,$ and we assume that $E X _ { i } = \mu .$
Describe the parameter space $\left( \alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n } \right) .$

3.36 Consider the pdf $f \left( x \right) = \frac { 6 3 } { 4 } \left( x ^ { 6 } - x ^ { 8 } \right) , - 1 < x < 1 .$ Graph $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right)$ for each
of the following on the same axes.

(a) $\mu = 0 ,$ $\sigma = 1$

(b) $\mu = 3 ,$ $\sigma = 1$

(c) $\mu = 3 ,$ $\sigma = 2$

3.37 Show that if $f \left( x \right)$ is a pdf, symmetric about 0, $\mu$ is the median of the location-scale
pdf $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right) , - \infty < x < \infty .$

3.38 Let $Z$ be a random variable with pdf $f \left( z \right) .$ Define $z _ { \alpha }$ to be a number that satisfies this
relationship:

$$\alpha = P \left( Z > z _ { \alpha } \right) = \int _ { z _ { \alpha } } ^ { \infty } f \left( z \right) d z .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="134" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.7" -->

Show that if $X$ is a random variable with pdf $\left( 1 / \sigma \right) f \left( \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right)$ and $x _ { \alpha } = \sigma z _ { \alpha } + \mu ,$
then $P \left( X > x _ { \alpha } \right) = \alpha .$ (Thus if a table of $z _ { \alpha }$ values were available, then values of $x _ { a }$
could be easily computed for any member of the location-scale family.)

3.39 Consider the Cauchy family defined in Section 3.3. This family can be extended to a
location-scale family yielding pdfs of the form

$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sigma \pi \left( 1 + \left( \frac { x - \mu } { \sigma } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty .$$

The mean and variance do not exist for the Cauchy distribution. So the parameters
$\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are not the mean and variance. But they do have important meaning. Show
that if $X$ is a random variable with a Cauchy distribution with parameters $\mu$ and o,
then:

(a) $\mu$ is the median of the distribution of $X ,$ that is, $P \left( X \geq \mu \right) = P \left( X \leq \mu \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$

(b) $\mu + \sigma$ and $\mu - \sigma$ are the quartiles of the distribution of $X ,$ that is, $P \left( X \geq \mu + \sigma \right) =$
$P \left( X \leq \mu - \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { 4 } .$ (Hint: Prove this first for $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$ and then use
Exercise 3.38.)

3.40 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be any pdf with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Show how to create a location-scale
family based on $f \left( x \right)$ such that the standard pdf of the family, say $f ^ { * } \left( x \right) ,$ has mean $0$
and variance 1.

3.41 $A$ family of cdfs $\left\{ F \left( x | \theta \right) , \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ is stochastically increasing in $\theta$ if $\theta _ { 1 } > \theta _ { 2 } \Rightarrow F \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right)$
is stochastically greater than $F \left( x | \theta _ { 2 } \right) .$ (See Exercise 1.49 for the definition of stochas-
tically greater.)

(a) Show that the $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ family is stochastically increasing in $\mu$ for fixed $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Show that the gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ family of (3.3.6) is stochastically increasing in $\beta$ (scale
parameter) for fixed $\alpha$ (shape parameter).

3.42 Refer to Exercise 3.41 for the definition of a stochastically increasing family.

(a) Show that a location family is stochastically increasing in its location parameter.

(b) Show that a scale family is stochastically increasing in its scale parameter if the
sample space is $\left[ 0 , \infty \right) .$

3.43 $\mathrm { A }$ family of cdfs $\left\{ F \left( x | \theta \right) , \theta \in \theta \right\}$ is stochastically decreasing in $\theta$ if $\theta _ { 1 } > \theta _ { 2 } \Rightarrow F \left( x | \theta _ { 2 } \right)$
is stochastically greater than $F \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) .$ (See Exercises 3.41 and 3.42.)

(a) Prove that if $X \sim F _ { X } \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where the sample space of $X$ is $\left( 0 , \infty \right)$ and $F _ { X } \left( x | \theta \right)$ is
stochastically increasing in $\theta ,$ then $F _ { Y } \left( y | \theta \right)$ is stochastically decreasing in $\theta ,$ where
$Y = 1 / X .$

(b) Prove that if $X \sim F _ { X } \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where $F _ { X } \left( x | \theta \right)$ is stochastically increasing in $\theta$ and
$\theta > 0 ,$ then $F _ { X } \left( x | \frac { 1 } { \theta } \right)$ is stochastically decreasing in $\theta .$

3.44 For any random variable $X$ for which $E X ^ { 2 }$ and $\mathrm { E } | X |$ exist, show that $P \left( | X | \geq b \right)$ does
not exceed either $E X ^ { 2 } / b ^ { 2 }$ or $\mathrm { E } | X | / b ,$ where $b$ is a positive constant. If $f \left( x \right) = e ^ { - x }$ for
$x > 0 ,$ show that one bound is better when $b = 3$ and the other when $b = \sqrt { 2 } .$ (Notice
Markov's Inequality in Miscellanea 3.8.2.)

3.45 Let $X$ be a random variable with moment-generating function $M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ $- h < t < h .$

(a) Prove that $P \left( X \geq a \right) \leq e ^ { - a t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ $0 < t < h .$ (A proof similar to that used for
Chebychev's Inequality will work.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="135" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.8" -->

(b) Similarly, prove that $P \left( X \leq a \right) \leq e ^ { - a t } M _ { X } \left( t \right) , \quad - h < t < 0 .$

(c) A special case of part $\left( a \right)$ is that $P \left( X \geq 0 \right) \leq E e ^ { t X }$ for all $t \geq 0$ for which
the mgf is defined. What are general conditions on a function $h \left( t , x \right)$ such that
$P \left( X \geq 0 \right) \leq \mathrm { E h } \left( t , X \right)$ for all $t \geq 0$ for which $\mathrm { E h } \left( t , X \right)$ exists? (In part (a),
$h \left( t , x \right) = e ^ { t x }$

3.46 Calculate $P \left( | X - \mu _ { X } | \geq k \sigma _ { X } \right)$ for $X \sim u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ and $X \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ and
compare your answers to the bound from Chebychev's Inequality.

3.47 If $Z$ is a standard normal random variable, prove this companion to the inequality in
Example 3.6.3:

$$P \left( | Z | \geq t \right) \geq \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { \pi } } \frac { t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } e ^ { - t ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$

3.48 Derive recursion relations, similar to the one given in (3.6.2), for the binomial, negative
binomial, and hypergeometric distributions.

3.49 Prove the following analogs to Stein's Lemma, assuming appropriate conditions on the
function $g .$

(a) If $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ then

$$E \left( g \left( X \right) \left( X - \alpha \beta \right) \right) = \beta E \left( X g ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) \right) .$$

(b) If $X \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ then

$$E \left[ g \left( X \right) \left( \beta - \left( \alpha - 1 \right) \frac { \left( 1 - X \right) } { X } \right) \right] = E \left( \left( 1 - X \right) g ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) \right)$$

3.50 Prove the identity for the negative binomial distribution given in Theorem 3.6.8, part
(b).


## 3.8 Miscellanea

3.8.1 The Poisson Postulates

The Poisson distribution can be derived from a set of basic assumptions, sometimes
called the Poisson postulates. These assumptions relate to the physical properties
of the process under consideration. While, generally speaking, the assumptions are
not very easy to verify, they do provide an experimenter with a set of guidelines
for considering whether the Poisson will provide a reasonable model. For a more
complete treatment of the Poisson postulates, see the classic text by Feller (1968)
or Barr and Zehna (1983).

Theorem 3.8.1 For each $t \geq 0 ,$ let $N _ { t }$ be an integer-valued random variable with
the following properties. (Think of $N _ { t }$ as denoting the number of arrivals in the
time period from time 0 to time $t$ t.)

$\left. i \right)$ $N _ { 0 } = 0$

ii) $s < t \Rightarrow N _ { s }$ and $N _ { t } - N _ { s }$ are independent.

(

(start with no arrivals)
arrivals in disjoint time
)

periods are independent

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="136" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.8" -->

iii) $N _ { s }$ and $N _ { t + s } - N _ { t }$ are identically distributed.
(
number of arrivals depends

only on period length
)

iv)
$\lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { P \left( N _ { t } = 1 \right) } { t } = \lambda$
arrival probability proportional
)

(
to period length, if length is small,

v)
$\lim _ { t \rightarrow 0 } \frac { P \left( N _ { t } > 1 \right) } { t } = 0$
(no simultaneous arrivals)

If $i - v$ hold, then for any integer n,

$$P \left( N _ { t } = n \right) = e ^ { - \lambda t } \frac { \left( \lambda t \right) ^ { n } } { n ! }$$

that is, $N _ { t } \sim P o i s s o n \left( \lambda t \right) .$

The postulates may also be interpreted as describing the behavior of objects spa-
tially (for example, movement of insects), giving the Poisson application in spatial
distributions.


### 3.8.2 Chebychev and Beyond

Ghosh and Meeden (1977) discuss the fact that Chebychev's Inequality is very
conservative and is almost never attained. If we write $\bar { X } _ { n }$ for the mean of the
random variables $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ then Chebychev's Inequality states

$$P \left( | \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu | \geq k \sigma \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { n k ^ { 2 } }$$

They prove the following theorem.

Theorem 3.8.2 If $0 < \sigma < \infty$ , then

a. If $n = 1 ,$ the inequality is attainable for $k \geq 1$ and unattainable for $0 < k <$ 1.

b. If $n = 2 ,$ the inequality is attainable $i f$ and only if $k = 1 .$

c. If $n \geq 3 ,$ the inequality is not attainable.

Examples are given for the cases when the inequality is attained. Most of their
technical arguments are based on the following inequality, known as Markov's
Inequality.

Lemma 3.8.3 (Markov's Inequality) If $P \left( Y \geq 0 \right) = 1$ and $P \left( Y = 0 \right) < 1 ,$
then, for any $r > 0 ,$

$$P \left( Y \geq r \right) \leq \frac { E Y } { r }$$

with equality $i f$ and only if $P \left( Y = r \right) = p = 1 - P \left( Y = 0 \right) , 0 < p \leq 1 .$

Markov's Inequality can then be applied to the quantity

$$Y = \frac { \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$

to get the above results.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="137" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.8" -->

One reason Chebychev's Inequality is so loose is that it puts no restrictions on
the underlying distribution. With the additional restriction of unimodality, we can
get tighter bounds and the inequalities of Gauss and Vysochanskii-Petunin. (See
Pukelsheim 1994 for details and elementary calculus-based proofs of these inequal-
ities.)

Theorem 3.8.4 (Gauss Inequality) Let $X \sim f ,$ where $f$ is unimodal with
mode v, and define $\tau ^ { 2 } = E \left( X - \nu \right) ^ { 2 } .$ Then

$$P \left( | X - \nu | > \varepsilon \right) \leq \left\{ \begin{array}{} \frac { 4 \tau ^ { 2 } } { 9 \varepsilon ^ { 2 } } & \text { for all } \varepsilon \geq \sqrt { 4 / 3 } \tau \\ 1 - \frac { \varepsilon } { \sqrt { 3 } \tau } & \text { for all } \varepsilon \leq \sqrt { 4 / 3 } \tau . \end{array} \right.$$ ☒ ☒

Although this is a tighter bound than Chebychev, the dependence on the mode
limits its usefulness. The extension of Vysochanskii-Petunin removes this limita-
tion.

Theorem 3.8.5 (Vysochanskii-Petunin Inequality) Let $X \sim f ,$ where $f$ is
unimodal, and define $\xi ^ { 2 } = E \left( X - \alpha \right) ^ { 2 }$ for an arbitrary point a. Then

$$P \left( | X - \alpha | > \varepsilon \right) \leq \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 4 \xi ^ { 2 } } { 9 \varepsilon ^ { 2 } } } & { \text { for all } \varepsilon \geq \sqrt { 8 / 3 } \xi } \\ { \frac { 4 \xi ^ { 2 } } { 9 \varepsilon ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } & \text { for all } \varepsilon \leq \sqrt { 8 / 3 } \xi } \end{array} \right.$$ ☒ ☒

Pukelsheim points out that taking $\alpha = \mu = E \left( X \right)$ and $\varepsilon = 3 \sigma ,$ where $\sigma ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } \left( X \right) ,$
yields

$$P \left( | X - \mu | > 3 \sigma \right) \leq \frac { 4 } { 8 1 } < . 0 5 ,$$

the so-called three-sigma rule, that the probability is less than 5% that $X$ is more
than three standard deviations from the mean of the population.


### 3.8.3 More on Exponential Families

Is the lognormal distribution in the exponential family? The density given in
(3.3.21) can be put into the form specified by (3.4.1). Hence, we have put the
lognormal into the exponential family.

According to Brown (1986, Section 1.1), to define an exponential family of distri-
butions we start with $a$ nonnegative function $\nu \left( x \right)$ and define the set $\mathcal{N}$ by

$$N = \left\{ \theta : \int _ { x } e ^ { \theta x } \nu \left( x \right) d x < \infty \right\}$$

If we let $\lambda \left( \theta \right) = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } e ^ { \theta x } \nu \left( x \right) d x ,$ the set of probability densities defined by

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { e ^ { \theta x } \nu \left( x \right) } { \lambda \left( \theta \right) } , \quad x \in \mathcal{X} , \quad \theta \in \mathcal{N} ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="138" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMMON FAMILIES OF DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 3.8" -->

is an exponential family. The moment-generating function of $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } e ^ { t x } f \left( x | \theta \right) d x = \frac { \lambda \left( t + \theta \right) } { \lambda \left( \theta \right) }$$

and hence exists by construction. If the parameter space $\Theta$ is equal to the set $\mathcal{N} ,$
the exponential family is called full. Cases where $\Theta$ is a lower-dimensional subset
of N give rise to curved exponential families.

Returning to the lognormal distribution, we know that it does not have an mgf, so
it can't satisfy Brown's definition of an exponential family. However, the lognor-
mal satisfies the expectation identities of Theorem 3.4.2 and enjoys the sufficiency
properties detailed in Section 6.2.1 (Theorem 6.2.10). For our purposes, these are
the major properties that we need and the main reasons for identifying a member
of the exponential family. More advanced properties, which we will not investigate
here, may need the existence of the mgf.

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Chapter 4 Multiple Random Variables

"I confess that $I$ have been blind as a mole, but $i t$ is better to learn wisdom late
than never $t o$ learn $i t$ at all."

Sherlock Holmes
The Man with the Twisted Lip


## 4.1 Joint and Marginal Distributions

In previous chapters, we have discussed probability models and computation of prob-
ability for events involving only one random variable. These are called univariate
models. In this chapter, we discuss probability models that involve more than one
random variable-naturally enough, called multivariate models.

In an experimental situation, it would be very unusual to observe only the value of
one random variable. That is, it would be an unusual experiment in which the total
data collected consisted of just one numeric value. For example, consider an experi-
ment designed to gain information about some health characteristics of a population
of people. It would be a modest experiment indeed if the only datum collected was
the body weight of one person. Rather, the body weights of several people in the
population might be measured. These different weights would be observations on dif-
ferent random variables, one for each person measured. Multiple observations could
also arise because several physical characteristics were measured on each person. For
example, temperature, height, and blood pressure, in addition to weight, might be
measured. These observations on different characteristics could also be modeled as
observations on different random variables. Thus, we need to know how to describe
and use probability models that deal with more than one random variable at a time.
For the first several sections we will discuss mainly bivariate models, models involving
two random variables.

Recall that, in Definition 1.4.1, a (univariate) random variable was defined to be a
function from a sample space $S$ into the real numbers. A random vector, consisting
of several random variables, is defined similarly.

Definition 4.1.1 An n-dimensional random vector is a function from a sample space
$S$ into $\mathfrak{R} ^ { n } ,$ n-dimensional Euclidean space.

Suppose, for example, that with each point in a sample space we associate an
ordered pair of numbers, that is, a point $\left( x , y \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ denotes the plane. Then

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="140" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

we have defined a two-dimensional (or bivariate) random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Example 4.1.2
illustrates this.

Example 4.1.2 (Sample space for dice) Consider the experiment of tossing
two fair dice. The sample space for this experiment has 36 equally likely points and
was introduced in Example 1.3.10. For example, the sample point (3,3) denotes the
outcome in which both dice show a 3; the sample point $\left( 4 , 1 \right)$ denotes the outcome in
which the first die shows a 4 and the second die a 1; etc. Now, with each of these 36
points associate two numbers, $X$ and $Y .$ Let

$$Y = | d i f f e r e n c e \quad o f \quad t h e \quad t w o \quad d i c e l .$$
X = sum of the two dice and

For the sample point (3, 3), $X = 3 + 3 = 6$ and $Y = | 3 \quad - \quad 3 | = 0 .$ For $\left( 4 , 1 \right) ,$ $X = 5$ and
$Y = 3 .$ These are also the values of $X$ and $Y$ for the sample point $\left( 1 , 4 \right) .$ For each of
the 36 sample points we could compute the values of $X$ and $Y .$ In this way we have
defined the bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$

Having defined a random vector $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ we can now discuss probabilities of events
that are defined in terms of $\left( X , Y \right) .$ The probabilities of events defined in terms of
$X$ and $Y$ are just defined in terms of the probabilities of the corresponding events
in the sample space $S .$ What is $P \left( X = 5 \right.$ and $Y =$ 3)? You can verify that the only
two sample points that yield $X = 5$ and $Y = 3$ are $\left( 4 , 1 \right)$ and $\left( 1 , 4 \right) .$ Thus the event
$X = 5$ and $Y = 3 ^ { \prime \prime }$ will occur if and only if the event $\left( 4 , 1 \right) , \left( 1 , 4 \right)$ occurs. Since
each of the 36 sample points in $S$ is equally likely,

$$P \left( \left\{ \left( 4 , 1 \right) , \left( 1 , 4 \right) \right\} \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } :$$

Thus,

$$P \left( X = 5 \text { and } Y = 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } .$$

Henceforth, we will write $P \left( X = 5 , Y = 3 \right)$ for $P \left( X = 5 \quad a n d \quad Y = 3 \right) .$ Read the comma
as "and." Similarly, $P \left( X = 6 , Y = 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 6 }$ because the only sample point that yields
these values of $X$ and $Y$ is $\left( 3 , 3 \right) .$ For more complicated events, the technique is the
same. For example, $P \left( X = 7 , Y \leq 4 \right) = \frac { 4 } { 3 6 } = \frac { 1 } { 9 } ,$ because the only four sample points
that $\mathrm { y }$ yield $X = 7$ and $Y \leq 4$ are $\left( 4 , 3 \right) ,$ $\left( 3 , 4 \right) ,$ $\left( 5 , 2 \right) ,$ and $\left( 2 , 5 \right) .$
I

The random vector $\left( X , Y \right)$ defined above is called a discrete random vector because
it has only a countable (in this case, finite) number of possible values. For a discrete
random vector, the function $f \left( x , y \right)$ defined by $f \left( x , y \right) = P \left( X = x , Y = y \right)$ can be
used to compute any probabilities of events defined in terms of $\left( X , Y \right) .$

Definition 4.1.3 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a discrete bivariate random vector. Then the func-
tion $f \left( x , y \right)$ from $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ into $\mathfrak{R}$ defined by $f \left( x , y \right) = P \left( X = x , Y = y \right)$ is called the
joint probability mass function or joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right) .$ If it is necessary to stress the
fact that $f$ is the joint pmf of the vector $\left( X , Y \right)$ rather than some other vector, the
notation $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$ will be used.
