</table>


The joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ completely defines the probability distribution of the ran-
dom vector $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ just as the pmf of a discrete univariate random variable completely
defines its distribution. For the $\left( X , Y \right)$ defined in Example 4.1.2 in terms of the roll of
a pair of dice, there are 21 possible values of $\left( X , Y \right) .$ The value of $f \left( x , y \right)$ for each of
these 21 possible values is given in Table 4.1.1. Two of these values, $f \left( 5 , 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 8 }$ and
$f \left( 6 , 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 6 } ,$ were computed above and the rest are obtained by similar reasoning.
The joint pmf $f \left( x , y \right)$ is defined for all $\left( x , y \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ not just the 21 pairs in Table 4.1.1.
For any other $\left( x , y \right) ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) = P \left( X = x , Y = y \right) = 0 .$

The joint pmf can be used to compute the probability of any event defined in terms
of $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Let $A$ be any subset of $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ Then

$$P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) = \sum _ { \left( x , y \right) \in A } f \left( x , y \right) .$$

Since $\left( X , Y \right)$ is discrete, $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero for at most a countable number of points
$\left( x , y \right) .$ Thus, the sum can be interpreted as a countable sum even if $A$ contains an
uncountable number of points. For example, let $A = \left( x , y \right) : x = 7$ and $\left. y \leq 4 \right\} .$ This
is a half-infinite line in $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ But from Table 4.1.1 we see that the only $\left( x , y \right) \in A$ for
which $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero are $\left( x , y \right) = \left( 7 , 1 \right)$ and $\left( x , y \right) = \left( 7 , 3 \right) .$ Thus,

$$P \left( X = 7 , Y \leq 4 \right) = P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) = f \left( 7 , 1 \right) + f \left( 7 , 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 9 } .$$

This, of course, is the same value computed in Example 4.1.2 by considering the
definition of $\left( X , Y \right)$ and sample points in $S .$ It is usually simpler to work with the
joint pmf than it is to work with the fundamental definition.

Expectations of functions of random vectors are computed just as with univariate
random variables. Let $g \left( x , y \right)$ be a real-valued function defined for all possible values
$\left( x , y \right)$ of the discrete random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Then $g \left( X , Y \right)$ is itself a random variable
and its expected value $\mathrm { E } g \left( X , Y \right)$ is given by

$$\mathrm { E g } \left( X , Y \right) = \sum _ { \left( x , y \right) \in \mathrm { I } \mathrm { R } ^ { 2 } } g \left( x , y \right) f \left( x , y \right) .$$

Example 4.1.4 (Continuation of Example 4.1.2) For the $\left( X , Y \right)$ whose joint
pmf is given in Table 4.1.1, what is the average value of $X Y ?$ Letting $g \left( x , y \right) = x y ,$ we

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="142" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

compute $E X Y = \mathrm { E } g \left( X , Y \right)$ by computing $x y f \left( x , y \right)$ for each of the 21 $\left( x , y \right)$ points in
Table 4.1.1 and summing these 21 terms. Thus,

$$E X Y = \left( 2 \right) \left( 0 \right) \frac { 1 } { 3 6 } + \left( 4 \right) \left( 0 \right) \frac { 1 } { 3 6 } + \cdots + \left( 8 \right) \left( 4 \right) \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } + \left( 7 \right) \left( 5 \right) \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } = 1 3 \frac { 1 1 } { 1 8 } .$$

The expectation operator continues to have the properties listed in Theorem 2.2.5
when the random variable $X$ is replaced by the random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ For example,
if $g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right)$ and $g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$ are two functions and a, $b ,$ and $c$ are constants, then

$$\mathrm { E } \left( a g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right) + b g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right) + c \right) = a \mathrm { E } g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right) + b \mathrm { E } g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right) + c .$$

These properties follow from the properties of sums exactly as in the univariate case
(see Exercise 4.2).

The joint pmf for any discrete bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right)$ must have certain
properties. For any $\left( x , y \right) ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) \geq 0$ since $f \left( x , y \right)$ is a probability. Also, since $\left( X , Y \right)$
is certain to be in $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } ,$

$$\sum _ { \left( x , y \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } } f \left( x , y \right) = P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } \right) = 1 .$$

It turns out that any nonnegative function from $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ into $\mathfrak{R}$ that is nonzero for at most
a countable number of $\left( x , y \right)$ pairs and sums to 1 is the joint pmf for some bivariate
discrete random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Thus, by defining $f \left( x , y \right) ,$ we can define a probability
model for $\left( X , Y \right)$ without ever working with the fundamental sample space $S .$

Example 4.1.5 (Joint pmf for dice) Define $f \left( x , y \right)$ by

$$f \left( 0 , 0 \right) = f \left( 0 , 1 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } ,$$
$$f \left( 1 , 0 \right) = f \left( 1 , 1 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } ,$$
$$f \left( x , y \right) = 0 \quad f o r \quad a n y \quad o t h e r \left( x , y \right) .$$

Then $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonnegative and sums to 1, so $f \left( x , y \right)$ is the joint pmf for some
bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ We can use $f \left( x , y \right)$ to compute probabilities such as
$P \left( X = Y \right) = f \left( 0 , 0 \right) + f \left( 1 , 1 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ All this can be done without reference to the
sample space $S .$ Indeed, there are many sample spaces and functions thereon that
lead to this joint pmf for $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Here is one. Let $S$ be the 36-point sample space for
the experiment of tossing two fair dice. Let $X = 0$ if the first die shows at most 2 and
$X = 1$ if the first die shows more than 2. Let $Y = 0$ if the second die shows an odd
number and $Y = 1$ if the second die shows an.even number. It is left as Exercise 4.3
to show that this definition leads to the above probability distribution for $\left( X , Y \right) .$ |

Even if we are considering a probability model for a random vector $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ there
may be probabilities or expectations of interest that involve only one of the random
variables in the vector. We may wish to know $P \left( X = 2 \right) ,$ for instance. The variable $X$
is itself a random variable, in the sense of Chapter 1, and its probability distribution

|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="143" -->
<!-- PageHeader="JOINT AND MARGINAL DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

is described by its pmf, namely, $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = P \left( X = x \right) .$ (As mentioned earlier, we now
use the subscript to distinguish $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ from the joint pmf $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$ We now call
$f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ the marginal pmf of $X$ to emphasize the fact that it is the pmf of $X$ but in
the context of the probability model that gives the joint distribution of the vector
$\left( X , Y \right) .$ The marginal pmf of $X$ or $Y$ is easily calculated from the joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right)$
as Theorem 4.1.6 indicates.

Theorem 4.1.6 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a discrete bivariate random vector with joint $p m f$
$f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) .$ Then the marginal pmfs of $X$ and $Y ,$ $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = P \left( X = x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) =$
$P \left( Y = y \right) ,$ are given by

and
$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \sum _ { y \in \mathfrak{R} } f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$$
$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \sum _ { x \in \mathfrak{R} } f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) .$$

Proof: We will prove the result for $f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ The proof for $f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ is similar. For any
$x \in \mathfrak{R} ,$ let $A _ { x } = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : - \infty < y < \infty \right\} .$ That is, $A _ { x }$ is the line in the plane with
first coordinate equal to x. Then, for any $x \in \mathfrak{R} ,$

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = P \left( X = x \right)$$
$$= P \left( X = x , - \infty < Y < \infty \right) \quad \left( P \left( - \infty < Y < \infty \right) = 1 \right)$$
$$= P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A _ { x } \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { \left( x , y \right) \in A _ { x } } f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { y \in \mathfrak{R} } f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) .$$
(definition of $A _ { x }$
☐

Example 4.1.7 (Marginal pmf for dice) Using the result of Theorem 4.1.6, we
can compute the marginal distributions for $X$ and $Y$ from the joint distribution given
in Table 4.1.1. To compute the marginal pmf of $Y ,$ for each possible value of $Y$ we
sum over the possible values of $X .$ In this way we obtain

$$f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) = f _ { X , Y } \left( 2 , 0 \right) + f _ { X , Y } \left( 4 , 0 \right) + f _ { X , Y } \left( 6 , 0 \right)$$
$$+ f _ { X , Y } \left( 8 , 0 \right) + f _ { X , Y } \left( 1 0 , 0 \right) + f _ { X , Y } \left( 1 2 , 0 \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 6 } .$$

Similarly, we obtain

$$f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) = \frac { 5 } { 1 8 } , \quad f _ { Y } \left( 2 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 9 } , \quad f _ { Y } \left( 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } , \quad f _ { Y } \left( 4 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 9 } , \quad f _ { Y } \left( 5 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 8 } .$$

Notice that $f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 2 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 3 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 4 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 5 \right) = 1 ,$ as it must, since these
are the only six possible values of $Y .$
Il

The marginal pmf of $X$ or $Y$ is the same as the pmf of $X$ or $Y$ defined in Chapter 1.
The marginal pmf of $X$ or $Y$ can be used to compute probabilities or expectations that
involve only $X$ or $Y .$ But to compute a probability or expectation that simultaneously
involves both $X$ and $Y ,$ we must use the joint pmf of $X$ and $Y .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="144" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

Example 4.1.8 (Dice probabilities) Using the marginal pmf of $Y$ computed in
Example 4.1.7, we can compute

$$P \left( Y < 3 \right) = f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) + f _ { Y } \left( 2 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 6 } + \frac { 5 } { 1 8 } + \frac { 2 } { 9 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }$$

Also,

$$E Y ^ { 3 } = 0 ^ { 3 } f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) + \cdots + 5 ^ { 3 } f _ { Y } \left( 5 \right) = 2 0 \frac { 1 1 } { 1 8 } .$$

The marginal distributions of $X$ and $Y ,$ described by the marginal pmfs $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and
$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$ do not completely describe the joint distribution of $X$ and $Y .$ Indeed, there are
many different joint distributions that have the same marginal distributions. Thus, it
is hopeless to try to determine the joint pmf, $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) ,$ from knowledge of only the
marginal pmfs, $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ The next example illustrates the point.

Example 4.1.9 (Same marginals, different joint pmf) Define a joint pmf by

$$f \left( 0 , 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } , \quad f \left( 1 , 0 \right) = \frac { 5 } { 1 2 } , \quad f \left( 0 , 1 \right) = f \left( 1 , 1 \right) = \frac { 3 } { 1 2 } ,$$

$f \left( x , y \right) = 0$ for all other values.

The marginal pmf of $Y$ is $f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) = f \left( 0 , 0 \right) + f \left( 1 , 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ $f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) = f \left( 0 , 1 \right) + f \left( 1 , 1 \right) =$
$\frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ The marginal pmf of $X$ is $f _ { X } \left( 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 }$ and $f _ { X } \left( 1 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 3 } .$ Now check that for the joint
pmf given in Example 4.1.5, which is obviously different from the one given here, the
marginal pmfs of both $X$ and $Y$ are exactly the same as the ones just computed.
Thus, we cannot determine what the joint pmf is if we know only the marginal pmfs.
The joint pmf tells us additional information about the distribution of $\left( X , Y \right)$ that is
not found in the marginal distributions.
I

To this point we have discussed discrete bivariate random vectors. We can also
consider random vectors whose components are continuous random variables. The
probability distribution of a continuous random vector is usually described using a
density function, as in the univariate case.

Definition 4.1.10 $\mathrm { A }$ function $f \left( x , y \right)$ from $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ into $\mathfrak{R}$ is called a joint probability
density function or $j o i n t$ pdf of the continuous bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right)$ if, for
every $A C R ^ { 2 }$ ,

$$P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) = \int _ { A } \int f \left( x , y \right) d x d y .$$

A joint $p d f$ is used just like a univariate pdf except now the integrals are double
integrals over sets in the plane. The notation $\int \int _ { A }$ simply means that the limits of
integration are set so that the function is integrated over all $\left( x , y \right) \in A .$ Expectations
of functions of continuous random vectors are defined as in the discrete case with
integrals replacing sums and the pdf replacing the pmf. That is, if $g \left( x , y \right)$ is a real-
valued function, then the expected value of $g \left( X , Y \right)$ is defined to be

(4.1.2)
$$\mathrm { E g } \left( X , Y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x , y \right) f \left( x , y \right) d x d y .$$

|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="145" -->
<!-- PageHeader="JOINT AND MARGINAL DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

It is important to realize that the joint pdf is defined for all $\left( x , y \right) \in R ^ { 2 } .$ The pdf may
equal 0 on a large set $A$ if $P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) = 0$ but the pdf is defined for the points in
$A .$

The marginal probability density functions of $X$ and $Y$ are also defined as in the
discrete case with integrals replacing sums. The marginal pdfs may be used to compute
probabilities or expectations that involve only $X$ or $Y .$ Specifically, the marginal pdfs
of $X$ and $Y$ are given by

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y , \quad - \infty < \infty ,$$
$$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d x , \quad - \infty < y < \infty .$$
(4.1.3)

Any function $f \left( x , y \right)$ satisfying $f \left( x , y \right) \geq 0$ for all $\left( x , y \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ and

$$1 = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$

is the joint pdf of some continuous bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ All of these
concepts regarding joint pdfs are illustrated in the following two examples.

Example 4.1.11 (Calculating joint probabilities-I) Define a joint pdf by

$$f \left( x , y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 6 x y ^ { 2 } } & { 0 < x < 1 \text { and } 0 < y < 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

(Henceforth, it will be understood that $f \left( x , y \right) = 0$ for $\left( x , y \right)$ values not specifically
mentioned in the definition.) First, we might check that $f \left( x , y \right)$ is indeed a joint pdf.
That $f \left( x , y \right) \geq 0$ for all $\left( x , y \right)$ in the defined range is fairly obvious. To compute the
integral of $f \left( x , y \right)$ over the whole plane, note that, since $f \left( x , y \right)$ is 0 except on the
unit square, the integral over the plane is the same as the integral over the square.
Thus we have

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d x d y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } 6 x y ^ { 2 } d x d y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } 3 x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } | _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } d y$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } 3 y ^ { 2 } d y = y ^ { 3 } | _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } = 1 .$$

Now, consider calculating a probability such as $P \left( X + Y \geq 1 \right) .$ Letting $A = \left( x , y \right) :$
$\left. x + y \geq 1 \right\} ,$ we can re-express this as $P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) .$ From Definition 4.1.10, to
calculate the probability we integrate the joint pdf over the set $A .$ But the joint pdf
is 0 except on the unit square. So integrating over $A$ is the same as integrating over
only that part of $A$ which is in the unit square. The set $A$ is $a$ half-plane in the
northeast part of the plane, and the part of $A$ in the unit square is the triangular
region bounded by the lines $x = 1 ,$ $y = 1 ,$ and $x + y = 1 .$ We can write

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="146" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.1" -->

$$A = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x + y \geq 1 , 0 < x < 1 , 0 < y < 1 \right\}$$
$$= \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x \geq 1 - y , 0 < x < 1 , 0 < y < 1 \right\}$$
$$= \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : 1 - y \leq x < 1 , 0 < y < 1 \right\} .$$

This gives us the limits of integration we need to calculate the probability. We have

$$P \left( X + Y \geq 1 \right) = \int _ { A } \int f \left( x , y \right) d x d y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 1 - y } ^ { 1 } 6 x y ^ { 2 } d x d y = \frac { 9 } { 1 0 } .$$

Using $\left( 4 . 1 . 3 \right) ,$ we can calculate the marginal pdf of $X$ or $Y .$ For example, to calculate
$f _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ we note that for $x \geq 1$ or $x \leq 0 ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) = 0$ for all values of y. Thus for $x \geq 1$
or $x \leq 0 ,$

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y = 0 .$$

For $0 < x < 1 ,$ $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero only if $0 < y < 1 .$ Thus for $0 < x <$ 1,

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } 6 x y ^ { 2 } d y = 2 x y ^ { 3 } | _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } = 2 x .$$

This marginal pdf of $X$ can now be used to calculate probabilities involving only $X .$
For example,

$$P \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } < X < \frac { 3 } { 4 } \right) = \int _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } 2 x d x = \frac { 5 } { 1 6 }$$

Example 4.1.12 (Calculating joint probabilities-II) As another example of a
joint pdf, let $f \left( x , y \right) = e ^ { - y } , 0 < x < y < \infty .$ Although $e ^ { - y }$ does not depend on $a ,$
$f \left( x , y \right)$ certainly is a function of $x$ since the set where $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero depends on
$x .$ This is made more obvious by using an indicator function to write

$$f \left( x , y \right) = e ^ { - y } I _ { \left\{ \left( u , v \right) : 0 < u < v < \infty \right\} } \left( x , y \right) .$$

To calculate $P \left( X + Y \geq 1 \right) ,$ we could integrate the joint pdf over the region that is the
intersection of the set $A = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x + y \geq 1 \right\}$ and the set where $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero.
Graph these sets and notice that this region is an unbounded region (lighter shading
in Figure 4.1.1) with three sides given by the lines $x = y , x + y = 1 ,$ and $x = 0 .$ To
integrate over this region we would have to break the region into at least two parts
in order to write the appropriate limits of integration.

The integration is easier over the intersection of the set $B = \left( x , y \right) : x + y < 1$
and the set where $f \left( x , y \right)$ is nonzero, the triangular region (darker shading in Figure
4.1.1) bounded by the lines $x = y ,$ $x + y = 1 ,$ and $x =$ 0. Thus

$$P \left( X + Y \geq 1 \right) = 1 - P \left( X + Y < 1 \right) = 1 - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \int _ { x } ^ { 1 - x } e ^ { - y } d y d x$$
$$= 1 - \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( e ^ { - x } - e ^ { - \left( 1 - x \right) } \right) d x = 2 e ^ { - 1 / 2 } - e ^ { - 1 } .$$

/

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="147" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS AND INDEPENDENCE" -->

<figcaption>Figure 4.1.1. Regions for Example 4.1.12</figcaption>

$x$

</figure>


This illustrates that it is almost always helpful to graph the sets of interest in deter-
mining the appropriate limits of integration for problems such as this.
Il

The joint probability distribution of $\left( X , Y \right)$ can be completely described with the
joint cdf (cumulative distribution function) rather than with the joint pmf or joint
pdf. The joint cdf is the function $F \left( x , y \right)$ defined by

$$F \left( x , y \right) = P \left( X \leq x , Y \leq y \right)$$

for all $\left( x , y \right) \in R ^ { 2 } .$ The joint cdf is usually not very handy to use for a discrete
random vector. But for a continuous bivariate random vector we have the important
relationship, as in the univariate case,

$$F \left( x , y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { x } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { y } f \left( s , t \right) d t d s .$$

From the bivariate Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, this implies that

(4.1.4)
$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F \left( x , y \right) } { \partial x \partial y } = f \left( x , y \right)$$

at continuity points of $f \left( x , y \right) .$ This relationship is useful in situations where an ex-
pression for $F \left( x , y \right)$ can be found. The mixed partial derivative can be computed to
find the joint pdf.


## 4.2 Conditional Distributions and Independence

Oftentimes when two random variables, $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ are observed, the values of the two
variables are related. For example, suppose that, in sampling from a human popu-
lation, $X$ denotes a person's height and $Y$ denotes the same person's weight. Surely
we would think it more likely that $Y > 2 0 0$ pounds if we were told that $X = 7 3$
inches than if we were told that $X = 4 1$ inches. Knowledge about the value of $X$
gives us some information about the value of $Y$ even if it does not tell us the value of
$Y$ exactly. Conditional probabilities regarding $Y$ given knowledge of the $X$ value can

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="148" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

be computed using the joint distribution of $\left( X , Y \right) .$ Sometimes, however, knowledge
about $X$ gives us no information about $Y .$ We will discuss these topics concerning
conditional probabilities in this section.

If $\left( X , Y \right)$ is a discrete random vector, then a conditional probability of the form
$P \left( Y = y | X = x \right)$ is interpreted exactly as in Definition 1.3.2. For a countable (maybe
finite) number of $x$ values, $P \left( X = x \right) > 0 .$ For these values of $x ,$ $P \left( Y = y | X = x \right)$ is
simply $P \left( X = x , Y = y \right) / P \left( X = x \right) ,$ according to the definition. The event $Y = y$
is the event $A$ in the formula and the event $X = x$ is the event $B .$ For a fixed value
of x, $P \left( Y = y | X = x \right)$ could be computed for all possible values of $y .$ In this way the
probability of various values of $y$ could be assessed given the knowledge that $X = x$
was observed. This computation can be simplified by noting that in terms of the joint
and marginal pmfs of $X$ and $Y ,$ the above probabilities are $P \left( X = x , Y = y \right) = f \left( x , y \right)$
and $P \left( X = x \right) = f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ This leads to the following definition.

Definition 4.2.1 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a discrete bivariate random vector with joint pmf
$f \left( x , y \right)$ and marginal pmfs $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ For any $x$ such that $P \left( X = x \right) = f _ { X } \left( x \right) >$
0, the conditional pmf of $Y$ given that $X = x$ is the function of $y$ denoted by $f \left( y | x \right)$
and defined by

$$f \left( y | x \right) = P \left( Y = y | X = x \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) }$$

For any $y$ such that $P \left( Y = y \right) = f _ { Y } \left( y \right) > 0 ,$ the conditional pmf of $X$ given that
$Y = y$ is the function of $x$ denoted by $f \left( x | y \right)$ and defined by

$$f \left( x | y \right) = P \left( X = x | Y = y \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { Y } \left( y \right) }$$

Since we have called $f \left( y | x \right)$ a pmf, we should verify that this function of $y$ does
indecd define a pmf for a random variable. First, $f \left( y | x \right) \geq 0$ for every $y$ since $f \left( x , y \right) \geq$
$0$ and $f _ { X } \left( x \right) > 0 .$ Second,

$$\sum _ { y } f \left( y | x \right) = \frac { \sum _ { y } f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) } = \frac { f _ { X } \left( x \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) } = 1$$

Thus, $f \left( y | x \right)$ is indeed a pmf and can be used in the usual way to compute probabilities
involving $Y$ given the knowledge that $X = x$ occurred.

Example 4.2.2 (Calculating conditional probabilities) Define the joint pmf
of $\left( X , Y \right)$ by

$$f \left( 0 , 1 0 \right) = f \left( 0 , 2 0 \right) = \frac { 2 } { 1 8 } , \quad f \left( 1 , 1 0 \right) = f \left( 1 , 3 0 \right) = \frac { 3 } { 1 8 } ,$$

f(1,20) =13, and $f \left( 2 , 3 0 \right) = \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } .$

We can use Definition 4.2.1 to compute the conditional pmf of $Y$ given $X$ for each of
the possible values of $X ,$ $x = 0 , 1 , 2 .$ First, the marginal pmf of $X$ is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="149" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS AND INDEPENDENCE" -->

$$f _ { X } \left( 0 \right) = f \left( 0 , 1 0 \right) + f \left( 0 , 2 0 \right) = \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } ,$$
$$f _ { X } \left( 1 \right) = f \left( 1 , 1 0 \right) + f \left( 1 , 2 0 \right) + f \left( 1 , 3 0 \right) = \frac { 1 0 } { 1 8 } ,$$
$$f _ { X } \left( 2 \right) = f \left( 2 , 3 0 \right) = \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } .$$

For $x = 0 ,$ $f \left( 0 , y \right)$ is positive only for $y = 1 0$ and $y = 2 0 .$ Thus $f \left( y | 0 \right)$ is positive only
for $y = 1 0$ and $y = 2 0 ,$ and

$$f \left( 1 0 | 0 \right) = \frac { f \left( 0 , 1 0 \right) } { f x \left( 0 \right) } = \frac { \frac { 2 } { 1 8 } } { \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$
$$f \left( 2 0 | 0 \right) = \frac { f \left( 0 , 2 0 \right) } { f _ { X } \left( 0 \right) } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$

That is, given the knowledge that $X = 0 ,$ the conditional probability distribution
for $Y$ is the discrete distribution that assigns probability $\frac { 1 } { 2 }$ to each of the two points
$y = 1 0$ and $y = 2 0 .$

For $x = 1 ,$ $f \left( y | 1 \right)$ is positive for $y = 1 0 , 2 0 ,$ and 30, and

$$f \left( 1 0 | 1 \right) = f \left( 3 0 | 1 \right) = \frac { \frac { 3 } { 1 8 } } { \frac { 1 0 } { 1 8 } } = \frac { 3 } { 1 0 } ,$$
$$f \left( 2 0 | 1 \right) = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } } { \frac { 1 0 } { 1 8 } } = \frac { 4 } { 1 0 } ,$$

and for $x = 2 ,$

$$f \left( 3 0 | 2 \right) = \frac { \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } } { \frac { 4 } { 1 8 } } = 1 .$$

The latter result reflects a fact that is also apparent from the joint pmf. $I f$ we know
that $X = 2 ,$ then we know that $Y$ must be 30.

Other conditional probabilities can be computed using these conditional pmfs. For
example,

$$P \left( Y > 1 0 | X = 1 \right) = f \left( 2 0 | 1 \right) + f \left( 3 0 | 1 \right) = \frac { 7 } { 1 0 }$$

or

$$P \left( Y > 1 0 | X = 0 \right) = f \left( 2 0 | 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$$

II

If $X$ and $Y$ are continuous random variables, then $P \left( X = x \right) = 0$ for every value of
$x .$ To compute a conditional probability such as $P \left( Y > 2 0 0 | X = 7 3 \right) ,$ Definition 1.3.2
cannot be used since the denominator, $P \left( X = 7 3 \right) ,$ is 0. Yet in actuality a value of $X$
is observed. If, to the limit of our measurement, we see $X = 7 3 ,$ this knowledge might
give us information about $Y$ (as the height and weight example at the beginning of
this section indicated). It turns out that the appropriate way to define a conditional
probability distribution for $Y$ given $X = x ,$ when $X$ and $Y$ are both contin ous, is
analogous to the discrete case with pdfs replacing pmfs (see Miscellanea 4.9.3).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="150" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

Definition 4.2.3 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a continuous bivariate random vector with joint
pdf $f \left( x , y \right)$ and marginal pdfs $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ For any $x$ such that $f _ { X } \left( x \right) > 0 ,$ the
conditional pdf of $Y$ given that $X = x$ is the function of $y$ denoted by $f \left( y | x \right)$ and
defined by

$$f \left( y | x \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) }$$

For any $y$ such that $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) > 0 ,$ the conditional pdf of $X$ given that $Y = y$ is the
function of $x$ denoted by $f \left( x | y \right)$ and defined by

$$f \left( x | y \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { Y } \left( y \right) }$$

To verify that $f \left( x | y \right)$ and $f \left( y | x \right)$ are indeed $\mathrm { p d f s } ,$ the same steps can be used as in
the earlier verification that Definition 4.2.1 had defined true pmfs with integrals now
replacing sums.

In addition to their usefulness for calculating probabilities, the conditional pdfs or
pmfs can also be used to calculate expected values. Just remember that $\left. f \left( y \right) x \right)$ as a
function of $y$ is a pdf or pmf and use it in the same way that we have previously used
unconditional pdfs or pmfs. If $g \left( Y \right)$ is a function of $Y ,$ then the conditional expected
value of $g \left( Y \right)$ given that $X = x$ is denoted by $E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right)$ and is given by

$$E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right) = \sum _ { y } g \left( y \right) f \left( y | x \right)$$
$$E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( y \right) f \left( y | x \right) d y$$
and

in the discrete and continuous cases, respectively. The conditional expected value has
all of the properties of the usual expected value listed in Theorem 2.2.5. Moreover,
$\mathrm { E } \left( Y | X \right)$ provides the best guess at $Y$ based on knowledge of $X ,$ extending the result
in Example 2.2.6. (See Exercise 4.13.)

Example 4.2.4 (Calculating conditional pdfs) As in Example 4.1.12, let the
continuous random vector $\left( X , Y \right)$ have joint pdf $f \left( x , y \right) = e ^ { - y } ,$ $0 < x < y < \infty .$
Suppose we wish to compute the conditional pdf of $Y$ given $X =$ x. The marginal pdf
of $X$ is computed as follows. If $x \leq 0 ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) = 0$ for all values of $y ,$ so $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 0 .$ If
$x > 0 ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) > 0$ only if $y > x .$ Thus

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d y = \int _ { x } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - y } d y = e ^ { - x } .$$

Thus, marginally, $X$ has an exponential distribution. From Definition 4.2.3, the con-
ditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ can be computed for any $x > 0$ (since these
are the values for which $f _ { X } \left( x \right) > 0 .$ For any such x,

$$f \left( y | x \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) } = \frac { e ^ { - y } } { e ^ { - x } } = e ^ { - \left( y - x \right) } , \quad \text { if } y > x ,$$

and

$$f \left( y | x \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) } = \frac { 0 } { e ^ { - x } } = 0 , \quad \text { if } y \leq x .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="151" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS AND INDEPENDENCE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

Thus, given $X = x ,$ $Y$ has an exponential distribution, where $x$ is the location pa-
rameter in the distribution of $Y$ and $\beta = 1$ is the scale parameter. The conditional
distribution of $Y$ is different for every value of $x .$ It then follows that

$$E \left( Y | X = x \right) = \int _ { x } ^ { \infty } y e ^ { - \left( y - x \right) } d y = 1 + x .$$

The variance of the probability distribution described by $f \left( y | x \right)$ is called the con-
ditional variance of $Y$ given $X = x .$ Using the notation $\mathrm { V a r } \left( Y | x \right)$ for this, we have,
using the ordinary definition of variance,

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( Y | x \right) = E \left( Y ^ { 2 } | x \right) - \left( E \left( Y | x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Applying this definition to our example, we obtain

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( Y | x \right) = \int _ { x } ^ { \infty } y ^ { 2 } e ^ { - \left( y - x \right) } d y - \left( \int _ { x } ^ { \infty } y e ^ { - \left( y - x \right) } d y \right) ^ { 2 } = 1 .$$

In this case the conditional variance of $Y$ given $X = x$ is the same for all values
of $x .$ In other situations, however, it may be different for different values of x. This
conditional variance might be compared to the unconditional variance of $Y .$ The
marginal distribution of $Y$ is $g a m m a \left( 2 , 1 \right) ,$ which has Var $Y = 2 .$ Given the knowledge
that $X = x ,$ the variability in $Y$ is considerably reduced.
Il

A physical situation for which the model in Example 4.2.4 might be used is this.
Suppose we have two light bulbs. The lengths of time each will burn are random
variables denoted by $X$ and $Z .$ The lifelengths $X$ and $Z$ are independent and both
have pdf $e ^ { - x } ,$ $x > 0 .$ The first bulb will be turned on. As soon as it burns out, the
second bulb will be turned on. Now consider observing $X ,$ the time when the first
bulb burns out, and $Y = X + Z ,$ the time when the second bulb burns out. Given that
$X = x$ is when the first burned out and the second is started, $Y = Z + x .$ This is like
Example 3.5.3. The value $x$ is acting as a location parameter, and the pdf of $Y ,$ in this
case the conditional pdf of $Y$ given $X = x ,$ is $f \left( y | x \right) = f _ { Z } \left( y - x \right) = e ^ { - \left( y - x \right) } , y > x .$

The conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ is possibly a different probability
distribution for each value of x. Thus we really have a family of probability distribu-
tions for $Y ,$ one for each $x .$ When we wish to describe this entire family, we will use the
phrase "the distribution of $Y | X .$ If, for example, $X$ is a positive integer-valued ran-
dom variable and the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ is binomial(x, $\left. p \right) ,$ then
we might say the distribution of $Y | X$ is binomial(X, $\left. p \right)$ or write $Y | X \sim b i n o m i a l \left( X , p \right) .$
Whenever we use the symbol $Y | X$ or have a random variable as the parameter of a
probability distribution, we are describing the family of conditional probability dis-
tributions. Joint pdfs or pmfs are sometimes defined by specifying the conditional
$\left. f \left( y \right) x \right)$ and the marginal $f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ Then the definition yields $f \left( x , y \right) = f \left( y | x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$
These types of models are discussed more in Section 4.4.

Notice also that $E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right)$ is a function of x. That $\mathrm { i s } ,$ for each value of $x ,$ $E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right)$
is a real number obtained by computing the appropriate integral or sum. Thus,
$E \left( g \left( Y \right) | X \right)$ is a random variable whose value depends on the value of $X .$ If $X = x ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="152" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

the value of the random variable $E \left( g \left( Y \right) | X \right)$ is $E \left( g \left( Y \right) | x \right) .$ Thus, in Example 4.2.4,
we can write $E \left( Y | X \right) = 1 + X .$

In all the previous examples, the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ was
different for different values of $x .$ In some situations, the knowledge that $X = x$ does
not give us any more information about $Y$ than what we already had. This important
relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is called independence. Just as with independent events
in Chapter 1, it is more convenient to define independence in a symmetric fashion
and then derive conditional properties like those we just mentioned. This we now do.

Definition 4.2.5 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a bivariate random vector with joint pdf or pmf
$f \left( x , y \right)$ and marginal pdfs or pmfs $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ Then $X$ and $Y$ are called inde-
pendent random variables if, for every $x \in \mathfrak{R}$ and $y \in \mathfrak{R} ,$

(4.2.1)
$$f \left( x , y \right) = f _ { X } \left( x \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$$

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, the conditional pdf of $Y$ given $X = x$ is

$$f \left( y | x \right) = \frac { f \left( x , y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) }$$
$$= \frac { f _ { X } \left( x \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) } { f _ { X } \left( x \right) }$$
$$= f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$$

(definition)

(from (4.2.1)

regardless of the value of x. Thus, for any $A \subset \mathfrak{R}$ and $x \in \mathfrak{R} ,$ $P \left( Y \in A | x \right) =$
$\int _ { A } f \left( y | x \right) d y = \int _ { A } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y = P \left( Y \in A \right) .$ The knowledge that $X = x$ gives us $n o$
additional information about $Y .$

Definition 4.2.5 is used in two different ways. We might start with a joint $\mathrm { p d f } \mathrm { o r }$
pmf and then check whether $X$ and $Y$ are independent. To do this we must verify
that (4.2.1) is true for every value of $x$ and $y .$ Or we might wish to define a model
in which $X$ and $Y$ are independent. Consideration of what $X$ and $Y$ represent might
indicate that knowledge that $X = x$ should give us no information about $Y .$ In this
case we could specify the marginal distributions of $X$ and $Y$ and then define the $\mathrm { j o i n t }$
distribution as the product given in (4.2.1).

Example 4.2.6 (Checking independence-I) Consider the discrete bivariate ran-
dom vector $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ with joint pmf given by

$$f \left( 1 0 , 1 \right) = f \left( 2 0 , 1 \right) = f \left( 2 0 , 2 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } ,$$
$$f \left( 1 0 , 2 \right) = f \left( 1 0 , 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 5 } , \quad \text { and } \quad f \left( 2 0 , 3 \right) = \frac { 3 } { 1 0 } .$$

The marginal pmfs are easily calculated to be

$$f _ { X } \left( 1 0 \right) = f _ { X } \left( 2 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$
$$f \left( 1 0 , 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 5 } \neq \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 2 } = f _ { X } \left( 1 0 \right) f _ { Y } \left( 3 \right) .$$
and fy(1) = 5, fx(2) = 10, and $f _ { Y } \left( 3 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$
1
3
The random variables $X$ and $Y$ are not independent because (4.2.1) is not true for
every $x$ and $y .$ For example,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="153" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS AND INDEPENDENCE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

The relationship (4.2.1) must hold for every choice of $x$ and $y$ if $X$ and $Y$ are to be
independent. Note that $f \left( 1 0 , 1 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 5 } = f _ { X } \left( 1 0 \right) f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) .$ That $\left( 4 . 2 . 1 \right)$ holds for
some values of $x$ and $y$ does not ensure that $X$ and $Y$ are independent. All values
must be checked.
II

The verification that $X$ and $Y$ are independent by direct use of (4.2.1) would
require the knowledge of $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ The following lemma makes the verification
somewhat easier.

Lemma 4.2.7 Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a bivariate random vector with joint pdf or pmf $f \left( x , y \right) .$
Then $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables $i f$ and only if there exist functions
$g \left( x \right)$ and $h \left( y \right)$ such $t h a t ,$ for every $x \in \mathfrak{R}$ and $y \in \mathfrak{R} ,$

$$f \left( x , y \right) = g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) .$$

Proof: The "only if" part is proved by defining $g \left( x \right) = f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $h \left( y \right) = f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ and
using (4.2.1). To prove the "if" part for continuous random variables, suppose that
$f \left( x , y \right) = g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) .$ Define

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) d x = c$$
$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( y \right) d y = d ,$$
and

where the constants $c$ and $d$ satisfy

$$c d = \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) d x \right) \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( y \right) d y \right)$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$
$$= 1 .$$

(4.2.2)

$\left( f \left( x , y \right) \right.$ is a joint pdf)

Furthermore, the marginal pdfs are given by

(4.2.3)
$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) d y = g \left( x \right) d \quad \text { and } \quad f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) d x = h \left( y \right) c .$$

Thus, using (4.2.2) and (4.2.3), we have

$$f \left( x , y \right) = g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) = g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) c d = f _ { X } \left( x \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$$

showing that $X$ and $Y$ are independent. Replacing integrals with sums proves the
lemma for discrete random vectors.
☐

Example 4.2.8 (Checking independence-II) Consider the joint pdf $f \left( x , y \right) =$
$\frac { 1 } { 3 8 4 } x ^ { 2 } y ^ { 4 } e ^ { - y - \left( x / 2 \right) } ,$ $x > 0$ and $y > 0 .$ If we define

$$g \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { x ^ { 2 } e ^ { - x / 2 } } & { x > 0 } \\ { 0 } & { x \leq 0 } \end{array} \right.$$
$$h \left( y \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { y ^ { 4 } e ^ { - y } / 3 8 4 } & { y > 0 } \\ { 0 } & { y \leq 0 } \end{array} \right.$$
and

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="154" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

then $f \left( x , y \right) = g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right)$ for all $x \in \mathfrak{R}$ and all $y \in \mathfrak{R} .$ By Lemma 4.2.7, we conclude that
$X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables. We do not have to compute marginal
pdfs.

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables, then from $\left( 4 . 2 . 1 \right)$ it is clear that
$f \left( x , y \right) > 0$ on the set $\left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x \in A \right.$ and $\left. y \in B \right\} ,$ where $A = \left\{ x : f _ { X } \left( x \right) > 0 \right\}$
and $B = \left\{ y : f _ { Y } \left( y \right) > 0 \right\} .$ A set of this form is called a cross-product and is usually
denoted by $A \times B .$ Membership in a cross-product can be checked by considering the $x$
$\mathrm { a n d } y$ values separately. If $f \left( x , y \right)$ is a joint pdf or pmf and the set where $f \left( x , y \right) > 0$
is not a cross-product, then the random variables $X$ and $Y$ with joint pdf or pmf
$f \left( x , y \right)$ are not independent. In Example 4.2.4, the set $0 < x < y < \infty$ is not a cross-
product. To check membership in this set we must check that not only $0 < x < \infty$
and $0 < y < \infty$ but also $x < y .$ Thus the random variables in Example 4.2.4 are not
independent. Example 4.2.2 gives an example of a joint pmf that is positive on a set
that is not a cross-product.

Example 4.2.9 (Joint probability model) As an example of using independence
to define a joint probability model, consider this situation. A student from an ele-
mentary school in Kansas City is randomly selected and $X = t h e$ number of living
parents of the student is recorded. Suppose the marginal distribution of $X$ is

fx(0) =. 01, fx(1) =. 09, and
$$f _ { X } \left( 2 \right) = . 9 0 .$$

A retiree from Sun City is randomly selected and $Y = t h e \quad n u m b e r$ of living parents
of the retiree is recorded. Suppose the marginal distribution of $Y$ is

fy(0) =. 70, fy(1) =. 25, and
$$f _ { Y } \left( 2 \right) = . 0 5 .$$

It seems reasonable to assume that these two random variables are independent.
Knowledge of the number of parents of the student tells us nothing about the number
of parents of the retiree. The only joint distribution of $X$ and $Y$ that reflects this
independence is the one defined by (4.2.1). Thus, for example,

$$f \left( 0 , 0 \right) = f _ { X } \left( 0 \right) f _ { Y } \left( 0 \right) = . 0 0 7 0$$
$$f \left( 0 , 1 \right) = f _ { X } \left( 0 \right) f _ { Y } \left( 1 \right) = . 0 0 2 5 .$$
and

This joint distribution can be used to calculate quantities such as

$$P \left( X = Y \right) = f \left( 0 , 0 \right) + f \left( 1 , 1 \right) + f \left( 2 , 2 \right)$$
$$= \left( . 0 1 \right) \left( . 7 0 \right) + \left( . 0 9 \right) \left( . 2 5 \right) + \left( . 9 0 \right) \left( . 0 5 \right) = . 0 7 4 5 .$$

It

Certain probabilities and expectations are easy to calculate if $X$ and $Y$ are inde-
pendent, as the next theorem indicates.

Theorem 4.2.10 Let $X$ and $Y$ be independent random variables.

a. For any $A \subset \mathfrak{R}$ and $B \subset \mathfrak{R}$ , $P \left( X \in A , Y \in B \right) = P \left( X \in A \right) P \left( Y \in B \right)$ that is,
the events $\left\{ X \in A \right\}$ and $\left\{ Y \in B \right\}$ are independent events.

!!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="155" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL DISTRIBUTIONS AND INDEPENDENCE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.2" -->

b. 'Let $g \left( x \right)$ be a function only of $x$ and $h \left( y \right)$ be a function only of $y .$ Then

$$E \left( g \left( X \right) h \left( Y \right) \right) = \left( \mathrm { E g } \left( X \right) \right) \left( E h \left( Y \right) \right) .$$

Proof: For continuous random variables, part (b) is proved by noting that

$$E \left( g \left( X \right) h \left( Y \right) \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) f \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( y \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x d y$$
$$= \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( x \right) f _ { X } \left( x \right) d x \right) \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } h \left( y \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y \right)$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { E } g \left( X \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { E } h \left( Y \right) \right) .$$

(by (4.2.1))

The result for discrete random variables is proved by replacing integrals by sums.
Part (a) can be proved by a series of steps similar to those above or by the following
argument. Let $g \left( x \right)$ be the indicator function of the set $A .$ Let $h \left( y \right)$ be the indicator
function of the set $B .$ Note that $g \left( x \right) h \left( y \right)$ is the indicator function of the set $C \subset \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$
defined by $C = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x \in A , y \in B \right\} .$ Also note that for an indicator function such
as $g \left( x \right) ,$ $\mathrm { E g } \left( X \right) = P \left( X \in A \right) .$ Thus using the expectation equality just proved, we
have

$$P \left( X \in A , Y \in B \right) = P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in C \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( g \left( X \right) h \left( Y \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { E } g \left( X \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { E } h \left( Y \right) \right) = P \left( X \in A \right) P \left( Y \in B \right) .$$

☐

Example 4.2.11 (Expectations of independent variables) Let $X$ and $Y$ be
independent exponential(1) random variables. From Theorem 4.2.10 we have

$$P \left( X \geq 4 , Y < 3 \right) = P \left( X \geq 4 \right) P \left( Y < 3 \right) = e ^ { - 4 } \left( 1 - e ^ { - 3 } \right) .$$

Letting $g \left( x \right) = x ^ { 2 }$ and $h \left( y \right) = y ,$ we see that

$$\mathrm { E } \left( X ^ { 2 } Y \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } X ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Y \right) = \left( \mathrm { V a r } X + \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \mathrm { E } Y = \left( 1 + 1 ^ { 2 } \right) 1 = 2 .$$

||

The following result concerning sums of independent random variables is a simple
consequence of Theorem 4.2.10.

Theorem 4.2.12 Let $X$ and $Y$ be independent random variables with moment gen-
erating functions $M _ { X } \left( t \right)$ and $M _ { Y } \left( t \right) .$ Then the moment generating function of the
random variable $Z = X + Y$ is given $b y$

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = M _ { X } \left( t \right) M _ { Y } \left( t \right) .$$

Proof: Using the definition of the mgf and Theorem 4.2.10, we have

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = \mathrm { E } e ^ { t Z } = \mathrm { E } e ^ { t \left( X + Y \right) } = \mathrm { E } \left( e ^ { t X } e ^ { t Y } \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } e ^ { t X } \right) \left( \mathrm { E } e ^ { t Y } \right) = M _ { X } \left( t \right) M _ { Y } \left( t \right) .$$

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="156" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

Example 4.2.13 (Mgf of a sum of normal variables) Sometimes Theorem
4.2.12 can be used to casily derive the distribution of $Z$ from knowledge of the distri-
bution of $X$ and $Y .$ For example, let $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and $Y \sim n \left( \gamma , \tau ^ { 2 } \right)$ be independent
normal random variables. From Exercise 2.33, the mgfs of $X$ and $Y$ are

$$M _ { X } \left( t \right) = \exp \left( \mu t + \sigma ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) \quad \text { and } \quad M _ { Y } \left( t \right) = \exp \left( \gamma t + \tau ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) .$$

Thus, from Theorem 4.2.12, the mgf of $Z = X + Y$ is

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = M _ { X } \left( t \right) M _ { Y } \left( t \right) = \exp \left( \left( \mu + \gamma \right) t + \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } + \tau ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) .$$

This is the mgf of a normal random variable with mean $\mu + \gamma$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } + \tau ^ { 2 } .$
This result is important enough to be stated as a theorem.

Theorem 4.2.14 Let $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and $Y \sim n \left( \gamma , \tau ^ { 2 } \right)$ be independent normal random
variables. Then the random variable $Z = X + Y$ has $n \left( \mu + \gamma , \sigma ^ { 2 } + \tau ^ { 2 } \right)$ distribution.

If $f \left( x , y \right)$ is the joint pdf for the continuous random vector $\left( X , Y \right) ,$ (4.2.1) may fail
to hold on a set $A$ of $\left( x , y \right)$ values for which $\int _ { A } \int d x d y = 0 .$ In such a case $X$ and $Y$
are still called independent random variables. This reflects the fact that two pdfs that
differ only on a set such as $A$ define the same probability distribution for $\left( X , Y \right) .$ To
see this, suppose $f \left( x , y \right)$ and $f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right)$ are two pdfs that are equal everywhere except
on a set $A$ for which $\int _ { A } \int d x d y = 0 .$ Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ have pdf $f \left( x , y \right) ,$ let $\left( X ^ { * } , Y ^ { * } \right)$ have
pdf $f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right) ,$ and let $B$ be any subset of $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ Then

$$P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in B \right) = \int _ { B } \int f \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { B \cap A ^ { c } } \int f \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { B \cap A ^ { c } } \int f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right) d x d y$$
$$= \int _ { B } \int f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right) d x d y = P \left( \left( X ^ { * } , Y ^ { * } \right) \in B \right) .$$

Thus $\left( X , Y \right)$ and $\left( X ^ { * } , Y ^ { * } \right)$ have the same probability distribution. So, for example,
$f \left( x , y \right) = e ^ { - x - y } ,$ $x > 0$ and $y > 0 ,$ is a pdf for two independent exponential random
variables and satisfies (4.2.1). But, $f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right) ,$ which is equal to $f \left( x , y \right)$ except that
$f ^ { * } \left( x , y \right) = 0$ if $x = y ,$ is also the pdf for two independent exponential random variables
even though $\left( 4 . 2 . 1 \right)$ is not true on the set $A = \left( x , x \right) : x > 0 .$


## 4.3 Bivariate Transformations

In Section 2.1, methods of finding the distribution of a function of a random variable
were discussed. In this section we extend these ideas to the case of bivariate random
vectors.

Let $\left( X , Y \right)$ be a bivariate random vector with a known probability distribution.
Now consider a new bivariate random vector $\left( U , V \right)$ defined by $U = g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right)$ and $V =$

!!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="157" -->
<!-- PageHeader="BIVARIATE TRANSFORMATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

$g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right) ,$ where $g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right)$ and $g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$ are some specified functions. If $B$ is any subset of
$1 2 ,$ then $\left( U , V \right) \in B$ and only if $\left( X , Y \right) \in A ,$ where $A = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : \left( g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) , g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) \right) \in \right.$
☒
$B .$ Thus $P \left( \left( U , V \right) \in B \right) = P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A \right) ,$ and the probability distribution of $\left( U , V \right)$
☒
is completely determined by the probability distribution of $\left( X , Y \right) .$

If $\left( X , Y \right)$ is a discrete bivariate random vector, then there is only a countable set
of values for which the joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ is positive. Call this set $A .$ Define the
☒
set $\mathcal{B} = \left\{ \left( u , v \right) : u = g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) \right.$ and $v = g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$ for some $\left. \left( x , y \right) \in A \right\} .$ Then $\mathcal{B}$ is the
countable set of possible values for the discrete random vector $\left( U , V \right) .$ And if, for any
$\left( u , v \right) \in \mathcal{B} ,$ $A _ { u v }$ is defined to be $\left\{ \left( x , y \right) \in A : g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) = u \right.$ and $\left. g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) = v \right\} ,$ then the
joint pmf of $\left( U , V \right) ,$ $f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) ,$ can be computed from the joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ by

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = P \left( U = u , V = v \right) = P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A _ { u v } \right) = \sum _ { \left( x , v \right) \in A _ { u v } } f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) .$$
(4.3.1)

Example 4.3.1 (Distribution of the sum of Poisson variables) Let $X$ and
$Y$ be independent Poisson random variables with parameters $\theta$ and $\lambda ,$ respectively.
Thus the joint pmf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ is
☒

$$f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) = \frac { \theta ^ { x } e ^ { - \theta } } { x ! } \frac { \lambda ^ { y } e ^ { - \lambda } } { y ! } , \quad x = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots , y = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots .$$

The set $\mathcal{A}$ is $\left\{ \left( x , y \right) : x = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots \right.$ and $\left. y = 0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots \right\} .$ Now define $U = X + Y$
and $V = Y .$ That is, $g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) = x + y$ and $g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) = y .$ We will describe the set
$\mathcal{B} ,$ the set of possible $\left( u , v \right)$ values. The possible values for $v$ are the nonnegative
integers. The variable $v = y$ and thus has the same set of possible values. For a
given value of v, $u = x + y = x + v$ must be an integer greater than or equal to $v$
since $x$ is a nonnegative integer. The set of all possible $\left( u , v \right)$ values is thus given by
$\mathcal{B} = \left\{ \left( u , v \right) : v = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots \right.$ and $u = v ,$ v + 1, $\left. v + 2 , \ldots \right\} .$ For any $\left( u , v \right) \in \mathcal{B} ,$ the only
$\left( x , y \right)$ value satisfying $x + y = u$ and $y = v$ is $x = u \quad - \quad v$ and $y =$ v. Thus, in this
example, $A _ { u v }$ always consists of only the single point $\left( u \quad - \quad v , v \right) .$ From (4.3.1) we thus
obtain the joint pmf of $\left( U , V \right)$ as

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = f _ { X , Y } \left( u - v , v \right) = \frac { \theta ^ { u - v } e ^ { - \theta } } { \left( u - v \right) ! } \frac { \lambda ^ { v } e ^ { - \lambda } } { v ! } , \quad \begin{array}{} v = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots , \\ u = v , v + 1 , v + 2 , \ldots . \end{array} .$$

In this example it is interesting to compute the marginal pmf of $U .$ For any fixed
nonnegative integer $u ,$ $f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) > 0$ only for $v = 0 , 1 , \ldots , u .$ This gives the set of $v$
values to sum over to obtain the marginal pmf of $U .$ It is

$$f _ { U } \left( u \right) = \sum _ { v = 0 } ^ { u } \frac { \theta ^ { u - v } e ^ { - \theta } } { \left( u - v \right) ! } \frac { \lambda ^ { v } e ^ { - \lambda } } { v ! } = e ^ { - \left( \theta + \lambda \right) } \sum _ { v = 0 } ^ { u } \frac { \theta ^ { u - v } } { \left( u - v \right) ! } \frac { \lambda ^ { v } } { v ! } , \quad u = 0 , 1 , 2 ,$$
....

This can be simplified by noting that, if we multiply and divide each term by $u ! ,$ we
can use the Binomial Theorem to obtain

$$f _ { U } \left( u \right) = \frac { e ^ { - \left( \theta + \lambda \right) } } { u ! } \sum _ { v = 0 } ^ { u } \binom { u } { v } \lambda ^ { v } \theta ^ { u - v } = \frac { e ^ { - \left( \theta + \lambda \right) } } { u ! } \left( \theta + \lambda \right) ^ { u } , \quad u = 0 , 1 , 2 ,$$
....

This is the pmf of a Poisson random variable with parameter $\theta + \lambda .$ This result is
significant enough to be stated as a theorem.
Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="158" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

Theorem 4.3.2 If $X \sim \text { Poisson } \left( \theta \right)$ and $Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ and $X$ and $Y$ are indepen-
dent, then $X + Y \sim \text { Poisson } \left( \theta + \lambda \right) .$

If $\left( X , Y \right)$ is a continuous random vector with joint pdf $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) ,$ then the joint pdf
of $\left( U , V \right)$ can be expressed in terms of $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$ in a manner analogous to (2.1.8). As
before, $\mathcal{A} = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) > 0 \right\}$ and $\mathcal{B} = \left\{ \left( u , v \right) : u = g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) \right.$ and $v = g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$
for some $\left. \left( x , y \right) \in A \right\} .$ The joint pdf $f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right)$ will be positive on the set $\mathcal{B} .$ For the
simplest version of this result we assume that the transformation $u = g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right)$ and
$v = g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$ defines a one-to-one transformation of $A$ onto $\mathcal{B} .$ The transformation is
onto because of the definition of $\mathcal{B} .$ We are assuming that for each $\left( u , v \right) \in \mathcal{B}$ there is
only one $\left( x , y \right) \in A$ such that $\left( u , v \right) = \left( g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) , g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) \right) .$ For such a one-to-one, onto
transformation, we can solve the equations $u = g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right)$ and $v = g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right)$ for $x \quad a n d \quad y$
in terms of $u$ and v. We will denote this inverse transformation by $x = h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right)$ and
$y = h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) .$ The role played by a derivative in the univariate case is now played by
a quantity called the Jacobian of the transformation. This function of $\left( u , v \right) ,$ denoted
by $J ,$ is the determinant of a matrix of partial derivatives. It is defined by

$$J = | \begin{array}{} { \frac { \partial x } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial x } { \partial v } } \\ { \frac { \partial y } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial y } { \partial v } } \end{array} | = \frac { \partial x } { \partial u } \frac { \partial y } { \partial v } - \frac { \partial y } { \partial u } \frac { \partial x } { \partial v }$$

where

$$\frac { \partial x } { \partial u } = \frac { \partial h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) } { \partial u } , \quad \frac { \partial x } { \partial v } = \frac { \partial h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) } { \partial v } , \quad \frac { \partial y } { \partial u } = \frac { \partial h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) } { \partial u } , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \frac { \partial y } { \partial v } = \frac { \partial h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) } { \partial v } .$$
.

We assume that $J$ is not identically $0$ on $\mathcal{B} .$ Then the joint pdf of $\left( U , V \right)$ is 0 outside
the set $\mathcal{B}$ and on the set $\mathcal{B}$ is given by

(4.3.2)
$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = f _ { X , Y } \left( h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) , h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) \right) | J | ,$$

where $| J |$ is the absolute value of $J .$ When we use (4.3.2), it is sometimes just as
☒
difficult to determine the set $\mathcal{B}$ and verify that the transformation is one-to-one as
it is to substitute into formula (4.3.2). Note these parts of the explanations in the
following examples.

Example 4.3.3 (Distribution of the product of beta variables) Let $X$ ~
$\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ and $Y \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha + \beta , \gamma \right)$ be independent random variables. The joint pdf
of $\left( X , Y \right)$ is

$$f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) = \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - x \right) ^ { \beta - 1 } \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } y ^ { \alpha + \beta - 1 } \left( 1 - y \right) ^ { \gamma - 1 } ,$$
$$0 < < < 1 , 0 < y < 1 .$$

Consider the transformation $U = X Y$ and $V = X .$ The set of possible values for $V$
is $0 < v < 1$ since $V = X .$ For a fixed value of $V = v ,$ $U$ must be between 0 and $v$
since $X = V = v$ and $Y$ is between 0 and 1. Thus, this transformation maps the set

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="159" -->
<!-- PageHeader="BIVARIATE TRANSFORMATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

A onto the set $\mathcal{B} = \left\{ \left( u , v \right) : 0 < u < v < 1 \right\} .$ For any $\left( u , v \right) \in \mathcal{B} ,$ the equations $u = x y$
and $v = x$ can be uniquely solved for $x = h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) = v$ and $y = h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) = u / v .$ Note
that if considered as a transformation defined on all of $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } ,$ this transformation is not
one-to-one. Any point $\left( 0 , y \right)$ is mapped into the point $\left( 0 , 0 \right) .$ But as a function defined
only on $\mathcal{A} ,$ it is a one-to-one transformation onto $\mathcal{B} .$ The Jacobian is given by

$$J = | \begin{array}{} { \frac { \partial x } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial x } { \partial v } } \\ { \frac { \partial y } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial y } { \partial v } } \end{array} | = | \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { \frac { 1 } { v } } & { - \frac { u } { v } } \end{array} | = - \frac { 1 } { v }$$

Thus, from (4.3.2) we obtain the joint pdf as

(4.3.3)
$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } v ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - v \right) ^ { \beta - 1 } \left( \frac { u } { v } \right) ^ { \alpha + \beta - 1 } \left( 1 - \frac { u } { v } \right) ^ { \gamma - 1 } \frac { 1 } { v } ,$$
$$0 < u < v < 1 .$$

The marginal distribution of $V = X$ is, of course, $a$ $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution. But the
distribution of $U$ is also a beta distribution:

$$f _ { U } \left( u \right) = \int _ { u } ^ { 1 } f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) d v$$
$$= \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } u ^ { \alpha - 1 } \int _ { u } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { u } { v } - u \right) ^ { \beta - 1 } \left( 1 - \frac { u } { v } \right) ^ { \gamma - 1 } \left( \frac { u } { v ^ { 2 } } \right) d v .$$

The expression (4.3.3) was used but some terms have been rearranged. Now make the
univariate change of variable $y = \left( u / v \quad - \quad u \right) / \left( 1 \quad - \quad u \right)$ so that $d y = - u / \left[ v ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - u \right) \right] d v$ to
obtain

$$f _ { U } \left( u \right) = \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } u ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - u \right) ^ { \beta + \gamma - 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } y ^ { \beta - 1 } \left( 1 - y \right) ^ { \gamma - 1 } d y$$
$$= \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } u ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - u \right) ^ { \beta + \gamma - 1 } \frac { \Gamma \left( \beta \right) \Gamma \left( \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \beta + \gamma \right) }$$
$$= \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + \gamma \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta + \gamma \right) } u ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - u \right) ^ { \beta + \gamma - 1 } , \quad 0 < u < 1 .$$

To obtain the second identity we recognized the integrand as the kernel of a beta
pdf and used (3.3.17). Thus we see that the marginal distribution of $U$ is $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta + \gamma \right) .$
11

Example 4.3.4 (Sum and difference of normal variables) Let $X$ and $Y$ be
independent, standard normal random variables. Consider the transformation $U =$
$X + Y$ and $V = X - Y$ . In the notation used above, $U = g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right)$ where $g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) = x + y$
and $V = g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right)$ where $g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) = x - y .$ The joint pdf of $X$ and $Y$ is, of course,
$f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) = \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { - 1 } \exp \left( - x ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) \exp \left( - y ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) , - \infty < x < \circ$ 0, -> < y < x. So the set

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="160" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

$\mathcal{A} = \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ To determine the set $\mathcal{B}$ on which $f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right)$ is positive, we must determine
all the values that

(4.3.4)
$$u = x + y \quad a n d \quad v = x - y$$

take on as $\left( x , y \right)$ range over the set $\mathcal{A} = \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ But we can set $u$ to be any number and
$v$ to be any number and uniquely solve equations $\left( 4 . 3 . 4 \right)$ for $x$ and $y$ to obtain

(4.3.5)
$$x = h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { u + v } { 2 } \quad \text { and } \quad y = h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { u - v } { 2 } .$$

This shows two things. For any $\left( u , v \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ there is an $\left( x , y \right) \in A$ (defined by (4.3.5)
such that $u = x + y$ and $v = x \quad - \quad y .$ So $\mathcal{B} ,$ the set of all possible $\left( u , v \right)$ values, is $9 Q ^ { 2 } .$
Since the solution $\left( 4 . 3 . 5 \right)$ is unique, this also shows that the transformation we have
considered is one-to-one. Only the $\left( x , y \right)$ given in (4.3.5) will yield $u = x + y$ and
$v = x \quad - y .$ From (4.3.5) the partial derivatives of $x$ and $y$ are easy to compute. We
obtain

$$J = | \begin{array}{} { \frac { \partial x } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial x } { \partial v } } \\ { \frac { \partial y } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial y } { \partial v } } \end{array} | = | \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } \end{array} |$$

Substituting the expressions (4.3.5) for $x$ and $y$ into $f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right)$ and using $| J | = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ we
obtain the joint pdf of $\left( U , V \right)$ from (4.3.2) as

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = f _ { X , Y } \left( h _ { 1 } \left( u , v \right) , h _ { 2 } \left( u , v \right) \right) | J | = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } e ^ { - \left( \left( u + v \right) / 2 \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - \left( \left( u - v \right) / 2 \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$

for $- \infty < u < \infty$ and $- \infty < v < \infty .$ Multiplying out the squares in the exponen-
tials, we see that the terms involving uv cancel. Thus after some simplification and
rearrangement we obtain

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sqrt { 2 } } e ^ { - u ^ { 2 } / 4 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sqrt { 2 } } e ^ { - v ^ { 2 } / 4 } \right) .$$

The joint pdf has factored into a function of $u$ and a function of $v$ v. By Lemma
4.2.7, U and $V$ are independent. From Theorem 4.2.14, the marginal distribution of
$U = X + Y$ is $n \left( 0 , 2 \right) .$ Similarly, Theorem 4.2.12 could be used to find that the marginal
distribution of $V$ is also $n \left( 0 , 2 \right) .$ This important fact, that sums and differences of
independent normal random variables are independent normal random variables, is
true regardless of the means of $X$ and $Y ,$ so long as $\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { V a r } Y .$ This result is
left as Exercise 4.27. Theorems 4.2.12 and 4.2.14 give us the marginal distributions
of $U$ and $V .$ But the more involved analysis here is required to determine that $U$ and
$V$ are independent.
II

In Example 4.3.4, we found that $U$ and $V$ are independent random variables. There
is a much simpler, but very important, situation in which new variables $U$ and $V ,$ de-
fined in terms of original variables $X$ and $Y ,$ are independent. Theorem 4.3.5 describes
this.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="161" -->
<!-- PageHeader="BIVARIATE TRANSFORMATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.3" -->

Theorem 4.3.5 Let $X$ and $Y$ be independent random variables. Let $g \left( x \right)$ be a func-
tion only $o f \quad x$ and $h \left( y \right)$ be a function only of y. Then the random variables $U = g \left( X \right)$
and $V = h \left( Y \right)$ are independent.

Proof: $\mathrm { W e }$ will prove the theorem assuming $U$ and $V$ are continuous random vari-
ables. For any $u \in \mathfrak{R}$ and $v \in \mathfrak{R}$ , define

and
$$F _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = P \left( U \leq u , V \leq v \right)$$
$$B _ { v } = \left\{ y : h \left( y \right) \leq v \right\} .$$
$$A _ { u } = \left\{ x : g \left( x \right) \leq u \right\}$$

Then the joint cdf of $\left( U , V \right)$ is

(definition of cdf)

$$\left. = P \left( X \in A _ { u } , Y \in B _ { v } \right) \quad \text { \left(definition of } U \mathrm { a n d } V \right)$$
$$= P \left( X \in A _ { u } \right) P \left( Y \in B _ { v } \right) .$$
(Theorem 4.2.10)

The joint pdf of $\left( U , V \right)$ is

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial u \partial v } F _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right)$$
$$= \left( \frac { d } { d u } P \left( X \in A _ { u } \right) \right) \left( \frac { d } { d v } P \left( Y \in B _ { v } \right) \right) ,$$
(by (4.1.4))

where, as the notation indicates, the first factor is a function only of $u$ and the second
factor is a function only of $v .$ Hence, by Lemma 4.2.7, $U$ and $V$ are independent.
☐

It may be that there is only one function, say $U = g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right) ,$ of interest. In such
cases, this method may still be used to find the distribution of $U .$ If another convenient
function, $V = g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right) ,$ can be chosen so that the resulting transformation from
$\left( X , Y \right)$ to $\left( U , V \right)$ is one-to-one on $A ,$ then the joint pdf of $\left( U , V \right)$ can be derived using
(4.3.2) and the marginal pdf of $U$ can be obtained from the joint pdf. In the previous
example, perhaps we were interested only in $U = X Y .$ We could choose to define
$V = X ,$ recognizing that the resulting transformation is one-to-one on $\mathcal{A} .$ Then we
would proceed as in the example to obtain the marginal pdf of $U .$ But other choices,
such as $V = Y ,$ would work as well (see Exercise 4.23).

Of course, in many situations, the transformation of interest is not one-to-one.
Just as Theorem 2.1.8 generalized the univariate method to many-to-one functions,
the same can be done here. As before, $\mathcal{A} = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) > 0 \right\} .$ Suppose
$A _ { 0 } , A _ { 1 } , \cdots , A _ { k }$ form a partition of $\mathcal{A}$ with these properties. The set $A _ { 0 } ,$ which may
be empty, satisfies $P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$ The transformation $U = g _ { 1 } \left( X , Y \right)$ and
$V = g _ { 2 } \left( X , Y \right)$ is a one-to-one transformation from $A _ { i }$ onto $\mathcal{B}$ for each $i = 1 , 2 , \cdots , k .$
Then for each $i ,$ the inverse functions from $\mathcal{B}$ to $A _ { i }$ can be found. Denote the ith
inverse by $x = h _ { 1 i } \left( u , v \right)$ and $y = h _ { 2 i } \left( u , v \right) .$ This ith inverse gives, for $\left( u , v \right) \in \mathcal{B} ,$ the
unique $\left( x , y \right) \in A _ { i }$ such that $\left( u , v \right) = \left( g _ { 1 } \left( x , y \right) , g _ { 2 } \left( x , y \right) \right) .$ Let $J _ { i }$ denote the Jacobian
computed from the $i$ ith inverse. Then assuming that these Jacobians do not vanish
identically on $\mathcal{B} ,$ we have the following representation of the joint pdf, $f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) :$

(4.3.6)
$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } f _ { X , Y } \left( h _ { 1 i } \left( u , v \right) , h _ { 2 i } \left( u , v \right) \right) | J _ { i } | .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="162" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4" -->

Example 4.3.6 (Distribution of the ratio of normal variables) Let $X$ and
$Y$ be independent $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables. Consider the transformation $U = X / Y$
and $V = Y | .$ $\left( U \right.$ and $V$ can be defined to be any value, say $\left( 1 , 1 \right) ,$ if $Y = 0$ since
$\left. P \left( Y = 0 \right) = 0 . \right)$ This transformation is not one-to-one since the points $\left( x , y \right)$ and
$\left( - x , - y \right)$ are both mapped into the same $\left( u , v \right)$ point. But if we restrict consideration
to either positive or negative values of $y ,$ then the transformation is one-to-one. In
the above notation, let

$$A _ { 1 } = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : y > 0 \right\} , \quad A _ { 2 } = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : y < 0 \right\} , \quad \text { and } \quad A _ { 0 } = \left\{ \left( x , y \right) : y = 0 \right.$$

$A _ { 0 } ,$ $A _ { 1 } ,$ and $A _ { 2 }$ form a partition of $\mathcal{A} = \mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 }$ and $P \left( \left( X , Y \right) \in A _ { 0 } \right) = P \left( Y = 0 \right) = 0 .$
For either $A _ { 1 }$ or $A _ { 2 } ,$ if $\left( x , y \right) \in A _ { i } ,$ $v = \left[ y | > 0 , \right.$ and for a fixed value of $v = \left[ 3 \right] ,$
$u = x / y$ can be any real number since $x$ can be any real number. Thus, $\left. B = u , v \right) :$
$v > 0$ is the image of both $A _ { 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 }$ under the transformation. Furthermore, the
inverse transformations from $\mathcal{B}$ to $A _ { 1 }$ and $\mathcal{B}$ to $A _ { 2 }$ are given by $x = h _ { 1 1 } \left( u , v \right) = u v ,$
$y = h _ { 2 1 } \left( u , v \right) = v ,$ and $x = h _ { 1 2 } \left( u , v \right) = - u v ,$ $y = h _ { 2 2 } \left( u , v \right) = - v .$ Note that the
first inverse gives positive values of $y$ and the second gives negative values of $y$ y. The
Jacobians from the two inverses are $J _ { 1 } = J _ { 2 } = v .$ Using

$$f _ { X , Y } \left( x , y \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - y ^ { 2 } / 2 } ,$$

from (4.3.6) we obtain

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } e ^ { - \left( u v \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - v ^ { 2 } / 2 } | v | + \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } e ^ { - \left( - u v \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } e ^ { - \left( - v \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 } | v |$$
$$= \frac { v } { \pi } e ^ { - \left( u ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) v ^ { 2 } / 2 } , \quad - \infty < u < \infty , \quad 0 < v < \infty .$$

From this the marginal pdf of $U$ can be computed to be

$$f _ { U } \left( u \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { v } { \pi } e ^ { - \left( u ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) v ^ { 2 } / 2 } d v$$
$$\left. = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \left( u ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) z / 2 } d z z = v ^ { 2 } \right) \quad \text { \left(change of variable\right) }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \frac { 2 } { \left( u ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } \quad \left( \text { integrand is kernel of } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \pi \left( u ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } , \quad - \infty < u < \infty$$

So we see that the ratio of two independent standard normal random variables is a
Cauchy random variable. (See Exercise 4.28 for more relationships between normal
and Cauchy random variables.)
|


## 4.4 Hierarchical Models and Mixture Distributions

In the cases we have seen thus far, a random variable has a single distribution, possibly
depending on parameters. While, in general, a random variable can have only one
distribution, it is often easier to model a situation by thinking of things in a hierarchy.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="163" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4 HIERARCHICAL MODELS AND MIXTURE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->

Example 4.4.1 (Binomial-Poisson hierarchy) Perhaps the most classic hierar-
chical model is the following. An insect lays a large number of eggs, each surviving
with probability $p .$ On the average, how many eggs will survive?

The "large number" of eggs laid is a random variable, often taken to be $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) .$
Furthermore, if we assume that each egg's survival is independent, then we have
Bernoulli trials. Therefore, if we let $X = n u m b e r$ of survivors and $Y = n u m b e r \quad o f$
eggs laid, we have

$$X | Y \sim b i n o m i a l \left( Y , p \right) ,$$
$$Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$$

a hierarchical model. (Recall that we use notation such as $X | Y \sim b i n o m i a l \left( Y , p \right)$ to
mean that the conditional distribution of $X$ given $Y = y$ is binomial(y, $\left. \left. p \right) . \right)$
|

The advantage of the hierarchy is that complicated processes may be modeled by
a sequence of relatively simple models placed in a hierarchy. Also, dealing with the
hierarchy is no more difficult than dealing with conditional and marginal distributions.
