Example 4.4.2 (Continuation of Example 4.4.1) The random variable of in-
terest, $X = \mathrm { n u m b }$ of survivors, has the distribution given by

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { \infty } P \left( X = x , Y = y \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { y = 0 } ^ { \infty } P \left( X = x | Y = y \right) P \left( Y = y \right) \quad \left( \text { definition of } = \rangle y = 0 \right.$$
$$= \sum _ { y = x } ^ { \infty } \left[ \left( \begin{array}{} { y } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { x } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { y - x } \right] \left[ \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { y } } { y ! } \right] , \left( \begin{array}{} \text { conditional probability } \\ \text { is } 0 \text { if } y < x \end{array} \right)$$

since $X | Y = y$ is binomial(y, $\left. p \right)$ and $Y$ is $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) .$ If we now simplify this last
expression, canceling what we can and multiplying by $\lambda ^ { x } / \lambda ^ { x } ,$ we get

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \frac { \left( \lambda p \right) ^ { x } e ^ { - \lambda } } { x ! } \sum _ { y = x } ^ { \infty } \frac { \left( \left( 1 - p \right) \lambda \right) ^ { y - x } } { \left( y - x \right) ! }$$
$$= \frac { \left( \lambda p \right) ^ { x } e ^ { - \lambda } } { x ! } \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \left( \left( 1 - p \right) \lambda \right) ^ { t } } { t ! } \left( t = y - x \right)$$
$$= \frac { \left( \lambda p \right) ^ { x } e ^ { - \lambda } } { x ! } e ^ { \left( 1 - p \right) \lambda }$$
$$= \frac { \left( \lambda p \right) ^ { x } } { x ! } e ^ { - \lambda p } ,$$
$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { sum is a kernel for } } \\ { \text { a Poisson distribution } } \end{array} \right)$

so $X \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda p \right) .$ Thus, any marginal inference on $X$ is with respect to a Pois-
$\mathrm { s o n } \left( \lambda p \right)$ distribution, with $Y$ playing no part at all. Introducing $Y$ in the hierarchy

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="164" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4" -->

was mainly to aid our understanding of the model. There was an added bonus in
that the parameter of the distribution of $X$ is the product of two parameters, each
relatively simple to understand.

The answer to the original question is now easy to compute:

$$E X = \lambda p ,$$

so, on the average, $\lambda p$ eggs will survive. If we were interested only in this mean and did
not need the distribution, we could have used properties of conditional expectations.
|

Sometimes, calculations can be greatly simplified be using the following theorem.
Recall from Section 4.2 that $E \left( X | y \right)$ is a function of $y$ and $\mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right)$ is a random
variable whose value depends on the value of $Y .$

Theorem 4.4.3 If $X$ and $Y$ are any two random variables, then

(4.4.1)
$$E X = E \left( E \left( X | Y \right) \right) ,$$

provided that the expectations $e x i s t .$

Proof: Let $f \left( x , y \right)$ denote the joint pdf of $X$ and $Y .$ By definition, we have

(4.4.2)
$$E X = \int \int x f \left( x , y \right) d x d y = \int \left[ \int x f \left( x | y \right) d x \right] f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y ,$$

where $f \left( x | y \right)$ and $f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ are the conditional pdf of $X$ given $Y = y$ and the marginal pdf
of $Y ,$ respectively. But now notice that the inner integral in $\left( 4 . 4 . 2 \right)$ is the conditional
expectation $E \left( X | y \right) ,$ and we have

$$E X = \int E \left( X | y \right) f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y = E \left( E \left( X | Y \right) \right) ,$$

as desired. Replace integrals by sums to prove the discrete case.
☐

Note that equation (4.4.1) contains an abuse of notation, since we have used the
"E" to stand for different expectations in the same equation. The "E" in the left-
hand side of (4.4.1) is expectation with respect to the marginal distribution of $X .$
The first "E" in the right-hand side of $\left( 4 . 4 . 1 \right)$ is expectation with respect to the
marginal distribution of $Y ,$ while the second one stands for expectation with respect
to the conditional distribution of $X | Y .$ However, there is really no cause for confusion
because these interpretations are the only ones that the symbol "E" can take!

We can now easily compute the expected number of survivors in Example 4.4.1.
From Theorem 4.4.3 we have

$$E X = E \left( E \left( X | Y \right) \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { E } \left( p Y \right) \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad$$
$$= p \lambda .$$
$$\left( \mathrm { s i n c e } Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="165" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4 HIERARCHICAL MODELS AND MIXTURE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->

The term mixture distribution in the title of this section refers to a distribution
arising from a hierarchical structure. Although there is no standardized definition for
this term, we will use the following definition, which seems to be a popular one.

Definition 4.4.4 A random variable $X$ is said to have a mixture distribution if the
distribution of $X$ depends on a quantity that also has a distribution.

Thus, in Example 4.4.1 the $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda p \right)$ distribution is a mixture distribution since
it is the result of combining a binomial(Y, $\left. p \right)$ with $Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) .$ In general, we can
say that hierarchical models lead to mixture distributions.

There is nothing to stop the hierarchy at two stages, but it should be easy to
see that any more complicated hierarchy can be treated as a two-stage hierarchy
theoretically. There may be advantages, however, in modeling a phenomenon as a
multistage hierarchy. It may be easier to understand.

Example 4.4.5 (Generalization of Example 4.4.1) Consider a generalization
of Example 4.4.1, where instead of one mother insect there are a large number of
mothers and one mother is chosen at random. We are still interested in knowing
the average number of survivors, but it is no longer clear that the number of eggs
laid follows the same Poisson distribution for each mother. The following three-stage
hierarchy may be more appropriate. Let $X$ = number of survivors in a litter; then

$$X | Y \sim b i n o m i a l \left( Y , p \right) ,$$
$$Y | \Lambda \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \Lambda \right) ,$$
$$\Lambda \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \beta \right) ,$$

where the last stage of the hierarchy accounts for the variability across different
mothers.

The mean of $X$ can easily be calculated as

$$E X = E \left( E \left( X | Y \right) \right)$$
$$= E \left( p Y \right)$$
$$= E \left( E \left( p Y | \Lambda \right) \right)$$
$$= E \left( p \Lambda \right)$$
$$= p \beta ,$$

(as before)

(exponential expectation)

completing the calculation.

II

In this example we have used a slightly different type of model than before in that
two of the random variables are discrete and one is continuous. Using these models
should present no problems. We can define a joint density, $f \left( x , y , \lambda \right)$ conditional
densities, $f \left( x \left( y \right) , \right.$ $f \left( x | y , \lambda \right) ,$ etc .; and marginal densities, $f \left( x \right) ,$ $f \left( x , y \right) ,$ etc. as before.
Simply understand that, when probabilities or expectations are calculated, discrete
variables are summed and continuous variables are integrated.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="166" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4" -->

Note that this three-stage model can also be thought of as a two-stage hierarchy

by combining the last two stages. If $Y | \Lambda \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \Lambda \right)$ and $\Lambda \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \beta \right) ,$ then
$$P \left( Y = y \right) = P \left( Y = y , 0 < \Lambda < \infty \right)$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } f \left( y , \lambda \right) d \lambda$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } f \left( y | \lambda \right) f \left( \lambda \right) d \lambda$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \left[ \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { y } } { y ! } \right] \frac { 1 } { \beta } e ^ { - \lambda / \beta } d \lambda$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \beta y ! } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \lambda ^ { y } e ^ { - \lambda \left( 1 + \beta ^ { - 1 } \right) } d \lambda$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \beta y ! } \Gamma \left( y + 1 \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 + \beta ^ { - 1 } } \right) ^ { y + 1 }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 + \beta \right) } \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 + \beta ^ { - 1 } } \right) ^ { y }$$
This expression for the pmf of $Y$ is the form (3.2.10) of the negative binomial pmf.

Therefore, our three-stage hierarchy in Example 4.4.5 is equivalent to the two-stage
hierarchy

$$X | Y \sim \mathrm { b i n o m i a l } \left( Y , p \right) ,$$
$$Y \sim \text { negative binomial } \left( p = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \beta } , r = 1 \right) .$$

However, in terms of understanding the model, the three-stage model is much easier
to understand!

A useful generalization is a Poisson gamma mixture, which is a generalization of a
part of the previous model. If we have the hierarchy

$$Y | \Lambda \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \Lambda \right) ,$$
$$\Lambda \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$$

then the marginal distribution of $Y$ is negative binomial (see Exercise 4.32). This
model for the negative binomial distribution shows that it can be considered to be
a "more variable" Poisson. Solomon (1983) explains these and other biological and
mathematical models that lead to the negative binomial distribution. (See Exercise
4.33.)

Aside from the advantage in aiding understanding, hierarchical models can often
make calculations easier. For example, a distribution that often occurs in statistics is
the noncentral $c h i$ squared distribution. With $p$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality
parameter $\lambda ,$ the pdf is given by

(4.4.3)
$$f \left( x | \lambda , p \right) = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { x ^ { p / 2 + k - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } } { \Gamma \left( p / 2 + k \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 + k } } \frac { \lambda ^ { k } e ^ { - \lambda } } { k ! }$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { g a m m a } \\ { p d f k e r n e l } \end{array} \right)$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="167" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4 HIERARCHICAL MODELS AND MIXTURE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->

an extremely messy expression. Calculating EX, for example, looks like quite a chore.
However, if we examine the pdf closely, we see that this is a mixture distribution,
made up of central chi squared densities (like those given in (3.2.10)) and Poisson
distributions. That is, if we set up the hierarchy

$$X | K \sim \chi _ { p + 2 K } ^ { 2 } ,$$
$$K \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$$

then the marginal distribution of $X$ is given by (4.4.3). Hence

$$E X = E \left( E \left( X | K \right) \right)$$
$$= E \left( p + 2 K \right)$$
$$= p + 2 \lambda ,$$

a relatively simple calculation. Var $X$ can also be calculated in this way.

We close this section with one more hierarchical model and illustrate one more
conditional expectation calculation.

Example 4.4.6 (Beta-binomial hierarchy) One generalization of the binomial
distribution is to allow the success probability to vary according to a distribution. A
standard model for this situation is

$$X | P \sim \text { binomial } \left( P \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$
$$P \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) .$$

By iterating the expectation, we calculate the mean of $X$ as

$$E X = E \left[ E \left( X | P \right) \right] = E \left[ n P \right] = n \frac { \alpha } { \alpha + \beta }$$

Calculating the variance of $X$ is only slightly more involved. We can make use of
a formula for conditional variances, similar in spirit to the expected value identity of
Theorem 4.4.3.

Theorem 4.4.7 (Conditional variance identity) For any two random variables
$X$ and $Y$ ,

(4.4.4)
$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | Y \right) \right) + \mathrm { V a r } \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right) ,$$

provided that the expectations exist.

Proof: By definition, we have

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { E } \left( \left[ X - \mathrm { E } X \right] ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) + \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

where in the last step we have added and subtracted $\mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) .$ Expanding the square
in this last expectation now gives

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { E } \left( \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \right) + \mathrm { E } \left( \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$+ 2 E \left( \left[ X \quad - \quad E \left( X | Y \right) \right] \left[ E \left( X | Y \right) - \quad E X \right] \right) .$$
(4.4.5)

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="168" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.4" -->

The last term in this expression is equal to 0, however, which can easily be seen by
iterating the expectation:

(4.4.6)
$$\mathrm { E } \left( \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { E } \left\{ \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] | Y \right\} \right) .$$

In the conditional distribution $X | Y ,$ $X$ is the random variable. So in the expression

$$\mathrm { E } \left\{ \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] | Y \right\} ,$$

$\mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right)$ and $\mathrm { E } X$ are constants. Thus,

$$\mathrm { E } \left\{ \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right] | Y \right\} = \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } \left\{ \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] | Y \right\} \right)$$
$$= \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( E \left( X | Y \right) - \quad E X \right) \left( 0 \right)$$
$$= 0 .$$

Thus, from (4.4.6), we have that $\mathrm { E } \left( \left( X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) - \mathrm { E } X \right) \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( 0 \right) = 0 .$
Referring back to equation (4.4.5), we see that

$$\left. \mathrm { E } \left( \left[ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { E } \left\{ X - \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } | Y \right\} \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | Y \right) \right)$$

and

$$E \left( \left[ E \left( X | Y \right) - E X \right] ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { V a r } \left( E \left( X | Y \right) \right) ,$$

☐
establishing (4.4.4).

Example 4.4.8 (Continuation of Example 4.4.6) To calculate the variance of
$X ,$ we have from (4.4.4),

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { V a r } \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | P \right) \right) + \mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | P \right) \right) .$$
.

Now $\mathrm { E } \left( X | P \right) = n P ,$ and since $P \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X | P \right) \right) = \mathrm { V a r } \left( n P \right) = n ^ { 2 } \frac { \alpha \beta } { \left( \alpha + \beta \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \alpha + \beta + 1 \right) } .$$

Also, since $X | P$ is binomial(n, $\left. P \right) ,$ $\mathrm { V a r } \left( X | P \right) = n P \left( 1 - P \right) .$ We then have

$$\mathrm { E } \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | P \right) \right] = n \mathrm { E } \left[ P \left( 1 - P \right) \right] = n \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } p \left( 1 - p \right) p ^ { \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { \beta - 1 } d p .$$

Notice that the integrand is the kernel of another beta pdf (with parameters $\alpha + 1$
and $\beta + 1$ so

$$\mathrm { E } \left( \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | P \right) \right) = n \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( \beta \right) } \left[ \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + 1 \right) \Gamma \left( \beta + 1 \right) } { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + 2 \right) } \right] = n \frac { \alpha \beta } { \left( \alpha + \beta \right) \left( \alpha + \beta + 1 \right) } .$$

Adding together the two pieces and simplifying, we get

$$\mathrm { V a r } X = n \frac { \alpha \beta \left( \alpha + \beta + n \right) } { \left( \alpha + \beta \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \alpha + \beta + 1 \right) }$$

HI

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="169" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COVARIANCE AND CORRELATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->


## 4.5 Covariance and Correlation

In earlier sections, we have discussed the absence or presence of a relationship be-
tween two random variables, independence or nonindependence. But if there is a
relationship, the relationship may be strong or weak. In this section we discuss two
numerical measures of the strength of a relationship between two random variables,
the covariance and correlation.

To illustrate what we mean by the strength of a relationship between two random
variables, consider two different experiments. In the first, random variables $X$ and $Y$
are measured, where $X$ is the weight of a sample of water and $Y$ is the volume of
the same sample of water. Clearly there is a strong relationship between $X$ and $Y .$ If
$\left( X , Y \right)$ pairs are measured on several samples and the observed data pairs are plotted,
the data points should fall on a straight line because of the physical relationship
between $X$ and $Y .$ This will not be exactly the case because of measurement errors,
impurities in the water, etc. But with careful laboratory technique, the data points
will fall very nearly on a straight line. Now consider another experiment in which $X$
and $Y$ are measured, where $X$ is the body weight of a human and $Y$ is the same
human's height. Clearly there is also a relationship between $X$ and $Y$ here but the
relationship is not nearly as strong. We would not expect a plot of $\left( X , Y \right)$ pairs
measured on different people to form a straight line, although we might expect to
see an upward trend in the plot. The covariance and correlation are two measures
that quantify this difference in the strength of a relationship between two random
variables.

Throughout this section we will frequently be referring to the mean and variance
of $X$ and the mean and variance of $Y .$ For these we will use the notation $E X = \mu _ { X } ,$
$\mathrm { E } Y = \mu _ { Y } ,$ $\mathrm { V a r } X = \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } ,$ and Var $Y = \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } .$ We will assume throughout that $0 < \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } <$
00 and $0 < \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } < \infty .$

Definition 4.5.1 The covariance of $X$ and $Y$ is the number defined by

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = E \left( \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) .$$

Definition 4.5.2 The correlation of $X$ and $Y$ is the number defined by

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } .$$

The value $\rho _ { X Y }$ is also called the correlation coefficient.

If large values of $X$ tend to be observed with large values of $Y$ and small values of
$X$ with small values of $Y ,$ then $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right)$ will be positive. If $X > \mu _ { X } ,$ then $Y > \mu _ { Y }$
is likely to be true and the product $\left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right)$ will be positive. If $X < \mu _ { X } ,$
then $Y < \mu _ { Y }$ is likely to be true and the product $\left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right)$ will again be
positive. Thus $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = E \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) > 0 .$ If large values of $X$ tend to be
observed with small values of $Y$ and small values of $X$ with large values of $Y ,$ then
$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right)$ will be negative because when $X > \mu _ { X } ,$ $Y$ will tend to be less than $\mu _ { Y }$
and vice versa, and hence $\left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right)$ will tend to be negative. Thus the sign
of $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right)$ gives information regarding the relationship between $X$ and $Y .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="170" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->

But $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right)$ can be any number and a given value of $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) ,$ say $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) =$
3, does not in itself give information about the strength of the relationship between
$X$ and $Y .$ On the other hand, the correlation is always between -1 and 1, with the
values -1 and 1 indicating a perfect linear relationship between $X$ and $Y .$ This is
proved in Theorem 4.5.7.

Before investigating these properties of covariance and correlation, we will first
calculate these measures in a given example. This calculation will be simplified by
the following result.

Theorem 4.5.3 For any random variables $X$ and $Y ,$

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \mathrm { E } X Y - \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y } .$$

Proof: $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = E \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right)$
(expanding the product)

$$= E \left( X Y - \mu _ { X } Y - \mu _ { Y } X + \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y } \right)$$

$$= \mathrm { E } X Y - \mu _ { X } \mathrm { E } Y - \mu _ { Y } \mathrm { E } X + \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y }$$
(μχ and $\mu _ { Y }$ are constants)

$$= E X Y - \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y } - \mu _ { Y } \mu _ { X } + \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y }$$
$$= \mathrm { E } X Y - \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y } .$$

☐

Example 4.5.4 (Correlation-I) Let the joint pdf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ be $f \left( x , y \right) = 1 , 0 <$
$x < 1 , x < y < x + 1 .$ See Figure 4.5.1. The marginal distribution of $X$ is uniform(0,1)
so $\mu _ { X } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ and $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } .$ The marginal pdf of $Y$ is $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = y ,$ $0 < y < 1 ,$ and
$f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = 2 - y , 1 \leq y < 2 ,$ with $\mu _ { Y } = 1$ and $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 6 } .$ We also have

$$E X Y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { x } ^ { x + 1 } x y d y d x = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 1 } { 2 } x y ^ { 2 } | _ { x } ^ { x + 1 } d x$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } x \right) d x = \frac { 7 } { 1 2 } .$$

<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->

Using Theorem 4.5.3, we have $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \frac { 7 } { 1 2 } - \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \left( 1 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } .$ The correlation is

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } = \frac { 1 / 1 2 } { \sqrt { 1 / 1 2 } \sqrt { 1 / 1 6 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } .$$

II

In the next three theorems we describe some of the fundamental properties of
covariance and correlation.

Theorem 4.5.5 If $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables, then $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = 0$
and $\rho _ { X Y } = 0 .$

Proof: Since X and $Y$ are independent, from Theorem 4.2.10 we have $E X Y =$
$\left( E X \right) \left( E Y \right) .$ Thus

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \mathrm { E } X Y - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Y \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Y \right) - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Y \right) = 0$$

and

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } = \frac { 0 } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } = 0 .$$

☐

Thus, the values $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \rho _ { X Y } = 0$ in some sense indicate that there is no
relationship between $X$ and $Y .$ It is important to note, however, that Theorem 4.5.5
does not say that if $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = 0 ,$ then $X$ and $Y$ are independent. For example, if
$X \sim f \left( x - \theta \right) ,$ symmetric around $0$ with $E X = \theta ,$ and $Y$ is the indicator function
$Y = I \left( | X - \theta | < 2 \right) ,$ then $X$ and $Y$ are obviously not independent. However,

$$\mathrm { E } \left( X Y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } x I \left( | x - \theta | < 2 \right) f \left( x - \theta \right) d x = \int _ { - 2 } ^ { 2 } \left( t + \theta \right) f \left( t \right) d t = \theta \int _ { - 2 } ^ { 2 } f \left( t \right) d t = \mathrm { E } X \mathrm { E } Y ,$$

where we used the fact that, by symmetry, $\int _ { - 2 } ^ { 2 } t f \left( t \right) d t = 0 .$ So it is easy to find
uncorrelated, dependent random variables.

Covariance and correlation measure only a particular kind of linear relationship
that will be described further in Theorem 4.5.7. Also see Example 4.5.9, which dis-
cusses two random variables that have a strong relationship but whose covariance and
correlation are $0$ because the relationship is not linear.

Covariance also plays an important role in understanding the variation in sums of
random variables, as the next theorem, a generalization of Theorem 2.3.4, indicates.
(See Exercise 4.44 for a further generalization.)

Theorem 4.5.6 If $X$ and $Y$ are any two random variables and $a$ and $b$ are any two
constants, $t h e n$

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( a X + b Y \right) = a ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } X + b ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } Y + 2 a b \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) .$$

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent random variables, then

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( a X + b Y \right) = a ^ { 2 } \text { Var } X + b ^ { 2 } \text { Var } Y .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="172" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->

Proof: The mean of $a X + b Y$ is $E \left( a X + b Y \right) = a E X + b E Y = a \mu _ { X } + b \mu _ { Y } .$ Thus

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( a X + b Y \right) = E \left( \left( a X + b Y \right) - \left( a \mu _ { X } + b \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E \left( a \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) + b \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E \left( a ^ { 2 } \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 a b \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right)$$
$$= a ^ { 2 } E \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } E \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 a b E \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right)$$
$$= a ^ { 2 } \text { Var } X + b ^ { 2 } \text { Var } Y + 2 a b \text { Cov } \left( X , Y \right) .$$

If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, then, from Theorem 4.5.5, $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = 0$ and the second
equality is immediate from the first.
☐

From Theorem 4.5.6 we see that if $X$ and $Y$ are positively correlated $\left( \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) \right.$
$\left. > 0 \right) ,$ then the variation in $X + Y$ is greater than the sum of the variations in $X$
and $Y .$ But if they are negatively correlated, then the variation in $X + Y$ is less than
the sum. For negatively correlated random variables, large values of one tend to be
observed with small values of the other and in the sum these two extremes cancel.
The result, $X + Y ,$ tends not to have as many extreme values and hence has smaller
variance. By choosing $a = 1$ and $b = - \quad 1$ we get an expression for the variance of the
difference of two random variables, and similar arguments apply.

The nature of the linear relationship measured by covariance and correlation is
somewhat explained by the following theorem.

Theorem 4.5.7 For any random variables $X$ and $Y$ ,

a. $- 1 \leq \rho _ { X Y } \leq 1 .$

b. $| \rho _ { X Y } | = 1$ if and only $i f$ there exist numbers $a \neq 0$ and $b$ such that $P \left( Y = \right.$
$\left. a X + b \right) = 1 .$ If $\rho _ { X Y } = 1 ,$ then $a > 0 ,$ and if $\rho _ { X Y } = - 1 ,$ then $a < 0 .$

Proof: Consider the function $h \left( t \right)$ defined by

$$h \left( t \right) = E \left( \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) t + \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Expanding this expression, we obtain

$$h \left( t \right) = t ^ { 2 } \mathrm { E } \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) ^ { 2 } + 2 t \mathrm { E } \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) + \mathrm { E } \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= t ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } + 2 t C o v \left( X , Y \right) + \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } .$$

This quadratic function of $t$ is greater than or equal to 0 for all values of $t$ since it is
the expected value of a nonnegative random variable. Thus, this quadratic function
can have at most one real root and thus must have a nonpositive discriminant. That
is,

$$\left( 2 C o v \left( X , Y \right) \right) ^ { 2 } - 4 \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \leq 0 .$$

This is equivalent to

$$- \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } \leq \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) \leq \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="173" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5 COVARIANCE AND CORRELATION" -->

Dividing by $\sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y }$ yields

$$- 1 \leq \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } = \rho _ { X Y } \leq 1 .$$

Also, $| \rho _ { X Y } | = 1$ if and only if the discriminant is equal to 0. That is, $| \rho _ { X Y } | = 1$ if and
only if $h \left( t \right)$ has a single root. But since $\left( \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) t + \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \geq 0 ,$ the expected
value $h \left( t \right) = E \left( \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) t + \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = 0$ if and only if

$$P \left( \left[ \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) t + \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } = 0 \right) = 1 .$$

This is equivalent to

$$P \left( \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) t + \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) = 0 \right) = 1 .$$

This is $P \left( Y = a x + b \right) = 1$ with $a = - \quad t$ and $b = \mu _ { X } t + \mu _ { Y } ,$ where $t$ is the root of
$h \left( t \right) .$ Using the quadratic formula, we see that this root is $t = - \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } .$ Thus
$a = - \quad t$ has the same sign as $\rho _ { X Y } ,$ proving the final assertion.
☐

In Section 4.7 we will prove a theorem called the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality. This
theorem has as a direct consequence that $\rho _ { X Y }$ is bounded between -1 and 1, and we
will see that, with this inequality, the preceding proof can be shortened.

If there is a line $y = a x + b ,$ with $a \neq 0 ,$ such that the values of $\left( X , Y \right)$ have a high
probability of being near this line, then the correlation between $X \quad a n d \quad Y$ will be near
$1 \quad o r \quad - 1 .$ But if no such line exists, the correlation will be near 0. This is an intuitive
notion of the linear relationship that is being measured by correlation. This idea will
be illustrated further in the next two examples.

Example 4.5.8 (Correlation-II) This example is similar to Example 4.5.4, but we
develop it differently to illustrate other model building and computational techniques.
Let $X$ have a $u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ distribution and Z have a uniform(0, $\left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right)$ distribution.
Suppose $X$ and $Z$ are independent. Let $Y = X + Z$ and consider the random vector
$\left( X , Y \right) .$ The joint distribution of $\left( X , Y \right)$ can be derived from the joint distribution of
$\left( X , Z \right)$ using the techniques of Section 4.3. The joint pdf of $\left( X , Y \right)$ is

$$f \left( x , y \right) = 1 0 , \quad 0 < x < 1 , \quad x < y < x + \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } .$$

Rather than using the formal techniques of Section 4.3, we can justify this as follows.
Given $X = x ,$ $Y = x + Z .$ The conditional distribution of $Z$ given $X = x$ is just
$\left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right)$ since $X$ and $Z$ are independent. Thus $x$ serves as a location parameter
in the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x ,$ and this conditional distribution
is just $\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( x , x + \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right) .$ Multiplying this conditional pdf by the marginal pdf of $X$
(uniform(0, $\left. \left. 1 \right) \right)$ yields the joint pdf above. This representation of $Y = X + Z$ makes
the computation of the covariance and correlation easy. The expected values of $X$
and $Y$ are $E X = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ and $E Y = E \left( X + Z \right) = E X + E Z = \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 0 } = \frac { 1 1 } { 2 0 } ,$ giving

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \mathrm { E } X Y - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Y \right)$$
$$= E X \left( X + Z \right) - \left( E X \right) \left( E \left( X + Z \right) \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="174" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->

$$= E X ^ { 2 } + E X Z - \left( E X \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( E X \right) \left( E Z \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { E } X ^ { 2 } - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Z \right) - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } Z \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { i n d e p e n d e n c e } \mathrm { o f } \\ X \mathrm { a n d } Z \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } .$$

From Theorem 4.5.6, the variance of $Y$ is $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } \left( X + Z \right) = \mathrm { V a r } X + \mathrm { V a r } Z =$
$\frac { 1 } { 1 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 2 0 0 }$
Thus

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } } { \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } } \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 2 0 0 } } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 0 0 } { 1 0 1 } }$$

This is much larger than the value of $\rho _ { X Y } = 1 / \sqrt { 2 }$ obtained in Example 4.5.4. The
sets on which $f \left( x , y \right)$ is positive for Example 4.5.4 and this example are illustrated in
Figure 4.5.1. (Recall that this set is called the support of a distribution.) In each case,
$\left( X , Y \right)$ is a random point from the set. In both cases there is a linearly increasing
relationship between $X$ and $Y ,$ but the relationship is much stronger in Figure 4.5.1b.
Another way to see this is by noting that in this example, the conditional distribution
of $Y$ given $X = x$ is uniform(x, $\left( x , x + \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right) .$ In Example 4.5.4, the conditional distribution
of $Y$ given $X = x \quad i s \quad u n i f o r m \left( x , x + 1 \right) .$ The knowledge that $X = x$ gives us much
more information about the value of $Y$ in this model than in the one in Example
4.5.4. Hence the correlation is nearer to 1 in this example.
|

The next example illustrates that there may be a strong relationship between $X$
and $Y ,$ but if the relationship is not linear, the correlation may be small.

Example 4.5.9 (Correlation-III) In this example, let $X$ have a $u n i f o r m \left( - 1 , 1 \right)$
distribution and let $Z$ have a uniform(0, $\left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right)$ distribution. Let $X$ and $Z$ be independent.
Let $Y = X ^ { 2 } + Z$ and consider the random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ As in Example 4.5.8,
given $X = x ,$ $Y = x ^ { 2 } + Z$ and the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ is
$\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( x ^ { 2 } , x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } \right) .$ The joint pdf of $X$ and $Y ,$ the product of this conditional pdf
and the marginal pdf of $X ,$ is thus

$$f \left( x , y \right) = 5 , \quad - 1 < x < 1 , \quad x ^ { 2 } < y < x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 0 } .$$

The set on which $f \left( x , y \right) > 0$ is illustrated in Figure 4.5.2. There is a strong rela-
tionship between $X$ and $Y ,$ as indicated by the conditional distribution of $Y$ given
$X = x .$ But the relationship is not linear. The possible values of $\left( X , Y \right)$ cluster around
a parabola rather than a straight line. The correlation does not measure this non-
linear relationship. In fact, $\rho _ { X Y } = 0 .$ Since $X$ has a $\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( - 1 , 1 \right)$ distribution,
$E X = E X ^ { 3 } = 0 ,$ and since $X$ and $Z$ are independent, $E X Z = \left( E X \right) \left( E Z \right) .$ Thus,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="175" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COVARIANCE AND CORRELATION" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 4.5.2. Region where $f \left( x , y \right) > 0$ for Example 4.5.9</figcaption>

$y$

1

1

1

I

1

-1

0

1

$x$

</figure>


$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( X \left( X ^ { 2 } + Z \right) \right) - \left( \mathrm { E } X \right) \left( \mathrm { E } \left( X ^ { 2 } + Z \right) \right)$$
$$= E X ^ { 3 } + E X Z - 0 E \left( X ^ { 2 } + Z \right)$$
$$= 0 + \left( E X \right) \left( E Z \right) = 0 \left( E Z \right) = 0 ,$$

and $\rho _ { X Y } = \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) / \left( \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } \right) = 0 .$
$\parallel$

We close this section by introducing $a$ very important bivariate distribution in
which the correlation coefficient arises naturally as a parameter.

Definition 4.5.10 Let $- \infty < \mu _ { X } < \infty ,$ $- \infty < \mu _ { Y } < \infty ,$ $0 < \sigma _ { X } ,$ $0 < \sigma _ { Y } ,$ and
$- 1 < \rho < 1$ be five real numbers. The bivariate normal pdf with means $\mu _ { X }$ and $\mu _ { Y } ,$
variances $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 }$ and $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ and correlation $\rho$ is the bivariate pdf given by

$$f \left( x , y \right) = \left( 2 \pi \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } \sqrt { 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\times \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) } \left( \left( \frac { x - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \right.$$
$$\left. \left. - 2 \rho \left( \frac { x - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right) \left( \frac { y - \mu _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { Y } } \right) + \left( \frac { y - \mu _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { Y } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \right)$$

for $- \infty < x < \infty$ and $- \infty < y < \infty .$

Although the formula for the bivariate normal pdf looks formidable, this bivariate
distribution is one of the most frequently used. (In fact, the derivation of the formula
need not be formidable at all. See Exercise 4.46.)

The many nice properties of this distribution include these:

a. The marginal distribution of $X$ is $n \left( \mu _ { X } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) .$

b. The marginal distribution of $Y$ is $n \left( \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right) .$

c. The correlation between $X$ and $Y$ is $\rho _ { X Y } = \rho .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="176" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.5" -->

d. For any constants $a$ and $b ,$ the distribution of $a X + b Y$ is $n \left( a \mu _ { X } + b \mu _ { Y } , a ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } + \right.$
$\left. b ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } + 2 a b \rho \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } \right) .$

We will leave the verification of properties (a), (b), and (d) as exercises (Exercise
4.45). Assuming (a) and (b) are true, we will prove (c). We have by definition

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } }$$
$$= \frac { E \left( X - \mu _ { X } \right) \left( Y - \mu _ { Y } \right) } { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } }$$
$$= E \left( \frac { X - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right) \left( \frac { Y - \mu _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { Y } } \right)$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( \frac { x - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right) \left( \frac { y - \mu _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { Y } } \right) f \left( x , y \right) d x d y .$$

Make the change of variable

$$s = \left( \frac { x - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right) \left( \frac { y - \mu _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { Y } } \right)$$
$$t = \left( \frac { x - \mu _ { X } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right)$$
and

Then $x = \sigma _ { X } t + \mu _ { X } ,$ $y = \left( \sigma _ { Y } s / t \right) + \mu _ { Y } ,$ and the Jacobian of the transformation is
$J = \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } / t .$ With this change of variable, we obtain

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } s f \left( \sigma _ { X } t + \mu _ { X } , \frac { \sigma _ { Y } s } { t } + \mu _ { Y } \right) | \frac { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } { t } | d s d t ,$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } s \left( 2 \pi \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } \sqrt { 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 }$$
$$\times \exp \left( - \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) } \left( t ^ { 2 } - 2 \rho s + \left( \frac { s } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \right) \frac { \sigma _ { X } \sigma _ { Y } } { | t | } d s d t .$$

Noting that $| t | = \sqrt { t ^ { 2 } }$ and $t ^ { 2 } - 2 \rho s + \left( \frac { s } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { s - \rho t ^ { 2 } } { t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } ,$ we can rewrite
this as

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \exp \left( - \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) \left[ \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { s } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sqrt { \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } } } \exp \left( - \frac { \left( s - \rho t ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } } \right) d s \right] d t .$$

The inner integral is $\mathrm { E } S ,$ where $S$ is a normal random variable with $E S = \rho t ^ { 2 }$ and
Var $S = \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } .$ Thus the inner integral is $\rho t ^ { 2 } .$ Hence we have

$$\rho _ { X Y } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { \rho t ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \exp \left( - \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) d t$$

But this integral is $\rho E T ^ { 2 } ,$ where $T$ is a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable. Hence $E T ^ { 2 } = 1$ and
$\rho _ { X Y } = \rho .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="177" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIVARIATE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

All the conditional distributions of $Y$ given $X = x$ and of $X$ given $Y = y$ are also
normal distributions. Using the joint and marginal pdfs given above, it is straightfor-
ward to verify that the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ is

$$n \left( \mu _ { Y } + \rho \left( \sigma _ { Y } / \sigma _ { X } \right) \left( x - \mu _ { X } \right) , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$

As $\rho$ converges to 1 or $- 1 ,$ the conditional variance $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right)$ converges to 0. Thus,
the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ becomes more concentrated about the
point $\mu _ { Y } + \rho \left( \sigma _ { Y } / \sigma _ { X } \right) \left( x - \mu _ { X } \right) ,$ and the joint probability distribution of $\left( X , Y \right)$ becomes
☒ ☒
more concentrated about the line $y = \mu _ { Y } + \rho \left( \sigma _ { Y } / \sigma _ { X } \right) \left( x - \mu _ { X } \right) .$ This illustrates again
the point made earlier that a correlation near 1 or -1 means that there is a line
$y = a x + b$ about which the values of $\left( X , Y \right)$ cluster with high probability.

Note one important fact: All of the normal marginal and conditional pdfs are de-
rived from the starting point of bivariate normality. The derivation does not go in the
opposite direction. That is, marginal normality does not imply joint normality. See
Exercise 4.47 for an illustration of this.


## 4.6 Multivariate Distributions

At the beginning of this chapter, we discussed observing more than two random
variables in an experiment. In the previous sections our discussions have concentrated
on a bivariate random vector $\left( X , Y \right) .$ In this section we discuss a multivariate random
☒
vector $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) .$ In the example at the beginning of this chapter, temperature,
height, weight, and blood pressure were observed on an individual. In this example,
$n = 4$ and the observed random vector is $\left( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 } , X _ { 4 } \right) ,$ where $X _ { 1 }$ is temperature,

☒ ☒
$X _ { 2 }$ is height, etc. The concepts from the earlier sections, including marginal and
conditional distributions, generalize from the bivariate to the multivariate setting.
We introduce some of these generalizations in this section.

$A$ note on notation: We will use boldface letters to denote multiple variates. Thus,
we write $X$ to denote the random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ and $\mathrm { x }$ to denote the sample
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$

The random vector $X = \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ has a sample space that is a subset of $\mathfrak{R} ^ { n }$
$\mathrm { I f } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is a discrete random vector (the sample space is countable), then the
joint pmf of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is the function defined by $f \left( x \right) = f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = P \left( X _ { 1 } = \right.$
☒
$\left. x _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } = x _ { n } \right)$ for each $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { n } .$ Then for any $A \subset \mathfrak{R} ^ { n } ,$

(4.6.1)
$$P \left( X \in A \right) = \sum _ { x \in A } f \left( x \right) .$$

If $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is a continuous random vector, the joint $p d f$ of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is a
function $f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ that satisfies

$$P \left( \mathrm { X } \in A \right) = \int \cdots \int _ { A } f \left( \mathrm { x } \right) d \mathrm { x } = \int \cdots \int _ { A } f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) d x _ { 1 } \cdots d x _ { n } .$$
(4.6.2)

These integrals are n-fold integrals with limits of integration set so that the integration
is over all points $\mathrm { x } \in A .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="178" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

Let $g \left( x \right) = g \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ be a real-valued function defined on the sample space of
☒
$\mathrm { X } .$ Then $g \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a random variable and the expected value of $g \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { E g } \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } g \left( \mathrm { x } \right) f \left( \mathrm { x } \right) d \mathrm { x } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { E } g \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = \sum _ { \mathrm { x } \in \mathbb{R} ^ { n } } g \left( \mathrm { x } \right) f \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

in the continuous and discrete cases, respectively. These and other definitions are
analogous to the bivariate definitions except that now the integrals or sums are over
the appropriate subset of $\mathfrak{R} ^ { n }$ rather than $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$

The marginal $p d f$ or $p m f$ of any subset of the coordinates of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ can be
☒

☒
computed by integrating or summing the joint pdf or pmf over all possible values of
the other coordinates. Thus, for example, the marginal distribution of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { k } \right) ,$

☒ ☒
the first $k$ coordinates of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) ,$ is given by the pdf or pmf
☒ ☒

(4.6.4)
$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) d x _ { k + 1 } \cdots d x _ { n }$$

or

(4.6.5)
$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) = \sum _ { \left( x _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { n - k } } f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$$

for every $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) \in \mathfrak{R} ^ { k } .$ The conditional pdf $o r$ pmf of a subset of the coordinates
of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ given the values of the remaining coordinates is obtained by dividing
☒ ☒
the joint pdf or pmf by the marginal pdf or pmf of the remaining coordinates. Thus,
for example, if $f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) > 0 ,$ the conditional pdf or pmf of $\left( X _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ given
☒ ☒

$X _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { k } = x _ { k }$ is the function of $\left( x _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ defined by
☒ ☒

(4.6.6)
$$f \left( x _ { k + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) = \frac { f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) } { f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { k } \right) } .$$

These ideas are illustrated in the following example.

Example 4.6.1 (Multivariate pdfs) Let $n = 4$ and

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) & 0 < x _ { i } < 1 , i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \\ 0 & \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e } . \end{array} \right.$$

This nonnegative function is the joint pdf of a random vector $\left( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 } , X _ { 4 } \right)$ and
☒ ☒

☒ ☒
it can be verified that

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 }$$
$$= 1 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="179" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIVARIATE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

This joint pdf can be used to compute probabilities such as

$$P \left( X _ { 1 } < \frac { 1 } { 2 } , X _ { 2 } < \frac { 3 } { 4 } , X _ { 4 } > \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \int _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 } .$$

Note how the limits of integration restrict the integration to those values of $\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } \right. ,$
$x _ { 4 }$ that are in the event in question and for which $f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) > 0 .$ Each of the
four terms, $\frac { 3 } { 4 } x ^ { 2 } ,$ $\frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,$ etc., can be integrated separately and the results summed. For
example,

$$\int _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 2 5 6 }$$

The other three integrals are $\frac { 7 } { 1 0 2 4 } ,$ $\frac { 3 } { 6 4 } ,$ and $\frac { 2 1 } { 2 5 6 } .$ Thus

$$P \left( X _ { 1 } < \frac { 1 } { 2 } , X _ { 2 } < \frac { 3 } { 4 } , X _ { 4 } > \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 3 } { 2 5 6 } + \frac { 7 } { 1 0 2 4 } + \frac { 3 } { 6 4 } + \frac { 2 1 } { 2 5 6 } = \frac { 1 5 1 } { 1 0 2 4 }$$

Using (4.6.4), we can obtain the marginal pdf of $\left( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } \right)$ by integrating out the
variables $x _ { 3 }$ and $x _ { 4 }$ to obtain

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 }$$

for $0 < x _ { 1 } < 1$ and $0 < x _ { 2 } < 1 .$ Any probability or expected value that involves only
$X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ can be computed using this marginal pdf. For example,

$$X _ { 1 } X _ { 2 } = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } x _ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 4 } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 1 6 } x _ { 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 8 } x _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } x _ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 } { 3 2 } + \frac { 3 } { 3 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 8 } = \frac { 5 } { 1 6 }$$

For any $\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right)$ with $0 < x _ { 1 } < 1$ and $0 < x _ { 2 } < 1 ,$ $f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) > 0$ and the conditional
pdf of $\left( X _ { 3 } , X _ { 4 } \right)$ given $X _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 } = x _ { 2 }$ can be found using (4.6.6). For any such
$\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) ,$ $f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) > 0$ $0 < x _ { 3 } < 1$ and $0 < x _ { 4 } < 1 ,$ and for these values of
$\left( x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) ,$ the conditional pdf is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="180" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

$$f \left( x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } | x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } \right) } { f \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) }$$
$$= \frac { \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) } { \frac { 3 } { 4 } \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$$
$$= \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$$

For example, the conditional pdf of $\left( X _ { 3 } , X _ { 4 } \right)$ given $X _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 }$ and $X _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 }$ is
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$f \left( x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } | x _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } , x _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) = \frac { \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } } { \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 } { 3 } } = \frac { 5 } { 1 1 } + \frac { 9 } { 1 1 } x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 9 } { 1 1 } x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } .$$

This can be used to compute

$$P \left( X _ { 3 } > \frac { 3 } { 4 } , X _ { 4 } < \frac { 1 } { 2 } | X _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } , X _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \int _ { \frac { 3 } { 4 } } ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 5 } { 1 1 } + \frac { 9 } { 1 1 } x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 9 } { 1 1 } x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 3 } d x _ { 4 }$$
$$= \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { 5 } { 4 4 } + \frac { 1 1 1 } { 7 0 4 } + \frac { 9 } { 4 4 } x _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } \right) d x _ { 4 }$$
$$= \frac { 5 } { 8 8 } + \frac { 1 1 1 } { 1 4 0 8 } + \frac { 3 } { 3 5 2 } = \frac { 2 0 3 } { 1 4 0 8 }$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Before giving examples of computations with conditional and marginal distributions
for a discrete multivariate random vector, we will introduce an important family of
discrete multivariate distributions. This family generalizes the binomial family to the
situation in which each trial has $n$ (rather than two) distinct possible outcomes.

Definition 4.6.2 Let $n$ and $m$ be positive integers and let $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { n }$ be num-
bers satisfying $0 \leq p _ { i } \leq 1 ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } p _ { i } = 1 .$ Then the random vec-
☒
tor $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ has a multinomial distribution with $m$ trials and cell probabilities
☒ ☒
$p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { n }$ if the joint pmf of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is
☒ ☒

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \frac { m ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n } ! } p _ { 1 } ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdot p _ { n } ^ { x _ { n } } = m ! \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { p _ { i } ^ { x _ { i } } } { x _ { i } ! }$$

on the set of $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ such that each $x _ { i }$ is a nonnegative integer and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } = m .$

The multinomial distribution is a model for the following kind of experiment. The
experiment consists of $m$ independent trials. Each trial results in one of $n$ distinct
possible outcomes. The probability of the ith outcome is $p _ { i }$ on every trial. And $X _ { i }$
is the count of the number of times the $i$ ith outcome occurred in the $m$ trials. For
$n = 2 ,$ this is just a binomial experiment in which each trial has $n = 2$ possible
outcomes and $X _ { 1 }$ counts the number of "successes" and $X _ { 2 } = m - X _ { 1 }$ counts the
☒ ☒
number of "failures" in $m$ trials. In a general multinomial experiment, there are $n$
different possible outcomes to count.

I!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="181" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIVARIATE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

Example 4.6.3 (Multivariate pmf) Consider tossing a six-sided die ten times.
Suppose the die is unbalanced so that the probability of observing a 1 is $\frac { 1 } { 2 1 } ,$ the
probability of observing a 2 is $\frac { 2 } { 2 1 } ,$ and, in general, the probability of observing an
$i$ is $\frac { i } { 2 1 } .$ Now consider the random vector $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { 6 } \right) ,$ where $X _ { i }$ counts the num-
☒
ber $\mathrm { o f }$ times $i$ comes up in the ten tosses. Then $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { 6 } \right)$ has $a$ multinomial
☒
distribution with $m = 1 0$ trials, $n = 6$ possible outcomes, and cell probabilities
$p _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 1 } , p _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 } { 2 1 } , \ldots , p _ { 6 } = \frac { 6 } { 2 1 } .$ The formula in Definition 4.6.2 may be used to
calculate the probability of rolling four 6s, three 5s, two 4s, and one 3 to be

$$f \left( 0 , 0 , 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \right) = \frac { 1 0 ! } { 0 ! 0 ! 1 ! 2 ! 3 ! 4 ! } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { 2 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 0 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 1 } \left( \frac { 4 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 5 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { 6 } { 2 1 } \right) ^ { 4 }$$
$$= . 0 0 5 9 .$$

The factor $m ! / \left( x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdots x _ { n } ! \right)$ is called a multinomial coefficient. It is the number
of ways that $m$ objects can be divided into $n$ groups with $x _ { 1 }$ in the first group, $x _ { 2 }$
in the second group, ... , and $x _ { n }$ in the nth group. A generalization of the Binomial
Theorem 3.2.2 is the Multinomial Theorem.

Theorem 4.6.4 (Multinomial Theorem) Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers. Let $A$
be the set of vectors $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ such that each $x _ { i }$ is a nonnegative integer and
$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } = m .$ Then, for any real numbers $p _ { 1 } , \ldots , p _ { n } ,$

$$\left( p _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { n } \right) ^ { m } = \sum _ { \mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{A} } \frac { m ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n } ! } p _ { 1 } ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdot p _ { n } ^ { x _ { n } } .$$

Theorem 4.6.4 shows that a multinomial pmf sums to 1. The set $A$ is the set of
points with positive probability in Definition 4.6.2. The sum of the pmf over all those
points is, by Theorem 4.6.4, $\left( p _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { n } \right) ^ { m } = 1 ^ { m } = 1 .$

Now we consider some marginal and conditional distributions for the multinomial
model. Consider a single coordinate $X _ { i } .$ If the occurrence of the ith outcome is labeled
a "success" and anything else is labeled a "failure," then $X _ { i }$ is the count of the
number of successes in $m$ independent trials where the probability of a success is $p _ { i }$
on each trial. Thus $X _ { i }$ should have a binomial(m, $\left( m , p _ { i } \right)$ distribution. To verify this the
marginal distribution of $X _ { i }$ should be computed using (4.6.5). For example, consider
the marginal pmf of $X _ { n } .$ For a fixed value of $x _ { n } \in \left\{ 0 , 1 , \ldots , n \right\} ,$ to compute the
marginal pmf $f \left( x _ { n } \right) ,$ we must sum over all possible values of $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } \right) .$ That $\mathrm { i s } ,$
we must sum over all $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } \right)$ such that the $x _ { i } s$ are all nonnegative integers
and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } x _ { i } = m - x _ { n } .$ Denote this set by $\mathcal{B} .$ Then

$$f \left( x _ { n } \right) = \sum _ { \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } \right) \in \mathcal{B} } \frac { m ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n } ! } \left( p _ { 1 } \right) ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \left( p _ { n } \right) ^ { x _ { n } }$$
$$= \sum _ { \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } \right) \in \mathcal{B} ^ { \left[ 1 \right] } } \frac { m ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n } ! } p _ { 1 } ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdot p _ { n } ^ { x _ { n } } \frac { \left( m - x _ { n } \right) ! } { \left( m - x _ { n } \right) ! } \frac { \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) ^ { m - x _ { n } } } { \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) ^ { m - x _ { n } } }$$

!!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="182" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

$$= \frac { m ! } { x _ { n } ! \left( m - x _ { n } \right) ! } p _ { n } ^ { x _ { n } } \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) ^ { m - x _ { n } }$$
$$\times \sum _ { \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } \right) \in \mathcal{B} } \frac { \left( m - x _ { n } \right) ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n - 1 } ! } \left( \frac { p _ { 1 } } { 1 - p _ { n } } \right) ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdot \left( \frac { p _ { n - 1 } } { 1 - p _ { n } } \right) ^ { x _ { n - 1 } } .$$

But using the facts that $x _ { 1 } + \cdots + x _ { n - 1 } = m - x _ { n }$ and $p _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { n - 1 } = 1 - p _ { n }$ and
Theorem 4.6.4, we see that the last summation is 1. Hence the marginal distribution
of $X _ { n }$ is binomial(m, $\left( m , p _ { n } \right) .$ Similar arguments show that each of the other coordinates
is marginally binomially distributed.

Given that $X _ { n } = x _ { n } ,$ there must have been $m - x _ { n }$ trials that resulted in one of the
first $n \quad - \quad 1$ outcomes. The vector $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } \right)$ counts the number of these $m - x _ { n }$
trials that are of each type. Thus it seems that given $X _ { n } = x _ { n } ,$ $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } \right)$ might
have a multinomial distribution. This is true. From (4.6.6), the conditional pmf of
$\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } \right)$ given $X _ { n } = x _ { n }$ is

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 } | x _ { n } \right) = \frac { f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) } { f \left( x _ { n } \right) }$$
$$= \frac { m ! } { \frac { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots x _ { n ! } } { m ! } \left( p _ { n } \right) ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \left( p _ { n } \right) ^ { x _ { n } } }$$
$$= \frac { \left( m - x _ { n } \right) ! } { x _ { 1 } ! \cdots \cdot x _ { n - 1 } ! } \left( \frac { p _ { 1 } } { 1 - p _ { n } } \right) ^ { x _ { 1 } } \cdots \left( \frac { p _ { n - 1 } } { 1 - p _ { n } } \right) ^ { x _ { n - 1 } } .$$

This is the pmf of a multinomial distribution with $m - x _ { n }$ trials and cell probabilities
$p _ { 1 } / \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) , \ldots , p _ { n - 1 } / \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) .$ In fact, the conditional distribution of any subset of
the coordinates of $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ given the values of the rest of the coordinates is a
multinomial distribution.

We see from the conditional distributions that the coordinates of the vector $\left( X _ { 1 } \right. ,$
$\ldots , X _ { n }$ are related. In particular, there must be some negative correlation. It turns
out that all of the pairwise covariances are negative and are given by (Exercise 4.39)

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( X _ { i } , X _ { j } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left[ \left( X _ { i } - p _ { i } \right) \left( X _ { j } - p _ { j } \right) \right] = - m p _ { i } p _ { j } .$$

Thus, the negative correlation is greater for variables with higher success probabilities.
This makes sense, as the variable total is constrained at $m ,$ so if one starts to get big,
the other tends not to.

Definition 4.6.5 Let $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ be random vectors with joint pdf or pmf
$f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { x } _ { n } \right) .$ Let $f _ { X _ { i } } \left( x _ { i } \right)$ denote the marginal pdf or pmf of $X _ { i } .$ Then $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$
are called mutually independent random vectors if, for every $\left( \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { x } _ { n } \right) ,$

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = f _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) \cdots f _ { X _ { n } } \left( x _ { n } \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f _ { X _ { i } } \left( x _ { i } \right) .$$

If the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are all one-dimensional, then $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are called mutually independent
random variables.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="183" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIVARIATE DISTRIBUTIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are mutually independent, then knowledge about the values of some
coordinates gives us no information about the values of the other coordinates. Using
Definition 4.6.5, one can show that the conditional distribution of any subset of the
coordinates, given the values of the rest of the coordinates, is the same as the marginal
distribution of the subset. Mutual independence implies that any pair, say $X _ { i }$ and
$X _ { j } ,$ are pairwise independent. That is, the bivariate marginal pdf or pmf, $f \left( x _ { i } , x _ { j } \right) ,$
satisfies Definition 4.2.5. But mutual independence implies more than pairwise inde-
pendence. As in Example 1.3.11, it is possible to specify a probability distribution for
$\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ with the property that each pair, $\left( X _ { i } , X _ { j } \right) ,$ is pairwise independent but
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are not mutually independent.

Mutually independent random variables have many nice properties. The proofs of
the following theorems are analogous to the proofs of their counterparts in Sections
4.2 and 4.3.

Theorem 4.6.6 (Generalization of Theorem 4.2.10) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be mutu-
ally independent random variables. Let $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { n }$ be real-valued functions such that
$g _ { i } \left( x _ { i } \right)$ is $a$ function only of $x _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n .$ Then

$$\mathrm { E } \left( g _ { 1 } \left( X _ { 1 } \right) \cdots \cdot g _ { n } \left( X _ { n } \right) \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } g _ { 1 } \left( X _ { 1 } \right) \right) \cdots \cdot \left( \mathrm { E } g _ { n } \left( X _ { n } \right) \right) .$$

Theorem 4.6.7 (Generalization of Theorem 4.2.12) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be mutu-
ally independent random variables with mgfs $M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( t \right) , \ldots , M _ { X _ { n } } \left( t \right) .$ Let $Z = X _ { 1 } + \cdots +$
$X _ { n } .$ Then the mgf of Z is

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( t \right) \cdots \cdot M _ { X _ { n } } \left( t \right) .$$

In particular, if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ all have the same distribution with mgf $M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ then

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = \left( M _ { X } \left( t \right) \right) ^ { n } .$$

Example 4.6.8 (Mgf of a sum of gamma variables) Suppose $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are
mutually independent random variables, and the distribution of $X _ { i }$ is $\mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha _ { i } , \beta \right) .$
From Example 2.3.8, the mgf of a gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution is $M \left( t \right) = \left( 1 - \beta t \right) ^ { - \alpha } .$
Thus, if $Z = X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n } ,$ the mgf of $Z$ is

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( t \right) \cdots \cdots M _ { X _ { n } } \left( t \right) = \left( 1 - \beta t \right) ^ { - \alpha _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdots \left( 1 - \beta t \right) ^ { - \alpha _ { n } } = \left( 1 - \beta t \right) ^ { - \left( \alpha _ { 1 } + \cdots + \alpha _ { n } \right) } .$$

This is the mgf of a gamma(a1 $\left( \alpha _ { 1 } + \cdots + \alpha _ { n } , \beta \right)$ distribution. Thus, the sum of inde-
pendent gamma random variables that have a common scale parameter $\beta$ also has a
gamma distribution.

A generalization of Theorem 4.6.7 is obtained if we consider a sum of linear functions
of independent random variables.

Corollary 4.6.9 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be mutually independent random variables with
mgfs $M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( t \right) , \ldots , M _ { X _ { n } } \left( t \right) .$ Let $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ and $b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n }$ be fixed constants. Let $Z =$
$\left( a _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } + b _ { 1 } \right) + \cdots + \left( a _ { n } X _ { n } + b _ { n } \right) .$ Then the mgf of $Z$ is

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = \left( e ^ { t \left( \Sigma b _ { i } \right) } \right) M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( a _ { 1 } t \right) \cdots \cdot M _ { X _ { n } } \left( a _ { n } t \right) .$$

!!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="184" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MULTIPLE RANDOM VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 4.6" -->

Proof: From the definition, the mgf of Z is

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = E e ^ { t Z }$$
$$= E e ^ { t \Sigma \left( a _ { i } X _ { i } + b _ { i } \right) }$$
$$= \left( e ^ { t \left( \Sigma b _ { i } \right) } \right) \mathrm { E } \left( e ^ { t a _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } } \cdots \cdot e ^ { t a _ { n } X _ { n } } \right) \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \text { properties of expontials } \\ \mathrm { a n d } \mathrm { e x p e c t a t i o n s } \end{array} \right)$$
$$= \left( e ^ { t \left( \Sigma b _ { i } \right) } \right) M _ { X _ { 1 } } \left( a _ { 1 } t \right) \cdots \cdot M _ { X _ { n } } \left( a _ { n } t \right) ,$$
(Theorem 4.6.6)

as was to be shown.

☐

Undoubtedly, the most important application of Corollary 4.6.9 is to the case of
normal random variables. A linear combination of independent normal random vari-
$a b l e s$ is normally distributed.

Corollary 4.6.10 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be mutually independent random variables with
$X _ { i } \sim n \left( \mu _ { i } , \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) .$ Let $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ and $b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n }$ be fixed constants. Then

$$Z = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( a _ { i } X _ { i } + b _ { i } \right) \sim n \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( a _ { i } \mu _ { i } + b _ { i } \right) , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Proof: Recall that the mgf of a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ random variable is $M \left( t \right) = e ^ { \mu t + \sigma ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } / 2 } .$
Substituting into the expression in Corollary 4.6.9 yields

$$M _ { Z } \left( t \right) = \left( e ^ { t \left( \Sigma b _ { i } \right) } \right) e ^ { \mu _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } t + \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } / 2 } \cdots \cdot e ^ { \mu _ { n } a _ { n } t + \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } a _ { n } ^ { 2 } t ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$
$$= e ^ { \left( \left( \Sigma \left( a _ { i } \mu _ { i } + b _ { i } \right) \right) t + \left( \Sigma a _ { i } ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) t ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) } .$$

the mgf of the indicated normal distribution.

Theorem 4.6.11 (Generalization of Lemma 4.2.7) Let $\mathrm { X i } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ be random
vectors. Then $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ are mutually independent random vectors if and only $i f$
there exist functions $g _ { i } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ such that the joint pdf or pmf $f \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$
can be written as

$$f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { x } _ { n } \right) = g _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } \right) \cdots \cdot g _ { n } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { n } \right) .$$

Theorem 4.6.12 (Generalization of Theorem 4.3.5) Let $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ be inde-
pendent random vectors. Let $g _ { i } \left( x _ { i } \right)$ be $a$ function only of $\mathrm { x } _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , \cdots , n .$ Then the
random variables $U _ { i } = g _ { i } \left( X _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ are mutually independent.

We close this section by describing the generalization of a technique for finding the
distribution of a transformation of a random vector. We will present the generalization
of formula (4.3.6) that gives the pdf of the new random vector in terms of the pdf
of the original random vector. Note that to fully understand the remainder of this
section, some knowledge of matrix algebra is required. (See, for example, Searle 1982.)
In particular, we will need to compute the determinant of a matrix. This is the only
place in the book where such knowledge is required.

Let $\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ be a random vector with pdf $f _ { \mathrm { X } } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$ Let $A = \left\{ x : f _ { X } \left( x \right) > \right.$
0}. Consider a new random vector $\left( U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { n } \right) ,$ defined by $U _ { 1 } = g _ { 1 } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) , U _ { 2 } =$
$g _ { 2 } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) , \ldots , U _ { n } = g _ { n } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) .$ Suppose that $A _ { 0 } , A _ { 1 } , \ldots , A _ { k }$ form a parti-
tion of $\mathcal{A}$ with these properties. The set $A _ { 0 } ,$ which may be empty, satisfies
$P \left( \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) \in A _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$ The transformation $\left( U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { n } \right) = \left( g _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { X } \right) , \ldots , g _ { n } \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right)$
is a one-to-one transformation from $A _ { i }$ onto $\mathcal{B}$ for each $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , k .$ Then for
each $i ,$ the inverse functions from $\mathcal{B}$ to $A _ { i }$ can be found. Denote the $i$ ith inverse by
$x _ { 1 } = h _ { 1 i } \left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) ,$ $x _ { 2 } = h _ { 2 i } \left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) , \ldots , x _ { n } = h _ { n i } \left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) .$ This ith inverse
gives, for $\left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) \in \mathcal{B} ,$ the unique $\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \in A _ { i }$ such that $\left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) =$
$\left( g _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) , \ldots , g _ { n } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \right) .$ Let $J _ { i }$ denote the Jacobian computed from the
ith inverse. That is,

the determinant of an $n \times n$ matrix. Assuming that these Jacobians do not vanish
identically on $\mathcal{B} ,$ we have the following representation of the joint pdf, $f _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) ,$
for $u \in \mathcal{B} :$
