<!-- PageNumber="218" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->


## 5.3 Sampling from the Normal Distribution

This section deals with the properties of sample quantities drawn from a normal
population-still one of the most widely used statistical models. Sampling from a
normal population leads to many useful properties of sample statistics and also to
many well-known sampling distributions.


### 5.3.1 Properties of the Sample Mean and Variance

We have already seen how to calculate the means and variances of $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ in
general. Now, under the additional assumption of normality, we can derive their full
distributions, and more. The properties of $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are summarized in the following
theorem.

Theorem 5.3.1 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ distribution,
and let $\bar { X } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$ and $S ^ { 2 } = \left[ 1 / \left( n - 1 \right) \right] \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } .$ Then

a. $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are independent random variables,
☒

b. $\bar { X }$ has a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right)$ distribution,

c. $\left( n - 1 \right) S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 }$ has a $c h i$ squared distribution with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom.

Proof: First note that, from Section 3.5 on location-scale families, we can assume,
without loss of generality, that $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1 .$ (Also see the discussion preceding
Theorem 5.2.11.) Furthermore, part (b) has already been established in Example 5.2.8,
leaving us to prove parts (a) and (c).

To prove part (a) we will apply Theorem 4.6.12, and show that $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are
functions of independent random vectors. Note that we can write $S ^ { 2 }$ as a function of
$n \quad - \quad 1$ deviations as follows:

$$S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - X \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left( \left( X _ { 1 } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left( \left[ \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X \right) \right.$$
since
\- X) = 0)

Thus, $S ^ { 2 }$ can be written as a function only of $\left( X _ { 2 } - \bar { X } , \ldots , X _ { n } - \bar { X } \right) .$ We will now
show that these random variables are independent of $\bar { X } .$ The joint pdf of the sample
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is given by

$$f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n / 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \Sigma _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } } , \quad - \infty < x _ { i } < \infty .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="219" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SAMPLING FROM THE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

Make the transformation

$$y _ { 1 } = \bar { x } ,$$
$$y _ { 2 } = x _ { 2 } - \bar { x } ,$$

ǀ

$$y _ { n } = x _ { n } - \bar { x } .$$

This is a linear transformation with a Jacobian equal to $1 / n .$ We have

$$f \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } \right)$$
$$= \frac { n } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n / 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \left( y _ { 1 } - \Sigma _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } y _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \Sigma _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } + y _ { 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } } , - \infty < y _ { i } < \infty$$
$$= \left[ \left( \frac { n } { 2 \pi } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } e ^ { \left( - n y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) / 2 } \right] \left[ \frac { n ^ { 1 / 2 } } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { \left( n - 1 \right) / 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \left[ \Sigma _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } y _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \left( \Sigma _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } y _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] } \right] , - \infty < y _ { i } <$$
00.

Since the joint pdf of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ factors, it follows from Theorem 4.6.11 that $Y _ { 1 }$ is
independent of $Y _ { 2 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ and, hence, from Theorem 4.6.12 that $\bar { X }$ is independent
of $S ^ { 2 } .$
☐

To finish the proof of the theorem we must now derive the distribution of $S ^ { 2 } .$ Before
doing so, however, we digress a little and discuss the chi squared distribution, whose
properties play an important part in the derivation of the pdf of $S ^ { 2 } .$ Recall from
Section 3.3 that the chi squared pdf is a special case of the gamma pdf and is given
by

$$f \left( x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( p / 2 \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } } x ^ { \left( p / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } , \quad 0 < x < \infty ,$$

where $p$ is called the degrees of freedom. We now summarize some pertinent facts
about the chi squared distribution.

Lemma 5.3.2 (Facts about chi squared random variables) We use the nota-
tion $x _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ to denote $a$ chi squared random variable with $p$ degrees of freedom.

a. If $Z$ is a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable, then $Z ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ that is, the square of a standard
normal random variable is a chi squared random variable.

b. If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are independent and $X _ { i } \sim \chi _ { p _ { i } } ^ { 2 } ,$ then $X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n } \sim \chi _ { p _ { 1 } + \cdots + p _ { n } } ^ { 2 }$
that $i s ,$ independent chi squared variables add to a $c h i$ squared variable, and the
degrees of freedom also add.

Proof: We have encountered these facts already. Part (a) was established in Exam-
ple 2.1.7. Part (b) is a special case of Example 4.6.8, which has to do with sums of
independent gamma random variables. Since a $\chi _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ random variable is a $g a m m a \left( p / 2 , 2 \right) ,$
application of the example gives part (b).
☐

Proof of Theorem 5.3.1(c): We will employ an induction argument to establish
the distribution of $S ^ { 2 } ,$ using the notation $\bar { X } _ { k }$ and $S _ { k } ^ { 2 }$ to denote the sample mean
and variance based on the first $k$ observations. (Note that the actual ordering of the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="220" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

observations is immaterial-we are just considering them to be ordered to facilitate
the proof.) It is straightforward to establish (see Exercise 5.15) that

(5.3.1)
$$\left( n - 1 \right) S _ { n } ^ { 2 } = \left( n - 2 \right) S _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n } \right) \left( X _ { n } - \bar { X } _ { n - 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Now consider $n =$ 2. Defining $0 \times S _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$ we have from (5.3.1) that

$$S _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( X _ { 2 } - X _ { 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$ ☒

Since the distribution of $\left( X _ { 2 } - X _ { 1 } \right) / \sqrt { 2 }$ is $n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ part (a) of Lemma 5.3.2 shows that
☒ ☒

☒
$S _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } .$ Proceeding with the induction, we assume that for $n = k ,$ $\left( k - 1 \right) S _ { k } ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { k - 1 } ^ { 2 } .$
For $n = k + 1$ we have from (5.3.1)

(5.3.2)
$$k S _ { k + 1 } ^ { 2 } = \left( k - 1 \right) S _ { k } ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { k } { k + 1 } \right) \left( X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$ ☒

According to the induction hypothesis, $\left( k - 1 \right) S _ { k } ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { k - 1 } ^ { 2 } .$ If we can establish that
$\left( k / \left( k + 1 \right) \right) \left( X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ,$ independent of $S _ { k } ^ { 2 } ,$ it will follow from part (b) of
Lemma 5.3.2 that $k S _ { k + 1 } ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { k } ^ { 2 } ,$ and the theorem will be proved.
☒ ☒ ☒

The independence of $\left( X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 }$ and $S _ { k } ^ { 2 }$ again follows from Theorem 4.6.12.
☒ ☒

The vector $\left( X _ { k + 1 } , \bar { X } _ { k } \right)$ is independent of $S _ { k } ^ { 2 }$ and so is any function of the vector.
☒ ☒

Furthermore, $X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k }$ is a normal random variable with mean 0 and variance
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k } \right) = \frac { k + 1 } { k } ,$$ ☒ ☒

and therefore $\left( k / \left( k + 1 \right) \right) \left( X _ { k + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ,$ and the theorem is established.

☒ ☒ ☐

The independence of $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ can be established in a manner different from that
☒
used in the proof of Theorem 5.3.1. Rather than show that the joint pdf factors, $\mathrm { W P }$
can use the following lemma, which ties together independence and correlation for
normal samples.

Lemma 5.3.3 Let $X _ { j } \sim n \left( \mu _ { j } , \sigma _ { j } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ independent. For constants $a _ { i j }$ and
$b _ { r j }$ (j = 1, ... , n; i = 1, ... , k; $\left. r = 1 , \ldots , m \right) ,$ where $k + m \leq n ,$ define

$$U _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } X _ { j } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , k ,$$
$$V _ { r } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } X _ { j } , \quad r = 1 , \ldots , m .$$ ☒

a. The random variables $U _ { i }$ and $V _ { r }$ are independent if and only if $\mathrm { C o v } \left( U _ { i } , V _ { r } \right) = 0 .$
☒
Furthermore, $\mathrm { C o v } \left( U _ { i } , V _ { r } \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } b _ { r j } \sigma _ { j } ^ { 2 } .$

b. The random vectors $\left( U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { k } \right)$ and $\left( V _ { 1 } , \ldots , V _ { m } \right)$ are independent $i f$ and only if
$U _ { i }$ is independent $V _ { r }$ pairs $i ,$ $r \left( i = 1 , \ldots , k ; r = 1 , \ldots , m \right) .$

Proof: It is sufficient to prove the lemma for $\mu _ { i } = 0$ and $\sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } = 1 ,$ since the general
statement of the lemma then follows quickly. Furthermore, the implication from in-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="221" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SAMPLING FROM THE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

dependence to 0 covariance is immediate (Theorem 4.5.5) and the expression for the
covariance is easily verified (Exercise 5.14). Note also that Corollary 4.6.10 shows that
$U _ { i }$ and $V _ { r }$ are normally distributed.

Thus, we are left with proving that if the constants satisfy the above restriction
(equivalently, the covariance is 0), then we have independence under normality. We
prove the lemma only for $n = 2 ,$ since the proof for general $n$ is similar but necessitates
a detailed n-variate transformation.

To prove part (a) start with the joint pdf of $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 } ,$

$$f _ { X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } } \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) } , \quad - \infty < x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } < \infty .$$

Make the transformation (we can suppress the double subscript in the $\left. n = 2 \quad c a s e \right)$

$$u = a _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } , \quad v = b _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } ,$$

SO

$$x _ { 1 } = \frac { b _ { 2 } u - a _ { 2 } v } { a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } , \quad x _ { 2 } = \frac { a _ { 1 } v - b _ { 1 } u } { a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } }$$

with Jacobian

$$J = | \begin{array}{} { \frac { \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial v } } \\ { \frac { \partial x _ { 2 } } { \partial u } } & { \frac { \partial x _ { 2 } } { \partial v } } \end{array} | = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } }$$

Thus, the pdf of $U$ and $V$ is

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = f _ { X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } } \left( \frac { b _ { 2 } u - a _ { 2 } v } { a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } , \frac { a _ { 1 } v - b _ { 1 } u } { a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } \right) | J |$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \exp \left\{ \frac { - 1 } { 2 \left( a _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } - b _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \left[ \left( b _ { 2 } u - a _ { 2 } v \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( a _ { 1 } v - b _ { 1 } u \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \right\} | J | ,$$

$- \infty < u ,$ $v < \infty .$ Expanding the squares in the exponent, we can write

$$\left( b _ { 2 } u - a _ { 2 } v \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( a _ { 1 } v - b _ { 1 } u \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) u ^ { 2 } + \left( a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) v ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( a _ { 1 } b _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } b _ { 2 } \right) u v .$$

The assumption on the constants shows that the cross-term is identically 0. Hence, the
pdf factors $s o ,$ by Lemma 4.2.7, $U$ and $V$ are independent and part (a) is established.

$A$ similar type of argument will work for part (b), the details of which we will
not go into. If the appropriate transformation is made, the joint pdf of the vectors
$\left( U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { k } \right)$ and $\left( V _ { 1 } , \ldots , V _ { m } \right)$ can be obtained. By an application of Theorem 4.6.11,
the vectors are independent if the joint pdf factors. From the form of the normal pdf,
this will happen if and only if $U _ { i }$ is independent of $V _ { r } ,$ for all pairs $i ,$ $r \left( i = 1 , \ldots , k \right.$ $r =$
$1 , \ldots , m .$
☐

This lemma shows that, if we start with independent normal random variables,
covariance and independence are equivalent for linear functions of these random vari-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="222" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

ables. Thus, we can check independence for normal variables by merely checking the
covariance term, a much simpler calculation. There is nothing magic about this; it
just follows from the form of the normal pdf. Furthermore, part (b) allows us to in-
fer overall independence of normal vectors by just checking pairwise independence, a
property that does not hold for general random variables.

We can use Lemma 5.3.3 to provide an alternative proof of the independence of $\overrightarrow { \mathrm { X } }$
and $S ^ { 2 }$ in normal sampling. Since we can write $S ^ { 2 }$ as a function of $n \quad - \quad 1$ deviations
$\left( X _ { 2 } - \bar { X } , \ldots , X _ { n } - \bar { X } \right) ,$ we must show that these random variables are uncorrelated
with $\bar { X } .$ The normality assumption, together with Lemma 5.3.3, will then allow us to
conclude independence.

As an illustration of the application of Lemma 5.3.3, write

$$\bar { X } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \right) X _ { i } ,$$
$$X _ { j } - X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \delta _ { i j } - \frac { 1 } { n } \right) X _ { i } ,$$

where $\delta _ { i j } = 1$ if $i = j$ and $\delta _ { i j } = 0$ otherwise. It is then easy to show that

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \bar { X } , X _ { j } - \bar { X } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \right) \left( \delta _ { i j } - \frac { 1 } { n } \right) = 0 ,$$

showing that $\bar { X }$ and $X _ { j } - \bar { X }$ are independent (as long as the $X _ { i } s$ have the same
variance).


### 5.3.2 The Derived Distributions: Student's $t$ and Snedecor's $F$

The distributions derived in Section 5.3.1 are, in a sense, the first $\mathrm { s t e p }$ in a statistical
analysis that assumes normality. In particular, in most practical cases the variance,
$\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ is unknown. Thus, to get any idea of the variability of $\bar { X }$ (as an estimate of $\mu ,$ it
is necessary to estimate this variance. This topic was first addressed by $\mathrm { W } .$ S. Gosset
(who published under the pseudonym of Student) in the early 1900s. The landmark
work of Student resulted in Student's $t$ distribution or, more simply, the $t$ distribution.

If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ we know that the quantity

(5.3.3)
$$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } }$$

is distributed as a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable. If we knew the value of $\sigma$ and we measured
$\bar { X } ,$ then we could use (5.3.3) as a basis for inference about $\mu ,$ since $\mu$ would then $b e$
the only unknown quantity. Most of the time, however, $\sigma$ is unknown. Student $\mathrm { d i d }$
the obvious thing- he looked at the distribution of

(5.3.4)
$$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { \bar { S } / \sqrt { n } }$$

a quantity that could be used as a basis for inference about $\mu$ when $\sigma$ was unknown.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="223" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SAMPLING FROM THE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

The distribution of (5.3.4) is easy to derive, provided that we first notice $a$ few
simplifying maneuvers. Multiply (5.3.4) by $\sigma / \sigma$ and rearrange slightly to obtain

(5.3.5)
$$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { S / \sqrt { n } } = \frac { \left( \bar { X } - \mu \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) } { \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } } } .$$

The numerator of (5.3.5) is a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable, and the denominator is
$\sqrt { x _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 1 \right) } ,$ independent of the numerator. Thus, the distribution of (5.3.4) can
be found by solving the simplified problem of finding the distribution of $U / \sqrt { V / p } ,$
☒
where $U$ is $n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $V$ is $\chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } ,$ and $U$ and $V$ are independent. This gives us Student's $t$
distribution.

Definition 5.3.4 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ distribution.
The quantity $\left( \bar { X } - \mu \right) / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right)$ has Student's t distribution with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of free-
dom. Equivalently, a random variable $T$ has Student's $t$ distribution with $p \quad d e g r e e s$
of freedom, and we write $T \sim t _ { p }$ if it has pdf

$$f _ { T } \left( t \right) = \frac { \Gamma \left( \frac { p + 1 } { 2 } \right) } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) } \frac { 1 } { \left( p \pi \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } / p \right) ^ { \left( p + 1 \right) / 2 } } , \quad - \infty < t < \infty .$$
(5.3.6)

Notice that if $p = 1 ,$ then (5.3.6) becomes the pdf of the Cauchy distribution, which
occurs for samples of size 2. Once again the Cauchy distribution has appeared in an
ordinary situation.

The derivation of the $t \mathrm { p d f }$ is straightforward. If we start with $U$ and $V$ defined
above, it follows from (5.3.5) that the joint pdf of $U$ and $V$ is

$$f _ { U , V } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } e ^ { - u ^ { 2 } / 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } } v ^ { \left( p / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - v / 2 } , \quad - \infty < \infty , \quad 0 < v < \infty .$$

(Recall that $U$ and $V$ are independent.) Now make the transformation

$$t = \frac { u } { \sqrt { v / p } } , \quad w = v$$

The Jacobian of the transformation is $\left( w / p \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,$ and the marginal $\mathrm { p d f }$ of $T$ is given
by

$$f _ { T } \left( t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } f _ { U , V } \left( t \left( \frac { w } { p } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } , w \right) \left( \frac { w } { p } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } d w$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) t ^ { 2 } w / p } w ^ { \left( p / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - w / 2 } \left( \frac { w } { p } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } d w$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } p ^ { 1 / 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } / p \right) w } w ^ { \left( \left( p + 1 \right) / 2 \right) - 1 } d w .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="224" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

Recognize the integrand as the kernel of a $\mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \left( p + 1 \right) / 2 , 2 / \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } / p \right) \right)$ pdf. We
therefore have

$$f _ { T } \left( t \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) 2 ^ { p / 2 } p ^ { 1 / 2 } } \Gamma \left( \frac { p + 1 } { 2 } \right) \left[ \frac { 2 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } / p } \right] ^ { \left( p + 1 \right) / 2 } ,$$

which is equal to (5.3.6).

Student's $t$ has no mgf because it does not have moments of all orders. In fact, if
there are $p$ degrees of freedom, then there are only $p \quad - \quad 1$ moments. Hence, a $t _ { 1 }$ has
no mean, a $t _ { 2 }$ has no variance, etc. It is easy to check (see Exercise 5.18) that if $T _ { p }$ is
a random variable with $a$ $t _ { p }$ distribution, then

$$E T _ { p } = 0 , \quad \text { if } p > 1 ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } T _ { p } = \frac { p } { p - 2 } , \quad \text { if } p > 2 .$$
(5.3.7)

Another important derived distribution is Snedecor's $F ,$ whose derivation is quite
similar to that of Student's $t$ t. Its motivation, however, is somewhat different. The $F$
distribution, named in honor of Sir Ronald Fisher, arises naturally as the distribution
of a ratio of variances.

Example 5.3.5 (Variance ratio distribution) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sam-
ple from a $n \left( \mu _ { X } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, and let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ be a random sample from an
independent $n \left( \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. If we were interested in comparing the variability
of the populations, one quantity of interest would be the ratio $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } .$ Information
about this ratio is contained in $S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ the ratio of sample variances. The $F$ distri-
bution allows us to compare these quantities by giving us a distribution of

(5.3.8)
$$\frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } }$$

Examination of (5.3.8) shows us how the $F$ distribution is derived. The ratios $S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 }$
and $S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 }$ are each scaled chi squared variates, and they are independent.
I

Definition 5.3.6 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu _ { X } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right)$ population,
and let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ be a random sample from an independent $n \left( \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right)$ population.
The random variable $F = \left( S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right)$ has Snedecor's $F$ distribution with
$n \quad - \quad 1$ and $m \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom. Equivalently, the random variable $F$ has the $F$
distribution with $p$ and $q$ degrees of freedom if it has pdf

$$\left( 5 . 3 . 9 \right) \quad f _ { F } \left( x \right) = \frac { \Gamma \left( \frac { p + q } { 2 } \right) } { \Gamma \left( \frac { p } { 2 } \right) \Gamma \left( \frac { q } { 2 } \right) } \left( \frac { p } { q } \right) ^ { p / 2 } \frac { x ^ { \left( p / 2 \right) - 1 } } { \left[ 1 + \left( p / q \right) x \right] ^ { \left( p + q \right) / 2 } } , \quad 0 < x < \infty .$$

The $F$ distribution can be derived in a more general setting than is done here.
$A$ variance ratio may have an $F$ distribution even if the parent populations are not
normal. Kelker (1970) has shown that as long as the parent populations have a cer-
tain type of symmetry (spherical symmetry), then the variance ratio will have an $F$
distribution.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="225" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SAMPLING FROM THE NORMAL DISTRIBUTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.3" -->

The derivation of the $F$ pdf, starting from normal distributions, is similar to the
derivation of Student's $t .$ In fact, in one special case the $F$ is a transform of the t. (See
Theorem 5.3.8.) Similar to what we did for the $t ,$ we can reduce the task of deriving
the $F$ pdf to that of finding the pdf of $\left( U / p \right) / \left( V / q \right) ,$ where $U$ and $V$ are independent,
$U \sim \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ and $V \sim \chi _ { q } ^ { 2 } .$ (See Exercise 5.17.)

Example 5.3.7 (Continuation of Example 5.3.5) To see how the $F$ distribution
may be used for inference about the true ratio of population variances, consider the
following. The quantity $\left( S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right)$ has an $F _ { n - 1 , m - 1 }$ distribution. (In general,
we use the notation $F _ { p , q }$ to denote an $F$ random variable with $p$ and $q$ degrees of
freedom.) We can calculate

$$E F _ { n - 1 , m - 1 } = E \left( \frac { \chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 1 \right) } { \chi _ { m - 1 } ^ { 2 } / \left( m - 1 \right) } \right)$$
$$= E \left( \frac { \chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } \right) E \left( \frac { m - 1 } { \chi _ { m - 1 } ^ { 2 } } \right)$$

(by definition)
(independence)

$$= \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n - 1 } \right) \left( \frac { m - 1 } { m - 3 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { m - 1 } { m - 3 }$$

Note that this last expression is finite and positive only if $m > 3 .$ We have that

$$E \left( \frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } \right) = E F _ { n - 1 , m - 1 } = \frac { m - 1 } { m - 3 }$$

and, removing expectations, we have for reasonably large $m ,$

$$\frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } / S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } \approx \frac { m - 1 } { m - 3 } \approx 1 ,$$

as we might expect.

Il

The $F$ distribution has many interesting properties and is related to a number of
other distributions. We summarize some of these facts in the next theorem, whose
proof is left as an exercise. (See Exercises 5.17 and 5.18.)


## Theorem 5.3.8

a. If $X \sim F _ { p , q } ,$ then $1 / X \sim F _ { q , p }$ that is, the reciprocal of an $F$ random variable is
again an $F$ random variable.

b. If $X \sim t _ { q } ,$ then $X ^ { 2 } \sim F _ { 1 , q } .$

c. If $X \sim F _ { p , q } ,$ then $\left( p / q \right) X / \left( 1 + \left( p / q \right) X \right) \sim b e t a \left( p / 2 , q / 2 \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="226" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->


### 5.4 Order Statistics

Sample values such as the smallest, largest, or middle observation from a random
sample can provide additional summary information. For example, the highest flood
waters or the lowest winter temperature recorded during the last 50 years might be
useful data when planning for future emergencies. The median price of houses sold
during the previous month might be useful for estimating the cost of living. These
are all examples of order statistics.

Definition 5.4.1 The order statistics of a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are the sample
values placed in ascending order. They are denoted by $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) } .$

The order statistics are random variables that satisfy $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \leq \cdots \leq X _ { \left( n \right) } .$ In partic-
ular,

$$X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = \min _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } X _ { i } ,$$
$$X _ { \left( 2 \right) } = \text { second smallest } X _ { i } ,$$

:

$$X _ { \left( n \right) } = \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } X _ { i } .$$

Since they are random variables, we can discuss the probabilities that they take on
various values. To calculate these probabilities we need the pdfs or pmfs of the order
statistics. The formulas for the pdfs of the order statistics of a random sample from
a continuous population will be the main topic later in this section, but first, we will
mention some statistics that are easily defined in terms of the order statistics.

The sample range, $R = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ is the distance between the smallest and largest
observations. It is a measure of the dispersion in the sample and should reflect the
dispersion in the population.

The sample median, which we will denote by $M ,$ is a number such that approxi-
mately one-half of the observations are less than $M$ and one-half are greater. In terms
of the order statistics, $M$ is defined by

(5.4.1)
$$M = \left\{ \begin{array}{} X _ { \left( \left( n + 1 \right) / 2 \right) } & \text { if } n \text { is odd } \\ \left( X _ { \left( n / 2 \right) } + X _ { \left( n / 2 + 1 \right) } \right) / 2 & \text { if } n \text { is even } . \end{array} \right.$$

The median is a measure of location that might be considered an alternative to the
sample mean. One advantage of the sample median over the sample mean is that it
is less affected by extreme observations. (See Section 10.2 for details.)

Although related, the mean and median usually measure different things. For exam-
ple, in recent baseball salary negotiations a major point of contention was the owners'
contributions to the players' pension fund. The owners' view could be paraphrased as,
"The average baseball player's annual salary is $433,659 so, with that kind of money,
the current pension is adequate." But the players' view was, "Over half of the players
make less than $250,000 annually and, because of the short professional life of most

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="227" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ORDER STATISTICS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->

players, need the security of a larger pension." (These figures are for the 1988 season,
not the year of the dispute.) Both figures were correct, but the owners were discussing
the mean while the players were discussing the median. About a dozen players with
salaries over $2 million can raise the average salary to $433,659 while the majority of
the players make less than $250,000, including all rookies who make $62,500. When
discussing salaries, prices, or any variable with a few extreme values, the median gives
a better indication of "typical" values than the mean. Other statistics that can be
defined in terms of order statistics and are less sensitive to extreme values (such as
the a-trimmed mean discussed in Exercise 10.20) are discussed in texts such as Tukey
(1977).

For any number $p$ between 0 and 1, the (100p)th sample percentile is the observation
such that approximately $n p$ of the observations are less than this observation and
$n \left( 1 \quad - \quad p \right)$ of the observations are greater. The 50th sample percentile $\left( p = . 5 \right)$ is
the sample median. For other values of $p ,$ we can more precisely define the sample
percentiles in terms of the order statistics in the following way.

Definition 5.4.2 The notation $b ,$ when appearing in a subscript, is defined to be
the number $b$ rounded to the nearest integer in the usual way. More precisely, if $i$ is
an integer and $i - . 5 \leq b < i + . 5 ,$ then $b = i .$

The $\left( 1 0 0 p \right) t h$ sample percentile is $X _ { \left( \left\{ n p \right\} \right) }$ if $\frac { 1 } { 2 n } < p < . 5$ and $X _ { \left( n + 1 - \left\{ n \left( 1 - p \right) \right\} \right) }$ if
$. 5 < p < 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 n } .$ For example, if $n = 1 2$ and the 65th percentile is wanted, we note
that $1 2 \times \left( 1 - . 6 5 \right) = 4 . 2$ and $1 2 + 1 \quad - \quad 4 = 9 .$ Thus the 65th percentile is $X _ { \left( 9 \right) } .$ There
is a restriction on the range of $p$ because the size of the sample limits the range of
sample percentiles.

The cases $p < . 5$ and $p > . 5$ are defined separately so that the sample percentiles
exhibit the following symmetry. If the (100p)th sample percentile is the $i$ ith smallest
observation, then the $\left( 1 0 0 \left( 1 \quad - p \right) \right) t h$ sample percentile should be the $i$ ith largest obser-
vation and the above definition achieves this. For example, if $n = 1 1 ,$ the 30th sample
percentile is $X _ { \left( 3 \right) }$ and the 70th sample percentile is $X _ { \left( 9 \right) } .$

In addition to the median, two other sample percentiles are commonly identified.
These are the lower quartile (25th percentile) and upper quartile (75th percentile). A
measure of dispersion that is sometimes used is the interquartile range, the distance
between the lower and upper quartiles.

Since the order statistics are functions of the sample, probabilities concerning order
statistics can be computed in terms of probabilities for the sample. If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
are iid discrete random variables, then the calculation of probabilities for the order
statistics is mainly a counting task. These formulas are derived in Theorem 5.4.3.
If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are a random sample from a continuous population, then convenient
expressions for the pdf of one or more order statistics are derived in Theorems 5.4.4
and 5.4.6. These can then be used to derive the distribution of functions of the order
statistics.

Theorem 5.4.3 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a discrete distribution
with pmf $f _ { X } \left( x _ { i } \right) = p _ { i } ,$ where $x _ { 1 } < x _ { 2 } < \cdots .$ are the possible values of $X$ in ascending

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="228" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->

order. Define

$$P _ { 0 } = 0$$
$$P _ { 1 } = p _ { 1 }$$
$$P _ { 2 } = p _ { 1 } + p _ { 2 }$$
$$P _ { i } = p _ { 1 } + p _ { 2 } + \cdots + p _ { i }$$
:

:

Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) }$ denote the order statistics from the sample. Then

(5.4.2)
$$P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) P _ { i } ^ { k } \left( 1 - P _ { i } \right) ^ { n - k }$$

and

$$P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } = x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) \left[ P _ { i } ^ { k } \left( 1 - P _ { i } \right) ^ { n - k } - P _ { i - 1 } ^ { k } \left( 1 - P _ { i - 1 } \right) ^ { n - k } \right] .$$
(5.4.3)

Proof: $\mathrm { F i x } i ,$ and let $Y$ be a random variable that counts the number of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
that are less than or equal to $x _ { i } .$ For each of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ call the event $\left\{ X _ { j } \leq x _ { i } \right\}$
"success" and $\left\{ X _ { j } > x _ { i } \right\}$ a "failure." Then $Y$ is the number of successes in $n$ trials.
The probability of a success is the same value, namely $P _ { i } = P \left( X _ { j } \leq x _ { i } \right) ,$ for each
trial, since $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are identically distributed. The success or failure of the $j \mathrm { t h }$
trial is independent of the outcome of any other trial, since $X _ { j }$ is independent of the
other $X _ { i } s .$ Thus, $Y \sim \text { binomial } \left( n , P _ { i } \right) .$

The event $\left\{ X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { i } \right\}$ is equivalent to the event $\left\{ Y \geq j \right\}$ that is, at least $j$ of the
☒
sample values are less than or equal to $x _ { i } .$ Equation (5.4.2) expresses this binomial
probability,

$$P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { i } \right) = P \left( Y \geq j \right) .$$

Equation (5.4.3) simply expresses the difference,

$$P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } = x _ { i } \right) = P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { i } \right) - P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { i - 1 } \right) .$$

The case $i = 1$ is exceptional in that $P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } = x _ { 1 } \right) = P \left( X _ { \left( j \right) } \leq x _ { 1 } \right) .$ The definition
of $P _ { 0 } = 0$ takes care of this exception in (5.4.3).
☐

If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are a random sample from a continuous population, then the situation
is simplified slightly by the fact that the probability is 0 that any two $X _ { j } s$ are equal,
freeing us from worrying about ties. Thus $P \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } < X _ { \left( 2 \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( n \right) } \right) = 1$ and the
sample space for $\left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) } \right)$ is $\left\{ \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) : x _ { 1 } < x _ { 2 } < \cdots < x _ { n } \right\} .$ In Theorems
5.4.4 and 5.4.6 we derive the pdf for one and the joint pdf for two order statistics,
again using binomial arguments.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="229" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ORDER STATISTICS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->

Theorem 5.4.4 Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) }$ denote the order statistics of a random sample,
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ from a continuous population with cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ Then the
pdf of $X _ { \left( j \right) }$ is

(5.4.4)
$$f _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right) = \frac { n ! } { \left( j - 1 \right) ! \left( n - j \right) ! } f _ { X } \left( x \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { j - 1 } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - j } .$$

Proof: We first find the $\mathrm { c d f }$ of $X _ { \left( j \right) }$ and then differentiate it to obtain the pdf. As
in Theorem 5.4.3, let $Y$ be a random variable that counts the number of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
less than or equal to x. Then, defining a "success" as the event $\left\{ X _ { j } \leq x \right\} ,$ we see that
$Y \sim \text { binomial } \left( n , F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right) .$ (Note that we can write $P _ { i } = F _ { X } \left( x _ { i } \right)$ in Theorem 5.4.3.
Also, although $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are continuous random variables, the counting variable $Y$
is discrete.) Thus,

$$F _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right) = P \left( Y \geq j \right) = \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n } \binom { n } { k } \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k } ,$$

and the pdf of $X _ { \left( j \right) }$ is

$$f _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right) = \frac { d } { d x } F _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) \left( k \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k - 1 } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k } f _ { X } \left( x \right) \right.$$
$$\left. - \left( n - k \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k - 1 } f _ { X } \left( x \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { j } \end{array} \right) j f _ { X } \left( x \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { j - 1 } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - j }$$
$$+ \sum _ { k = j + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) k \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k - 1 } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k } f _ { X } \left( x \right)$$

(chain rule)

$$- \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) \left( n - k \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k - 1 } f _ { X } \left( x \right)$$
$$k = n \quad t e r m$$

$$= \frac { n ! } { \left( j - 1 \right) ! \left( n - j \right) ! } f _ { X } \left( x \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { j - 1 } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - j }$$

(5.4.5)
$$+ \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} n \\ k + 1 \end{array} \right) \left( k + 1 \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k - 1 } f _ { X } \left( x \right) \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { c h a n g } \\ \mathrm { d u m m } \\ \mathrm { v a r i a b } \end{array} \right.$$
$$- \sum _ { k = j } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) \left( n - k \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( x \right) \right] ^ { n - k - 1 } f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$$

(5.4.6)
$$\left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { k + 1 } \end{array} \right) \left( k + 1 \right) = \frac { n ! } { k ! \left( n - k - 1 \right) ! } = \left( \binom { n } { k } \right) \left( n - k \right)$$

1
Noting that

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="230" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->

we see that the last two sums in (5.4.5) cancel. Thus, the pdf $f _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right)$ is given by the
expression in $\left( 5 . 4 . 4 \right) .$

Example 5.4.5 (Uniform order statistic pdf) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
$u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ so $f _ { X } \left( x \right) = 1$ for $x \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ and $F _ { X } \left( x \right) = x$ for $x \in \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Using
(5.4.4), we see that the pdf of the jth order statistic is

$$f _ { X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( x \right) = \frac { n ! } { \left( j - 1 \right) ! \left( n - j \right) ! } x ^ { j - 1 } \left( 1 - x \right) ^ { n - j } \quad \text { for } x \in \left( 0 , 1 \right)$$
$$= \frac { \Gamma \left( n + 1 \right) } { \Gamma \left( j \right) \Gamma \left( n - j + 1 \right) } x ^ { j - 1 } \left( 1 - x \right) ^ { \left( n - j + 1 \right) - 1 } .$$

Thus, the jth order statistic from a uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ sample has a $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( j , n - j + 1 \right)$
distribution. From this we can deduce that

$$E X _ { \left( j \right) } = \frac { j } { n + 1 }$$

and
Var X(j) =
(n +1)2(n + 2)
j(n-j+1)
II

The joint distribution of two or more order statistics can be used to derive the
distribution of some of the statistics mentioned at the beginning of this section. The
joint pdf of any two order statistics is given in the following theorem, whose proof is
left to Exercise 5.26.

Theorem 5.4.6 Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) }$ denote the order statistics $o f$ a random sample,
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ from a continuous population with cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right) .$ Then the
joint $p d f$ of $X _ { \left( i \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( j \right) } ,$ $1 \leq i < j \leq n ,$ is

$$f _ { X _ { \left( i \right) } , X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( u , v \right) = \frac { n ! } { \left( i - 1 \right) ! \left( j - 1 - i \right) ! \left( n - j \right) ! } f _ { X } \left( u \right) f _ { X } \left( v \right) \left[ F _ { X } \left( u \right) \right] ^ { i - 1 }$$
$$\times \left[ F _ { X } \left( v \right) - F _ { X } \left( u \right) \right] ^ { j - 1 - i } \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( v \right) \right] ^ { n - j }$$
(5.4.7)

for $- \infty < u < v < \infty .$

The joint pdf of three or more order statistics could be derived using similar but
even more involved arguments. Perhaps the other most useful pdf is $f _ { X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) } }$
$\left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ,$ the joint pdf of all the order statistics, which is given by

$${ } _ { \left. 1 \right) , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) } } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} n ! f _ { X } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) \cdots \cdot f _ { X } \left( x _ { n } \right) & - \infty < x _ { 1 } < \cdots < x _ { n } < \infty \\ 0 & \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e . } \end{array} \right.$$
fx(1)

The $n !$ naturally comes into this formula because, for any set of values $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$
there are $n !$ equally likely assignments for these values to $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ that all yield the
same values for the order statistics. This joint pdf and the techniques from Chapter
4 can be used to derive marginal and conditional distributions and distributions of
other functions of the order statistics. (See Exercises 5.27 and 5.28.)

We now use the joint pdf $\left( 5 . 4 . 7 \right)$ to derive the distribution of some of the functions
mentioned at the beginning of this section.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="231" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ORDER STATISTICS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.4" -->

Example 5.4.7 (Distribution of the midrange and range) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be
iid $u n i f o r m \left( 0 , a \right)$ and let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( n \right) }$ denote the order statistics. The range was
☒
earlier defined as $R = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } .$ The midrange, a measure of location like the
sample median or the sample mean, is defined by $V = \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } + X _ { \left( n \right) } \right) / 2 .$ We will derive
the joint pdf of $R$ and $V$ from the joint pdf of $X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } .$ From $\left( 5 . 4 . 7 \right)$ we have
☒
that

$$f _ { X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , X _ { \left( n \right) } } \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { n } \right) = \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) } { a ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { x _ { n } } { a } - \frac { x _ { 1 } } { a } \right) ^ { n - 2 }$$
$$= \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) \left( x _ { n } - x _ { 1 } \right) ^ { n - 2 } } { a ^ { n } } , \quad 0 < x _ { 1 } < x _ { n } < a$$

Solving for $X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } ,$ we obtain $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = V - R / 2$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } = V + R / 2 .$ The $J a -$
cobian for this transformation $i s \quad - 1 .$ The transformation from $\left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , X _ { \left( n \right) } \right)$ to $\left( R , V \right)$
☒
maps $\left\{ \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { n } \right) : 0 < x _ { 1 } < x _ { n } < a \right\}$ onto the set $\left. r , v \right) : 0 < < < a ,$ $r / 2 < < a \sim \sim / 2 .$
To see this, note that obviously $0 < r < a$ and for a fixed value $o f \quad r , v$ ranges from $r / 2$
(corresponding to $x _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ $x _ { n } = r$ to $a \quad - \quad r / 2$ (corresponding to $\left. x _ { 1 } = a - r , x _ { n } = a \right) .$
Thus, the joint pdf of $\left( R , V \right)$ is

$$f _ { R , V } \left( r , v \right) = \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } } { a ^ { n } } , \quad 0 < r < a , \quad r / 2 < v < a - r / 2 .$$

The marginal pdf of $R$ is thus

$$f _ { R } \left( r \right) = \int _ { r / 2 } ^ { a - r / 2 } \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } } { a ^ { n } } d v$$
$$= \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } \left( a - r \right) } { a ^ { n } } , \quad 0 < r < a$$
(5.4.8)

If $a = 1 ,$ we see that $R$ has a $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( n - 1 , 2 \right)$ distribution. $O r ,$ for arbitrary a, it is easy
to deduce from $\left( 5 . 4 . 8 \right)$ that $R / a$ has a beta distribution. Note that the constant $a$ is
a scale parameter.

The set where $f _ { R , V } \left( r , v \right) > 0$ is shown in Figure 5.4.1, where we see that the range
of integration of $r$ depends on whether $v > a / 2$ or $v \leq a / 2 .$ Thus, the marginal pdf
of $V$ is given by

$$f _ { V } \left( v \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 v } \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } } { a ^ { n } } d r = \frac { n \left( 2 v \right) ^ { n - 1 } } { a ^ { n } } , \quad 0 < v \leq a / 2 ,$$

and

$$f _ { V } \left( v \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \left( a - v \right) } \frac { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } } { a ^ { n } } d r = \frac { n \left[ 2 \left( a - v \right) \right] ^ { n - 1 } } { a ^ { n } } , \quad a / 2 < v \leq a$$

This pdf is symmetric about $a / 2$ and has a peak at $a / 2 .$

I/
