## 5.5 Convergence Concepts

This section treats the somewhat fanciful idea of allowing the sample size to approach
infinity and investigates the behavior of certain sample quantities as this happens.
Although the notion of an infinite sample size is a theoretical artifact, it can often
provide us with some useful approximations for the finite-sample case, since it usually
happens that expressions become simplified in the limit.

We are mainly concerned with three types of convergence, and we treat them in
varying amounts of detail. (A full treatment of convergence is given in Billingsley
1995 or Resnick 1999, for example.) In particular, we want to look at the behavior of
$\bar { X } _ { n } ,$ the mean of $n$ observations, as $n \rightarrow \infty .$


### 5.5.1 Convergence in Probability

This type of convergence is one of the weaker types and, hence, is usually quite easy
to verify.

Definition 5.5.1 A sequence of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ converges in proba-
bility to a random variable $X$ if, for every $\epsilon > 0 ,$

lim P(|Xn - X| > <) = 0 or, equivalently,

$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P \left( | X _ { n } - X | < \epsilon \right) = 1 .$

200

The $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ in Definition 5.5.1 (and the other definitions in this section) are
typically not independent and identically distributed random variables, as in a random
sample. The distribution of $X _ { n }$ changes as the subscript changes, and the convergence
concepts discussed in this section describe different ways in which the distribution of
$X _ { n }$ converges to some limiting distribution as the subscript becomes large.

Frequently, statisticians are concerned with situations in which the limiting random
variable is a constant and the random variables in the sequence are sample means (of
some sort). The most famous result of this type is the following.

Theorem 5.5.2 (Weak Law of Large Numbers) Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be iid random
variables with $E X _ { i } = \mu$ and Var $X _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } < \infty .$ Define $\bar { X } _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } .$ Then,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="233" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

for every $\epsilon > 0 ,$

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P \left( | \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu | < \epsilon \right) = 1$$

that is, $\bar { X } _ { n }$ converges in probability to $\mu .$

Proof: The proof is quite simple, being a straightforward application of Chebychev's
Inequality. We have, for every $\epsilon > 0 ,$

$$P \left( | \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu | \geq \epsilon \right) = P \left( \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } \geq \epsilon ^ { 2 } \right) \leq \frac { \mathrm { E } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mathrm { V a r } \bar { X } } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n \epsilon ^ { 2 } } .$$

$$P \left( | \widetilde { X } _ { n } - \mu | < \epsilon \right) = 1 - P \left( | \widetilde { X } _ { n } - \mu | \geq \epsilon \right) \geq 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } / \left( n \epsilon ^ { 2 } \right) \rightarrow 1 , \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty .$$
Hence,

☐

The Weak Law of Large Numbers (WLLN) quite elegantly states that, under gen-
eral conditions, the sample mean approaches the population mean as $n \rightarrow \infty .$ In fact,
there are more general versions of the WLLN, where we need assume only that the
mean is finite. However, the version stated in Theorem 5.5.2 is applicable in most
practical situations.

The property summarized by the WLLN, that a sequence of the "same" sample
quantity approaches a constant as $n \rightarrow \infty ,$ is known as consistency. We will examine
this property more closely in Chapter 7.

Example 5.5.3 (Consistency of $S ^ { 2 }$ Suppose we have a sequence $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ of
iid random variables with $E X _ { i } = \mu$ and Var $X _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } < \infty .$ If we define

$$S _ { n } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } _ { n } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

can we prove a WLLN for $S _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ Using Chebychev's Inequality, we have

$$P \left( | S _ { n } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } | \geq \epsilon \right) \leq \frac { \mathrm { E } \left( S _ { n } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mathrm { V a r } S _ { n } ^ { 2 } } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } }$$

and thus, a sufficient condition that $S _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ converges in probability to $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is that Var $S _ { n } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow$
0 as $n \rightarrow \infty .$

Il

A natural extension of Definition 5.5.1 relates to functions of random variables.
That is, if the sequence $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \cdots .$ converges in probability to a random variable $X$ or
to a constant a, can we make any conclusions about the sequence of random variables
$h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) , h \left( X _ { 2 } \right) , \cdots .$ for some reasonably behaved function $h ?$ This next theorem shows
that we can. (See Exercise 5.39 for a proof.)

Theorem 5.5.4 Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ converges in probability to a random vari-
able $X$ and that h is a continuous function. Then $h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) , h \left( X _ { 2 } \right) , \ldots .$ converges in prob-
ability to $h \left( X \right) .$

Example 5.5.5 (Consistency of $\left. S \right)$ If $S _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ is a consistent estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ then
by Theorem 5.5.4, the sample standard deviation $S _ { n } = \sqrt { S _ { n } ^ { 2 } } = h \left( S _ { n } ^ { 2 } \right)$ is a consistent
estimator of o. Note that $S _ { n }$ is, in fact, a biased estimator of $\sigma$ (see Exercise 5.11),
but the bias disappears asymptotically.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="234" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->


### 5.5.2 Almost Sure Convergence

A type of convergence that is stronger than convergence in probability is almost sure
convergence (sometimes confusingly known as convergence with probability 1). This
type of convergence is similar to pointwise convergence of a sequence of functions,
except that the convergence need not occur on a set with probability 0 (hence the
"almost" sure).

Definition 5.5.6 A sequence of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \cdots .$ , converges almost
surely to a random variable $X$ if, for every $\epsilon > 0 ,$

$$P \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } | X _ { n } - X | < \epsilon \right) = 1 .$$

Notice the similarity in the statements of Definitions 5.5.1 and 5.5.6. Although they
look similar, they are very different statements, with Definition 5.5.6 much stronger.
To understand almost sure convergence, we must recall the basic definition of a ran-
dom variable as given in Definition 1.4.1. $A$ random variable is a real-valued function
defined on a sample space $S .$ If $a$ sample space $S$ has elements denoted by $s ,$ then
$X _ { n } \left( s \right)$ and $X \left( s \right)$ are all functions defined on $S .$ Definition 5.5.6 states that $X _ { n }$ con-
verges to $X$ almost surely if the functions $X _ { n } \left( s \right)$ converge to $X \left( s \right)$ for all $s \in S$
except perhaps for $s \in N ,$ where $N \subset S$ and $P \left( N \right) = 0 .$ Example 5.5.7 illustrates al-
most sure convergence. Example 5.5.8 illustrates the difference between convergence
in probability and almost sure convergence.

Example 5.5.7 (Almost sure convergence) Let the sample space $S$ be the
closed interval $\left[ 0 , 1 \right]$ with the uniform probability distribution. Define random vari-
ables $X _ { n } \left( s \right) = s + s ^ { n }$ and $X \left( s \right) = s .$ For every $s \in \left[ 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $s ^ { n } \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$ and
$X _ { n } \left( s \right) \rightarrow s = X \left( s \right) .$ However, $X _ { n } \left( 1 \right) = 2$ for every $n$ so $X _ { n } \left( 1 \right)$ does not converge to
$1 = X \left( 1 \right) .$ But since the convergence occurs on the set $\left[ 0 , 1 \right)$ and $P \left( \left[ 0 , 1 \right) \right) = 1 , X _ { n }$
converges to $X$ almost surely.
I!

Example 5.5.8 (Convergence in probability, not almost surely) In this ex-
ample we describe a sequence that converges in probability, but not almost surely.
Again, let the sample space $S$ be the closed interval [0,1] with the uniform probability
distribution. Define the sequence $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ as follows:

$$X _ { 1 } \left( s \right) = s + I _ { \left[ 0 , 1 \right] } \left( s \right) , \quad X _ { 2 } \left( s \right) = s + I _ { \left[ 0 , \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right] } \left( s \right) ,$$

$$X _ { 4 } \left( s \right) = s + I _ { \left[ 0 , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right] } \left( s \right) , \quad X _ { 5 } \left( s \right) = s + I _ { \left[ \frac { 1 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } \right] } \left( s \right) ,$$

X3(s) = s + \[},1](s),
X6(s) = s + I\[2,1](s),

etc. Let $X \left( s \right) = s .$ It is straightforward to see that $X _ { n }$ converges to $X$ in probability.
As $n \rightarrow \infty ,$ $P \left( | X _ { n } - X | \geq \epsilon \right)$ is equal to the probability of an interval of $s$ values
whose length is going to 0. However, $X _ { n }$ does not converge to $X$ almost surely.
Indeed, there is no value of $s \in S$ for which $X _ { n } \left( s \right) \rightarrow s = X \left( s \right) .$ For every $s ,$ the
value $X _ { n } \left( s \right)$ alternates between the values $s$ and $s + 1$ infinitely often. For example,
if $s = \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 1 } \left( s \right) = 1 \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 2 } \left( s \right) = 1 \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 3 } \left( s \right) = \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 4 } \left( s \right) = \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 5 } \left( s \right) = 1 \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ $X _ { 6 } \left( s \right) = \frac { 3 } { 8 } ,$ etc.
No pointwise convergence occurs for this sequence.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="235" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

As might be guessed, convergence almost surely, being the stronger criterion, implies
convergence in probability. The converse is, of course, false, as Example 5.5.8 shows.
However, if a sequence converges in probability, it is possible to find a subsequence
that converges almost surely. (Resnick 1999, Section 6.3, has a thorough treatment of
the connections between the two types of convergence.)

Again, statisticians are often concerned with convergence to a constant. We now
state, without proof, the stronger analog of the WLLN, the Strong Law of Large
Numbers (SLLN). See Miscellanea 5.8.4 for an outline of $a$ proof.

Theorem 5.5.9 (Strong Law of Large Numbers) Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be iid random
variables with $E X _ { i } = \mu$ and Var $X _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } < \infty ,$ and define $\bar { X } _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } .$
Then, for every $\epsilon > 0 ,$

$$P \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } | X _ { n } - \mu | < \epsilon \right) = 1$$

that $i s ,$ $\bar { X } _ { n }$ converges almost surely to $\mu .$

For both the Weak and Strong Laws of Large Numbers we had the assumption
of a finite variance. Although such an assumption is true (and desirable) in most
applications, it is, in fact, a stronger assumption than is needed. Both the weak and
strong laws hold without this assumption. The only moment condition needed is that
$E | X _ { i } | < \infty$ (see Resnick 1999, Chapter 7, or Billingsley 1995, Section 22).


### 5.5.3 Convergence in Distribution

We have already encountered the idea of convergence in distribution in Chapter 2.
Remember the properties of moment generating functions (mgfs) and how their con-
vergence implies convergence in distribution (Theorem 2.3.12).

Definition 5.5.10 $A$ sequence of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ converges in distri-
bution to a random variable $X$ if

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } F _ { X _ { n } } \left( x \right) = F _ { X } \left( x \right)$$

at all points $x$ where $F _ { X } \left( x \right)$ is continuous.

Example 5.5.11 (Maximum of uniforms) If $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ are iid uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ and
$X _ { \left( n \right) } = \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } X _ { i } ,$ let us examine if (and to where) $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ converges in distribution.
As $n \rightarrow \infty ,$ we expect $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ to get close to 1 and, as $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ must necessarily be less
than 1, we have for any $\varepsilon > 0 ,$

$$P \left( | X _ { \left( n \right) } - 1 | \geq \varepsilon \right) = P \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } \geq 1 + \varepsilon \right) + P \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } \leq 1 - \varepsilon \right)$$
$$= 0 + P \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } \leq 1 - \varepsilon \right) .$$

Next using the fact that we have an $i i d$ sample, we can write

$$P \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } \leq 1 - \varepsilon \right) = P \left( X _ { i } \leq 1 - \varepsilon , i = 1 , \ldots n \right) = \left( 1 - \varepsilon \right) ^ { n } ,$$
$$\begin{array}{} \end{array}$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="236" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

which goes to 0. So we have proved that $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ converges to 1 in probability. However,
if we take $\varepsilon = t / n ,$ we then have

$$P \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } \leq 1 - t / n \right) = \left( 1 - t / n \right) ^ { n } \rightarrow e ^ { - t } ,$$

which, upon rearranging, yields

$$P \left( n \left( 1 - X _ { \left( n \right) } \right) \leq t \right) \rightarrow 1 - e ^ { - t }$$

that is, the random variable $n \left( 1 - X _ { \left( n \right) } \right)$ converges in distribution to an exponential(1)
random variable.

Note that although we talk of a sequence of random variables converging in dis-
tribution, it is really the cdfs that converge, not the random variables. In this very
fundamental way convergence in distribution is quite different from convergence in
probability or convergence almost surely. However, it is implied by the other types of
convergence.

Theorem 5.5.12 $I f$ the sequence of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ converges in prob-
ability to a random variable $X ,$ the sequence also converges in distribution to $X .$

See Exercise 5.40 for a proof. Note also that, from Section 5.5.2, convergence in
distribution is also implied by almost sure convergence.

In a special case, Theorem 5.5.12 has a converse that turns out to be useful. See
Example 10.1.13 for an illustration and Exercise 5.41 for a proof.

Theorem 5.5.13 The sequence of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ , converges in prob-
ability to a constant $\mu$ if and only $i f$ the sequence also converges in distribution to $\mu .$
That $i s ,$ the statement

$$P \left( | X _ { n } - \mu | > \varepsilon \right) \rightarrow 0 \text { for every } \varepsilon > 0$$

is equivalent to

$$P \left( X _ { n } \leq x \right) \rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { \text { if } x < \mu } \\ { 1 } & { \text { if } x > \mu } \end{array} \right.$$

The sample mean is one statistic whose large-sample behavior is quite important.
In particular, we want to investigate its limiting distribution. This is summarized in
one of the most startling theorems in statistics, the Central Limit Theorem (CLT).

Theorem 5.5.14 (Central Limit Theorem) Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be a sequence of $i i d$
random variables whose mgfs exist in a neighborhood of $0$ (that is, $M _ { X _ { i } } \left( t \right)$ exists for
$I t | < h ,$ for some positive $\left. h \right) .$ Let $E X _ { i } = \mu$ and Var $X _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } > 0 .$ (Both $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are
finite since the mgf exists. ) Define $\bar { X } _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } .$ Let $G _ { n } \left( x \right)$ denote the cdf of
$\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma .$ Then, for any x, $- \infty < x < \infty ,$

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } G _ { n } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { x } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - y ^ { 2 } / 2 } d y$$

that is, $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma$ has a limiting standard normal distribution.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="237" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

Before we prove this theorem (the proof is somewhat anticlimactic) we first look at
its implications. Starting from virtually no assumptions (other than independence and
finite variances), we end up with normality! The point here is that normality comes
from sums of "small" (finite variance), independent disturbances. The assumption of
finite variances is essentially necessary for convergence to normality. Although it can
be relaxed somewhat, it cannot be eliminated. (Recall Example 5.2.10, dealing with
the Cauchy distribution, where there is no convergence to normality.)

While we revel in the wonder of the CLT, it is also useful to reflect on its limi-
tations. Although it gives us a useful general approximation, we have no automatic
way of knowing how good the approximation is in general. In fact, the goodness of
the approximation is a function of the original distribution, and so must be checked
case by case. Furthermore, with the current availability of cheap, plentiful computing
power, the importance of approximations like the Central Limit Theorem is somewhat
lessened. However, despite its limitations, it is still a marvelous result.

Proof of Theorem 5.5.14: We will show that, for $| t | < h ,$ the mgf of $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma$
converges to $e ^ { t ^ { 2 } / 2 } ,$ the mgf of a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable.

Define $Y _ { i } = \left( X _ { i } - \mu \right) / \sigma ,$ and let $M _ { Y } \left( t \right)$ denote the common mgf of the $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s } ,$ which
exists for $| t | < \sigma h$ and is given by Theorem 2.3.15. Since

(5.5.1)
$$\frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { \sigma } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i }$$

we have, from the properties of mgfs (see Theorems 2.3.15 and 4.6.7),

(5.5.2)
$$M _ { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma } \left( t \right) = M _ { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i } / \sqrt { n } } \left( t \right)$$
$$= M _ { \Sigma _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i } } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$= \left( M _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) \right) ^ { n } .$$

(Theorem 2.3.15)

(Theorem 4.6.7)

We now expand $M _ { Y } \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right)$ in a Taylor series (power series) around 0. (See Defini-
tion 5.5.20.) We have

(5.5.3)
$$M _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } M _ { Y } ^ { \left( k \right) } \left( 0 \right) \frac { \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { k } } { k ! }$$

where $M _ { Y } ^ { \left( k \right) } \left( 0 \right) = \left( d ^ { k } / d t ^ { k } \right) M _ { Y } \left( t \right) | _ { t = 0 } .$ Since the mgfs exist for $| t | < h ,$ the power
series expansion is valid if $t < \sqrt { n } \sigma h .$

Using the facts that $M _ { Y } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } = 1 ,$ $M _ { Y } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = 0 ,$ and $M _ { Y } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } = 1$ (by construction, the mean
and variance of $Y$ are 0 and 1), we have

(5.5.4)
$$M _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) = 1 + \frac { \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } + R _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="238" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

where $R _ { Y }$ is the remainder term in the Taylor expansion,

$$R _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) = \sum _ { k = 3 } ^ { \infty } M _ { Y } ^ { \left( k \right) } \left( 0 \right) \frac { \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { k } } { k ! }$$

An application of Taylor's Theorem (Theorem 5.5.21) shows that, for fixed $t \neq 0 ,$ we
have

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \frac { R _ { Y } \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) } { \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } = 0$$

Since $t$ is fixed, we also have

(5.5.5)
$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \frac { R _ { Y } \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) } { \left( 1 / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } n R _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) = 0 ,$$

and (5.5.5) is also true at $t = 0$ since $R _ { Y } \left( 0 / \sqrt { n } \right) = 0 .$ Thus, for any fixed t, we can
write

(5.5.6)
$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( M _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) \right) ^ { n } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left[ 1 + \frac { \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } + R _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) \right] ^ { n }$$
$$= \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left[ 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + n R _ { Y } \left( \frac { t } { \sqrt { n } } \right) \right) \right] ^ { n }$$
$$= e ^ { t ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$

by an application of Lemma 2.3.14, where we set $a _ { n } = \left( t ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) + n R _ { Y } \left( t / \sqrt { n } \right) .$ (Note
that (5.5.5) implies that $a _ { n } \rightarrow t ^ { 2 } / 2$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$ Since $e ^ { t ^ { 2 } / 2 }$ is the mgf of the $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$
distribution, the theorem is proved.
☐

The Central Limit Theorem is valid in much more generality than is stated in
Theorem 5.5.14 (see Miscellanea 5.8.1). In particular, all of the assumptions about
mgfs are not needed-the use of characteristic functions (see Miscellanea 2.6.2) can
replace them. We state the next theorem without proof. It is a version of the Central
Limit Theorem that is general enough for almost all statistical purposes. Notice that
the only assumption on the parent distribution is that it has finite variance.

Theorem 5.5.15 (Stronger form of the Central Limit Theorem) Let
$X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be a sequence of iid random variables with $E X _ { i } = \mu$ and $0 < \mathrm { V a r } X _ { i } =$
$\sigma ^ { 2 } < \infty .$ Define $\bar { X } _ { n } = \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } .$ Let $G _ { n } \left( x \right)$ denote the $c d f$ of $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma .$
Then, for any x, $- \infty < x < \infty ,$

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } G _ { n } \left( x \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { x } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - y ^ { 2 } / 2 } d y$$

that is, $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) / \sigma$ has a limiting standard normal distribution.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="239" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

The proof is almost identical to that of Theorem 5.5.14, except that characteristic
functions are used instead of mgfs. Since the characteristic function of a distribution
always exists, it is not necessary to mention them in the assumptions of the theorem.
The proof is more delicate, however, since functions of complex variables must be
dealt with. Details can be found in Billingsley (1995, Section 27).

The Central Limit Theorem provides us with an all-purpose approximation (but
remember the warning about the goodness of the approximation). In practice, it can
always be used for a first, rough calculation.

Example 5.5.16 (Normal approximation to the negative binomial) Suppose
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are a random sample from a negative binomial(r, $\left. p \right)$ distribution. Recall
that

$$E X = \frac { r \left( 1 - p \right) } { p } , \quad \text { Var } X = \frac { r \left( 1 - p \right) } { p ^ { 2 } }$$

and the Central Limit Theorem tells us that

$$\frac { \sqrt { n } \left( X - r \left( 1 - p \right) / p \right) } { \sqrt { r \left( 1 - p \right) / p ^ { 2 } } }$$

is approximately $n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ The approximate probability calculations are much easier
than the exact calculations. For example, if $r = 1 0 ,$ $p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ and $n = 3 0 ,$ an exact
calculation would be

$$P \left( \bar { X } \leq 1 1 \right) = P \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 0 } X _ { i } \leq 3 3 0 \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { x = 0 } ^ { 3 3 0 } \left( \begin{array}{} { 3 0 0 + x - 1 } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 0 0 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { x }$$
$$= . 8 9 1 6 ,$$

which is a very difficult calculation. (Note that this calculation is difficult even with
the aid of a computer-the magnitudes of the factorials cause great difficulty. Try it
if you don't believe it!) The CLT gives us the approximation

$$P \left( \bar { X } \leq 1 1 \right) = P \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 0 } \left( \bar { X } - 1 0 \right) } { \sqrt { 2 0 } } \leq \frac { \sqrt { 3 0 } \left( 1 1 - 1 0 \right) } { \sqrt { 2 0 } } \right)$$
$$\approx P \left( Z \leq 1 . 2 2 4 7 \right) = . 8 8 8 8 .$$

See Exercise 5.37 for some further refinement.
II

An approximation tool that can be used in conjunction with the Central Limit
Theorem is known as Slutsky's Theorem.

Theorem 5.5.17 (Slutsky's Theorem) If $X _ { n } \rightarrow X$ in distribution and $Y _ { n } \rightarrow a ,$ a
constant, in probability, then

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="240" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

a. $Y _ { n } X _ { n } \rightarrow a X$ in distribution.

b. $X _ { n } + Y _ { n } \rightarrow X + a$ in distribution.

The proof of Slutsky's Theorem is omitted, since it relies on a characterization
of convergence in distribution that we have not discussed. A typical application is
illustrated by the following example.

Example 5.5.18 (Normal approximation with estimated variance) Suppose
that

$$\frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { \sigma } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$$

but the value of $\sigma$ is unknown. We have seen in Example 5.5.3 that, if $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \mathrm { V a r } S _ { n } ^ { 2 } ,$
$= 0 ,$ then $S _ { n } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \sigma ^ { 2 }$ in probability. By Exercise 5.32, $\sigma / S _ { n } \rightarrow 1$ in probability. Hence,
Slutsky's Theorem tells us

$$\frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { S _ { n } } = \frac { \sigma } { S _ { n } } \frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { \sigma } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$$


### 5.5.4 The Delta Method

The previous section gives conditions under which a standardized random variable
has a limit normal distribution. There are many times, however, when we are not
specifically interested in the distribution of the random variable itself, but rather
some function of the random variable.
1

Example 5.5.19 (Estimating the odds) Suppose we observe $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ in-
dependent Bernoulli(p) random variables. The typical parameter of interest is $p ,$ the
success probability, but another popular parameter is $\frac { p } { 1 - p } ,$ the odds. For example, if
the data represent the outcomes of a medical treatment with $p = 2 / 3 ,$ then a person
has odds $2 : 1$ of getting better. Moreover, if there were another treatment with suc-
cess probability r, biostatisticians often estimate the odds ratio $\frac { p } { 1 - p } / \frac { r } { 1 - r } ,$ giving the
relative odds of one treatment over another.

As we would typically estimate the success probability $p$ with the observed success
probability $\widehat { p } = \sum _ { i } X _ { i } / n ,$ we might consider using $\frac { \widehat { p } } { 1 - \widehat { p } }$ as an estimate of $\frac { p } { 1 - p } .$ But
what are the properties of this estimator? How might we estimate the variance of
$\frac { \widehat { p } } { 1 - \widehat { p } }$ Moreover, how can we approximate its sampling distribution?

Intuition abandons us, and exact calculation is relatively hopeless, so we have to
rely on an approximation. The Delta Method will allow us to obtain reasonable,
approximate answers to our questions.
|

One method of proceeding is based on using a Taylor series approximation, which
allows us to approximate the mean and variance of a function of a random variable.
We will also see that these rather straightforward approximations are good enough
to obtain a CLT. We begin with a short review of Taylor series.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="241" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

Definition 5.5.20 If a function $g \left( x \right)$ has derivatives of order r, that is, $g ^ { \left( r \right) } \left( x \right) =$
$\frac { d ^ { r } } { d x ^ { r } } g \left( x \right)$ exists, then for any constant a, the Taylor polynomial of order $r$ about $a$ is

$$T _ { r } \left( x \right) = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { r } \frac { g ^ { \left( i \right) } \left( a \right) } { i ! } \left( x - a \right) ^ { i }$$

Taylor's major theorem, which we will not prove here, is that the remainder from
the approximation, $g \left( x \right) - T _ { r } \left( x \right) ,$ always tends to 0 faster than the highest-order
explicit term.

Theorem 5.5.21 (Taylor) If $g ^ { \left( r \right) } \left( a \right) = \frac { d ^ { r } } { d x ^ { r } } g \left( x \right) | _ { x = a }$ exists, then

$$\lim _ { x \rightarrow a } \frac { g \left( x \right) - T _ { r } \left( x \right) } { \left( x - a \right) ^ { r } } = 0$$

In general, we will not be concerned with the explicit form of the remainder. Since
we are interested in approximations, we are just going to ignore the remainder. There
are, however, many explicit forms, one useful one being

$$g \left( x \right) - T _ { r } \left( x \right) = \int _ { a } ^ { x } \frac { g ^ { \left( r + 1 \right) } \left( t \right) } { r ! } \left( x - t \right) ^ { r } d t$$

For the statistical application of Taylor's Theorem, we are most concerned with
the first-order Taylor series, that is, an approximation using just the first derivative
(taking $r = 1$ in the above formulas). Furthermore, we will also find use for a multi-
variate Taylor series. Since the above detail is univariate, some of the following will
have to be accepted on faith.

Let $T _ { 1 } , \ldots , T _ { k }$ be random variables with means $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { k } ,$ and define $T = \left( T _ { 1 } , \ldots , \right. ,$
$T _ { k }$ and $\theta = \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { k } \right) .$ Suppose there is a differentiable function $g \left( T \right)$ (an estimator
of some parameter) for which we want an approximate estimate of variance. Define

$$g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial t _ { i } } g \left( t \right) | _ { t _ { 1 } = \theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , t _ { k } = \theta _ { k } }$$

The first-order Taylor series expansion of $g$ about $\theta$ is

$$g \left( t \right) = g \left( \theta \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \left( t _ { i } - \theta _ { i } \right) + \text { Remainder } .$$

For our statistical approximation we forget about the remainder and write

(5.5.7)
$$g \left( t \right) \approx g \left( \theta \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \left( t _ { i } - \theta _ { i } \right) .$$

Now, take expectations on both sides of (5.5.7) to get

(5.5.8)
$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) \approx g \left( \theta \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) E _ { \theta } \left( T _ { i } - \theta _ { i } \right)$$
$$= g \left( \theta \right) .$$

$$\left( T _ { i } \text { has mean } \theta _ { i } \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="242" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

We can now approximate the variance of $g \left( T \right)$ by

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) \approx E _ { \theta } \left( \left[ g \left( T \right) - g \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$\approx E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \left( T _ { i } - \theta _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(using (5.5.8))
(using (5.5.7))

(5.5.9)
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \left[ g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \text { Var } _ { \theta } T _ { i } + 2 \sum _ { i > j } g _ { i } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) g _ { j } ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \text { Cov } _ { \theta } \left( T _ { i } , T _ { j } \right) ,$$

where the last equality comes from expanding the square and using the definition
of variance and covariance (similar to Exercise 4.44). Approximation (5.5.9) is very
useful because it gives us a variance formula for a general function, using only simple
variances and covariances. Here are two examples.

Example 5.5.22 (Continuation of Example 5.5.19) Recall that we are inter-
ested in the properties of $\frac { \widehat { p } } { 1 - \widehat { p } } ,$ as an estimate of $\frac { p } { 1 - p } ,$ where $p$ is a binomial success
probability. In our above notation, take $g \left( p \right) = \frac { p } { 1 - p } ,$ so $g ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 2 } }$ and

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \frac { \widehat { p } } { 1 - \widehat { p } } \right) \approx \left[ g ^ { \prime } \left( p \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } \left( \widehat { p } \right)$$
$$= \left[ \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 } \frac { p \left( 1 - p \right) } { n } = \frac { p } { n \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 3 } }$$

Il

giving us an approximation for the variance of our estimator.

Example 5.5.23 (Approximate mean and variance) Suppose $X$ is a random
variable with $E _ { \mu } X = \mu \neq 0 .$ If we want to estimate a function $g \left( \mu \right) ,$ a first-order
☒
approximation would give us

$$g \left( X \right) = g \left( \mu \right) + g ^ { \prime } \left( \mu \right) \left( X - \mu \right) .$$ ☒

If we use $g \left( X \right)$ as an estimator of $g \left( \mu \right) ,$ we can say that approximately
☒

$$E _ { \mu } g \left( X \right) \approx g \left( \mu \right) ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \mu } g \left( X \right) \approx \left[ g ^ { \prime } \left( \mu \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \text { Var } _ { \mu } X .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

For a specific example, take $g \left( \mu \right) = 1 / \mu .$ We estimate $1 / \mu$ with $1 / X ,$ and we can say
☒

$$E _ { \mu } \left( \frac { 1 } { X } \right) \approx \frac { 1 } { \mu } ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \mu } \left( \frac { 1 } { X } \right) \approx \left( \frac { 1 } { \mu } \right) ^ { 4 } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \mu } X$$

1

Using these Taylor series approximations for the mean and variance, we get the
following useful generalization of the Central Limit Theorem, known as the Delta
Method.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="243" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONVERGENCE CONCEPTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

Theorem 5.5.24 (Delta Method) Let $Y _ { n }$ be a sequence of random variables that
satisfies $\sqrt { n } \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ in distribution. For a given function $g$ and a specific
value of $\theta ,$ suppose that $g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right)$ exists and is not 0. Then

(5.5.10)
$$\sqrt { n } \left[ g \left( Y _ { n } \right) - g \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \left[ g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \right) \text { in distribution } .$$

Proof: The Taylor expansion of $g \left( Y _ { n } \right)$ around $Y _ { n } = \theta$ is

(5.5.11)
$$g \left( Y _ { n } \right) = g \left( \theta \right) + g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) + \text { Remainder } ,$$

where the remainder $\rightarrow 0$ as $Y _ { n } \rightarrow \theta .$ Since $Y _ { n } \rightarrow \theta$ in probability it follows that the
remainder $\rightarrow 0$ in probability. By applying Slutsky's Theorem (Theorem 5.5.17) to

$$\sqrt { n } \left[ g \left( Y _ { n } \right) - g \left( \theta \right) \right] = g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \sqrt { n } \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) ,$$

the result now follows. See Exercise 5.43 for details.
☐

Example 5.5.25 (Continuation of Example 5.5.23) Suppose now that we have
the mean of a random sample $\overrightarrow { X } .$ For $\mu \neq 0 ,$ we have

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { X } - \frac { 1 } { \mu } \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \left( \frac { 1 } { \mu } \right) ^ { 4 } \text { Var } _ { \mu } X _ { 1 } \right)$$

in distribution.

If we do not know the variance of $X _ { 1 } ,$ to use the above approximation requires an
estimate, say $S ^ { 2 } .$ Moreover, there is the question of what to do with the $1 / \mu$ term, as
we also do not know $\mu .$ We can estimate everything, which gives us the approximate
variance

$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( \frac { 1 } { X } \right) \approx \left( \frac { 1 } { X } \right) ^ { 4 } S ^ { 2 } .$$

Furthermore, as both $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are consistent estimators, we can again apply Slutsky's
Theorem to conclude that for $\mu \neq 0 ,$

$$\frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { X } - \frac { 1 } { \mu } \right) } { \left( \frac { 1 } { X } \right) ^ { 2 } S } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$$

in distribution.

Note how we wrote this latter quantity, dividing through by the estimated standard
deviation and making the limiting distribution a standard normal. This is the only way
that makes sense if we need to estimate any parameters in the limiting distribution.
We also note that there is an alternative approach when there are parameters to
estimate, and here we can actually avoid using an estimate for $\mu$ in the variance (see
the score test in Section 10.3.2).
11

There are two extensions of the basic Delta Method that we need to deal with to
complete our treatment. The first concerns the possibility that $g ^ { \prime } \left( \mu \right) = 0 .$ This could

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="244" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.5" -->

happen, for example, if we were interested in estimating the variance of a binomial
variance (see Exercise 5.44).

If $g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) = 0 ,$ we take one more term in the Taylor expansion to get

$$g \left( Y _ { n } \right) = g \left( \theta \right) + g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) + \frac { g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta \right) } { 2 } \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } + \text { Remainder } .$$

If we do some rearranging (setting $g ^ { \prime } = 0 ,$ we have

(5.5.12)
$$g \left( Y _ { n } \right) - g \left( \theta \right) = \frac { g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta \right) } { 2 } \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } + \text { Remainder } .$$

Now recall that the square of a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ is a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ (Example 2.1.9), which implies that

$$\frac { n \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \rightarrow \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$$

in distribution. Therefore, an argument similar to that used in Theorem 5.5.24 will
establish the following theorem.

Theorem 5.5.26 (Second-order Delta Method) Let $Y _ { n }$ be $a$ sequence of random
variables that satisfies $\sqrt { n } \left( Y _ { n } - \theta \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ in distribution. For a given function g
and a specific value of $\theta ,$ suppose that $g ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) = 0$ and $g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta \right)$ exists and is not 0. Then

(5.5.13)
$$n \left[ g \left( Y _ { n } \right) - g \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow \sigma ^ { 2 } \frac { g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta \right) } { 2 } \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } .$$

Approximation techniques are very useful when more than one parameter makes
up the function to be estimated and more than one random variable is used in the
estimator. One common example is in growth studies, where a ratio of weight/height
is a variable of interest. (Recall that in Chapter 3 we saw that a ratio of two normal
random variables has a Cauchy distribution. The ratio problem, while being important
to experimenters, is nasty in theory.)

This brings us to the second extension of the Delta Method, to the multivariate
case. As we already have Taylor's Theorem for the multivariate case, this extension
contains no surprises.

Example 5.5.27 (Moments of a ratio estimator) Suppose $X$ and $Y$ are random
variables with nonzero means $\mu _ { X }$ and $\mu _ { Y } ,$ respectively. The parametric function to
be estimated is $g \left( \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } \right) = \mu _ { X } / \mu _ { Y } .$ It is straightforward to calculate

$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \mu _ { X } } g \left( \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { Y } }$$

and

$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \mu _ { Y } } g \left( \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } \right) = \frac { - \mu _ { X } } { \mu _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="245" -->
<!-- PageHeader="GENERATING A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

The first-order Taylor approximations (5.5.8) and (5.5.9) give

$$E \left( \frac { X } { Y } \right) \approx \frac { \mu _ { X } } { \mu _ { Y } }$$

and

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \frac { X } { Y } \right) \approx \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } \mathrm { V a r } X + \frac { \mu _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { Y } ^ { 4 } } \mathrm { V a r } Y - 2 \frac { \mu _ { X } } { \mu _ { Y } ^ { 3 } } \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right)$$
$$= \left( \frac { \mu _ { X } } { \mu _ { Y } } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \mathrm { V a r } X } { \mu _ { X } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mathrm { V a r } Y } { \mu _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) } { \mu _ { X } \mu _ { Y } } \right) .$$

Thus, we have an approximation for the mean and variance of the ratio estimator, and
the approximations use only the means, variances, and covariance of $\bar { X }$ and $Y .$ Exact
calculations would be quite hopeless, with closed-form expressions being unattainable.
11

We next present a CLT to cover an estimator such as the ratio estimator. Note that
we must deal with multiple random variables although the ultimate CLT is $a$ univari-
ate one. Suppose the vector-valued random variable $X = \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { p } \right)$ has mean
☒
$\mu = \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { p } \right)$ and covariances $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X _ { i } , X _ { j } \right) = \sigma _ { i j } ,$ and we observe an independent
random sample $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ and calculate the means $\bar { X } _ { i } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i k } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , p .$
For a function $g \left( x \right) = g \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p } \right)$ we can use the development after $\left( 5 . 5 . 7 \right)$ to write
☒

$$g \left( \bar { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \bar { x } _ { p } \right) \approx g \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { p } \right) + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { k } ^ { \prime } \left( \mu \right) \left( \bar { x } _ { k } - \mu _ { k } \right) ,$$

and we then have the following theorem.

Theorem 5.5.28 (Multivariate Delta Method) Let $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathrm { X } _ { n }$ be a random
sample with $E \left( X _ { i j } \right) = \mu _ { i }$ and $\mathrm { C o v } \left( X _ { i k } , X _ { j k } \right) = \sigma _ { i j } .$ For a given function $g$ with
continuous first partial derivatives and a specific value of $\mu = \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { p } \right)$ for which
$\tau ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { \sigma _ { i j } } \frac { \partial g \left( \mu \right) } { \partial \mu _ { i } } \cdot \frac { \partial g \left( \mu \right) } { \partial \mu _ { j } } > 0 ,$

distribution .
$$\sqrt { n } \left[ g \left( \bar { X } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \bar { X } _ { s } \right) - g \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { p } \right) \right] \rightarrow \mathrm { n } \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) i n$$

The proof necessitates dealing with the convergence of multivariate random vari-
ables, and we will not deal with such multivariate intricacies here, but will take
Theorem 5.5.28 on faith. The interested reader can find more details in Lehmann and
Casella (1998, Section 1.8).


## 5.6 Generating a Random Sample

Thus far we have been concerned with the many methods of describing the behav-
ior of random variables-transformations, distributions, moment calculations, limit
theorems. In practice, these random variables are used to describe and mc lel real
phenomena, and observations on these random variables are the data that we collect.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="246" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

Thus, typically, we observe random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a distribution $f \left( x | \theta \right)$
and are most concerned with using properties of $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ to describe the behavior of
the random variables. In this section we are, in effect, going to turn that strategy
around. Here we are concerned with generating a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a
given distribution $f \left( x | \theta \right) .$

Example 5.6.1 (Exponential lifetime) Suppose that a particular electrical com-
ponent is to be modeled with an exponential(A) lifetime. The manufacturer is inter-
ested in determining the probability that, out of $c$ components, at least $t$ of them will
last $h$ hours. Taking this one $s t e p \quad a t$ a time, we have

$$p _ { 1 } = P \left( \text { component lasts at least } h \text { hours } \right)$$

(5.6.1)
$$= P \left( X \geq h | \lambda \right) ,$$

and assuming that the components are independent, we can model the outcomes of
the $c$ components as Bernoulli trials, so

$$p _ { 2 } = P \left( \text { at least } t \text { components last } h \text { hours } \right)$$

(5.6.2)
$$= \sum _ { k = t } ^ { c } \left( \begin{array}{} { c } \\ { k } \end{array} \right) p _ { 1 } ^ { k } \left( 1 - p _ { 1 } \right) ^ { c - k } .$$

Although calculation of (5.6.2) is straightforward, it can be computationally bur-
densome, especially if both $t$ and $c$ are large numbers. Moreover, the exponential
model has the advantage that $p _ { 1 }$ can be expressed in closed form, that is,

(5.6.3)
$$p _ { 1 } = \int _ { h } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - x / \lambda } d x = e ^ { - h / \lambda } .$$

However, if each component were modeled with, say, a gamma distribution, then $p _ { 1 }$
may not be expressible in closed form. This would make calculation of $p _ { 2 }$ even more
involved.

!!

A simulation approach to the calculation of expressions such as (5.6.2) is to generate
random variables with the desired distribution and then use the Weak Law of Large
Numbers (Theorem 5.5.2) to validate the simulation. That is, if $Y _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ are
iid, then a consequence of that theorem (provided the assumptions hold) is

(5.6.4)
$$\frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } h \left( Y _ { i } \right) \rightarrow E h \left( Y \right)$$

in probability, as $n \rightarrow \infty$ . (Expression (5.6.4) also holds almost everywhere, a conse-
quence of Theorem 5.5.9, the Strong Law of Large Numbers.)

Example 5.6.2 (Continuation of Example 5.6.1) The probability $p _ { 2 }$ can be
calculated using the following steps. For $j = 1 , \ldots , n :$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="247" -->
<!-- PageHeader="GENERATING A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

a. Generate $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { c }$ iid ~ exponential(X).

$b .$ Set $Y _ { j } = 1$ if at least $t X _ { i } s$ are $\geq h$ otherwise, set $Y _ { j } = 0 .$
Then, because $Y _ { j } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( p _ { 2 } \right)$ and $\mathrm { E } Y _ { j } = p _ { 2 } ,$

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { j } \rightarrow p _ { 2 } \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty .$$

||

Examples 5.6.1 and 5.6.2 highlight the major concerns of this section. First, we must
examine how to generate the random variables that we need, and second, we then use
a version of the Law of Large Numbers to validate the simulation approximation.

Since we have to start somewhere, we start with the assumption that we can gen-
erate iid uniform random variables $U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { m } .$ (This problem of generating uniform
random numbers has been worked on, with great success, by computer scientists.)
There exist many algorithms for generating pseudo-random numbers that will pass
almost all tests for uniformity. Moreover, most good statistical packages have a rea-
sonable uniform random number generator. (See Devroye 1985 or Ripley 1987 for
more on generating pseudo-random numbers.)

Since we are starting from the uniform random variables, our problem here is really
not the problem of generating the desired random variables, but rather of transforming
the uniform random variables to the desired distribution. In essence, there are two
general methodologies for doing this, which we shall (noninformatively) call direct
and indirect methods.


### 5.6.1 Direct Methods

A direct method of generating a random variable is one for which there exists a closed-
form function $g \left( u \right)$ such that the transformed variable $Y = g \left( U \right)$ has the desired
distribution when $U \sim u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ As might be recalled, this was already accom-
plished for continuous random variables in Theorem 2.1.10, the Probability Integral
Transform, where any distribution was transformed to the uniform. Hence the inverse
transformation solves our problem.

Example 5.6.3 (Probability Integral Transform) If $Y$ is a continuous random
variable with cdf $F _ { Y } ,$ then Theorem 2.1.10 implies that the random variable $F _ { Y } ^ { - 1 } \left( U \right) ,$
where $U \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ has distribution $F _ { Y } .$ If $Y \sim \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ then

$$F _ { Y } ^ { - 1 } \left( U \right) = - \lambda \log \left( 1 - U \right)$$

is an exponential(1) random variable (see Exercise 5.49).

Thus, if we generate $U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { n }$ as iid uniform random variables, $Y _ { i } = - \lambda \log \left( 1 - \right.$
$U _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ are id exponential() random variables. As an example, for $n =$
10,000, we generate $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \cdots , u _ { 1 0 , 0 0 0 }$ and calculate

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \sum u _ { i } = . 5 0 1 9$$
$$\frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum \left( u _ { i } - \bar { u } \right) ^ { 2 } = . 0 8 4 2 .$$
and

From (5.6.4), which follows from the WLLN (Theorem 5.5.2), we know that $\bar { U } \rightarrow$
$E U = 1 / 2$ and, from Example 5.5.3, $S ^ { 2 } \rightarrow V a r U = 1 / 1 2 = . 0 8 3 3 ,$ so our estimates

<!-- PageNumber="248" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

<figcaption>Figure 5.6.1. Histogram of 10, 000 observations from an exponential pdf with $\lambda = 2 ,$ together with the pdf</figcaption>

$y$

</figure>


are quite close to the true parameters. The transformed variables $Y _ { i } = - 2 \log \left( 1 - u _ { i } \right)$
have an exponential(2) distribution, and we find that

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \sum y _ { i } = 2 . 0 0 0 4$$
$$\frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } = 4 . 0 9 0 8 ,$$
and

in close agreement with $E Y = 2$ and Var $Y = 4 .$ Figure 5.6.1 illustrates the agreement
between the sample histogram and the population pdf.
The relationship between the exponential and other distributions allows the quick
generation of many random variables. For example, if $U _ { j }$ are iid uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ random
variables, then $Y _ { j } = - \lambda \log \left( u _ { j } \right)$ are iid exponential $\left( \lambda \right)$ random variables and
11
(5.6.5)

$$Y = - 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \nu } \log \left( U _ { j } \right) \sim \chi _ { 2 \nu } ^ { 2 } ,$$
$$Y = - \beta \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { a } \log \left( U _ { j } \right) \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( a , \beta \right) ,$$
$$Y = \frac { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { a } \log \left( U _ { j } \right) } { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { a + b } \log \left( U _ { j } \right) } \sim \text { beta } \left( a , b \right)$$

Many other variations are possible (see Exercises 5.47-5.49), but all are being driven
by the exponential-uniform transformation.

Unfortunately, there are limits to this transformation. For example, we cannot use
it to generate $x ^ { 2 }$ random variables with odd degrees of freedom. Hence, we cannot
get a $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ , which would in turn get us a normal(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ - an extremely useful variable
to be able to generate. We will return to this problem in the next subsection.

Recall that the basis of Example 5.6.3 and hence the transformations in (5.6.5) was
the Probability Integral Transform, which, in general, can be written as

(5.6.6)
$$F _ { Y } ^ { - 1 } \left( u \right) = y \leftrightarrow u = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { y } f _ { y } \left( t \right) d t .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="249" -->
<!-- PageHeader="GENERATING A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

Application of this formula to the exponential distribution was particularly handy,
as the integral equation had a simple solution (see also Exercise 5.56). However, in
many cases no closed-form solution for (5.6.6) will exist. Thus, each random variable
generation will necessitate a solution of an integral equation, which, in practice, could
be prohibitively long and complicated. This would be the case, for example, if (5.6.6)
were used to generate a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } .$

When no closed-form solution for (5.6.6) exists, other options should be explored.
These include other types of generation methods and indirect methods. As an example
of the former, consider the following.

Example 5.6.4 (Box-Muller algorithm) Generate $U _ { 1 }$ and $U _ { 2 } ,$ two independent
$\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables, and set

$$R = \sqrt { - 2 \log U _ { 2 } } \text { and } \theta = 2 \pi U _ { 1 } .$$

Then

$$X = R \cos \theta \quad \text { and } \quad Y = R \sin \theta$$

are independent normal(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ random variables. Thus, although we had no quick trans-
formation for generating a single $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable, there is such a method for
generating two variables. (See Exercise 5.50.)
Il

Unfortunately, solutions such as those in Example 5.6.4 are not plentiful. Moreover,
they take advantage of the specific structure of certain distributions and are, thus,
less applicable as general strategies. It turns out that, for the most part, generation of
other continuous distributions (than those already considered) is best accomplished
through indirect methods. Before exploring these, we end this subsection with a look
at where $\left( 5 . 6 . 6 \right)$ is quite useful: the case of discrete random variables.

If $Y$ is a discrete random variable taking on values $y _ { 1 } < y _ { 2 } < \cdots < y _ { k } ,$ then
analogous to (5.6.6) we can write

(5.6.7)
$$P \left[ F _ { Y } \left( y _ { i } \right) < U \leq F _ { Y } \left( y _ { i + 1 } \right) \right] = F _ { Y } \left( y _ { i + 1 } \right) - F _ { Y } \left( y _ { i } \right)$$
$$= P \left( Y = y _ { i + 1 } \right) .$$

Implementation of (5.6.7) to generate discrete random variables is actually quite
straightforward and can be summarized as follows. To generate $Y _ { i } \sim F _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$

a. Generate $U \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

b. If $F _ { y } \left( y _ { i } \right) < U \leq F _ { y } \left( y _ { i + 1 } \right) ,$ set $Y = y _ { i + 1 } .$

We define $y _ { 0 } = - \infty$ and $F _ { Y } \left( y _ { 0 } \right) = 0 .$

Example 5.6.5 (Binomial random variable generation) To generate a $Y$ ~
binomial(4, $\left( 4 , \frac { 5 } { 8 } \right) ,$ for example, generate $U \sim u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ and set

$$Y = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { \text { if } 0 } & { < U \leq . 0 2 0 } \\ { 1 } & { \text { if } . 0 2 0 < U \leq . 1 5 2 } \\ { 2 } & { \text { if } . 1 5 2 < U \leq . 4 8 1 } \\ { 3 } & { \text { if } . 4 8 1 < U \leq . 8 4 7 } \\ { 4 } & { \text { if } . 8 4 7 < U \leq . 8 4 7 } \end{array} \right.$$
(5.6.8)

|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="250" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

The algorithm (5.6.8) also works if the range of the discrete random variable is
infinite, say Poisson or negative binomial. Although, theoretically, this could require a
large number of evaluations, in practice this does not happen because there are simple
and clever ways of speeding up the algorithm. For example, instead of checking each
$y _ { i }$ in the order $1 , 2 , \cdots .$ , it can be much faster to start checking $y _ { i } s$ near the mean.
(See Ripley 1987, Section 3.3, and Exercise 5.55.)

We will see many uses of simulation methodology. To start off, consider the following
exploration of the Poisson distribution, which is a version of the parametric bootstrap
that we will see in Section 10.1.4.

Example 5.6.6 (Distribution of the Poisson variance) If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid
$\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ then by either Theorem 5.2.7 or 5.2.11 the distribution of $\sum X _ { i }$ is
$\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( n \lambda \right) .$ Thus, it is quite easy to describe the distribution of the sample mean $\bar { X } .$
However, describing the distribution of the sample variance, $S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$
is not a simple task.

The distribution of $S ^ { 2 }$ is quite simple to simulate, however. Figure 5.6.2 shows
such a histogram. Moreover, the simulated samples can also be used to calculate
probabilities about $S ^ { 2 } .$ If $S _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ is the value calculated from the $i$ ith simulated sample,
then

$$\frac { 1 } { M } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { M } I \left( S _ { i } ^ { 2 } \geq a \right) \rightarrow P _ { \lambda } \left( S ^ { 2 } \geq a \right)$$

as $M \rightarrow \infty .$

To illustrate the use of such methodology consider the following sample of bay
anchovy larvae counts taken from the Hudson River in late August 1984:

(5.6.9)
$$1 9 , 3 2 , 2 9 , 1 3 , 8 , 1 2 , 1 6 , 2 0 , 1 4 , 1 7 , 2 2 , 1 8 , 2 3 .$$

If it is assumed that the larvae are distributed randomly and uniformly in the river,
then the number that are collected in a fixed size net should follow a Poisson dis-
tribution. Such an argument follows from a spatial version of the Poisson postulates
(see the Miscellanea of Chapter 2). To see if such an assumption is tenable, we can
check whether the mean and variance of the observed data are consistent with the
Poisson assumptions.

For the data in $\left( 5 . 6 . 9 \right)$ we calculate $\bar { x } = 1 8 . 6 9$ and $s ^ { 2 } = 4 4 . 9 0 .$ Under the Poisson
assumptions we expect these values to be the same. Of course, due to sampling vari-
ability, they will not be exactly the same, and we can use a simulation to get some
idea of what to expect. In Figure 5.6.2 we simulated 5,000 samples of size $n = 1 3$
from a Poisson distribution with $\lambda = 1 8 . 6 9 ,$ and constructed the relative frequency
histogram of $S ^ { 2 } .$ Note that the observed value of $S ^ { 2 } = 4 4 . 9 0$ falls into the tail of
the distribution. In fact, since 27 of the values of $S ^ { 2 }$ were greater than 44.90, we can
estimate

$$P \left( S ^ { 2 } > 4 4 . 9 0 | \lambda = 1 8 . 6 9 \right) \approx \frac { 1 } { 5 0 0 0 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 5 0 0 0 } I \left( S _ { i } ^ { 2 } > 4 4 . 9 0 \right) = \frac { 2 7 } { 5 0 0 0 } = . 0 0 5 4 ,$$

<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->
<!-- PageNumber="251" -->
<!-- PageHeader="GENERATING $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->

which leads us to question the Poisson assumption; see Exercise 5.54. (Such findings
spawned the extremely bad bilingual pun "Something is fishy in the Hudson-the
Poisson has failed.")

Il


### 5.6.2 Indirect Methods

When no easily found direct transformation is available to generate the desired ran-
dom variable, an extremely powerful indirect method, the Accept/Reject Algorithm,
can often provide a solution. The idea behind the Accept/Reject Algorithm is, per-
haps, best explained through a simple example.

Example 5.6.7 (Beta random variable generation-I) Suppose the goal is to
generate $Y \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( a , b \right) .$ If both $a$ and $b$ are integers, then the direct transformation
method (5.6.5) can be used. However, if $a$ and $b$ are not integers, then that method
will not work. For definiteness, set $a = 2 . 7$ and $b = 6 . 3 .$ In Figure 5.6.3 we have put
the beta density $f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ inside a box with sides 1 and $c \geq \max _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ Now consider
the following method of calculating $P \left( Y \leq y \right) .$ If $\left( U , V \right)$ are independent uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$
random variables, then the probability of the shaded area is

$$P \left( V \leq y , U \leq \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { y } \int _ { 0 } ^ { f _ { Y } \left( v \right) / c } d u d v .$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { c } \int _ { 0 } ^ { y } f _ { Y } \left( v \right) d v$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { c } P \left( Y \leq y \right) .$$

(5.6.10)

So we can calculate the beta probabilities from the uniform probabilities, which sug-
gests that we can generate the beta random variable from the uniform random vari-
ables.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="252" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

<figcaption>Figure 5.6.3. The beta distribution with $a = 2 . 7$ and $b = 6 . 3$ with $c = \max _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) = 2 . 6 6 9 .$ The uniform random variable $V$ gives the x-coordinate, and we use $U$ to test if we are under the density.</figcaption>
