</figure>


From (5.6.10), if we set $y = 1 ,$ then we have $\frac { 1 } { c } = P \left( U < \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) ,$ so

$$P \left( Y \leq y \right) = \frac { P \left( V \leq y , U \leq \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) } { P \left( U \leq \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) }$$
$$= P \left( V \leq y | U \leq \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) ,$$

which suggests the following algorithm.
To generate $Y \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( a , b \right) :$
a. Generate $\left( U , V \right)$ independent $u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$
b. If $U < \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) ,$ set $Y = V ;$ otherwise, return to step (a).
This algorithm generates a $b e t a \left( a , b \right)$ random variable as long as $c \geq \max _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ and,
in fact, can be generalized to any bounded density with bounded support (Exercises
5.59 and 5.60).
11
(5.6.11)

It should be clear that the optimal choice of $c$ is $c = \max _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$ To see why this
is so, note that the algorithm is open-ended in the sense that we do not know how
many $\left( U , V \right)$ pairs will be needed in order to get one $Y$ variable. However, if we define
the random variable

(5.6.12)
$$N = n u m b e r \quad o f \left( U , V \right) p a i r s \quad r e q u i r e d \quad f o r \quad o n e \quad Y ,$$

then, recalling that $\frac { 1 } { c } = P \left( U \leq \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) \right) ,$ we see that $N$ is a $g e o m e t r i c \left( 1 / c \right)$ random
variable. Thus to generate one $Y$ we expect to need $E \left( N \right) = c$ pairs $\left( U , V \right) ,$ and in
this sense minimizing $c$ will optimize the algorithm.

Examining Figure 5.6.3 we see that the algorithm is wasteful in the area where
$U > \frac { 1 } { c } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) .$ This is because we are using a uniform random variable $V$ to get a beta
random variable $Y .$ To improve, we might start with something closer to the beta.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="253" -->
<!-- PageHeader="GENERATING $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

The testing step, step (b) of the algorithm, can be thought of as testing whether
the random variable $V$ "looks like" it could come from the density $f _ { Y } .$ Suppose that
$V \sim f _ { V } ,$ and we compute

$$M = \sup _ { y } \frac { f _ { Y } \left( y \right) } { f _ { V } \left( y \right) } < \infty .$$

A generalization of step (b) is to compare $U \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ to $\frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) .$ The
larger this ratio is, the more $V$ "looks like" a random variable from the density $f _ { Y } ,$
and the more likely it is that $U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) .$ This is the basis of the general
Accept/Reject Algorithm.


### 5.6.3 The Accept/Reject Algorithm

Theorem 5.6.8 Let $Y \sim f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ and $V \sim f _ { V } \left( V \right) ,$ where $f _ { Y }$ and $f _ { V }$ have common
support with

$$M = \sup _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) / f _ { V } \left( y \right) < \infty .$$

To generate a random variable $Y \sim f _ { Y }$ :

a. Generate $U \sim u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $V \sim f _ { V } ,$ independent.

$b .$ If $U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) ,$ set $Y = V ;$ otherwise, return to step (a).

Proof: The generated random variable $Y$ has cdf

$$P \left( Y \leq y \right) = P \left( V \leq y | \text { stop } \right)$$
$$= P \left( V \leq y | U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) \right)$$
$$= \frac { P \left( V \leq y , U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) \right) } { P \left( U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) \right) }$$
$$= \frac { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { y } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( v \right) / f _ { V } \left( v \right) } d u f _ { V } \left( v \right) d v } { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( v \right) / f _ { V } \left( v \right) } d u f _ { V } \left( v \right) d v }$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { y } f _ { Y } \left( v \right) d v ,$$

which is the desired cdf.

Note also that

$$M = \sup _ { u } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) / f _ { V } \left( y \right)$$
$$= \left[ P \left( U < \frac { 1 } { M } f _ { Y } \left( V \right) / f _ { V } \left( V \right) \right) \right] ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { P \left( \text { Stop } \right) }$$

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="254" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.6" -->

so the number of trials needed to generate one $Y$ is a $g e o m e t r i c \left( 1 / M \right)$ random variable,
and $M$ is the expected number of trials.

Example 5.6.9 (Beta random variable generation-II) To generate $Y$ ~
$\mathrm { b e t a } \left( 2 . 7 , 6 . 3 \right)$ consider the algorithm:

a. Generate $U \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $V \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( 2 , 6 \right) .$

b. If $U < \frac { 1 } { M } \frac { f _ { Y } \left( V \right) } { f _ { V } \left( V \right) } ,$ set $Y = V ;$ otherwise, return to step (a).

This Accept/Reject Algorithm will generate the required $Y$ as long as
$\sup _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) / f _ { V } \left( y \right) \leq M < \infty .$ For the given densities we have $M = 1 . 6 7 ,$ $s o$ the
requirement is satisfied. (See Exercise 5.63.)

For this algorithm $E N = 1 . 6 7 ,$ while for the algorithm of Example 5.6.7, which
uses the uniform $V ,$ we have $E N = 2 . 6 7 .$ Although this seems to indicate that the
latter algorithm is faster, remember that generating a beta(2,6) random variable
will need eight uniform random variables. Thus, comparison of algorithms is not
always straightforward and will include consideration of both computer speed and
programming ease.
I

The importance of the requirement that $M < \infty$ should be stressed. This can be
interpreted as requiring the density of $V$ (often called the candidate density) to have
heavier tails than the density of $Y$ (often called the target density). This requirement
tends to ensure that we will obtain a good representation of the values of $Y ,$ even
those values that are in the tails. For example, if $V \sim \mathrm { C a u c h y }$ and $Y \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ then
we expect the range of $V$ samples to be wider than that of $Y$ samples, and we should
get good performance from an Accept/Reject Algorithm based on these densities.
However, it is much more difficult to change $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables into Cauchy
random variables because the extremes will be underrepresented.

There are cases, however, where the target density has heavy tails, and it is $\mathrm { d i f f i c y l }$
to get candidate densities that will result in finite values of $M .$ In such cases the
Accept/Reject Algorithm will no longer apply, and one is led to another class of
methods known as Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods. Special cases of
such methods are known as the Gibbs Sampler and the Metropolis Algorithm. We
state the latter.

Metropolis Algorithm Let $Y \sim f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ and $V \sim f _ { V } \left( v \right) ,$ where $f _ { Y }$ and $f _ { V }$ have
common support. To generate $Y \sim f _ { Y } :$

0\. Generate $V \sim f _ { V } .$ Set $Z _ { 0 } = V .$

For $i = 1 , 2 , \cdots :$

1\. Generate $U _ { i } \sim u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ , $V _ { i } \sim f _ { V } ,$ and calculate

$$\rho _ { i } = \min \left\{ \frac { f _ { Y } \left( V _ { i } \right) } { f _ { V } \left( V _ { i } \right) } \cdot \frac { f _ { V } \left( Z _ { i - 1 } \right) } { f _ { Y } \left( Z _ { i - 1 } \right) } , 1 \right\} .$$

2\. Set

$$Z _ { i } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { V _ { i } } & { \text { if } U _ { i } \leq \rho _ { i } } \\ { Z _ { i - 1 } } & { \text { if } U _ { i } > \rho _ { i } } \end{array} \right.$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="255" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

Then, as $i \rightarrow \infty$ , $Z _ { i }$ converges to $Y$ in distribution.

Although the algorithm does not require a finite $M ,$ it does not produce a random
variable with exactly the density $f _ { Y } ,$ but rather a convergent sequence. In practice,
after the algorithm is run for a while (¿ gets big), the $Z s$ that are produced behave
very much like variables from $f _ { Y } .$ (See Chib and Greenberg 1995 for an elementary
introduction to the Metropolis Algorithm.)

Although MCMC methods can be traced back to at least Metropolis et al. (1953),
they entered real prominence with the work of Gelfand and Smith (1990), building
on that of Geman and Geman (1984). See Miscellanea 5.8.5 for more details.


## 5.7 Exercises

5.1 Color blindness appears in 1% of the people in a certain population. How large must
a sample be if the probability of its containing a color-blind person is to be .95 or
more? (Assume that the population is large enough to be considered infinite, so that
sampling can be considered to be with replacement.)

5.2 Suppose $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ are jointly continuous and independent, each distributed with
☒ ☒ ☒

marginal pdf $f \left( x \right) ,$ where each $X _ { i }$ represents annual rainfall at a given location.

(a) Find the distribution of the number of years until the first year's rainfall, $X _ { 1 } ,$ is
☒

exceeded for the first time.

(b) Show that the mean number of years until $X _ { 1 }$ is exceeded for the first time is
infinite.
☒

5.3 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid random variables with continuous cdf $F _ { X } ,$ and suppose $\mathrm { E } X _ { i } = \mu .$
Define the random variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ by
☒

$$Y _ { i } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \text { if } X _ { i } > \mu } \\ { 0 } & { \text { if } X _ { i } \leq \mu } \end{array} \right.$$

Find the distribution of $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i } .$
☒

5.4 A generalization of iid random variables is exchangeable random variables, an idea
due to deFinetti (1972). A discussion of exchangeability can also be found in Feller
(1971). The random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are exchangeable if any permutation of any
☒ ☒

subset of them of size $k$ $\left( k \leq n \right.$ has the same distribution. In this exercise we will
see an example of random variables that are exchangeable but not $\mathrm { i i d } .$ Let $X _ { i } | P \sim \mathrm { i i d }$
☒
Bernoulli(P), $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ and let $P \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

(a) Show that the marginal distribution of any $k$ of the $X s$ is the same as

$$P \left( X _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { k } = x _ { k } \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } p ^ { t } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { k - t } d p = \frac { t ! \left( k - t \right) ! } { \left( k + 1 \right) ! } ,$$

where $t = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } x _ { i } .$ Hence, the $X \mathrm { s }$ are exchangeable.

(b) Show that, marginally,

$$P \left( X _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } = x _ { n } \right) \neq \Pi _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } P \left( X _ { i } = x _ { i } \right) ,$$ ☒

so the distribution of the $X s$ is exchangeable but not iid.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="256" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

(deFinetti proved an elegant characterization theorem for an infinite sequence of ex-
changeable random variables. He proved that any such sequence of exchangeable ran-
dom variables is a mixture of iid random variables.)

5.5 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ and let $\bar { X }$ denote the sample mean. Show that
☒ ☒

$$f _ { \bar { X } } \left( x \right) = n f _ { X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n } } \left( n x \right) ,$$

even $\mathrm { i f }$ the mgf of $X$ does not exist.

5.6 If $X$ has pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $Y ,$ independent of $X ,$ has pdf $f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$ establish formulas, similar
to (5.2.3), for the random variable $Z$ in each of the following situations.

(a) $Z = X \quad - Y$

(b) $Z = X Y$

(c) $Z = X / Y$

5.7 In Example 5.2.10, a partial fraction decomposition is needed to derive the distribution
of the sum of two independent Cauchy random variables. This exercise provides the
details that are skipped in that example.

(a) Find the constants $A ,$ $B ,$ $C ,$ and $D$ that satisfy

$$\frac { 1 } { 1 + \left( w / \sigma \right) ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { 1 + \left( \left( z - w \right) / \tau \right) ^ { 2 } } =$$
$$\frac { A w } { 1 + \left( w / \sigma \right) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { B } { 1 + \left( w / \sigma \right) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { C w } { 1 + \left( \left( z - w \right) / \tau \right) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { D } { 1 + \left( \left( z - w \right) / \tau \right) ^ { 2 } } ,$$

where $A ,$ $B ,$ $C ,$ and $D$ may depend on $z$ but not on w.

(b) Using the facts that

$$\int \frac { t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \quad \text { and } \quad \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } } d t = \arctan \left( t \right) + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,$$ ☒

evaluate (5.2.4) and hence verify (5.2.5).

(Note that the integration in part (b) is quite delicate. Since the mean of a Cauchy
does not exist, the integrals $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { A w } { 1 + \left( w / \sigma \right) ^ { 2 } } d w$ and $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { C w } { 1 + \left( \left( z - w \right) / \tau \right) ^ { 2 } }$ dw do not exist.
However, the integral of the difference does exist, which is all that is needed.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="257" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

5.8 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample, where $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are calculated in the usual way.

(a) Show that

$$S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 n \left( n - 1 \right) } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - X _ { j } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Assume now that the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ have a finite fourth moment, and denote $\theta _ { 1 } = \mathrm { E X } _ { i } ,$ $\theta _ { j } =$
$\mathrm { E } \left( X _ { i } - \theta _ { 1 } \right) ^ { j } ,$ $j = 2 , 3 , 4 .$

(b) Show that Var $S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \theta _ { 4 } - \frac { n - 3 } { n - 1 } \theta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) .$

(c) Find $\mathrm { C o v } \left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ in terms of $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { 4 } .$ Under what conditions is $\mathrm { C o v } \left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right) = 0 ?$

5.9 Establish the Lagrange Identity, that for any numbers $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ and $b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots , b _ { n } ,$

$$\left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) - \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } b _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sum _ { j = i + 1 } ^ { n } \left( a _ { i } b _ { j } - a _ { j } b _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Use the identity to show that the squared correlation coefficient is equal to 1 if and
only if all of the sample points lie on a straight line (Wright 1992). (Hint: Establish
the identity for $n = 2 ;$ then induct.)

5.10 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population.

(a) Find expressions for $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { 4 } ,$ as defined in Exercise 5.8, in terms of $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Use the results of Exercise 5.8, together with the results of part (a), to calculate
Var $S ^ { 2 } .$

(c) Calculate Var $S ^ { 2 }$ a completely different (and easier) way: Use the fact that
$\left( n - 1 \right) S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } .$

5.11 Suppose $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are calculated from a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ drawn from a
population with finite variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ We know that $E S ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Prove that $\mathrm { E } S < \sigma ,$ and
if $\sigma ^ { 2 } > 0 ,$ then $\mathrm { E } S < \sigma .$

5.12 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ population. Define

$$Y _ { 1 } = | \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } | , \quad Y _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } | X _ { i } | .$$

Calculate $\mathrm { E } Y _ { 1 }$ and $\mathrm { E } Y _ { 2 } ,$ and establish an inequality between them.

5.13 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ Find a function of $S ^ { 2 } ,$ the sample variance, say $g \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
that satisfies $\mathrm { E g } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = \sigma .$ (Hint: Try $g \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = c \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } } ,$ where $c$ is a constant.)

5.14 (a) Prove that the statement of Lemma 5.3.3 follows from the special case of $\mu _ { i } = 0$
and $\sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } = 1 .$ That is, show that if $X _ { j } = \sigma _ { j } Z _ { j } + \mu _ { j }$ and $Z _ { j } \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , n ,$
all independent, $a _ { i j } ,$ $b _ { r j }$ are constants, and

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } Z _ { j } , \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } Z _ { j } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } Z _ { j } \mathrm { a n d } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } \mathrm { a r e } \mathrm { i n d e } _ { \mathrm { I } } ,$$

then

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } X _ { j } , \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } X _ { j } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } X _ { j } \mathrm { a n d } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } X _ { j } \text { are in }$$
independent.

(b) Verify the expression for $\mathrm { C o v } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } X _ { j } , \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { r j } X _ { j } \right)$ in Lemma 5.3.3.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="258" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF $A$ RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

5.15 Establish the following recursion relations for means and variances. Let $\bar { X } _ { n }$ and $S _ { n } ^ { 2 }$ be
the mean and variance, respectively, of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } .$ Then suppose another observation,
☒
$X _ { n + 1 } ,$ becomes available. Show that

(a) $\bar { X } _ { n + 1 } = \frac { X _ { n + 1 } + n \bar { X } _ { n } } { n + 1 }$

(b) $n S _ { n + 1 } ^ { 2 } = \left( n - 1 \right) S _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { n } { n + 1 } \right) \left( X _ { n + 1 } - \bar { X } _ { n } \right) ^ { 2 } .$
☒
situations, use the $X _ { i } s$ to construct a statistic with the indicated distribution.
☒
·

5.16 Let $X _ { i } ,$ $= 1 , 2 , 3 ,$ be independent with $n \left( i , i ^ { 2 } \right)$ distributions. For each of the following

(a) chi squared with 3 degrees of freedom

(b) t distribution with 2 degrees of freedom

(c) $F$ distribution with 1 and 2 degrees of freedom

5.17 Let $X$ be a random variable with an $F _ { p , q }$ distribution.
☒

(a) Derive the pdf of $X .$

(b) Derive the mean and variance of $X .$

(c) Show that $1 / X$ has an $F _ { q , p }$ distribution.
☒

(d) Show that $\left( p / q \right) X / \left[ 1 + \left( p / q \right) X \right]$ has a beta distribution with parameters $p / 2$ and
☒ ☒
$q / 2 .$

5.18 Let $X$ be a random variable with a Student's t distribution with $p$ degrees of freedom.

(a) Derive the mean and variance of $X .$
☒

(b) Show that $X ^ { 2 }$ has an $F$ distribution with 1 and $p$ degrees of freedom.
☒

(c) Let $f \left( x | p \right)$ denote the pdf of $X .$ Show that

$$\lim _ { p \rightarrow \infty } f \left( x | p \right) \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$

at each value of x, $- \infty < x < \infty .$ This correctly suggests that as $p \rightarrow \infty ,$ $X$ con-
verges in distribution to a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable. (Hint: Use Stirling's Formula.)

(d) Use the results of parts (a) and (b) to argue that, as $p \rightarrow \infty ,$ $X ^ { 2 }$ converges in
distribution to a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ random variable.
☒

(e) What might you conjecture about the distributional limit, as $p \rightarrow \infty ,$ of $q F _ { q , p }$

5.19 (a) Prove that the $x ^ { 2 }$ distribution is stochastically increasing in its degrees of freedom;
☒
that is, if $p > q ,$ then for any a, $P \left( x _ { p } ^ { 2 } > a \right) \geq P \left( x _ { q } ^ { 2 } > a \right) ,$ with strict inequality for
some $a .$

(b) Use the results of part $\left( a \right)$ to prove that for any $\nu ,$ $k F _ { k , \nu }$ is stochastically increasing
in $k .$

(c) Show that for any $k ,$ $\nu$ v, and $\alpha ,$ $k F _ { \alpha , k , \nu } > \left( k - 1 \right) F _ { \alpha , k - 1 , \nu } .$ (The notation $F _ { \alpha , k - 1 , \nu } ,$
denotes a level-a cutoff point; see Section 8.3.1. Also see Miscellanea 8.5.1 and
Exercise 11.15.)

5.20 (a) We can see that the $t$ distribution is a mixture of normals using the following
argument:

$$P \left( T _ { \nu } \leq t \right) = P \left( \frac { Z } { \sqrt { \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } / \nu } } \leq t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } P \left( Z \leq t \sqrt { x } / \sqrt { \nu } \right) P \left( \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } = x \right) d x ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="259" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

where $T _ { \nu }$ is a $t$ random variable with $\nu$ degrees of freedom. Using the Fundamental
Theorem of Calculus and interpreting $P \left( \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } = \nu x \right)$ as a pdf, we obtain

$$f _ { T _ { \nu } } \left( t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - t ^ { 2 } x / 2 \nu } \frac { \sqrt { x } } { \sqrt { \nu } } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \nu / 2 \right) 2 ^ { \nu / 2 } } \left( x \right) ^ { \left( \nu / 2 \right) - 1 } e ^ { - x / 2 } d x ,$$ ☒

a scale mixture of normals. Verify this formula by direct integration.

(b) A similar formula holds for the $F$ distribution; that is, it can be written as a
mixture of chi squareds. If $F _ { 1 , \nu }$ is an $F$ random variable with 1 and $\nu$ degrees of
freedom, then we can write

$$P \left( F _ { 1 , \nu } \leq \nu t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } P \left( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \leq t y \right) f _ { \nu } \left( y \right) d y ,$$

where $f _ { \nu } \left( y \right)$ is a $x ^ { 2 }$ pdf. Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to obtain an
integral expression for the pdf of $F _ { 1 , \nu } ,$ and show that the integral equals the pdf.

(c) Verify that the generalization of part (b),

$$P \left( F _ { m , \nu } \leq \frac { \nu } { m } t \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } P \left( x _ { m } ^ { 2 } \leq t y \right) f _ { \nu } \left( y \right) d y ,$$

is valid for all integers $m > 1 .$

5.21 What is the probability that the larger of two continuous iid random variables will
exceed the population median? Generalize this result to samples of size n.

5.22 Let $X$ and $Y$ be iid $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables, and define $Z = \min \left( X , Y \right) .$ Prove that
☒ ☒ ☒

$Z ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } .$

5.23 Let $U _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , 2 , \cdots ,$ be independent $u n i f o r m \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables, and let $X$ have
☒
distribution

$$P \left( X = x \right) = \frac { c } { x ! } , \quad x = 1 , 2 , 3 , \cdots ,$$

where $c = 1 / \left( e \quad - \quad 1 \right) .$ Find the distribution of

$$Z = \min \left\{ U _ { 1 } , \ldots , U _ { X } \right\} .$$

(Hint: Note that the distribution of $Z | X = x$ is that of the first-order statistic from
a sample of size x.)
☒

5.24 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a population with pdf
☒ ☒

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 / \theta } & { \text { if } 0 < x < \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( n \right) }$ be the order statistics. Show that $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } / X _ { \left( n \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ are
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

☒
independent random variables.

5.25 As a generalization of the previous exercise, let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf
☒ ☒

$$f _ { X } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \frac { a } { \theta ^ { a } } x ^ { a - 1 } } & { \text { if } 0 < x < \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( n \right) }$ be the order statistics. Show that $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } / X _ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$ $X _ { \left( 2 \right) } / X _ { \left( 3 \right) } , \ldots ,$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
$X _ { \left( n - 1 \right) } / X _ { \left( n \right) } ,$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) }$ are mutually independent random variables. Find the distri-
☒ ☒ ☒

bution of each of them.

5.26 Complete the proof of Theorem 5.4.6.

(a) Let $U$ be a random variable that counts the number of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ less than or
☒ ☒
equal to $u ,$ and let $V$ be a random variable that counts the number of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
☒ ☒ ☒
greater than $u$ and less than or equal to $v .$ Show that $\left( U , V , n \quad - \quad U \quad - \quad V \right)$ is a
multinomial random vector $w i t h \quad n$ trials and cell probabilities $\left( F _ { X } \left( u \right) , F _ { X } \left( v \right) - \right.$
$\left. F _ { X } \left( u \right) , 1 - F _ { X } \left( v \right) \right) .$

(b) Show that the joint $c d f$ of $X _ { \left( i \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( j \right) }$ can be expressed as
☒ ☒

$$F _ { X _ { \left( i \right) } , X _ { \left( j \right) } } \left( u , v \right) = P \left( U \geq i , U + V \geq j \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { k = i } ^ { j - 1 } \sum _ { m = j - k } ^ { n - k } P \left( U = k , V = m \right) + P \left( U \geq j \right)$$
$$= \sum _ { k = i } ^ { j - 1 } \sum _ { m = j - k } ^ { n - k } \frac { n ! } { k ! m ! \left( n - k - m \right) ! } \left[ F _ { X } \left( u \right) \right] ^ { k } \left[ F _ { X } \left( v \right) - F _ { X } \left( u \right) \right] ^ { m }$$
$$\times \left[ 1 - F _ { X } \left( v \right) \right] ^ { n - k - m } + P \left( U \geq j \right) .$$

(c) Find the joint pdf by computing the mixed partial as indicated in $\left( 4 . 1 . 4 \right) .$
(The mixed partial of $P \left( U \geq j \right)$ is 0 since this term depends only on $u ,$ not $v .$ For
the other terms, there is much cancellation using relationships like (5.4.6).)

5.27 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ and let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( n \right) }$ be
☒ ☒ ☒

the order statistics.

(a) Find an expression for the conditional $p d f$ of $X _ { \left( i \right) }$ given $X _ { \left( j \right) }$ in terms of $f _ { X }$ and
$F _ { X } .$
☒ ☒

(b) Find the pdf of $V | R = r ,$ where $V$ and $R$ are defined in Example 5.4.7.

5.28 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf $f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and $\mathrm { c d f } F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ and let $X _ { \left( i _ { 1 } \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( i _ { l } \right) }$ and
☒ ☒ ☒
$X _ { \left( j _ { 1 } \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( j _ { m } \right) }$ be any two disjoint groups of order statistics. In terms of the pdf
$f _ { X } \left( x \right)$ and the cdf $F _ { X } \left( x \right) ,$ find expressions for
☒

(a) The marginal cdf and pdf of $X _ { \left( i _ { 1 } \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( i _ { l } \right) } .$
☒ ☒

(b) The conditional $\mathrm { c d f }$ and pdf of $X _ { \left( i _ { 1 } \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( i _ { l } \right) }$ given $X _ { \left( j _ { 1 } \right) } , \ldots , X _ { \left( j _ { m } \right) } .$
☒ ☒

5.29 $A$ manufacturer of booklets packages them in boxes of 100. It is known that, on the
average, the booklets weigh 1 ounce, with a standard deviation of .05 ounce. The
manufacturer is interested in calculating

$$P \left( 1 0 0 \quad b o o k l e t s \quad w e i g h \quad m o r e \quad t h a n \quad 1 0 0 . 4 \quad o u n c e s \right) ,$$

a number that would help detect whether too many booklets are being put in a box.
Explain how you would calculate the (approximate?) value of this probability. Mention
any relevant theorems or assumptions needed.

5.30 If $\bar { X } _ { 1 }$ and $\bar { X } _ { 2 }$ are the means of two independent samples of size $n$ from a population
with variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ find a value for $n$ so that $P \left( | \bar { X } _ { 1 } - \bar { X } _ { 2 } | < \sigma / 5 \right) \approx . 9 9 .$ Justify your
calculations.
☒

5.31 Suppose $\bar { X }$ is the mean of 100 observations from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance
$\sigma ^ { 2 } = 9 .$ Find limits between which $\bar { X } - \mu$ will lie with probability at least .90. Use
☒
both Chebychev's Inequality and the Central Limit Theorem, and comment on each.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="261" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

5.32 Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be a sequence of random variables that converges in probability to a
constant a. Assume that $P \left( X _ { i } > 0 \right) = 1$ for all $i .$

(a) Verify that the sequences defined by $Y _ { i } = \sqrt { X _ { i } }$ and $Y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = a / X _ { i }$ converge in prob-
ability.

(b) Use the results in part (a) to prove the fact used in Example 5.5.18, that $\sigma / S _ { n }$
converges in probability to 1.

5.33 Let $X _ { n }$ be a sequence of random variables that converges in distribution to a random
variable $X .$ Let $Y _ { n }$ be a sequence of random variables with the property that for any
finite number $c$ c,

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P \left( Y _ { n } > c \right) = 1 .$$

Show that for any finite number c,

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P \left( X _ { n } + Y _ { n } > c \right) = 1 .$$

(This is the type of result used in the discussion of the power properties of the tests
described in Section 10.3.2.)

5.34 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$
Show that

$$E \frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { \sigma } = 0 \quad \text { and } \quad \text { Var } \frac { \sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu \right) } { \sigma } = 1 .$$

Thus, the normalization of $\bar { X } _ { n }$ in the Central Limit Theorem gives random variables
that have the same mean and variance as the limiting $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ distribution.

5.35 Stirling's Formula (derived in Exercise 1.28), which gives an approximation for facto-
rials, can be easily derived using the CLT.

(a) Argue that, if $X _ { i }$ ~ exponential(1), $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots ,$ all independent, then for every x,

$$P \left( \frac { \bar { X } _ { n } - 1 } { 1 / \sqrt { n } } \leq x \right) \rightarrow P \left( Z \leq x \right) ,$$

where $Z$ is a standard normal random variable.

(b) Show that differentiating both sides of the approximation in part (a) suggests

$$\frac { \sqrt { n } } { \Gamma \left( n \right) } \left( x \sqrt { n } + n \right) ^ { n - 1 } e ^ { - \left( x \sqrt { n } + n \right) } \approx \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - x ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$

and that $x = 0$ gives Stirling's Formula.

5.36 Given that $N = n ,$ the conditional distribution of $Y$ is $\chi _ { 2 n } ^ { 2 } .$ The unconditional distri-
bution of $N$ is $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \theta \right) .$

(a) Calculate $\mathrm { E } Y$ and Var $Y$ (unconditional moments).

(b) Show that, as $\theta \rightarrow \infty ,$ $\left( Y - E Y \right) / \sqrt { \text { Var } Y } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ in distribution.

5.38 The following extensions of the inequalities established in Exercise 3.45 are useful
in establishing a SLLN (see Miscellanea 5.8.4). Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with mgf
$M _ { X } \left( t \right) ,$ $- h < t < h ,$ and let $S _ { n } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$ and $\bar { X } _ { n } = S _ { n } / n .$

(a) Show that $P \left( S _ { n } > a \right) \leq e ^ { - a t } \left[ M _ { X } \left( t \right) \right] ^ { n } ,$ for $0 < t < h ,$ and $P \left( S _ { n } \leq a \right) \leq$
$e ^ { - a t } \left[ M _ { X } \left( t \right) \right] ^ { n } ,$ for $- \quad h < t < 0 .$

(b) Use the facts that $M _ { X } \left( 0 \right) = 1$ and $M _ { X } ^ { \prime } \left( 0 \right) = E \left( X \right)$ to show that, if $E \left( X \right) < 0 ,$
then there is a $0 < c < 1$ with $P \left( S _ { n } > a \right) \leq c ^ { n } .$ Establish a similar bound for
$P \left( S _ { n } \leq a \right) .$

(c) Define $Y _ { i } = X _ { i } - \mu - \varepsilon$ and use the above argument, with $a = 0 ,$ to establish that
$P \left( \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu > \varepsilon \right) \leq c ^ { n } .$

(d) Now define $Y _ { i } = - X _ { i } + \mu - \varepsilon ,$ establish an equality similar to part (c), and combine
the two to get

$$P \left( | \bar { X } _ { n } - \mu | > \varepsilon \right) \leq 2 c ^ { n } \text { for some } 0 < c < 1 .$$

5.39 This exercise, and the two following, will look at some of the mathematical details of
convergence.

(a) Prove Theorem 5.5.4. (Hint: Since $h$ is continuous, given $\varepsilon > 0$ we can find a 8
such that $| h \left( x _ { n } \right) - h \left( x \right) | < \varepsilon$ whenever $| x _ { n } - x | < \delta .$ Translate this into probability
statements.)

(b) In Example 5.5.8, find a subsequence of the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ that converges almost surely, that
is, that converges pointwise.

5.40 Prove Theorem 5.5.12 for the case where $X _ { n }$ and $X$ are continuous random variables.

(a) Given $t$ and a, show that $P \left( X \leq t - \varepsilon \right) \leq P \left( X _ { n } \leq t \right) + P \left( | X _ { n } - X | \geq \varepsilon \right) .$ This
gives a lower bound on $P \left( X _ { n } \leq t \right) .$

(b) Use a similar strategy to get an upper bound on $P \left( X _ { n } \leq t \right) .$

(c) By pinching, deduce that $P \left( X _ { n } \leq t \right) \rightarrow P \left( X \leq t \right) .$

5.41 Prove Theorem 5.5.13; that is, show that

$$P \left( | X _ { n } - \mu | > \varepsilon \right) \rightarrow 0 \text { for every } \varepsilon \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad P \left( X _ { n } \leq x \right) \rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{} 0 & \mathrm { i f } x < \mu \\ 1 & \mathrm { i f } x > \mu . \end{array} \right.$$

(a) Set $\varepsilon = | x - \mu |$ and show that if $x > \mu ,$ then $P \left( X _ { n } \leq x \right) \geq P \left( | X _ { n } - \mu | \leq \varepsilon \right) ,$ while
if $x < \mu ,$ then $P \left( X _ { n } \leq x \right) \leq P \left( | X _ { n } - \mu | \geq \varepsilon \right) .$ Deduce the => implication.

(b) Use the fact that $\left\{ x : | x - \mu | > \varepsilon \right\} = \left\{ x : x - \mu < - \varepsilon \right\} \cup \left\{ x : x - \mu > \varepsilon \right\}$ to deduce
the $\Leftarrow$ implication.

(See Billingsley 1995, Section 25, for a detailed treatment of the above results.)

5.42 Similar to Example 5.5.11, let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be id and $X _ { \left( n \right) } = \max _ { 1 \leq i \leq n } X _ { i } .$

(a) If $X _ { i }$ $\left( 1 , \beta \right) ,$ find a value of $\nu$ so that $n ^ { \nu } \left( 1 - X _ { \left( n \right) } \right)$ converges in distribution.

(b) If $X _ { i }$ exponential(1), find a sequence $a _ { n }$ so that $X _ { \left( n \right) } - a _ { n }$ converges in distribution.

(a) Show that $\sqrt { n } \left( Y _ { n } - p \right) \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , p \left( 1 - p \right) \right]$ in distribution.

(b) Show that for $p \neq 1 / 2 ,$ the estimate of variance $Y _ { n } \left( 1 - Y _ { n } \right)$ satisfies $\sqrt { n } \left[ Y _ { n } \left( 1 - Y _ { n } \right) \right.$
$\left. - p \left( 1 - p \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , \left( 1 - 2 p \right) ^ { 2 } p \left( 1 - p \right) \right]$ in distribution.

(c) Show that for $p = 1 / 2 ,$ $n \left[ Y _ { n } \left( 1 - Y _ { n } \right) - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \right] \rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ in distribution. (If this ap-
pears strange, note that $Y _ { n } \left( 1 - Y _ { n } \right) \leq 1 / 4 ,$ so the left-hand side is always negative.
An equivalent form is $4 n \left[ \frac { 1 } { 4 } - Y _ { n } \left( 1 - Y _ { n } \right) \right] \rightarrow \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$

standard normal random variables from uniform random variables is to take $X =$

5.54 Refer to Example 5.6.6. Another sample of bay anchovy larvae counts yielded the data

$$1 5 8 , 1 4 3 , 1 0 6 , 5 7 , 9 7 , 8 0 , 1 0 9 , 1 0 9 , 3 5 0 , 2 2 4 , 1 0 9 , 2 1 4 , 8 4 .$$

(a) Use the technique of Example 5.6.6 to construct a simulated distribution of $S ^ { 2 }$ to
see if the assumption of Poisson counts is tenable.

(b) $A$ possible explanation of the failure of the Poisson assumptions (and increased
variance) is the failure of the assumption that the larvae are uniformly distributed
in the river. If the larvae tend to clump, the negative binomial(r, $\left. p \right)$ distribution
(with mean $\mu = r \frac { 1 - p } { p }$ and variance $\left. \mu + \frac { \mu ^ { 2 } } { r } \right)$ is a reasonable alternative model. For
$\mu = \bar { x } ,$ what values of $r$ lead to simulated distributions that are consistent with
the data?

5.57 Park et al. (1996) describe a method for generating correlated binary variables based
on the follow scheme. Let $X _ { 1 } ,$ $X _ { 2 } ,$ $X _ { 3 }$ be independent Poisson random variables with
mean $\lambda _ { 1 } ,$ $\lambda _ { 2 } ,$ $\lambda _ { 3 } ,$ respectively, and create the random variables

and
$$Y _ { 1 } = X _ { 1 } + X _ { 3 }$$
$$Y _ { 2 } = X _ { 2 } + X _ { 3 } .$$

(a) Show that $\mathrm { C o v } \left( Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } \right) = \lambda _ { 3 } .$

(b) Define $Z _ { i } = I \left( Y _ { i } = 0 \right)$ and $p _ { i } = e ^ { - \left( \lambda _ { i } + \lambda _ { 3 } \right) } .$ Show that $Z _ { i }$ are Bernoulli(pi) with

$$\mathrm { C o r r } \left( Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } \right) = \frac { p _ { 1 } p _ { 2 } \left( e ^ { \lambda _ { 3 } } - 1 \right) } { \sqrt { p _ { 1 } \left( 1 - p _ { 1 } \right) } \sqrt { p _ { 2 } \left( 1 - p _ { 2 } \right) } } .$$

(c) Show that the correlation of $Z _ { 1 }$ and $Z _ { 2 }$ is not unrestricted in the range $\left[ - 1 , 1 \right] ,$
but

$$\mathrm { C o r r } \left( Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } \right) \leq \min \left\{ \sqrt { \frac { p _ { 2 } \left( 1 - p _ { 1 } \right) } { p _ { 1 } \left( 1 - p _ { 2 } \right) } } , \sqrt { \frac { p _ { 1 } \left( 1 - p _ { 2 } \right) } { p _ { 2 } \left( 1 - p _ { 1 } \right) } } \right\} .$$

5.58 Suppose that $U _ { 1 } , U _ { 2 } , \ldots , U _ { n }$ are iid uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ random variables, and let $S _ { n } =$
$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } U _ { i } .$ Define the random variable $N$ by

$$N = \min \left\{ k : S _ { k } > 1 \right\} .$$

(a) Show that $P \left( S _ { k } \leq t \right) = t ^ { k } / k$ if $0 \leq t \leq 1 .$

(b) Show that $P \left( N = n \right) = P \left( S _ { n - 1 } < 1 \right) - P \left( S _ { n } < 1 \right)$ and, surprisingly, that $E \left( N \right) =$
e, the base of the natural logarithms.

(c) Use the result of part (b) to calculate the value of $e$ by simulation.

(d) How large should $n$ be so that you are 95% confident that you have the first four
digits of $e$ correct?

(Russell 1991, who attributes the problem to Gnedenko 1978, describes such a simu-
lation experiment.)

5.59 Prove that the algorithm of Example 5.6.7 generates a $b e t a \left( a , b \right)$ random variable.

5.60 Generalize the algorithm of Example 5.6.7 to apply to any bounded pdf; that is, for
an arbitrary bounded pdf $f \left( x \right)$ on $\left[ a , b \right] ,$ define $c = \max _ { a \leq x \leq b } f \left( x \right) .$ Let $X$ and $Y$ be
independent, with $X \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( a , b \right)$ and $Y \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , c \right) .$ Let $d$ be a number greater
than $b ,$ and define a new random variable

$$W = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { X } & { \text { if } Y < f \left( X \right) } \\ { d } & { \text { if } Y \geq f \left( X \right) } \end{array} \right.$$

(a) Show that $P \left( W \leq w \right) = \int _ { a } ^ { w } f \left( t \right) d t / \left[ c \left( b - a \right) \right]$ for $a \leq w \leq b .$

(b) Using part (a), explain how a random variable with pdf $f \left( x \right)$ can be generated.
(Hint: Use a geometric argument; a picture will help.)

gers. Show that using $V \sim \mathrm { b e t a } \left( \left[ a \right] , \left[ b \right] \right)$ will result in a finite value of $M =$
$\sup _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) / f _ { V } \left( y \right)$ .

tegers. Show that using $V \sim g a m m a \left( \left[ a \right] , b \right)$ will result in a finite value of $M =$
$\sup _ { y } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) / f _ { V } \left( y \right)$ .

be infinite.

(d) In each of parts (a) and (b) find optimal values for the parameters of $V$ in the
sense of minimizing $E \left( N \right)$ (see (5.6.12)).

(Recall that $\left[ a \right] =$ greatest integer $\left. \leq a . \right)$

using $U \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ and

(a) $V \sim \mathrm { C a u c h y } .$

(b) $V \sim \mathrm { d o u b l e } \mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } .$

probability). What value of $\lambda$ will optimize this algorithm?

5.64 A technique similar to Accept/Reject is importance sampling, which is quite useful for
calculating features of a distribution. Suppose that $X \sim f ,$ but the pdf $f$ is difficult

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="266" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.7" -->

to simulate from. Generate $Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , \ldots , Y _ { m } ,$ iid from $g ,$ and, for any function $h ,$ calcu-
late $\frac { 1 } { m } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { f \left( Y _ { i } \right) } { g \left( Y _ { i } \right) } h \left( Y _ { i } \right) .$ We assume that the supports of $f$ and $g$ are the same and
Var $h \left( X \right) < \infty .$

(a) Show that $E \left( \frac { 1 } { m } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { f \left( Y _ { i } \right) } { g \left( Y _ { i } \right) } h \left( Y _ { i } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E } h \left( X \right) .$

(b) Show that $\frac { 1 } { m } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { f \left( Y _ { i } \right) } { g \left( Y _ { i } \right) } h \left( Y _ { i } \right) \rightarrow E h \left( X \right)$ in probability.

(c) Although the estimator of part (a) has the correct expectation, in practice the
estimator

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( \frac { f \left( Y _ { i } \right) / g \left( Y _ { i } \right) } { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } f \left( Y _ { j } \right) / g \left( Y _ { j } \right) } \right) h \left( Y _ { i } \right)$$

is preferred. Show that this estimator converges in probability to $\mathrm { E } h \left( X \right) .$ Moreover,
☒
show that if $h$ is constant, this estimator is superior to the one in part (a). (Casella
and Robert 1996 further explore the properties of this estimator.)

5.65 A variation of the importance sampling algorithm of Exercise 5.64 can actually produce
an approximate sample from $f .$ Again let $X \sim f$ and generate $Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , \ldots , Y _ { m } ,$ iid from
$g .$ Calculate $q _ { i } = \left[ f \left( Y _ { i } \right) / g \left( Y _ { i } \right) \right] / \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } f \left( Y _ { j } \right) / g \left( Y _ { j } \right) \right] .$ Then generate random variables
☒
$X ^ { * }$ from the discrete distribution on $Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , \ldots , Y _ { m } ,$ where $P \left( X ^ { * } = Y _ { k } \right) = q _ { k } .$ Show

that $X _ { 1 } ^ { * } , X _ { 2 } ^ { * } , \ldots , X _ { r } ^ { * }$ is approximately $a$ random sample from $f .$
☒

☒ ☒
(Hint: Show that $P \left( X ^ { * } \leq x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } q _ { i } I \left( Y _ { i } \leq x \right) ,$ let $m \rightarrow \infty ,$ and use the WLLN in
the numerator and denominator.)

This algorithm is called the Sampling/Importance Resampling (SIR) algorithm by
Rubin (1988) and is referred to as the weighted bootstrap by Smith and Gelfand (1992).

5.66 If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ we have seen that the distribution of the sample mean
$\bar { X }$ is $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) .$ If we are interested in using a more robust estimator of location, such
as the median $\left( 5 . 4 . 1 \right) ,$ it becomes a more difficult task to derive its distribution.

(a) Show that $M$ is the median of the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ if and only if $\left( M - \mu \right) / \sigma$ is the median of
$\left( X _ { i } - \mu \right) / \sigma .$ Thus, we only need consider the distribution of the median from a
$n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ sample.

(b) For a sample of size $n = 1 5$ from a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ simulate the distribution of the median
$M .$

(c) Compare the $d i s t r i b u t i o n$ in part (b) to the asymptotic distribution of the median
$\sqrt { n } \left( M - \mu \right) \sim n \left[ 0 , 1 / 4 f ^ { 2 } \left( 0 \right) \right] ,$ where $f$ is the pdf. Is $n = 1 5$ large enough for the
asymptotics to be valid?

5.67 In many instances the Metropolis Algorithm is the algorithm of choice because either
(i) there are no obvious candidate densities that satisfy the Accept/Reject supremum
condition, or (ii) the supremum condition is difficult to verify, or (iii) laziness leads us
to substitute computing power for brain power.

For each of the following situations show how to implement the Metropolis Algorithm
to generate a sample of size 100 from the specified distribution.

(a) $X \sim \frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left[ \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma \right] ,$ f = Student's $t$ with $\nu$ degrees of freedom, v, $\mu ,$ and $\sigma$ known

(b) $X \sim \log \mathrm { n o r m a l } \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\mu ,$ $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known

(c) $X \sim \mathrm { W e i b u l l } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ a, $\beta$ known

5.68 If we use the Metropolis Algorithm rather than the Accept/Reject Algorithm we are
freed from verifying the supremum condition of the Accept/Reject Algorithm. Of

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="267" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.8" -->

course, we give up the property of getting random variables with exactly the dis-
tribution we want and must settle for an approximation.

(a) Show how to use the Metropolis Algorithm to generate a random variable with
an approximate Student's $t$ distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom, starting from
$n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variables.

(b) Show how to use the Accept/Reject Algorithm to generate a random variable with
a Student's $t$ distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom, starting from Cauchy random
variables.

(c) Show how to use transformations to generate directly a random variable with a
Student's $t$ distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom.

(d) For $\nu = 2 , 1 0 , 2 5$ compare the methods by generating a sample of size 100. Which
method do you prefer? Why?

(Mengersen and Tweedie 1996 show that the convergence of the Metropolis Algorithm
is much faster if the supremum condition is satisfied, that is, if sup $f / g \leq M < \infty ,$
where $f$ is the target density and $g$ is the candidate density.)

5.69 Show that the pdf $f _ { Y } \left( y \right)$ is a stable point of the Metropolis Algorithm. That is, if
$Z _ { i } \sim f _ { Y } \left( y \right) ,$ then $Z _ { i + 1 } \sim f _ { Y } \left( y \right) .$


### 5.8 Miscellanea


#### 5.8.1 More on the Central Limit Theorem

For the case of a sequence of iid random variables, necessary and sufficient con-
ditions for convergence to normality are known, with probably the most famous
result due to Lindeberg and Feller. The following special case is due to Lévy. Let
$X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \cdots .$ be an id sequence with $E X _ { i } = \mu < \infty ,$ and let $V _ { n } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } .$ The
sequence $V _ { n }$ will converge to a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ random variable (when suitably normalized)
if and only if

$$\lim _ { t \rightarrow \infty } \frac { t ^ { 2 } P \left( | X _ { 1 } - \mu | > t \right) } { E \left( \left( X _ { 1 } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } I _ { \left[ - t , t \right] } \left( X _ { 1 } - \mu \right) \right) } = 0$$

Note that the condition is a variance condition. While it does not quite require
that the variances be finite, it does require that they be "almost" finite. This is an
important point in the convergence to normality-normality comes from summing
up small disturbances.

Other types of central limit theorems abound-in particular, ones aimed at relaxing
the independence assumption. While this assumption cannot be done away with,
it can be made less restrictive (see Billingsley 1995, Section 27, or Resnick 1999,
Chapter 8).


#### 5.8.2 The Bias of $S ^ { 2 }$

Most of the calculations that we have done in this chapter have assumed that the
observations are independent, and calculations of some expectations have relied on
this fact. David (1985) pointed out that, if the observations are dependent, then
$S ^ { 2 }$ may be a biased estimate of $a ^ { 2 } .$ That is, it may not happen that $E S ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$
However, the range of the possible bias is easily calculated. If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="268" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.8" -->

random variables (not necessarily independent) with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$
then

$$\left( n - 1 \right) E S ^ { 2 } = E \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } - n \left( \bar { X } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = n \sigma ^ { 2 } - n V a r X .$$

Var $\bar { X }$ can vary, according to the amount and type of dependence, from 0 (if
all of the variables are constant) to $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ (if all of the variables are copies of $X _ { 1 } .$
Substituting these values in the above equation, we get the range of $E S ^ { 2 }$ under
dependence as

$$0 \leq E S ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { n } { n - 1 } \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$


#### 5.8.3 Chebychev's Inequality Revisited

In Section 3.6 we looked at Chebychev's Inequality (see also Miscellanea 3.8.2),
and in Example 3.6.2 we saw a particularly useful form. That form still requires
knowledge of the mean and variance of a random variable, and in some cases we
might be interested in bounds using estimated values for the mean and variance.
If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a random sample from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$
Chebychev's Inequality says

$$P \left( | X - \mu | \geq k \sigma \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { k ^ { 2 } } .$$

Saw et al. (1984) showed that if we substitute $\bar { X }$ for $\mu$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ for $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ we obtain

$$P \left( | X - \bar { X } | \geq k S \right) \leq \frac { 1 } { n + 1 } g \left( \frac { n \left( n + 1 \right) k ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 + \left( n + 1 \right) k ^ { 2 } } \right) ,$$

where

$$g \left( t \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \nu } & { \text { if } v \text { is even } } \\ { \nu } & { \text { if } \nu \text { is odd and } t < a } \\ { \nu - 1 } & { \text { if } \nu \text { is odd and } t > a } \end{array} \right.$$

and

$$\nu = \text { largest integer } < \frac { n + 1 } { t } , \quad a = \frac { \left( n + 1 \right) \left( n + 1 - \nu \right) } { 1 + \nu \left( n + 1 - \nu \right) } .$$


#### 5.8.4 More on the Strong Law

As mentioned, the Strong Law of Large Numbers, Theorem 5.5.9, can be proved
under the less restrictive condition that the random variables have only a finite
mean (see, for example, Resnick 1999, Chapter 7, or Billingsley 1995, Section 22).
However, under the assumptions of the existence of an mgf, Koopmans (1993) has
presented a proof that uses only calculus.

The type of convergence asserted in the SLLN is the convergence that we are most
familiar with; it is pointwise convergence of the sequence of random variables $\bar { X } _ { n }$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="269" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.8" -->

to their common mean $\mu .$ As we saw in Example 5.5.8, this is stronger form of
convergence than convergence in probability, the convergence of the weak law.

The conclusion of the SLLN is that

$$P \left( \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } | X _ { n } - \mu | < \epsilon \right) = 1$$

that is, with probability 1, the limit of the sequence of $\left\{ \bar { X } _ { n } \right\}$ is $\mu .$ Alternatively,
the set where the sequence diverges has probability 0. For the sequence to diverge,
there must exist $\delta > 0$ such that for every $n$ there is a $k > n$ with $| \bar { X } _ { k } - \mu | > \delta .$
The set of all $\bar { X } _ { k }$ that satisfy this is a divergent sequence and is represented by the
set

$$A _ { \delta } = \cap _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \cup _ { k = n } ^ { \infty } \left\{ | \bar { X } _ { k } - \mu | > \delta \right\} .$$

We can get an upper bound on $P \left( A _ { \delta } \right)$ by dropping the intersection term, and then
the probability of the set where the sequence $\left\{ \bar { X } _ { n } \right\}$ diverges is bounded above by
☒

$$P \left( A _ { \delta } \right) \leq P \left( U _ { k = n } ^ { \infty } \left\{ | \bar { X } _ { k } - \mu | > \delta \right\} \right)$$
$$\leq \sum _ { k = n } ^ { \infty } P \left( \left\{ | \bar { X } _ { k } - \mu | > \delta \right\} \right)$$
(Boole's Inequality, Theorem 1.2.11)

$$\leq 2 \sum _ { k = n } ^ { \infty } c ^ { k } , \quad 0 < c < 1 ,$$

where Exercise 5.38(d) can be used to establish the last inequality. We then note
that we are summing the geometric series, and it follows from (1.5.4) that

$$P \left( A _ { \delta } \right) \leq 2 \sum _ { k = n } ^ { \infty } c ^ { k } = 2 \frac { c ^ { n } } { 1 - c } \rightarrow 0 \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty ,$$

and, hence, the set where the sequence $\left\{ \bar { X } _ { n } \right\}$ diverges has probability 0 and the
SLLN is established.


#### 5.8.5 Markov Chain Monte Carlo

Methods that are collectively known as Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) meth-
ods are used in the generation of random variables and have proved extremely
useful for doing complicated calculations, most notably, calculations involving in-
tegrations and maximizations. The Metropolis Algorithm (see Section 5.6) is an
example of an MCMC method.

As the name suggests, these methods are based on Markov chains, a probabilistic
structure that we haven't explored (see Chung 1974 or Ross 1988 for an introduc-
tion). The sequence of random variables $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ is a Markov chain if
☒ ☒

$$P \left( X _ { k + 1 } \in A | X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { k } \right) = P \left( X _ { k + 1 } \in A | X _ { k } \right) ;$$

that is, the distribution of the present random variable depends, at most, on the
immediate past random variable. Note that this is a generalization of independence.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="270" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PROPERTIES OF A RANDOM SAMPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 5.8" -->

The Ergodic Theorem, which is a generalization of the Law of Large Numbers, says
that if the Markov chain $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ satisfies some regularity conditions (which are
☒ ☒
often satisfied in statistical problems), then

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } h \left( X _ { i } \right) \rightarrow E h \left( X \right) \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty ,$$ ☒ ☒

provided the expectation exists. Thus, the calculations of Section 5.6 can be ex-
tended to Markov chains and MCMC methods.

To fully understand MCMC methods it is really necessary to understand more
about Markov chains, which we will not do here. There is already a vast liter-
ature on MCMC methods, encompassing both theory and applications. Tanner
(1996) provides a good introduction to computational methods in statistics, as
does Robert (1994, Chapter 9), who provides a more theoretical treatment with a
Bayesian flavor. An easier introduction to this topic via the Gibbs sampler (a par-
ticular MCMC method) is given by Casella and George (1992). The Gibbs sampler
is, perhaps, the MCMC method that is still the most widely used and is respon-
sible for the popularity of this method (due to the seminal work of Gelfand and
Smith 1990 expanding on Geman and Geman 1984). The list of references involving
MCMC methods is prohibitively long. Some other introductions to this literature
are through the papers of Gelman and Rubin (1992), Geyer and Thompson (1992),
and Smith and Roberts (1993), with a particularly elegant theoretical introduction
given by Tierney (1994). Robert and Casella (1999) is a textbook-length treatment
of this field.

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Chapter 6 Principles of Data Reduction

" ... we are suffering from a plethora of surmise, conjecture and hypothesis. The
difficulty is to detach the framework of fact - of absolute undeniable fact - from
the embellishments of theorists and reporters."

Sherlock Holmes
Silver Blaze


## 6.1 Introduction

An experimenter uses the information in a sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ to make inferences
about an unknown parameter $\theta .$ If the sample size $n$ is large, then the observed sam-
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ is a long list of numbers that may be hard to interpret. An experimenter
might wish to summarize the information in a sample by determining a few key fea-
tures of the sample values. This is usually done by computing statistics, functions of
the sample. For example, the sample mean, the sample variance, the largest observa-
tion, and the smallest observation are four statistics that might be used to summarize
some key features of the sample. Recall that we use boldface letters to denote multiple
variates, so $\mathrm { X }$ denotes the random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ and $\mathrm { x }$ denotes the sample
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$

Any statistic, $T \left( X \right) ,$ defines a form of data reduction or data summary. An experi-
menter who uses only the observed value of the statistic, $T \left( x \right) ,$ rather than the entire
observed sample, $x ,$ will treat as equal two samples, $x$ and $\mathrm { y } ,$ that satisfy $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right)$
even though the actual sample values may be different in some ways.

Data reduction in terms of a particular statistic can be thought of as a partition
of the sample space $\mathcal{X} .$ Let $T = t : t = T \left( x \right)$ for some $\left. x \in \mathcal{X} \right\}$ be the image of
☒
$\mathcal{X}$ under $T \left( x \right) .$ Then $T \left( x \right)$ partitions the sample space into sets $A _ { t } ,$ $t \in T ,$ defined
by $A _ { t } = \left\{ x : T \left( x \right) = t \right\} .$ The statistic summarizes the data in that, rather than
reporting the entire sample x, it reports only that $T \left( x \right) = t$ or, equivalently, $\mathrm { x } \in A _ { t } .$
For example, if $T \left( x \right) = x _ { 1 } + \cdots + x _ { n } ,$ then $T \left( x \right)$ does not report the actual sample
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
values but only the sum. There may be many different sample points that have the
same sum. The advantages and consequences of this type of data reduction are the
topics of this chapter.

We study three principles of data reduction. We are interested in methods of data
reduction that do not discard important information about the unknown parameter $\theta$
and methods that successfully discard information that is irrelevant as far as gaining
knowledge about $\theta$ is concerned. The Sufficiency Principle promotes a method of data

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="272" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

reduction that does not discard information about $\theta$ while achieving some summa-
rization of the data. The Likelihood Principle describes a function of the parameter,
determined by the observed sample, that contains all the information about $\theta$ that is
available from the sample. The Equivariance Principle prescribes yet another method
of data reduction that still preserves some important features of the model.


## 6.2 The Sufficiency Principle

A sufficient statistic for a parameter $\theta$ is a statistic that, in a certain sense, captures
all the information about $\theta$ contained in the sample. Any additional information
in the sample, besides the value of the sufficient statistic, does not contain any more
information about $\theta .$ These considerations lead to the data reduction technique known
as the Sufficiency Principle.

SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE: If $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta ,$ then any inference
☒
about $\theta$ should depend on the sample $X$ only through the value $T \left( X \right) .$ That is, if $x$
☒
and $\mathrm { y }$ arc two sample points such that $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) ,$ then the inference about $\theta$
should be the same whether $X = x$ or $X = y$ is observed.
☒ ☒

In this section we investigate some aspects of sufficient statistics and the Sufficiency
Principle.


### 6.2.1 Sufficient Statistics

A sufficient statistic is formally defined in the following way.

Definition 6.2.1 A statistic $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ if the conditional
distribution of the sample $\mathrm { X }$ given the value of $T \left( X \right)$ does not depend on $\theta .$
☒ ☒ ☒

If $T \left( X \right)$ has a continuous distribution, then $P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = t \right) = 0$ for all values of $t .$ A
☒ ☒

more sophisticated notion of conditional probability than that introduced in Chapter
1 is needed to fully understand Definition 6.2.1 in this case. A discussion of this can be
found in more advanced texts such as Lehmann (1986). We will do our calculations in
the discrete case and will point out analogous results that are true in the continuous
case.

To understand Definition 6.2.1, let $t$ be a possible value of $T \left( X \right) ,$ that is, a value
☒
such that $P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = t \right) > 0 .$ We wish to consider the conditional probability $P _ { \theta } \left( X = \right.$
☒ ☒

$\left. x | T \left( X \right) = t \right) .$ If $\mathrm { x }$ is a sample point such that $T \left( x \right) \neq t ,$ then clearly $P _ { \theta } \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = \right.$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
$\left. t \right) = 0 .$ Thus, we are interested in $P \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) .$ By the definition, if $T \left( X \right)$
is a sufficient statistic, this conditional probability is the same for all values of $\theta$ so
we have omitted the subscript.
☒ ☒ ☒

A sufficient statistic captures all the information about $\theta$ in this sense. Consider
Experimenter 1, who observes $X = x$ and, of course, can compute $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) .$ To
☒ ☒
make an inference about $\theta$ he can use the information that $X = x$ and $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) .$
☒ ☒
Now consider Experimenter 2, who is not told the value of $X$ but only that $T \left( X \right) =$
☒
$T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) .$ Experimenter 2 knows $P \left( X = y | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) ,$ a probability distribution on
☒ ☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="273" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

$A _ { T \left( x \right) } = \left\{ y : T \left( y \right) = T \left( x \right) \right\} ,$ because this can be computed from the model without
☒
knowledge of the true value of $\theta .$ Thus, Experimenter 2 can use this distribution and a
randomization device, such as a random number table, to generate an observation $\mathrm { Y }$
satisfying $P \left( Y = y | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) = P \left( X = y | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) .$ It turns out that, for
☒

☒ ☒
each value of $\theta ,$ $\mathrm { X }$ and $\mathrm { Y }$ have the same unconditional probability distribution, as we
☒

shall see below. So Experimenter 1, who knows $X ,$ and Experimenter 2, who knows $\mathrm { Y } ,$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
have equivalent information about $\theta .$ But surely the use of the random number table
to generate $\mathrm { Y }$ has not added to Experimenter 2's knowledge of $\theta .$ All his knowledge
about $\theta$ is contained in the knowledge that $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) .$ So Experimenter 2, who
knows only $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) ,$ has just as much information about $\theta$ as does Experimenter
1, who knows the entire sample $X = x .$

To complete the above argument, we need to show that $\mathrm { X }$ and $\mathrm { Y }$ have the same
unconditional distribution, that is, $P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( Y = x \right)$ for all $x$ and $\theta .$ Note
☒ ☒

that the events $X = x$ and $Y = x$ are both subsets of the event $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) .$
☒ ☒

☒ ☒
Also recall that

$$P \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) = P \left( Y = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

and these conditional probabilities do not depend on 0. Thus we have

$$P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \text { and } T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$
$$= P \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

definition of
conditional probability
Ca
)

$$= P \left( Y = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( Y = x \right) .$$ ☒
= Pe(Y = x and $\left. T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$
☒

To use Definition 6.2.1 to verify that a statistic $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for
☒
$\theta ,$ we must verify that for any fixed values of $\mathrm { x }$ and t, the conditional probability
$P _ { \theta } \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = t \right)$ is the same for all values of $\theta .$ Now, this probability is 0 for all
☒ ☒
values of $\theta$ if $T \left( x \right) \neq t .$ So, we must verify only that $P _ { \theta } \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$ does
☒ ☒ ☒

☒
not depend on $\theta .$ But since $\left\{ \mathrm { X } = \mathrm { x } \right\}$ is a subset of $T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) ,$
☒ ☒

$$P _ { \theta } \left( \mathrm { X } = \mathrm { x } | T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = \frac { P _ { \theta } \left( \mathrm { X } = \mathrm { x } \mathrm { a n d } T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) } { P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) }$$
$$= \frac { P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right) } { P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) }$$
$$= \frac { p \left( x | \theta \right) } { q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) }$$ ☒

where $p \left( x | \theta \right)$ is the joint pmf of the sample $\mathrm { X }$ and $q \left( t | \theta \right)$ is the pmf of $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) .$ Thus,
☒
$T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ if and only if, for every x, the above ratio of pmfs
☒
is constant as a function of $\theta .$ If $\mathrm { X }$ and $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ have continuous distributions, then the
☒ ☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="274" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

above conditional probabilities cannot be interpreted in the sense of Chapter 1. But
it is still appropriate to use the above criterion to determine if $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient
☒
statistic for $\theta .$

Theorem 6.2.2 If $p \left( x | \theta \right)$ is the joint pdf or pmf of $\mathrm { X }$ and $q \left( t | \theta \right)$ is the pdf or pmf
of $T \left( X \right) ,$ then $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ $i f ,$ for every $x$ in the sample space,
☒ ☒
the ratio $p \left( x | \theta \right) / q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right)$ is constant as a function of $\theta .$
☒

We now use Theorem 6.2.2 to verify that certain common statistics are sufficient
statistics.

Example 6.2.3 (Binomial sufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid Bernoulli
random variables with parameter $\theta ,$ $0 < \theta < 1 .$ We will show that $T \left( X \right) = X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n }$
☒
is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ Note that $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ counts the number of $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ that equal 1,
☒
so $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ has a binomial(n, $\left( n , \theta \right)$ distribution. The ratio of pmfs is thus
☒

$$\frac { p \left( x | \theta \right) } { q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) } = \frac { \Pi \theta ^ { x _ { i } } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 1 - x _ { i } } } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ t \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { t } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n - t } }$$
$$= \frac { \theta ^ { \sum x _ { i } } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { \sum \left( 1 - x _ { i } \right) } } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { t } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n - t } }$$
$$= \frac { \theta ^ { t } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n - t } } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { t } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { n - t } }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { 2 x _ { i } } \end{array} \right) }$$

(define t = Ex;)

$\left( \Pi \theta ^ { x _ { i } } = \theta ^ { \Sigma x _ { i } } \right)$

Since this ratio does not depend on $\theta ,$ by Theorem 6.2.2, $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic
☒
for $\theta .$ The interpretation is this: The total number of 1s in this Bernoulli sample
contains all the information about $\theta$ that is in the data. Other features of the data,
such as the exact value of $X _ { 3 } ,$ contain no additional information.
☒
11

Example 6.2.4 (Normal sufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
☒
where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known. We wish to show that the sample mean, $T \left( X \right) = \bar { X } = \left( X _ { 1 } + \cdots + \right.$
☒ ☒
$\left. X _ { n } \right) / n ,$ is a sufficient statistic for u. The joint pdf of the sample $X$ is
☒

$$f \left( x | \mu \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \exp \left( - \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right)$$
$$= \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="275" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->

$$= \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } + \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) \quad \left( \mathrm { a d } \mathrm { d } \right.$$
and subtract $\bar { x }$

$$= \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } + n \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$
(6.2.1)

The last equality is true because the cross-product term $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right)$ may
be rewritten as $\left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ,$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) = 0 .$ Recall that the sample
mean $\bar { \mathrm { X } }$ has a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right)$ distribution. Thus, the ratio of pdfs is

$$\frac { f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) } { q \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta \right) } = \frac { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } + n \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) } { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \exp \left( - n \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) }$$
$$= n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - \left( n - 1 \right) / 2 } \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) , .$$

which does not depend on $\mu .$ By Theorem 6.2.2, the sample mean is a sufficient
statistic for $\mu .$

In the next example we look at situations in which a substantial reduction of the
sample is not possible.

Example 6.2.5 (Sufficient order statistics) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid from a pdf
$\mathrm { f } ,$ where we are unable to specify any more information about the pdf (as is the case
in nonparametric estimation). It then follows that the sample density is given by

(6.2.2)
$$f \left( x \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { \left( i \right) } \right) ,$$

where $x _ { \left( i \right) } \leq x _ { \left( 2 \right) } \leq \cdots \leq x _ { \left( n \right) }$ are the order statistics. By Theorem 6.2.2, we can
show that the order statistics are a sufficient statistic. Of course, this is not much of a
reduction, but we shouldn't expect more with so little information about the density
$f .$

However, even if we do specify more about the density, we still may not be able to
get much of a sufficiency reduction. For example, suppose that $f$ is the Cauchy pdf
$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \pi \left( x - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } }$ or the logistic pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) } } { \left( 1 + e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) } \right) ^ { 2 } } .$ We then have the same
reduction as in (6.2.2), and no more. So reduction to the order statistics is the most
we can get in these families (see Exercises 6.8 and 6.9 for more examples).

It turns out that outside of the exponential family of distributions, it is rare to have
a sufficient statistic of smaller dimension than the size of the sample, so in many cases
it will turn out that the order statistics are the best that we can do. (See Lehmann
and Casella 1998, Section 1.6, for further details.)
11

It may be unwieldy to use the definition of a sufficient statistic to find a sufficient
statistic for a particular model. To use the definition, we must guess a statistic $T \left( X \right)$
to be sufficient, find the pmf or pdf of $T \left( X \right) ,$ and check that the ratio of pdfs or
☒

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="276" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

pmfs does not depend on $\theta .$ The first step requires a good deal of intuition and
the second sometimes requires some tedious analysis. Fortunately, the next theorem,
due to Halmos and Savage (1949), allows us to find a sufficient statistic by simple
inspection of the pdf or pmf of the sample.1

Theorem 6.2.6 (Factorization Theorem) Let $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ denote the joint pdf or

☒
pmf of a sample $\mathrm { X } .$ $A$ statistic $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ if and only if there
☒

exist functions $g \left( t | \theta \right)$ and $h \left( x \right)$ such that, for all sample points $x$ and all parameter
points $\theta ,$
☒

(6.2.3)
$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) .$$

Proof: We give the proof only for discrete distributions.

Suppose $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic. Choose $g \left( t | \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = t \right)$ and $h \left( x \right) =$
☒ ☒ ☒
$P \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) .$ Because $T \left( X \right)$ is sufficient, the conditional probability
☒ ☒ ☒

☒
defining $h \left( x \right)$ does not depend on 0. Thus this choice of $h \left( x \right)$ and $g \left( t | \theta \right)$ is legitimate,
☒ ☒
and for this choice we have

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \text { and } T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) P \left( X = x | T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right)$$
$$= g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

(sufficiency)

So factorization (6.2.3) has been exhibited. We also see from the last two lines above
that

$$P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = T \left( x \right) \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) ,$$ ☒

so $g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right)$ is the pmf of $T \left( X \right) .$
☒ ☒

Now assume the factorization (6.2.3) exists. Let $q \left( t | \theta \right)$ be the pmf of $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) .$ To
☒
show that $T \left( X \right)$ is sufficient we examine the ratio $f \left( x | \theta \right) / q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) .$ Define $A _ { T \left( x \right) } =$
☒ ☒ ☒
$\left\{ \mathrm { y } : T \left( \mathrm { y } \right) = T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right\} .$ Then
☒

$$\frac { f \left( x | \theta \right) } { q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) } = \frac { g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) } { q \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) }$$
$$\left( s i n c e \left( 6 . 2 . 3 \right) i s \quad s a t i s f i e d \right)$$
$$= \frac { g \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta \right) h \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } { \sum _ { A _ { T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } g \left( T \left( \mathrm { y } \right) | \theta \right) h \left( \mathrm { y } \right) } } \quad \text { \left(definition of the pmf of } T \mathrm { \left. \right) }$$
$$= \frac { g \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta \right) h \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } { g \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta \right) \Sigma _ { A _ { T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } } h \left( \mathrm { y } \right) } \quad \left( \mathrm { s i n c e } T \text { is constant on } A _ { T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } \right) .$$
$$= \frac { h \left( x \right) } { \sum _ { A _ { T \left( x \right) } } h \left( y \right) }$$

1 Although, according to Halmos and Savage, their theorem "may be recast in a form more akin
in spirit to previous investigations of the concept of sufficiency." The investigations are those of
Neyman (1935). (This was pointed out by Prof. J. Beder, University of Wisconsin, Milwaukee.)
