Section 6.2

Since the ratio does not depend on $\theta ,$ by Theorem 6.2.2, $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic
☒
for $\theta .$
☐

To use the Factorization Theorem to find a sufficient statistic, we factor the joint
pdf of the sample into two parts, with one part not depending on 0. The part that
does not depend on $\theta$ constitutes the $h \left( x \right)$ function. The other part, the one that
depends on $\theta ,$ usually depends on the sample $\mathrm { x }$ only through some function $T \left( x \right)$ and
☒
this function is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ This is illustrated in the following example.

Example 6.2.7 (Continuation of Example 6.2.4) For the normal model de-
scribed earlier, we saw that the pdf could be factored as

$$f \left( x | \mu \right) = \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) \exp \left( - n \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$
(6.2.4)

We can define

$$h \left( x \right) = \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) , .$$

which does not depend on the unknown parameter $\mu .$ The factor in (6.2.4) that
contains $\mu$ depends on the sample $\mathrm { x }$ only through the function $T \left( x \right) = \bar { x }$ , the sample
☒
mean. So we have

$$g \left( t | \mu \right) = \exp \left( - n \left( t - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right)$$

and note that

$$f \left( x | \mu \right) = h \left( x \right) g \left( T \left( x \right) | u \right) .$$

Thus, by the Factorization Theorem, $T \left( X \right) = X$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu .$
☒
11

The Factorization Theorem requires that the equality $f \left( x | \theta \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right)$ hold
for all $\mathrm { x }$ and $\theta .$ If the set of $\mathrm { x }$ on which $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is positive depends on $\theta ,$ care must
be taken in the definition of h and $g$ to ensure that the product is 0 where $f$ is 0. Of
course, correct definition of $h$ and $g$ makes the sufficient statistic evident, as the next
example illustrates.

Example 6.2.8 (Uniform sufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid observa-
tions from the discrete uniform distribution on $1 , \ldots , \theta .$ That is, the unknown param-
eter, $\theta ,$ is a positive integer and the pmf of $X _ { i }$ is

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 1 } { \theta } } & { x = 1 , 2 , \cdots , \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Thus the joint pmf of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is

$$f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \theta ^ { - n } & x _ { i } \in \left\{ 1 , \ldots , \theta \right\} \mathrm { f o r } i = 1 , \ldots , n \\ 0 & \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e } \end{array} \right.$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="278" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

The restriction ${ } ^ { \mathfrak{c} \mathfrak{c} } x _ { i } \in \left\{ 1 , \ldots , \theta \right\}$ for $i = 1 , \cdots , n ^ { \prime \prime }$ can be re-expressed as $x _ { i }$
$\left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots \right\}$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ (note that there is no $\theta$ in this restriction) and $\max _ { i } x _ { i } \leq \theta$
$I f$ we define $T \left( x \right) = \max _ { i } x _ { i } ,$
☒

$$h \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { x _ { i } \in \left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots \right\} \text { for } i = 1 , \ldots , n } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right.$$

and

$$g \left( t | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \theta ^ { - n } } & { t \leq \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right. :$$

it is easily verified that $f \left( x | \theta \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right)$ for all $\mathrm { x }$ and 0. Thus, the largest

☒
order statistic, $T \left( X \right) = \max _ { i } X _ { i } ,$ is a sufficient statistic in this problem.
☒ ☒

This type of analysis can sometimes be carried out more clearly and concisely using
indicator functions. Recall that $I _ { A } \left( x \right)$ is the indicator function of the set $A ;$ that is,
it is equal to 1 if $x \in A$ and equal to $0$ otherwise. Let $\mathcal{N} = \left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots \right\}$ be the set of
positive integers and let $\mathcal{N} _ { \theta } = \left\{ 1 , 2 , \ldots , \theta \right\} .$ Then the joint pmf of $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is
☒

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \theta ^ { - 1 } I _ { N _ { \theta } } \left( x _ { i } \right) = \theta ^ { - n } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } I _ { \mathcal{N} _ { \theta } } \left( x _ { i } \right) .$$

Defining $T \left( x \right) = \max _ { i } x _ { i } ,$ we see that
☒

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } I _ { \mathcal{N} _ { \theta } } \left( x _ { i } \right) = \left( \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } I _ { \mathcal{N} } \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) I _ { \mathcal{N} _ { \theta } } \left( T \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Thus we have the factorization

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta ^ { - n } I _ { \mathcal{N} _ { \theta } } \left( T \left( x \right) \right) \left( \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } I _ { \mathcal{N} } \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) .$$

The first factor depends on $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ only through the value of $T \left( x \right) = \max _ { i } x _ { i }$
☒
and the second factor does not depend on $\theta .$ By the Factorization Theorem, $T \left( X \right) =$
☒
$\max _ { i } X _ { i }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$
☒

In all the previous examples, the sufficient statistic is a real-valued function of the
sample. All the information about $\theta$ in the sample $x$ is summarized in the single
number $T \left( x \right) .$ Sometimes, the information cannot be summarized in one number and
several numbers are required instead. In such cases, a sufficient statistic is a vector,
say $T \left( X \right) = \left( T _ { 1 } \left( X \right) , \ldots , T _ { r } \left( X \right) \right) .$ This situation often occurs when the parameter is
☒ ☒ ☒
also a vector, say $\theta = \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { s } \right) ,$ and it is usually the case that the sufficient statistic
and the parameter vectors are of equal length, that is, $r =$ s. Different combinations of
lengths are possible, however, as the exercises and Examples 6.2.15, 6.2.18, and 6.2.20
illustrate. The Factorization Theorem may be used to find a vector-valued sufficient
statistic, as in Example 6.2.9.

$1 1$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="279" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

Example 6.2.9 (Normal sufficient statistic, both parameters unknown)
Again assume that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ but, unlike Example 6.2.4, assume
that both $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are unknown so the parameter vector is $\theta = \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ Now when
we use the Factorization Theorem, any part of the joint pdf that depends on either
$\mu$ or $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ must be included in the $g$ function. From (6.2.1) it is clear that the pdf
depends on the sample $\mathrm { x }$ only through the two values $T _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = \bar { x }$ and $T _ { 2 } \left( x \right) = s ^ { 2 } =$
☒
$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 1 \right) .$ Thus we can define $h \left( x \right) = 1$ and

$$g \left( t | \theta \right) = g \left( t _ { 1 } , t _ { 2 } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \left( n \left( t _ { 1 } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( n - 1 \right) t _ { 2 } \right) / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$

Then it can be seen that

(6.2.5)
$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = g \left( T _ { 1 } \left( x \right) , T _ { 2 } \left( x \right) | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) h \left( x \right) .$$

Thus, by the Factorization Theorem, $T \left( X \right) = \left( T _ { 1 } \left( X \right) , T _ { 2 } \left( X \right) \right) = \left( X , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ is a sufficient
statistic for $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ in this normal model.
☒ ☒
11

Example 6.2.9 demonstrates that, for the normal model, the common practice of
summarizing a data $s e t$ by reporting only the sample mean and variance is justified.
The sufficient statistic $\left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ contains all the information about $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ that is avail-
able in the sample. The experimenter should remember, however, that the definition
of a sufficient statistic is model-dependent. For another model, that is, another family
of densities, the sample mean and variance may not be a sufficient statistic for the
population mean and variance. The experimenter who calculates only $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ and
totally ignores the rest of the data would be placing strong faith in the normal model
assumption.

It is easy to find a sufficient statistic for an exponential family of distributions
using the Factorization Theorem. The proof of the following important result is left
as Exercise 6.4.

Theorem 6.2.10 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid observations from a pdf or pmf $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ that
belongs to an exponential family given $b y$

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) ,$$

where $\theta = \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \theta _ { d } \right) ,$ $d \leq k .$ Then

$$T \left( X \right) = \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { 1 } \left( X _ { j } \right) , \cdots , \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { k } \left( X _ { j } \right) \right)$$

is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$


### 6.2.2 Minimal Sufficient Statistics

In the preceding section we found one sufficient statistic for each model considered.
In any problem there are, in fact, many sufficient statistics.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="280" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

It is always true that the complete sample, $X ,$ is a sufficient statistic. We can factor
☒
the pdf or pmf of $\mathrm { X }$ as $f \left( x | \theta \right) = f \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) ,$ where $T \left( x \right) = x$ and $h \left( x \right) = 1$ for all
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
$\mathrm { x } .$ By the Factorization Theorem, $T \left( X \right) = X$ is a sufficient statistic.
☒ ☒

Also, it follows that any one-to-one function of a sufficient statistic is a sufficient
statistic. Suppose $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic and define $T ^ { * } \left( x \right) = r \left( T \left( x \right) \right)$ for $a l l \quad x ,$
☒ ☒ ☒
where $r$ is a one-to-one function with inverse $r ^ { - 1 } .$ Then by the Factorization Theorem
there exist $g$ and $h$ such that

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) = g \left( r ^ { - 1 } \left( T ^ { * } \left( x \right) \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) .$$ ☒

Defining $g ^ { * } \left( t | \theta \right) = g \left( r ^ { - 1 } \left( t \right) | \theta \right) ,$ we see that

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = g ^ { * } \left( T ^ { * } \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right) .$$

So, by the Factorization Theorem, $T ^ { * } \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic.
☒

Because of the numerous sufficient statistics in a problem, we might ask whether one
sufficient statistic is any better than another. Recall that the purpose of a sufficient
statistic is to achieve data reduction without loss of information about the parameter
$\theta$ thus, a statistic that achieves the most data reduction while still retaining all the
information about $\theta$ might be considered preferable. The definition of such a statistic
is formalized now.

Definition 6.2.11 $A$ sufficient statistic $T \left( X \right)$ is called a minimal sufficient statistic
☒
if, for any other sufficient statistic $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) , T \left( x \right)$ is a function $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒

To say that $T \left( x \right)$ is a function of $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ simply means that if $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) = T ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) ,$ then
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
$T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) .$ In terms of the partition sets described at the beginning of the chapter,
if $\left\{ B _ { t ^ { \prime } } : t ^ { \prime } \in \mathcal{I} ^ { \prime } \right\}$ are the partition sets for $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ and $\left\{ A _ { t } : t \in T \right\}$ are the partition sets
☒
for $T \left( x \right) ,$ then Definition 6.2.11 states that every $B _ { t }$ is a subset of some $A _ { t } .$ Thus, the
☒
partition associated with a minimal sufficient statistic, is the coarsest possible parti-
tion for a sufficient statistic, and a minimal sufficient statistic achieves the greatest
possible data reduction for a sufficient statistic.

Example 6.2.12 (Two normal sufficient statistics) The model considered in
Example 6.2.4 has $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ with $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. Using factorization (6.2.4),
☒ ☒
we concluded that $T \left( X \right) = \bar { X }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu .$ Instead, we could write
☒ ☒
down factorization (6.2.5) for this problem $\left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right.$ is a known value now) and correctly
conclude that $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) = \left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu$ in this problem. Clearly
$T \left( X \right)$
☒ ☒ ☒
$T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right)$
☒
variance if we know only $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) .$ We can write $T \left( x \right)$ as a function of $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ by defining
☒ ☒ ☒
the function $r \left( a , b \right) = a .$ Then $T \left( x \right) = \bar { x } = r \left( \bar { x } , s ^ { 2 } \right) = r \left( T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \right) .$ Since $T \left( X \right)$ and $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right)$
☒ ☒ ☒
are both sufficient statistics, they both contain the same information about u. Thus,
the additional information about the value of $S ^ { 2 } ,$ the sample variance, does not add
to our knowledge of $\mu$ since the population variance $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known. Of course, if $\sigma ^ { 2 }$
is unknown, as in Example 6.2.9, $T \left( X \right) = \bar { X }$ is not a sufficient statistic and $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right)$ $1$
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
contains more information about the parameter $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ than does $T \left( X \right) .$
☒

Using Definition 6.2.11 to find a minimal sufficient statistic is impractical, as was
using Definition 6.2.1 to find sufficient statistics. We would need to guess that $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$
☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="281" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

that we did not show that $\bar { X }$ is a minimal sufficient statistic in Example 6.2.12.)
☒
Fortunately, the following result of Lehmann and Scheffé (1950, Theorem 6.3) gives
an easier way to find a minimal sufficient statistic.

Theorem 6.2.13 Let $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ be the pmf $o r$ $p d f$ of a sample $\mathrm { X } .$ Suppose there exists $a$
function $T \left( x \right)$ such that, for every two sample points $x$ and $\mathrm { y } ,$ the ratio $f \left( x | \theta \right) / f \left( y | \theta \right)$
☒

is constant as a function of $\theta$ $i f$ and only if $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) .$ Then $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a minimal

☒ ☒
sufficient statistic $f o r$ 0.

Proof: To simplify the proof, we assume $f \left( x | \theta \right) > 0$ for all $\mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X}$ and $\theta .$
☒

First we show that $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic. Let $T = t : t = T \left( x \right)$ for some
☒

☒
$\left. \mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X} \right\}$ be the image of $\mathcal{X}$ under $T \left( x \right) .$ Define the partition sets induced by $T \left( x \right)$ as
☒ ☒
$A _ { t } = \left\{ x : T \left( x \right) = t \right\} .$ For each $A _ { t } ,$ choose and fix one element $\mathrm { x } _ { t } \in A _ { t } .$ For any $\mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X} ,$
$X _ { T \left( x \right) }$ is the fixed element that is in the same set, $A _ { t } ,$ as $x .$ Since $x$ and $\mathrm { x } _ { T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) }$ are
☒
in the same set $A _ { t } ,$ $T \left( x \right) = T \left( x _ { T \left( x \right) } \right)$ and, hence, $f \left( x | \theta \right) / f \left( x _ { T \left( x \right) } | \theta \right)$ is constant as a
function of 0. Thus, we can define a function on $\mathcal{X}$ by $h \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) / f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } | \theta \right)$ and
$h$ does not depend on 0. Define a function on $T$ by $g \left( t | \theta \right) = f \left( x _ { t } | \theta \right) .$ Then it can be
seen that

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { f \left( x _ { T \left( x \right) } | \theta \right) f \left( x | \theta \right) } { f \left( x _ { T \left( x \right) } | \theta \right) } = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h \left( x \right)$$

and, by the Factorization Theorem, $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$
☒

Now to show that $T \left( X \right)$ is minimal, let $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right)$ be any other sufficient statistic.
☒ ☒
By the Factorization Theorem, there exist functions $g ^ { \prime }$ and $h ^ { \prime }$ such that $f \left( x | \theta \right) =$
$g ^ { \prime } \left( T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) .$ Let $\mathrm { x }$ and $\mathrm { y }$ be any two sample points with $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) = T ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) .$ Then
☒ ☒ ☒

$$\frac { f \left( x | \theta \right) } { f \left( y | \theta \right) } = \frac { g ^ { \prime } \left( T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) | \theta \right) h ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) } { g ^ { \prime } \left( T ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) | \theta \right) h ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) } = \frac { h ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) } { h ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) }$$ ☒

Since this ratio does not depend on $\theta ,$ the assumptions of the theorem imply that
$T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) .$ Thus, $T \left( x \right)$ is a function of $T ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ and $T \left( x \right)$ is minimal.
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Example 6.2.14 (Normal minimal sufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
$n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ both $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ unknown. Let $x$ and $\mathrm { y }$ denote two sample points, and let
$\left( \bar { x } , s _ { x } ^ { 2 } \right)$ and $\left( \bar { y } , s _ { \mathrm { y } } ^ { 2 } \right)$ be the sample means and variances corresponding to the $x$ and $\mathrm { y }$
samples, respectively. Then, using (6.2.5), we see that the ratio of densities is

$$\frac { f \left( \mathrm { x } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } { f \left( \mathrm { y } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } = \frac { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \left[ n \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( n - 1 \right) s _ { \mathrm { x } } ^ { 2 } \right] / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) } { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left( - \left[ n \left( \bar { y } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( n - 1 \right) s _ { \mathrm { y } } ^ { 2 } \right] / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) }$$
$$= \exp \left( \left[ - n \left( \bar { x } ^ { 2 } - \bar { y } ^ { 2 } \right) + 2 n \mu \left( \bar { x } - \bar { y } \right) - \left( n - 1 \right) \left( s _ { \mathrm { x } } ^ { 2 } - s _ { \mathrm { y } } ^ { 2 } \right) \right] / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$

This ratio will be constant as a function of $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ if and only if $\bar { x } = \bar { y }$ and $s _ { x } ^ { 2 } = s _ { y } ^ { 2 } .$
Thus, by Theorem 6.2.13, $\left( X , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ is a minimal sufficient statistic for $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$
☒

If the set of $x s$ on which the pdf or pmf is positive depends on the parameter $\theta ,$
then, for the ratio in Theorem 6.2.13 to be constant as a function of $\theta ,$ the numerator

☐

Il

<!-- PageNumber="282" -->
