<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

and denominator must be positive for exactly the same values of $\theta .$ This restriction
is usually reflected in a minimal sufficient statistic, as the next example illustrates.

Example 6.2.15 (Uniform minimal sufficient statistic) Suppose $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
are iid uniform observations on the interval $\left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right) , - \infty < \theta < \infty .$ Then the joint
pdf of $X$ is

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \theta < x _ { i } < \theta + 1 , i = 1 , \ldots , n } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right. ,$$

which can be written as

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \max _ { i } x _ { i } - 1 < \theta < \min _ { i } x _ { i } } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Thus, for two sample points $\mathrm { x }$ and $\mathrm { y } ,$ the numerator and denominator of the ratio
$f \left( x | \theta \right) / f \left( y | \theta \right)$ will be positive for the same values of $\theta$ if and only if $\min _ { i } x _ { i } = \min _ { i } y _ { i }$
and $\max _ { i } x _ { i } = \max _ { i } y _ { i } .$ And, if the minima and maxima are equal, then the ratio is
constant and, in fact, equals 1. Thus, letting $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = \min _ { i } X _ { i }$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } = \max _ { i } X _ { i } ,$

☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
we have that $T \left( X \right) = \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , X _ { \left( n \right) } \right)$ is a minimal sufficient statistic. This is a case in
☒
which the dimension of a minimal sufficient statistic does not match the dimension
of the parameter.

Il

A minimal sufficient statistic is not unique. Any one-to-one function of a minimal
sufficient statistic is also a minimal sufficient statistic. So, for example, $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) =$
☒
$\left( X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } + X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) / 2 \right)$ is also a minimal sufficient statistic in Example 6.2.15
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
and $T ^ { \prime } \left( X \right) = \left( \Sigma _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } , \Sigma _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right)$ is also a minimal sufficient statistic in Example 6.2.14.
☒ ☒ ☒


### 6.2.3 Ancillary Statistics

In the preceding sections, we considered sufficient statistics. Such statistics, in a sense,
contain all the information about $\theta$ that is available in the sample. In this section we
introduce a different sort of statistic, one that has a complementary purpose.

Definition 6.2.16 A statistic $S \left( X \right)$ whose distribution does not depend on the
☒
parameter $\theta$ is called an ancillary statistic.

Alone, an ancillary statistic contains no information about $\theta .$ An ancillary statistic
is an observation on a random variable whose distribution is fixed and known, unre-
lated to $\theta .$ Paradoxically, an ancillary statistic, when used in conjunction with other
statistics, sometimes does contain valuable information for inferences about $\theta .$ We will
investigate this behavior in the next section. For now, we just give some examples of
ancillary statistics.

Example 6.2.17 (Uniform ancillary statistic)
As in Example 6.2.15, let
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be id uniform observations on the interval $\left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right) , - \infty < \theta < \infty .$
Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } < \cdots < X _ { \left( n \right) }$ be the order statistics from the sample. We show below that
the range statistic, $R = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ is an ancillary statistic by showing that the pdf
☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="283" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

of $R$ does not depend on $\theta .$ Recall that the cdf of each $X _ { i }$ is

$$F \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { x \leq \theta } \\ { x - \theta } & { \theta < x < \theta + 1 } \\ { 1 } & { \theta + 1 \leq x } \end{array} \right.$$

Thus, the joint pdf of $X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } ,$ as given by (5.4.7), is

$$g \left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } , x _ { \left( n \right) } | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { n \left( n - 1 \right) \left( x _ { \left( n \right) } - x _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) ^ { n - 2 } } & { \theta < x _ { \left( 1 \right) } < x _ { \left( n \right) } < \theta + 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Making the transformation $R = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $M = \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } + X _ { \left( n \right) } \right) / 2 ,$ which has the
inverse transformation $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = \left( 2 M - R \right) / 2$ and $X _ { \left( n \right) } = \left( 2 M + R \right) / 2$ with Jacobian 1,
we see that the joint pdf of $R$ and $M$ is

$$h \left( r , m | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } } & { 0 < r < 1 , \theta + \left( r / 2 \right) < m < \theta + 1 - \left( r / 2 \right) } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

(Notice the rather involved region of positivity for $h \left( r , m | \theta \right)$ Thus, the pdf for $R$ is

$$h \left( r | \theta \right) = \int _ { \theta + \left( r / 2 \right) } ^ { \theta + 1 - \left( r / 2 \right) } n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } d m$$
$$= n \left( n - 1 \right) r ^ { n - 2 } \left( 1 - r \right) , \quad 0 < r < 1 .$$

This is a beta pdf with $\alpha = n - 1$ and $\beta = 2 .$ More important, the pdf is the same
for all $\theta .$ Thus, the distribution of $R$ does not depend on $\theta ,$ and $R$ is ancillary.
II

In Example 6.2.17 the range statistic is ancillary because the model considered there
is a location parameter model. The ancillarity of $R$ does not depend on the uniformity
of the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s } ,$ but rather on the parameter of the distribution being a location parameter.
We now consider the general location parameter model.

Example 6.2.18 (Location family ancillary statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
observations from a location parameter family with $\mathrm { c d f } F \left( x - \theta \right) , - \infty < \theta < \infty .$ We
will show that the range, $R = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$ is an ancillary statistic. We use Theorem
3.5.6 and work with $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ iid observations from $F \left( x \right)$ (corresponding to $\theta = 0$
with $X _ { 1 } = Z _ { 1 } + \theta , \ldots , X _ { n } = Z _ { n } + \theta .$ Thus the $\mathrm { c d f }$ of the range statistic, $R ,$ is

$$F _ { R } \left( r | \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( R \leq r \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \max _ { i } X _ { i } - \min _ { i } X _ { i } \leq r \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \max _ { i } \left( Z _ { i } + \theta \right) - \min _ { i } \left( Z _ { i } + \theta \right) \leq r \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \max _ { i } Z _ { i } - \min _ { i } Z _ { i } + \theta - \theta \leq r \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \max _ { i } Z _ { i } - \min _ { i } Z _ { i } \leq r \right) .$$

The last probability does not $\theta$ because the distribution of $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ does
not depend on $\theta .$ Thus, the $\mathrm { c d f }$ of $R$ does not depend on $\theta$ and, hence, $R$ is an ancillary
statistic.

<!-- PageNumber="Il" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="284" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

Example 6.2.19 (Scale family ancillary statistic) Scale parameter families
also have certain kinds of ancillary statistics. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid observations from
a scale parameter family with cdf $F \left( x / \sigma \right) ,$ $\sigma > 0 .$ Then any statistic that depends
on the sample only through the $n \quad - \quad 1$ values $X _ { 1 } / X _ { n } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } / X _ { n }$ is an ancillary
☒
statistic. For example,

$$\frac { X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n } } { X _ { n } } = \frac { X _ { 1 } } { X _ { n } } + \cdots + \frac { X _ { n - 1 } } { X _ { n } } + 1$$

is an ancillary statistic. To see this fact, let $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ be iid observations from $F \left( x \right)$
(corresponding to $\sigma = 1$ with $X _ { i } = \sigma Z _ { i } .$ The joint cdf of $X _ { 1 } / X _ { n } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } / X _ { n }$ is

$$F \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n - 1 } | \sigma \right) = P _ { \sigma } \left( X _ { 1 } / X _ { n } \leq y _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } / X _ { n } \leq y _ { n - 1 } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \sigma } \left( \sigma Z _ { 1 } / \left( \sigma Z _ { n } \right) \leq y _ { 1 } , \ldots , \sigma Z _ { n - 1 } / \left( \sigma Z _ { n } \right) \leq y _ { n - 1 } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \sigma } \left( Z _ { 1 } / Z _ { n } \leq y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n - 1 } / Z _ { n } \leq y _ { n - 1 } \right) .$$

The last probability does not depend on $\sigma$ because the distribution of $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { n }$ does
not depend on o. So the distribution of $X _ { 1 } / X _ { n } , \ldots , X _ { n - 1 } / X _ { n }$ is independent of $\sigma ,$
as is the distribution of any function of these quantities.

In particular, let $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ be iid $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ observations. From the above result,
we see that $X _ { 1 } / X _ { 2 }$ has a distribution that is the same for every value of $\sigma .$ But, in
Example 4.3.6, we saw that, if $\sigma = 1 , X _ { 1 } / X _ { 2 }$ has a Cauchy(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ distribution. Thus,
for any $\sigma > 0 ,$ the distribution of $X _ { 1 } / X _ { 2 }$ is this same Cauchy distribution.
Il

In this section, we have given examples, some rather general, of statistics that are
ancillary for various models. In the next section we will consider the relationship
between sufficient statistics and ancillary statistics.


### 6.2.4 Sufficient, Ancillary, and Complete Statistics

A minimal sufficient statistic is a statistic that has achieved the maximal amount of
data reduction possible while still retaining all the information about the parameter
$\theta .$ Intuitively, a minimal sufficient statistic eliminates all the extraneous information
in the sample, retaining only that piece with information about $\theta .$ Since the distri-
bution of an ancillary statistic does not depend on $\theta ,$ it might be suspected that a
minimal sufficient statistic is unrelated to (or mathematically speaking, functionally
independent of) an ancillary statistic. However, this is not necessarily the case. In
this section, we investigate this relationship in some detail.

We have already discussed a situation in which an ancillary statistic is not indepen-
dent of a minimal sufficient statistic. Recall Example 6.2.15 in which $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ were
iid observations from a uniform(0, $\left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right)$ distribution. At the end of Section 6.2.2, we
noted that the statistic $\left( X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \left( X _ { \left( n \right) } + X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) / 2 \right)$ is a minimal sufficient statistic,

this case, the ancillary statistic is an important $X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ component of the minimal sufficient

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="285" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

statistic. Certainly, the ancillary statistic and the minimal sufficient statistic are not
independent.

To emphasize the point that an ancillary statistic can sometimes give important
information for inferences about $\theta ,$ we give another example.

Example 6.2.20 (Ancillary precision) Let $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ be iid observations from
the discrete distribution that satisfies

$$P _ { \theta } \left( X = \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = \theta + 1 \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = \theta + 2 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 3 } ,$$

where $\theta ,$ the unknown parameter, is any integer. Let $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \leq X _ { \left( 2 \right) }$ be the order statistics
for the sample. It can be shown with an argument similar to that in Example 6.2.15
that $\left( R , M \right) ,$ where $R = X _ { \left( 2 \right) } - X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ and $M = \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } + X _ { \left( 2 \right) } \right) / 2 ,$ is a minimal sufficient
statistic. Since this is a location parameter family, by Example 6.2.17, $R$ is an ancillary
statistic. To see how $R$ might give information about $\theta ,$ even though it is ancillary,
consider a sample point $\left( r , m \right) ,$ where $m$ is an integer. First we consider only $m ;$ for
this sample point to have positive probability, $\theta$ must be one of three values. Either
$\theta = m$ or $\theta = m - 1$ or $\theta = m - 2 .$ With only the information that $M = m ,$ all
three $\theta$ values are possible values. But now suppose we get the additional information
that $R = 2 .$ Then it must be the case that $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = m - 1$ and $X _ { \left( 2 \right) } = m + 1 .$ With
this additional information, the only possible value for $\theta$ is $\theta = m - 1$ 1. Thus, the
knowledge of the value of the ancillary statistic $R$ has increased our knowledge about
$\theta .$ Of course, the knowledge of $R$ alone would give us no information about $\theta .$ (The
idea that an ancillary statistic gives information about the precision of an estimate
of $\theta$ is not new. See Cox 1971 or Efron and Hinkley 1978 for more ideas.)
II

For many important situations, however, our intuition that $a$ minimal sufficient
statistic is independent of any ancillary statistic is correct. A description of situations
in which this occurs relies on the next definition.

Definition 6.2.21 Let $f \left( t | \theta \right)$ be a family of pdfs or pmfs for a statistic $T \left( X \right) .$ The
family of probability distributions is called complete if $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) = 0$ for all $\theta$ implies
$P _ { \theta } \left( g \left( T \right) = 0 \right) = 1$ for all $\theta .$ Equivalently, $T \left( X \right)$ is called a complete statistic.

Notice that completeness is a property of a family of probability distributions, not
of a particular distribution. For example, if $X$ has a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ distribution, then defining
$g \left( x \right) = x ,$ we have that $E g \left( X \right) = E X = 0 .$ But the function $g \left( x \right) = x$ satisfies
$P \left( g \left( X \right) = 0 \right) = P \left( X = 0 \right) = 0 ,$ not 1. However, this is a particular distribution, not a
family of distributions. If $X$ has a $n \left( \theta , 1 \right)$ distribution, $- \infty < \theta < \infty ,$ we shall see that
no function of $X ,$ except one that is 0 with probability 1 for all $\theta ,$ satisfies $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( X \right) = 0$
for all $\theta .$ Thus, the family of $n \left( \theta , 1 \right)$ distributions, $- \infty < \theta < \infty$ , is complete.

Example 6.2.22 (Binomial complete sufficient statistic) Suppose that $T$ has
a binomial(n, $\left. p \right)$ distribution, $0 < p < 1 .$ Let $g$ be a function such that $E _ { p } g \left( T \right) = 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="286" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

Then

$$0 = \mathrm { E } _ { p } g \left( T \right) = \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { n } g \left( t \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) p ^ { t } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - t }$$
$$= \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n } \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { n } g \left( t \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) ^ { t }$$

for all $p ,$ $0 < p < 1 .$ The factor $\left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n }$ is not 0 for any $p$ in this range. Thus it must
be that

$$0 = \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { n } g \left( t \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) ^ { t } = \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { n } g \left( t \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) r ^ { t }$$

for all $r ,$ $0 < r < \infty .$ But the last expression is a polynomial of degree $n$ in $r ,$ where the
coefficient of $r ^ { t }$ is $g \left( t \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right) .$ For the polynomial to be 0 for $\mathrm { a l l } r$ r, each coefficient must be
0. Since none of the $\left( \begin{array}{} { n } \\ { t } \end{array} \right)$ terms $i s \quad 0 ,$ this implies that $g \left( t \right) = 0$ for $t = 0 , 1 , \ldots , n .$ Since
$T$ takes on the values $0 , 1 , \ldots , n$ with probability 1, this yields that $P _ { p } \left( g \left( T \right) = 0 \right) = 1$
for all $p ,$ the desired conclusion. Hence, $T$ is a complete statistic.

Example 6.2.23 (Uniform complete sufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be
☒
iid uniform(0, $\left( 0 , \theta \right)$ observations, $0 < \theta < \infty .$ Using an argument similar to that in
Example 6.2.8, we can see that $T \left( X \right) = \max _ { i } X _ { i }$ is a sufficient statistic and, by
☒ ☒
Theorem 5.4.4, the pdf of $T \left( X \right)$ is
☒

$$f \left( t | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { n t ^ { n - 1 } \theta ^ { - n } } & { 0 < t < \theta } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Suppose $g \left( t \right)$ is a function satisfying $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) = 0$ for all $\theta .$ Since $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right)$ is constant
as a function of $\theta ,$ its derivative with respect to $\theta$ is 0. Thus we have that

$$0 = \frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) = \frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } g \left( t \right) n t ^ { n - 1 } \theta ^ { - n } d t$$
$$= \left( \theta ^ { - n } \right) \frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } n g \left( t \right) t ^ { n - 1 } d t + \left( \frac { d } { d \theta } \theta ^ { - n } \right) \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } n g \left( t \right) t ^ { n - 1 } d t$$
$$= \theta ^ { - n } n g \left( \theta \right) \theta ^ { n - 1 } + 0$$
$$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { applying the product } } \\ { \text { rule for differentiation } } \end{array} \right)$$

$$= \theta ^ { - 1 } n g \left( \theta \right) .$$

The first term in the next to last line is the result of an application of the Fundamental
Theorem of Calculus. The second term is 0 because the integral is, except for a
constant, equal to $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) ,$ which is 0. Since $\theta ^ { - 1 } n g \left( \theta \right) = 0$ and $\theta ^ { - 1 } n \neq 0 ,$ it must
be that $g \left( \theta \right) = 0 .$ This is true for every $\theta > 0$ hence, $T$ is a complete statistic. (On
a somewhat pedantic note, realize that the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus does

1

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="287" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

not apply to all functions, but only to functions that are Riemann-integrable. The
equation

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } g \left( t \right) d t = g \left( \theta \right)$$

is valid only at points of continuity of Riemann-integrable $g .$ Thus, strictly speaking,
the above argument does not show that $T$ is a complete statistic, since the condition
of completeness applies to all functions, not just Riemann-integrable ones. From a
more practical view, however, this distinction is not of concern since the condition of
Riemann-integrability is so general that $i t$ includes virtually any function we could
think of.)
11

We now use completeness to state a condition under which a minimal sufficient
statistic is independent of every ancillary statistic.

Theorem 6.2.24 (Basu's Theorem) If $T \left( X \right)$ is a complete and minimal suffi-
cient statistic, then $T \left( X \right)$ is independent of every ancillary statistic.
☒ ☒

Proof: We give the proof only for discrete distributions.

Let $S \left( X \right)$ be any ancillary statistic. Then $P \left( S \left( X \right) = s \right)$ does not depend on $\theta$ since
☒ ☒
$S \left( X \right)$ is ancillary. Also the conditional probability,
☒

$$P \left( S \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = s | T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = t \right) = P \left( \mathrm { X } \in \left\{ \mathrm { x } : S \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = s \right\} | T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = t \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

does not depend on $\theta$ because $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic (recall the definition!).
☒
Thus, to show that $S \left( X \right)$ and $T \left( X \right)$ are independent, it suffices to show that
☒ ☒

(6.2.6)
$$P \left( S \left( X \right) = s | T \left( X \right) = t \right) = P \left( S \left( X \right) = s \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

for all possible values $t \in T .$ Now,

$$P \left( S \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = s \right) = \sum _ { t \in T } P \left( S \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = s | T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = t \right) P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = t \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Furthermore, since $\sum _ { t \in T } P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = t \right) = 1 ,$ we can write
☒

$$P \left( S \left( X \right) = s \right) = \sum _ { t \in T } P \left( S \left( X \right) = s \right) P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( X \right) = t \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

Therefore, if we define the statistic

$$g \left( t \right) = P \left( S \left( X \right) = s | T \left( X \right) = t \right) - \quad P \left( S \left( X \right) = s \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

the above two equations show that

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( T \right) = \sum _ { t \in \mathcal{I} } g \left( t \right) P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = t \right) = 0 \quad \text { for all } \theta .$$ ☒

Since $T \left( X \right)$ is a complete statistic, this implies that $g \left( t \right) = 0$ for all possible values
☒
$t \in T .$ Hence (6.2.6) is verified.

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="288" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

Basu's Theorem is useful in that it allows us to deduce the independence of two
statistics without ever finding the joint distribution of the two statistics. To use
Basu's Theorem, we need to show that a statistic is complete, which is sometimes a
rather difficult analysis problem. Fortunately, most problems we are concerned with
are covered by the following theorem. We will not prove this theorem but note that
its proof depends on the uniqueness of a Laplace transform, a property that was
mentioned in Section 2.3.

Theorem 6.2.25 (Complete statistics in the exponential family)
Let
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid observations from an exponential family with $p d f$ or pmf of the
☒ ☒
form

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { j } \left( \theta \right) t _ { j } \left( x \right) \right) ,$$
(6.2.7)

where $\theta = \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \theta _ { k } \right) .$ Then the statistic

$$T \left( X \right) = \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { 1 } \left( X _ { i } \right) , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { 2 } \left( X _ { i } \right) , \ldots , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { k } \left( X _ { i } \right) \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

is complete if $\left\{ \left( w _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) , \ldots , w _ { k } \left( \theta \right) \right) \right. :$ 0 E O} contains an open set in $\mathfrak{R} ^ { k } .$

The condition that the parameter space contain an open set is needed to avoid a sit-
uation like the following. The $n \left( \theta , \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$ distribution can be written in the form (6.2.7);
however, the parameter space $\left( \theta , \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$ does not contain a two-dimensional open set,
as it consists of only the points on a parabola. As a result, we can find a transfor-
mation of the statistic $T \left( X \right)$ that is an unbiased estimator of 0 (see Exercise 6.15).
☒
(Recall that exponential families such as the $n \left( \theta , \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where the parameter space is a
lower-dimensional curve, are called curved exponential families; see Section 3.4.) The
relationship between sufficiency, completeness, and minimality in exponential families
is an interesting one. For a brief introduction, see Miscellanea 6.6.3.

We now give some examples of the use of Basu's Theorem, Theorem 6.2.25, and
many of the earlier results in this chapter.

Example 6.2.26 (Using Basu's Theorem-I) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid exponential
☒
observations with parameter $\theta .$ Consider computing the expected value of

$$g \left( X \right) = \frac { X _ { n } } { X _ { 1 } + \cdots + X _ { n } }$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

We first note that the exponential distributions form a scale parameter family and
thus, by Example 6.2.19, $g \left( X \right)$ is an ancillary statistic. The exponential distributions
☒
also form an exponential family with $t \left( x \right) = x$ and $s o ,$ by Theorem 6.2.25,

$$T \left( X \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$$ ☒ ☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="289" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.2" -->

is a complete statistic and, by Theorem 6.2.10, $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic. (As noted
☒
below, we need not verify that $T \left( X \right)$ is minimal, although it could easily be verified
☒
using Theorem 6.2.13.) Hence, by Basu's Theorem, $T \left( X \right)$ and $g \left( X \right)$ are independent.
☒ ☒
Thus we have

$$\theta = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } X _ { n } = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) g \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = \left( \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) \left( \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) = n \theta \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( \mathrm { X } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

Hence, for any $\theta , \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } g \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = n ^ { - 1 } .$
☒

Example 6.2.27 (Using Basu's Theorem-II) As another example of the use
of Basu's Theorem, we consider the independence of $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 } ,$ the sample mean
☒
and variance, when sampling from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. We have, of course, already
shown that these statistics are independent in Theorem 5.3.1, but we will illustrate
the use of Basu's Theorem in this important context. First consider $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ fixed and $\det \mu$
vary, $- \infty < \mu < \infty .$ By Example 6.2.4, $\bar { X }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu .$ Theorem 6.2.25
may be used to deduce that the family of $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right)$ distributions, $- \infty < \mu < \infty ,$
$\sigma ^ { 2 } / n$ known, is a complete family. Since this is the distribution of $\bar { X } ,$ $\bar { X }$ is a complete
☒ ☒
statistic. Now consider $S ^ { 2 } .$ An argument similar to those used in Examples 6.2.18 and
6.2.19 could be used to show that in any location parameter family (remember $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is
fixed, $\mu$ is the location parameter), $S ^ { 2 }$ is an ancillary statistic. Or, for this normal
model, we can use Theorem 5.3.1 to see that the distribution of $S ^ { 2 }$ depends on the
fixed quantity $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ but not on the parameter $\mu .$ Either way, $S ^ { 2 }$ is ancillary and $s o ,$ by
Basu's Theorem, $S ^ { 2 }$ is independent of the complete sufficient statistic $\check { \bar { X } } .$ For any $\mu$
☒
and the fixed $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are independent. But since $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ was arbitrary, we have that
☒

the sample mean and variance are independent for any choice of $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Note that
neither $\bar { X }$ nor $S ^ { 2 }$ is ancillary in this model when both $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are unknown. Yet, by
this argument, we are still able to use Basu's Theorem to deduce independence. This
kind of argument is sometimes useful, but the fact remains that it is often harder to
show that a statistic is complete than it is to show that two statistics are independent.
Il

It should be noted that the "minimality" of the sufficient statistic was not used
in the proof of Basu's Theoren1. Indeed, the theorem is true with this word omitted,
because a fundamental property of a complete statistic is that it is minimal.

Theorem 6.2.28 If a minimal sufficient statistic exists, then any complete statistic
is also a minimal sufficient statistic.

So even though the word "minimal" is redundant in the statement of Basu's Theo-
rem, it was stated in this way as a reminder that the statistic $T \left( X \right)$ in the theorem is
☒
a minimal sufficient statistic. (More about the relationship between complete statis-
tics and minimal sufficient statistics can be found in Lehmann and Scheffé 1950 and
Schervish 1995, Section 2.1.):

Basu's Theorem gives one relationship between sufficient statistics and ancillary
statistics using the concept of complete statistics. There are other possible definitions
of ancillarity and completeness. Some relationships between sufficiency and ancillarity
for these definitions are discussed by Lehmann (1981).

||

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="290" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->


## 6.3 The Likelihood Principle

In this section we study a specific, important statistic called the likelihood function
that also can be used to summarize data. There are many ways to use the likelihood
function some of which are mentioned in this section and some in later chapters. But
the main consideration in this section is an argument which indicates that, if certain
other principles are accepted, the likelihood function must be used as a data reduction
device.


### 6.3.1 The Likelihood Function

Definition 6.3.1 Let $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ denote the joint pdf or pmf of the sample $X =$
$\left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) .$ Then, given that $X = x$ is observed, the function of $\theta$ defined by

$$L \left( \theta | x \right) = f \left( x | \theta \right)$$

is called the likelihood function.

If $\mathrm { X }$ is a discrete random vector, then $L \left( \theta | x \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right) .$ If we compare the
likelihood function at two parameter points and find that

$$P _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \left( X = x \right) = L \left( \theta _ { 1 } | x \right) > L \left( \theta _ { 2 } | x \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( X = x \right) ,$$

then the sample we actually observed is more likely to have occurred if $\theta = \theta _ { 1 }$ than if
$\theta = \theta _ { 2 } ,$ which can be interpreted as saying that $\theta _ { 1 }$ is a more plausible value for the true
value of $\theta$ than is $\theta _ { 2 } .$ Many different ways have been proposed to use this information,
but certainly it seems reasonable to examine the probability of the sample we actually
observed under various possible values of 0. This is the information provided by the
likelihood function.

If $X$ is a continuous, real-valued random variable and if the pdf of $X$ is continuous
in x, then, for small $\epsilon$ €, $P _ { \theta } \left( x - \epsilon < X < x + \epsilon \right)$ is approximately $2 \epsilon f \left( x | \theta \right) = 2 \epsilon L \left( \theta | x \right)$
(this follows from the definition of a derivative). Thus,

$$\frac { P _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \left( x - \epsilon < X < x + \epsilon \right) } { P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( x - \epsilon < X < x + \epsilon \right) } \approx \frac { L \left( \theta _ { 1 } | x \right) } { L \left( \theta _ { 2 } | x \right) } ,$$

and comparison of the likelihood function at two parameter values again gives an
approximate comparison of the probability of the observed sample value, x.

Definition 6.3.1 almost seems to be defining the likelihood function to be the same
as the pdf or pmf. The only distinction between these two functions is which variable
is considered fixed and which is varying. When we consider the pdf or pmf $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$
we are considering $\theta$ as fixed and $x$ as the variable; when we consider the likelihood
function $L \left( \theta | x \right) ,$ we are considering $\mathrm { x }$ to be the observed sample point and $\theta$ to be
varying over all possible parameter values.

Example 6.3.2 (Negative binomial likelihood) Let $X$ have a negative bino-
mial distribution with $r = 3$ and success probability $p .$ If $x = 2$ is observed, then the
likelihood function is the fifth-degree polynomial on $0 \leq p \leq 1$ defined by

$$L \left( p | 2 \right) = P _ { p } \left( X = 2 \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { 4 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) p ^ { 3 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="291" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->

In general, if $X = x$ is observed, then the likelihood function is the polynomial of
degree $3 + x ,$

$$L \left( p | x \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { 3 + x - 1 } \\ { x } \end{array} \right) p ^ { 3 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { x } .$$

The Likelihood Principle specifies how the likelihood function should be used as a
data reduction device.

LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE: If $\mathrm { x }$ and $\mathrm { y }$ are two sample points such that $L \left( \theta | x \right)$ is
proportional to $L \left( \theta | y \right) ,$ that is, there exists a constant $C \left( x , y \right)$ such that

(6.3.1)
$$L \left( \theta | x \right) = C \left( x , y \right) L \left( \theta | y \right) \quad \text { for all } \theta ,$$

then the conclusions drawn from $x$ and $\mathrm { y }$ should be identical.

Note that the constant $C \left( x , y \right)$ in (6.3.1) may be different for different $\left( x , y \right)$ pairs
but $C \left( x , y \right)$ does not depend on $\theta .$

In the special case of $C \left( x , y \right) = 1 ,$ the Likelihood Principle states that if two
sample points result in the same likelihood function, then they contain the same
information about $\theta .$ But the Likelihood Principle goes further. It states that even
if two sample points have only proportional likelihoods, then they contain equivalent
information about $\theta .$ The rationale is this: The likelihood function is used to compare
the plausibility of various parameter values, and if $L \left( \theta _ { 2 } | x \right) = 2 L \left( \theta _ { 1 } | x \right) ,$ then, in some
sense, $\theta _ { 2 }$ is twice as plausible as $\theta _ { 1 } .$ If (6.3.1) is also true, then $L \left( \theta _ { 2 } | y \right) = 2 L \left( \theta _ { 1 } | y \right) .$
Thus, whether we observe $x$ or $\mathrm { y }$ we conclude that $\theta _ { 2 }$ is twice as plausible as $\theta _ { 1 } .$

We carefully used the word "plausible" rather than "probable" in the preceding
paragraph because we often think of $\theta$ as a fixed (albeit unknown) value. Furthermore,
although $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ as a function of $\mathrm { X } ,$ is a pdf, there is no guarantee that $L \left( \theta | x \right) ,$ as a
function of $\theta ,$ is a pdf.

One form of inference, called fiducial inference, sometimes interprets likelihoods
as probabilities for $\theta$ 0. That is, $L \left( \theta | x \right)$ is multiplied by $M \left( x \right) = \left( \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } L \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta \right) ^ { - 1 }$
(the integral is replaced by a sum if the parameter space is countable) and then
$M \left( x \right) L \left( \theta | x \right)$ is interpreted as a pdf for $\theta$ (provided, of course, that $M \left( x \right)$ is finite!).
Clearly, $L \left( \theta | x \right)$ and $L \left( \theta | y \right)$ satisfying (6.3.1) will yield the same pdf since the constant
$C \left( x , y \right)$ will simply be absorbed into the normalizing constant. Most statisticians do
not subscribe to the fiducial theory of inference but it has a long history, dating back
to the work of Fisher (1930) on what was called inverse probability (an application of
the probability integral transform). For now, we will for history's sake compute one
fiducial distribution.

Example 6.3.3 (Normal fiducial distribution) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
$\sigma ^ { 2 } k n o w n .$ Using expression (6.2.4) for $L \left( \mu | x \right) ,$ we note first that (6.3.1) is satisfied if
and only if $\bar { x } = \bar { y } ,$ in which case

$$C \left( x , y \right) = \exp \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) .$$

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="292" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->

Thus, the Likelihood Principle states that the same conclusion about $\mu$ should be
drawn for any two sample points satisfying $\bar { x } = \bar { y } .$ To compute the fiducial pdf for $\mu ,$
we see that if we define $M \left( x \right) = n ^ { n / 2 } \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right) ,$ then $M \left( x \right) L \left( \mu | x \right)$
(as a function of $\mu$ is a $n \left( \bar { x } , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right)$ pdf. This is the fiducial distribution of $\mu ,$ and a
fiducialist can make the following probability calculation regarding $\mu .$

The parameter $\mu$ has a $n \left( \bar { x } , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right)$ distribution. Hence, $\left( \mu - \bar { x } \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right)$ has a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$
distribution. Thus we have

$$. 9 5 = P \left( - 1 . 9 6 < \frac { \mu - \bar { x } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } < 1 . 9 6 \right)$$
$$= P \left( - 1 . 9 6 \sigma / \sqrt { n } < \mu - \bar { x } < 1 . 9 6 \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right)$$
$$= P \left( \bar { x } - 1 . 9 6 \sigma / \sqrt { n } < \mu < \bar { x } + 1 . 9 6 \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) .$$

This algebra is similar to earlier calculations but the interpretation is quite different.
Here $\bar { x }$ is a fixed, known number, the observed data value, and $\mu$ is the variable with
the normal probability distribution.
$I l$

We will discuss other more common uses of the likelihood function in later chapters
when we discuss specific methods of inference. But now we consider an argument
that shows that the Likelihood Principle is a necessary consequence of two other
fundamental principles.


### 6.3.2 The Formal Likelihood Principle

For discrete distributions, the Likelihood Principle can be derived from two intuitively
simpler ideas. This is also true, with some qualifications, for continuous distributions.
In this subsection we will deal only with discrete distributions. Berger and Wolpert
(1984) provide a thorough discussion of the Likelihood Principle in both the discrete
and continuous cases. These results were first proved by Birnbaum (1962) in a land-
mark paper, but our presentation more closely follows that of Berger and Wolpert.

Formally, we define an experiment $E$ to be a triple $\left( X , \theta , \left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) \right\} \right) ,$ where $X$ is a
random vector with pmf $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ for some $\theta$ in the parameter space $\Theta .$ An experimenter,
knowing what experiment $E$ was performed and having observed a particular sample
$X = x ,$ will make some inference or draw some conclusion about $\theta .$ This conclusion
we denote by $\mathrm { E v } \left( E , x \right) ,$ which stands for the evidence about $\theta$ arising from $E$ and $\mathrm { X } .$

Example 6.3.4 (Evidence function) Let $E$ be the experiment consisting of
observing $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. Since the sample mean, $\bar { X } ,$ is a sufficient
statistic for $\mu$ and $\mathrm { E } \bar { X } = \mu ,$ we might use the observed value $\bar { X } = \bar { x }$ as an estimate
of $\mu .$ To give a measure of the accuracy of this estimate, it is common to report the
standard deviation of $\sigma / \sqrt { n } .$ Thus we could define $\mathrm { E v } \left( E , x \right) = \left( \bar { x } , \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) .$ Here
we see that the $\bar { x }$ coordinate depends on the observed sample $\mathrm { x } ,$ while the $\sigma / \sqrt { n }$
coordinate depends on the knowledge of $E .$
11

To relate the concept of an evidence function to something familiar we now restate
the Sufficiency Principle of Section 6.2 in terms of these concepts.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="293" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->

FORMAL SUFFICIENCY PRINCIPLE: Consider experiment $E = \left( X , \theta , \left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) \right\} \right)$
and suppose $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ If $\mathrm { x }$ and $\mathrm { y }$ are sample points satisfying
$T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) ,$ then $\mathrm { E v } \left( E , \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E , \mathrm { y } \right) .$

Thus, the Formal Sufficiency Principle goes slightly further than the Sufficiency
Principle of Section 6.2. There no mention was made of the experiment. Here, we are
agreeing to equate evidence if the sufficient statistics match. The Likelihood Principle
can be derived from the Formal Sufficiency Principle and the following principle, an
eminently reasonable one.

CONDITIONALITY PRINCIPLE: Suppose that $E _ { 1 } = \left( X _ { 1 } , \theta , \left\{ f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } | \theta \right) \right\} \right)$ and $E _ { 2 }$
$= \left( X _ { 2 } , \theta , \left\{ f _ { 2 } \left( x _ { 2 } | \theta \right) \right\} \right)$ are two experiments, where only the unknown parameter $\theta$
need be common between the two experiments. Consider the mixed experiment in
which the random variable $J$ is observed, where $P \left( J = 1 \right) = P \left( J = 2 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$
(independent of $\theta ,$ $\mathrm { X } _ { 1 } ,$ or $X _ { 2 } ,$ and then experiment $E _ { J }$ is performed. Formally,
the experiment performed is $E ^ { * } = \left( X ^ { * } , \theta , \left\{ f ^ { * } \left( x ^ { * } | \theta \right) \right\} \right) ,$ where $X ^ { * } = \left( j , X _ { j } \right)$ and
$f ^ { * } \left( \mathrm { x } ^ { * } | \theta \right) = f ^ { * } \left( \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) | \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } f _ { j } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { j } | \theta \right) .$ Then

(6.3.2)
$$\mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { j } , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) .$$

The Conditionality Principle simply says that if one of two experiments is randomly
chosen and the chosen experiment is done, yielding data x, the information about $\theta$
depends only on the experiment performed. That is, it is the same information as
would have been obtained if it were decided (nonrandomly) to do that experiment
from the beginning, and data $x$ had been observed. The fact that this experiment
was performed, rather than some other, has not increased, decreased, or changed
knowledge of $\theta .$

Example 6.3.5 (Binomial/negative binomial experiment) Suppose the $p a -$
rameter of interest is the probability $p ,$ $0 < p < 1 ,$ where $p$ denotes the probability
that a particular coin will land "heads" when it is flipped. Let $E _ { 1 }$ be the experiment
consisting of tossing the coin 20 times and recording the number of heads in those
20 tosses. $E _ { 1 }$ is a binomial experiment and $\left\{ f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } | p \right) \right\}$ is the family of binomial(20, $\left. p \right)$
pmfs. Let $E _ { 2 }$ be the experiment consisting of tossing the coin until the seventh head
occurs and recording the number of tails before the seventh head. $E _ { 2 }$ is a negative
binomial experiment. Now suppose the experimenter uses a random number table to
choose between these two experiments, happens to choose $E _ { 2 } ,$ and collects data con-
sisting of the seventh head occurring on trial 20. The Conditionality Principle says
that the information about $\theta$ that the experimenter now has, $E v \left( E ^ { * } , \left( 2 , 1 3 \right) \right) ,$ is the
same as that which he would have, $E v \left( E _ { 2 } , 1 3 \right) ,$ if he had just chosen to do the negative
binomial experiment and had never contemplated the binomial experiment.
II

The following Formal Likelihood Principle can now be derived from the Formal
Sufficiency Principle and the Conditionality Principle.

FORMAL LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE: Suppose that we have two experiments,
$E _ { 1 } = \left( X _ { 1 } , \theta , \left\{ f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } | \theta \right) \right\} \right)$ and $E _ { 2 } = \left( X _ { 2 } , \theta , \left\{ f _ { 2 } \left( x _ { 2 } | \theta \right) \right\} \right) ,$ where the unknown parameter
$\theta$ is the same in both experiments. Suppose $\mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * }$ and $\mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * }$ are sample points from $E _ { 1 }$ and

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="294" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->

$E _ { 2 } ,$ respectively, such that

(6.3.3)
$$L \left( \theta | x _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) = C L \left( \theta | x _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right)$$

for all $\theta$ and for some constant $C$ that may depend on $\mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * }$ and $\mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * }$ but not $\theta .$ Then

$$\mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 1 } , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 2 } , \mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) .$$

The Formal Likelihood Principle is different from the Likelihood Principle in Section
6.3.1 because the Formal Likelihood Principle concerns two experiments, whereas the
Likelihood Principle concerns only one. The Likelihood Principle, however, can be
derived from the Formal Likelihood Principle by letting $E _ { 2 }$ be an exact replicate of
$E _ { 1 } .$ Thus, the two-experiment setting in the Formal Likelihood Principle is something
of an artifact and the important consequence is the following corollary, whose proof
is left as an exercise. (See Exercise 6.32.)

LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE COROLLARY: If $E = \left( X , \theta , \left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) \right\} \right)$ is an experiment,
then $\mathrm { E v } \left( E , \mathrm { x } \right)$ should depend on $E$ and $\mathrm { x }$ only through $L \left( \theta | x \right) .$

Now we state Birnbaum's Theorem and then investigate its somewhat surprising
consequences.

Theorem 6.3.6 (Birnbaum's Theorem) The Formal Likelihood Principle fol-
lows from the Formal Sufficiency Principle and the Conditionality $P r i n c i p l e .$ The
converse is also true.

Proof: We only outline the proof, leaving details to Exercise 6.33. Let $E _ { 1 } ,$ $E _ { 2 } ,$ $\mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } ,$
and $\mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * }$ be as defined in the Formal Likelihood Principle, and let $E ^ { * }$ be the mixed
experiment from the Conditionality Principle. On the sample space of $E ^ { * }$ define the
statistic

$$T \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \left( 1 , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) & \mathrm { i f } j = 1 \mathrm { a n d } \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } = \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \mathrm { o r } \mathrm { i f } j = 2 \mathrm { a n d } \mathrm { x } _ { 2 } = \mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * } \\ \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) & \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e } . \end{array} \right.$$

The Factorization Theorem can be used to prove that $T \left( J , X _ { J } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic
in the $E ^ { * }$ experiment. Then the Formal Sufficiency Principle implies

(6.3.4)
$$\mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( 1 , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( 2 , \mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) \right) ,$$

the Conditionality Principle implies

(6.3.5)
$$\mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( 1 , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 1 } , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right)$$
$$\mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( 2 , x _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 2 } , x _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) ,$$

and we can deduce that $\mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 1 } , \mathrm { x } _ { 1 } ^ { * } \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E _ { 2 } , \mathrm { x } _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) ,$ the Formal Likelihood Principle.

To prove the converse, first let one experiment be the $E ^ { * }$ experiment and the other
$E _ { j } .$ It can be shown that $\mathrm { E v } \left( E ^ { * } , \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \mathrm { V } } \left( E _ { j } , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) ,$ the Conditionality Principle.
Then, if $T \left( X \right)$ is sufficient and $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) ,$ the likelihoods are proportional and the
☐ ☒
Formal Likelihood Principle implies that $\mathrm { E v } \left( E , \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { E v } \left( E , \mathrm { y } \right) ,$ the Formal Sufficiency
Principle.
0
:

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="295" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE LIKELIHOOD PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.3" -->

Example 6.3.7 (Continuation of Example 6.3.5) Consider again the binomial
and negative binomial experiments with the two sample points $x _ { 1 } = 7$ (7 out of 20
heads in the binomial experiment) and $x _ { 2 } = 1 3$ (the 7th head occurs on the 20th flip
of the coin). The likelihood functions are

$$L \left( p | x _ { 1 } = 7 \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { 2 0 } \\ { 7 } \end{array} \right) p ^ { 7 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 1 3 } \quad \text { for the binomial experil }$$

and

$$L \left( p | x _ { 2 } = 1 3 \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { 1 9 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right) p ^ { 7 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 1 3 } \quad \text { for the negative binc }$$
experiment.

These are proportional likelihood functions, so the Formal Likelihood Principle states
that the same conclusion regarding $p$ should be made in both cases. In particular,
the Formal Likelihood Principle asserts that the fact that in the first case sampling
ended because 20 trials were completed and in the second case sampling stopped
because the 7th head was observed is immaterial as far as our conclusions about $p$ are
concerned. Lindley and Phillips (1976) give a thorough discussion of the binomial-
negative binomial inference problem.
Il

This point, of equivalent inferences from different experiments, may be amplified by
considering the sufficient statistic, $T ,$ defined in the proof of Birnbaum's Theorem and
the sample points $x _ { 1 } ^ { * } = 7$ and $x _ { 2 } ^ { * } = 1 3 .$ For any sample points in the mixed experiment,
other than $\left( 1 , 7 \right)$ or $\left( 2 , 1 3 \right) ,$ $T$ tells which experiment, binomial or negative binomial,
was performed and the result of the experiment. But for (1,7) and $\left( 2 , 1 3 \right)$ we have
$T \left( 1 , 7 \right) = T \left( 2 , 1 3 \right) = \left( 1 , 7 \right) .$ If we use only the sufficient statistic to make an inference
and if $T = \left( 1 , 7 \right) ,$ then all we know is that 7 out of 20 heads were observed. We do
not know whether the 7 or the 20 was the fixed quantity.

Many common statistical procedures violate the Formal Likelihood Principle. With
these procedures, different conclusions would be reached for the two experiments dis-
cussed in Example 6.3.5. This violation of the Formal Likelihood Principle may seem
strange because, by Birnbaum's Theorem, we are then violating either the Sufficiency
Principle or the Conditionality Principle. Let us examine these two principles more
closely.

The Formal Sufficiency Principle is, in essence, the same as that discussed in Section
6.1. There, we saw that all the information about $\theta$ is contained in the sufficient
statistic, and knowledge of the entire sample cannot add any information. Thus,
basing evidence on the sufficient statistic is an eminently plausible principle. One
shortcoming of this principle, one that invites violation, is that it is very model-
dependent. As mentioned in the discussion after Example 6.2.9, belief in this principle
necessitates belief in the model, something that may not be easy to do.

Most data analysts perform some sort of "model checking" when analyzing a set
of data. Most model checking is, necessarily, based on statistics other than a suffi-
cient statistic. For example, it is common practice to examine residuals from a model,
statistics that measure variation in the data not accounted for by the model. (We will
see residuals in more detail in Chapters 11 and 12.) Such a practice immediately vio-
lates the Sufficiency Principle, since the residuals are not based on sufficient statistics.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="296" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.4" -->

(Of course, such a practice directly violates the Likelihood Principle also.) Thus, it
must be realized that before considering the Sufficiency Principle (or the Likelihood
Principle), we must be comfortable with the model.

The Conditionality Principle, stated informally, says that "only the experiment
actually performed matters." That is, in Example 6.3.5, if we did the binomial ex-
periment, and not the negative binomial experiment, then the (not done) negative
binomial experiment should in no way influence our conclusion about 0. This princi-
ple, also, seems to be eminently plausible.

How, then, can statistical practice violate the Formal Likelihood Principle, when
it would mean violating either the Principle of Sufficiency or Conditionality? Several
authors have addressed this question, among them Durbin (1970) and Kalbfleisch
(1975). One argument, put forth by Kalbfleisch, is that the proof of the Formal
Likelihood Principle is not compelling. This is because the Sufficiency Principle is
applied in ignorance of the Conditionality Principle. The sufficient statistic, $T \left( J , X _ { J } \right) ,$
used in the proof of Theorem 6.3.6 is defined on the mixture experiment. If the
Conditionality Principle were invoked first, then separate sufficient statistics would
have to be defined for each experiment. In this case, the Formal Likelihood Principle
would no longer follow. (A key argument in the proof of Birnbaum's Theorem is that
$T \left( J , X _ { J } \right)$ can take on the same value for sample points from each experiment. This
☒
cannot happen with separate sufficient statistics.)

At any rate, since many intuitively appealing inference procedures do violate the
Likelihood Principle, it is not universally accepted by all statisticians. Yet it $\mathrm { i s }$ math-
ematically appealing and does suggest a useful data reduction technique.


## 6.4 The Equivariance Principle

The previous two sections both describe data reduction principles in the following
way. A function $T \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ of the sample is specified, and the principle states that if $\mathrm { x }$ and
☒
$\mathrm { y }$ are two sample points with $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) ,$ then the same inference about $\theta$ should be
☒

made whether $\mathrm { X }$ or $\mathrm { y }$ is observed. The function $T \left( x \right)$ is a sufficient statistic when the
☒ ☒

Sufficiency Principle is used. The "value" of $T \left( x \right)$ is the set of all likelihood functions
proportional to $L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right)$ if the Likelihood Principle is used. The Equivariance Principle
describes a data reduction technique in a slightly different way. In any application of
the Equivariance Principle, a function $T \left( x \right)$ is specified, but if $T \left( x \right) = T \left( y \right) ,$ then the
☒

☒ ☒
Equivariance Principle states that the inference made if $\mathrm { x }$ is observed should have a
certain relationship to the inference made if $\mathrm { y }$ is observed, although the two inferences
may not be the same. This restriction on the inference procedure sometimes leads to a
simpler analysis, just as do the data reduction principles discussed in earlier sections.2

Although commonly combined into what is called the Equivariance Principle, the
data reduction technique we will now describe actually combines two different equi-
variance considerations.

2 As in many other texts (Schervish 1995; Lehmann and Casella 1998; Stuart, Ord, and Arnold
1999) we distinguish between equivariance, in which the estimate changes in a prescribed way as
the data are transformed, and invariance, in which the estimate remains unchanged as the data
are transformed.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="297" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE EQUIVARIANCE PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.4" -->

The first type of equivariance might be called measurement equivariance. It pre-
scribes that the inference made should not depend on the measurement scale that is
used. For example, suppose two foresters are going to estimate the average diameter
of trees in a forest. The first uses data on tree diameters expressed in inches, and
the second uses the same data expressed in meters. Now both are asked to produce
an estimate in inches. (The second might conveniently estimate the average diame-
ter in meters and then transform the estimate to inches.) Measurement equivariance
requires that both foresters produce the same estimates. No doubt, almost all would
agree that this type of equivariance is reasonable.

The second type of equivariance, actually an invariance, might be called formal
invariance. It states that if two inference problems have the same formal structure in
terms of the mathematical model used, then the same inference procedure should be
used in both problems. The elements of the model that must be the same are: $\Theta ,$ the
parameter space; $\left\{ f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\} ,$ the set of pdfs or pmfs for the sample; and the
set of allowable inferences and consequences of wrong inferences. This last element
has not been discussed much prior to this; for this section we will assume that the
set of possible inferences is the same as $\Theta$ that is, an inference is simply a choice of
an element of $\Theta$ as an estimate or guess at the true value of $\theta .$ Formal invariance is
concerned only with the mathematical entities involved, not the physical description
of the experiment. For example, $\Theta$ may be $\Theta = \left\{ \theta : \theta > 0 \right\}$ in two problems. But in one
problem $\theta$ may be the average price of a dozen eggs in the United States (measured in
cents) and in another problem $\theta$ may refer to the average height of giraffes in Kenya
(measured in meters). Yet, formal invariance equates these two parameter spaces since
they both refer to the same set of real numbers.

EQUIVARIANCE PRINCIPLE: If $Y = g \left( X \right)$ is a change of measurement scale such
☒
that the model for $\mathrm { Y }$ has the same formal structure as the model for $\mathrm { X } ,$ then an in-
ference procedure should be both measurement equivariant and formally equivariant.

We will now illustrate how these two concepts of equivariance can work together
to provide useful data reduction.

Example 6.4.1 (Binomial equivariance) Let $X$ have a binomial distribution
with sample size $n$ known and success probability $p$ unknown. Let $T \left( x \right)$ be the estimate
of $p$ that is used when $X = x$ is observed. Rather than using the number of successes,
$X ,$ to make an inference about $p ,$ we could use the number of failures, $Y = n \quad - \quad X .$
$Y$ also has a binomial distribution with parameters $\left( n , q = 1 \quad - \quad p \right) .$ Let $T ^ { * } \left( y \right)$ be the
estimate of $q$ that is used when $Y = y$ is observed, so that $1 - T ^ { * } \left( y \right)$ is the estimate
of $p$ when $Y = y$ is observed. If $x$ successes are observed, then the estimate of $p$ is
$T \left( x \right) .$ But if there are $x$ successes, then there are $n \quad - \quad x$ failures and $1 - T ^ { * } \left( n - x \right)$ is
also an estimate of $p .$ Measurement equivariance requires that these two estimates be
equal, that is, $T \left( x \right) = 1 - T ^ { * } \left( n - x \right) ,$ since the change from $X$ to $Y$ is just a change
in measurement scale. Furthermore, the formal structures of the inference problems
based on $X$ and $Y$ are the same. $X$ and $Y$ both have binomial(n, $\left( n , \theta \right)$ distributions,
$0 \leq \theta \leq 1 .$ So formal invariance requires that $T \left( z \right) = T ^ { * } \left( z \right)$ for all $z = 0 , \ldots , n .$ Thus,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="298" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.4" -->

measurement and formal invariance together require that

(6.4.1)
$$T \left( x \right) = 1 - T ^ { * } \left( n - x \right) = 1 - T \left( n - x \right) .$$

If we consider only estimators satisfying $\left( 6 . 4 . 1 \right) ,$ then we have greatly reduced and sim-
plified the set of estimators we are willing to consider. Whereas the specification of an
arbitrary estimator requires the specification of $T \left( 0 \right) , T \left( 1 \right) , \ldots , T \left( n \right) ,$ the specification
of an estimator satisfying (6.4.1) requires the specification only of
$T \left( 0 \right) , T \left( 1 \right) , \ldots , T \left( \left[ n / 2 \right] \right) ,$ where $\left[ n / 2 \right]$ is the greatest integer not larger than $n / 2 .$ The
remaining values of $T \left( x \right)$ are determined by those already specified and (6.4.1). For
example, $T \left( n \right) = 1 - T \left( 0 \right)$ and $T \left( n - 1 \right) = 1 - T \left( 1 \right) .$ This is the type of data reduction
that is always achieved by the Equivariance Principle. The inference to be made for
some sample points determines the inference to be made for other sample points.

Two estimators that are equivariant for this problem are $T _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = x / n$ and $T _ { 2 } \left( x \right) =$
$. 9 \left( x / n \right) + . 1 \left( . 5 \right) .$ The estimator $T _ { 1 } \left( x \right)$ uses the sample proportion of successes to
estimate $p .$ $T _ { 2 } \left( x \right)$ "shrinks" the sample proportion toward .5, an estimator that might
be sensible if there is reason to think that $p$ is near .5. Condition $\left( 6 . 4 . 1 \right)$ is easily
verified for both of these estimators and so they are both equivariant. An estimator
that is not equivariant is $T _ { 3 } \left( x \right) = . 8 \left( x / n \right) + . 2 \left( 1 \right) .$ Condition $\left( 6 . 4 . 1 \right)$ is not satisfied
since $T _ { 3 } \left( 0 \right) = . 2 \neq 0 = 1 - T _ { 3 } \left( n - 0 \right) .$ See Exercise 6.39 for more on measurement vs.
formal invariance.

!

A key to the equivariance argument in Example 6.4.1 and to any equivariance argu-
ment is the choice of the transformations. The data transformation used in Example
6.4.1 is $Y = n - \quad X .$ The transformations (changes of measurement scale) used in any
application of the Equivariance Principle are described by a set of functions on the
sample space called a group of transformations.

Definition 6.4.2 A set of functions $\left\{ g \left( x \right) : g \in \mathcal{G} \right\}$ from the sample space $\mathcal{X}$ onto
$\mathcal{X}$ is called a group of transformations of $\mathcal{X}$ if

(i) (Inverse) For every $g \in \mathcal{G}$ there is a $g ^ { \prime } \in \mathcal{G}$ such that $g ^ { \prime } \left( g \left( x \right) \right) = x$ for all $\mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X} .$

(ii) (Composition) For every $g \in \mathcal{G}$ and $g ^ { \prime } \in \mathcal{G}$ there exists $g ^ { \prime \prime } \in \mathcal{G}$ such that
$g ^ { \prime } \left( g \left( x \right) \right) = g ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x \right)$ for all $\mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X} .$

Sometimes the third requirement,

(iii) (Identity) The identity, $e \left( x \right) ,$ defined by $e \left( x \right) = x$ is an element of $\mathcal{G} ,$
☒
is stated as part of the definition of a group. But (iii) is a consequence of (i) and $\left( \mathrm { i i } \right)$
and need not be verified separately. (See Exercise 6.38.)

Example 6.4.3 (Continuation of Example 6.4.1) For this problem, only two
transformations are involved so we may set $G = \left\{ g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } \right\} ,$ with $g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) = n - x$ and
$g _ { 2 } \left( x \right) = x .$ Conditions (i) and (ii) are easily verified. The choice of $g ^ { \prime } = g$ verifies (i),
that is, each element is its own inverse. For example,

$$g _ { 1 } \left( g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \right) = g _ { 1 } \left( n - x \right) = n - \left( n - x \right) = x .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="299" -->
<!-- PageHeader="THE EQUIVARIANCE PRINCIPLE" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.4" -->

In (ii), if $g ^ { \prime } = g ,$ then $g ^ { \prime \prime } = g _ { 2 } ,$ while if $g ^ { \prime } \neq g ,$ then $g ^ { \prime \prime } = g _ { 1 }$ satisfies the equality. For
example, take $g ^ { \prime } \neq g = g _ { 1 } .$ Then

$$g _ { 2 } \left( g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) \right) = g _ { 2 } \left( n - x \right) = n - x = g _ { 1 } \left( x \right) .$$

!I

To use the Equivariance Principle, we must be able to apply formal invariance to
the transformed problem. That is, after changing the measurement scale we must
still have the same formal structure. As the structure does not change, we want the
underlying model, or family of distributions, to be invariant. This requirement is
summarized in the next definition.

Definition 6.4.4 Let $\mathcal{F} = \left\{ f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ be a set of pdfs or pmfs for $\mathrm { X } ,$ and let
$\mathcal{G}$ be a group of transformations of the sample space $\mathcal{X} .$ Then $\mathcal{F}$ is invariant under
the group $\mathcal{G}$ if for every $\theta \in \Theta$ and $g \in \mathcal{G}$ there exists a unique $\theta ^ { \prime } \in \Theta$ such that
$Y = g \left( X \right)$ has the distribution $f \left( y | \theta ^ { \prime } \right)$ if $\mathrm { X }$ has the distribution $f \left( x | \theta \right) .$
☒

Example 6.4.5 (Conclusion of Example 6.4.1) In the binomial problem,
we must check both $g _ { 1 }$ and $g _ { 2 } .$ If $X \sim b i n o m i a l \left( n , p \right) ,$ then $g _ { 1 } \left( X \right) = n - X \sim$
☒
binomial(n, $\left. 1 \quad - \quad p \right)$ so $p ^ { \prime } = 1 - p ,$ where $p$ plays the role of $\theta$ in Definition 6.4.4.
Also $g _ { 2 } \left( X \right) = X \sim$ binomial(n, p) so $p ^ { \prime } = p$ in this case. Thus the set of binomial
☒
pmfs is invariant under the group $\mathcal{G} = \left\{ g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } \right\} .$
II

In Example 6.4.1, the group of transformations had only two elements. In many
cases, the group of transformations is infinite, as the next example illustrates (see
also Exercises 6.41 and 6.42).

Example 6.4.6 (Normal location invariance) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
both $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ unknown. Consider the group of transformations defined by $\mathcal{G} =$
$\left\{ g _ { a } \left( x \right) , - \infty < a < \infty \right\}$ , where $g _ { a } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left( x _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , x _ { n } + a \right)$ ). To verify that
this set of transformations is a group, conditions (i) and (ii) from Definition 6.4.2
must be verified. For (i) note that

$$g _ { - a } \left( g _ { a } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \right) = g _ { - a } \left( x _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , x _ { n } + a \right)$$
$$= \left( x _ { 1 } + a - a , \ldots , x _ { n } + a - a \right)$$
$$= \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$$

So if $g = g _ { a } ,$ then $g ^ { \prime } = g _ { - a }$ satisfies (i). For (ii) note that

$$g _ { a _ { 2 } } \left( g _ { a _ { 1 } } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \right) = g _ { a _ { 2 } } \left( x _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } + a _ { 1 } \right)$$
$$= \left( x _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } + a _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= g _ { a _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$$

So if $g = g _ { a _ { 1 } }$ and $g ^ { \prime } = g _ { a _ { 2 } } ,$ then $g ^ { \prime \prime } = g _ { a _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } }$ satisfies (ii), and Definition 6.4.2 is
verified. $\mathcal{G}$ is a group of transformations.

The set $\mathcal{F}$ in this problem is the set of all joint densities $f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ for
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ defined by ${ } ^ { \mathfrak{c} } X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are id $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ for some $- \infty < \mu < \infty$ and

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="300" -->
<!-- PageHeader="PRINCIPLES OF DATA REDUCTION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 6.5" -->

$\sigma ^ { 2 } > 0$ 0." For any $a , - \infty < a < \infty ,$ the random variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ defined by

$$\left( Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } \right) = g _ { a } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) = \left( X _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , X _ { n } + a \right)$$

are iid $n \left( \mu + a , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ random variables. Thus, the joint distribution of $Y = g _ { a } \left( X \right)$ is in
☒
$\mathcal{F}$ and hence $\mathcal{F}$ is invariant under $\mathcal{G} .$ In terms of the notation in Definition 6.4.4, if
$\theta = \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ then $\theta ^ { \prime } = \left( \mu + a , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$
Il

Remember, once again, that the Equivariance Principle is composed of two distinct
types of equivariance. One type, measurement equivariance, is intuitively reasonable.
When many people think of the Equivariance Principle, they think that it refers
only to measurement equivariance. If this were the case, the Equivariance Principle
would probably be universally accepted. But the other principle, formal invariance, is
quite different. It equates any two problems with the same mathematical structure,
regardless of the physical reality they are trying to explain. It says that one inference
procedure is appropriate even $i f$ the physical realities are quite different, an assumption
that is sometimes difficult to justify.

But like the Sufficiency Principle and the Likelihood Principle, the Equivariance
Principle is a data reduction technique that restricts inference by prescribing what
other inferences must be made at related sample points. All three principles pre-
scribe relationships between inferences at different sample points, restricting the set
of allowable inferences and, in this way, simplifying the analysis of the problem.


## 6.5 Exercises

6.1 Let $X$ be one observation from a $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Is $| X |$ a sufficient statistic?
☒ ☒

6.2 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be independent random variables with densities

$$f _ { X _ { i } } \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { i \theta - x } } & { x \geq i \theta } \\ { 0 } & { x < i \theta } \end{array} \right.$$

Prove that $T = \min _ { i } \left( X _ { i } / i \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$

6.3 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from the pdf

$$f \left( x | \mu , \sigma \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sigma } e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) / \sigma } , \mu < x < \infty , 0 < \sigma < \infty .$$

Find a two-dimensional sufficient statistic for $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) .$

6.4 Prove Theorem 6.2.10.

6.5 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be independent random variables with pdfs

$$f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \frac { 1 } { 2 i \theta } } & { - i \left( \theta - 1 \right) < x _ { i } < i \left( \theta + 1 \right) } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right.$$

where $\theta > 0 .$ Find a two-dimensional sufficient statistic for $\theta .$

6.6 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ population. Find a two-
dimensional sufficient statistic for $\left( \alpha , \beta \right) .$

6.7 Let $f \left( x , y | \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \theta _ { 3 } , \theta _ { 4 } \right)$ be the bivariate pdf for the uniform distribution on the rectan-
gle with lower left corner $\left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } \right)$ and upper right corner $\left( \theta _ { 3 } , \theta _ { 4 } \right)$ in $\mathfrak{R} ^ { 2 } .$ The parameters
satisfy $\theta _ { 1 } < \theta _ { 3 }$ and $\theta _ { 2 } < \theta _ { 4 } .$ Let $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ be a random sample from this
pdf. Find a four-dimensional sufficient statistic for $\left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } , \theta _ { 3 } , \theta _ { 4 } \right) .$

sample, and define $Y _ { i } = X _ { \left( n \right) } - X _ { \left( i \right) } , i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1 .$

6.12 $A$ natural ancillary statistic in most problems is the sample size. For example, let $N$
be a random variable taking values $1 , 2 , \cdots .$ with known probabilities $p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ where
$\Sigma p _ { i } = 1 .$ Having observed $N = n ,$ perform $n$ Bernoulli trials with success probability
$\theta ,$ getting $X$ successes.

(a) Prove that the pair $\left( X , N \right)$ is minimal sufficient and $N$ is ancillary for $\theta .$ (Note
the similarity to some of the hierarchical models discussed in Section 4.4.)

(b) Prove that the estimator $X / N$ is unbiased for $\theta$ and has variance $\theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) \mathrm { E } \left( 1 / N \right) .$
6.13 Suppose $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ are id observations from the pdf $f \left( x | \alpha \right) = \alpha x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x ^ { \alpha } } , x > 0 , \alpha >$

ancillary statistic, where $M$ is the sample median.

6.31 Boos and Hughes-Oliver (1998) detail a number of instances where application of
Basu's Theorem can simplify calculations. Here are a few.

(a) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known.
☒ ☒

(i) Show that $\bar { X }$ is complete sufficient for $\mu ,$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ is ancillary. Hence by Basu's
☒

Theorem, $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are independent.
☒

(ii) Show that this independence carries over even if $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown, as knowledge

involved Theorem 5.3.1(a).)

(b) A Monte Carlo swindle is a technique for improving variance estimates. Suppose
that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and that we want to compute the variance of the
☒ ☒

median, $M .$

☒ ☒
only have to simulate the $\mathrm { V a r } \left( M - \bar { X } \right)$ piece $\mathrm { o f } \mathrm { V a r } \left( M \right)$ (since $\left. \mathrm { V a r } \left( \bar { X } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒

(ii) Show that the swindle estimate is more precise by showing that the variance of
$M$ is approximately $2 \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( M \right) \right] ^ { 2 } / \left( N - 1 \right)$ and that of $M - \bar { X }$ is approximately
☒
$2 \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( M - \bar { X } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } / \left( N - 1 \right) ,$ where $N$ is the number of Monte Carlo samples.
☒

(c) (i) If $X / Y$ and $Y$ are independent random variables, show that
☒

$$E \left( \frac { X } { Y } \right) ^ { k } = \frac { E \left( X ^ { k } \right) } { E \left( Y ^ { k } \right) } .$$

(ii) Use this result and Basu's Theorem to show that if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid
☒ ☒
gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ where $\alpha$ is known, then for $T = \sum _ { i } X _ { i }$

$$\mathrm { E } \left( X _ { \left( i \right) } | T \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \frac { X _ { \left( i \right) } } { T } T | T \right) = T \frac { \mathrm { E } \left( X _ { \left( i \right) } \right) } { \mathrm { E } T } .$$

6.32 Prove the Likelihood Principle Corollary. That is, assuming both the Formal Sufficiency

☒ ☒

☒

$$h \left( j , x _ { j } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { C } & { \text { if } \left( j , x _ { j } \right) = \left( 2 , x _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right) } \\ { 1 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Show that $T \left( j , x _ { j } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic in the $E ^ { * }$ experiment by verifying that

$$g \left( T \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) | \theta \right) h \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) = f ^ { * } \left( \left( j , \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) | \theta \right)$$

6.34 Consider the model in Exercise 6.12. Show that the Formal Likelihood Principle implies

6.35 A risky experimental treatment is to be given to at most three patients. The treatment

model and show that, according to the Formal Likelihood Principle, the inference
about $p$ should not depend on the fact that the sample size was determined by the
data.

6.36 One advantage of using a minimal sufficient statistic is that unbiased estimators will
have smaller variance, as the following exercise will show. Suppose that $T _ { 1 }$ is sufficient
and $T _ { 2 }$ is minimal sufficient, $U$ is an unbiased estimator of $\theta ,$ and define $U _ { 1 } = E \left( U | T _ { 1 } \right)$
and $U _ { 2 } = E \left( U | T _ { 2 } \right) .$

(a) Show that $U _ { 2 } = E \left( U _ { 1 } | T _ { 2 } \right) .$

(b) Now use the conditional variance formula (Theorem 4.4.7) to show that Var $U _ { 2 } <$
$\mathrm { V a r } U _ { 1 } .$

(See Pena and Rohatgi 1994 for more on the relationship between sufficiency and
unbiasedness.)

$$f \left( x , y | \theta \right) = \exp \left\{ - \left( \theta x + y / \theta \right) \right\} , \quad x > 0 , \quad y > 0 .$$

$$T = \sqrt { \sum Y _ { i } / \sum X _ { i } } \text { and } U = \sqrt { \sum X _ { i } \sum Y _ { i } } ,$$ ☒ ☒
