we can usually apply more than one of these methods in a particular situation, we
are often faced with the task of choosing between estimators. Of course, it is possible
that different methods of finding estimators will yield the same answer, which makes
evaluation a bit easier, but, in many cases, different methods will lead to different
estimators.

The general topic of evaluating statistical procedures is part of the branch of statis-
tics known as decision theory, which will be treated in some detail in Section 7.3.4.
However, no procedure should be considered until some clues about its performance
have been gathered. In this section we will introduce some basic criteria for evaluating
estimators, and examine several estimators against these criteria.


#### 7.3.1 Mean Squared Error

We first investigate finite-sample measures of the quality of an estimator, beginning
with its mean squared error.

Definition 7.3.1 The mean squared error (MSE) of an estimator $W$ of a parameter
$\theta$ is the function of $\theta$ defined by $E _ { \theta } \left( W - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } .$

Notice that the MSE measures the average squared difference between the estimator
$W$ and the parameter $\theta ,$ a somewhat reasonable measure of performance for a point
estimator. In general, any increasing function of the absolute distance $| W - \theta |$ would
serve to measure the goodness of an estimator (mean absolute error, $E _ { \theta } \left( | W - \theta | \right) ,$ is
a reasonable alternative), but MSE has at least two advantages over other distance
measures: First, it is quite tractable analytically and, second, it has the interpretation

$$E _ { \theta } \left( W - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + \left( \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + \left( \mathrm { B i a s } _ { \theta } W \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$
(7.3.1)

where we define the bias of an estimator as follows.

Definition 7.3.2 The bias of a point estimator $W$ of a parameter $\theta$ is the difference
between the expected value of $W$ and $\theta$ that is, $\mathrm { B i a s } _ { \theta } W = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W - \theta .$ An estimator
whose bias is identically (in $\theta$ equal to 0 is called unbiased and satisfies $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W = \theta$ for
all $\theta .$

Thus, MSE incorporates two components, one measuring the variability of the
estimator (precision) and the other measuring its bias (accuracy). An estimator that
has good MSE properties has small combined variance and bias. To find an estimator
with good MSE properties, we need to find estimators that control both variance and
bias. Clearly, unbiased estimators do a good job of controlling bias.

For an unbiased estimator we have

$$E _ { \theta } \left( W - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ,$$

and so, if an estimator is unbiased, its MSE is equal to its variance.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="331" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Example 7.3.3 (Normal MSE) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ The statistics $\bar { X }$
and $S ^ { 2 }$ are both unbiased estimators since

$$E X = \mu , \quad E S ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } , \quad \text { for all } \mu \text { and } \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$

(This is true without the normality assumption; see Theorem 5.2.6.) The MSEs of
these estimators are given by

$$E \left( \bar { X } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } \bar { X } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n } ,$$
$$E \left( S ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \text { Var } S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n - 1 }$$

The MSE of $\bar { X }$ remains $\sigma ^ { 2 } / n$ even if the normality assumption is dropped. However,
the above expression for the MSE of $S ^ { 2 }$ does not remain the same if the normality
assumption is relaxed (see Exercise 5.8).

!!

Although many unbiased estimators are also reasonable from the standpoint of
MSE, be aware that controlling bias does not guarantee that MSE is controlled. In
particular, it is sometimes the case that a trade-off occurs between variance and bias
in such a way that a small increase in bias can be traded for a larger decrease in
variance, resulting in an improvement in MSE.

Example 7.3.4 (Continuation of Example 7.3.3) An alternative estimator
for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is the maximum likelihood estimator $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { n - 1 } { n } S ^ { 2 } .$ It is

☒ ☒
straightforward to calculate

$$E \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = E \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n } S ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { n - 1 } { n } \sigma ^ { 2 }$$

so $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is a biased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ The variance of $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ can also be calculated as

$$\mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n } S ^ { 2 } \right) = \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n } \right) ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 \left( n - 1 \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } } ,$$

and, hence, its MSE is given by

$$E \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 \left( n - 1 \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { n - 1 } { n } \sigma ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { 2 n - 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } .$$

We thus have

$$E \left( \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { 2 n - 1 } { n ^ { 2 } } \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } < \left( \frac { 2 } { n - 1 } \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } = \mathrm { E } \left( S ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

showing that $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ has smaller MSE than $S ^ { 2 } .$ Thus, by trading off variance for bias, the
MSE is improved.
|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="332" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

We hasten to point out that the above example does not imply that $S ^ { 2 }$ should be
abandoned as an estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ The above argument shows that, on the average, $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$
will be closer to $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ than $S ^ { 2 }$ if MSE is used as a measure. However, $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is biased and
will, on the average, underestimate $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ This fact alone may make us uncomfortable
about using $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ as an estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Furthermore, it can be argued that MSE, while a
reasonable criterion for location parameters, is not reasonable for scale parameters, $s o$
the above comparison should not even be made. (One problem is that $\mathrm { M S E }$ penalizes
equally for overestimation and underestimation, which is fine in the location case. In
the scale case, however, 0 is a natural lower bound, so the estimation problem is not
symmetric. Use of MSE in this case tends to be forgiving of underestimation.) The
end result of this is that no absolute answer is obtained but rather more information
is gathered about the estimators in the hope that, for a particular situation, a good
estimator is chosen.

In general, since MSE is a function of the parameter, there will not be one "best"
estimator. Often, the MSEs of two estimators will cross each other, showing that
each estimator is better (with respect to the other) in only a portion of the parameter
space. However, even this partial information can sometimes provide guidelines for
choosing between estimators.

Example 7.3.5 (MSE of binomial Bayes estimator) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
Bernoulli(p). The $\mathrm { M S E } \mathrm { o f } \widehat { p } ,$ the MLE, as an estimator of $p ,$ is

$$E _ { p } \left( \widehat { p } - p \right) ^ { 2 } = \text { Var } _ { p } \bar { X } = \frac { p \left( 1 - p \right) } { n }$$

Let $Y = \sum X _ { i }$ and recall the Bayes estimator derived in Example 7.2.14, $\widehat { p } _ { \mathrm { B } } = \frac { Y + \alpha } { \alpha + \beta + n }$
The MSE of this Bayes estimator of $p$ is

$$E _ { p } \left( \widehat { p } _ { B } - p \right) ^ { 2 } = \text { Var } _ { p } \widehat { p } _ { B } + \left( \text { Bias } _ { p } \widehat { p } _ { B } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { V a r } _ { p } \left( \frac { Y + \alpha } { \alpha + \beta + n } \right) + \left( \mathrm { E } _ { p } \left( \frac { Y + \alpha } { \alpha + \beta + n } \right) - p \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { n p \left( 1 - p \right) } { \left( \alpha + \beta + n \right) ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { n p + \alpha } { \alpha + \beta + n } - p \right) ^ { 2 }$$

In the absence of good prior information about $p ,$ we might try to choose $\alpha$ and $\beta$
to make the MSE of $\widehat { p } _ { B }$ constant. The details are not too difficult to work out (see
Exercise 7.33), and the choice $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt { n / 4 }$ yields

$$\widehat { p } _ { B } = \frac { Y + \sqrt { n / 4 } } { n + \sqrt { n } }$$
$$E \left( \widehat { p } _ { B } - p \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { n } { 4 \left( n + \sqrt { n } \right) ^ { 2 } }$$
and

If we want to choose between $\widehat { p } _ { \mathrm { B } }$ and $\widehat { p }$ on the basis of MSE, Figure 7.3.1 is helpful.
For small $n ,$ $\widehat { p } _ { \mathrm { B } }$ is the better choice (unless there is a strong belief that $p$ is near $0$
or 1). For large $n ,$ $\widehat { p }$ is the better choice (unless there is a strong belief that $p$ is close
to $\frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ Even though the MSE criterion does not show one estimator to be uniformly
better than the other, useful information is provided. This information, combined

<!-- PageNumber="333" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

with the knowledge of the problem at hand, can lead to choosing the better estimator
for the situation.

|

In certain situations, particularly in location parameter estimation, MSE can be
a helpful criterion for finding the best estimator in a class of equivariant estimators
(see Section 6.4). For a fixed $g$ in the group $\mathcal{G} ,$ denote the function that takes $\theta \rightarrow \theta ^ { \prime }$
by $\bar { g } \left( \theta \right) = \theta ^ { \prime } .$ Then if $W \left( X \right)$ estimates $\theta$ we have
☒

Measurement Equivariance: $W \left( x \right)$ estimates $\theta \Rightarrow \bar { g } \left( W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right)$ estimates $\bar { g } \left( \theta \right) = \theta ^ { \prime } .$
☒ ☒

Formal Invariance: $W \left( x \right)$ estimates $\theta \Rightarrow W \left( g \left( x \right) \right)$ estimates $\bar { g } \left( \theta \right) = \theta ^ { \prime } .$
☒ ☒

Putting these two requirements together gives $W \left( g \left( x \right) \right) = \bar { g } \left( W \left( x \right) \right) .$
☒

Example 7.3.6 (MSE of equivariant estimators) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be id $f \left( x - \right.$
$\theta .$ For an estimator $W \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ to satisfy $W \left( g _ { a } \left( x \right) \right) = \bar { g } _ { a } \left( W \left( x \right) \right) ,$ we must have
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

(7.3.2)
$$W \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) + a = W \left( x _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , x _ { n } + a \right) ,$$

which specifies the equivariant estimators with respect to the group of transformations
defined by $\mathcal{G} = \left\{ g _ { a } \left( x \right) : - \infty < a < \infty \right\} ,$ where $g _ { a } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left( x _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , x _ { n } + a \right) .$
☒
For these estimators we have

$$E _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) - \theta \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X _ { 1 } + a , \ldots , X _ { n } + a \right) - a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= E _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X _ { 1 } - \theta , \ldots , X _ { n } - \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( W \left( x _ { 1 } - \theta , \ldots , x _ { n } - \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } - \theta \right) d x _ { i }$$
$$= \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \cdots \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left( W \left( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( u _ { i } \right) d u _ { i } .$$ ☒ ☒
$\left( u _ { i } = x _ { i } - \theta \right)$

$$\left( a = - \theta \right)$$

(7.3.3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="334" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

This last expression does not depend on $\theta$ hence, the MSEs of these equivariant esti-
mators are not functions of $\theta .$ The MSE can therefore be used to order the equivariant
estimators, and an equivariant estimator with smallest MSE can be found. In fact,
this estimator is the solution to the mathematical problem of finding the function $W$
that minimizes (7.3.3) subject to (7.3.2). (See Exercises 7.35 and 7.36.)
II


#### 7.3.2 Best Unbiased Estimators

As noted in the previous section, a comparison of estimators based on MSE consider-
ations may not yield a clear favorite. Indeed, there is no one "best MSE" estimator.
Many find this troublesome or annoying, and rather than doing MSE comparisons of
candidate estimators, they would rather have a "recommended" one.

The reason that there is no one "best MSE" estimator is that the class of all
estimators is too large a class. (For example, the estimator $\widehat { \theta } = 1 7$ cannot be beaten
in MSE at $\theta = 1 7$ but is a terrible estimator otherwise.) One way to make the problem
of finding a "best" estimator tractable is to limit the class of estimators. A popular
way of restricting the class of estimators, the one we consider in this section, is to
consider only unbiased estimators.

If $W _ { 1 }$ and $W _ { 2 }$ are both unbiased estimators of a parameter $\theta ,$ that is, $E _ { \theta } W _ { 1 } =$
$E _ { \theta } W _ { 2 } = \theta ,$ then their mean squared errors are equal to their variances, $s o$ we should
choose the estimator with the smaller variance. If we can find an unbiased estimator
with uniformly smallest variance-a best unbiased estimator-then our task is done.

Before proceeding we note that, although we will be dealing with unbiased esti-
mators, the results here and $\mathrm { i r }$ the next section are actually more general. Suppose
that there is an estimator $W ^ { * }$ of $\theta$ with $E _ { \theta } W ^ { * } = \tau \left( \theta \right) \neq \theta ,$ and we are interested in
investigating the worth of $W ^ { * }$ *. Consider the class of estimators

$$\mathcal{C} _ { \tau } = \left\{ W : \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) \right\} .$$

For any $W _ { 1 } ,$ $W _ { 2 } \in \mathcal{C} _ { \tau } ,$ Biaso $W _ { 1 } = \text { Bias } _ { \theta } W _ { 2 } ,$ SO

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( W _ { 1 } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } - \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( W _ { 2 } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W _ { 1 } - \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W _ { 2 } ,$$

and $M S E$ comparisons, within the class $\mathcal{C} _ { \tau } ,$ can be based on variance alone. Thus,
although we speak in terms of unbiased estimators, we really are comparing estimators
that have the same expected value, $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

The goal of this section is to investigate a method for finding a "best" unbiased
estimator, which we define in the following way.

Definition 7.3.7 An estimator $W ^ { * }$ is a best unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ if it satisfies
$E _ { \theta } W ^ { * } = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ for all $\theta$ and, for any other estimator $W$ with $E _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ we have
$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ^ { * } \leq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W$ for all $\theta .$ $W ^ { * }$ is also called a uniform minimum variance unbiased
estimator (UMVUE) of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Finding a best unbiased estimator (if one exists!) is not an easy task for a variety
of reasons, two of which are illustrated in the following example.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="335" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Example 7.3.8 (Poisson unbiased estimation)
Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
$\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ and let $\bar { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ be the sample mean and variance, respectively. Re-
☒
call that for the Poisson pmf both the mean and variance are equal to $\lambda .$ Therefore,
applying Theorem 5.2.6, we have

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \lambda } \bar { X } = \lambda , \quad \text { for all } \lambda ,$$

and

$$E _ { \lambda } S ^ { 2 } = \lambda , \quad \text { for all } \lambda ,$$

so both $\overrightarrow { X }$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are unbiased estimators

To determine the better estimator, $\bar { X }$ $\begin{array}{} \text { tors of } \lambda . \\ \bar { X } \text { or } S ^ { 2 } \end{array}$ $S ^ { 2 } ,$ we should now compare variances.
Again from Theorem 5.2.6, we have $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \lambda } \bar { X } = \lambda / n ,$ but $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \lambda } S ^ { 2 }$ is quite a lengthy
calculation (resembling that in Exercise $\left. 5 . 1 0 \left( b \right) \right) .$ This is one of the first problems in
finding a best unbiased estimator. Not only may the calculations be long and involved,
but they may be for naught (as in this case), for we will see that $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \lambda } \bar { X } \leq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \lambda } S ^ { 2 }$
for all $\lambda .$

Even if we can establish that $\bar { X }$ is better than $S ^ { 2 } ,$ consider the class of estimators
☒

$$W _ { a } \left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right) = a \bar { X } + \left( 1 - a \right) S ^ { 2 } .$$

For every constant $a , \mathrm { E } _ { \lambda } W _ { a } \left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right) = \lambda ,$ so we now have infinitely many unbiased
estimators of $\lambda .$ Even if $\bar { X }$ is better than $S ^ { 2 } ,$ is it better than every $W _ { a } \left( X , S ^ { 2 } \right)$
☒ ☒
Furthermore, how can we be sure that there are not other, better, unbiased estimators
lurking about?
11

This example shows some of the problems that might be encountered in trying to
find a best unbiased estimator, and perhaps that a more comprehensive approach is
desirable. Suppose that, for estimating a parameter $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ of a distribution $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ we
can specify a lower bound, say $B \left( \theta \right) ,$ on the variance of any unbiased estimator of
$\tau \left( \theta \right) .$ If we can then find an unbiased estimator $W ^ { * }$ satisfying Vare $W ^ { * } = B \left( \theta \right) ,$ we
☒
have found a best unbiased estimator. This is the approach taken with the use of the
Cramér-Rao Lower Bound.

Theorem 7.3.9 (Cramer-Rao Inequality) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a sample with pdf
$f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ and let $W \left( X \right) = W \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ be any estimator satisfying
☒

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \left[ W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) \right] d \mathrm { x }$$
(7.3.4)
and

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \mathrm { I } V \left( \mathrm { X } \right) < \infty .$$

Then

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) \geq \frac { \left( \frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) } .$$
(7.3.5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="336" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Proof: The proof of this theorem is elegantly simple and is a clever application of the
Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality or, stated statistically, the fact that for any two random
variables $X$ and $Y ,$

(7.3.6)
$$\left[ \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \leq \left( \mathrm { V a r } X \right) \left( \mathrm { V a r } Y \right) .$$

If we rearrange (7.3.6) we can get a lower bound on the variance of $X ,$

$$\mathrm { V a r } X \geq \frac { \left[ \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { V a r } Y } .$$

The cleverness in this theorem follows from choosing $X$ to be the estimator $W \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$
and $Y$ to be the quantity $\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right)$ and applying the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality.

First note that

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } E _ { \theta } W \left( X \right) = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } W \left( x \right) \left[ \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x | \theta \right) \right] d x$$
$$= E _ { \theta } \left[ W \left( X \right) \frac { \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( X | \theta \right) } { f \left( X | \theta \right) } \right]$$

(multiply by $f \left( X | \theta \right) / f \left( X | \theta \right)$
(7.3.7)

$$= E _ { \theta } \left[ W \left( X \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right] ,$$
(property of logs)

which suggests a covariance between $W \left( X \right)$ and $\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) .$ For it to be a co-
variance, we need to subtract the product of the expected values, so we calculate
$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) .$ But if we apply (7.3.7) with $W \left( x \right) = 1 ,$ we have

(7.3.8)
$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) = \frac { d } { d \theta } E _ { \theta } \left[ 1 \right] = 0 .$$

Therefore $\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X \right) , \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right)$ is equal to the expectation of the product, and
it follows from (7.3.7) and $\left( 7 . 3 . 8 \right)$ that

$$\left( 7 . 3 . 9 \right) \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) , \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) = \frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) .$$

Also, since $E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) = 0$ we have

(7.3.10)
$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Using the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality together with (7.3.9) and (7.3.10), we obtain

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X \right) \right) \geq \frac { \left( \frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( \mathrm { X } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) } ,$$

proving the theorem.

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="337" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

If we add the assumption of independent samples, then the calculation of the lower
bound is simplified. The expectation in the denominator becomes a univariate calcu-
lation, as the following corollary shows.

Corollary 7.3.10 (Cramér-Rao Inequality, iid case) $I f$ the assumptions of The-
orem 7.3.9 are satisfied and, additionally, if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are vid with pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ then

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \geq \frac { \left( \frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { n \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) } .$$

Proof: We only need to show that

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = n E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Since $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are independent,

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \quad \text { \left(property of logs\right) }$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) \quad \text { \left(expand the square\right) }$$
$$+ \sum _ { i \neq j } E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { j } | \theta \right) \right)$$

(7.3.11)

For $i \neq j$ we have

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { j } | \theta \right) \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { j } | \theta \right) \right) \quad \left( \mathrm { i n d e p e n d e n c e } \right)$$
$$= 0 .$$
(from (7.3.8))

Therefore the second sum in (7.3.11) is 0, and the first term is

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = n E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) , \left( \text { identic } \right.$$
distributions)

which establishes the corollary.

☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="338" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Before going on we note that although the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound is stated
for continuous random variables, it also applies to discrete random variables. The
key condition, (7.3.4), which allows interchange of integration and differentiation,
undergoes the obvious modification. If $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is a pmf, then we must be able to
interchange differentiation and summation. (Of course, this assumes that even though
$f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is a pmf and not differentiable in $x ,$ it is differentiable in $\theta .$ This is the case
for most common pmfs.)

The quantity $E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$ is called the information number, or Fisher
information of the sample. This terminology reflects the fact that the information
number gives a bound on the variance of the best unbiased estimator of $\theta .$ As the
information number gets bigger and we have more information about $\theta ,$ we have a
smaller bound on the variance of the best unbiased estimator.

In fact, the term Information Inequality is an alternative to Cramér-Rao Inequality,
and the Information Inequality exists in much more general forms than is presented
here. A key difference of the more general form is that all assumptions about the can-
didate estimators are removed and are replaced with assumptions on the underlying
density. In this form, the Information Inequality becomes very useful in comparing the
performance of estimators. See Lehmann and Casella (1998, Section 2.6) for details.

For any differentiable function $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ we now have a lower bound on the variance of
any estimator $W$ satisfying (7.3.4) and $E _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) .$ The bound depends only on $\tau \left( \theta \right)$
and $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ and is a uniform lower bound on the variance. Any candidate estimator
satisfying $E _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ and attaining this lower bound is a best unbiased estimator
of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Before looking at some examples, we present a computational result that aids in
the application of this theorem. Its proof is left to Exercise 7.39.

Lemma 7.3.11 If $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ satisfies

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) = \int \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \left[ \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( x | \theta \right) \right) f \left( x | \theta \right) \right] d x$$

(true for an exponential family), then

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = - \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) .$$

Using the tools just developed, we return to, and settle, the Poisson example.

Example 7.3.12 (Conclusion of Example 7.3.8) Here $\tau \left( \lambda \right) = \lambda ,$ So $\tau ^ { \prime } \left( \lambda \right) = 1 .$
Also, since we have an exponential family, using Lemma 7.3.11 gives us

$$E _ { \lambda } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( X _ { i } | \lambda \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = - n \mathrm { E } _ { \lambda } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } \log f \left( X | \lambda \right) \right)$$
$$= - n E _ { \lambda } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } \log \left( \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \lambda ^ { X } } { X ! } \right) \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="339" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->

$$= - n E _ { \lambda } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } \left( - \lambda + X \log \lambda - \log X ! \right) \right)$$
$$= - n E _ { \lambda } \left( - \frac { X } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } \right)$$
$$= \frac { n } { \lambda } .$$

Hence for any unbiased estimator, $W ,$ of $\lambda ,$ we must have

$$V a r _ { \lambda } W \geq \frac { \lambda } { n } .$$

Since $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \lambda } \bar { X } = \lambda / n ,$ $\bar { X }$ is a best unbiased estimator of $\lambda .$
1

It is important to remember that a key assumption in the Cramer-Rao Theorem
is the ability to differentiate under the integral sign, which, of course, is somewhat
restrictive. As we have seen, densities in the exponential class will satisfy the assump-
tions but, in general, such assumptions need to be checked, or contradictions such as
the following will arise.

Example 7.3.13 (Unbiased estimator for the scale uniform) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots .$ ,
$X _ { n }$ be id with pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right) = 1 / \theta , 0 < x < \theta .$ Since $\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( x | \theta \right) = - 1 / \theta ,$ we have

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \theta ^ { 2 } }$$

The Cramer-Rao Theorem would seem to indicate that if $W$ is any unbiased estimator
of $\theta ,$

$$V a r _ { \theta } W \geq \frac { \theta ^ { 2 } } { n } .$$

We would now like to find an unbiased estimator with small variance. As a first guess,
consider the sufficient statistic $Y = \max \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) ,$ the largest order statistic. The
☒
pdf of $Y$ is $f _ { Y } \left( y | \theta \right) = n y ^ { n - 1 } / \theta ^ { n } ,$ $0 < y < \theta ,$ so

$$E _ { \theta } Y = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } \frac { n y ^ { n } } { \theta ^ { n } } d y = \frac { n } { n + 1 } \theta$$

showing that $\frac { n + 1 } { n } Y$ is an unbiased estimator of $\theta .$ We next calculate

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { n + 1 } { n } Y \right) = \left( \frac { n + 1 } { n } \right) ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } Y$$
$$= \left( \frac { n + 1 } { n } \right) ^ { 2 } \left[ \text { E } _ { \theta } Y ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { n } { n + 1 } \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right]$$
$$= \left( \frac { n + 1 } { n } \right) ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { n } { n + 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } - \left( \frac { n } { n + 1 } \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n \left( n + 2 \right) } \theta ^ { 2 }$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="340" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

which is uniformly smaller than $\theta ^ { 2 } / n .$ This indicates that the Cramer-Rao Theorem
is not applicable to this pdf. To see that this is so, we can use Leibnitz's Rule (Section
2.4) to calculate

$$\frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } h \left( x \right) f \left( x | \theta \right) d x = \frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } h \left( x \right) \frac { 1 } { \theta } d x$$
$$= \frac { h \left( \theta \right) } { \theta } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } h \left( x \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \left( \frac { 1 } { \theta } \right) d x$$
$$\neq \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } h \left( x \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } f \left( x | \theta \right) d x ,$$

unless $h \left( \theta \right) / \theta = 0$ for all $\theta .$ Hence, the Cramér-Rao Theorem does not apply. In
general, if the range of the pdf depends on the parameter, the theorem will not be
applicable.

A shortcoming of this approach to finding best unbiased estimators is that, even if
the Cramer-Rao Theorem is applicable, there is no guarantee that the bound is sharp.
That is to say, the value of the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound may be strictly smaller than
the variance of any unbiased estimator. In fact, in the usually favorable case of $f \left( x | \theta \right)$
being a one-parameter exponential family, the most that we can say is that there
exists a parameter $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ with an unbiased estimator that achieves the Cramér-Rao
Lower Bound. However, in other typical situations, for other parameters, the bound
may not be attainable. These situations cause concern because, if we cannot find an
estimator that attains the lower bound, we have to decide whether no estimator can
attain it or whether we must look at more estimators.

Example 7.3.14 (Normal variance bound) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
☒
and consider estimation of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\mu$ is unknown. The normal pdf satisfies the
assumptions of the Cramer-Rao Theorem and Lemma 7.3.11, so we have

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \log \left( \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } - \frac { \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 6 } }$$

and

$$- \mathrm { E } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \log f \left( X | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = - \mathrm { E } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } - \frac { \left( X - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 6 } } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } }$$

Thus, any unbiased estimator, $W ,$ of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ must satisfy

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( W | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \geq \frac { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n } .$$

$I I$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="341" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

In Example 7.3.3 we saw

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( S ^ { 2 } | \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n - 1 } ,$$

so $S ^ { 2 }$ does not attain the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound.
11

In the above example we are left with an incomplete answer; that is, is there a better
unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ than $S ^ { 2 } ,$ or is the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound unattainable?

The conditions for attainment of the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound are actually quite
simple. Recall that the bound follows from an application of the Cauchy-Schwarz
Inequality, so conditions for attainment of the bound are the conditions for equality
in the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality (see Section 4.7). Note also that Corollary 7.3.15 is
a useful tool because it implicitly gives us a way of finding a best unbiased estimator.

Corollary 7.3.15 (Attainment) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ sat-
isfies the conditions of the Cramer-Rao Theorem. Let $L \left( \theta | x \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right)$ denote
the likelihood function. $I f W \left( X \right) = W \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is any unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$
☒
then $W \left( X \right)$ attains the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound $i f$ and only if
☒

(7.3.12)
$$a \left( \theta \right) \left[ W \left( x \right) - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right] = \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log L \left( \theta | x \right)$$

for some function $a \left( \theta \right) .$

Proof: The Cramér-Rao Inequality, as given in (7.3.6), can be written as

$$\left[ \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) , \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \leq \dot { \mathrm { V } } \mathrm { a r } _ { \theta } W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) ,$$

and, recalling that $E _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ $E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( X _ { i } | \theta \right) \right) = 0 ,$ and using the results
☒
of Theorem 4.5.7, we can have equality if and only if $W \left( x \right) - \tau \left( \theta \right)$ is proportional to
☒

$\frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right) .$ That is exactly what is expressed in (7.3.12).
☐

Example 7.3.16 (Continuation of Example 7.3.14) Here we have

$$L \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } | x \right) = \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { n / 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) \Sigma _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$

and hence

$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \sigma ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } | x \right) = \frac { n } { 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } } { n } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$

Thus, taking $a \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = n / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } \right)$ shows that the best unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is
$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / n ,$ which is calculable only if $\mu$ is known. If $\mu$ is unknown, the bound
cannot be attained.

1/

<!-- PageNumber="342" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

The theory developed in this section still leaves some questions unanswered. First,
what can we do if $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ does not satisfy the assumptions of the Cramér-Rao Theo-
rem? (In Example 7.3.13, we still do not know if $\frac { n + 1 } { n } Y$ is a best unbiased estimator.)
Second, what if the bound is unattainable by allowable estimators, as in Example
7.3.14? There, we still do not know if $S ^ { 2 }$ is a best unbiased estimator.

One way of answering these questions is to search for methods that are more widely
applicable and yield sharper (that is, greater) lower bounds. Much research has been
done on this topic, with perhaps the most well-known bound being that of Chapman
and Robbins (1951). Stuart, Ord, and Arnold (1999, Chapter 17) have a good treat-
ment of this subject. Rather than take this approach, however, we will continue the
study of best unbiased estimators from another view, using the concept of sufficiency.


#### 7.3.3 Sufficiency and Unbiasedness

In the previous section, the concept of sufficiency was not used in our search for
unbiased estimates. We will now see that consideration of sufficiency is a powerful
tool, indeed.

The main theorem of this section, which relates sufficient statistics to unbiased
estimates, is, as in the case of the Cramér-Rao Theorem, another clever application
of some well-known theorems. Recall from Chapter 4 that if $X$ and $Y$ are any two
random variables, then, provided the expectations exist, we have

(7.3.13)
$$E X = E \left[ E \left( X | Y \right) \right] ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } X = \mathrm { V a r } \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( X | Y \right) \right] + \mathrm { E } \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( X | Y \right) \right] .$$

Using these tools we can prove the following theorem.

Theorem 7.3.17 (Rao-Blackwell) Let $W$ be any unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ and
let $T$ be a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ Define $\phi \left( T \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( W | T \right) .$ Then $E _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ and
$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) \leq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W$ for all $\theta$ that $i s ,$ $\phi \left( T \right)$ is a uniformly better unbiased estimator
of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Proof: From (7.3.13) we have

$$\tau \left( \theta \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( W | T \right) \right] = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) ,$$

so $\phi \left( T \right)$ is unbiased for $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$ Also,

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( W | T \right) \right] + \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( W | T \right) \right]$$
$$= \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) + \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left[ \mathrm { V a r } \left( W | T \right) \right]$$
$$\geq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) .$$

$$\left( \mathrm { V a r } \left( W | T \right) \geq 0 \right)$$

Hence $\phi \left( T \right)$ is uniformly better than $W ,$ and it only remains to show that $\phi \left( T \right)$ is
indeed an estimator. That is, we must show that $\phi \left( T \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( W | T \right)$ is a function of only

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="343" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

the sample and, in particular, is independent of $\theta .$ But it follows from the definition of
sufficiency, and the fact that $W$ is a function only of the sample, that the distribution
$o f \quad W | T$ is independent of $\theta .$ Hence $\phi \left( T \right)$ is a uniformly better unbiased estimator of
☒

☐
$\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Therefore, conditioning any unbiased estimator on a sufficient statistic will result
in a uniform improvement, so we need consider only statistics that are functions of a
sufficient statistic in our search for best unbiased estimators.

The identities in (7.3.13) make no mention of sufficiency, so it might at first seem
that conditioning on anything will result in an improvement. This $\mathrm { i s } ,$ in effect, true,
but the problem is that the resulting quantity will probably depend on $\theta$ and not be
an estimator.

Example 7.3.18 (Conditioning on an insufficient statistic) Let $X _ { 1 } ,$ $X _ { 2 }$ be
$n \left( \theta , 1 \right) .$ The statistic $\bar { X } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( X _ { 1 } + X _ { 2 } \right)$ has
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

$$E _ { \theta } = \theta \text { and } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \bar { X } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$$ ☒ ☒

Consider conditioning on $X _ { 1 } ,$ which is not sufficient. Let $\phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \bar { X } | X _ { 1 } \right) .$ It follows
☒ ☒ ☒

☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
from (7.3.13) that $E _ { \theta } \phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right) = \theta$ and $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right) \leq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \bar { X } ,$ so $\phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right)$ is better than
$\overrightarrow { X } .$ However,
☒

$$\phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right) = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( \bar { X } | X _ { 1 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } E _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 1 } | X _ { 1 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } E _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 2 } | X _ { 1 } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } X _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \theta ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

since $E _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 2 } | X _ { 1 } \right) = E _ { \theta } X _ { 2 }$ by independence. Hence, $\phi \left( X _ { 1 } \right)$ is not an estimator.
☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

We now know that, in looking for a best unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ we need
consider only estimators based on a sufficient statistic. The question now arises that
if we have $E _ { \theta } \phi = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ and $\phi$ is based on a sufficient statistic, that is, $E \left( \phi | T \right) = \phi ,$ how
do we know that $\phi$ is best unbiased? Of course, if $\phi$ attains the Cramer-Rao Lower
Bound, then it is best unbiased, but if it does not, have we gained anything? For
example, if $\phi ^ { * }$ is another unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ how does $\mathrm { E } \left( \phi ^ { * } | T \right)$ compare to
$\phi ^ { \prime }$ The next theorem answers this question in part by showing that a best unbiased
estimator is unique.

Theorem 7.3.19 If $W$ is a best unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ then $W$ is unique.

Proof: Suppose $W ^ { \prime }$ is another best unbiased estimator, and consider the estimator
$W ^ { * } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( W + W ^ { \prime } \right) .$ Note that $E _ { \theta } W ^ { * } = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ and
☒

11

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="344" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ^ { * } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } W + \frac { 1 } { 2 } W ^ { \prime } \right)$$

$$= \frac { 1 } { 4 } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , W ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$\leq \frac { 1 } { 4 } \text { Var } _ { \theta } W + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \text { Var } _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \text { Var } _ { \theta } W \right) \left( \text { Var } _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } \right) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 }$$
$$= \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W .$$
(7.3.14)
(Cauchy-Schwarz)
$$\left( V a r _ { \theta } W = V a r _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } \right)$$

(Exercise 4.44)

But if the above inequality is strict, then the best unbiasedness of $W$ is contradicted,
so we must have cquality for all $\theta .$ Since the inequality is an application of Cauchy-
Schwarz, we can have equality only if $W ^ { \prime } = a \left( \theta \right) W + b \left( \theta \right) .$ Now using properties of
covariance, we have

$$\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , W ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left[ W , a \left( \theta \right) W + b \left( \theta \right) \right]$$
$$= \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left[ W , a \left( \theta \right) W \right]$$
$$= a \left( \theta \right) \text { Var } _ { \theta } W ,$$

but $\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , W ^ { \prime } \right) = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W$ since we had equality in (7.3.14). Hence $a \left( \theta \right) = 1$ and,
since $E _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } = \tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ we must have $b \left( \theta \right) = 0$ and $W = W ^ { \prime } ,$ showing that $W$ is unique.
☐

To see when an unbiased estimator is best unbiased, we might ask how could we
improve upon a given unbiased estimator? Suppose that $W$ satisfies $E _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) ,$
and we have another estimator, $U ,$ that satisfies $E _ { \theta } U = 0$ for all $\theta ,$ that is, $U$ is an
unbiased estimator of 0. The estimator

$$\phi _ { a } = W + a U ,$$

where a is a constant, satisfies $E _ { \theta } \phi _ { a } = \tau \left( \theta \right)$ and hence is also an unbiased estimator
of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$ Can $\phi _ { a }$ be better than $W ?$ The variance of $\phi _ { a }$ is

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \phi _ { a } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W + a U \right) = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + 2 a \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , U \right) + a ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } U .$$

Now, if for some $\theta = \theta _ { 0 } ,$ $\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( W , U \right) < 0 ,$ then we can make $2 a \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( W , U \right) +$
$a ^ { 2 } \text { Var } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } U < 0$ by choosing $a \in \left( 0 , - 2 \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( W , U \right) / \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } U \right) .$ Hence, $\phi _ { a }$ will be better
than $W$ at $\theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ and $W$ cannot be best unbiased. A similar argument will show that
if $\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( W , U \right) > 0$ for any $\theta _ { 0 } ,$ $W$ also cannot be best unbiased. (See Exercise 7.53.)
Thus, the relationship of $W$ with unbiased estimators of 0 is crucial $\mathrm { i n }$ evaluating
whether $W$ is best unbiased. This relationship, in fact, characterizes best unbiased-
ness.

Theorem 7.3.20 If $\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } W = \tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ $W$ is the best unbiased estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ if and
only if $W$ is uncorrelated with all unbiased estimators of $0 .$

Proof: If $W$ is best unbiased, the above argument shows that $W$ must satisfy
$\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , U \right) = 0$ for all $\theta ,$ for any $U$ satisfying $E _ { \theta } U = 0 .$ Hence the necessity is
established.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="345" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Suppose now that we have an unbiased estimator $W$ that is uncorrelated with all
unbiased estimators of 0. Let $W ^ { \prime }$ be any other estimator satisfying $E _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } = E _ { \theta } W =$
$r \left( \theta \right) .$ We will show that $W$ is better than $W ^ { \prime } .$ Write

$$W ^ { \prime } = W + \left( W ^ { \prime } - W \right) ,$$

and calculate

(7.3.15)
$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W ^ { \prime } - W \right) + 2 \mathrm { C } \mathrm { o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , W ^ { \prime } - W \right)$$
$$= \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W + \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W ^ { \prime } - W \right) ,$$

where the last equality is true because $W ^ { \prime } - W$ is an unbiased estimator of 0 and
is uncorrelated with $W$ by assumption. Since $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \left( W ^ { \prime } - W \right) \geq 0 ,$ (7.3.15) implies
that $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W ^ { \prime } \geq \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W .$ Since $W ^ { \prime }$ is arbitrary, it follows that $W$ is the best unbiased
estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$
☐

Note that an unbiased estimator of 0 is nothing more than random noise; that $\mathrm { i s } ,$
there is no information in an estimator of 0. (It makes sense that the most sensible
way to estimate 0 is with 0, not with random noise.) Therefore, if an estimator
could be improved by adding random noise to $\mathrm { i t } ,$ the estimator probably is defective.
(Alternatively, we could question the criterion used to evaluate the estimator, but in
this case the criterion seems above suspicion.) This intuition is what is formalized in
Theorem 7.3.20.

Although we now have an interesting characterization of best unbiased estimators,
its usefulness is limited in application. It is often a difficult task to verify that an
estimator is uncorrelated with all unbiased estimators of 0 because it is usually difficult
to describe all unbiased estimators of 0. However, it is sometimes useful in determining
that an estimator is not best unbiased.

Example 7.3.21 (Unbiased estimators of zero) Let $X$ be an observation from
$a \text { uniform } \left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right)$ distribution. Then

$$E _ { \theta } X = \int _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } x d x = \theta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$$

and so $X - \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ is an unbiased estimator of $\theta ,$ and it is easy to check that $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } X = \frac { 1 } { 1 2 } .$

For this $p d f ,$ unbiased estimators of zero are periodic functions with period 1. This
follows from the fact that if $h \left( x \right)$ satisfies

$$\int _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } h \left( x \right) d x = 0 , \quad \text { for all } \theta ,$$

then

$$0 = \frac { d } { d \theta } \int _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } h \left( x \right) d x = h \left( \theta + 1 \right) - h \left( \theta \right) , \quad \text { for all } \theta . ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="346" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Such a function is $h \left( x \right) = \sin \left( 2 \pi x \right) .$ Now

$$\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( X - \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \sin \left( 2 \pi X \right) \right) = \mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( X , \sin \left( 2 \pi X \right) \right)$$
$$= \int _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } x \sin \left( 2 \pi x \right) d x$$
$$= - \frac { x \cos \left( 2 \pi x \right) } { 2 \pi } | _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } + \int _ { \theta } ^ { \theta + 1 } \frac { \cos \left( 2 \pi x \right) } { 2 \pi } d x$$
$$= - \frac { \cos \left( 2 \pi \theta \right) } { 2 \pi }$$
(integration by parts)

where we used $\cos \left( 2 \pi \left( \theta + 1 \right) \right) = \cos \left( 2 \pi \theta \right)$ and $\sin \left( 2 \pi \left( \theta + 1 \right) \right) = \sin \left( 2 \pi \theta \right) .$

Hence $X - \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ is correlated with an unbiased estimator of zero, and cannot be a
best unbiased estimator of $\theta .$ In fact, it is straightforward to check that the estimator
$X - \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \sin \left( 2 \pi X \right) / \left( 2 \pi \right)$ is unbiased for $\theta$ and has variance less than $\frac { 1 } { 1 2 }$ for some $\theta$
values.

To answer the question about existence of a best unbiased estimator, what is needed
is some characterization of all unbiased estimators of zero. Given such a character-
ization, we could then see if our candidate for best unbiased estimator $\mathrm { i s } ,$ in fact,
optimal.

Characterizing the unbiased estimators of zero is not an easy task and requires
conditions on the pdf (or pmf) with which we are working. Note that, thus far in
this section, we have not specified conditions on pdfs (as were needed, for example,
in the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound). The price we have paid for this generality is the
difficulty in verifying the existence of the best unbiased estimator.

If a family of pdfs or pmfs $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ has the property that there are no unbiased
estimators of zero (other than zero itself), then our search would be ended, since any
statistic $W$ satisfies $\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( W , 0 \right) = 0 .$ Recall that the property of completeness, defined
in Definition 6.1.4, guarantees such a situation.

Example 7.3.22 (Continuation of Example 7.3.13) For $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ iid uni-
$\mathrm { f o r m } \left( 0 , \theta \right) ,$ we saw that $\frac { n + 1 } { n } Y$ is an unbiased estimator of $\theta ,$ where $Y = \max \left\{ X _ { 1 } , \ldots , \right. ,$
$\left. X _ { n } \right\} .$ The conditions of the Cramer-Rao Theorem are not satisfied, and we have not
yet established whether this estimator is best unbiased. In Example 6.2.23, however,
it was shown that $Y$ is a complete sufficient statistic. This means that the family of
pdfs of $Y$ is complete, and there are no unbiased estimators of zero that are based
on $Y .$ (By sufficiency, in the form of the Rao-Blackwell Theorem, we need consider
only unbiased estimators of zero based on $\left. Y . \right)$ Therefore, $\frac { n + 1 } { n } Y$ is uncorrelated with
all unbiased estimators of zero (since the only one is zero itself) and thus $\frac { n + 1 } { n } Y$ is
the best unbiased estimator of $\theta .$
1

It is worthwhile to note once again that what is important is the completeness of the
family of distributions of the sufficient statistic. Completeness of the original family
is of no consequence. This follows from the Rao-Blackwell Theorem, which says that
we can restrict attention to functions of a sufficient statistic, so all expectations wil
be taken with respect to its distribution.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="347" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

We sum up the relationship between completeness and best unbiasedness in the
following theorem.

Theorem 7.3.23 Let $T$ be a complete sufficient statistic for $a$ parameter $\theta ,$ and
let $\phi \left( T \right)$ be any estimator based only on $T .$ Then $\phi \left( T \right)$ is the unique best unbiased
estimator of its expected value.

We close this section with an interesting and useful application of the theory de-
veloped here. In many situations, there will be no obvious candidate for an unbiased
estimator of a function $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ much less a candidate for best unbiased estimator. How-
ever, in the presence of completeness, the theory of this section tells us that if we
can find any unbiased estimator, we can find the best unbiased estimator. If $T$ is
a complete sufficient statistic for a parameter $\theta$ and $h \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is any unbiased
estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ then $\phi \left( T \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( h \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) | T \right)$ is the best unbiased estimator of
$\tau \left( \theta \right)$ (see Exercise 7.56).

Example 7.3.24 (Binomial best unbiased estimation) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
binomial(k, $\left( k , \theta \right) .$ The problem is to estimate the probability of exactly one success from
a binomial(k, $\left( k , \theta \right) ,$ that is, estimate

$$\tau \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = 1 \right) = k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 } .$$

Now $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \sim \text { binomial } \left( k n , \theta \right)$ is a complete sufficient statistic, but no unbiased
estimator based on it is immediately evident. When in this situation, try for the
simplest solution. The simple-minded estimator

$$h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \text { if } X _ { 1 } = 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise } } \end{array} \right.$$

satisfies

$$E _ { \theta } h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) = \sum _ { x _ { 1 } = 0 } ^ { k } h \left( x _ { 1 } \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { x _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { x _ { 1 } } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - x _ { 1 } }$$
$$= k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 }$$

and hence is an unbiased estimator of $k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 } .$ Our theory now tells us that the
estimator

$$\phi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \right) = E \left( h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \right)$$

is the best unbiased estimator of $k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 } .$ (Notice that we do not need to actually
calculate the expectation of $\phi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \right)$ we $k n o w$ that it has the correct expected
value by properties of iterated expectations.) We must, however, be able to evaluate
$\phi .$ Suppose that we observe $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t .$ Then

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="348" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->

$$\phi \left( t \right) = E \left( h \left( X _ { 1 } \right) | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right)$$
$$= P \left( X _ { 1 } = 1 | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right)$$
$$= \frac { P _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 1 } = 1 , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right) } { P _ { \theta } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right) }$$
$$= \frac { P _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 1 } = 1 , \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t - 1 \right) } { P _ { \theta } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right) }$$
$$= \frac { P _ { \theta } \left( X _ { 1 } = 1 \right) P _ { \theta } \left( \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t - 1 \right) } { P _ { \theta } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } = t \right) }$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { the expectation does } } \\ { \text { not depend on } \theta } \end{array} \right)$

$$\left( h \quad i s \quad 0 \quad o r \quad 1 \right)$$

(.
definition of
conditional probability
)

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { X _ { 1 } = 1 \text { is } } \\ { \text { redundant } } \end{array} \right)$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} X _ { 1 } \text { is independent } \\ \mathrm { o f } X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \end{array} \right)$
)

Now $X _ { 1 } \sim \text { binomial } \left( k , \theta \right) ,$ $\sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \sim \text { binomial } \left( k \left( n - 1 \right) , \theta \right) ,$ and $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$
binomial(kn, $\left( k n , \theta \right) .$ Using these facts we have

$$\phi \left( t \right) = \frac { \left. \left[ k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 } \right] \left[ \binom { k \left( n - 1 \right) } { t - 1 } \right) \theta ^ { t - 1 } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k \left( n - 1 \right) - \left( t - 1 \right) } \right] } { \binom { k n } { t } \theta ^ { t } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k n - t } }$$
$$= k \frac { \left( k \left( n - 1 \right) \right. } { \left( k n \right) }$$

Note that all of the $\theta s$ cancel, as they must since $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$ is sufficient. Hence, the
best unbiased estimator of $k \theta \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { k - 1 }$ is

$$\phi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \right) = k \frac { \left( \begin{array}{} k \left( n - 1 \right) \\ \Sigma X _ { i } - 1 \end{array} \right) } { \left( \begin{array}{} { k n } \\ { \Sigma X _ { i } } \end{array} \right) }$$

We can assert unbiasedness without performing the difficult evaluation of
$E _ { \theta } \left[ \phi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } \right) \right] .$
11


#### 7.3.4 Loss Function Optimality

Our evaluations of point estimators have been based on their mean squared error
performance. Mean squared error is a special case of a function called a loss function.
The study of the performance, and the optimality, of estimators evaluated through
loss functions is a branch of decision theory.

After the data $X = x$ are observed, where $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ $\theta \in \Theta ,$ a decision regarding
$\theta$ is made. The set of allowable decisions is the action space, denoted by $\mathcal{A} .$ Often in
point estimation problems $A$ is equal to $\Theta ,$ the parameter space, but this will change
in other problems (such as hypothesis testing-see Section 8.3.5).

The loss function in a point estimation problem reflects the fact that if an action
$a \text { is close to } \theta ,$ then the decision $a$ is reasonable and little loss is incurred. If $a$ is far

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="349" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

from $0 ,$ then a large loss is incurred. The loss function is a nonnegative function that
generally increases as the distance between a and $\theta$ increases. If $\theta$ is real-valued, two
commonly used loss functions are

$$L \left( \theta , a \right) = | a - \theta | ,$$

and

squared error loss, $L \left( \theta , a \right) = \left( a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } .$

Both of these loss functions increase as the distance between $\theta$ and $a$ increases, with
minimum value $L \left( \theta , \theta \right) = 0 .$ That is, the loss is minimum if the action is correct.
Squared error loss gives relatively more penalty for large discrepancies, and absolute
error loss gives relatively more penalty for small discrepancies. A variation of squared
error loss, one that penalizes overestimation more than underestimation, is

$$L \left( \theta , a \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \left( a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } & { \text { if } a < \theta } \\ { 1 0 \left( a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } & { \text { if } a \geq \theta } \end{array} \right.$$

A loss that penalizes errors in estimation more if $\theta$ is near $0$ than if $| \theta |$ is large, a
relative squared error loss, is

$$L \left( \theta , a \right) = \frac { \left( a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } { | \theta | + 1 }$$

Notice that both of these last variations of squared error loss could have been based
instead on absolute error loss. In general, the experimenter must consider the con-
sequences of various errors in estimation for different values of $\theta$ and specify a loss
function that reflects these consequences.

In a loss function or decision theoretic analysis, the quality of an estimator is quan-
tified in its $r$ risk function; that is, for an estimator $\delta \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ of $\theta ,$ the $r i s k$ function, a
☒
function of $\theta ,$ is

(7.3.16)
$$R \left( \theta , \delta \right) = E _ { \theta } L \left( \theta , \delta \left( X \right) \right) .$$

$A t \quad a \quad g i v e n \quad 0 ,$ the risk function is the average loss that will be incurred if the estimator
$\delta \left( x \right)$ is used.
☒

Since the true value of $\theta$ is unknown, we would like to use an estimator that has
a small value of $R \left( \theta , \delta \right)$ for all values of $\theta .$ This would mean that, regardless of the
true value of $\theta ,$ the estimator will have a small expected loss. If the qualities of two
different estimators, $\delta _ { 1 }$ and $\delta _ { 2 } ,$ are to be compared, then they will be compared by
comparing their risk functions, $R \left( \theta , \delta _ { 1 } \right)$ and $R \left( \theta , \delta _ { 2 } \right) .$ If $R \left( \theta , \delta _ { 1 } \right) < R \left( \theta , \delta _ { 2 } \right)$ for all
$\theta \in \Theta ,$ then $\delta _ { 1 }$ is the preferred estimator because $\delta _ { 1 }$ performs better for all $\theta .$ More
typically, the two risk functions will cross. Then the judgment as to which estimator
is better may not be so clear-cut.

The risk function for an estimator $\delta$ is the expected loss, as defined in (7.3.16). For
squared error loss, the risk function is a familiar quantity, the mean squared error
(MSE) that was used in Section 7.3.1. There the MSE of an estimator was defined as

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="350" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

$M S E \left( \theta \right) = E _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( X \right) - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ which is just $E _ { \theta } L \left( \theta , \delta \left( X \right) \right) = R \left( \theta , \delta \right)$ if $L \left( \theta , a \right) = \left( a - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } .$
As in Chapter 7 we have that, for squared error loss,

$$R \left( \theta , \delta \right) = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) + \left( \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) + \left( \mathrm { B i a s } _ { \theta } \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
(7.3.17)

This risk function for squared error loss clearly indicates that a good estimator should
have both a small variance and a small bias. A decision theoretic analysis would judge
how well an estimator succeeded in simultaneously minimizing these two quantities.

It would be an atypical decision theoretic analysis in which the set $\mathcal{D}$ of allowable
estimators was restricted to the set of unbiased estimators, as was done in Section
7.3.2. Then, minimizing the risk would just be minimizing the variance. A decision
theoretic analysis would be more comprehensive in that both the variance and bias
are in the risk and will be considered simultaneously. An estimator would be judged
good if it had a small, but probably nonzero, bias combined with a small variance.

Example 7.3.25 (Binomial risk functions) In Example 7.3.5 we considered
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ a random sample from a Bernoulli(p) population. We considered two
estimators,

$$\widehat { p } _ { \mathrm { B } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } + \sqrt { n / 4 } } { n + \sqrt { n } }$$
$$\bar { X } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$$
and

The risk functions for these two estimators, for $n = 4$ and $n = 4 0 0 ,$ were graphed in
Figure 7.3.1, and the comparisons of these risk functions are as stated in Example
7.3.5. On the basis of risk comparison, the estimator $\widehat { p } _ { \mathrm { B } }$ would be preferred for small
$n$ and the estimator $\bar { X }$ would be preferred for large n.
$I l$

Example 7.3.26 (Risk of normal variance) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample
from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider estimating $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ using squared error loss. We will
consider estimators of the form $\delta _ { b } \left( X \right) = b S ^ { 2 } ,$ where $S ^ { 2 }$ is the sample variance and $b$
☒
can be any nonnegative constant. Recall that $E S ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 }$ and, for a normal sample,
$\mathrm { V a r } S ^ { 2 } = 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } / \left( n - 1 \right) .$ Using (7.3.17), we can compute the risk function for $\delta _ { b }$ as

$$R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \delta _ { b } \right) = \text { Var } b S ^ { 2 } + \left( \text { Eb } S ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= b ^ { 2 } \text { Var } S ^ { 2 } + \left( b E S ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { b ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma ^ { 4 } } { n - 1 } + \left( b - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 4 }$$
$$= \left[ \frac { 2 b ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } + \left( b - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \sigma ^ { 4 } .$$

(using Var S2)

The risk function for $\delta _ { b }$ does not depend on $\mu$ and is a quadratic function of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ This
quadratic function is of the form $c _ { b } \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ where $c _ { b }$ is a positive constant. To compare
two risk functions, and hence the worth of two estimators, note that if $c _ { b } < c _ { b ^ { \prime } } ,$ then

$$R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \delta _ { b } \right) = c _ { b } \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } < c _ { b ^ { \prime } } \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } = R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \delta _ { b ^ { \prime } } \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="351" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
