for all values of $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ Thus $\delta _ { b }$ would be a better estimator than $\delta _ { b ^ { \prime } } .$ The value of $b$
that gives the overall minimum value of

(7.3.18)
$$c _ { b } = \frac { 2 b ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } + \left( b - 1 \right) ^ { 2 }$$

yields the best estimator $\delta _ { b }$ in this class. Standard calculus methods show that $b =$
$\left( n - 1 \right) / \left( n + 1 \right)$ is the minimizing value. Thus, at every value of $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ the estimator

$$\widetilde { S } ^ { 2 } = \frac { n - 1 } { n + 1 } S ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n + 1 } \sum \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 }$$

has the smallest risk among all estimators of the form $b S ^ { 2 } .$ For $n = 5 ,$ the risk
functions for this estimator and two other estimators in this class are shown in Figure
7.3.2. The other estimators are $S ^ { 2 } ,$ the unbiased estimator, and $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { n - 1 } { n } S ^ { 2 } ,$ the
MLE of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ It is clear that the risk function for $\widetilde { S } ^ { 2 }$ is smallest everywhere.
II

Example 7.3.27 (Variance estimation using Stein's loss) Again we consider
estimating a population variance $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ with an estimator of the form $b S ^ { 2 } .$ In this analysis
we can be quite general and assume only that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a random sample from
some population with positive, finite variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Now we will use the loss function

$$L \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } , a \right) = \frac { a } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } - 1 - \log \frac { a } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } ,$$

attributed to Stein (James and Stein 1961; see also Brown 1990a). This loss is more
complicated than squared error loss but it has some reasonable properties. Note that
if $a = \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ the loss is 0. Also, for any fixed value of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $L \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } , a \right) \rightarrow \infty$ as $a \rightarrow$
0 or $a \rightarrow \infty .$ That is, gross underestimation is penalized just as heavily as gross
overestimation. (A criticism of squared error loss in a variance estimation problem is
that underestimation has only a finite penalty, while overestimation has an infinite
penalty.) The loss function also arises out of the likelihood function for $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ if this is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="352" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

a sample from a normal population, and thus ties together good decision theoretic
properties with good likelihood properties (see Exercise 7.61).

For the estimator $\delta _ { b } = b S ^ { 2 } ,$ the risk function is

$$R \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } , \delta _ { b } \right) = E \left( \frac { b S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } - 1 - \log \frac { b S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right)$$
$$= b E \frac { S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } - 1 - E \log \frac { b S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$
$$= b - \log b - 1 - \mathrm { E l o g } \frac { S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } .$$
$$\left( E \frac { S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } = 1 \right)$$

The quantity $E \log \left( S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ may be a function of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ and other population parameters
but it is not a function of $b$ b. Thus $R \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } , \delta _ { b } \right)$ is minimized in $b ,$ for all $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ by the value
$\mathrm { o f } b$ that minimizes $b - \log b ,$ that is, $b =$ 1. Therefore the estimator of the form $b S ^ { 2 }$
that has the smallest risk for all values of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is $\delta _ { 1 } = S ^ { 2 } .$
1

We can also use a Bayesian approach to the problem of loss function optimality,
where we would have a prior distribution, $\pi \left( \theta \right) .$ In a Bayesian analysis we would use
this prior distribution to compute an average risk

$$\int _ { \Theta } R \left( \theta , \delta \right) \pi \left( \theta \right) d \theta ,$$

known as the Bayes risk. Averaging the risk function gives us one number for assessing
the performance of an estimator with respect to a given loss function. Moreover, we
can attempt to find the estimator that yields the smallest value of the Bayes risk. Such
an estimator is called the Bayes rule with respect to a prior $\pi$ and is often denoted
$\delta ^ { \pi } .$

Finding the Bayes decision rule for a given prior $\pi$ may look like a daunting task,
but it turns out to be rather mechanical, as the following indicates. (The technique
of finding Bayes rules by the method given below works in greater generality than
presented here; see Brown and Purves 1973.)

For $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right)$ and $\theta \sim \pi ,$ the Bayes risk of a decision rule $\delta$ can be written as

$$\int _ { \Theta } R \left( \theta , \delta \right) \pi \left( \theta \right) d \theta = \int _ { \Theta } \left( \int _ { \mathcal{X} } L \left( \theta , \delta \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) d \mathrm { x } \right) \pi \left( \theta \right) d \theta .$$

Now if we write $f \left( x | \theta \right) \pi \left( \theta \right) = \pi \left( \theta | x \right) m \left( x \right) ,$ where $\pi \left( \theta | x \right)$ is the posterior distribution
☒
of $\theta$ and $m \left( x \right)$ is the marginal distribution of $\mathrm { X } ,$ we can write the Bayes risk as
☒ ☒

(7.3.19)
$$\int _ { \Theta } R \left( \theta , \delta \right) \pi \left( \theta \right) d \theta = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } \left[ \int _ { \Theta } L \left( \theta , \delta \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) \pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) d \theta \right] m \left( \mathrm { x } \right) d \mathrm { x } .$$

The quantity in square brackets is the expected value of the loss function with respect
to the posterior distribution, called the posterior expected loss. It is a function only of
$\mathrm { x } ,$ and not a function of $\theta .$ Thus, for each $\mathrm { x } ,$ if we choose the action $\delta \left( x \right)$ to minimize
☒
the posterior expected loss, we will minimize the Bayes risk.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="353" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.3" -->

Notice that we now have a recipe for constructing a Bayes rule. For a given obser-
vation $\mathrm { X } ,$ the Bayes rule should minimize the posterior expected loss. This is quite
unlike any prescription we have had in previous sections. For example, consider the
methods of finding best unbiased estimators discussed previously. To use Theorem
7.3.23, first we need to find a complete sufficient statistic $T .$ Then we need to find
a function $\phi \left( T \right)$ that is an unbiased estimator of the parameter. The Rao-Blackwell
Theorem, Theorem 7.3.17, may be helpful if we know of some unbiased estimator of
the parameter. But if we cannot dream up some unbiased estimator, then the method
does not tell us how to construct one.

Even if the minimization of the posterior expected loss cannot be done analytically,
the integral can be evaluated and the minimization carried out numerically. In fact,
having observed $X = x ,$ we need to do the minimization only for this particular $x .$
However, in some problems we can explicitly describe the Bayes rule.

Example 7.3.28 (Two Bayes rules) Consider a point estimation problem for a
real-valued parameter $\theta .$

a. For squared error loss, the posterior expected loss is

$$\int _ { \theta } \left( \theta - a \right) ^ { 2 } \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta = E \left( \left( \theta - a \right) ^ { 2 } | X = x \right) .$$

Here $\theta$ is the random variable with distribution $\pi \left( \theta | x \right) .$ By Example 2.2.6, this
expected value is minimized by $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) .$ So the Bayes rule is the mean of
the posterior distribution.

b. For absolute error loss, the posterior expected loss is $E \left( | \theta - a | | X = x \right) .$ By applying
Exercise 2.18, we see that this is minimized by choosing $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { m e c }$ of $\pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) .$
$\parallel$

In Section 7.2.3, the Bayes estimator we discussed was $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ the posterior
mean. We now see that this is the Bayes estimator with respect to squared error loss.
If some other loss function is deemed more appropriate than squared error loss, the
Bayes estimator might be a different statistic.

Example 7.3.29 (Normal Bayes estimates) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sam-
$p l e$ from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population and let $\pi \left( \theta \right)$ be $n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) .$ The values $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\mu ,$ and $\tau ^ { 2 }$ are
known. In Example 7.2.16, as extended in Exercise 7.22, we found that the posterior
distribution of $\theta$ given $\bar { X } = \bar { x }$ is normal with

$$E \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) = \frac { \tau ^ { 2 } } { \tau ^ { 2 } + \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) } \bar { x } + \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } / n } { \tau ^ { 2 } + \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) } \mu$$

and

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) = \frac { \tau ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } / n } { \tau ^ { 2 } + \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) } .$$

For squared error $\log ,$ the Bayes estimator is $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) .$ Since the posterior
distribution is normal, it is symmetric about its mean and the median of $\pi \left( \theta | x \right)$ is
equal to $\mathrm { E } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) .$ Thus, for absolute error loss, the Bayes estimator is also $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) =$
$E \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) .$
II

Example 7.3.30 (Binomial Bayes estimates)
Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
$\mathrm { B e r n o u l l i } \left( p \right)$ and let $Y = \sum X _ { i } .$ Suppose the prior on $p$ is $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) .$ In Example
7.2.14 we found that the posterior distribution depends on the sample only through
the observed value of $Y = y$ and is $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( y + \alpha , n - y + \beta \right) .$ Hence, $\delta ^ { \pi } \left( y \right) = \mathrm { E } \left( p | y \right) =$
$\left( y + \alpha \right) / \left( \alpha + \beta + n \right)$ is the Bayes estimator of $p$ for squared error loss.

For absolute error loss, we need to find the median of $\pi \left( p | y \right) = \mathrm { b e t a } \left( y + \alpha , n - y + \beta \right) .$
In general, there is no simple expression for this median. The median is implicitly
defined to be the number, $m ,$ that satisfies

$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { m } \frac { \Gamma \left( \alpha + \beta + n \right) } { \Gamma \left( y + \alpha \right) \Gamma \left( n - y + \beta \right) } p ^ { y + \alpha - 1 } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - y + \beta - 1 } d p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$$

This integral can be evaluated numerically to find (approximately) the value $m$ that
satisfies the equality. We have done this for $n = 1 0$ and $\alpha = \beta = 1 ,$ the uniform(0,1)
prior. The Bayes estimator for absolute error loss is given in Table 7.3.1. In the table
we have also listed the Bayes estimator for squared error loss, derived above, and the
MLE, $\widehat { p } = y / n .$

Notice in Table 7.3.1 that, unlike the MLE, neither Bayes estimator estimates $p$ to
be 0 or 1, even if $y \quad i s \quad 0$ or $n .$ It is typical of Bayes estimators that they would not
take on extreme values in the parameter space. No matter how large the sample size,
the prior always has some influence on the estimator and tends to draw it away from
the extreme values. In the above expression for $\mathrm { E } \left( p | y \right) ,$ you can see that even if $y = 0$
and $n$ is large, the Bayes estimator is a positive number.
I!

$\theta \in \left\{ 1 , 2 , 3 \right\} .$ Find the MLE of $\theta .$

7.2 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a gamma(a, $\left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ population.

(a) Find the MLE of $\beta ,$ assuming $\alpha$ is known.

(b) If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are both unknown, there is no explicit formula for the MLEs of $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$
but the maximum can be found numerically. The result in part (a) can be used to
reduce the problem to the maximization of a univariate function. Find the MLEs
for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for the data in Exercise $7 . 1 0 \left( c \right) .$

7.3 Given a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a population with pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ show that max-
imizing the likelihood function, $L \left( \theta | x \right) ,$ as a function of $\theta$ is equivalent to maximizing
$\log L \left( \theta | x \right) .$

7.4 Prove the assertion in Example 7.2.8. That is, prove that $\widehat { \theta }$ given there is the MLE
when the range of $\theta$ is restricted to the positive axis.

7.5 Consider estimating the binomial parameter $k$ as in Example 7.2.9.

(a) Prove the assertion that the integer $\widehat { k }$ that satisfies the inequalities and is the MLE
is the largest integer less than or equal to $1 / \widehat { z } .$

(b) Let $p = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ $n = 4 ,$ and $X _ { 1 } = 0 ,$ $X _ { 2 } = 2 0 ,$ $X _ { 3 } = 1$ 1, and $X _ { 4 } = 1 9 .$ What is $\widehat { k }$

7.6 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from the pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta x ^ { - 2 } , \quad 0 < \theta \leq x < \infty .$$

(a) What is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$

(b) Find the MLE of $\theta .$

(c) Find the method of moments estimator of $\theta .$

7.7 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with one of two pdfs. If $\theta = 0 ,$ then

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \text { if } 0 < x < 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise, } } \end{array} \right.$$

while if $\theta = 1 ,$ then

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 / \left( 2 \sqrt { x } \right) } & { \text { if } 0 < x < 1 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise. } } \end{array} \right.$$

Find the MLE of $\theta .$

<!-- PageNumber="356" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.4" -->

7.8 One observation, $X ,$ is taken from a $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population.

(a) Find an unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Find the MLE of o.

(c) Discuss how the method of moments estimator of $\sigma$ might be found.

7.9 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \theta } , \quad 0 \leq x \leq \theta , \quad \theta > 0 .$$

Estimate $\theta$ using both the method of moments and maximum likelihood. Calculate the
means and variances of the two estimators. Which one should be preferred and why?

7.10 The independent random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ have the common distribution

$$P \left( X _ { i } \leq x | \alpha , \beta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { \left( x / \beta \right) ^ { \alpha } } & { \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq \beta } \\ { 1 } & { \text { if } x > \beta } \end{array} \right.$$

where the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive.

(a) Find a two-dimensional sufficient statistic for $\left( \alpha , \beta \right) .$

(b) Find the MLEs of $\alpha$ and $\beta .$

(c) The length (in millimeters) of cuckoos' eggs found in hedge sparrow nests can be
modeled with this distribution. For the data

22.0, 23.9, 20.9, 23.8, 25.0, 24.0, 21.7, 23.8, 22.8, 23.1, 23.1, 23.5, 23.0, 23.0,

find the MLEs of $\alpha$ and $\beta .$

7.11 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid with pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta x ^ { \theta - 1 } , \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1 , \quad 0 < \theta < \infty .$$

(a) Find the MLE of $\theta ,$ and show that its variance $\rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty .$

(b) Find the method of moments estimator of $\theta .$

7.12 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a population with pmf

$$P _ { \theta } \left( X = x \right) = \theta ^ { x } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 1 - x } , \quad x = 0 \mathrm { o r } 1 , \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$$

(a) Find the method of moments estimator and MLE of $\theta .$

(b) Find the mean squared errors of each of the estimators.

(c) Which estimator is preferred? Justify your choice.

7.13 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a sample from a population with double exponential pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - | x - \theta | } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty , \quad - \infty < \theta < \infty .$$

Find the MLE of $\theta .$ (Hint: Consider the case of even $n$ separate from that of odd
$n ,$ and find the MLE in terms of the order statistics. A complete treatment of this
problem is given in Norton 1984.)

7.14 Let $X$ and $Y$ be independent exponential random variables, with

$$f \left( x | \lambda \right) = \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - x / \lambda } , x > 0 , \quad f \left( y | \mu \right) = \frac { 1 } { \mu } e ^ { - y / \mu } , y > 0 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="357" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.4" -->

We observe $Z$ and $W$ with

$$Z = \min \left( X , Y \right) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad W = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 1 & \mathrm { i f } Z = X \\ 0 & \mathrm { i f } Z = Y . \end{array} \right.$$

In Exercise 4.26 the joint distribution of $Z$ and $W$ was obtained. Now assume that
$\left( Z _ { i } , W _ { i } \right) , i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ are $n$ iid observations. Find the MLEs of $\lambda$ and $\mu .$

7.15 Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a sample from the inverse Gaussian pdf,

$$f \left( x | \mu , \lambda \right) = \left( \frac { \lambda } { 2 \pi x ^ { 3 } } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \exp \left\{ - \lambda \left( x - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \mu ^ { 2 } x \right) \right\} , \quad x > 0 .$$

(a) Show that the MLEs of $\mu$ and $\lambda$ are

$$\widehat { \mu } _ { n } = \bar { X } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \widehat { \lambda } _ { n } = \frac { n } { \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { 1 } { X _ { i } } - \frac { 1 } { \widetilde { X } } \right) } .$$

(b) Tweedie (1957) showed that $\widehat { \mu } _ { n }$ and $\widehat { \lambda } _ { n }$ are independent, $\widehat { \mu } _ { n }$ having an inverse
Gaussian distribution with parameters $\mu$ and $n \lambda ,$ and $n \lambda / \widehat { \lambda } _ { n }$ having a $\chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distri-
bution. Schwarz and Samanta (1991) give a proof of these facts using an induction
argument.

(i) Show that $\widehat { \mu } _ { 2 }$ has an inverse Gaussian distribution with parameters $\mu$ and $2 \lambda ,$
$2 \lambda / \widehat { \lambda } _ { 2 }$ has a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution, and they are independent.

(ii) Assume the result is true for $n = k$ and that we get a new, independent
observation x. Establish the induction step used by Schwarz and Samanta
(1991), and transform the pdf $f \left( x , \widehat { \mu } _ { k } , \widehat { \lambda } _ { k } \right)$ to $f \left( x , \widehat { \mu } _ { k + 1 } , \widehat { \lambda } _ { k + 1 } \right) .$ Show that this
density factors in the appropriate way and that the result of Tweedie follows.

7.16 Berger and Casella (1992) also investigate power means, which we have seen in Exercise
4.57. Recall that a power mean is defined as $\left[ \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { r } \right] ^ { 1 / r } .$ This definition can be
further generalized by noting that the power function $x ^ { r }$ can be replaced by any
continuous, monotone function $h ,$ yielding the generalized mean $h ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } h \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) .$

(a) The least squares problem $\min _ { a } \sum _ { i } \left( x _ { i } - a \right) ^ { 2 }$ is sometimes solved using transformed
variables, that is, solving $\min _ { a } \sum \left[ h \left( x _ { i } \right) - h \left( a \right) \right] ^ { 2 } .$ Show that the solution to this
latter problem is $a = h ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i } h \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) .$

(b) Show that the arithmetic mean is the solution to the untransformed least squares
problem, the geometric mean is the solution to the problem transformed by $h \left( x \right) =$
$\log x ,$ and the harmonic mean is the solution to the problem transformed by $h \left( x \right) =$
$1 / x .$

(c) Show that if the least squares problem is transformed with the $B o x - C o x \quad T r a n s f o r -$
mation (see Exercise 11.3), then the solution is a generalized mean with $h \left( x \right) = x ^ { \lambda } .$

(d) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a sample from a lognormal(µ, $\left. \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Show that the
MLE of $\mu$ is the geometric mean.

(e) Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are a sample from a one-parameter exponential family
$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \exp \left\{ \theta h \left( x \right) - H \left( \theta \right) \right\} g \left( x \right) ,$ where $h = H ^ { \prime }$ and $h$ is an increasing function.

(i) Show that the MLE of $\theta$ is $\widehat { \theta } = h ^ { - 1 } \left( \left( 1 / n \right) \sum _ { i } h \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) .$

(ii) Show that two densities that satisfy $h = H ^ { \prime }$ are the normal and the inverted
gamma with pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right) = \theta x ^ { - 2 } \exp \left\{ - \theta / x \right\}$ for $x > 0 ,$ and for the normal the
MLE is the arithmetic mean and for the inverted gamma it is the harmonic
mean.

7.17 The Borel Paradox (Miscellanea 4.9.3) can also arise in inference problems. Suppose
that $X _ { 1 }$ and $X _ { 2 }$ are iid exponential(0) random variables.
☒

(a) If we observe only $X _ { 2 } ,$ show that the MLE of $\theta$ is $\widehat { \theta } = X _ { 2 } .$

(b) Suppose that we instead observe only $Z = \left( X _ { 2 } - 1 \right) / X _ { 1 } .$ Find the joint distribution
☒
of $\left( X _ { 1 } , Z \right) ,$ and integrate out $X _ { 1 }$ to get the likelihood function.

(c) Suppose that $X _ { 2 } = 1 .$ Compare the MLEs for $\theta$ from parts (a) and (b).

(d) Bayesian analysis is not immune to the Borel Paradox. If $\pi \left( \theta \right)$ is a prior density
for 0, show that the posterior distributions, at $X _ { 2 } = 1 ,$ are different in parts (a)
and (b).

(Communicated by $L .$ Mark Berliner, Ohio State University.)

7.18 Let $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ be iid bivariate normal random variables (pairs) where all
☒
five parameters are unknown.

(a) Show $\mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } ,$ $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\widetilde { \mu } _ { X } =$
, $\widetilde { \mu } _ { Y } = \bar { y } , \widetilde { \sigma } _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } , \widetilde { \sigma } _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } , \widetilde { \rho } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \left( y _ { i } \right)$ -
$\left. \bar { y } \right) / \left( \widetilde { \sigma } _ { X } \widetilde { \sigma } _ { Y } \right) .$

(b) Derive the MLEs of the unknown parameters and show that they are the same as
the method of moments estimators. (One attack is to write the joint pdf as the
product of a conditional and a marginal, that is, write

$$f \left( x , y | \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho \right) = f \left( y | x , \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho \right) f \left( x | \mu _ { X } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

and argue that the MLEs for $\mu _ { X }$ and $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 }$ are given by $\bar { x }$ and $\frac { 1 } { n } \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } .$
Then, turn things around $t o$ get the MLEs for $\mu _ { Y }$ and $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } .$ Finally, work with the
"partially maximized" likelihood function $L \left( \bar { x } , \bar { y } , \widehat { \sigma } _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \widehat { \sigma } _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho | \mathrm { x } , \mathrm { y } \right)$ to get the $M L E$
for $\rho .$ As might be guessed, this is a difficult problem.)

7.19 Suppose that the random variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ satisfy

$$Y _ { i } = \beta x _ { i } + \epsilon _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are fixed constants, and $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n }$ are id $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ unknown.

(a) Find a two-dimensional sufficient statistic for $\left( \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$

(b) Find the $\mathrm { M L E } \mathrm { o f } \mathcal{L} ,$ and show that it is an unbiased estimator of $\beta .$

(c) Find the distribution of the MLE of $\beta .$

7.20 Consider $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ as defined in Exercise 7.19.

(a) Show that $\sum Y _ { i } / \sum x _ { i }$ is an unbiased estimator of $\beta .$

(b) Calculate the exact variance of $\sum Y _ { i } / \sum x _ { i }$ and compare it to the variance of the
MLE.

7.21 Again, let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ be as defined in Exercise 7.19.

(a) Show that $\left[ \sum \left( Y _ { i } / x _ { i } \right) \right] / n$ is also an unbiased estimator of $\beta .$

(b) Calculate the exact variance of $\left[ \sum \left( Y _ { i } / x _ { i } \right) \right] / n$ and compare it to the variances of
the estimators in the previous two exercises.

7.22 This exercise will prove the assertions in Example 7.2.16, and more. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be
a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, and suppose that the prior distribution
on $\theta$ is $n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) .$ Here we assume that $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\mu ,$ and $\tau ^ { 2 }$ are all known.

(a) Find the joint pdf of $\bar { X }$ and $\theta .$
☒

(b) Show that $m \left( \bar { x } | \sigma ^ { 2 } , \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ the marginal distribution of $\bar { X } ,$ is $n \left( \mu , \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) + \tau ^ { 2 } \right) .$
☒

(c) Show that $\pi \left( \theta | \bar { x } , \sigma ^ { 2 } , \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ the posterior distribution of $\theta ,$ is normal with mean
and variance given by (7.2.10).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="359" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.4" -->

7.23 If $S ^ { 2 }$ is the sample variance based on a sample of size $n$ from a normal population,
we know that $\left( n - 1 \right) S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 }$ has a $\chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution. The conjugate prior for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is the
inverted gamma pdf, $\mathrm { I G } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ given by

$$\pi \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } \frac { 1 } { \left( \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { \alpha + 1 } } e ^ { - 1 / \left( \beta \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } , \quad 0 < \sigma ^ { 2 } < \infty ,$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. Show that the posterior distribution of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is
$I G \left( \alpha + \frac { n - 1 } { 2 } , \left[ \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) S ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \beta } \right] ^ { - 1 } \right) .$ Find the mean of this distribution, the Bayes estimator
of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

7.24 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ and let $\lambda$ have a $\mathrm { g a m m a } \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution, the
conjugate family for the Poisson.

(a) Find the posterior distribution of $\lambda .$

(b) Calculate the posterior mean and variance.

7.25 We examine a generalization of the hierarchical (Bayes) model considered in Example
7.2.16 and Exercise 7.22. Suppose that we observe $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ where
☒

$$X _ { i } | \theta _ { i } \sim n \left( \theta _ { i } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n , \quad \text { independent, } ,$$
$$\theta _ { i } \sim n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n , \quad \text { independent } .$$

(a) Show that the marginal distribution of $X _ { i }$ is $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } + \tau ^ { 2 } \right)$ and that, marginally,
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid. (Empirical Bayes analysis would use the marginal distribution
of the $X _ { i } s$ to estimate the prior parameters $\mu$ and $\tau ^ { 2 } .$ See Miscellanea 7.5.6.)

(b) Show, in general, that if

$$X _ { i } | \theta _ { i } \sim f \left( x | \theta _ { i } \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n , \quad \text { independent } ,$$
$$\theta _ { i } \sim \pi \left( \theta | \tau \right) , i = 1 , \ldots , n , \mathrm { i n d e p e n d e n t } ,$$

then marginally, $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid.

7.26 In Example 7.2.16 we saw that the normal distribution is its own conjugate family.
It is sometimes the case, however, that a conjugate prior does not $\mathrm { a c c u r a t e l y }$ reflect
prior knowledge, and a different prior is sought. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and let
☒ ☒
$\theta$ have a double exponential distribution, that is, $\pi \left( \theta \right) = e ^ { - | \theta | / a } / \left( 2 a \right) ,$ a known. Find
the mean of the posterior distribution of $\theta .$

7.27 Refer to Example 7.2.17.

(a) Show that the likelihood estimators from the complete-data likelihood (7.2.11) are
given by (7.2.12).

(b) Show that the limit of the EM sequence in (7.2.23) satisfies (7.2.16)

(c) A direct solution of the original (incomplete-data) likelihood equations is possible.
Show that the solution to (7.2.16) is given by

$$\widehat { \beta } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } y _ { i } } { \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } x _ { i } } , \quad \widehat { \tau } _ { 1 } = \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \widehat { \beta } } , \quad \widehat { \tau } _ { j } = \frac { x _ { j } + y _ { j } } { \widehat { \beta } + 1 } , \quad j = 2 , 3 , \ldots , n ,$$

and that this is the limit of the EM sequence in (7.2.23).

and

$$j = 1 , 2 , \cdots , n .$$

7.30 The EM algorithm is useful in a variety of situation, and the definition of "miss-
ing data" can be stretched $t o$ accommodate many different models. Suppose that
we have a mixture density $p f \left( x \right) + \left( 1 \quad - \quad p \right) g \left( x \right) ,$ where $p$ is unknown. If we observe
$X = \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) ,$ the sample density is
☒

$$\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ p f \left( x _ { i } \right) + \left( 1 - p \right) g \left( x _ { i } \right) \right] ,$$

which could be $d i f f i c u l t$ to deal with. (Actually, a mixture of two is not terrible, but
consider what the likelihood would look like with a mixture $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } p _ { i } f _ { i } \left( x \right)$ for large $k$ k.)
The $E M$ solution is to augment the observed (or incomplete) data with $Z = \left( Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , \right. ,$
$Z _ { n } ,$ where $Z _ { i }$ tells which component of the mixture $X _ { i }$ came from; that is,
☒

$$X _ { i } | z _ { i } = 1 \sim f \left( x _ { i } \right) \quad \text { and } \quad X _ { i } | z _ { i } = 0 \sim g \left( x _ { i } \right) ,$$

and $P \left( Z _ { i } = 1 \right) = p .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="361" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.4" -->

(a) Show that the joint density of $\left( X , Z \right)$ is given by $\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ p f \left( x _ { i } \right) ^ { z _ { i } } \right] \left[ \left( 1 - p \right) g \left( x _ { i } \right) ^ { 1 - z _ { i } } \right] .$

(b) Show that the missing data distribution, the distribution of $Z _ { i } | x _ { i } , p$ is Bernoulli
with success probability $p f \left( x _ { i } \right) / \left( p f \left( x _ { i } \right) + \left( 1 - p \right) g \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) .$

(c) Calculate the expected complete-data $\log$ likelihood, and show that the EM se-
quence is given by

$$\widehat { p } ^ { \left( r + 1 \right) } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \widehat { p } ^ { \left( r \right) } f \left( x _ { i } \right) } { \widehat { p } ^ { \left( r \right) } f \left( x _ { i } \right) + \left( 1 - \widehat { p } ^ { \left( r \right) } \right) g \left( x _ { i } \right) } .$$

7.31 Prove Theorem 7.2.20.

(a) Show that, using (7.2.19), we can write

$$\log L \left( \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } | \mathrm { y } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left[ \log L \left( \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } | \mathrm { y } , \mathrm { X } \right) | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right] - \mathrm { E } \left[ \log k \left( \mathrm { X } | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right) | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right] ,$$

and, since $\widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r + 1 \right) }$ is a maximum, $\log L \left( \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r + 1 \right) } | \mathrm { y } , \mathrm { X } \right) \geq \mathrm { E } \left[ \log L \left( \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } | \mathrm { y } , \mathrm { X } \right) | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right] .$
When is the inequality an equality?

(b) Now use Jensen's inequality to show that

$$\mathrm { E } \left[ \log k \left( \mathrm { X } | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r + 1 \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right) | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right] \leq \mathrm { E } \left[ \log k \left( \mathrm { X } | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right) | \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } , \mathrm { y } \right] ,$$

which together with part (a) proves the theorem.

(Hint: If $f$ and $g$ are densities, since $\log$ is a concave function, Jensen's inequality
(4.7.7) implies

$$\int \log \left( \frac { f \left( x \right) } { g \left( x \right) } \right) g \left( x \right) d x \leq \log \left( \int \frac { f \left( x \right) } { g \left( x \right) } g \left( x \right) d x \right) = \log \left( \int f \left( x \right) d x \right) = 0 .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

By the property of logs, this in turn implies that

$$\int \log \left[ f \left( x \right) \right] g \left( x \right) d x \leq \int \log \left[ g \left( x \right) \right] g \left( x \right) d x$$ ☒

7.32 The algorithm of Exercise 5.65 can be adapted to simulate (approximately) a sample
from the posterior distribution using only a sample from the prior distribution. Let
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where $\theta$ has prior distribution . Generate $\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { m }$ from TT,
and calculate $q _ { i } = L \left( \theta _ { i } | x \right) / \sum _ { j } L \left( \theta _ { j } / | x \right) ,$ where $L \left( \theta | x \right) = \prod _ { i } f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right)$ is the likelihood
function.

(a) Generate $\theta _ { 1 } ^ { * } , \ldots , \theta _ { r } ^ { * } ,$ where $P \left( \theta ^ { * } = \theta _ { i } \right) = q _ { i } .$ Show that this is a (approximate)
sample from the posterior in the sense that $P \left( \theta ^ { * } \leq t \right)$ converges to $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta .$

(b) Show that the estimator $\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { r } h \left( \theta _ { j } ^ { * } \right) / r$ converges to $E \left[ h \left( \theta \right) | x \right] ,$ where the expec-
tation is with respect to the posterior distribution.

(c) Ross (1996) suggests that Rao-Blackwellization can improve the estimate in part
(b). Show that for any $j ,$

$$\mathrm { E } \left[ h \left( \theta _ { j } ^ { * } \right) | \theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { m } \right] = \frac { 1 } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } L \left( \theta _ { i } | \mathrm { x } \right) } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } h \left( \theta _ { i } \right) L \left( \theta _ { i } | \mathrm { x } \right)$$

has the same mean and smaller variance than the estimator in part (b).

7.34 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a binomial(n, $\left. p \right) .$ We want to find equivariant
point estimators of $p$ using the group described in Example 6.4.1.

(a) Find the class of estimators that are equivariant with respect to this group.

(b) Within the class of Bayes estimators of Example 7.2.14, find the estimators that
are equivariant with respect to this group.

(c) From the equivariant Bayes estimators of part (b), find the one with the smallest
MSE.

7.35 The Pitman Estimator of Location (see Lehmann and Casella 1998 Section 3.1, or the
original paper by Pitman 1939) is given by

$$d _ { P } \left( X \right) = \frac { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } t \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } - t \right) d t } { \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } - t \right) d t }$$ ☒

where we observe a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from $f \left( x - \theta \right) .$ Pitman showed that
this estimator is the location-equivariant estimator with smallest mean squared error
(that is, it minimizes (7.3.3)). The goals of this exercise are more modest.

(a) Show that $d _ { \mathrm { P } } \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is invariant with respect to the location group of Example 7.3.6.
☒

(b) Show that if $f \left( x - \theta \right)$ is $n \left( \theta , 1 \right) ,$ then $d _ { P } \left( X \right) = \bar { X } .$
☒

(c) Show that if $f \left( x - \theta \right)$ is $\mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( \theta - \frac { 1 } { 2 } , \theta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ,$ then $d _ { P } \left( X \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } + X _ { \left( n \right) } \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒

7.36 The Pitman Estimator of Scale is given by

$$d _ { \mathrm { P } } ^ { r } \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = \frac { \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } t ^ { n + r - 1 } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( t x _ { i } \right) d t } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } t ^ { n + 2 r - 1 } \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( t x _ { i } \right) d t } ,$$ ☒

where we observe a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from $\frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left( x / \sigma \right) .$ Pitman showed that this
estimator is the scale-equivariant estimator of $\sigma ^ { r }$ with smallest scaled mean squared
error (that is, it minimizes $\left. E \left( d - \sigma ^ { r } \right) ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 r } \right) .$

(a) Show that $d _ { \mathrm { P } } ^ { r } \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is equivariant with respect to the scale group, that is, it satisfies
☒

$$d _ { \mathrm { P } } ^ { r } \left( c x _ { 1 } , \ldots , c x _ { n } \right) = c ^ { r } d _ { \mathrm { P } } ^ { r } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) . ,$$

for any constant $c > 0 .$

(b) Find the Pitman scale-equivariant estimator for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$

(c) Find the Pitman scale-equivariant estimator for $\beta$ if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid exponential(3)
☒

(d) Find the Pitman scale-equivariant estimator for $\theta$ if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid uniform(0,0).

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \theta } , \quad - \theta < x < \theta , \quad \theta > 0 .$$

7.41 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

(a) Show that the estimator $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } X _ { i }$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu$ if $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } = 1 .$
☒

(b) Among all unbiased estimators of this form (called linear unbiased estimators) find
the one with minimum variance, and calculate the variance.

7.42 Let $W _ { 1 } , \ldots , W _ { k }$ be unbiased estimators of a parameter $\theta$ with Var $W _ { i } = \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ and
$\mathrm { C o v } \left( W _ { i } , W _ { j } \right) = 0$ if $i \neq j .$

(a) Show that, of all estimators of the form $\sum a _ { i } W _ { i } ,$ where the $a _ { i } s$ are constant and
$E _ { \theta } \left( \sum a _ { i } W _ { i } \right) = \theta ,$ the estimator
$W ^ { * } = \frac { \sum W _ { i } / \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { \sum \left( 1 / \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) }$
has minimum variance.

(b) Show that Var
$W ^ { * } = \frac { 1 } { \sum \left( 1 / \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) }$

7.43 Exercise 7.42 established that the optimal weights are $q _ { i } ^ { * } = \left( 1 / \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( \sum _ { j } 1 / \sigma _ { j } ^ { 2 } \right) .$ A result
due to Tukey (see Bloch and Moses 1988) states that if $W = \sum _ { i } q _ { i } W _ { i }$ is an estimator
based on another sets of weights $q _ { i } \geq 0$ 0, $\sum _ { i } q _ { i } = 1 ,$ then

$$\frac { \mathrm { V a r } W } { \mathrm { V a r } W ^ { * } } \leq \frac { 1 } { 1 - \lambda ^ { 2 } } ,$$

where $\lambda$ satisfies $\left( 1 + \lambda \right) / \left( 1 - \lambda \right) = b _ { \max } / b _ { \min } ,$ and $b _ { \max }$ and $b _ { \min }$ are the largest and
smallest of $b _ { i } = q _ { i } / q _ { i } ^ { * } .$

(a) Prove Tukey's inequality.

(b) Use the inequality to assess the performance of the usual mean $\sum _ { i } W _ { i } / k$ as a
function of $\sigma _ { \max } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { \min } ^ { 2 }$

7.44 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be $n \left( \theta , 1 \right) .$ Show that the best unbiased estimator of $\theta ^ { 2 }$ is $\bar { X } ^ { 2 } - \left( 1 / n \right) .$
☒ ☒ ☒
Calculate its variance (use Stein's Identity from Section 3.6), and show that it is greater
than the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound.

7.45 Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid from a distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ and let $S ^ { 2 }$
☒

☒ ☒
be the usual unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ In Example 7.3.4 we saw that, under normality,
the MLE has smaller MSE than $S ^ { 2 } .$ In this exercise will explore variance estimates
some more.

(a) Show that, for any estimator of the form $a S ^ { 2 } ,$ where a is a constant,

$$\mathrm { M S E } \left( a S ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { E } \left[ a S ^ { 2 } - \sigma ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 2 } = a ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( a - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 4 } .$$

(b) Show that

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( S ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \kappa - \frac { n - 3 } { n - 1 } \right) \sigma ^ { 4 } ,$$

where $\kappa = E \left[ X - \mu \right] ^ { 4 } / \sigma ^ { 4 }$ is the kurtosis. (You may have already done this in
Exercise 5.8(b).)

(c) Show that, under normality, the kurtosis is 3 and establish that, in this case, the
estimator of the form $a S ^ { 2 }$ with the minimum MSE is $\frac { n - 1 } { n + 1 } S ^ { 2 } .$ (Lemma 3.6.5 may
be helpful.)

(d) If normality is not assumed, show that $\mathrm { M S E } \left( a S ^ { 2 } \right)$ is minimized at

$$a = \frac { n - 1 } { \left( n + 1 \right) + \frac { \left( \kappa - 3 \right) \left( n - 1 \right) } { n } }$$

which is useless as it depends on a parameter.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="364" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.4" -->

(e) Show that

(i) for distributions with $\kappa > 3 ,$ the optimal $a$ will satisfy $a < \frac { n - 1 } { n + 1 }$

(ii) for distributions with $\kappa < 3 ,$ the optimal a will satisfy $\frac { n - 1 } { n + 1 } < a < 1 .$
See Searls and Intarapanich (1990) for more details.

7.46 Let $X _ { 1 } ,$ $X _ { 2 } ,$ and $X _ { 3 }$ be a random sample of size three from a uniform(0, $\left( \theta , 2 \theta \right)$ distribution,
where $\theta > 0 .$

(a) Find the method of moments estimator of $\theta .$

(b) Find the MLE, $\widehat { \theta }$ , and find a constant $k$ such that $E _ { \theta } \left( k \widehat { \theta } \right) = \theta .$

(c) Which of the two estimators can be improved by using sufficiency? How?

1.29, .86, 1.33,

three observations of average berry sizes (in centimeters) of wine grapes.

a $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ distribution. If $n$ independent measurements are made, find an unbiased
estimator of the area of the circle. Is it best unbiased?

(b) For $n \geq 4 ,$ show that the product $X _ { 1 } X _ { 2 } X _ { 3 } X _ { 4 }$ is an unbiased estimator of $p ^ { 4 } ,$ and

use this fact to find the best unbiased estimator of $p ^ { 4 } .$

(a) Find an unbiased estimator of $\lambda$ based only on $Y = \min \left\{ X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right\} .$

(b) Find a better estimator than the one in part (a). Prove that it is better.

(c) The following data are high-stress failure times (in hours) of Kevlar/epoxy spher-
ical vessels used in a sustained pressure environment on the space shuttle:

50.1, 70.1, 137.0, 166.9, 170.5, 152.8, 80.5, 123.5, 112.6, 148.5, 160.0, 125.4.

Failure times are often modeled with the exponential distribution. Estimate the
mean failure time using the estimators from parts (a) and (b).

7.50 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\theta > 0 .$ For this model both $\bar { X }$ and $c S$ are unbiased
☒

estimators of $\theta ,$ where
$c = \frac { \sqrt { n - 1 } \Gamma \left( \left( n - 1 \right) / 2 \right) } { \sqrt { 2 } \Gamma \left( n / 2 \right) }$
.

(a) Prove that for any number $a$ the estimator $a \bar { X } + \left( 1 - a \right) \left( c S \right)$ is an unbiased estimator
of $\theta .$

(b) Find the value of $a$ that produces the estimator with minimum variance.

(c) Show that $\left( \bar { X } , S ^ { 2 } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ but it is not a complete sufficient
☒

statistic.

7.66 The jackknife is a general technique for reducing bias in an estimator (Quenouille,
1956). A one-step jackknife estimator is defined as follows. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random
sample, and let $T _ { n } = T _ { n } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ be some estimator of a parameter $\theta .$ In order to
"jackknife" $T _ { n }$ we calculate the $n$ statistics $T _ { n }$ (1), $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ where $T _ { n }$ is calculated
just as $T _ { n }$ but using the $n \quad - \quad 1$ observations with $X _ { i }$ removed from the sample. The
jackknife estimator of $\theta ,$ $\mathrm { d e n o t e c }$ by $\mathrm { J K } \left( T _ { n } \right) ,$ is given by

$$J K \left( T _ { n } \right) = n T _ { n } - \frac { n - 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } T _ { n } ^ { \left( i \right) } .$$

(In general, $\mathrm { J K } \left( T _ { n } \right)$ will have a smaller bias than $T _ { n } .$ See Miller 1974 for a good review
of the properties of the jackknife.)

Now, to be specific, let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid Bernoulli(0). The $\mathrm { o b j e c t }$ is to estimate $\theta ^ { 2 } .$

(a) Show that the MLE of $\theta ^ { 2 } ,$ $\left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } / n \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ is a biased estimator of $\theta ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Derive the one-step jackknife estimator based on the MLE.

(c) Show that the one-step jackknife estimator is an unbiased estimator of $\theta ^ { 2 } .$ (In
general, jackknifing only reduces bias. In this special case, however, it removes it
entirely.)

(d) Is this jackknife estimator the best unbiased estimator of $\theta ^ { 2 }$ If so, prove it. If not,
find the best unbiased estimator.


### 7.5 Miscellanea


#### 7.5.1 Moment Estimators and MLEs

In general, method of moments estimators are not functions of sufficient statistics;
hence, they can always be improved upon by conditioning on a sufficient statistic.
In the case of exponential families, however, there can be a correspondence between
a modified method of moments strategy and maximum likelihood estimation. This
correspondence is discussed in detail by Davidson and Solomon (1974), who also
relate some interesting history.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="368" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.5" -->

Suppose that we have a random sample $X = \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ from a pdf in the
☒
exponential family (see Theorem 5.2.11)

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = h \left( x \right) c \left( \theta \right) \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) t _ { i } \left( x \right) \right) ,$$

where the range of $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is independent of $\theta .$ (Note that $\theta$ may be a vector.) The
likelihood function is of the form

$$L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) = H \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \left[ c \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { n } \exp \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { i } \left( x _ { j } \right) \right) , .$$

and a modified method of moments would estimate $w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , k ,$ by $\widehat { w } _ { i } \left( \theta \right) ,$
the solutions to the $k$ equations

$$\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { i } \left( x _ { j } \right) = E _ { \theta } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } t _ { i } \left( X _ { j } \right) \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , k .$$

Davidson and Solomon, extending work of Huzurbazar (1949), show that the esti-
mators $\widehat { w } _ { i } \left( \theta \right)$ are, in fact, the MLEs of $w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) .$ If we define $\eta _ { i } = w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , k ,$
then the MLE of $g \left( \eta _ { i } \right)$ is equal to $g \left( \widehat { \eta } _ { i } \right) = g \left( \widehat { w _ { i } } \left( \theta \right) \right)$ for any one-to-one function
$g .$ Calculation of the above expectations may be simplified by using the facts
(Lehmann 1986, Section 2.7) that

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( t _ { i } \left( X _ { j } \right) \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) } \log \left( c \left( \theta \right) \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , k , \quad j = 1 , \ldots , n ;$$
$$\mathrm { C o v } _ { \theta } \left( t _ { i } \left( X _ { j } \right) , t _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left( X _ { j } \right) \right) = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial w _ { i } \left( \theta \right) \partial w _ { i ^ { \prime } } \left( \theta \right) } \log \left( c \left( \theta \right) \right) , \quad i , i ^ { \prime } = 1 , \ldots , k , \quad j = 1 , \ldots .$$
, n.


#### 7.5.2 Unbiased Bayes Estimates

As was seen in Section 7.2.3, if a Bayesian calculation is done, the mean of the
posterior distribution usually is taken as a point estimator. To be specific, if $X$
has pdf $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ with $E _ { \theta } \left( X \right) = \theta$ and there is a prior distribution $\pi \left( \theta \right) ,$ then the
posterior mean, a Bayesian point estimator of $\theta ,$ is given by

$$E \left( \theta | x \right) = \int \theta \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta .$$

A question that could be asked is whether $E \left( \theta | X \right)$ can be an unbiased estimator
☒
of $\theta$ and thus satisfy the equation

$$E _ { \theta } \left[ E \left( \theta | X \right) \right] = \int \left[ \int \theta \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta \right] f \left( x | \theta \right) d x = \theta .$$

The answer is no. That is, posterior means are never unbiased estimators. If they
were, then taking the expectation over the joint distribution of $X$ and $\theta ,$ we could

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="369" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.5" -->

write

$$E \left[ \left( X - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = E \left[ X ^ { 2 } - 2 X \theta + \theta ^ { 2 } \right]$$
$$= E \left( E \left( X ^ { 2 } - 2 X \theta + \theta ^ { 2 } | \theta \right) \right)$$

$$= E \left( E \left( X ^ { 2 } | \theta \right) - 2 \theta ^ { 2 } + \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= E \left( E \left( X ^ { 2 } | \theta \right) - \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= E \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) - E \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$$

(expand the square)
(iterate the expectation)
$\left( E \left( X | \theta \right) = E _ { \theta } X = \theta \right)$

(properties of expectations)

doing the conditioning one way, and conditioning on $X ,$ we could similarly calculate

$$E \left[ \left( X - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = E \left( E \left[ X ^ { 2 } - 2 X \theta + \theta ^ { 2 } | X \right] \right)$$
$$= E \left( X ^ { 2 } - 2 X ^ { 2 } + E \left( \theta ^ { 2 } | X \right) \right) \quad \left( \begin{array}{} E \left( \theta | X \right) = X \\ \text { by assumption } \end{array} \right)$$
$$= E \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \right) - E \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Comparing the two calculations, we see that the only way that there is no contra-
diction is if $E \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) = E \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ which then implies that $E \left( X - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } = 0 ,$ so $X = \theta .$
This occurs only if $P \left( X = \theta \right) = 1 ,$ an uninteresting situation, so we have argued to
a contradiction. Thus, either $E \left( X | \theta \right) \neq \theta$ or $E \left( \theta | X \right) \neq X ,$ showing that posterior
means cannot be unbiased estimators. Notice that we have implicitly made the
assumption that $E \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) < \infty ,$ but, in fact, this result holds under more general
conditions. Bickel and Mallows (1988) have a more thorough development of this
topic. At a more advanced level, this connection is characterized by Noorbaloochi
and Meeden (1983).


#### 7.5.3 The Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem

The Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem represents a major achievement in mathematical
statistics, tying together sufficiency, completeness, and uniqueness. The develop-
ment in the text is somewhat complementary to the Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem,
and thus we never stated it in its classical form (which is similar to Theorem
7.3.23). In fact, the Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem is contained in Theorems 7.3.19
and 7.3.23.

Theorem 7.5.1 (Lehmann-Scheffé) Unbiased estimators based on complete
sufficient statistics are unique.

Proof: Suppose $T$ is a complete sufficient statistic, and $\phi \left( T \right)$ is an estimator with
$E _ { \theta } \phi \left( T \right) = \tau \left( \theta \right) .$ From Theorem 7.3.23 we know that $\phi \left( T \right)$ is the best unbiased
estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) ,$ and from Theorem 7.3.19, best unbiased estimators are unique.
☐

This theorem can also be proved without Theorem 7.3.19, using just the conse-
quences of completeness, and provides a slightly different route to Theorem 7.3.23.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="370" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.5" -->


##### 7.5.4 More on the EM Algorithm

The EM algorithm has its roots in work done in the 1950s (Hartley 1958) but
really came into statistical prominence after the seminal work of Dempster, Laird,
and Rubin (1977), which detailed the underlying structure of the algorithm and
illustrated its use in a wide variety of applications.

One of the strengths of the EM algorithm is that conditions for convergence to the
incomplete-data MLEs are known, although this topic has obtained an additional
bit of folklore. Dempster, Laird, and Rubin's (1977) original proof of convergence
had a flaw, but valid convergence proofs were later given by Boyles (1983) and Wu
(1983); see also Finch, Mendell, and Thode (1989).

In our development we stopped with Theorem 7.2.20, which guarantees that the
likelihood will increase at each iteration. However, this may not be enough to con-
clude that the sequence $\left\{ \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } \right\}$ converges to a maximum likelihood estimator. Such
a guarantee requires further conditions. The following theorem, due to Wu (1983),
guarantees convergence to a stationary point, which may be a local maximum or
saddlepoint.

Theorem 7.5.2 If the expected complete-data log likelihood $E \left[ \log L \left( \theta | y , x \right) | \theta ^ { \prime } , y \right]$
is continuous in both $\theta$ and $\theta ^ { \prime } ,$ then all limit points of an EM sequence $\left\{ \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } \right\}$ are
stationary points of $L \left( \theta | y \right) ,$ and $L \left( \widehat { \theta } ^ { \left( r \right) } | \mathrm { y } \right)$ converges monotonically to $L \left( \widehat { \theta } | \mathrm { y } \right)$ for
some stationary point $\widehat { \theta } .$

In an exponential family computations become simplified because the log likelihood
will be linear in the missing data. We can write

$$\mathrm { E } \left[ \log L \left( \theta | \mathrm { y } , \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta ^ { \prime } , \mathrm { y } \right] = \mathrm { E } _ { \theta ^ { \prime } } \left[ \log \left( h \left( \mathrm { y } , \mathrm { X } \right) e ^ { \sum \eta _ { i } \left( \theta \right) T _ { i } - B \left( \theta \right) } \right) | \mathrm { y } \right]$$
$$= E _ { \theta ^ { \prime } } \left[ \log h \left( y , X \right) \right] + \sum \eta _ { i } \left( \theta \right) E _ { \theta ^ { \prime } } \left[ T _ { i } | y \right] - B \left( \theta \right) .$$

Thus, calculating the complete-data MLE involves only the simpler expectation
$E _ { \theta ^ { \prime } } \left[ T _ { i } | y \right] .$

Good overviews of the EM algorithm are provided by Little and Rubin (1987),
Tanner (1996), and Shafer (1997); see also Lehmann and Casella (1998, Section
6.4). McLachlan and Krishnan (1997) provide a book-length treatment of EM.


#### 7.5.5 Other Likelihoods

In this chapter we have used the method of maximum likelihood and seen that it
not only provides us with a method for finding estimators, but also brings along a
large-sample theory that is quite useful for inference.

Likelihood has many modifications. Some are used to deal with nuisance parameters
(such as profile likelihood); others are used when a more robust specification is
desired (such as quasi likelihood); and others are useful when the data are censored
(such as partial likelihood).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="371" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.5" -->

There are many other variations, and they all can provide some improvement
over the plain likelihood that we have described here. Entries to this wealth of
likelihoods can be found in the review article of Hinkley (1980) or the volume of
review articles edited by Hinkley, Reid, and Snell (1991).


#### 7.5.6 Other Bayes Analyses

1\. Robust Bayes Analysis The fact that Bayes rules may be quite sensitive to the
(subjective) choice of a prior distribution is a cause of concern for many Bayesian
statisticians. The paper of Berger (1984) introduced the idea of a robust $B a y e s$
analysis. This is a Bayes analysis in which estimators are sought that have good
properties for a range of prior distributions. That is, we look for an estimator
$\delta ^ { * }$ whose performance is robust in that it is not sensitive to which prior T, in a
class of priors, is the correct prior. Robust Bayes estimators can also have good
frequentist performance, making then rather attractive procedures. The review
papers by Berger (1990, 1994) and Wasserman (1992) provide an entry to this
topic.

2\. Empirical Bayes Analysis In a standard Bayesian analysis, there are usually
parameters in the prior distribution that are to be specified by the experimenter.
For example, consider the specification

$$X | \theta \sim n \left( \theta , 1 \right) ,$$
$$\theta | \tau ^ { 2 } \sim n \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

The Bayesian experimenter would specify a prior value for $\tau ^ { 2 }$ and a Bayesian
analysis can be done. However, as the marginal distribution of $X$ is $n \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ,$
it contains information about $\tau$ and can be used to estimate T. This idea of esti-
mation of prior parameters from the marginal distribution is what distinguishes
empirical Bayes analysis. Empirical Bayes methods are useful in constructing
improved procedures, as illustrated in Morris (1983) and Casella and Hwang
(1987). Gianola and Fernando (1986) have successfully applied these types of
methods to solve practical problems. A comprehensive treatment of empirical
Bayes is Carlin and Louis (1996), and less technical introductions are found in
Casella (1985, 1992).

3\. Hierarchical Bayes Analysis Another way of dealing with the specification
above, without giving a prior value to $\tau ^ { 2 } ,$ is with a hierarchical specification,
that is, a specification of a second-stage prior on $\tau ^ { 2 } .$ For example, we could use

$$X | \theta \sim n \left( \theta , 1 \right) ,$$
$$\theta | \tau ^ { 2 } \sim n \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$\tau ^ { 2 } \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( 0 , \infty \right) \text { \left(improper prior\right) } .$$

Hierarchical modeling, both Bayes and non-Bayes, is a very effective tool and
usually gives answers that are reasonably robust to the underlying model. Their
usefulness was demonstrated by Lindley and Smith (1972) and, since then, their
use and development have been quite widespread. The seminal paper of Gelfand

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="372" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 7.5" -->

and Smith (1990) tied hierarchical models to computing algorithms, and the ap-
plicability of Bayesian methods exploded. Lehmann and Casella (1998, Section
4.5) give an introduction to the theory of hierarchical Bayes, and Robert and
Casella (1999) cover applications and connections to computational algorithms.

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Chapter 8 Hypothesis Testing

"It is a mistake to confound strangeness with mystery."

Sherlock Holmes
A Study in Scarlet


## 8.1 Introduction

In Chapter 7 we studied a method of inference called point estimation. Now we move
to another inference method, hypothesis testing. Reflecting the need both to find and
to evaluate hypothesis tests, this chapter is divided into two parts, as was Chapter 7.
We begin with the definition of a statistical hypothesis.


### Definition 8.1.1 A hypothesis is a statement about a population parameter.

The definition of a hypothesis is rather general, but the important point is that a
hypothesis makes a statement about the population. The goal of a hypothesis test
is to decide, based on a sample from the population, which of two complementary
hypotheses is true.

Definition 8.1.2 The two complementary hypotheses in a hypothesis testing prob-
lem are called the $n$ null hypothesis and the alternative hypothesis. They are denoted
by $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 } ,$ respectively.

If $\theta$ denotes a population parameter, the general format of the null and alternative
hypotheses is $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } ,$ where $\Theta _ { 0 }$ is some subset of the parameter
space and $\Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ is its complement. For example, if $\theta$ denotes the average change in a
patient's blood pressure after taking a drug, an experimenter might be interested in
testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq 0 .$ The null hypothesis states that, on the average,
the drug has no effect on blood pressure, and the alternative hypothesis states that
there is some effect. This common situation, in which $H _ { 0 }$ states that a treatment has
no effect, has led to the term "null" hypothesis. As another example, a consumer might
be interested in the proportion of defective items produced by a supplier. If $\theta$ denotes
the proportion of defective items, the consumer might wish to test $H _ { 0 } : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 } .$ The value $\theta _ { 0 }$ is the maximum acceptable proportion of defective items,
and $H _ { 0 }$ states that the proportion of defectives is unacceptably high. Problems in
which the hypotheses concern the quality of a product are called acceptance s. mpling
problems.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="374" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

In a hypothesis testing problem, after observing the sample the experimenter must
decide either to accept $H _ { 0 }$ as true or to reject $H _ { 0 }$ as false and decide $H _ { 1 }$ is true.

Definition 8.1.3 A hypothesis testing procedure or hypothesis test is a rule that
specifies:

i. For which sample values the decision is made to accept $H _ { 0 }$ as true.

ii. For which sample values $H _ { 0 }$ is rejected and $H _ { 1 }$ is accepted as truc.

The subset of the sample space for which $H _ { 0 }$ will be rejected is called the rejection re-
gion or critical region. The complement $\mathrm { o f }$ the rejection region is called the acceptance
region.

On a philosophical level, some people worry about the distinction between "reject-
ing $H _ { 0 }$ and "accepting $H _ { 1 }$ In the first case, there is nothing implied about what
state the experimenter $i s$ accepting, only that the state defined by $H _ { 0 }$ is being rejected.
Similarly, a distinction can be made between "accepting $H _ { 0 }$ and "not rejecting $H _ { 0 }$
The first phrase implies that the experimenter is willing to assert the state of nature
specified by $H _ { 0 } ,$ while the second phrase implies that the experimenter really does not
believe $H _ { 0 }$ but does not have the evidence to reject it. For the most part, we will not
be concerned with these issues. We view $\mathrm { a }$ hypothesis testing problem as a problem
in which one of two actions is going to be taken-the actions being the assertion of
$H _ { 0 }$ or $H _ { 1 } .$

Typically, a hypothesis test is specified in terms of a test statistic $W \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$
= $W \left( X \right) ,$ a function of the sample. For example, a test might specify that $H _ { 0 }$ is to be
rejected if $\bar { X } ,$ the sample mean, is greater than 3. In this case $W \left( X \right) = \bar { X }$ is the test
statistic and the rejection region is $\left\{ \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) : \bar { x } > 3 \right\} .$ In Section 8.2, methods
of choosing test statistics and rejection regions are discussed. Criteria for evaluating
tests are introduced in Section 8.3. As with point estimators, the methods of finding
tests carry no guarantees; the tests they yield must be evaluated before their worth
is established.


## 8.2 Methods of Finding Tests

We will detail four methods of finding test procedures, procedures that are useful in
different situations and take advantage of different aspects of a problem. We start
with a very general method, one that is almost always applicable and is also optimal
in some cases.


### 8.2.1 Likelihood Ratio Tests

The likelihood ratio method of hypothesis testing is related to the maximum likelihood
estimators discussed in Section 7.2.2, and likelihood ratio tests are as widely applicable
as maximum likelihood estimation. Recall that if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a random sample from

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="375" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

a population with pdf or pmf $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ $\acute { e }$ may be a vector), the likelihood function is
defined as

$$L \left( \theta | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) = f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right) .$$

Let $\Theta$ denote the entire parameter space. Likelihood ratio tests are defined as follows.

Definition 8.2.1
The likelihood ratio test statistic for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ is

$$\lambda \left( x \right) = \frac { \sup _ { \theta } L \left( \theta | x \right) } { \sin p L \left( \theta | x \right) }$$

A likelihood ratio test (LRT) is any test that has a rejection region of the form $\left\{ \mathrm { x } : \lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right.$
☒
$\leq c ,$ where $c$ is any number satisfying $0 \leq c \leq 1 .$

The rationale behind LRTs may best be understood in the situation in which $f \left( x | \theta \right)$
is the pmf of a discrete random variable. In this case, the numerator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is the
☒
maximum probability of the observed sample, the maximum being computed over
parameters in the null hypothesis. (See Exercise 8.4.) The denominator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is
☒
the maximum probability of the observed sample over all possible parameters. The
ratio of these two maxima is small if there are parameter points in the alternative
hypothesis for which the observed sample is much more likely than for any parameter
point in the null hypothesis. In this situation, the LRT criterion says $H _ { 0 }$ should be
rejected and $H _ { 1 }$ accepted as true. Methods for selecting the number $c$ are discussed
in Section 8.3.

If we think of doing the maximization over both the entire parameter space (unre-
stricted maximization) and a subset of the parameter space (restricted maximization),
then the correspondence between LRTs and MLEs becomes more clear. Suppose $\widehat { \theta } ,$
an MLE of $\theta ,$ exists; $\widehat { \theta }$ is obtained by doing an unrestricted maximization of $L \left( \theta | x \right) .$
We can also consider the MLE of $\theta ,$ call it $\widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } ,$ obtained by doing a restricted max-
imization, assuming $\Theta _ { 0 }$ is the parameter space. That is, $\check { \theta } _ { 0 } = \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is the value of
☒
$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ that maximizes $L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) .$ Then, the LRT statistic is

$$\lambda \left( x \right) = \frac { L \left( \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } | x \right) } { L \left( \widehat { \theta } | x \right) }$$

Example 8.2.2 (Normal LRT)
Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a
$n \left( \theta , 1 \right)$ population. Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 } .$ Here $\theta _ { 0 }$ is a
number fixed by the experimenter prior to the experiment. Since there is only one
value of $\theta$ specified by $H _ { 0 } ,$ the numerator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is $L \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) .$ In Example 7.2.5 the
☒ ☒
(unrestricted) MLE of 0 was found to be $\bar { X } ,$ the sample mean. Thus the denominator
of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is $L \left( \bar { x } | \mathrm { x } \right) .$ So the LRT statistic is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="376" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

(8.2.1)
$$\lambda \left( x \right) = \frac { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left[ - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 \right] } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { - n / 2 } \exp \left[ - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 \right] }$$
$$= \exp \left[ \left( - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) / 2 \right] .$$

The expression for $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ can be simplified by noting that
☒

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } + n \left( \bar { x } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Thus the LRT statistic is

(8.2.2)
$$\lambda \left( x \right) = \exp \left[ - n \left( \bar { x } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 \right] .$$ ☒

An LRT is a test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for small values of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) .$ From (8.2.2), the rejection
☒
region, $\left\{ x : \lambda \left( x \right) \leq c \right\} ,$ can be written as
☒

$$\left\{ x : | \bar { x } - \theta _ { 0 } | \geq \sqrt { - 2 \left( \log c \right) / n } \right\} .$$

As $c$ ranges between 0 and 1, $\sqrt { - 2 \left( \log c \right) / n }$ ranges between 0 and $\infty .$ Thus the
LRTs are just those tests that reject $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ if the sample mean differs from the
hypothesized value $\theta _ { 0 }$ by more than a specified amount.

//

The analysis in Example 8.2.2 is typical in $\mathrm { t h a t }$ first the expression for $\lambda \left( X \right)$ from
☒
Definition 8.2.1 is found, as we did in (8.2.1). Then the description of the rejection
region is simplified, if possible, to an expression involving a simpler statistic, $| \bar { \bar { X } } - \theta _ { 0 } |$

☒
in the example.

Example 8.2.3 (Exponential LRT) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from
an exponential population with pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) } } & { x \geq \theta } \\ { 0 } & { x < \theta } \end{array} \right.$$

where $- \infty < \theta < \infty .$ The likelihood function is

$$L \left( \theta | x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { e ^ { - \Sigma x _ { i } + n \theta } } & { \theta \leq x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } \\ { 0 } & { \theta > x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } \end{array} \right.$$

$$\left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } = \min x _ { i } \right)$$

Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } ,$ where $\theta _ { 0 }$ is a value specified by the
☒
experimenter. Clearly $L \left( \theta | x \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta$ on $- \infty < \theta \leq x _ { \left( 1 \right) } .$ Thus,
☒
the denominator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ the unrestricted maximum of $L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ is
☒ ☒

$$L \left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } | x \right) = e ^ { - \sum x _ { i } + n x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } .$$ ☒

If $x _ { \left( 1 \right) } \leq \theta _ { 0 } ,$ the numerator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is also $L \left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } | x \right) .$ But since we are maximizing
☒

☒ ☒
$L \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right)$ over $\theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } ,$ the numerator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is $L \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right)$ if $x _ { \left( 1 \right) } > \theta _ { 0 } .$ Therefore, the
☒ ☒ ☒
likelihood ratio test statistic is

$$\lambda \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { x _ { \left( 1 \right) } \leq \theta _ { 0 } } \\ { e ^ { - n \left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) } } & { x _ { \left( 1 \right) } > \theta _ { 0 } } \end{array} \right.$$

Example 8.2.3 again illustrates the point, expressed in Section 7.2.2, that differ-

rather than on the sample $\mathrm { X }$ and its likelihood function $L \left( \theta | x \right) .$ Let $\lambda ^ { * } \left( t \right)$ denote

Theorem 8.2.4 If $T \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for 0 and $\lambda ^ { * } \left( t \right)$ and $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ are the
LRT statistics based on $T$ and $X ,$ respectively, then $\lambda ^ { * } \left( T \left( x \right) \right) = \lambda \left( x \right)$ for every $x$ in
the sample space.
