Example 8.2.5 (LRT and sufficiency) In Example 8.2.2, we can recognize that
$\bar { X }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ We could use the likelihood function associated with
$\bar { X } \left( \bar { X } \sim n \left( \theta , \frac { 1 } { n } \right) \right)$ to more easily reach the conclusion that a likelihood ratio test of
$H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 }$ rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for large values of $| \widetilde { X } - \theta _ { 0 } | .$
☒

Similarly in Example 8.2.3, $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } = \min X _ { i }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ The likeli-
☒
hood function of $X _ { \left( 1 \right) }$ (the pdf of $\left. X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right)$ is
☒

$$L ^ { * } \left( \theta | x _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { n e ^ { - n \left( x _ { \left( 1 \right) } - \theta \right) } } & { \theta \leq x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } \\ { 0 } & { \theta > x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } \end{array} \right.$$

This likelihood could also be used to derive the fact that a likelihood ratio test of
$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$ rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for large values of $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } .$
|

Likelihood ratio tests are also useful in situations where there are nuisance param-
eters, that $\mathrm { i s } ,$ parameters that are present in a model but are not of direct inferential
interest. The presence of such nuisance parameters does not affect the LRT construc-
tion method but, as might be expected, the presence of nuisance parameters might
lead to a different test.

Example 8.2.6 (Normal LRT with unknown variance) Suppose $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$
are a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and an experimenter is interested only in in-
ferences about $\mu ,$ such as testing $H _ { 0 } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu > \mu _ { 0 } .$ Then the parameter
$\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is a nuisance parameter. The LRT statistic is

$$\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \frac { \left\{ \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \geq 0 \right\} } { \left\{ \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } : - \infty < \mu < \infty , \sigma ^ { 2 } > 0 \right\} } L \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } \right)$$
$$= \frac { \left\{ \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \geq 0 \right\} } { L \left( \widehat { \mu } , \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } \right) } ,$$

where $\widehat { \mu }$ and $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ are the MLEs of $\mu$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ (see Example 7.2.11). Furthermore, if
$\widehat { \mu } \leq \mu _ { 0 } ,$ then the restricted maximum is the same as the unrestricted maximum,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="379" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

while if $\widehat { \mu } > \mu _ { 0 } ,$ the restricted maximum is $L \left( \mu _ { 0 } , \widehat { \sigma } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ where $\widehat { \sigma } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = \Sigma \left( x _ { i } - \mu _ { 0 } \right) ^ { 2 } / n .$
☒
Thus

$$\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \mathrm { i f } \widehat { \mu } \leq \mu _ { 0 } } \\ { \frac { L \left( \mu _ { 0 } , \widehat { \sigma } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } \right) } { L \left( \widehat { \mu } , \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } \right) } } & { \mathrm { i f } \widehat { \mu } > \mu _ { 0 } . \end{array} \right.$$

With some algebra, it can be shown that the test based on $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is equivalent to a test
II
☒
based on Student's t statistic. Details are left to Exercise 8.37. (Exercises 8.38-8.42
also deal with nuisance parameter problems.)


### 8.2.2 Bayesian Tests

Hypothesis testing problems may also be formulated in a Bayesian model. Recall
from Section 7.2.3 that a Bayesian model includes not only the sampling distribution
$f \left( x | \theta \right)$ but also the prior distribution $\pi \left( \theta \right) ,$ with the prior distribution reflecting the
experimenter's opinion about the parameter $\theta$ prior to sampling.

The Bayesian paradigm prescribes that the sample information be combined with
the prior information using Bayes' Theorem to obtain the posterior distribution
$\pi \left( \theta | x \right) .$ All inferences about $\theta$ are now based on the posterior distribution.

In a hypothesis testing problem, the posterior distribution may be used to calculate
the probabilities that $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 }$ are true. Remember, $\pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right)$ is a probability distri-
☒
bution for a random variable. Hence, the posterior probabilities $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } | x \right) = P \left( H _ { 0 } \right.$
☒
is $\left. \mathrm { t r u e } | \mathrm { x } \right)$ and $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } | x \right) = P \left( H _ { 1 } \text { is true } | x \right)$ may be computed.
☒

The probabilities $P \left( H _ { 0 } \right.$ is $\left. \mathrm { t r u e } | \mathrm { x } \right)$ and $P \left( H _ { 1 } \right.$ is true|x) are not meaningful to the
☒ ☒
classical statistician. The classical statistician considers $\theta \mathrm { t o }$ be a fixed number. Con-
sequently, a hypothesis is either true or false. If $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ $P \left( H _ { 0 } \text { is true } | x \right) = 1$ and
☒
$P \left( H _ { 1 } \text { is true } | x \right) = 0$ for all values of $x .$ If $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ these values are reversed. Since
these probabilities are unknown (since $\theta$ is unknown) and do not depend on the sam-
ple $\mathrm { x } ,$ they are not used by the classical statistician. In a Bayesian formulation of a
hypothesis testing problem, these probabilities depend on the sample $\mathrm { x }$ and can give
useful information about the veracity of $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 } .$

One way a Bayesian hypothesis tester may choose to use the posterior distribution
is to decide to accept $H _ { 0 }$ as true if $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } | X \right) \geq P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } | X \right)$ and to reject $H _ { 0 }$
☒ ☒ ☒
otherwise. In the terminology of the previous sections, the test statistic, a function
of the sample, is $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } | X \right)$ and the rejection region is $\left\{ x : P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } | x \right) > \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right\} .$
☒
Alternatively, if the Bayesian hypothesis tester wishes to guard against falsely reject-
ing $H _ { 0 } ,$ he may decide to reject $H _ { 0 }$ only if $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } | \mathrm { X } \right)$ is greater than some large
number, .99 for example.
☒

Example 8.2.7 (Normal Bayesian test) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and
let the prior distribution on $\theta$ be $n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\mu ,$ and $\tau ^ { 2 }$ are known. Consider
testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$ From Example 7.2.16, the posterior $\pi \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right)$ is
normal with mean $\left( n \tau ^ { 2 } \bar { x } + \sigma ^ { 2 } \mu \right) / \left( n \tau ^ { 2 } + \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } \tau ^ { 2 } / \left( n \tau ^ { 2 } + \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$

If we decide to accept $H _ { 0 }$ if and only if $P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } | X \right) \geq P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } | X \right) ,$ then we will

☒ ☒
accept $H _ { 0 }$ if and only if

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 } \leq P \left( \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } | X \right) = P \left( \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } | X \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="380" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

Since $\pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right)$ is symmetric, this is true if and only if the mean of $\pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right)$ is less $t h a n$ .
☒ ☒
or equal to $\theta _ { 0 } .$ Therefore $H _ { 0 }$ will be accepted as true if

$$\bar { X } \leq \theta _ { 0 } + \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \theta _ { 0 } - \mu \right) } { n \tau ^ { 2 } }$$

and $H _ { 1 }$ will be accepted as true otherwise. In particular, if $\mu = \theta _ { 0 }$ so that prior
to experimentation probability $\frac { 1 } { 2 }$ is assigned to both $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 } ,$ then $H _ { 0 }$ will be
accepted as true if $\bar { x } \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 }$ accepted otherwise.
11

Other methods that use the posterior distribution to make inferences in hypothesis
testing problems are discussed in Section 8.3.5.


### 8.2.3 Union-Intersection and Intersection-Union Tests

In some situations, tests for complicated null hypotheses can be developed from tests
for simpler null hypotheses. We discuss two related methods.

The union-intersection method of test construction might be useful when the null
hypothesis is conveniently expressed as an intersection, say

(8.2.3)
$$H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma } .$$

Here $\Gamma$ is an arbitrary index set that $m a y$ be finite or infinite, depending on the
problem. Suppose that tests are available for each of the problems of testing $H _ { 0 \gamma } : \theta \in$
$\Theta _ { \gamma }$ versus $H _ { 1 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma } ^ { \mathrm { c } } .$ Say the rejection region for the test of $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ is $\left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \in R _ { \gamma } \right\} .$
☒
Then the rejection region for the union-intersection test is

(8.2.4)
$$\bigcup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \in R _ { \gamma } \right\} .$$

The rationale is simple. If any one of the hypotheses $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ is rejected, then $H _ { 0 } ,$ which,
by (8.2.3), is true only if $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ is true for every $\gamma ,$ must also be rejected. Only if each of
the hypotheses $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ is $a c c e p t e d$ as true will the intersection $H _ { 0 }$ be accepted as true.

In some situations a simple expression for the rejection region of a union-intersection
test can be found. In particular, suppose that each of the individual tests has a rejec-
tion region of the form $\left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) > c \right\} ,$ where $c$ does not depend on $\gamma .$ The rejection
region for the union-intersection test, given in (8.2.4), can be expressed as

$$\bigcup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) > c \right\} = \left\{ \mathrm { x } : \sup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) > c \right\} .$$ ☒

Thus the test statistic for testing $H _ { 0 }$ is $T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \sup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) .$ Some examples in which
$T \left( x \right)$ has a simple formula may be found in Chapter 11.
☒

Example 8.2.8 (Normal union-intersection test) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a ran-
dom sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq$
$\mu _ { 0 } ,$ where $\mu _ { 0 }$ is a specified number. We can write $H _ { 0 }$ as the intersection of two sets,

$$H _ { 0 } : \left\{ \mu : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 } \right\} \cap \left\{ \mu : \mu \geq \mu _ { 0 } \right\} .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="381" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.2" -->

The LRT of $H _ { 0 L } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 L } : \mu > \mu _ { 0 }$ is

reject $H _ { 0 L } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 }$ in favor of $H _ { 1 L } : \mu > \mu _ { 0 }$ if
$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq t _ { L }$
(see Exercise 8.37). Similarly, the LRT of $H _ { 0 \mathrm { U } } : \mu \geq \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } u : \mu < \mu _ { 0 }$ is
reject $H _ { 0 } u : \mu \geq \mu _ { 0 }$ in favor of $H _ { 1 U } : \mu < \mu _ { 0 }$ if
$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \leq t _ { \mathrm { U } } .$

Thus the union-intersection test of $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq \mu _ { 0 }$ formed from these
two LRTs is

$$H _ { 0 } \text { if } \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq t _ { \text { L } } \text { or } \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \leq t _ { U }$$

If $t _ { L } = - t _ { U } \geq 0 ,$ the union-intersection test can be more simply expressed as

$$\mathrm { r e j e c t } H _ { 0 } \text { if } \frac { | \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } | } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq t _ { L }$$

It turns out that this union-intersection test is also the LRT for this problem (see
Exercise 8.38) and is called the two-sided $t$ test.

|

The union-intersection method of test construction is useful if the null hypothesis
is conveniently expressed as an intersection. Another method, the intersection-union
method, may be useful if the null hypothesis is conveniently expressed as a union.
Suppose we wish to test the null hypothesis

(8.2.5)
$$H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \bigcup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma } .$$

Suppose that for each $\gamma \in \Gamma ,$ $\left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \in R _ { \gamma } \right\}$ is the rejection region for a test of
$H _ { 0 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma }$ versus $H _ { 1 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma } ^ { c } .$ Then the rejection region for the intersection-union
test of $H _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 }$ is

(8.2.6)
$$\bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \in R _ { \gamma } \right\} .$$

From (8.2.5), $H _ { 0 }$ is false if and only if all of the $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ are false, so $H _ { 0 }$ can be rejected
if and only if each of the individual hypotheses $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ can be rejected. Again, the test
can be greatly simplified if the rejection regions for the individual hypotheses are all
of the form $\left\{ x : T _ { \gamma } \left( x \right) \geq c \right\}$ ${ } ^ { ^ { \prime } } c$ independent of y). In such cases, the rejection region
for $H _ { 0 }$ is

$$\bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \left\{ \mathrm { x } : T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \geq c \right\} = \left\{ \mathrm { x } : \inf _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \geq c \right\} .$$

Here, the intersection-union test statistic is $\inf _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } T _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { X } \right) ,$ and the test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for
large values of this statistic.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="382" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Example 8.2.9 (Acceptance sampling) The topic of acceptance sampling pro-
vides an extremely useful application of an intersection-union test, as this example
will illustrate. (See Berger 1982 for a more detailed treatment of this problem.)

Two parameters that are important in assessing the quality of upholstery fabric
are $\theta _ { 1 } ,$ the mean breaking strength, and $\theta _ { 2 } ,$ the probability of passing a flammability
test. Standards may dictate that $\theta _ { 1 }$ should be over 50 pounds and $\theta _ { 2 }$ should be over
.95, and the fabric is acceptable only if it meets both of these standards. This can be
modeled with the hypothesis test

$$H _ { 0 } : \left\{ \theta _ { 1 } \leq 5 0 \text { or } \theta _ { 2 } \leq . 9 5 \right\}$$
$$H _ { 1 } : \left\{ \theta _ { 1 } > 5 0 \text { and } \theta _ { 2 } > . 9 5 \right\} ,$$
versus

where a batch of material is acceptable only if $H _ { 1 }$ is accepted.

Suppose $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are measurements of breaking strength for $n$ samples and
are assumed to be id $n \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ The LRT of $H _ { 0 1 } :$ $\theta _ { 1 } \leq 5 0$ will reject $H _ { 0 1 }$ if $\left( \overrightarrow { X } - \right.$
$\left. 5 0 \right) / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right) > t .$ Suppose that we also have the results of $m$ flammability tests,
denoted by $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m } ,$ where $Y _ { i } = 1$ if the $i$ ith sample passes the test and $Y _ { i } = 0$
otherwise. If $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ are modeled as iid Bernoulli(02) random variables, the LRT
will reject $H _ { 0 2 } : \theta _ { 2 } \leq . 9 5$ if $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } Y _ { i } > b$ (see Exercise 8.3). Putting all of this together,
the rejection region for the intersection-union test is given by

$$\left\{ \left( x , y \right) : \frac { \bar { x } - 5 0 } { s / \sqrt { n } } > t \text { and } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } y _ { i } > b \right\}$$

Thus the intersection-union test decides the product is acceptable, that is, $H _ { 1 }$ is true,
if and only if it decides that each of the individual parameters meets its standard, that
$\mathrm { i s } ,$ $H _ { 1 i }$ is true. If more than two parameters define a product's quality, individual tests
for each parameter can be combined, by means of the intersection-union method, to
yield an overall test of the product's quality.
Il


## 8.3 Methods of Evaluating Tests

In deciding to accept or reject the null hypothesis $H _ { 0 } ,$ an experimenter might be
making a mistake. Usually, hypothesis tests are evaluated and compared through
their probabilities of making mistakes. In this section we discuss how these error
probabilities can be controlled. In some cases, it can even be determined which tests
have the smallest possible error probabilities.


### 8.3.1 Error Probabilities and the Power Function

$A$ hypothesis test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c }$ might make one of two types
of errors. These two types of errors traditionally have been given the non-mnemonic
names, Type I Error and Type II Error. If $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ but the hypothesis test incorrectly
decides to reject $H _ { 0 } ,$ then the test has made a Type I Error. If, on the other hand,
$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ but the test decides to accept $H _ { 0 } ,$ a Type II Error has been made. These two
different situations are depicted in Table 8.3.1.

Suppose $R$ denotes the rejection region for a test. $T h e n$ for $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ the test will
make a mistake if $\mathrm { x } \in R ,$ so the probability of a Type I Error is $P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) .$ For

<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ the probability of $a$ Type II Error is $P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R ^ { c } \right) .$ This switching from $R$ to
$R ^ { c }$ is a bit confusing, but, if we realize that $P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R ^ { c } \right) = 1 - P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) ,$ then the
function of $\theta$ $, P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) ,$ contains all the information about the test with rejection
region $R .$ We have

$$P _ { \theta } \left( \mathrm { X } \in R \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \text { probability of a Type I Error } \\ \text { one minus the probability of a T } \end{array} \right.$$
$$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } .$$
if A E OO
Type II Error
if

This consideration leads to the following definition.

Definition 8.3.1 The power function of a hypothesis test with rejection region $R$
is the function of $\theta$ defined by $\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) .$

The ideal power function is 0 for all $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and 1 for all $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } .$ Except in trivial
situations, this ideal cannot be attained. Qualitatively, a good test has power function
near 1 for most $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ and near 0 for most $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$

Example 8.3.2 (Binomial power function) Let $X \sim \text { binomial } \left( 5 , \theta \right) .$ Consider
testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ Consider first the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if and
only if all "successes" are observed. The power function for this test is

$$\beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = 5 \right) = \theta ^ { 5 } .$$

The graph of $\beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right)$ is in Figure 8.3.1. In examining this power function, we might
decide that although the probability of a Type I Error is acceptably low $\left( \beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) \leq \right.$
$\left. \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 5 } = . 0 3 1 2 \right)$ for all $\theta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ the probability of a Type $I I$ Error is too high $\left( \beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) \right.$ is
too small) for most $\theta > \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ The probability of a Type $I I$ Error is less than $\frac { 1 } { 2 }$ only if
$\theta > \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 5 } = . 8 7 .$ To achieve smaller Type II Error probabilities, we might consider
using the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $X = 3 ,$ 4, or 5. The power function for this test is

$$\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X = 3 , 4 , \mathrm { o r } 5 \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 5 \\ 3 \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { 3 } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \begin{array}{} 5 \\ 4 \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { 4 } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 1 } + \left( \begin{array}{} 5 \\ 5 \end{array} \right) \theta ^ { 5 } \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 0 } .$$

The graph of $\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right)$ is also in Figure 8.3.1. It can be seen in Figure 8.3.1 that the
second test has achieved a smaller Type II Error probability in that $\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right)$ is larger for
$\theta > \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ But the Type $I$ Error probability is larger for the second test; $\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right)$ is larger
for $\theta \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ If $a$ choice is to be made between these two tests, the researcher must
decide which error structure, that described by $\beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right)$ or that described by $\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right) ,$ is
more acceptable.

Example 8.3.3 (Normal power function) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample
from $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. An LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$ is a
test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\left( \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) > c$ (see Exercise 8.37). The constant $c$ can
be any positive number. The power function of this test is

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } > c \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \theta } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } > c + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$= P \left( Z > c + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$

where $Z$ is a standard normal random variable, since $\left( \bar { X } - \theta \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ $\mathrm { A s } \theta$
increases from $- \infty$ to $\infty ,$ it is easy to see that this normal probability increases from
0 to 1. Therefore, it follows that $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta ,$ with

$$\lim _ { \theta \rightarrow - \infty } \beta \left( \theta \right) = 0 , \quad \lim _ { \theta \rightarrow \infty } \beta \left( \theta \right) = 1 , \quad \text { and } \quad \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha$$
$P \left( Z > c \right) = \alpha .$

A graph of $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ for $c = 1 . 2 8$ is given in Figure 8.3.2.
I/

Typically, the power function of a test will depend on the sample size n. If $n$ can be
chosen by the experimenter, consideration of the power function might help determine
what sample size is appropriate in an experiment.

Example 8.3.4 (Continuation of Example 8.3.3) Suppose the experimenter
wishes to have a maximum Type I Error probability of .1. Suppose, in addition, the
experimenter wishes to have a maximum Type II Error probability of .2 if $\theta \geq \theta _ { 0 } + \sigma .$
We now show how to choose $c$ and $n$ to achieve these goals, using a test that rejects
$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ if $\left( \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) > c .$ As noted above, the power function of such a
test is

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P \left( Z > c + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$

Because $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is increasing in $\theta ,$ the requirements will be met if

$$\beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = . 1 \quad \text { and } \quad \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } + \sigma \right) = . 8 .$$

By choosing $c = 1 . 2 8 ,$ we achieve $\beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = P \left( Z > 1 . 2 8 \right) = . 1 ,$ regardless of n. Now we
wish to choose $n$ so that $\beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } + \sigma \right) = P \left( Z > 1 . 2 8 - \sqrt { n } \right) = . 8 .$ But, $P \left( Z > - . 8 4 \right) = . 8 .$
So setting $1 . 2 8 - \sqrt { n } = - . 8 4$ and solving for $n$ yield $n = 4 . 4 9 .$ Of course $n$ must be an
integer. So choosing $c = 1 . 2 8$ and $n = 5$ yield a test with error probabilities controlled
as specified by the experimenter.
11

For a fixed sample size, it is usually impossible to make both types of error proba-
bilities arbitrarily small. In searching for a good test, it is common to restrict consid-
eration to tests that control the Type I Error probability at a specified level. Within
this class of tests we then search for tests that have Type II Error probability that
is as small as possible. The following two terms are useful when discussing tests that
control Type I Error probabilities.

Definition 8.3.5 For $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$ a test with power function $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is a size $\alpha$ test
if $\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( \theta \right) = \alpha .$

Definition 8.3.6 For $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$ a test with power function $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is a level $\alpha$ test
if $\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( \theta \right) \leq \alpha .$

Some authors do not make che distinction between the terms size and level that
we have made, and sometimes these terms are used interchangeably. But according
to our definitions, the set of level $\alpha$ tests contains the set of size $\alpha$ tests. Moreover,
the distinction becomes important in complicated models and complicated testing
situations, where it is often computationally impossible to construct a size $\alpha$ test. In
such situations, an experimenter must be satisfied with a level $\alpha$ test, realizing that
some compromises may be made. We will see some examples, especially in conjunction
with union-intersection and intersection-union tests.

Experimenters commonly specify the level of the test they wish to use, with typical
choices being $\alpha = . 0 1 ,$ .05, and .10. Be aware that, in fixing the level of the test, the
experimenter is controlling only the Type I Error probabilities, not the Type II Error.
If this approach is taken, the experimenter should specify the null and alternative
hypotheses so that it is most important to control the Type I Error probability.
For example, suppose an experimenter expects an experiment to give support to a
particular hypothesis, but she does not wish to make the assertion unless the data

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="386" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

really do give convincing support. The test can be set up so that the alternative
hypothesis is the one that she expects the data to support, and hopes to prove. (The
alternative hypothesis is sometimes called the research hypothesis in this context.)
By using a level $\alpha$ test with small $\alpha ,$ the experimenter is guarding against saying the
data support the research hypothesis when it is false.

The methods of Section 8.2 usually yield test statistics and general forms for rejec-
tion regions. However, they do not generally lead to one specific test. For example, an
LRT (Definition 8.2.1) is one that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\lambda \left( X \right) \leq c ,$ but $c$ was unspecified, so
☒
not one but an entire class of LRTs is defined, one for each value of c. The restriction
to size $\alpha$ tests may now lead to the choice of one out of the class of tests.

Example 8.3.7 (Size of LRT) In general, a size & LRT is constructed by choosing
$c$ such that $\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( \lambda \left( X \right) \leq c \right) = \alpha .$ How that $c$ is determined depends on the
particular problem. For example, in Example 8.2.2, $\Theta _ { 0 }$ consists of the single point
$\theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ and $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ if $\theta = \theta _ { 0 } .$ So the test

$$H _ { 0 } \text { if } | \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } | \geq z _ { \alpha / 2 } / \sqrt { n } ,$$

where $z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ satisfies $P \left( Z \geq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \right) = \alpha / 2$ with $Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ is the size & LRT. Specif-
ically, this corresponds to choosing $c = \exp \left( - z _ { \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) ,$ but this is not an important
point.

For the problem described in Example 8.2.3, finding a size $\alpha$ LRT is complicated
by the fact that the null hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ consists of more than one point. The
LRT rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c ,$ where $c$ is chosen so that this is a size $\alpha$ test. But if
$c = \left( - \log \alpha \right) / n + \theta _ { 0 } ,$ then

$$P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c \right) = e ^ { - n \left( c - \theta _ { 0 } \right) } = \alpha .$$

Since $\theta$ location parameter for $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } ,$

$$P _ { \theta } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c \right) \leq P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c \right) \quad \text { for any } \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } .$$

Thus

$$\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( \theta \right) = \sup _ { \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \geq c \right) = \alpha$$

and this $c$ yields the size & LRT.

A note on notation: In the above example we used the notation $z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ to denote the
point having probability $\alpha / 2$ to the right of it for a standard normal pdf. We will
use this notation in general, not just for the normal but for other distributions as
well (defining what we need to for clarity's sake). For example, the point $z _ { \alpha }$ satisfies
$P \left( Z > z _ { \alpha } \right) = \alpha ,$ where $Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ;$ $t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 }$ satisfies $P \left( T _ { n - 1 } > t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } \right) = \alpha / 2 ,$
where $T _ { n - 1 } \sim t _ { n - 1 }$ and $\chi _ { p , 1 - \alpha } ^ { 2 }$ satisfies $P \left( \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } > \chi _ { p , 1 - \alpha } ^ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \alpha ,$ where $\chi _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ is a chi
squared random variable with $p$ degrees of freedom. Points like $z _ { \alpha / 2 } ,$ $z _ { \alpha } ,$ $t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } ,$ and
$\chi _ { p , 1 - \alpha } ^ { 2 }$ are known as cutoff points.

Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="387" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Example 8.3.8 (Size of union-intersection test)
The problem of finding a
size $\alpha$ union-intersection test in Example 8.2.8 involves finding constants $t _ { L }$ and $t _ { \mathrm { U } }$
such that

$$\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n } } \geq t _ { \mathrm { L } } \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n } } \leq t _ { \mathrm { U } } \right) = \alpha .$$

But for any $\left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ $\mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ and thus $\left( \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } \right) / \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n }$ has a Student's
☒
$t$ distribution with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom. So any choice of $t _ { U } = t _ { n - 1 , 1 - \alpha _ { 1 } }$ and
$t _ { L } = t _ { n - 1 , \alpha _ { 2 } } ,$ with $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha ,$ will yield a test with Type I Error probability of
exactly $\alpha$ for all $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ The usual choice is $t _ { \mathrm { L } } = - t _ { \mathrm { U } } = t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } .$
I/

Other than $\alpha$ levels, there are other features of a test that might also be of concern.
For example, we would like a test to be more likely to reject $H _ { 0 }$ if $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ than if
$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ All of the power functions in Figures 8.3.1 and 8.3.2 have this property,
yielding tests that are called unbiased.

Definition 8.3.9 A test with power function $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is unbiased if $\beta \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) \geq \beta \left( \theta ^ { \prime \prime } \right)$ for
every $\theta ^ { \prime } \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ and $\theta ^ { \prime \prime } \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$

Example 8.3.10 (Conclusion of Example 8.3.3) An LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$ has power function

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P \left( Z > c + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$

where $Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Since $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta$ (for fixed $\theta _ { 0 } ,$ it follows
that

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) > \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \max _ { t \leq \theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( t \right) \quad \text { for all } \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$$

and, hence, that the test is unbiased.

In most problems there are many unbiased tests. (See Exercise 8.45.) Likewise,
there are many size $\alpha$ tests, likelihood ratio tests, etc. In some cases we have imposed
enough restrictions to narrow consideration to one test. For the two problems in
Example 8.3.7, there is only one size $\alpha$ likelihood ratio test. In other cases there
remain many tests from which to choose. We discussed only the one that rejects
$H _ { 0 }$ for large values of $T .$ In the following sections we will discuss other criteria for
selecting one out of a class of tests, criteria that are all related to the power functions
of the tests.


### 8.3.2 Most Powerful Tests

In previous sections we have described various classes of hypothesis tests. Some of
these classes control the probability of a Type I Error; for example, level $\alpha$ tests have
Type I Error probabilities at most $\alpha$ for all $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ $A$ good test in such a class would

!!

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="388" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

also have a small Type II Error probability, that is, a large power function for $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } .$
If one test had a smaller Type II Error probability than all other tests in the class, it
would certainly be a strong contender for the best test in the class, a notion that is
formalized in the next definition.

Definition 8.3.11 Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of tests for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \in$
$\Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } .$ A test in class $\mathcal{C} ,$ with power function $\beta \left( \theta \right) ,$ is a uniformly most powerful (UMP)
class $\mathcal{C}$ test if $\beta \left( \theta \right) \geq \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right)$ for every $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c }$ and every $\beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right)$ that is a power function
of a test in class $\mathcal{C} .$

In this section, the class $\mathcal{C}$ will be the class of all level $\alpha$ tests. The test described
in Definition 8.3.11 is then called a UMP level $\alpha$ test. For this test to be interesting,
restriction to the class $\mathcal{C}$ must involve some restriction on the Type I Error probability.
$A$ minimization of the Type II Error probability without some control of the Type
I Error probability is not very interesting. (For example, a test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ with
probability 1 will never make a Type II Error. See Exercise 8.16.)

The requirements in Definition 8.3.11 are so strong that UMP tests do not exist in
many realistic problems. But in problems that have UMP tests, a UMP test might
well be considered the best test in the class. Thus, we would like to be able to identify
UMP tests if they exist. The following famous theorem clearly describes which tests
are UMP level $\alpha$ tests in the situation where the null and alternative hypotheses both
consist of only one probability distribution for the sample (that is, when both $H _ { 0 }$ and
$H _ { 1 }$ are simple hypotheses).

Theorem 8.3.12 (Neyman-Pearson Lemma) Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = \theta _ { 1 } ,$ where the pdf or pmf corresponding to $\theta _ { i }$ is $f \left( x | \theta _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 0 , 1 ,$ using
$a$ test with rejection region $R$ that satisfies

$$x \in R \text { if } f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) > k f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$
(8.3.1)
and

$$\mathrm { x } \in R ^ { \mathrm { c } } \mathrm { i f } f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) < k f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$$

for some $k \geq 0 ,$ and

(8.3.2)
$$\alpha = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X \in R \right) .$$

Then

a. (Sufficiency) Any test that satisfies (8.3.1) and (8.3.2) is a UMP level $\alpha$ test.

b. (Necessity) If there exists a test satisfying (8.3.1) and $\left( 8 . 3 . 2 \right)$ with $k > 0 ,$ then
every UMP level a test is a size a test (satisfies $\left. \left( 8 . 3 . 2 \right) \right)$ and every $U M P$ level $\alpha$ test
satisfies (8.3.1) except perhaps on a set $A$ satisfying $P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X \in A \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \left( X \in A \right) =$
0.

Proof: We will prove the theorem for the case that $f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ and $f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right)$ are pdfs of
continuous random variables. The proof for discrete random variables can be accom-
plished by replacing integrals with sums. (See Exercise 8.21.)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="389" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Note first that any test satisfying (8.3.2) is a size $\alpha$ and, hence, a level $\alpha$ test
because $\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X \in R \right) = \alpha ,$ since $\Theta _ { 0 }$ has only one point.

To ease notation, we define a test function, a function on the sample space that
is 1 if $\mathrm { x } \in R$ and 0 if $\mathrm { x } \in R ^ { c } .$ That is, it is the indicator function of the rejection
region. Let $\phi \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ be the test function of a test satisfying $\left( 8 . 3 . 1 \right)$ and (8.3.2). Let $\phi ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$
be the test function of any other level $\alpha$ test, and let $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ and $\beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right)$ be the power
functions corresponding to the tests $\phi$ and $\phi ^ { \prime } ,$ respectively. Because $0 \leq \phi ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \leq 1 ,$
(8.3.1) implies that $\left( \phi \left( \mathrm { x } \right) - \phi ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) \left( f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) - k f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) \geq 0$ for every $\mathrm { x }$ (since $\phi = 1$ if
$f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) > k f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ and $\phi = 0$ if $\left. f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) < k f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$ Thus

(8.3.3)
$$0 \leq \int \left[ \phi \left( \mathrm { x } \right) - \phi ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right] \left[ f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) - k f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right] d \mathrm { x }$$
$$= \beta \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - k \left( \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) .$$

Statement $\left( \mathrm { a } \right)$ is proved by noting that, since $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is a level $\alpha$ test and $\phi$ is a size $\alpha$
test, $\beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \geq 0 .$ Thus (8.3.3) and $k \geq 0$ imply that

$$0 \leq \beta \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - k \left( \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right) \leq \beta \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) ,$$

showing that $\beta \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) \geq \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right)$ and hence $\phi$ has greater power $\phi .$ Since $\phi ^ { \prime }$ was an
arbitrary level $\alpha$ test and $\theta _ { 1 }$ is the only point in $\Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ $\phi$ is a UMP level $\alpha$ test.

To prove statement (b), let $\phi ^ { \prime }$ now be the test function for any UMP level $\alpha$ test.
By part (a), $\phi ,$ the test satisfying (8.3.1) and $\left( 8 . 3 . 2 \right) ,$ is also a UMP level $\alpha$ test, thus
$\beta \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) = \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) .$ This fact, (8.3.3), and $k > 0$ imply

$$\alpha - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) - \beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq 0 .$$

Now, since $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is a level $\alpha$ test, $\beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq \alpha .$ Thus $\beta ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha ,$ that is, $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is a size $\alpha$
test, and this also implies that (8.3.3) is an equality in this case. But the nonnegative
integrand $\left( \phi \left( \mathrm { x } \right) - \phi ^ { \prime } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) \left( f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) - k f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right)$ will have a zero integral only if $\phi ^ { \prime }$ satisfies
(8.3.1), except perhaps on a set $A$ with $\int _ { A } f \left( x | \theta _ { i } \right) d x = 0 .$ This implies that the last
assertion in statement (b) is true.
☐

The following corollary connects the Neyman-Pearson Lemma to sufficiency.

Corollary 8.3.13 Consider the hypothesis problem posed in Theorem 8.3.12. $S u p -$
pose $T \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta$ and $g \left( t | \theta _ { i } \right)$ is the pdf or pmf of $T$ corresponding
to $\theta _ { i } ,$ $i = 0 , 1 .$ Then any test based on $T$ with rejection region $S$ (a subset of the sample
space of $\left. T \right)$ is a $U M P$ level $\alpha$ test $i f$ $i t$ satisfies

$$t \in S \quad \text { if } g \left( t | \theta _ { 1 } \right) > k g \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$

(8.3.4)
and
$$t \in S ^ { \mathrm { c } } \quad \mathrm { i f } \quad g \left( t | \theta _ { 1 } \right) < k g \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$$

for some $k \geq 0 ,$ where

(8.3.5)
$$\alpha = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( T \in S \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="390" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Proof: In terms of the original sample $\mathrm { X } ,$ the test based on $T$ has the rejection
region $R = \left\{ x : T \left( x \right) \in S \right\} .$ By the Factorization Theorem, the pdf or pmf of $\mathrm { X }$ can
be written as $f \left( x | \theta _ { i } \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta _ { i } \right) h \left( x \right) ,$ $i = 0 , 1 ,$ for some nonnegative function $h \left( x \right) .$
Multiplying the inequalities in (8.3.4) by this nonnegative function, we see that $R$
satisfies

$$\mathrm { x } \in R \mathrm { i f } f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) = g \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta _ { 1 } \right) h \left( \mathrm { x } \right) > k g \left( T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) | \theta _ { 0 } \right) h \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = k f \left( \mathrm { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$

and

$x \in R ^ { c }$ if
$$f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) = g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta _ { 1 } \right) h \left( x \right) < k g \left( T \left( x \right) | \theta _ { 0 } \right) h \left( x \right) = k f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right) .$$

Also, by (8.3.5),

$$P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X \in R \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( T \left( X \right) \in S \right) = \alpha .$$

So, by the sufficiency part of the Neyman-Pearson Lemma, the test based on $T$ is a
UMP level $\alpha$ test.
☐

When we derive a test that satisfies the inequalities (8.3.1) or $\left( 8 . 3 . 4 \right) ,$ and hence is a
UMP level $\alpha$ test, it is usually easier to rewrite the inequalities as $f \left( x | \theta _ { 1 } \right) / f \left( x | \theta _ { 0 } \right) > k .$
(We must be careful about dividing by 0.) This method is used in the following
examples.

Example 8.3.14 (UMP binomial test) Let $X \sim \text { binomial } \left( 2 , \theta \right) .$ We want $t o$
test $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = \frac { 3 } { 4 } .$ Calculating the ratios of the pmfs gives

f(0|0 = 3) _1
f(1)0 3) _3

$$\frac { f \left( 2 | \theta = \frac { 3 } { 4 } \right) } { f \left( 2 | \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 9 } { 4 }$$
( Ole = = ) - 4' F (110 = ) - 4' and

If we choose $\frac { 3 } { 4 } < k < \frac { 9 } { 4 } ,$ the Neyman-Pearson Lemma says that the test that rejects
$H _ { 0 }$ if $X = 2$ is the UMP level $\alpha = P \left( X = 2 | \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 4 }$ test. If we choose $\frac { 1 } { 4 } < k < \frac { 3 } { 4 } ,$
the Neyman-Pearson Lemma says that the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $X = 1$ or 2 is the
UMP level $\alpha = P \left( X = 1 \right.$ or $\left. 2 | \theta = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) = \frac { 3 } { 4 }$ test. Choosing $k < \frac { 1 } { 4 }$ or $k > \frac { 9 } { 4 }$ yields the
UMP level $\alpha = 1$ or level $\alpha = 0$ test.

Note that if $k = \frac { 3 } { 4 } ,$ then (8.3.1) says we must reject $H _ { 0 }$ for the sample point $x = 2$
and accept $H _ { 0 }$ for $x = 0$ but leaves our action for $x = 1$ undetermined. But if we
accept $H _ { 0 }$ for $x =$ 1, we get the UMP level $\alpha = \frac { 1 } { 4 }$ test as above. If we reject $H _ { 0 }$ for
$x = 1 ,$ we get the UMP level $\alpha = \frac { 3 } { 4 }$ test as above.
|

Example 8.3.14 also shows that for a discrete distribution, the $\alpha$ level at which a
test can be done is a function of the particular pmf with which we are dealing. (No
such problem arises in the continuous case. Any $\alpha$ level can be attained.)

Example 8.3.15 (UMP normal test) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from
a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. The sample mean $\bar { X }$ is a sufficient statistic for $\theta .$
Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = \theta _ { 1 } ,$ where $\theta _ { 0 } > \theta _ { 1 } .$ The inequality (8.3.4),
$g \left( \bar { x } | \theta _ { 1 } \right) > k g \left( \bar { x } | \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$ is equivalent to

$$\bar { x } < \frac { \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \log k \right) / n - \theta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + \theta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \left( \theta _ { 1 } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="391" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

The fact that $\theta _ { 1 } - \theta _ { 0 } < 0$ was used to obtain this inequality. The right-hand side
increases from $- \infty$ to $\infty$ as $k$ increases from 0 to co. Thus, by Corollary 8.3.13, the
test with rejection region $\bar { x } < c$ is the UMP level $\alpha$ test, where $\alpha = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( X < c \right) .$ If $a$
particular $\alpha$ is specified, then the UMP test rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\bar { X } < c = - \sigma z _ { \alpha } / \sqrt { n } + \theta _ { 0 } .$
This choice of $c$ ensures that (8.3.5) is true.

Hypotheses, such as $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 }$ in the Neyman-Pearson Lemma, that specify only
one possible distribution for the sample $X$ are called simple hypotheses. In most real-
istic problems, the hypotheses of interest specify more than one possible distribution
for the sample. Such hypotheses are called composite hypotheses. Since Definition
8.3.11 requires a UMP test to be most powerful against each individual $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } ,$
the Neyman-Pearson Lemma can be used to find UMP tests in problems involving
composite hypotheses.

In particular, hypotheses that assert that a univariate parameter is large, for exam-
ple, $H : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 } ,$ or small, for example, $H : \theta < \theta _ { 0 } ,$ are called one-sided hypotheses. Hy-
potheses that assert that a parameter is either large or small, for example, $H : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 } ,$
are called two-sided hypotheses. $A$ large class of problems that admit UMP level $\alpha$
tests involve one-sided hypotheses and pdfs or pmfs with the monotone likelihood
ratio property.

Definition 8.3.16 A family of pdfs or pmfs $\left\{ g \left( t | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ for a univariate random
variable $T$ with real-valued parameter $\theta$ has a monotone likelihood ratio (MLR) if,
for every $\theta _ { 2 } > \theta _ { 1 } ,$ $g \left( t | \theta _ { 2 } \right) / g \left( t | \theta _ { 1 } \right)$ is a monotone (nonincreasing or nondecreasing)
function of $t$ on $\left\{ t : g \left( t | \theta _ { 1 } \right) > 0 \right.$ or $\left. g \left( t | \theta _ { 2 } \right) > 0 \right\} .$ Note that $c / 0$ is defined as $\infty$ if $0 < c .$

Many common families of distributions have an MLR. For example, the normal
(known variance, unknown mean), Poisson, and binomial all have an MLR. Indeed,
any regular exponential family with $g \left( t | \theta \right) = h \left( t \right) c \left( \theta \right) e ^ { w \left( \theta \right) t }$ has an MLR if $w \left( \theta \right)$ is a
nondecreasing function (see Exercise 8.25).

Theorem 8.3.17 (Karlin-Rubin) Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 }$ :
$\theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$ Suppose that $T$ is a sufficient statistic $\tan \theta$ and the family of pdfs or $p m f s$
$\left\{ g \left( t | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ of $T$ has an $M L R .$ Then for any $t _ { 0 } ,$ the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if and
only if $T > t _ { 0 }$ is a $U M P$ level $\alpha$ test, where $\alpha = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( T > t _ { 0 } \right) .$

Proof: Let $\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( T > t _ { 0 } \right)$ be the power function of the test. Fix $\theta ^ { \prime } > \theta _ { 0 }$ and
consider testing $H _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } : \theta = \theta ^ { \prime } .$ Since the family of pdfs or pmfs of $T$
has an $M L R ,$ $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ is nondecreasing (see Exercise 8.34), so

i. $\sup _ { \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( \theta \right) = \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha ,$ and this is a level $\alpha$ test.

ii. $I f$ we define

$$k ^ { \prime } = \inf _ { t \in T } \frac { g \left( t | \theta ^ { \prime } \right) } { g \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) }$$

where $T = \left\{ t : t > t _ { 0 } \right.$ and either $g \left( t | \theta ^ { \prime } \right) > 0$ or $\left. g \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) > 0 \right\} ,$ it follows that

$$T > t _ { 0 } \Leftrightarrow \frac { g \left( t | \theta ^ { \prime } \right) } { g \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) } > k ^ { \prime } .$$

11

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="392" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Together with Corollary 8.3.13, (i) and (ii) imply that $\beta \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) \geq \beta ^ { * } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) ,$ where $\beta ^ { * } \left( \theta \right)$
is the power function for any other level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ,$ that is, any test satisfying
$\beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq \alpha .$ However, any level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 }$ satisfies $\beta ^ { * } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq \sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta ^ { * } \left( \theta \right) \leq \alpha .$
Thus, $\beta \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) \geq \beta ^ { * } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right)$ for any level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } .$ Since $\theta ^ { \prime }$ was arbitrary, the test is a
UMP level $\alpha$ test.
☐

By an analogous argument, it can be shown that under the conditions of Theorem
8.3.17, the test that rejects $H _ { 0 } : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$ in favor of $H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 } ,$ if and only if $T < t _ { 0 }$ is
a UMP level $\alpha = P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( T < t _ { 0 } \right)$ test.

Example 8.3.18 (Continuation of Example 8.3.15) Consider testing $H _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } : \theta \geq$
$\theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 }$ using the test that rejects $H _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ if

$$\bar { X } < - \frac { \sigma z _ { \alpha } } { \sqrt { n } } + \theta _ { 0 } .$$

As $\bar { X }$ is sufficient and its distribution has an MLR (see Exercise 8.25), it follows from
Theorem 8.3.17 that the test is a UMP level $\alpha$ test in this problem.

As the power function of this test,

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( \bar { X } < - \frac { \sigma z _ { \alpha } } { \sqrt { n } } + \theta _ { 0 } \right) ,$$

is a decreasing function of $\theta \text { \left(since } 6$ is a location parameter in the distribution of $\bar { X } ,$
the value of $\alpha$ is given by $\sup _ { \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 } } \beta \left( \theta \right) = \beta \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha .$
Il

Although most experimenters would choose to use a UMP level $\alpha$ test if they knew
of one, unfortunately, for many problems there is no UMP level $\alpha$ test. That is, no
UMP test exists because the class of level $\alpha$ tests is so large that no one test dominates
all the others in terms of power. In such cases, a common method of continuing the
search for a good test is to consider some subset of the class of level $\alpha$ tests and
attempt to find a UMP test in this subset. This tactic should be reminiscent of what
we did in Chapter 7, when we restricted attention to unbiased point estimators in
order to investigate optimality. We illustrate how restricting attention to the subset
consisting of unbiased tests can result in finding $a$ best test.

First we consider an example that illustrates a typical situation in which a UMP
level $\alpha$ test does not exist.

Example 8.3.19 (Nonexistence of UMP test) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$
$\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 } .$ For a specified value of $\alpha ,$
a level $\alpha$ test in this problem is any test that satisfies

(8.3.6)
$$P _ { \theta _ { 0 } } \left( \text { reject } H _ { 0 } \right) \leq \alpha .$$

Consider an alternative parameter point $\theta _ { 1 } < \theta _ { 0 } .$ The analysis in Example 8.3.18
shows that, among all tests that satisfy (8.3.6), the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\bar { X } <$
$- \sigma z _ { \alpha } / \sqrt { n } + \theta _ { 0 }$ has the highest possible power at $\theta _ { 1 } .$ Call this Test 1. Furthermore, by
part (b) (necessity) of the Neyman-Pearson Lemma, any other level $\alpha$ test that has

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="393" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

as high a power as Test 1 at $\theta _ { 1 }$ must have the same rejection region as Test 1 except
possibly for a set $A$ satisfying $\int _ { A } f \left( x | \theta _ { i } \right) d x = 0 .$ Thus, if a UMP level $\alpha$ test exists
for this problem, it must be Test 1 because no other test has as high a power as Test
1 at $\theta _ { 1 } .$

☒

Now consider Test 2, which rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\bar { X } > \sigma z _ { \alpha } / \sqrt { n } + \theta _ { 0 } .$ Test 2 is also a level
a test. Let $\beta _ { i } \left( \theta \right)$ denote the power function of Test i. For any $\theta _ { 2 } > \theta _ { 0 } ,$
$$\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta _ { 2 } \right) = P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( \bar { X } > \frac { \sigma z _ { \alpha } } { \sqrt { n } } + \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \theta _ { 2 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } > z _ { \alpha } + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta _ { 2 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$> P \left( Z > z _ { \alpha } \right)$$
$$= P \left( Z < - z _ { \alpha } \right)$$
$$> P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \theta _ { 2 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } < - z _ { \alpha } + \frac { \theta _ { 0 } - \theta _ { 2 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right) \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { a g a i n } , > \mathrm { s i n c e } \\ \theta _ { 0 } - \theta _ { 2 } < 0 \end{array} \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( \bar { X } < - \frac { \sigma z _ { \alpha } } { \sqrt { n } } + \theta _ { 0 } \right)$$
$$= \beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta _ { 2 } \right) .$$
$$\left( \begin{array}{} { Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) } \\ { > \sin c e \theta _ { 0 } - \theta _ { 2 } < 0 } \end{array} \right)$$

Thus Test 1 is not a UMP level $\alpha$ test because Test 2 has a higher power than Test
1 at $\theta _ { 2 } .$ Earlier we showed that if there were a UMP level $\alpha$ test, it would have to be
Test 1. Therefore, no UMP level $\alpha$ test exists in this problem.

Example 8.3.19 illustrates again the usefulness of the Neyman-Pearson Lemma.
Previously, the sufficiency part of the lemma was used to construct UMP level $\alpha$
tests, but to show the nonexistence of a UMP level $\alpha$ test, the necessity part of the
lemma is used.

Example 8.3.20 (Unbiased test) When no UMP level $\alpha$ test exists within the
class of all tests, we might try to find a UMP level $\alpha$ test within the class of unbiased
tests. The power function, $\beta _ { 3 } \left( \theta \right) ,$ of Test 3, which rejects $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ in favor of
$H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 }$ if and only if

$$\bar { X } > \sigma z _ { \alpha / 2 } / \sqrt { n } + \theta _ { 0 } \quad \mathrm { o r } \quad \bar { X } < - \sigma z _ { \alpha / 2 } / \sqrt { n } + \theta _ { 0 } ,$$

as well as $\beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right)$ and $\beta _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right)$ from Example 8.3.19, is shown in Figure 8.3.3. Test 3 is
actually a UMP unbiased level $\alpha$ test; that is, it is UMP in the class of unbiased tests.

Note that although Test 1 and Test 2 have slightly higher powers than Test 3 for
some parameter points, Test 3 has much higher power than Test 1 and Test 2 at
other parameter points. For example, $\beta _ { 3 } \left( \theta _ { 2 } \right)$ is near 1, whereas $\beta _ { 1 } \left( \theta _ { 2 } \right)$ is near 0. If
the interest is in rejecting $H _ { 0 }$ for both large and small values of $\theta ,$ Figure 8.3.3 shows
that Test 3 is better overall than either Test 1 or Test 2.
