### 8.3.3 Sizes of Union-Intersection and Intersection-Union Tests

Because of the simple way in which they are constructed, the sizes of union-intersection
tests (UIT) and intersection-union tests (IUT) can often be bounded above by the
sizes of some other tests. Such bounds are useful if a level $\alpha$ test is wanted, but the
size of the UIT or IUT is too difficult to evaluate. In this section we discuss these
bounds and give examples in which the bounds are sharp, that is, the size of the test
is equal to the bound.

First consider UITs. Recall that, in this situation, we are testing a null hypothesis
of the form $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ where $\Theta _ { 0 } = \bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma } .$ To be specific, let $\lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ be the LRT
statistic for testing $H _ { 0 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma }$ versus $H _ { 1 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma } ^ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ and let $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ be the LRT
statistic for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } .$ Then we have the following
relationships between the overall LRT and the UIT based on $\lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) .$

Theorem 8.3.21 Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } ,$ where $\Theta _ { 0 } =$
$\bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma }$ and $\lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is defined in the previous paragraph. Define $T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \inf _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) ,$
and form the UIT with rejection region

$$\left\{ \mathrm { x } : \lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) < c \text { for some } \gamma \in \Gamma \right\} = \left\{ \mathrm { x } : T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) < c \right\} .$$

Also consider the usual LRT with rejection region $\left\{ x : \lambda \left( x \right) < c \right\} .$ Then

a. $T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \geq \lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ for every x;

b. If $\beta _ { T } \left( \theta \right)$ and $\beta _ { \lambda } \left( \theta \right)$ are the power functions for the tests based on $T$ and $\lambda ,$ respec-
tively, then $\beta _ { T } \left( \theta \right) \leq \beta _ { \lambda } \left( \theta \right)$ for every $\theta \in \Theta$

c. If the $L R T$ is a level $\alpha$ test, then the $U I T$ is a level $\alpha$ test.

Proof: Since $\Theta _ { 0 } = \bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma } \subset \Theta _ { \gamma }$ for any $\gamma ,$ from Definition 8.2.1 we see that for
any $\mathrm { X } ,$

$$\lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \geq \lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \quad \text { for each } \gamma \in \Gamma$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="395" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

because the region of maximization is bigger for the individual $\lambda _ { \gamma } .$ Thus $T \left( x \right) =$
$\inf _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \lambda _ { \gamma } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \geq \lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ proving (a). By (a), $\left\{ x : T \left( x \right) < c \right\} \subset \left\{ x : \lambda \left( x \right) < c \right\} ,$ so

$$\beta _ { T } \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( T \left( \mathrm { X } \right) < c \right) \leq P _ { \theta } \left( \lambda \left( \mathrm { X } \right) < c \right) = \beta _ { \lambda } \left( \theta \right) ,$$

proving (b). Since (b) holds for every $\theta$ $\theta , \sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta _ { T } \left( \theta \right) \leq \sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \beta _ { \lambda } \left( \theta \right) \leq \alpha ,$ proving
☐
(c).

Example 8.3.22 (An equivalence) In some situations, $T \left( x \right) = \lambda \left( x \right)$ in $\mathrm { T h e o r e m }$
8.3.21. The UIT built up from individual LRTs is the same as the overall LRT. This
was the case in Example 8.2.8. There the UIT formed from two one-sided $t$ tests was
equivalent to the two-sided LRT.
II

Since the LRT is uniformly more powerful than the UIT in Theorem 8.3.21, we
might ask why we should use the UIT. One reason is that the UIT has a smaller Type
I Error probability for every $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ Furthermore, if $H _ { 0 }$ is rejected, we may wish to
look at the individual tests of $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ to see why. As yet, we have not discussed inferences
for the individual $H _ { 0 \gamma } .$ The error probabilities for such inferences would have to be
examined before such an inference procedure were adopted. But the possibility of
gaining additional information by looking at the $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ individually, rather than looking
only at the overall LRT, is evident.

Now we investigate the sizes of IUTs. A simple bound for the size of an IUT is
related to the sizes of the individual tests that are used to define the IUT. Recall that
in this situation the null hypothesis is expressible as a union; that is, we are testing

$$\Theta _ { 0 } = \bigcup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \Theta _ { \gamma } .$$
Ho: A E Oo versus H1: 0 E OG, where

An IUT has a rejection region of the form $R = \bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } R _ { \gamma } ,$ where $R _ { \gamma }$ is the rejection
region for a test of $H _ { 0 \gamma } : \theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma } .$

Theorem 8.3.23 Let $\alpha _ { \gamma }$ be the $s i z e$ of the test of $H _ { 0 \gamma }$ with rejection region $R _ { \gamma } .$
Then the $I U T$ with rejection region $R = \bigcap _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } R _ { \gamma }$ is $a$ level $\alpha = \sup _ { \gamma \in \Gamma } \alpha _ { \gamma }$ test.

Proof: Let $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ Then $\theta \in \Theta _ { \gamma }$ for some $\gamma$ and

$$P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) \leq P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R _ { \gamma } \right) \leq \alpha _ { \gamma } \leq \alpha .$$

Since $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ was arbitrary, the $I U T$ is a level $\alpha$ test.
☐

Typically, the individual rejection regions $R _ { \gamma }$ are chosen so that $\alpha _ { \gamma } = \alpha$ for all $\gamma .$
In such a case, Theorem 8.3.23 states that the resulting $I U T$ is a level $\alpha$ test.

Theorem 8.3.23, which provides an upper bound for the size of an IUT, is somewhat
more useful than Theorem 8.3.21, which provides an upper bound for the size of a
UIT. Theorem 8.3.21 applies only to UITs constructed from likelihood ratio tests. In
contrast, Theorem 8.3.23 applies to any IUT.

The bound in Theorem 8.3.21 is the size of the LRT, which, in a complicated
problem, may be difficult to compute. In Theorem 8.3.23, however, the LRT need not

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="396" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

be used to obtain the upper bound. Any test of $H _ { 0 } \gamma$ with known size $\alpha _ { \gamma }$ can be used,
and then the upper bound on the $\mathrm { s i z e }$ of the IUT is given in terms of the known sizes
$\alpha _ { \gamma } ,$ $\gamma \in \Gamma .$

The IUT in Theorem 8.3.23 is a level $\alpha$ test. But the size of the $I U T$ may be much
less than a; the IUT may be very conservative. The following theorem gives conditions
under which the size of the IUT is exactly $\alpha$ and the IUT is not too conservative.

Theorem 8.3.24 Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \in \bigcup _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \Theta _ { j } ,$ where $k$ is a finite positive
integer. For each $j = 1 , \ldots , k ,$ let $R _ { j }$ be the rejection region of a level $\alpha$ test of
$H _ { 0 j } .$ Suppose that for some $i = 1 , \ldots , k ,$ there exists a sequence of parameter points,
$\theta _ { l } \in \Theta _ { i } ,$ $l = 1 , 2 , \ldots ,$ such that

i. $\lim _ { l \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in R _ { i } \right) = \alpha ,$

ii. for each $j = 1 , \ldots , k ,$ $j \neq i ,$ $\lim _ { l \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in R _ { j } \right) = 1 .$

Then, the $I U T$ with rejection region $R = \bigcap _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } R _ { j }$ is a size $\alpha$ test.

Proof: By Theorem 8.3.23, $R$ is a level $\alpha$ test, that is,

(8.3.7)
$$\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) \leq \alpha .$$

But, because all the parameter points $\theta _ { l }$ satisfy $\theta _ { l } \in \Theta _ { i } \subset \Theta _ { 0 } ,$

$$\sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) \geq \lim _ { l \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in R \right)$$
$$= \lim _ { l \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in \bigcap _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } R _ { j } \right)$$
$$\geq \lim _ { l \rightarrow \infty } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( \mathrm { X } \in R _ { j } \right) - \left( k - 1 \right) \quad \binom { \mathrm { B o n f e r r o n i s } } { \mathrm { I n e q u a l i t y } }$$
$$= \left( k - 1 \right) + \alpha - \left( k - 1 \right)$$
$$= \alpha .$$ ☒
(by (i) and (ii)

This and (8.3.7) imply the test has size exactly equal to $\alpha .$
0
☐

Example 8.3.25 (Intersection-union test) In Example 8.2.9, let $n = m = 5 8 ,$
$t = 1 . 6 7 2 ,$ and $b = 5 7 .$ Then each of the individual tests has size $\alpha = . 0 5$ (approx-
imately). Therefore, by Theorem 8.3.23, the IUT is a level $\alpha = . 0 5$ test; that is,
the probability of deciding the product is good, when in fact it is not, is no more
than .05. In fact, this test is a size $\alpha = . 0 5$ test. To see this consider a sequence of
parameter points $\theta _ { l } = \left( \theta _ { 1 l } , \theta _ { 2 } \right) ,$ with $\theta _ { 1 l } \rightarrow \infty$ as $l \rightarrow \infty$ and $\theta _ { 2 } = . 9 5 .$ $A l l$ such
parameter points are in $\Theta _ { 0 }$ because $\theta _ { 2 } \leq . 9 5 .$ Also, $P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in R _ { 1 } \right) \rightarrow 1$ as $\theta _ { 1 l } \rightarrow \infty ,$
while $P _ { \theta _ { l } } \left( X \in R _ { 2 } \right) = . 0 5$ for all $l$ because $\theta _ { 2 } = . 9 5 .$ Thus, by Theorem 8.3.24, the
$I U T$ is a size $\alpha$ test.

Note that, in Example 8.3.25, only the marginal distributions of the $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ and
$Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ were used to find the size of the test. This point is extremely important

$I$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="397" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

and directly relates to the usefulness of IUTs, because the joint distribution is often
difficult to know and, if known, often difficult to work with. For example, $X _ { i }$ and
$Y _ { i }$ may be related if they are measurements on the same piece of fabric, but this
relationship would have to be modeled and used to calculate the exact power of $\mathrm { t h e }$
IUT at any particular parameter value.


### 8.3.4 $p -$ Values

After a hypothesis test is done, the conclusions must be reported in some statistically
meaningful way. One method of reporting the results of a hypothesis test is to report
the size, $\alpha ,$ of the test used and the decision to reject $H _ { 0 }$ or accept $H _ { 0 } .$ The size of
the test carries important information. If $\alpha$ is small, the decision to reject $H _ { 0 }$ is fairly
convincing, but if $\alpha$ is large, the decision to reject $H _ { 0 }$ is not very convincing because
the test has a large probability of incorrectly making that decision. Another way of
reporting the results of a hypothesis test is to report the value of a certain kind of
test statistic called a p-value.

Definition 8.3.26 A p-value $p \left( X \right)$ is a test statistic satisfying $0 \leq p \left( x \right) \leq 1$ for
☒
every sample point $\mathrm { x } .$ Small values of $p \left( X \right)$ give evidence that $H _ { 1 }$ is true. A $p$ p-value
is valid if, for every $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and every $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$

(8.3.8)
$$P _ { \theta } \left( p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha \right) \leq \alpha .$$

If $p \left( X \right)$ is a valid p-value, it is easy to construct a level $\alpha$ test based on $p \left( X \right) .$ The
☒

☒
test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if and only if $p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha$ is a level $\alpha$ test because of (8.3.8). An
☒
advantage to reporting a test result via a p-value is that each reader can choose the $\alpha$
he or she considers appropriate and then can compare the reported $p \left( x \right)$ to $\alpha$ and know
☒
whether these data lead to acceptance or rejection of $H _ { 0 } .$ Furthermore, the smaller
the p-value, the stronger the evidence for rejecting $H _ { 0 } .$ Hence, a $D$ p-value reports the
results of a test on a more continuous scale, rather than just the dichotomous decision
"Accept $H _ { 0 }$ or "Reject $H _ { 0 }$

The most common way to define a valid p-value is given in Theorem 8.3.27.

Theorem 8.3.27 Let $W \left( X \right)$ be $a$ test statistic such that large values of $W$ give
☒
evidence that $H _ { 1 }$ is true. For each sample point $\mathrm { x } ,$ define

(8.3.9)
$$p \left( x \right) = \sup _ { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } P _ { \theta } \left( W \left( X \right) \geq W \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Then, $p \left( X \right)$ is $a$ valid p-value.

Proof: Fix $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ Let $F _ { \theta } \left( w \right)$ denote the cdf of $- W \left( X \right) .$ Define
☒

$$p _ { \theta } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( - W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \leq - W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = F _ { \theta } \left( - W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

Then the random variable $p _ { \theta } \left( X \right)$ is equal to $F _ { \theta } \left( - W \left( X \right) \right) .$ Hence, by the Probability
☒ ☒
Integral Transformation or Exercise 2.10, the distribution of $p _ { \theta } \left( X \right)$ is stochastically
☒

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="398" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

greater than or equal to a uniform(0, $\left. 1 \right)$ distribution. That is, for every $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$
$P _ { \theta } \left( p _ { \theta } \left( X \right) \leq \alpha \right) \leq \alpha .$ Because $p \left( x \right) = \sup _ { \theta ^ { \prime } \in \Theta _ { 0 } } p _ { \theta ^ { \prime } } \left( x \right) \geq p _ { \theta } \left( x \right)$ for every x,
☒

$$P _ { \theta } \left( p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha \right) \leq P _ { \theta } \left( p _ { \theta } \left( X \right) \leq \alpha \right) \leq \alpha .$$

This is true for every $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and for every $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ 1; $p \left( \mathrm { X } \right)$ is a valid p-value.
☒ ☐

The calculation of the supremum in (8.3.9) might be difficult. The next two ex-
amples illustrate common situations in which it is not too difficult. In the first, no
supremum is necessary; in the second, it is easy to determine the $\theta$ value at which
the supremum occurs.

Example 8.3.28 (Two-sided normal p-value) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random
sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq \mu _ { 0 } .$
By Exercise 8.38, the $L R T$ rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for large values of $W \left( X \right) = | X - \mu _ { 0 } | / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right) .$ If
$\mu = \mu _ { 0 } ,$ regardless of the value of o, $\left( X - \mu _ { 0 } \right) / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right)$ has a Student's $t$ distribution
☒
with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom. Thus, in calculating (8.3.9), the probability is the
same for all values of $\theta ,$ that is, all values of o. Thus, the p-value from (8.3.9) for this
two-sided $t$ test is $p \left( x \right) = 2 P \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq | \bar { x } - \mu _ { 0 } | / \left( s / \sqrt { n } \right) \right) ,$ where $T _ { n - 1 }$ has a Student's
t distribution with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom.
☒
1l

Example 8.3.29 (One-sided normal p-value) Again consider the normal model
of Example 8.3.28, but consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \mu \leq \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu > \mu _ { 0 } .$ By Exercise
8.37, the LRT rejects $H _ { 0 }$ for large values of $W \left( X \right) = \left( \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } \right) / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right) .$ The following
☒ ☒
argument shows that, for this statistic, the supremum in $\left( 8 . 3 . 9 \right)$ always occurs at a
parameter $\left( \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma \right) ,$ and the value of $\sigma$ used does not matter. Consider any $\mu \leq \mu _ { 0 }$ and
any $\sigma :$

$$P _ { \mu , \sigma } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = P _ { \mu , \sigma } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right)$$
$$= P _ { \mu , \sigma } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq W \left( x \right) + \frac { \mu _ { 0 } - \mu } { S / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \mu , \sigma } \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq W \left( x \right) + \frac { \mu _ { 0 } - \mu } { S / \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$\leq P \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq W \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Here again, $T _ { n - 1 }$ has a Student's $t$ distribution with $n \quad - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom. The
inequality in the last line is true because $\mu _ { 0 } \geq \mu$ and $\left( \mu _ { 0 } - \mu \right) / \left( S / \sqrt { n } \right)$ is a nonnegative
random variable. The subscript on $P$ is dropped here, because this probability does
not depend on $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) .$ Furthermore,

$$P \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = P _ { \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma } \left( \frac { \bar { X } - \mu _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { n } } \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) = P _ { \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma } \left( W \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \geq W \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) ,$$

and this probability is one of those considered in the calculation of the supremum in
(8.3.9) because $\left( \mu _ { 0 } , \sigma \right) \in \Theta _ { 0 } .$ Thus, the p-value from (8.3.9) for this one-sided $t$ test
is $p \left( x \right) = P \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq W \left( x \right) \right) = P \left( T _ { n - 1 } \geq \left( \bar { x } - \mu _ { 0 } \right) / \left( s / \sqrt { n } \right) \right) .$
$I I$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="399" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->

Another method for defining a valid p-value, an alternative to using (8.3.9), involves
conditioning on a sufficient statistic. Suppose $S \left( X \right)$ is a sufficient statistic for the
☒
model $\left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } \right\} .$ (To avoid tests with low power it is important that $S$ is
sufficient only for the null model, not the entire model $\left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ If the null
hypothesis is true, the conditional distribution of $\mathrm { X }$ given $S = s$ does not depend on
$\theta .$ Again, let $W \left( X \right)$ denote a test statistic for which large values give evidence that
$H _ { 1 }$ is true. Then, for each sample point $x$ define

(8.3.10)
$$p \left( x \right) = P \left( W \left( X \right) \geq W \left( x \right) | S = S \left( x \right) \right) .$$

Arguing as in Theorem 8.3.27, but considering only the single distribution that is the
conditional distribution of $X$ given $S = s ,$ we see that, for any $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$

$$P \left( p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha | S = s \right) \leq \alpha .$$

Then, for any $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ,$ unconditionally we have

$$P _ { \theta } \left( p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha \right) = \sum _ { s } P \left( p \left( X \right) \leq \alpha | S = s \right) P _ { \theta } \left( S = s \right) \leq \sum _ { s } \alpha P _ { \theta } \left( S = s \right) \leq \alpha .$$

Thus, $p \left( X \right)$ defined by (8.3.10) is a valid $\mathrm { p }$ p-value. Sums can be replaced by integrals for
continuous $S ,$ but this method is usually used for discrete $S ,$ as in the next example.

Example 8.3.30 (Fisher's Exact Test) Let $S _ { 1 }$ and $S _ { 2 }$ be independent ob-
servations with $S _ { 1 } \sim \text { binomial } \left( n _ { 1 } , p _ { 1 } \right)$ and $S _ { 2 } \sim \text { binomial } \left( n _ { 2 } , p _ { 2 } \right) .$ Consider testing
$H _ { 0 } : p _ { 1 } = p _ { 2 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : p _ { 1 } > p _ { 2 } .$ Under $H _ { 0 } ,$ if we let $p$ denote the common value of
$p _ { 1 } = p _ { 2 } ,$ the joint pmf of $\left( S _ { 1 } , S _ { 2 } \right)$ is

$$f \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } | p \right) = \binom { n _ { 1 } } { s _ { 1 } } p ^ { s _ { 1 } } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n _ { 1 } - s _ { 1 } } \binom { n _ { 2 } } { s _ { 2 } } p ^ { s _ { 2 } } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n _ { 2 } - s _ { 2 } }$$
$$= \left( \begin{array}{} { n _ { 1 } } \\ { s _ { 1 } } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} { n _ { 2 } } \\ { s _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right) p ^ { s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } - \left( s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } \right) }$$

Thus, $S = S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 }$ is a sufficient statistic under $H _ { 0 } .$ Given the value of $S = s ,$ it is
reasonable to use $S _ { 1 }$ as a test statistic and reject $H _ { 0 }$ in favor of $H _ { 1 }$ for large values
of $S _ { 1 } ,$ because large values of $S _ { 1 }$ correspond to small values of $S _ { 2 } = s - S _ { 1 } .$ The
conditional distribution $S _ { 1 }$ given $S = s$ is hypergeometric(n1+n2, $c \left( n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } , n _ { 1 } , s \right)$ (see Exercise
8.48). Thus the conditional p-value in (8.3.10) is

$$\min \left\{ n _ { 1 } , s \right\}$$
$$p \left( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } \right) = \sum _ { j = s _ { 1 } } ^ { \min \left\{ n _ { 1 } , s \right\} } f \left( j | s \right) ,$$

the sum of hypergeometric probabilities. The test defined by this p-value is called
Fisher's Exact Test.

<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->


### 8.3.5 Loss Function Optimality

A decision theoretic analysis, as in Section 7.3.4, may be used to compare hypothesis
tests, rather than just comparing them via their power functions. To carry out this
kind of analysis, we must specify the action space and loss function for our hypothesis
testing problem.

In a hypothesis testing problem, only two actions are allowable, "accept $H _ { 0 }$ or
"reject $H _ { 0 }$ These two actions might be denoted $a _ { 0 }$ and $a _ { 1 } ,$ respectively. The action
space in hypothesis testing is the two-point set $A = \left\{ a _ { 0 } , a _ { 1 } \right\} .$ A decision rule $\delta \left( x \right)$ (a
hypothesis test) is a function on $\mathcal{X}$ that takes on only two values, $a _ { 0 }$ and $a _ { 1 } .$ The set
$\left\{ x : \delta \left( x \right) = a _ { 0 } \right\}$ is the acceptance region for the test, and the set $\left\{ x : \delta \left( x \right) = a _ { 1 } \right\}$ is
the rejection region, just as in Definition 8.1.3.

The loss function in a hypothesis testing problem should reflect the fact that, if
$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and decision $a _ { 1 }$ is made, or if $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } }$ and decision $a _ { 0 }$ is made, a mistake has
been made. But in the other two possible cases, the correct decision has been made.
Since there are only two possible actions, the loss function $L \left( \theta , a \right)$ in a hypothesis
testing problem is composed of only two parts. The function $L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right)$ is the loss
incurred for various values of $\theta$ if the decision to accept $H _ { 0 }$ is made, and $L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right)$ is
the loss incurred for various values of $\theta$ if the decision to reject $H _ { 0 }$ is made.

The simplest kind of loss in a testing problem is called 0-1 loss and is defined by

L(0,a0) =
{

0

1

and

$$L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \\ { 0 } & { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } } \end{array} \right.$$

With $0 - 1$ loss, the value 0 is lost if a correct decision is made and the value 1 is lost
if an incorrect decision is made. This is a particularly simple situation in which both
types of error have the same consequence. A slightly more realistic loss, one that gives
different costs to the two types of error, is generalized 0-1 loss,

$$\left( 8 . 3 . 1 1 \right) \quad L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 0 & \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } \\ c _ { \mathrm { I I } } & \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } \end{array} \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) = \right.$$
$$L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { c _ { 1 } } & { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } } \\ { 0 } & { \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { c } } \end{array} \right.$$

In this loss, $\mathcal{C} _ { I }$ is the cost of a Type I Error, the error of falsely rejecting $H _ { 0 } ,$ and $c _ { I I }$
is the cost of a Type II Error, the error of falsely accepting $H _ { 0 } .$ (Actually, when we
compare tests, all that really matters is the ratio $c _ { I I } / c _ { I } ,$ not the two individual values.
If $c _ { I } = c _ { I I } ,$ we essentially have 0-1 loss.)

In a decision theoretic analysis, the risk function (the expected loss) is used $t o$
evaluate a hypothesis testing procedure. The risk function of a test is closely related
to its power function, as the following analysis shows.

Let $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ be the power function of the test based on the decision rule 8. That is, if
$R = \left\{ x : \delta \left( x \right) = a _ { 1 } \right\}$ denotes the rejection region of the test, then

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X \in R \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( X \right) = a _ { 1 } \right) .$$

The risk function associated with (8.3.11) and, in particular, 0-1 loss is very simple.
For any value of $\theta \in \Theta ,$ $L \left( \theta , a \right)$ takes on only two values, 0 and $c _ { I }$ if $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 }$ and 0 and
$c _ { I I }$ if $\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } .$ Thus the risk is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="401" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING TESTS" -->

$$R \left( \theta , \delta \right) = 0 P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = a _ { 0 } \right) + c _ { \mathrm { I } } P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = a _ { \mathrm { I } } \right) = c _ { \mathrm { I } } \beta \left( \theta \right) \quad \mathrm { i f } \theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } . ,$$
$$R \left( \theta , \delta \right) = c _ { \mathrm { I I } } P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = a _ { 0 } \right) + 0 P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( \mathrm { X } \right) = a _ { 1 } \right) = c _ { \mathrm { I I } } \left( 1 - \beta \left( \theta \right) \right) \quad \mathrm { i f }$$ ☒
(8.3.12)
$\theta \in \Theta _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { c } } .$

This similarity between a decision theoretic approach and a more traditional power
approach is due, in part, to the form of the loss function. But in all hypothesis testing
problems, as we shall see below, the power function plays an important role in the
risk function.

Example 8.3.31 (Risk of UMP test)
Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample
from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. The UMP level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 }$ is the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\left( \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) < - z _ { \alpha }$ (see Example 8.3.15).
The power function for this test is

$$\beta \left( \theta \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( Z < - z _ { \alpha } - \frac { \theta - \theta _ { 0 } } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right) .$$

where $Z$ has a $n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ distribution. For $\alpha = . 1 0 ,$ the risk function (8.3.12) for $c _ { I } = 8$
and $c _ { I I } = 3$ is shown in Figure 8.3.4. Notice the discontinuity in the risk function $\mathrm { a t }$
$\theta = \theta _ { 0 } .$ This is due to the fact that at $\theta _ { 0 }$ the expression in the risk function changes
from $\beta \left( \theta \right)$ to $1 - \beta \left( \theta \right)$ as well as to the difference between $c _ { I }$ and $c _ { I I } .$
II

The 0-1 loss judges only whether a decision is right or wrong. It may be the case
that some wrong decisions are more serious than others and the loss function should
reflect this. When we test $H _ { 0 } : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 } ,$ it is a Type I Error to
reject $H _ { 0 }$ if $\theta$ is slightly bigger than $\theta _ { 0 } ,$ but it may not be a very serious mistake. The
adverse consequences of rejecting $H _ { 0 }$ may be much worse if $\theta$ is much larger than $\theta _ { 0 } .$
$A$ loss function that reflects this is

(8.13) 020
$$\theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$$
$$L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 0 & \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 } \\ b \left( \theta _ { 0 } - \theta \right) & \theta < \theta _ { 0 } \end{array} \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} c \left( \theta - \theta \right) & \\ 0 & \end{array} \right. \right.$$


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 8.3.4. Risk function for test in Example 8.3.31</figcaption>

$R \left( \theta , \delta \right)$

3

2

1

$\theta - \theta .$

-3 -2 -1 0 1 2 3

$\sigma l \sqrt { n }$

</figure>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="402" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

where $b$ and $c$ are positive constants. For example, if an experimenter is testing
whether a drug lowers cholesterol level, $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 }$ might be set up like this with
$\theta _ { 0 } =$ acceptable cholesterol level. Since a high cholesterol level is associated
with heart disease, the consequences of rejecting $H _ { 0 }$ when $\theta$ is large are quite serious.
A loss function like (8.3.13) reflects such a consequence. $A$ similar type of loss function
is advocated by Vardeman (1987).

Even for a general loss function like (8.3.13), the risk function and the power func-
tion are closely related. For any fixed value of $\theta ,$ the loss is either $L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right)$ or $L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) .$
Thus the expected loss is

$$R \left( \theta , \delta \right) = L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right) P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( X \right) = a _ { 0 } \right) + L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) P _ { \theta } \left( \delta \left( X \right) = a _ { 1 } \right)$$

(8.3.14)
$$= L \left( \theta , a _ { 0 } \right) \left( 1 - \beta \left( \theta \right) \right) + L \left( \theta , a _ { 1 } \right) \beta \left( \theta \right) .$$

The power function of a test is always important when evaluating a hypothesis test.
But in a decision theoretic analysis, the weights given by the loss function are also
important.


## 8.4 Exercises

8.1 In 1,000 tosses of a coin, 560 heads and 440 tails appear. Is it reasonable to assume
that the coin is fair? Justify your answer.

8.2 In a given city it is assumed that the number of automobile accidents in a given year
follows a Poisson distribution. In past years the average number of accidents per year
was 15, and this year it was 10. Is it justified to claim that the accident rate has
dropped?

8.3 Here, the LRT alluded to in Example 8.2.9 will be derived. Suppose that we observe
$m$ iid Bernoulli(0) random variables, denoted by $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m } .$ Show that the LRT of
$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$ will reject $H _ { 0 }$ if $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } Y _ { i } > b .$

8.4 Prove the assertion made in the text after Definition 8.2.1. If $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ is the pmf of a dis-
crete random variable, then the numerator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right) ,$ the LRT statistic, is the maximum
☒
probability of the observed sample when the maximum is computed over parameters in
the null hypothesis. Furthermore, the denominator of $\lambda \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is the maximum probability
of the observed sample over all possible parameters.
☒

8.5 A random sample, $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ is drawn from a Pareto population with pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta , \nu \right) = \frac { \theta \nu ^ { \theta } } { x ^ { \theta + 1 } } I _ { \left[ \nu , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) , \quad \theta > 0 , \quad \nu > 0 .$$

(a) Find the MLEs of $\theta$ and v.

(b) Show that the LRT of

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta = 1 , \nu \text { unknown, } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq 1 , \nu \text { unknown } ,$$

has critical region of the form $\left\{ \mathrm { x } : T \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \leq c _ { 1 } \right.$ or $\left. T \left( x \right) \geq c _ { 2 } \right\} ,$ where $0 < c _ { 1 } < c _ { 2 }$
and

$$T = \log \left[ \frac { \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } } { \left( \min _ { i } X _ { i } \right) ^ { n } } \right]$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="403" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

(c) Show that, under $H _ { 0 } ,$ $2 T$ has a chi squared distribution, and find the number of
degrees of freedom. (Hint: Obtain the joint distribution of the $n \quad - \quad 1$ nontrivial
terms $X _ { i } / \left( \min _ { i } X _ { i } \right)$ conditional on $\min _ { i } X _ { i } .$ Put these $n \quad - \quad 1$ terms together, and
notice that the distribution of $T$ given $\min _ { i } X _ { i }$ does not depend on $\min _ { i } X _ { i } ,$ so it
is the unconditional distribution of $\left. T . \right)$

8.6 Suppose that we have two independent random samples: $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are exponential(0),
and $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ are $\mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \mu \right) .$

(a) Find the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \mu$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \mu .$

(b) Show that the test in part (a) can be based on the statistic

$$T = \frac { \sum X _ { i } } { \sum X _ { i } + \sum Y _ { i } }$$

(c) Find the distribution of $T$ when $H _ { 0 }$ is true.

8.7 We have already seen the usefulness of the LRT in dealing with problems with nuisance
parameters. We now look at some other nuisance parameter problems.

(a) Find the LRT of

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta < 0 \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0$$

based on a sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a population with probability density function
$f \left( x | \theta , \lambda \right) = \frac { 1 } { \lambda } e ^ { - \left( x - \theta \right) / \lambda } I _ { \left[ \theta , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) ,$ where both $\theta$ and $\lambda$ are unknown.

(b) We have previously seen that the exponential pdf is a special case of a gamma pdf.
Generalizing in another way, the exponential pdf can be considered as a special case
of the Weibull(y, $\left( \gamma , \beta \right) .$ The Weibull pdf, which reduces to the exponential if $\gamma = 1 ,$
is very important in modeling reliability of systems. Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a
random sample from a Weibull population with both $\gamma$ and $\beta$ unknown. Find the
LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \gamma = 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \gamma \neq 1 .$

8.8 $\mathrm { A }$ special case of a normal family is one in which the mean and the variance are related,
the $n \left( \theta , a \theta \right)$ family. If we are interested in testing this relationship, regardless of the
value of $\theta ,$ we are again faced with a nuisance parameter problem.

(a) Find the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : a = 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : a \neq 1$ based on a sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from
a $n \left( \theta , a \theta \right)$ family, where $\theta$ is unknown.

(b) A similar question can be asked about a related family, the $n \left( \theta , a \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$ family. Thus,
if $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( \theta , a \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\theta$ is unknown, find the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : a = 1$
versus $H _ { 1 } : a \neq 1 .$

8.9 Stefanski (1996) establishes the arithmetic-geometric-harmonic mean inequality (see
Example 4.7.8 and Miscellanea 4.9.2) using a proof based on likelihood ratio tests.
Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are independent with pdfs $\lambda _ { i } e ^ { - \lambda _ { i } y _ { i } } ,$ and we want to test
$H _ { 0 } : \lambda _ { 1 } = \cdots = \lambda _ { n }$ VS. $H _ { 1 } : \lambda _ { i }$ are not all equal.

(a) Show that the LRT statistic is given by $\left( \bar { Y } \right) ^ { - n } / \left( \prod _ { i } Y _ { i } \right) ^ { - 1 }$ and hence deduce the
arithmetic-geometric mean inequality.

(b) Make the transformation $X _ { i } = 1 / Y _ { i } ,$ and show that the LRT statistic based on
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is given by $\left[ n / \sum _ { i } \left( 1 / X _ { i } \right) \right] ^ { n } / \prod _ { i } X _ { i }$ and hence deduce the geometric-
harmonic mean inequality.

8.10 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ and let $\lambda$ have a gamma(a, $a \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ distribution, the
conjugate family for the Poisson. In Exercise 7.24 the posterior distribution of $\lambda$ was
found, including the posterior mean and variance. Now consider a Bayesian test of
$H _ { 0 } : \lambda \leq \lambda _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \lambda > \lambda _ { 0 } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="404" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

(a) Calculate expressions for the posterior probabilities of $H _ { 0 }$ and $H _ { 1 } .$

(b) If $\alpha = \frac { 5 } { 2 }$ and $\beta = 2 ,$ the prior distribution is a chi squared distribution with 5
degrecs of freedom. Explain how a chi squared table could be used to perform a
Bayesian test.

8.11 In Exercise 7.23 the posterior distribution of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ the variance of a normal population,
given $S ^ { 2 } ,$ the sample variance based on a sample of size n, was found using a conjugate
prior for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ (the inverted gamma pdf with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ Based on observing
$S ^ { 2 } ,$ a decision about the hypotheses $H _ { 0 } : \sigma \leq 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \sigma > 1$ is to be made.

(a) Find the region of the sample space for which $P \left( \sigma \leq 1 | s ^ { 2 } \right) > P \left( \sigma > 1 | s ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ the
region for which a Bayes test will decide that $\sigma \leq 1 .$

(b) Compare the region in part (a) with the acceptance region of an LRT. Is there
any choice of prior parameters for which the regions agree?

8.12 For samples of size $n = 1 , 4 , 1 6 , 6 4 , 1 0 0$ from a normal population with mean $\mu$ and
known variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ plot the power function of the following LRTs. Take $\alpha = . 0 5 .$

(a) $H _ { 0 } : \mu \leq 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu > 0$

(b) $H _ { 0 } : \mu = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq 0$

8.13 Let $X _ { 1 } ,$ $X _ { 2 }$ be id uniform(0, $\left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right) .$ For testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0 ,$ we have
two competing tests:

$$\phi _ { 1 } \left( X _ { 1 } \right) : \text { Reject Ho if } X _ { 1 } > . 9 5 ,$$
$$\phi _ { 2 } \left( X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } \right) : \text { Reject Ho if } X _ { 1 } + X _ { 2 } > C .$$

(a) Find the value of $C$ so that $\phi _ { 2 }$ has the same size as $\phi _ { 1 } .$

(b) Calculate the power function of each test. Draw a well-labeled graph of cach power
function.

(c) Prove or disprove: $\phi _ { 2 }$ is a more powerful test than $\phi _ { 1 } .$

(d) Show how to get a test that has the same size but is more powerful than $\phi _ { 2 } .$

8.14 For a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ of $\mathrm { B e r n o u l l i } \left( p \right)$ variables, it is desired to test

$$H _ { 0 } : p = . 4 9 \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : p = . 5 1 .$$

Use the Central Limit Theorem to determine, approximately, the sample size needed
so that the two probabilities of error are both about .01. Use a test function that
rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$ is large.

8.15 Show that for a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, the most
powerful test of $H _ { 0 } : \sigma = \sigma _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \sigma = \sigma _ { 1 } ,$ where $\sigma _ { 0 } < \sigma _ { 1 } ,$ is given by

$$\phi \left( \Sigma X _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 1 & \quad \mathrm { i f } \Sigma X _ { i } ^ { 2 } > c \\ 0 & \quad \mathrm { i f } \Sigma X _ { i } ^ { 2 } \leq c . \end{array} \right.$$

For a given value of a, the size of the Type I Error, show how the value of $c$ is explicitly
determined.

8.16 One very striking abuse of $\alpha$ levels is to choose them after seeing the data and to
choose them in such a way as to force rejection (or acceptance) of a null hypothesis.
To see what the true Type I and Type II Error probabilities of such a procedure are,
calculate size and power of the following two trivial tests:

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="405" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

(a) Always reject $H _ { 0 } ,$ no matter what data are obtained (equivalent to the practice
of choosing the $\alpha$ level to force rejection of $H _ { 0 } .$

(b) Always accept $H _ { 0 } ,$ no matter what data are obtained (equivalent $t o$ the practice
of choosing the $\alpha$ level to force acceptance of $H _ { 0 } .$

8.17 Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid with a $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \mu , 1 \right)$ pdf and $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ are iid with a
$\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \theta , 1 \right)$ pdf. Also assume that the $X s$ are independent of the $Y \mathrm { s } .$

(a) Find an LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \mu$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \mu .$

(b) Show that the test in part (a) can be based on the statistic

$$T = \frac { \sum \log X _ { i } } { \sum \log X _ { i } + \sum \log Y _ { i } }$$

(c) Find the distribution of $T$ when $H _ { 0 }$ is true, and then show how to get a test of
size $\alpha = . 1 0 .$

8.18 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. An LRT
of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 }$ is a test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $| \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } | / \left( \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right) > c .$

(a) Find an expression, in terms of standard normal probabilities, for the power func-
tion of this test.

(b) The experimenter desires a Type I Error probability of .05 and a maximum $\mathrm { T y p e }$
II Error probability of .25 at $\theta = \theta _ { 0 } + \sigma .$ Find values of $n$ and $c$ that will achieve
this.

8.19 The random variable $X$ has pdf $f \left( x \right) = e ^ { - x } ,$ $x >$ 0. One observation is obtained on
the random variable $Y = X ^ { \theta } ,$ and a test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = 2$ needs to
be constructed. Find the UMP level $\alpha = . 1 0$ test and compute the Type $I I$ Error
probability.

8.20 Let $X$ be a random variable whose pmf under $H _ { 0 }$ and $I I _ { 1 }$ is given by

Use the Neyman-Pearson Lemma to find the most powerful test for $H _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 }$ with
size $\alpha = . 0 4 .$ Compute the probability of Type II Error for this test.

8.21 In the proof of Theorem 8.3.12 (Neyman-Pearson Lemma), it was stated that the
proof, which was given for continuous random variables, can easily be adapted to cover
discrete random variables. Provide the details; that is, prove the Neyman-Pearson
Lemma for discrete random variables. Assume that the $\alpha$ level is attainable.

8.22 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { 1 0 }$ be iid Bernoulli(p).

(a) Find the most powerful test of size $\alpha = . 0 5 4 7$ of the hypotheses $H _ { 0 } : p = \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : p = \frac { 1 } { 4 } .$ Find the power of this test.

(b) For testing $H _ { 0 } : p \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : p > \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ find the size and sketch the power
function of the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 1 0 } X _ { i } \geq 6 .$

(c) For what $\alpha$ levels does there exist a $U M P$ test of the hypotheses in part (a)?

8.23 Suppose $X$ is one observation from a population with $\mathrm { b e t a } \left( \theta , 1 \right)$ pdf.

(a) For testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > 1 ,$ find the size and sketch the power function
of the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if $X > \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$

(b) Find the most powerful level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = 2 .$

(c) Is there a UMP test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq 1$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > 1 ? ,$ find it. If not, prove so.

8.24 Find the LRT of a simple $H _ { 0 }$ versus a simple $H _ { 1 } .$ Is this test equivalent to the one
obtained from the Neyman-Pearson Lemma? (This relationship is treated in some
detail by Solomon 1975.)

8.25 Show that each of the following families has an MLR.

(a) $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ family with $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known

(b) $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \theta \right)$ family

(c) binomial(n, $\left( n , \theta \right)$ family with $n$ known

8.26 (a) Show that if a family of pdfs $\left\{ f \left( x | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\}$ has an MLR, then the corresponding
family of cdfs is stochastically increasing in $\theta .$ (See the Miscellanea section.)

(b) Show that the converse of part (a) is false; that is, give an example of a family
of cdfs that is stochastically increasing in $\theta$ for which the corresponding family of
pdfs does not have an MLR.

8.27 Suppose $g \left( t | \theta \right) = h \left( t \right) c \left( \theta \right) e ^ { w \left( \theta \right) t }$ is a one-parameter exponential family for the random
variable $T .$ Show that this family has an MLR if $w \left( \theta \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta .$
Give three examples of such a family.

8.28 Let $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ be the logistic location pdf

$$f \left( x | \theta \right) = \frac { e ^ { \left( x - \theta \right) } } { \left( 1 + e ^ { \left( x - \theta \right) } \right) ^ { 2 } } , \quad - \infty < x < \infty , \quad - \infty < \theta < \infty .$$

(a) Show that this family has an MLR.

(b) Based on one observation, $X ,$ find the most powerful size $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0$
☒
versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = 1 .$ For $\alpha = . 2 ,$ find the size of the Type II Error.

(c) Show that the test in part (b) is UMP size $\alpha$ for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0 .$
What can be said about $U M P$ tests in general for the logistic location family?
☒

8.29 Let $X$ be one observation from a $\mathrm { C a u c h y } \left( \theta \right)$ distribution.

(a) Show that this family does not have an $M L R .$

(b) Show that the test

$$\phi \left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \text { if } 1 < x < 3 } \\ { 0 } & { \text { otherwise } } \end{array} \right.$$

is most powerful of its size for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta = 1 .$ Calculate the
Type I and Type II Error probabilities.

(c) Prove or disprove: The test in part (b) is UMP for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq 0$ versus
$H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0 .$ What can be said about UMP tests in general for the Cauchy location
family?

☒ ☒
distribution of $| X |$ does have an MLR.

8.32 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , 1 \right) ,$ and let $\theta _ { 0 }$ be a specified value of $\theta .$

(a) Find the UMP, size $\alpha ,$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta \geq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 0 } .$

(b) Show that there does not exist a UMP, size $\alpha ,$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 } .$

8.33 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from the uniform(0,0 $\left( \theta , \theta + 1 \right)$ distribution. To test
$H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0 ,$ use the test

reject $H _ { 0 }$ $Y _ { n } \geq 1$ or $Y _ { 1 } \geq k ,$

where $k$ is a constant, $Y _ { 1 } = \min \left\{ X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right\} , Y _ { n } = \max \left\{ X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right\} .$

(c) Prove that the test is UMP size a.

if $\theta > 1 .$

8.34 In each of the following two situations, show that for any number $c ,$ if $\theta _ { 1 } \leq \theta _ { 2 } ,$ then

$$P _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \left( T > c \right) \leq P _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \left( T > c \right) .$$

(a) $\theta$ is a location parameter in the distribution of the random variable $T .$

(b) The family $o f \quad p d f s \quad o f \quad T ,$ $\left\{ g \left( t | \theta \right) : \theta \in \Theta \right\} ,$ has an MLR.

8.35 The usual $t$ distribution, as derived in Section 5.3.2, is also known as a central $t$
distribution. It can be thought of as the pdf of a random variable of the form $T =$
$n \left( 0 , 1 \right) / \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } / \nu } ,$ where the normal and the chi squared random variables are indepen-
dent. A generalization of the $t$ distribution, the noncentral $t ,$ is of the form $T ^ { \prime } =$
$n \left( \mu , 1 \right) / \sqrt { \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } / \nu } ,$ where the normal and the chi squared random variables are indepen-
☒
dent and we can have $\mu \neq 0 .$ (We have already seen a noncentral pdf, the noncentral
chi squared, in $\left. \left( 4 . 4 . 3 \right) . \right)$ Formally, if $X \sim n \left( \mu , 1 \right)$ and $Y \sim \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } . ,$ independent of $X ,$
then $T ^ { \prime } = X / \sqrt { Y / \nu }$ has a noncentral $t$ distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom and
noncentrality parameter $\delta = \sqrt { \mu ^ { 2 } } .$

☒ ☒

(a) Calculate the mean and variance of $T ^ { \prime } .$

(b) The pdf of $T ^ { \prime }$ is given by

$$f _ { T ^ { \prime } } \left( t | \delta \right) = \frac { e ^ { - \delta ^ { 2 } / 2 } } { \Gamma \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \Gamma \left( \frac { \nu } { 2 } \right) \sqrt { \nu } } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \left( 2 / \nu \right) ^ { k / 2 } \left( \delta t \right) ^ { k } } { k ! } \frac { \Gamma \left( \left[ \nu + k + 1 \right] / 2 \right) } { \left( 1 + \left( t ^ { 2 } / \nu \right) \right) ^ { \left( \nu + k + 1 \right) / 2 } } .$$

Show that this pdf reduces to that of a central $t$ if $\delta = 0 .$

(c) Show that the pdf of $T ^ { \prime }$ has an $M L R \quad i n$ its noncentrality parameter.

8.36 We have one observation from a $\left( 1 , \theta \right)$ population.

(a) $\mathrm { T o }$ test $H _ { 0 } : \theta _ { 1 } < \theta < \theta _ { 2 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 1 } ,$ or $\theta > \theta _ { 2 } ,$ where $\theta _ { 1 } = 1$ and $\theta _ { 2 } = 2 ,$
a test satisfies $E _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \phi = . 5$ and $E _ { \theta _ { 2 } } \phi = . 3 .$ Find a test that is as good, and explain
why it is as good.

(b) For testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 1 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 1 } ,$ with $\theta _ { 1 } = 1 ,$ find a two-sided test (other
than $\phi \equiv . 1$ that satisfies $E _ { \theta _ { 1 } } \phi = . 1$ and $\frac { d } { d \theta } E _ { \theta } \left( \phi \right) | _ { \theta = \theta _ { 1 } } = 0 .$

8.37 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider testing

versus
$$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$$
$$H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$$

(a) If $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known, show that the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ when

☒
$$\bar { X } > \theta _ { 0 } + z _ { \alpha } \sqrt { \sigma ^ { 2 } / n }$$

is a test of size $\alpha .$ Show that the test can be derived as an LRT.

(b) Show that the test in part (a) is a UMP test.

(c) If $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ show that the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ when

$$\bar { X } > \theta _ { 0 } + t _ { n - 1 , \alpha } \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n }$$ ☒

is a test of size a. Show that the test can be derived as an LRT.

8.38 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\theta _ { 0 }$ is a specified value of $\theta$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown.
We are interested in testing

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 } .$$

(a) Show that the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$ when

$$| \bar { X } - \theta _ { 0 } | > t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n }$$ ☒

is a test of size $\alpha .$

(b) Show that the test in part $\left( a \right)$ can be derived as an LRT.

8.39 Let $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ be a random sample from a bivariate normal distribution
☒ ☒
with parameters $\mu _ { X } ,$ $\mu _ { Y } ,$ $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\rho .$ We are interested in testing

$$H _ { 0 } : \mu _ { X } = \mu _ { Y } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \mu _ { X } \neq \mu _ { Y } .$$

(a) Show that the random variables $W _ { i } = X _ { i } - Y _ { i }$ are iid $n \left( \mu w , \sigma _ { W } ^ { 2 } \right) .$

(b) Show that the above hypothesis can be tested with the statistic

$$T _ { W } = \frac { \bar { W } } { \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } S _ { W } ^ { 2 } } } ,$$ ☒

where $\bar { W } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } W _ { i }$ and $S _ { W } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \left( n - 1 \right) } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( W _ { i } - \bar { W } \right) ^ { 2 } .$ Furthermore, show
that, under $H _ { 0 } ,$ $T w$ ~ Student's $t$ with $n - \quad 1$ degrees of freedom. (This test is
known as the paired-sample $t$ test.)

8.40 Let $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ be a random sample from a bivariate normal distribution
☒ ☒
with parameters $\mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho .$

(a) Derive the LRT of

$$H _ { 0 } : \mu _ { X } = \mu _ { Y } \quad \mathrm { v e r s u s } \quad H _ { 1 } : \mu _ { X } \neq \mu _ { Y } ,$$

where $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ and $\rho$ are unspecified and unknown.

(b) Show that the test derived in part (a) is equivalent to the paired $t$ test of Exercise
8.39.

(Hint: Straightforward maximization of the bivariate likelihood is possible but
somewhat nasty. Filling in the gaps of the following argument gives a more elegant
proof.)

Make the transformation $u = x \quad - y ,$ $v = x + y .$ Let $f \left( x , y \right)$ denote the bivariate
normal pdf, and write

$$f \left( x , y \right) = g \left( v / u \right) h \left( u \right) ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="409" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

where $g \left( v u \right)$ is the conditional pdf of $V$ given $U ,$ and $h \left( u \right)$ is the marginal pdf
of $U .$ Argue that (1) the likelihood can be equivalently factored and (2) the piece
involving $g \left( v | u \right)$ has the same maximum whether or not the means are restricted.
Thus, it can be ignored (since it will cancel) and the LRT is based only on $h \left( u \right) .$
However, $h \left( u \right)$ is a normal pdf with mean $\mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } ,$ and the $L R T$ is the usual
one-sample $t$ test, as derived in Exercise 8.38.

8.41 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu _ { X } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ be an
independent random sample from a $n \left( \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right) .$ We are interested in testing

$$H _ { 0 } : \mu _ { X } = \mu _ { Y } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \mu _ { X } \neq \mu _ { Y }$$

with the assumption that $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$

(a) Derive the LRT for these hypotheses. Show that the LRT can be based on the
statistic

$$T = \frac { \bar { X } - \bar { Y } } { \sqrt { S _ { \mathrm { P } } ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { 1 } { m } \right) } } ,$$

where

$$S _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \left( n + m - 2 \right) } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( Y _ { i } - \bar { Y } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

(The quantity $S _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ is sometimes referred to as a pooled variance estimate. This type
of estimate will be used extensively in Section 11.2.)

(b) Show that, under $H _ { 0 } ,$ $T \sim t _ { n + m - 2 } .$ (This test is known as the two-sample t test.)

(c) Samples of wood were obtained from the core and periphery of a certain Byzantine
church. The date of the wood was determined, giving the following data.

8.42 The assumption of equal variances, which was made in Exercise 8.41, is not always
tenable. In such a case, the distribution of the statistic is no longer a $t$ t. Indeed, there
is doubt as to the wisdom of calculating a pooled variance estimate. (This problem, of
making inference on means when variances are unequal, is, in general, quite a difficult
one. It is known as the Behrens-Fisher Problem.) A natural test to try is the following
modification of the two-sample $t$ test: Test

$$H _ { 0 } : \mu _ { X } = \mu _ { Y } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \mu _ { X } \neq \mu _ { Y } ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="410" -->
<!-- PageHeader="HYPOTHESIS TESTING" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 8.4" -->

where we do not assume that $\sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } ,$ using the statistic

$$T ^ { \prime } = \frac { \bar { X } - \bar { Y } } { \sqrt { \left( \frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { n } + \frac { S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { m } \right) } } ,$$

where

$$S _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { m - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \left( Y _ { i } - \bar { Y } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$
and

The exact distribution of $T ^ { \prime }$ is not pleasant, but we can approximate the distribution
using Satterthwaite's approximation (Example 7.2.3).

(a) Show that

$$\frac { \frac { s _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { n } + \frac { s _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { m } } { \frac { \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { n } + \frac { \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { m } } \sim \frac { \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } } { \nu } \quad \left( \mathrm { a p p r o x i m a t e l y } \right) ,$$

where $\nu$ can be estimated with

$$\widehat { \nu } = \frac { \left( \frac { S _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { n } + \frac { S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { m } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \frac { S _ { X } ^ { 4 } } { n ^ { 2 } \left( n - 1 \right) } + \frac { S _ { Y } ^ { 4 } } { m ^ { 2 } \left( m - 1 \right) } } .$$

(b) Argue that the distribution of $T ^ { \prime }$ can be approximated by a $t$ distribution with $\widehat { \nu }$
degrees of freedom.

(c) Re-examine the data from Exercise 8.41 using the approximate $t$ test of this ex-
ercise; that is, test if the mean age of the core is the same as the mean age of the
periphery using the $T ^ { \prime }$ statistic.

(d) Is there any statistical evidence that the variance of the data from the core may
be different from the variance of the data from the periphery? (Recall Example
5.4.1.)

8.43 Sprott and Farewell (1993) note that in the two-sample $t$ test, a valid $t$ statistic can be
derived as long as the ratio of variances is known. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n _ { 1 } }$ be a sample from a
$n \left( \mu _ { 1 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n _ { 2 } }$ a sample from a $n \left( \mu _ { 2 } , \rho ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\rho ^ { 2 }$ is known. Show that

$$\frac { \left( \bar { X } - \bar { Y } \right) - \left( \mu _ { 1 } - \mu _ { 2 } \right) } { \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n _ { 1 } } + \frac { \rho ^ { 2 } } { n _ { 2 } } \sqrt { \frac { \left( n _ { 1 } - 1 \right) s _ { X } ^ { 2 } + \left( n _ { 2 } - 1 \right) s _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \rho ^ { 2 } } { n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } - 2 } } }$$

has Student's $t$ distribution with $n _ { 1 } + n _ { 2 } - 2$ degrees of freedom and
$\frac { s _ { Y } ^ { 2 } } { \rho ^ { 2 } s _ { X } ^ { 2 } }$
has an $F$
distribution with $n _ { 2 } - 1$ and $n _ { 1 } - 1$ degrees of freedom.

Sprott and Farewell also note that the $t$ statistic is maximized at
$\rho ^ { 2 } = \frac { n _ { 1 } \sqrt { n _ { 1 } - 1 } s _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { n _ { 2 } \sqrt { n _ { 2 } - 1 } s _ { Y } ^ { 2 } }$
☒
and they suggest plotting the statistic for plausible values of $p ^ { 2 } ,$ possibly those in a
confidence interval.

8.45 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider testing

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$$

Let $\bar { X } _ { m }$ denote the sample mean of the first $m$ observations, $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { m } ,$ for $m =$
$1 , \ldots , n .$ If $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known, show that for each $m = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ the test that rejects $H _ { 0 }$
when

$$\bar { X } _ { m } > \theta _ { 0 } + z _ { \alpha } \sqrt { \sigma ^ { 2 } / m }$$

is an unbiased size $\alpha$ test. Graph the power function for each of these tests if $n = 4 .$
8.46 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Consider testing

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta _ { 1 } \leq \theta \leq \theta _ { 2 } \quad \mathrm { v e r s u s } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta < \theta _ { 1 } \mathrm { o r } \theta > \theta _ { 2 } .$$

(a) Show that the test

$$\text { reject } H _ { 0 } \text { if } \bar { X } > \theta _ { 2 } + t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n } \quad \text { or } \quad \bar { X } < \theta _ { 1 } - t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n }$$ ☒ ☒

is not a size $\alpha$ test.

(b) Show that, for an appropriately chosen constant $k ,$ a size $\alpha$ test is given by

$$| X - 6 | > k \sqrt { S ^ { 2 } / n } ,$$

where $\bar { \theta } = \left( \theta _ { 1 } + \theta _ { 2 } \right) / 2 .$

(c) Show that the tests in parts (a) and (b) are unbiased of their size. (Assume that
the noncentral $t$ distribution has an MLR.)

8.47 Consider two independent normal samples with equal variances, as in Exercise 8.41.

Consider testing $H _ { 0 } : \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } \leq - \delta$ oς $\mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } \geq \delta$ versus $H _ { 1 } : - \delta < \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } < \delta ,$
where $\delta$ is a specified positive constant. (This is called an equivalence testing problem.)

(a) Show that the size $\alpha$ LRT of $H _ { 0 } ^ { - } : \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } \leq - \delta$ versus $H _ { 1 } ^ { - } : \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } > - \delta$
rejects $H _ { 0 } ^ { - }$ if

$$T ^ { - } = \frac { \bar { X } - \bar { Y } - \left( - \delta \right) } { \sqrt { S _ { p } ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { 1 } { m } \right) } } \geq t _ { n + m - 2 , \alpha } .$$

(b) Find the size $\alpha$ LRT of $H _ { 0 } ^ { + } : \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } \geq \delta$ versus $H _ { 1 } ^ { + } : \mu _ { X } - \mu _ { Y } < \delta .$

(c) Explain how the tests in (a) and (b) can be combined into a level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } .$

(d) Show that the test in (c) is a size $\alpha$ test. (Hint: Consider $\sigma \rightarrow 0$ 0.)

This procedure is sometimes known as the two one-sided tests procedure and was de-
rived by Schuirmann (1987) (see also Westlake 1981) for the problem of testing bioe-
quivalence. See also the review article by Berger and $H s u$ (1996) and Exercise 9.33 for
a confidence interval counterpart.

tribution, that is, $\pi \left( \theta \right) = e ^ { - | \theta | / a } / \left( 2 a \right) ,$ a known. A Bayesian test of the hypotheses

8.51 Here is another common interpretation of p-values. Consider a problem of testing $H _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } .$ Let $W \left( X \right)$ be a test statistic. Suppose that for each $\alpha ,$ $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 ,$ a critical

8.53 In Example 8.2.7 we saw an example of a one-sided Bayesian hypothesis test. Now we
will consider a similar situation, but with a two-sided test. We want to test

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta = 0 \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq 0 ,$$

and we observe $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known.
$\mathrm { A }$ type of prior distribution that is often used in this situation is a mixture of a point
mass on $\theta = 0$ and a pdf spread out over $H _ { 1 } .$ $A$ typical choice is to take $P \left( \theta = 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$
and if $\theta \neq 0 ,$ take the prior distribution to be $\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\tau ^ { 2 }$ is known.

(a) Show that the prior defined above is proper, that $\mathrm { i s } ,$ $P \left( - \infty < \theta < \infty \right) = 1 .$

(b) Calculate the posterior probability that $H _ { 0 }$ is true, $P \left( \theta = 0 | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$

(c) Find an expression for the p-value corresponding to a value of $\bar { x } .$

(d) For the special case $\sigma ^ { 2 } = \tau ^ { 2 } = 1 ,$ compare $P \left( \theta = 0 | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ and the p-value
for a range of values of $\bar { x } .$ In particular,

(i) For $n = 9 ,$ plot the p-value and posterior probability as a function of a, and
show that the Bayes probability is greater than the p-value for moderately
large values of $\bar { x } .$

(ii) Now, for $\alpha = . 0 5 ,$ set $\bar { x } = Z _ { \alpha / 2 } / \sqrt { n } ,$ fixing the p-value at $\alpha$ for all n. Show
☒
that the posterior probability at $\bar { x } = Z _ { \alpha / 2 } / \sqrt { n }$ goes to 1 as $n \rightarrow \infty .$ This is
☒
Lindley's Paradox.

Note that small values of $P \left( \theta = 0 | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ are evidence against $H _ { 0 } ,$ and thus
this quantity is similar in spirit to a p-value. The fact that these two quantities
can have very different values was noted by Lindley (1957) and is also examined
by Berger and Sellke (1987). (See the Miscellanea section.)

8.54 The discrepancies between p-values and Bayes posterior probabilities are not as dra-
matic in the one-sided problem, as is discussed by Casella and Berger (1987) and also
mentioned in the Miscellanea section. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq 0 \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta > 0 .$$

The prior distribution on $\theta$ is $n \left( 0 , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\tau ^ { 2 }$ known, which is symmetric about the hy-
potheses $\mathrm { i n }$ the sense that $P \left( \theta \leq 0 \right) = P \left( \theta > 0 \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } .$

for values of $\bar { x } > 0 .$ Show that the Bayes probability is always greater than the
p-value.

(d) Using the expression derived in parts (a) and (b), show that

$$\lim _ { r ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \infty } P \left( \theta \leq 0 | x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \text { p-value } ,$$

an equality that does not occur in the two-sided problem.
