<!-- PageNumber="432" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->

$a ,$ an @-level acceptance region of the hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ is (see Exercise 9.11)

$$\left\{ t : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} ,$$

with associated confidence set

$$\left\{ \theta : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} .$$

Now to guarantee that the confidence set is an interval, we need to have $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ to
be monotone in $\theta .$ But we have seen this already, in the definitions of stochastically
increasing and stochastically decreasing. (See the Miscellanea section of Chapter 8
and Exercise 8.26, or Exercises 3.41-3.43.) A family of cdfs $F \left( t | \theta \right)$ is stochastically
increasing in $\theta$ (stochastically decreasing in $\theta$ if, for each $t \in T ,$ the sample space of
$T , F \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing (increasing) function of $\theta .$ In what follows, we need only the
fact that $F$ is monotone, either increasing or decreasing. The more statistical concepts
of stochastic increasing or decreasing merely serve as interpretational tools.

Theorem 9.2.12 (Pivoting a continuous $\mathrm { c d f }$ Let $T$ be a statistic with contin-
vous cdf $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) .$ Let $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$ be fixed values. Suppose that for
each $t \in T ,$ the functions $\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right)$ can be defined as follows.

i. If $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing function of $\theta$ for each t, define $\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ by

$$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 1 } , \quad F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } .$$

ii. If $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta$ for each t, define $\theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ by

$$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } , \quad F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 1 } .$$

Then the random interval $\left[ \theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( T \right) , \theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( T \right) \right]$ is a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\theta .$

Proof: We will prove only part (i). The proof of part (ii) is similar and is left as
Exercise 9.19.

Assume that we have constructed the $1 - \alpha$ acceptance region

$$\left\{ t : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} .$$

Since $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing function of $\theta$ for each $t$ and $1 - \alpha _ { 2 } > \alpha _ { 1 } ,$ $\theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( t \right) < \theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right) ,$
and the values $\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ are unique. Also,

$$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) < \alpha _ { 1 } \Leftrightarrow \theta > \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) ,$$
$$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) > 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \Leftrightarrow \theta < \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) ,$$
$$\left\{ \theta : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} = \left\{ \theta : \theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( T \right) \leq \theta \leq \theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( T \right) \right\} .$$
and hence

☐

We note that, in the absence of additional information, it is common to choose
$\alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha / 2 .$ Although this may not always be optimal (see Theorem 9.3.2), it is
certainly a reasonable strategy in most situations. $I f$ a one-sided interval is desired,
however, this can easily be achieved by choosing either $\alpha _ { 1 }$ or $\alpha _ { 2 }$ equal to 0.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="433" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->

The equations for the stochastically increasing case,

(9.2.15)
$$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 1 } , \quad F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } ,$$

can also be expressed in terms of the pdf of the statistic $T .$ The functions $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ and
$\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ can be defined to satisfy

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { t } f _ { T } \left( u | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) d u = \alpha _ { 1 }$$
$$\int _ { t } ^ { \infty } f _ { T } \left( u | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) d u = \alpha _ { 2 } .$$
and

A similar set of equations holds for the stochastically decreasing case.

Example 9.2.13 (Location exponential interval) This method can be used to
get a confidence interval for the location exponential pdf. (In Exercise 9.25 the answer
here is compared to that obtained by likelihood and pivotal methods. See also Exercise
9.41.)

If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid with pdf $f \left( x | \mu \right) = e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) } I _ { \left[ \mu , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) ,$ then $Y = \min \left\{ X _ { 1 } \right. ,$
$\left. \ldots , X _ { n } \right\}$ is sufficient for $\mu$ with pdf

$$f _ { Y } \left( y | \mu \right) = n e ^ { - n \left( y - \mu \right) } I _ { \left[ \mu , \infty \right) } \left( y \right) .$$

$\mathrm { F i x } \alpha$ and define $\mu _ { L } \left( y \right)$ and $\mu _ { U } \left( y \right)$ to satisfy

$$\int _ { \mu _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( y \right) } ^ { y } n e ^ { - n \left( u - \mu _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( y \right) \right) } d u = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } , \quad \int _ { y } ^ { \infty } n e ^ { - n \left( u - \mu _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( y \right) \right) } d u = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } .$$

These integrals can be evaluated to give the equations

$$1 - e ^ { - n \left( y - \mu _ { U } \left( y \right) \right) } = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } , \quad e ^ { - n \left( y - \mu _ { L } \left( y \right) \right) } = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } ,$$

which give us the solutions

$$\mu _ { U } \left( y \right) = y + \frac { 1 } { n } \log \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { 2 } \right) , \quad \mu _ { L } \left( y \right) = y + \frac { 1 } { n } \log \left( \frac { \alpha } { 2 } \right) .$$

Hence, the random interval

$$C \left( Y \right) = \left\{ \mu : Y + \frac { 1 } { n } \log \left( \frac { \alpha } { 2 } \right) \leq \mu \leq Y + \frac { 1 } { n } \log \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { 2 } \right) \right\} ,$$

a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\mu .$
||

Note two things about the use of this method. First, the actual equations (9.2.15)
need to be solved only for the value of the statistics actually observed. If $T = t _ { 0 }$ is
observed, then the realized confidence interval on $\theta$ will be $\left[ \theta _ { L } \left( t _ { 0 } \right) , \theta _ { U } \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \right] .$ Thus, we
need to solve only the two equations

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { t _ { 0 } } f _ { T } \left( u | \theta _ { U } \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \right) d u = \alpha _ { 1 }$$
$$\int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { \infty } f _ { T } \left( u | \theta _ { L } \left( t _ { 0 } \right) \right) d u = \alpha _ { 2 }$$
and

for $\theta _ { L } \left( t _ { 0 } \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t _ { 0 } \right) .$ Second, realize that even if these equations cannot be solved
analytically, we really only need to solve them numerically since the proof that we
have a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval did not require an analytic solution.

We now consider the discrete case.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="434" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->

Theorem 9.2.14 (Pivoting a discrete cdf) Let $T$ be a discrete statistic with cdf
$F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) = P \left( T \leq t | \theta \right) .$ Let $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$ be fixed values. Suppose that
for each $t \in \mathcal{I}$ , $\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( t \right)$ can $b e$ defined as follows.

i. If $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing function of $\theta$ for each t, define $\theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ by

$$P \left( T \leq t | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 1 } , \quad P \left( T \geq t | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 2 } .$$

ii. If $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is an increasing function of $\theta$ for each t, define $\theta _ { L } \left( t \right)$ and $\theta _ { U } \left( t \right)$ by

$$P \left( T \geq t | \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 1 } , \quad P \left( T \leq t | \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \right) = \alpha _ { 2 } .$$

Then the random interval $\left[ \theta _ { L } \left( T \right) , \theta _ { U } \left( T \right) \right]$ is a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\theta .$

Proof: We will only sketch the proof of part (i). The details, as well as the proof of
part (ii), are left to Exercise 9.20.

First recall Exercise 2.10, where it was shown that $F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right)$ is stochastically greater
than a uniform random variable, that is, $P _ { \theta } \left( F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq x \right) \leq x .$ Furthermore, this
property is shared by $\bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) = P \left( T \geq t | \theta \right) ,$ and this implies that the set

$$\left\{ \theta : F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 1 } \text { and } \bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 2 } \right\}$$

is a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set.

The fact that $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing function of $\theta$ for each t implies that $\bar { F } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is
a nondecreasing function of $\theta$ for each $t .$ It therefore follows that

$$\theta > \theta _ { U } \left( t \right) \Rightarrow F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) < \frac { \alpha } { 2 } ,$$
$$\theta < \theta _ { L } \left( t \right) \Rightarrow \overrightarrow { F } _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) < \frac { \alpha } { 2 } ,$$

and hence $\left\{ \theta : F _ { T ^ { \prime } } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 1 } \right.$ and $\left. \bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} = \left\{ \theta : \theta _ { \mathrm { L } } \left( T \right) \leq \theta \leq \theta _ { \mathrm { U } } \left( T \right) \right\} .$
☐

We close this section with an example to illustrate the construction of Theorem
9.2.14. Notice that an alternative interval can be constructed by inverting an LRT
(see Exercise 9.23).

Example 9.2.15 (Poisson interval estimator) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random
sample from a Poisson population with parameter $\lambda$ and define $Y = \sum X _ { i } .$ $Y$ is
sufficient for $\lambda$ and $Y \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( n \lambda \right) .$ Applying the above method with $\alpha _ { 1 } = \alpha _ { 2 } =$
$\alpha / 2 ,$ if $Y = y _ { 0 }$ is observed, we are led to solve for $\lambda$ in the equations

(9.2.16)
$$\sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { y _ { 0 } } e ^ { - n \lambda } \frac { \left( n \lambda \right) ^ { k } } { k ! } = \frac { \alpha } { 2 }$$
$$\sum _ { k = y _ { 0 } } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - n \lambda } \frac { \left( n \lambda \right) ^ { k } } { k ! } = \frac { \alpha } { 2 }$$
and

Recall the identity, from Example 3.3.1, linking the Poisson and gamma families.
Applying that identity to the sums in (9.2.16), we can write (remembering that $y _ { 0 }$ is
the observed value of $\left. Y \right)$

$$\frac { \alpha } { 2 } = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { y _ { 0 } } e ^ { - n \lambda } \frac { \left( n \lambda \right) ^ { k } } { k ! } = P \left( Y \leq y _ { 0 } | \lambda \right) = P \left( \chi _ { 2 \left( y _ { 0 } + 1 \right) } ^ { 2 } > 2 n \lambda \right) ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="435" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->

where $\chi _ { 2 \left( y _ { 0 } + 1 \right) } ^ { 2 }$ is a chi squared random variable with $2 \left( y _ { 0 } + 1 \right)$ degrees of freedom.
Thus, the solution to the above equation is to take

$$\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 \left( y _ { 0 } + 1 \right) , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } .$$

Similarly, applying the identity to the other equation in (9.2.16) yields

$$\frac { \alpha } { 2 } = \sum _ { k = y _ { 0 } } ^ { \infty } e ^ { - n \lambda } \frac { \left( n \lambda \right) ^ { k } } { k ! } = P \left( Y \geq y _ { 0 } | \lambda \right) = P \left( \chi _ { 2 y _ { 0 } } ^ { 2 } < 2 n \lambda \right) .$$

Doing some algebra, we obtain the $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\lambda$ as

(9.2.17)
$$\left\{ \lambda : \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 y _ { 0 } , 1 - \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 \left( y _ { 0 } + 1 \right) , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right\} .$$

(At $y _ { 0 } = 0$ we define $x _ { 0 , 1 - \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } = 0$

These intervals were first derived by Garwood (1936). A graph of the coverage
probabilities is given in Figure 9.2.5. Notice that the graph is quite jagged. The jumps
occur at the endpoints of the different confidence intervals, where terms are added or
subtracted from the sum that makes up the coverage probability. (See Exercise 9.24.)

For a numerical example, consider $n = 1 0$ and observe $y _ { 0 } = \sum x _ { i } = 6 .$ A 90%
confidence interval for $\lambda$ is given by

$$\frac { 1 } { 2 0 } \chi _ { 1 2 , 9 5 } ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 0 } \chi _ { 1 4 , 0 5 } ^ { 2 } ,$$

which is

$$. 2 6 2 \leq \lambda \leq 1 . 1 8 4 .$$

Similar derivations, involving the negative binomial and binomial distributions, are
given in the exercises.
||


### 9.2.4 Bayesian Intervals

Thus far, when describing the interactions between the confidence interval and the
parameter, we have carefully said that the interval covers the parameter, not that
the parameter is inside the interval. This was done on purpose. We wanted to stress
that the random quantity is the interval, not the parameter. Therefore, we tried to
make the action verbs apply to the interval and not the parameter.

In Example 9.2.15 we saw that if $y _ { 0 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 1 0 } x _ { i } = 6 ,$ then a 90% confidence interval
for $\lambda$ is $. 2 6 2 \leq \lambda \leq 1 . 1 8 4 .$ It is tempting to say (and many experimenters do) that "the
probability is 90% that $\lambda$ is in the interval [.262, 1.184]." Within classical statistics,
however, such a statement is invalid since the parameter is assumed fixed. Formally,
the interval [.262, 1.184] is one of the possible realized values of the random interval
$\left[ \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 Y , . 9 5 } ^ { 2 } \right. ,$ $\left. \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 \left( Y + 1 \right) , 0 5 } ^ { 2 } \right]$ and, since the parameter $\lambda \text { does not }$ move, $\lambda$ is in the realized
interval $\left[ . 2 6 2 , 1 . 1 8 4 \right]$ with probability either 0 or 1. When we say that the realized

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="436" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->

interval [.262, 1.184] has a 90% chance of coverage, we only mean that we know that
90% of the sample points of the random interval cover the true parameter.

In contrast, the Bayesian setup allows us to say that $\lambda$ is inside [.262, 1.184] with
some probability, not 0 or 1. This is because, under the Bayesian model, $\lambda$ is a random
variable with a probability distribution. All Bayesian claims of coverage are made with
respect to the posterior distribution of the parameter.

To keep the distinction between Bayesian and classical sets clear, since the sets
make quite different probability assessments, the Bayesian set estimates are referred
to as credible sets rather than confidence sets.

Thus, if $\pi \left( \theta | x \right)$ is the posterior distribution of $\theta$ given $X = x ,$ then for any set
$A \subset \Theta ,$ the credible probability of $A$ is

(9.2.18)
$$P \left( \theta \in A | x \right) = \int _ { A } \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d \theta ,$$

and $A$ is a credible $s e t$ for $\theta .$ If $\pi \left( \theta | x \right)$ is a pmf, we replace integrals with sums in the
above expressions.

Notice that both the interpretation and construction of the Bayes credible set are
more straightforward than those of a classical confidence set. However, remember that
nothing comes free. The ease of construction and interpretation comes with additional
assumptions. The Bayesian model requires more input than the classical model.

Example 9.2.16 (Poisson credible set) We now construct a credible set for the
problem of Example 9.2.15. Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $\mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right)$ and assume that $\lambda$ has
a gamma prior pdf, $\lambda \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( a , b \right) .$ The posterior pdf of $\lambda$ (see Exercise 7.24) is

(9.2.19)
$$\pi \left( \lambda | \sum X = \sum x \right) = \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( a + \sum x , \left[ n + \left( 1 / b \right) \right] ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$

We can form a credible set for $\lambda$ in many different ways, as any set $A$ satisfying
(9.2.18) will do. One simple way is to split the $a$ equally between the upper and lower
endpoints. From (9.2.19) it follows that $\frac { 2 \left( n b + 1 \right) } { b } \lambda \sim \chi _ { 2 \left( a + \Sigma x _ { i } \right) } ^ { 2 }$ (assuming that $a$ is an
integer), and thus a $1 - \alpha$ credible interval is

(9.2.20)
$$\left\{ \lambda : \frac { b } { 2 \left( n b + 1 \right) } \chi _ { 2 \left( \Sigma x + a \right) , 1 - \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda \leq \lambda \leq \frac { b } { 2 \left( n b + 1 \right) } \chi _ { 2 \left( \Sigma x + a \right) , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right\} .$$

If we take $a = b = 1 ,$ the posterior distribution of $\lambda$ given $\sum X = \sum x$ can then
be expressed as $2 \left( n + 1 \right) \lambda \sim \chi _ { 2 \left( \Sigma x + 1 \right) } ^ { 2 } .$ As in Example 9.2.15, assume $n = 1 0$ and
$\sum x = 6 .$ Since $\chi _ { 1 4 , . 9 5 } ^ { 2 } = 6 . 5 7 1$ and $\chi _ { 1 4 , . 0 5 } ^ { 2 } = 2 3 . 6 8 5 ,$ a 90% credible set for $\lambda$ is given
by [.299, 1.077].

The realized 90% credible set is different from the 90% confidence set obtained in
Example 9.2.15, [.262, 1.184]. To better see the differences, look at Figure 9.2.3, which
shows the 90% credible intervals and 90% confidence intervals for a range of $x$ values.
Notice that the credible set has somewhat shorter intervals, and the upper endpoints
are closer to 0. This reflects the prior, which is pulling the intervals toward 0.
|

It is important not to confuse credible probability (the Bayes posterior probability)
with coverage probability (the classical probability). The probabilities are very differ-
ent entities, with different meanings and interpretations. Credible probability comes

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="437" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 9.2.3. The 90% credible intervals (dashed lines) and 90% confidence intervals (solid lines) from Example 9.2.16</figcaption>

$x$

8

6

4

2

0

L

1

1

.2

.4

.6

.8

1.0

1.2

$\lambda$

</figure>


from the posterior distribution, which in turn gets its probability from the prior dis-
tribution. Thus, credible probability reflects the experimenter's subjective beliefs, as
expressed in the prior distribution and updated with the data to the posterior dis-
tribution. A Bayesian assertion of 90% coverage means that the experimenter, upon
combining prior knowledge with data, is 90% sure of coverage.

Coverage probability, on the other hand, reflects the uncertainty in the sampling
procedure, getting its probability from the objective mechanism (^ repeated experi-
mental trials. A classical assertion of 90% coverage means that in a long sequence of
identical trials, 90% of the realized confidence sets will cover the true parameter.

Statisticians sometimes argue as to which is the better way to do statistics, classical
or Bayesian. We do not want to argue or even defend one over another. In fact,
we believe that there is no one best way to do statistics; some problems are best
solved with classical statistics and some are best solved with Bayesian statistics. The
important point to realize is that the solutions may be quite different. A Bayes solution
is often not reasonable under classical evaluations and vice versa.

Example 9.2.17 (Poisson credible and coverage probabilities) The 90% con-
fidence and credible sets of Example 9.2.16 maintain their respective probability guar-
antees, but how do they fare under the other criteria? First, lets look at the credible
probability of the confidence set (9.2.17), which is given by

(9.2.21)
$$P \left\{ \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 \Sigma x , 1 - \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \leq \lambda \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \chi _ { 2 \left( \Sigma x + 1 \right) , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right\} ,$$

where $\lambda$ has the distribution (9.2.19). Figure 9.2.4 shows the credible probability of
the set (9.2.20), which is constant at $1 - \alpha ,$ along with the credible probability of the
confidence set (9.2.21).

This latter probability seems to be steadily decreasing, and we want to know if it
remains above 0 for all values of $\sum x _ { i }$ (for each fixed n). To do this, we evali ste the
probability as $\sum x _ { i } \rightarrow \infty .$ Details are left to Exercise 9.30, but it is the case that, as

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="438" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 9.2.4. Credible probabilities of the 90% credible intervals (dashed line) and 90% con- fidence intervals (solid line) from Example 9.2.16</figcaption>

Cred. prob.

.98

.94

.90

.86

1

10

50

$x$

</figure>


$\sum x _ { i } \rightarrow \infty ,$ the probability $\left( 9 . 2 . 2 1 \right) \rightarrow 0$ unless $b = 1 / n .$ Thus, the confidence interval
cannot maintain a nonzero credible probability.

The credible set (9.2.20) does not fare much better when evaluated as a confidence
set. Figure 9.2.5 suggests that the coverage probability of the credible set is going to
0 as $\lambda \rightarrow \infty .$ To evaluate the coverage probability, write

$$\lambda = \frac { \lambda } { \chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 } } \chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 } ,$$

where $\chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 }$ is a chi squared random variable with $2 Y$ degrees of freedom, and $Y$ ~
Poisson $\left( n \lambda \right) .$ Then, as $\lambda \rightarrow \infty ,$ $\lambda / \chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 1 / \left( 2 n \right) ,$ and the coverage probability of
(9.2.20) becomes

$$P \left( \frac { n b } { n b + 1 } \chi _ { 2 \left( Y + a \right) , 1 - \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \leq \chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { n b } { n b + 1 } \chi _ { 2 \left( Y + a \right) , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$
(9.2.2)

That this probability goes to 0 as $\lambda \rightarrow \infty$ is established in Exercise 9.31.
II

The behavior exhibited in Example 9.2.17 is somewhat typical. Here is an example
where the calculations can be done explicitly.

Example 9.2.18 (Coverage of a normal credible set) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid
$n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and let $\theta$ have the prior pdf $n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\mu ,$ $\sigma ,$ and $\tau$ are all known. In
Example 7.2.16 we saw that

$$\pi \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) \sim n \left( \delta ^ { B } \left( \bar { x } \right) , \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) \right) ,$$

where

$$\delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } + n \tau ^ { 2 } } \mu + \frac { n \tau ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } + n \tau ^ { 2 } } \bar { x } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } \tau ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } + n \tau ^ { 2 } } .$$

It therefore follows that under the posterior distribution,

$$\frac { \theta - \delta ^ { B } \left( \bar { x } \right) } { \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) } } \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$$

<!-- PageNumber="439" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.2" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF FINDING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->

</figure>


and a $1 - \alpha$ credible set for $\theta$ is given by

(9.2.23)
$$\delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) - z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) } \leq \theta \leq \delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) + z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) } .$$

We now calculate the coverage probability of the Bayesian region (9.2.23). Under
the classical model $\bar { X }$ is the random variable, $\theta$ is fixed, and $\bar { X } \sim n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) .$ For ease
☒ ☒
of notation define $\gamma = \sigma ^ { 2 } / \left( n \tau ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and from the definitions of $\delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { X } \right)$ and $\mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { X } \right)$ and

☒ ☒
a little algebra, the coverage probability of (9.2.23) is

$$P _ { \theta } \left( | \theta - \delta ^ { B } \left( \bar { X } \right) | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { X } \right) } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( | \theta - \left( \frac { \gamma } { 1 + \gamma } \mu + \frac { 1 } { 1 + \gamma } \bar { X } \right) | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n \left( 1 + \gamma \right) } } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( - \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \theta - \mu \right) } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \leq Z \leq \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \theta - \mu \right) } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right) ,$$ ☒

where the last equality used the fact that $\sqrt { n } \left( \bar { X } - \theta \right) / \sigma = Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

Although we started with a $1 - \alpha$ credible set, we do not have a $1 - \alpha$ confidence
set, as can be seen by considering the following parameter configuration. Fix $\theta \neq \mu$
and let $\tau = \sigma / \sqrt { n } ,$ so that $\gamma = 1 .$ Also, let $\sigma / \sqrt { n }$ be very small $\left( \rightarrow 0 \right.$ Then it is easy
☒ ☒
to see that the above probability goes to 0, since if $\theta > \mu$ the lower bound goes to
infinity, and if $\theta < \mu$ the upper bound goes to $- \infty .$ If $\theta = \mu ,$ however, the coverage
probability is bounded away from 0.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="440" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

On the other hand, the usual $1 - \alpha$ confidence set for $\theta$ is $\left\{ \theta : | \theta - \bar { x } | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right\} .$
The credible probability of this $\mathrm { s e t }$ (now $\left. \theta \sim \pi \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) \right)$ is given by

$$P _ { \bar { x } } \left( | \theta - \bar { x } | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \bar { x } } \left( | \left[ \theta - \delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) \right] + \left[ \delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) - \bar { x } \right] | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \bar { x } } \left( - \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) } { \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \leq Z \leq \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) } { \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } \sigma / } . \right.$$

where the last equality used the fact that $\left( \theta - \delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) \right) / \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) } = Z \sim \mathrm { n } \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$
Again, it is fairly easy to show that this probability is not bounded away from 0,
showing that the confidence set is also not, in general, a credible set. Details are in
Exercise 9.32.
II


## 9.3 Methods of Evaluating Interval Estimators

We now have seen many methods for deriving confidence sets and, in fact, we can
derive different confidence sets for the same problem. In such situations we would,
of course, want to choose a best one. Therefore, we now examine some methods and
criteria for evaluating set estimators.

In set estimation two quantities vie against each other, size and coverage probability.
Naturally, we want our set to have small size and large coverage probability, but such
sets are usually difficult to construct. (Clearly, we can have a large coverage probability
by increasing the size of our set. The interval $\left( - \infty , \infty \right)$ has coverage probability 1!)
Before we can optimize a set with respect to size and coverage probability, we must
decide how to measure these quantities.

The coverage probability of a confidence set will, except in special cases, be a func-
tion of the parameter, so there is not one value to consider but an infinite number
of values. For the most part, however, we will measure coverage probability perfor-
mance by the confidence coefficient, the infimum of the coverage probabilities. This
is one way, but not the only available way of summarizing the coverage probability
information. (For example, we could calculate an average coverage probability.)

When we speak of the size of a confidence set we will usually mean the length of the
confidence set, if the set is an interval. If the set is not an interval, or if we are dealing
with a multidimensional set, then length will usually become volume. (There are also
cases where a size measure other than length is natural, especially if equivariance is
a consideration. This topic is treated by Schervish 1995, Chapter 6, and Berger 1985,
Chapter 6.)


### 9.3.1 Size and Coverage Probability

We now consider what appears to be a simple, constrained minimization problem. For
a given, specified coverage probability find the confidence interval with the shortest
length. We first consider an example.

).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="441" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

Example 9.3.1 (Optimizing length) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\sigma$ is
known. From the method of Section 9.2.2 and the fact that

$$Z = \frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } }$$

is a pivot with a standard normal distribution, any $a$ and $b$ that satisfy

$$P \left( a \leq Z \leq b \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

will give the $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval

$$\left\{ \mu : \bar { x } - b \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } - a \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right\} .$$

Which choice of $a$ and $b$ is best? More formally, what choice of $a$ and $b$ will minimize
the length of the confidence interval while maintaining $1 - \alpha$ coverage? Notice that
the length of the confidence interval is equal to $\left( b - a \right) \sigma / \sqrt { n }$ but, since the factor
$\sigma / \sqrt { n }$ is part of each interval length, it can be ignored and length comparisons can
be based on the value of $b \quad -$ a. Thus, we want to find a pair of numbers $a$ and $b$ that
satisfy $P \left( a \leq Z \leq b \right) = 1 - \alpha$ and minimize $b \quad - \quad a .$

In Example 9.2.1 we took $a = - z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ and $b = z _ { \alpha / 2 } ,$ but no mention was made of
optimality. If we take $1 - \alpha = . 9 0 ,$ then any of the following pairs of numbers give
90% intervals:

This numerical study suggests that the choice $a = - \quad 1 . 6 5$ and $b = 1 . 6 5$ gives the best
interval and, in fact, it does. In this case splitting the probability $\alpha$ equally is an
optimal strategy.
Il

The strategy of splitting @ equally, which is optimal in the above case, is not always
optimal. What makes the equal $\alpha$ split optimal in the above case is the fact that the
height of the pdf is the same at $- z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ and $z _ { \alpha / 2 } .$ We now prove a theorem that will
demonstrate this fact, a theorem that is applicable in some generality, needing only
the assumption that the pdf is unimodal. Recall the definition of unimodal: A pdf
$f \left( x \right)$ is unimodal if there exists $x ^ { * }$ such that $f \left( x \right)$ is nondecreasing for $x \leq x ^ { * }$ and
$f \left( x \right)$ is nonincreasing for $x \geq x ^ { * } .$ (This is a rather weak requirement.)

Theorem 9.3.2 Let $f \left( x \right)$ be $\mathfrak{a}$ unimodal pdf. If the interval $\left[ a , b \right]$ satisfies

i. $\int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x = 1 - \alpha ,$

ii. $f \left( a \right) = f \left( b \right) > 0 ,$ and

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="442" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

iii. $a \leq x ^ { * } \leq b ,$ where $x ^ { * }$ is a mode of $f \left( x \right) ,$

then $\left[ a , b \right]$ is the shortest among all intervals that satisfy (i).

Proof: Let $\left[ a ^ { \prime } , b ^ { \prime } \right]$ be any interval with $b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } < b - a .$ We will show that this implies
$\int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { b ^ { \prime } } f \left( x \right) d x < 1 - \alpha .$ The result will be proved only for $a ^ { \prime } \leq a ,$ the proof being similar
if $a < a ^ { \prime } .$ Also, two cases need to be considered, $b ^ { \prime } \leq a$ and $b ^ { \prime } > a .$

If $b ^ { \prime } \leq a ,$ then $a ^ { \prime } \leq b ^ { \prime } \leq a \leq x ^ { * }$ and

$$\int _ { - 1 } ^ { b ^ { \prime } } f \left( x \right) d x \leq f \left( b ^ { \prime } \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } \right) \quad \left( x \leq b ^ { \prime } \leq x ^ { * } \Rightarrow f \left( x \right) \leq f \left( b ^ { \prime } \right) \right)$$
$$\leq f \left( a \right) \left( b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } \right) \quad \left( b ^ { \prime } \leq a \leq x ^ { * } \Rightarrow f \left( b ^ { \prime } \right) \leq f \left( a \right) \right)$$
$$< f \left( a \right) \left( b \quad - \quad a \right)$$
$$\leq \int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x$$
(b' - a' < b - a and f(a) > 0)
$d x \quad \left( \begin{array}{} { \text { \left(ii\right), } \left( \text { iii } \right) , \text { and unin } } \\ { \Rightarrow f \left( x \right) \geq f \left( a \right) \text { for } } \end{array} \right.$ _ x <b,
)

$$= 1 - \alpha ,$$
(i)

completing the proof in the first case.

If $b ^ { \prime } > a ,$ then $a ^ { \prime } \leq a < b ^ { \prime } < b$ for, if $b ^ { \prime }$ were greater than or equal to $b ,$ then $b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime }$
would be greater than or equal to $b \quad - \quad a .$ In this case, we can write

$$\int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { b ^ { \prime } } f \left( x \right) d x = \int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x + \left[ \int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { a } f \left( x \right) d x - \int _ { b ^ { \prime } } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x \right]$$
$$= \left( 1 - \alpha \right) + \left[ \int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { a } f \left( x \right) d x - \int _ { b ^ { \prime } } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x \right] ,$$

and the theorem will be proved if we show that the expression in square brackets is
negative. Now, using the unimodality of $f ,$ the ordering $a ^ { \prime } \leq a < b ^ { \prime } < b ,$ and (ii), we
have

$$\int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { a } f \left( x \right) d x \leq f \left( a \right) \left( a - a ^ { \prime } \right)$$

and

$$\int _ { b ^ { \prime } } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x \geq f \left( b \right) \left( b - b ^ { \prime } \right) .$$

Thus,

$$\int _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { a } f \left( x \right) d x - \int _ { b ^ { \prime } } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x \leq f \left( a \right) \left( a - a ^ { \prime } \right) - f \left( b \right) \left( b - b ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$= f \left( a \right) \left[ \left( a - a ^ { \prime } \right) - \left( b - b ^ { \prime } \right) \right] \quad \left( f \left( a \right) = f \left( b \right) \right)$$
$$= f \left( a \right) \left[ \left( b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } \right) - \left( b - a \right) \right] ,$$

which is negative if $\left( b ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } \right) < \left( b - a \right)$ and $f \left( a \right) > 0 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="443" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

If we are willing to put more assumptions on $f ,$ for instance, that $f$ is continuous,
then we can simplify the proof of Theorem 9.3.2. (See Exercise 9.38.)

Recall the discussion after Example 9.2.3 about the form of likelihood regions, which
we now see is an optimal construction by Theorem 9.3.2. A similar argument, given
in Corollary 9.3.10, shows how this construction $\mathrm { y }$ yields an optimal Bayesian region.
Also, we can see now that the equal a split, which is optimal in Example 9.3.1, will
be optimal for any symmetric unimodal pdf (see Exercise 9.39). Theorem 9.3.2 may
even apply when the optimality criterion is somewhat different from minimum length.

Example 9.3.3 (Optimizing expected length) For normal intervals based on
the pivot
$\frac { \bar { X } - \mu } { S / \sqrt { n } }$
we know that the shortest length $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval of the form

$$x - b \frac { s } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } - a \frac { s } { \sqrt { n } }$$

has $a = - t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 }$ and $b = t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } .$ The interval length is a function of s, with
general form

$$\mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( s \right) = \left( b - a \right) \frac { s } { \sqrt { n } }$$

It is easy to see that if we had considered the criterion of expected length and wanted
to find a $1 - \alpha$ interval to minimize

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \sigma } \left( \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( S \right) \right) = \left( b - a \right) \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { \sigma } S } { \sqrt { n } } = \left( b - a \right) c \left( n \right) \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } ,$$

then Theorem 9.3.2 applies and the choice $a = - t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 }$ and $b = t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 }$ again
gives the optimal interval. (The quantity $c \left( n \right)$ is a constant dependent only on $n .$ See
Exercise 7.50.)
11

In some cases, especially when working outside of the location problem, we must be
careful in the application of Theorem 9.3.2. In scale cases in particular, the theorem
may not be directly applicable, but a variant may be.

Example 9.3.4 (Shortest pivotal interval) Suppose $X \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( k , \beta \right) .$ The
quantity $Y = X / \beta$ is a pivot, with $Y \sim \mathrm { g a m m a } \left( k , 1 \right) ,$ so we can get a confidence
interval by finding constants $a$ and $b$ to satisfy

(9.3.1)
$$P \left( a \leq Y \leq b \right) = 1 - \alpha .$$

However, blind application of Theorem 9.3.2 will not give the shortest confidence
interval. That is, choosing a and $b$ to satisfy (9.3.1) and also $f _ { Y } \left( a \right) = f _ { Y } \left( b \right)$ is not
optimal. This is because, based on (9.3.1), the interval on $\beta$ is of the form

$$\left\{ \beta : \frac { x } { b } \leq \beta \leq \frac { x } { a } \right\} ,$$

so the length of the interval is $\left( \frac { 1 } { a } - \frac { 1 } { b } \right) x$ that is, it is proportional to $\left( 1 / a \right) - \left( 1 / b \right)$
and not to $b \quad - \quad a .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="444" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

Although Theorem 9.3.2 is not directly applicable here, a modified argument can
solve this problem. Condition (a) in Theorem 9.3.2 defines $b$ as a function of a, say
$b \left( a \right) .$ We must solve the following constrained minimization problem:

Minimize, with respect to a: $\frac { 1 } { a } - \frac { 1 } { b \left( a \right) }$

$$\int _ { a } ^ { b \left( a \right) } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y = 1 - \alpha .$$

Differentiating the first equation with respect to $a$ and setting it equal to 0 yield the
identity $d b / d a = b ^ { 2 } / a ^ { 2 } .$ Substituting this in the derivative of the second equation,
which must equal 0, gives $f \left( b \right) b ^ { 2 } = f \left( a \right) a ^ { 2 }$ (see Exercise 9.42). Equations like these
also arise in interval estimation of the variance of a normal distribution; see Example
9.2.10 and Exercise 9.52. Note that the above equations define not the shortest overall
interval, but the shortest pivotal interval, that is, the shortest interval based on the
pivot $X / \beta .$ For a generalization of this result, involving the Neyman-Pearson Lemma,
see Exercise 9.43.
||


### 9.3.2 Test-Related Optimality

Since there is a one-to-one correspondence between confidence sets and tests of hy-
potheses (Theorem 9.2.2), there is some correspondence between optimality of tests
and optimality of confidence sets. Usually, test-related optimality properties of con-
fidence sets do not directly relate to the size of the set but rather to the probability
of the set covering false values.

The probability of covering false values, or the probability of false coverage, indi-
rectly measures the size of a confidence set. Intuitively, smaller sets cover fewer values
and, hence, are less likely to cover false values. Moreover, we will later see an equation
that links size and probability of false coverage.

We first consider the general situation, where $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ and we construct a $1 - \alpha$
confidence set for $\theta ,$ $C \left( x \right) ,$ by inverting an acceptance region, $A \left( \theta \right) .$ The probability
of coverage of $C \left( x \right) ,$ that is, the probability of true coverage, is the function of $\theta$ given
by $P _ { \theta } \left( \theta \in C \left( X \right) \right) .$ The probability of false coverage is the function of $\theta$ and $\theta ^ { \prime }$ defined
by

$$P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) , \theta \neq \theta ^ { \prime } , \text { if } C \left( X \right) = \left[ L \left( X \right) , U \left( X \right) \right] ,$$

(9.3.2)
$$P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) , \theta ^ { \prime } < \theta , \quad \text { if } C \left( X \right) = \left[ L \left( X \right) , \infty \right) ,$$
$$P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) , \theta ^ { \prime } > \theta , \text { if } C \left( X \right) = \left( - \infty , U \left( X \right) \right] ,$$

the probability of covering $\theta ^ { \prime }$ when $\theta$ is the true parameter.

It makes sense to define the probability of false coverage differently for one-sided
and two-sided intervals. For example, if we have a lower confidence bound, we are
asserting that $\theta$ is greater than a certain value and false coverage would occur only if
we cover values of $\theta$ that are too small. A similar argument leads us to the definitions
used for upper confidence bounds and two-sided bounds.

A $1 - \alpha$ confidence set that minimizes the probability of false coverage over a
class of $1 - \alpha$ confidence sets is called a uniformly most accurate (UMA) confidence

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="445" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

set. Thus, for example, we would consider looking for a UMA confidence set among
sets of the form $\left[ L \left( x \right) , \infty \right) .$ UMA confidence sets are constructed by inverting the
acceptance regions of UMP tests, as we will prove below. Unfortunately, although a
UMA confidence set is a desirable set, it exists only in rather rare circumstances (as
do UMP tests). In particular, since UMP tests are generally one-sided, so are UMA
intervals. They make for elegant theory, however. In the next theorem we see that a
UMP test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$ yields a UMA lower confidence bound.

Theorem 9.3.5 Let $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where $\theta$ is a real-valued parameter. For each
$\theta _ { 0 } \in \Theta ,$ let $A ^ { * } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ be the $U M P$ level $\alpha$ acceptance region of a test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } .$ Let $C ^ { * } \left( x \right)$ be the $1 \quad - \quad a$ confidence set formed by inverting the $U M P$
acceptance regions. Then for any other $1 - \alpha$ confidence set $C ,$

$$P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C ^ { * } \left( X \right) \right) \leq P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) \text { for all } \theta ^ { \prime } < \theta .$$

Proof: Let $\theta ^ { \prime }$ be any value less than $\theta .$ Let $A \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right)$ be the acceptance region of the level
$\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta ^ { \prime }$ obtained by inverting $C .$ Since $A ^ { * } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right)$ is the UMP acceptance
region for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta ^ { \prime }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta ^ { \prime } ,$ and since $\theta > \theta ^ { \prime } ,$ we have

$$P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C ^ { * } \left( X \right) \right) = P _ { \theta } \left( X \in A ^ { * } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) \right)$$

(invert the confidence set)

$$\leq P _ { \theta } \left( X \in A \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \right) \right) \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \text { true for any } A \\ \text { since } A ^ { * } \text { is UMP } \end{array} \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) .$$
$$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { invert } A \text { to } } \\ { \text { obtain } C } \end{array} \right)$$

Notice that the above inequality is $\leq$ because we are working with probabilities of
acceptance regions. This is 1 - power, so UMP tests will minimize these acceptance
region probabilities. Therefore, we have established that for $\theta ^ { \prime } < \theta ,$ the probabil-
ity of false coverage is minimized by the interval obtained from inverting the UMP
test.
☐

Recall our discussion in Section 9.2.1. The UMA confidence set in the above theorem
is constructed by inverting the family of tests for the hypotheses

$$H _ { 0 } : \quad \theta = \theta _ { 0 } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \quad \theta > \theta _ { 0 } ,$$

where the form of the confidence set is governed by the alternative hypothesis. The
above alternative hypotheses, which specify that $\theta _ { 0 }$ is less than a particular value,
lead to lower confidence bounds; that is, if the sets are intervals, they are of the form
$\left[ L \left( X \right) , \infty \right) .$

Example 9.3.6 (UMA confidence bound) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where
$\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known. The interval

$$C \left( \bar { x } \right) = \left\{ \mu : \mu \geq \bar { x } - z _ { \alpha } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right\}$$

is a $1 - \alpha$ UMA lower confidence bound since it can be obtained by inverting the
UMP test of $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu > \mu _ { 0 } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="446" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

The more common two-sided interval,

$$C \left( \bar { x } \right) = \left\{ \mu : \bar { x } - z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } + z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right\} , .$$

is not UMA, since it is obtained by inverting the two-sided acceptance region from
the test of $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq \mu _ { 0 } ,$ hypotheses for which no UMP test exists.
Il

In the testing problem, when considering two-sided tests, we found the property of
unbiasedness to be both compelling and useful. In the confidence interval problem,
similar ideas apply. When we deal with two-sided confidence intervals, it is reasonable
to restrict consideration to unbiased confidence sets. Remember that an unbiased test
is one in which the power in the alternative is always greater than the power in the
null. Keep that in mind when reading the following definition.

Definition 9.3.7 A $1 - \alpha$ confidence set $C \left( x \right)$ is unbiased if $P _ { \theta } \left( \theta ^ { \prime } \in C \left( X \right) \right) \leq 1 - \alpha$
☒ ☒

for all $\theta \neq \theta ^ { \prime } .$

Thus, for an unbiased confidence set, the probability of false coverage is never
more than the minimum probability of true coverage. Unbiased confidence sets can
be obtained by inverting unbiased tests. That is, if $A \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ is an unbiased level $\alpha$
acceptance region of a test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 }$ and $C \left( x \right)$ is the $1 - \alpha$
☒
confidence set formed by inverting the acceptance regions, then $C \left( x \right)$ is an unbiased
☒
$1 - \alpha$ confidence set (see Exercise 9.46).

Example 9.3.8 (Continuation of Example 9.3.6) The two-sided normal interval

$$C \left( \bar { x } \right) = \left\{ \mu : \bar { x } - z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } + z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right\}$$

is an unbiased interval. It can be obtained by inverting the unbiased test of $H _ { 0 } : \mu = \mu _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } : \mu \neq \mu _ { 0 }$ given in Example 8.3.20. Similarly, the interval (9.2.14) based on
the $t$ distribution is also an unbiased interval, since it also can be obtained by inverting
a unbiased test (see Exercise 8.38).

Il

Sets that minimize the probability of false coverage are also called Neyman-shortest.
The fact that there is a length connotation to this name is somewhat justified by the
following theorem, due to Pratt (1961).

Theorem 9.3.9 (Pratt) Let $X$ be $a$ real-valued random variable with $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$
☒
where $\theta$ is a real-valued parameter. Let $C \left( x \right) = \left[ L \left( x \right) , U \left( x \right) \right]$ be a confidence interval
for $\theta .$ If $L \left( x \right)$ and $U \left( x \right)$ are both increasing functions $o f \quad x ,$ then for any value $\theta ^ { * } ,$

(9.3.3)
$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left[ C \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right] \right) = \int _ { \theta \neq \theta ^ { * } } P _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( \theta \in C \left( \mathrm { X } \right) \right) d \theta .$$ ☒ ☒

Theorem 9.3.9 says that the expected length of $C \left( x \right)$ is equal to a sum (integral) of
the probabilities of false coverage, the integral being taken over all false values of the
parameter.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="447" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

Proof: From the definition of expected value we can write

$$\mathrm { E } _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left[ C \left( X \right) \right] \right) = \int _ { \mathcal{X} } \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left[ C \left( x \right) \right] f \left( x | \theta ^ { * } \right) d x$$
$$= \int _ { \mathcal{X} } \left[ U \left( x \right) - L \left( x \right) \right] f \left( x | \theta ^ { * } \right) d x$$
$$= \int _ { x } \left[ \int _ { L \left( x \right) } ^ { U \left( x \right) } d \theta \right] f \left( x | \theta ^ { * } \right) d x$$

(definition of length)

$$= \int _ { \Theta } \left[ \int _ { U ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) } ^ { L ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) } f \left( x | \theta ^ { * } \right) d x \right] d \theta \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \text { invert the order of } \\ \text { integration-see below } \end{array} \right)$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { using } \theta \text { as a } } \\ { \text { dummy variable } } \end{array} \right)$

$$= \int _ { \Theta } \left[ P _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( U ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) \leq X \leq L ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) \right) \right] d \theta$$
(definition)

$$= \int _ { \theta } \left[ P _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( \theta \in C \left( X \right) \right) \right] d \theta$$
$$= \int _ { \theta \neq \theta ^ { * } } \left[ P _ { \theta ^ { * } } \left( \theta \in C \left( X \right) \right) \right] d \theta .$$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { invert the } } \\ { \text { acceptance region } } \end{array} \right)$

$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { one point does } } \\ { \text { not change value } } \end{array} \right)$

The string of equalities establishes the identity and proves the theorem. The inter-
change of integrals is formally justified by Fubini's Theorem (Lehmann and Casella
1998, Section 1.2) but is easily seen to be justified as long as all of the integrands
are finite. The inversion of the confidence interval is standard, where we use the
relationship

$$\theta \in \left\{ \theta : L \left( x \right) \leq \theta \leq U \left( x \right) \right\} \Leftrightarrow x \in \left\{ x : U ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) \leq x \leq L ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right) \right\} ,$$

which is valid because $\mathrm { n } ^ { \left\{ \right. }$ the assumption that $L$ and $U$ are increasing. Note that the
theorem could be modified to apply to an interval with decreasing endpoints.
☐

Theorem 9.3.9 shows that there is a formal relationship between the length of a
confidence interval and its probability of false coverage. In the two-sided case, this
implies that minimizing the probability of false coverage carries along some guarantee
of length optimality. In the one-sided case, however, the analogy does not quite work.
In that case, intervals that are set up to minimize the probability of false coverage
are concerned with parameters in only a portion of the parameter space and length
optimality may not obtain. Madansky (1962) has given an example of a $1 - \alpha \mathrm { U M A }$
interval (one-sided) that can be beaten in the sense that another, shorter $1 - \alpha$ interval
can be constructed. (See Exercise 9.45.) Also, Maatta and Casella (1987) have shown
that an interval obtained by inverting a UMP test can be suboptimal when measured
against other reasonable criteria.


### 9.3.3 Bayesian Optimality

The goal of obtaining a smallest confidence set with a specified coverage probability
can also be attained using Bayesian criteria. If we have a posterior distribution $\pi \left( \theta | x \right) ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="448" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

the posterior distribution of $\theta$ given $X = x ,$ we would like to find the set $C \left( x \right)$ that
☒
satisfies

(i) $\int _ { C \left( x \right) } \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d x = 1 - \alpha$

(ii) Size $\left( C \left( x \right) \right) \leq \text { Size } \left( C ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) \right)$
☒ ☒

for any set $C ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ satisfying $\int _ { C ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) } \pi \left( \theta | x \right) d x \geq 1 - \alpha .$
☒

If we take our measure of size to be length, then we can apply Theorem 9.3.2 and
obtain the following result.

Corollary 9.3.10 $I f$ the posterior density $\pi \left( \theta | x \right)$ is unimodal, then for a given value
☒
of $\alpha ,$ the shortest credible interval for $\theta$ is given by

$$\left\{ \theta : \pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) \geq k \right\} \quad \mathrm { w h e r e } \quad \int _ { \left\{ \theta : \pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) \geq k \right\} } \pi \left( \theta | \mathrm { x } \right) d \theta = 1 - \alpha .$$

The credible set described in Corollary 9.3.10 is called a highest posterior density
(HPD) region, as it consists of the values of the parameter for which the posterior
density is highest. Notice the similarity in form between the HPD region and the
likelihood region.

Example 9.3.11 (Poisson HPD region) In Example 9.2.16 we derived a $1 \quad - \quad a$
credible set for a Poisson parameter. We now construct an HPD region. By Corollary
9.3.10, this region is given by $\left\{ \lambda : \pi \left( \lambda | \sum x \right) \geq k \right\} ,$ where $k$ is chosen so that

$$1 - \alpha = \int _ { \left\{ \lambda : \pi \left( \lambda | \Sigma x \right) \geq k \right\} } \pi \left( \lambda | \sum x \right) d \lambda .$$

Recall that the posterior pdf of $\lambda$ is $\left( a + \sum x \right. ,$ $\left. \left[ n + \left( 1 / b \right) \right] ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$ so we need to find
$\lambda _ { L }$ and $\lambda _ { U }$ such that

$$\pi \left( \lambda _ { L } | \sum x \right) = \pi \left( \lambda _ { U } | \sum x \right)$$
$$\int _ { \lambda _ { L } } ^ { \lambda _ { U } } \pi \left( \lambda | \sum x \right) d \lambda = 1 - \alpha .$$
and

If we take $a = b = 1$ (as in Example 9.2.16), the posterior distribution of $\lambda \mathrm { g i v e r }$
$\sum X = \sum x$ can be expressed as $2 \left( n + 1 \right) \lambda \sim \chi _ { 2 \left( \Sigma x + 1 \right) } ^ { 2 }$ and, if $n = 1 0$ and $\sum x = 6 ,$
the 90% HPD credible set for $\lambda \text { is giver }$ by [.253, 1.005].

In Figure 9.3.1 we show three $1 - \alpha$ intervals for $\lambda :$ the $1 - \alpha$ equal-tailed Bayes
credible set of Example 9.2.16, the HPD region derived here, and the classical $1 \quad - \quad a$
confidence set of Example 9.2.15.
/

The shape of the HPD region is determined by the shape of the posterior distribu-
tion. In general, the HPD region is not symmetric about a Bayes point estimator but,
like the likelihood region, is rather asymmetric. For the Poisson distribution this is
clearly true, as the above example shows. Although it will not always happen, we can
usually expect asymmetric HPD regions for scale parameter problems and symmetric
HPD regions for location parameter problems.

<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->
<!-- PageNumber="449" -->
<!-- PageHeader="METHODS OF EVALUATING INTERVAL ESTIMATORS" -->

Example 9.3.12 (Normal HPD region) The equal-tailed credible set derived in
Example 9.2.18 is, in fact, an HPD region. Since the posterior distribution of $\theta$ is
normal with mean $\delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } ,$ it follows that $\left\{ \theta : \pi \left( \theta | \bar { x } \right) \geq k \right\} = \left\{ \theta : \theta \in \delta ^ { B } \pm k ^ { \prime } \right\}$ for some $k ^ { \prime }$
(see Exercise 9.40). So the HPD region is symmetric about the mean $\delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { x } \right) .$
||


### 9.3.4 Loss Function Optimality

In the previous two sections we looked at optimality of interval estimators by first
requiring them to have a minimum coverage probability and then looking for the
shortest interval. However, it is possible to put these requirements together in one
loss function and use decision theory to search for an optimal estimator. In interval
estimation, the action space $\mathcal{A}$ will consist of subsets of the parameter space $\Theta$ and,
more formally, we might talk of "set estimation," since an optimal rule may not
necessarily be an interval. However, practical considerations lead us to mainly consider
set estimators that are intervals and, happily, many optimal procedures turn out to
be intervals.

We use $C$ (for confidence interval) to denote elements of $\mathcal{A} ,$ with the meaning of
the action $C$ being that the interval estimate $\theta \in C ^ { \prime \prime }$ is made. A decision rule $\delta \left( x \right)$
simply specifies, for each $\mathrm { x } \in \mathcal{X} ,$ which set $C \in \mathcal{A}$ will be used as an estimate of $\theta$ if
$X = x$ is observed. Thus we will use the notation $C \left( x \right) ,$ as before.

The loss function in an interval estimation problem usually includes two quantities:
a measure of whether the set estimate correctly includes the true value $\theta$ and a
measure of the size of the set estimate. We will, for the most part, consider only sets
$C$ that are intervals, so a natural measure of size is Length(C) $\left( C \right) = \mathrm { l e n g t h }$ of $C .$ To

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="450" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.3" -->

express the correctness measure, it is common to use

$$I _ { C } \left( \theta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { \theta \in C } \\ { 0 } & { \theta \notin C } \end{array} \right.$$

That is, $I _ { C } \left( \theta \right) = 1$ if the estimate is correct and 0 otherwise. In fact, $I _ { C } \left( \theta \right)$ is just the
indicator function for the set $C .$ But realize that $C$ will be a random set determined
by the value of the data $\mathrm { X } .$

The loss function should reflect the fact that a good estimate would have $\mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C \right)$
small and $I _ { C } \left( \theta \right)$ large. One such loss function is

(9.3.4)
$$L \left( \theta , C \right) = b \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C \right) - I _ { C } \left( \theta \right) ,$$

where $b$ is a positive constant that reflects the relative weight that we want to give to
the two criteria, a necessary consideration since the two quantities are very different.
If there is more concern with correct estimates, then $b$ should be small, while a large
$b$ should be used if there is more concern with interval length.

The risk function associated with (9.3.4) is particularly simple, given by

$$R \left( \theta , C \right) = b E _ { \theta } \left[ \text { Length } \left( C \left( X \right) \right) \right] - E _ { \theta } I _ { C \left( X \right) } \left( \theta \right)$$
$$= b E _ { \theta } \left[ \text { Length } \left( C \left( X \right) \right) \right] - P _ { \theta } \left( I _ { C \left( X \right) } \left( \theta \right) = 1 \right)$$
$$= b E _ { \theta } \left[ \text { Length } \left( C \left( X \right) \right) \right] - P _ { \theta } \left( \theta \in C \left( X \right) \right) .$$

The risk has two components, the expected length of the interval and the coverage
probability of the interval estimator. The risk reflects the fact that, simultaneously,
we want the expected length to be small and the coverage probability to be high,
just as in the previous sections. But now, instead of requiring $a$ minimum coverage
probability and then minimizing length, the trade-off between these two quantities is
specified in the risk function. Perhaps a smaller coverage probability will be acceptable
if it results in a greatly decreased length.

By varying the size of $b$ in the loss (9.3.4), we can vary the relative importance
of size and coverage probability of interval estimators, something that could not be
done previously. As an example of the flexibility of the present $s e t u p ,$ consider some
limiting cases. If $b = 0 ,$ then size does not matter, only coverage probability, so the
interval estimator $C = \left( - \infty , \infty \right) ,$ which has coverage probability 1, is the best decision
rule. Similarly, if $b = \infty ,$ then coverage probability does not matter, so point sets are
optimal. Hence, an entire range of decision rules are possible candidates. In the next
example, for a specified finite range of $b ,$ choosing a good rule amounts to using the
risk function to decide the confidence coefficient while, if $b$ is outside this range, the
optimal decision rule is a point estimator.

Example 9.3.13 (Normal interval estimator) Let $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ and assume $\sigma ^ { 2 }$
is known. $X$ would typically be a sample mean and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ would have the form $\tau ^ { 2 } / n ,$
where $\tau ^ { 2 }$ is the known population variance and $n$ is the sample size. For each $c \geq 0 ,$
define an interval estimator for $\mu$ by $C \left( x \right) = \left[ x - c \sigma , x + c \sigma \right] .$ We will compare these
estimators using the loss in (9.3.4). The length of an interval, $L e n g t h \left( C \left( x \right) \right) = 2 c ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="451" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

does not depend on x. Thus, the first term in the $\mathrm { r i s k }$ is $b \left( 2 c \sigma \right) .$ The second term in
the risk is

$$P _ { \mu } \left( \mu \in C \left( X \right) \right) = P _ { \mu } \left( X - c \sigma \leq \mu \leq X + c \sigma \right)$$
$$= P _ { \mu } \left( - c \leq \frac { X - \mu } { \sigma } \leq c \right)$$
$$= 2 P \left( Z \leq c \right) - 1 ,$$

where $Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ Thus, the risk function for an interval estimator in this class is

(9.3.5)
$$R \left( \mu , C \right) = b \left( 2 c \sigma \right) - \left[ 2 P \left( Z \leq c \right) - 1 \right] .$$

The risk function is constant, as it does not depend on $\mu ,$ and the best interval
estimator in this class is the one corresponding to the value $c$ that minimizes (9.3.5).

If $b \sigma > 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } ,$ it can be shown that $R \left( \mu , C \right)$ is minimized at $c = 0 .$ That is, the
length portion completely overwhelms the coverage probability portion of the loss, and
the best interval estimator is the point estimator $C \left( x \right) = \left[ x , x \right] .$ But if $b \sigma \leq 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } ,$
the risk is minimized at $c = \sqrt { - 2 \log \left( b \sigma \sqrt { 2 \pi } \right) } .$ If we express $c$ as $z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ for some a,
☒
then the interval estimator that minimizes the risk is just the usual $1 \quad - \&$ confidence
interval. (See Exercise 9.53 for details.)
11

The use of decision theory in interval estimation problems is not as widespread as
in point estimation or hypothesis testing problems. One reason for this is the difficulty
in choosing $b$ in (9.3.4) (or in Example 9.3.13). We saw in the previous example that
a choice that might seem reasonable could lead to unintuitive results, indicating that
the loss in (9.3.4) may not be appropriate. Some who would use decision theoretic
analysis for other problems still prefer to use only interval estimators with a fixed
confidence coefficient $\left( 1 - \alpha \right) .$ They then use the risk function to judge other qualities
like the size of the set.

Another difficulty is in the restriction of the shape of the allowable sets in $A .$ Ideally,
the loss and risk functions would be used to judge which shapes are best. But one
can always add isolated points to an interval estimator and get an improvement in
coverage probability with no loss penalty regarding size. In the previous example we
could have used the estimator

$$C \left( x \right) = \left[ x - c \sigma , x + c \sigma \right] \cup \left\{ \text { all integer values of } \mu \right\} .$$

The "length" of these sets is the same as before, but now the coverage probability is
1 for all integer values of $\mu .$ Some more sophisticated measure of size must be used
to avoid such anomalies. (Joshi 1969 addressed this problem by defining equivalence
classes of estimators.)


## 9.4 Exercises

9.1 If $L \left( x \right)$ and $U \left( x \right)$ satisfy $P _ { \theta } \left( L \left( X \right) \leq \theta \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 1 }$ and $P _ { \theta } \left( U \left( X \right) \geq \theta \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } ,$ and
$L \left( x \right) \leq U \left( x \right)$ for all x, show that $P _ { \theta } \left( L \left( X \right) \leq \theta \leq U \left( X \right) \right) = 1 - \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha _ { 2 } .$

9.2 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , 1 \right) .$ A 95% confidence interval for $\theta$ is $\bar { x } \pm 1 . 9 6 / \sqrt { n } .$ $L e t \quad p$
denote the probability that an additional independent observation, $X _ { n + 1 } ,$ will fall in
this interval. Is $p$ greater than, less than, or equal to .95? Prove your answer.

9.3 The independent random variables $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ have the common distribution
☒

$$P \left( X _ { i } \leq x \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { \left( x / \beta \right) ^ { \alpha } } & { \text { if } 0 < x < \beta } \\ { 1 } & { \text { if } x \geq \beta } \end{array} \right.$$

(a) In Exercise 7.10 the MLEs of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ were found. If $\alpha$ is a known constant, $\alpha _ { 0 } ,$
find an upper confidence limit for $\beta$ with confidence coefficient .95.

(b) Use the data of Exercise 7.10 to construct an interval estimate for $\beta .$ Assume that
$\alpha$ is known and equal to its MLE.

9.4 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( 0 , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ and let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { m }$ be a random
sample from a $n \left( 0 , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ independent of the $X \mathrm { s } .$ Define $\lambda = \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } .$

(a) Find the level $\alpha$ LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \lambda \neq \lambda _ { 0 } .$

(b) Express the rejection region of the LRT of part (a) in terms of an $F$ random
variable.

(c) Find a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\lambda .$

9.5 In Example 9.2.5 a lower confidence bound was put on $p ,$ the success probability from
a sequence of Bernoulli trials. This exercise will derive an upper confidence bound.
That is, observing $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } ,$ where $X _ { i } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( p \right) ,$ we want an interval of the
☒ ☒
form $0 , U \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) ,$ where $P _ { p } \left( p \in \left[ 0 , U \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right) \right) \right) \geq 1 - \alpha .$
☒ ☒

(a) Show that inversion of the acceptance region of the test

$$H _ { 0 } : p = p _ { 0 } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : p < p _ { 0 }$$

will give a confidence interval of the desired confidence level and form.

(b) Find equations, similar to those given in (9.2.8), that can be used to construct the
confidence interval.

9.6 (a) Derive a confidence interval for a binomial $p$ by inverting the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : p = p _ { 0 }$
versus $H _ { 1 } : p \neq p _ { 0 } .$

(b) Show that the interval is a highest density region from $p ^ { y } \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { n - y }$ and is not
equal to the interval $\mathrm { i n } \left( 1 0 . 4 . 4 \right) .$

9.7 (a) Find the $1 - \alpha$ confidence set for $a$ that is obtained by inverting the LRT of
$H _ { 0 } : a = a _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : a \neq a _ { 0 }$ based on a sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from $n \left( \theta , a \theta \right)$
family, where $0$ is unknown.

(b) $A$ similar question can be asked about the related family, the $n \left( \theta , a \theta ^ { 2 } \right)$ family. If
$X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( \theta , a \theta ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\theta$ is unknown, find the $1 - \alpha$ confidence set
based on inverting the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : a = a _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : a \neq a _ { 0 } .$

9.8 Given a sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from a pdf of the form $\frac { 1 } { \sigma } f \left( \left( x - \theta \right) / \sigma \right) ,$ list at least five
different pivotal quantities.

9.9 Show that each of the three quantities listed in Example 9.2.7 is a pivot.

9.10 (a) Suppose that $T$ is a real-valued statistic. Suppose that $Q \left( t , \theta \right)$ is a monotone
function of $t$ for each value of $\theta \in \Theta .$ Show that $i f$ the pdf of $T ,$ $f \left( t | \theta \right) ,$ can be
expressed in the form (9.2.11) for some function $g ,$ then $Q \left( T , \theta \right)$ is a pivot.

(b) Show that (9.2.11) is satisfied by taking $g = 1$ and $Q \left( t , \theta \right) = F _ { \theta } \left( t \right) ,$ the cdf of $T .$
(This is the Probability Integral Transform.)

9.11 If $T$ is a continuous random variable with $\mathrm { c d f } F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ and $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha ,$ show that an $\alpha$
level acceptance region of the hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ is $\left\{ t : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta _ { 0 } \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} ,$
with associated confidence $1 - \alpha$ set $\left\{ \theta : \alpha _ { 1 } \leq F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) \leq 1 - \alpha _ { 2 } \right\} .$

where $\theta > 0 .$ Use the pivotal quantity to set up a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\theta .$

(b) Find a pivotal quantity and use it to set up a confidence interval having the same
confidence coefficient as the interval in part (a).

(c) Compare the two confidence intervals.

9.14 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where both parameters are unknown. Simultaneous
inference on both $\mu$ and $\sigma$ can be made using the Bonferroni Inequality in a number
of ways.

(a) Using the Bonferroni Inequality, combine the two confidence sets

$$\left\{ \mu : \bar { x } - \frac { k s } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } + \frac { k s } { \sqrt { n } } \right\}$$
$$\left\{ \sigma ^ { 2 } : \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { b } \leq \sigma ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { a } \right\}$$
and

into one confidence set for $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) .$ Show how to choose a, $b ,$ and $k$ to make the
simultaneous set a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set.

(b) Using the Bonferroni Inequality, combine the two confidence sets

$$\left\{ \mu : \bar { x } - \frac { k \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \mu \leq \bar { x } + \frac { k \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right\}$$
$$\left\{ \sigma ^ { 2 } : \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { b } \leq \sigma ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { a } \right\}$$
and

into one confidence set for $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) .$ Show how to choose a, $b ,$ and $k$ to make the
simultaneous set a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set.

(c) Compare the confidence sets in parts (a) and (b).

9.15 Solve for the roots of the quadratic equation that defines Fieller's confidence set for the
ratio of normal means (see Miscellanea 9.5.3). Find conditions on the random variables
for which

(a) the parabola opens upward (the confidence set is an interval).

(b) the parabola opens downward (the confidence set is the complement of an interval).

(c) the parabola has no real roots.

In each case, give an interpretation of the meaning of the confidence set. For example,
what would you tell an experimenter if, for his data, the parabola had no real roots?

9.16 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known. For each of the following hypotheses,
write out the acceptance region of a level $\alpha$ test and the $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval that

(c) $H _ { 0 } : \theta \leq \theta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 }$

9.20 Some of the details of the proof of Theorem 9.2.14 need to be filled in, and the second
part of the theorem needs to be proved.

(a) Show that if $F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right)$ is stochastically greater than or equal to a uniform random
variable, then so is $\bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) .$ That is, if $P _ { \theta } \left( F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq x \right) \leq x$ for every $x ,$ $0 \leq x \leq 1 ,$
then $P _ { \theta } \left( \bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq x \right) \leq x$ for every $x ,$ $0 \leq x \leq 1 .$

(b) Show that for $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } = \alpha ,$ the set $\left\{ \theta : F _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 1 } \right.$ and $\left. \bar { F } _ { T } \left( T | \theta \right) \leq \alpha _ { 2 } \right\}$ is a
$1 - \alpha$ confidence set.

(c) If the cdf $F _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ is a decreasing function of $\theta$ for each t, show that the function
$\bar { F } _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right)$ defined by $\bar { F } _ { T } \left( t | \theta \right) = P \left( T \geq t | \theta \right)$ is a nondecreasing function of $\theta$ for each
$t .$

(d) Prove part (b) of Theorem 9.2.14.

9.21 In Example 9.2.15 it was shown that a confidence interval for a Poisson parameter can
be expressed in terms of chi squared cutoff points. Use a similar technique to show
that if $X \sim \mathrm { b i n o m i a l } \left( n , p \right) ,$ then a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $p$ is

$$\frac { 1 } { 1 + \frac { n - x + 1 } { x } F _ { 2 \left( n - x + 1 \right) , 2 x , \alpha / 2 } } \leq p \leq \frac { \frac { x + 1 } { n - x } F _ { 2 \left( x + 1 \right) , 2 \left( n - x \right) , \alpha / 2 } } { 1 + \frac { x + 1 } { n - x } F _ { 2 \left( x + 1 \right) , 2 \left( n - x \right) , \alpha / 2 } } ,$$

where $F _ { \nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , \alpha }$ is the upper $\alpha$ cutoff from an $F$ distribution with $\nu _ { 1 }$ and $\nu _ { 2 }$ degrees of
freedom, and we make the endpoint adjustment that the lower endpoint is 0 if $x = 0$
and the upper endpoint is 1 if $x = n .$ These are the Clopper and Pearson (1934)
intervals.

(Hint: Recall the following identity from Exercise 2.40, which can be interpreted in
the following way. If $X \sim \text { binomial } \left( n , \theta \right) ,$ then $P _ { \theta } \left( X \geq x \right) = P \left( Y \leq \theta \right) ,$ where $Y \sim$
$\mathrm { b e t a } \left( x , n - x + 1 \right) .$ Use the properties of the $F$ and beta distributions from Chapter
5.)

9.22 If $X \sim n e g a t i v e \quad b i n o m i a l \left( r , p \right) ,$ use the relationship between the binomial and negative
binomial to show that a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $p$ is given by

$$\frac { 1 } { 1 + \frac { x + 1 } { r } F _ { 2 \left( x + 1 \right) , 2 r , \alpha / 2 } } \leq p \leq \frac { \frac { r } { x } F _ { 2 r , 2 x , \alpha / 2 } } { 1 + \frac { r } { x } F _ { 2 r , 2 x , \alpha / 2 } } ,$$

with a suitable modification if $x = 0 .$

9.25 If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are id with pdf $f \left( x | \mu \right) = e ^ { - \left( x - \mu \right) } I _ { \left[ \mu , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) ,$ then $Y = \min \left\{ X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right\}$
is sufficient for $\mu$ with pdf

$$f _ { Y } \left( y | \mu \right) = n e ^ { - n \left( y - \mu \right) } I _ { \left[ \mu , \infty \right) } \left( y \right) .$$

In Example 9.2.13 a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\mu$ was found using the method of

Show how $\mathrm { t o }$ find a $1 - \alpha$ Bayes HPD credible set for $\lambda .$

tion, based on the sample variance $s ^ { 2 }$ and using a conjugate $I G \left( a , b \right)$ prior for

9.28 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where both $\theta$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are unknown, but there is only
interest on inference about $\theta .$ Consider the prior pdf

$$\pi \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } | \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } , a , b \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi \tau ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } } } e ^ { - \left( \theta - \mu \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \tau ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( a \right) b ^ { a } } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { a + 1 } e ^ { - 1 / \left( b \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) } ,$$

a $n \left( \mu , \tau ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ multiplied by an $I G \left( a , b \right) .$
