$$\frac { x ^ { 2 } - \nu } { \sqrt { 2 \nu } } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$$

$2 \lambda ,$ $\mathrm { V a r } \left( \chi _ { 2 Y } ^ { 2 } \right) = 8 \lambda ,$ the mgf of $x _ { Y } ^ { 2 }$ is given by $\exp \left( - \lambda + \frac { \lambda } { 1 - 2 t } \right) ,$ and

goes to 0.

9.32 In this exercise we will calculate the classical coverage probability of the HPD re-
gion in (9.2.23), that is, the coverage probability of the Bayes HPD region using the
probability model $\bar { X } \sim n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } / n \right) .$

(a) Using the definitions given in Example 9.3.12, prove that

$$P _ { \theta } \left( | \theta - \delta ^ { \mathrm { B } } \left( \bar { X } \right) | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sqrt { \mathrm { V a r } \left( \theta | \bar { X } \right) } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \theta } \left[ - \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \theta - \mu \right) } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \leq Z \leq \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } z _ { \alpha / 2 } + \frac { \gamma \left( \theta - \mu \right) } { \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right] .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="457" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

(b) Show that the above set, although a $1 - \alpha$ credible set, is not a $1 - \alpha$ confidence
set. (Fix $\theta \neq \mu ,$ let $\tau = \sigma / \sqrt { n } ,$ so that $\gamma = 1 .$ Prove that as $\sigma ^ { 2 } / n \rightarrow 0 ,$ the above
probability goes to 0.)

(c) If $\theta = \mu ,$ however, prove that the coverage probability is bounded away from 0.
Find the minimum and maximum of this coverage probability.

(d) Now we will look at the other side. The usual $1 - \alpha$ confidence set for $\theta$ is
$\left\{ \theta : | \theta - \bar { x } | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right\} .$ Show that the credible probability of this set is
given by

$$P _ { \bar { x } } \left( | \theta - \bar { x } | \leq z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right)$$
$$= P _ { \bar { x } } \left[ - \sqrt { 1 + \gamma z _ { \alpha / 2 } } + \frac { \gamma \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) } { \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \leq Z \leq \sqrt { 1 + \gamma z _ { \alpha / 2 } } + \frac { \gamma \left( \bar { x } - \mu \right) } { \sqrt { 1 + \gamma } \sigma / \sqrt { n } } \right]$$

and that this probability is not bounded away from 0. Hence, the $1 - \alpha$ confidence
set is not a $1 - \alpha$ credible set.

9.33 Let $X \sim n \left( \mu , 1 \right)$ and consider the confidence interval

$$C _ { a } \left( x \right) = \left\{ \mu : \min \left\{ 0 , \left( x - a \right) \right\} \leq \mu \leq \max \left\{ 0 , \left( x + a \right) \right\} \right\} .$$

(a) For $a = 1 . 6 4 5 ,$ prove that the coverage probability of $C _ { a } \left( x \right)$ is exactly .95 for all
$\mu ,$ with the exception of $\mu = 0 ,$ where the coverage probability is 1.

(b) Now consider the so-called noninformative prior $\pi \left( \mu \right) = 1 .$ Using this prior and
again taking $a = 1 . 6 4 5 ,$ show that the posterior credible probability of $C _ { a } \left( x \right)$ is
exactly .90 for $- 1 . 6 4 5 \leq x \leq 1 . 6 4 5$ and increases to .95 as $| x | \rightarrow \infty .$

This type of interval arises in the problem of bioequivalence, where the objective
is to decide if two treatments (different formulations of a drug, different delivery
systems of a treatment) produce the same effect. The formulation of the prob-
lem results in "turning around" the roles of the null and alternative hypotheses
(see Exercise 8.47), resulting in some interesting statistics. See Berger and Hsu
(1996) for a review of bioequivalence and Brown, Casella, and Hwang (1995) for
generalizations of the confidence set.

9.34 Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ is a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population.

(a) If $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known, find a minimum value for $n$ to guarantee that a .95 confidence
interval for $\mu$ will have length no more than $\sigma / 4 .$

(b) If $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown, find a minimum value for $n$ to guarantee, with probability .90,
that a .95 confidence interval for $\mu$ will have length no more than $\sigma / 4 .$

9.35 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population. Compare expected
lengths of $1 - \alpha$ confidence intervals for $\mu$ that are computed assuming

(a) $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known.

(b) $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown.

9.36 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be independent with pdfs $f _ { X _ { i } } \left( x | \theta \right) = e ^ { i \theta - x } I _ { \left[ i \theta , \infty \right) } \left( x \right) .$ Prove that
$T = \min _ { i } \left( X _ { i } / i \right)$ is $a$ sufficient statistic for $\theta .$ Based on $T ,$ find the $1 - \alpha$ confidence
interval for $0$ of the form $\left[ T + a , T + b \right]$ which is of minimum length.

9.37 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid uniform(0,0). Let $Y$ be the largest order statistic. Prove that
$Y / \theta$ is a pivotal quantity and show that the interval

$$\left\{ \theta : y \leq \theta \leq \frac { y } { \alpha ^ { 1 / n } } \right\}$$

is the shortest $1 - \alpha$ pivotal interval.

<!-- PageNumber="458" -->

<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

9.38 If, in Theorem 9.3.2, we assume that $f$ is continuous, then we can simplify the proof.
For fixed $c ,$ consider the integral $\int _ { a } ^ { a + c } f \left( x \right) d x .$

(a) Show that $\frac { d } { d a } \int _ { a } ^ { a + c } f \left( x \right) d x = f \left( a + c \right) - f \left( a \right) .$

(b) Prove that the unimodality of $f$ implies that $\int _ { a } ^ { a + c } f \left( x \right) d x$ is maximized when a
satisfies $f \left( a + c \right) - \quad f \left( a \right) = 0 .$

(c) Suppose that, given a, we choose $c ^ { * }$ and $a ^ { * }$ to satisfy $\int _ { a ^ { * } } ^ { a ^ { * } + c ^ { * } } f \left( x \right) d x = 1 -$
$\alpha$ and $f \left( a ^ { * } + c ^ { * } \right) - f \left( a ^ { * } \right) = 0 .$ Prove that this is the shortest $1 - \alpha$ inter-
val.

9.39 Prove a special case of Theorem 9.3.2. Let $X \sim f \left( x \right) ,$ where $f$ is a symmetric unimodal
pdf. For a fixed value of $1 - \alpha ,$ of all intervals $\left[ a , b \right]$ that satisfy $\int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x = 1 - \alpha ,$ the
shortest is obtained by choosing a and $b$ so that $\int _ { - \infty } ^ { a } f \left( x \right) d x = \alpha / 2$ and $\int _ { b } ^ { \infty } f \left( x \right) d x =$
$\alpha / 2 .$

9.40 Building on Exercise 9.39, show that if $f$ is symmetric, the optimal interval is of the
form $m \pm k ,$ where $m$ is the mode of $f$ and $k$ is a constant. Hence, show that $\left( a \right)$
symmetric likelihood functions produce likelihood regions that are symmetric about
the MLE if $k ^ { \prime }$ does not depend on the parameter (see (9.2.7)), and (b) symmet-
ric posterior densities produce HPD regions that are symmetric about the posterior
mean.

9.41 (a) Prove the following, which is related to Theorem 9.3.2. Let $X \sim f \left( x \right) ,$ where $f$ is
a strictly decreasing pdf on $\left[ 0 , \infty \right) .$ For a fixed value of $1 - \alpha ,$ of all intervals $\left[ a , b \right]$
that satisfy $\int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x = 1 - \alpha ,$ the shortest is obtained by choosing $a = 0$ and $b$
so that $\int _ { 0 } ^ { b } f \left( x \right) d x = 1 - \alpha .$

(b) Use the result of part (a) to find the shortest $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval in Example
9.2.13.

9.42 Referring to Example 9.3.4, to find the shortest pivotal interval for a gamma scale
parameter, we had $t o$ solve a constrained minimization problem.

(a) Show that the solution is given by the a and b $b$ that satisfy $\int _ { a } ^ { b } f _ { Y } \left( y \right) d y = 1 - a$
and $f \left( b \right) b ^ { 2 } = f \left( a \right) a ^ { 2 } .$

(b) With one observation from a gamma(k, $\left( k , \beta \right)$ pdf with known shape parameter $k ,$
find the shortest $1 - \alpha$ (pivotal) confidence interval of the form $\left\{ \beta : x / b \leq \beta \leq \right.$
$x / a .$

9.43 Juola (1993) makes the following observation. If we have a pivot $Q \left( X , \theta \right) ,$ a $1 - \alpha$
confidence interval involves finding $a$ and $b$ so that $P \left( a < Q < b \right) = 1 - \alpha .$ Typically
the length of the interval on $\theta$ will be some function of $a$ and $b$ like $b - a$ or $1 / b ^ { 2 } - 1 / a ^ { 2 } .$
If $Q$ has density $f$ and the length can be expressed as $\int _ { a } ^ { b } g \left( t \right) d t$ the shortest pivotal
interval is the solution to

$$\min _ { \left\{ a , b \right\} } \int _ { a } ^ { b } g \left( t \right) d t \text { subject to } \int _ { a } ^ { b } f \left( t \right) d t = 1 - \alpha$$

or, more generally,

$$\min _ { C } \int _ { C } g \left( t \right) d t \text { subject to } \int _ { C } f \left( t \right) d t \geq 1 - \alpha .$$

(a) Prove that the solution is $C = \left\{ t : g \left( t \right) < \lambda f \left( t \right) \right\} ,$ where $\lambda$ is chosen so that
$\int _ { C } f \left( t \right) d t = 1 - \alpha .$ (Hint: You can adapt the proof of Theorem 8.3.12, the Neyman-
Pearson Lemma.)

<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->

(b) Apply the result in part (a) to get the shortest intervals in Exercises 9.37 and
9.42.

9.44 (a) Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid Poisson(X). Find a UMA $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval based on
inverting the UMP level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \lambda > \lambda _ { 0 } .$

(b) Let $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ be the logistic(0, $\left( \theta , 1 \right)$ location pdf. Based on one observation, x, find the
UMA one-sided $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval of the form $\left\{ \theta : \theta \leq U \left( x \right) \right\} .$

9.45 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid $\mathrm { e x p o n e n t i a l } \left( \lambda \right) .$

(a) Find a UMP size $\alpha$ hypothesis test of $H _ { 0 } : \lambda = \lambda _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \lambda < \lambda _ { 0 } .$

(b) Find a UMA $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval based on inverting the test in part (a). Show
that the interval can be expressed as

$$C ^ { * } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left\{ \lambda : 0 \leq \lambda \leq \frac { 2 \sum x _ { i } } { \chi _ { 2 n , \alpha } ^ { 2 } } \right\} .$$

(c) Find the expected length of $C ^ { * } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$

(d) Madansky (1962) exhibited a $1 - \alpha$ interval whose expected length is shorter than
that of the UMA interval. In general, Madansky's interval is difficult to calculate,
but in the following situation calculation is relatively simple. Let $1 - \alpha = . 3$ and
$n = 1 2 0 .$ Madansky's interval is

$$C ^ { M } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left\{ \lambda : 0 \leq \lambda \leq - \frac { x _ { \left( 1 \right) } } { \log \left( . 9 9 \right) } \right\} ,$$

which is a 30% confidence interval. Use the fact that $\chi _ { 2 4 0 , . 7 } ^ { 2 } = 2 5 1 . 0 4 6$ to show
that the 30% UMA interval satisfies

$$\mathrm { E } \left[ \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C ^ { * } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \right) \right] = . 9 5 6 \lambda > \mathrm { E } \left[ \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C ^ { \mathrm { M } } \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) \right) \right] = . 8 2 9 \lambda .$$

9.46 Show that if $A \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ is an unbiased level $\alpha$ acceptance region of a test of $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ ver-
sus $H _ { 1 } : \theta \neq \theta _ { 0 }$ and $C \left( x \right)$ is the $1 - \alpha$ confidence set formed by inverting the acceptance

regions, then $C \left( x \right)$ is an unbiased $1 \quad - \quad a$ confidence set.

9.47 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known.
Show that the usual one-sided $1 - \alpha$ upper confidence bound $\left\{ \theta : \theta \leq \bar { x } + z _ { \alpha } \sigma / \sqrt { n } \right\}$ is
unbiased, and so is the corresponding lower confidence bound.

9.48 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is un-
known.

(a) Show that the interval $\theta \leq \bar { x } + t _ { n - 1 , \alpha } \frac { s } { \sqrt { n } }$ can be derived by inverting the acceptance
region of an LRT.

(b) Show that the corresponding two-sided interval in (9.2.14) can also derived by
inverting the acceptance region of an LRT.

(c) Show that the intervals in parts (a) and (b) are unbiased intervals.

9.49 $\left( \mathrm { C o x } ^ { \prime } s \text { Paradox } \right)$ We are to test

$$H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 } \quad \text { versus } \quad H _ { 1 } : \theta > \theta _ { 0 } ,$$

where $\theta$ is the mean of one of two normal distributions and $\theta _ { 0 }$ is a fixed but arbitrary
value of $\theta .$ We observe the random variable $X$ with distribution

$$X \sim \left\{ \begin{array}{} { n \left( \theta , 1 0 0 \right) } & { \text { with probability } p } \\ { n \left( \theta , 1 \right) } & { \text { with probability } 1 - p } \end{array} \right.$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="460" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

(a) Show that the test given by

$$X > \theta _ { 0 } + z _ { \alpha } ,$$

where $\sigma = 1$ or 10 depending on which population is sampled, is a level $\alpha$
test. Derive $1 - \alpha$ confidence set by inverting the acceptance region of this
test.

(b) Show that a more powerful level $\alpha$ test (for $\alpha > p$ is given by

reject $H _ { 0 }$ if $X > \theta _ { 0 } + z _ { \left( \alpha - p \right) / \left( 1 - p \right) }$ and $\sigma = 1$ otherwise always reject $H _ { 0 } .$

Derive a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set by inverting the acceptance region of this test, and
show that it is the empty set with positive probability. (Cox's Paradox states that
classic optimal procedures sometimes ignore the information about conditional
distributions and provide us with a procedure that, while optimal, is somehow
unreasonable; see Cox 1958 or Cornfield 1969.)

9.50 Let $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ and suppose that the interval $\left\{ \theta : a \left( X \right) \leq \theta \leq b \left( X \right) \right\}$ is a UMA
confidence set for $\theta .$

(a) Find a UMA confidence set for $1 / 0 .$ Note that if $a \left( x \right) < 0 < b \left( x \right) ,$ this set is
$\left\{ 1 / \theta : 1 / b \left( x \right) \leq 1 / \theta \right\} \cup \left\{ 1 / \theta : 1 / \theta \leq 1 / a \left( x \right) \right\} .$ Hence it is possible for the UMA
confidence set to be neither an interval nor bounded.

(b) Show that, if $h$ is a strictly increasing function, the set $\left\{ h \left( \theta \right) : h \left( a \left( X \right) \right) \leq h \left( \theta \right) \leq \right.$
$h \left( b \left( X \right) \right)$ is a UMA confidence set for $h \left( \theta \right) .$ Can the condition on $h$ be relaxed?

9.51 If $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are id from a location pdf $f \left( x - \theta \right) ,$ show that the confidence set

$$C \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \left\{ \theta : \bar { x } - k _ { 1 } \leq \theta \leq \bar { x } + k _ { 2 } \right\} ,$$

where $k _ { 1 }$ and $k _ { 2 }$ are constants, has constant coverage probability. (Hint: The $\mathrm { p d f } \mathrm { o f }$
$\bar { X }$ is of the form $f _ { X } \left( \bar { x } - \theta \right)$

9.52 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be a random sample from a $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ population, where both $\mu$ and
$\sigma ^ { 2 }$ are unknown. Each of the following methods of finding confidence intervals for $\sigma ^ { 2 }$
results in intervals of the form

$$\left\{ \sigma ^ { 2 } : \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { b } \leq \sigma ^ { 2 } \leq \frac { \left( n - 1 \right) s ^ { 2 } } { a } \right\} ,$$

but in each case $a$ and $b$ will satisfy different $\mathrm { c o n s t r a i n t s } .$ The intervals given in
this exercise are derived by Tate and Klett (1959), who also tabulate some cutoff
points.

Define $f _ { p } \left( t \right)$ to be the pdf of a $\chi _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ random variable with $p$ degrees of freedom. In order
to have a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval, $a$ and $b$ must satisfy

$$\int _ { a } ^ { b } f _ { n - 1 } \left( t \right) d t = 1 - \alpha ,$$

but additional constraints are required to define $a$ and $b$ uniquely. Verify that each of
the following constraints can be derived as stated.

(a) The likelihood ratio interval: The $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval obtained by inverting
the LRT of $H _ { 0 } : \sigma = \sigma _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \sigma \neq \sigma _ { 0 }$ is of the above form, where $a$ and $b$
also satisfy $f _ { n + 2 } \left( a \right) = f _ { n + 2 } \left( b \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="461" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

(b) The minimum length interval: For intervals of the above form, the $1 - \alpha$ confi-
dence interval obtained by minimizing the interval length constrains $a$ and $b$ to
satisfy $f _ { n + 3 } \left( a \right) = f _ { n + 3 } \left( b \right) .$

(c) The shortest unbiased interval: For intervals of the above form, the $1 - \alpha$ confi-
dence interval obtained by minimizing the probability of false coverage among all
unbiased intervals constrains $a$ and $b$ to satisfy $f _ { n + 1 } \left( a \right) = f _ { n + 1 } \left( b \right) .$ This interval
can also be derived by minimizing the ratio of the endpoints.

(d) The equal-tail interval: For intervals of the above form, the $1 - \alpha$ confidence in-
terval obtained by requiring that the probability above and below the interval be
equal constrains $a$ and $b$ to satisfy

$$\int _ { 0 } ^ { a } f _ { n - 1 } \left( t \right) d t = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } , \quad \int _ { b } ^ { \infty } f _ { n - 1 } \left( t \right) d t = \frac { \alpha } { 2 } ,$$

(This interval, although very common, is clearly nonoptimal no matter what length
criterion is used.)

(e) For $\alpha = . 1$ and $n = 3 ,$ find the numerical values of $a$ and $b$ for each of the above
cases. Compare the length of this intervals.

9.53 Let $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ known. For each $c \geq 0 ,$ define an interval estimator for $\mu$ by
$C \left( x \right) = \left[ x - c \sigma , x + c \sigma \right]$ and consider the loss in $\left( 9 . 3 . 4 \right) .$

(a) Show that the risk function, $R \left( \mu , C \right) ,$ is given by

$$R \left( \mu , C \right) = b \left( 2 c \sigma \right) - P \left( - c \leq Z \leq c \right) .$$

(b) Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, show that

$$\frac { d } { d c } R \left( \mu , C \right) = 2 b \sigma - \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } e ^ { - c ^ { 2 } / 2 }$$

and, hence, the derivative is an increasing function of $c$ for $c \geq 0 .$

(c) Show that if $b \sigma > 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } ,$ the derivative is positive for all $c \geq 0$ and, hence,
$R \left( \mu , C \right)$ is minimized at $c = 0 .$ That is, the best interval estimator is the point
estimator $C \left( x \right) = \left[ x , x \right] .$

(d) Show that if $b \sigma \leq 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } ,$ the $c$ that minimizes the risk is $c = \sqrt { - 2 \log \left( b \sigma \sqrt { 2 \pi } \right) } .$
Hence, if $b$ is chosen so that $c = z _ { \alpha / 2 }$ for some $\alpha ,$ then the interval estimator that
minimizes the risk is just the usual $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval.

9.54 Let $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ but now consider $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ unknown. For each $c \geq 0 ,$ define an interval
estimator for $\mu$ by $C \left( x \right) = \left[ x \quad - \quad c s , x + c s \right] ,$ where $s ^ { 2 }$ is an estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ independent
of $X ,$ $v S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 }$ (for example, the usual sample variance). Consider a modification
of the loss in $\left( 9 . 3 . 4 \right) ,$

$$L \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C \right) = \frac { b } { \sigma } \text { Length } \left( C \right) - I _ { C } \left( \mu \right) .$$

(a) Show that the risk function, $R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C \right) ,$ is given by

$$R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C \right) = b \left( 2 c M \right) - \left[ 2 P \left( T \leq c \right) - 1 \right] ,$$

where $T \sim t _ { \nu }$ and $M = E S / \sigma .$

(b) If $b \leq 1 / \sqrt { 2 \pi } ,$ show that the $c$ that minimizes the risk satisfies

$$b = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \left( \frac { \nu } { \nu + c ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { \left( \nu + 1 \right) / 2 }$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="462" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.4" -->

(c) Reconcile this problem with the known $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ case. Show that as $\nu \rightarrow \infty ,$ the solu-
tion here converges to the solution in the known $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ problem. (Be careful of the
rescaling done to the loss function.)

9.55 The decision theoretic approach to set estimation can be quite useful (see Exercise
9.56) but it can also give some unsettling results, showing the need for thoughtful
implementation. Consider again the case of $X \sim n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ unknown, and suppose
that we have an interval estimator for $\mu$ by $C \left( x \right) = \left[ x \quad - \quad c s , x + c s \right] ,$ where $s ^ { 2 }$ is an
estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ independent of $X ,$ $v S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } .$ This is, of course, the usual $t$ interval,
one of the great statistical procedures that has stood the test of time. Consider the
loss

$$L \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C \right) = b \mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C \right) - I _ { C } \left( \mu \right) ,$$

similar to that used in Exercise 9.54, but without scaling the length. Construct another
procedure $C ^ { \prime }$ as

$$C ^ { \prime } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} { \left[ x - c s , x + c s \right] } & { \text { if } s < K } \\ { 0 } & { \text { if } s \geq K } \end{array} \right.$$

where $K$ is a positive constant. Notice that $C ^ { \prime }$ does exactly the wrong thing. When $s ^ { 2 }$
is big and there is a lot of uncertainty, we would want the interval to be wide. But $C ^ { \prime }$
is empty! Show that we can find a value of $K$ so that

$$R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C ^ { \prime } \right) \leq R \left( \left( \mu , \sigma \right) , C \right) \quad \text { for every } \left( \mu , \sigma \right)$$

with strict inequality for some $\left( \mu , \sigma \right) .$

9.56 Let $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right)$ and suppose that we want to estimate $\theta$ with an interval estimator
$C$ using the loss in (9.3.4). If $\theta$ has the prior pdf $\pi \left( \theta \right) ,$ show that the Bayes rule is
given by

$$C ^ { \pi } = \left\{ \theta : \pi \left( \theta | x \right) \geq b \right\} .$$

(Hint: Write $\mathrm { L e n g t h } \left( C \right) = \int _ { C } 1 d \theta$ and use the Neyman-Pearson Lemma.)

The following two problems relate to Miscellanea 9.5.4.

9.57 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be id $n \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ where $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is known. We know that a $1 - \alpha$ confidence
interval for $\mu$ is $\bar { x } \pm z _ { \alpha / 2 } \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } .$

(a) Show that a $1 - \alpha$ prediction interval for $X _ { n + 1 }$ is $\bar { x } \pm z _ { \alpha / 2 } \sigma \sqrt { 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } } .$

(b) Show that a $1 - \alpha$ tolerance interval for $1 0 0 p$ of the underlying population is
given by $\bar { x } \pm \sigma \left( z _ { p / 2 } + \frac { z _ { \alpha } / 2 } { \sqrt { n } } \right) .$

(c) Find a $1 - \alpha$ prediction interval for $X _ { n + 1 }$ if $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown.

(If $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is unknown, the $1 - \alpha$ tolerance interval is quite an involved calculation.)

9.58 Let $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ be iid observations from a population with median $m .$ Distribution-
free intervals can be based on the order statistics $X _ { \left( 1 \right) } \leq \cdots \leq X _ { \left( n \right) }$ in the following
way.

(a) Show that the one-sided intervals $\left( - \infty , x _ { \left( n \right) } \right]$ and $\left[ x _ { \left( 1 \right) } , \infty \right)$ are each confidence in-
tervals for $m$ with confidence coefficient $1 - \left( 1 / 2 \right) ^ { n } ,$ and the confidence coefficient
of the interval $\left[ x _ { \left( 1 \right) } \right. ,$ $\left. x _ { \left( n \right) } \right]$ is $1 - 2 \left( 1 / 2 \right) ^ { n } .$

(b) Show that the one-sided intervals of part (a) are prediction intervals with coeffi-
cient $n / \left( n + 1 \right)$ and the two-sided interval is a prediction interval with coefficient
$\left( n - 1 \right) / \left( n + 1 \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="463" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.5" -->

(c) The intervals in part (a) can also be used as tolerance intervals for proportion $p$ of
the underlying population. Show that, when considered as tolerance intervals, the
one-sided intervals have coefficient $1 - p ^ { n }$ and the two-sided interval has coefficient
$1 - p ^ { n } - n \left( 1 - p \right) p ^ { n - 1 } .$ Vardeman (1992) refers to this last calculation as a "nice
exercise in order statistics."


## 9.5 Miscellanea


### 9.5.1 Confidence Procedures

Confidence sets and tests can be related formally by defining an entity called a
confidence procedure (Joshi 1969). If $X \sim f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ where $x \in \mathcal{X}$ and $\theta \in \Theta ,$ then $a$
confidence procedure is a set in the space $\mathcal{X} \times \Theta ,$ the Cartesian product space. It
is defined as

$$\left\{ \left( x , \theta \right) : \left( x , \theta \right) \in C \right\}$$

for a set $\mathrm { C } \in \mathcal{X} \times \Theta .$

From the confidence procedure we can define two slices, or sections, obtained by
holding one of the variables constant. For fixed $x ,$ we define the $\theta$ 0-section or confi-
dence set as

$$C \left( x \right) = \left\{ \theta : \left( x , \theta \right) \in C \right\} .$$

For fixed $\theta ,$ we define the x-section or acceptance region as

$$A \left( \theta \right) = \left\{ x : \left( x , \theta \right) \in C \right\} .$$

Although this development necessitates working with the product space $\mathcal{X} \times \Theta ,$
which is one reason we do not use it here, it does provide a more straightforward
way of seeing the relationship between tests and sets. Figure 9.2.1 illustrates this
correspondence in the normal case.


### 9.5.2 Confidence Intervals in Discrete Distributions

The construction of optimal (or at least improved) confidence intervals for param-
eters from discrete distributions has a long history, as indicated in Example 9.2.11,
where we looked at the Sterne (1954) modification to the intervals of Clopper and
Pearson (1934). Of course, there are difficulties with the Sterne construction, but
the basic idea is sound, and Crow (1956) and Blyth and Still (1983) modified
Sterne's construction, with the latter producing the shortest set of exact intervals.
Casella (1986) gave an algorithm to find a class of shortest binomial confidence
intervals.

The history of Poisson interval research (which often includes other discrete dis-
tributions) is similar. The Garwood (1936) construction is exactly the Clopper-
Pearson argument applied to the binomial, and Crow and Gardner (1959) improved
the intervals. Casella and Robert (1989) found a class of shortest Poisson intervals.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="464" -->
<!-- PageHeader="INTERVAL ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.5" -->

Blyth (1986) produces very accurate approximate intervals for a binomial param-
eter, Leemis and Trivedi (1996) compare normal and Poisson approximations, and
Agresti and Coull (1998) argue that requiring discrete intervals to maintain cov-
erage above the nominal level may be too stringent. Blaker (2000) constructs im-
proved intervals for binomial, Poisson, and other discrete distributions that have a
nesting property: For $\alpha < \alpha ^ { \prime } ,$ the $1 - \alpha$ intervals contain the corresponding $1 - \alpha ^ { \prime }$
intervals.


### 9.5.3 Fieller's Theorem

Fieller's Theorem (Fieller 1954) is a clever argument to get an exact confidence set
on a ratio of normal means.

Given a random sample $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ from a bivariate normal distribution
with parameters ( $\left( \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho \right) ,$ a confidence set on $\theta = \mu _ { Y } / \mu _ { X }$ can be formed
in the following way. For $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ define $Z _ { \theta i } = Y _ { i } - \theta X _ { i }$ and $\bar { Z } _ { \theta } = \bar { Y } - \theta \bar { X } .$ It
can be shown that $\bar { Z } _ { \theta }$ is normal with mean 0 and variance

$$V _ { \theta } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } - 2 \theta \rho \sigma _ { Y } \sigma _ { X } + \theta ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

$V _ { \theta }$ can be estimated with $\widehat { V } _ { \theta } ,$ given by

$$\widehat { V } _ { \theta } = \frac { 1 } { n \left( n - 1 \right) } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( Z _ { \theta i } - \bar { Z } _ { \theta } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \left( S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } - 2 \theta S _ { Y X } + \theta ^ { 2 } S _ { X } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

where

$$S _ { Y } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( Y _ { i } - \bar { Y } \right) ^ { 2 } , \quad S _ { X } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) ^ { 2 } , \quad S _ { Y X } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( Y _ { i } - \bar { Y } \right) \left( X _ { i } - \bar { X } \right) .$$

Furthermore, it also can be shown that $E \widehat { V } _ { \theta } = V _ { \theta } ,$ $\widehat { V } _ { \theta }$ is independent of $\bar { Z } _ { \theta } ,$ and
$\left( n - 1 \right) V _ { \theta } / V _ { \theta } \sim \chi _ { n - 1 } ^ { 2 } .$ Hence, $\bar { Z } _ { \theta } / \sqrt { \widehat { V } _ { \theta } } \sim t _ { n - 1 }$ and the set

$$\left\{ \theta : \frac { \bar { z } _ { \theta } ^ { 2 } } { \widehat { v } _ { \theta } } \leq t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right\}$$

defines a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set for $\theta ,$ the ratio of the means. This set defines a
parabola in $\theta ,$ and the roots of the parabola give the endpoints of the confidence
set. Writing the set in terms of the original variables, we get

$$\left\{ \theta : \left( \bar { x } ^ { 2 } - \frac { t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } S _ { x } ^ { 2 } \right) \theta ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( \bar { x } \bar { y } - \frac { t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } S _ { y x } \right) \theta + \left( \bar { y } ^ { 2 } - \frac { t _ { n - 1 , \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } } { n - 1 } S _ { y } ^ { 2 } \right) \leq 0 \right\}$$

One interesting feature of this set is that, depending on the roots of the parabola,
it can be an interval, the complement of an interval, or the entire real line (see

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="465" -->
<!-- PageHeader="MISCELLANEA" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 9.5" -->

Exercise 9.15). Furthermore, to maintain $1 - \alpha$ confidence, this interval must be
infinite with positive probability. See Hwang (1995) for an alternative based on
bootstrapping, and Tsao and Hwang (1998, 1999) for an alternative confidence
approach.


### 9.5.4 What About Other Intervals?

Vardeman (1992) asks the question in the title of this Miscellanea, arguing that
mainstream statistics should spend more time on intervals other than two-sided
confidence intervals. In particular, he lists (a) one-sided intervals, (b) distribution-
free intervals, (c) prediction intervals, and (d) tolerance intervals.

We have seen one-sided intervals, and distribution-free intervals are intervals whose
probability guarantee holds with little (or no) assumption on the underlying cdf
(see Exercise 9.58). The other two interval definitions, together with the usual
confidence interval, provide use with a hierarchy of inferences, each more stringent
than the previous.

$X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are iid from a population with $\mathrm { c d f } F \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ and $C \left( x \right) = \left[ \left( x \right) , u \left( x \right) \right]$
☒ ☒ ☒
is an interval, for a specified value $1 - \alpha$ it is a

(i) confidence interval if $P _ { \theta } \left[ l \left( X \right) \leq \theta \leq u \left( X \right) \right] \geq 1 - \alpha$
☒ ☒

(ii) prediction interval if $P _ { \theta } \left[ l \left( X \right) \leq X _ { n + 1 } \leq u \left( X \right) \right] \geq 1 - \alpha$
☒ ☒ ☒

(iii) tolerance interval if, for a specified value $p$ p, $P _ { \theta } \left[ F \left( u \left( X \right) | \theta \right) - F \left( l \left( X \right) | \theta \right) \geq p \right] \geq$
$1 - \alpha .$
☒ ☒

So a confidence interval covers a mean, a prediction interval covers a new random
variable, and a tolerance interval covers a proportion of the population. Thus, each
gives a different inference, with the appropriate one being dictated by the problem
at hand.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageBreak -->


# Chapter 10 Asymptotic Evaluations

"I know, my dear Watson, that you share my love of all that is bizarre and
outside the conventions and humdrum routine of everyday life."

Sherlock Holmes
The Red-headed League

All of the criteria we have considered thus far have been finite-sample criteria. In
contrast, we might consider asymptotic properties, properties describing the behavior
of a procedure as the sample size becomes infinite. In this section we will look at
some of such properties and consider point estimation, hypothesis testing, and inter-
val estimation separately. We will place particular emphasis on the asymptotics of
maximum likelihood procedures.

The power of asymptotic evaluations is that, when we let the sample size become
infinite, calculations simplify. Evaluations that were impossible in the finite-sample
case become routine. This simplification also allows us to examine some other tech-
niques (such as bootstrap and M-estimation) that typically can be evaluated only
asymptotically.

Letting the sample size increase without bound (sometimes referred to as “asymp-
topia") should not be ridiculed as merely a fanciful exercise. Rather, asymptotics
uncover the most fundamental properties of a procedure and give us a very powerful
and general evaluation tool.


## 10.1 Point Estimation


### 10.1.1 Consistency

The property of consistency seems to be quite a fundamental one, requiring that the
estimator converges to the "correct" value as the sample size becomes infinite. It is
such a fundamental property that the worth of an inconsistent estimator should be
questioned (or at least vigorously investigated).

Consistency (as well as all asymptotic properties) concerns a sequence of estimators
rather than a single estimator, although it is common to speak of a "consistent esti-
mator." If we observe $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ according to a distribution $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ we can construct
a sequence of estimators $W _ { n } = W _ { n } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ merely by performing the same esti-
mation procedure for each sample size n. For example, $\bar { X } _ { 1 } = X _ { 1 } ,$ $\bar { X } _ { 2 } = \left( X _ { 1 } + X _ { 2 } \right) / 2 ,$
$\bar { X } _ { 3 } = \left( X _ { 1 } + X _ { 2 } + X _ { 3 } \right) / 3 ,$ etc. We can now define a consistent sequence.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="468" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

Definition 10.1.1 A sequence of estimators $W _ { n } = W _ { n } \left( X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } \right)$ is a consistent
sequence of estimators of the parameter $\theta$ if, for every $\epsilon > 0$ and every $\theta \in \Theta ,$

(10.1.1)
$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta } \left( | W _ { n } - \theta | < \epsilon \right) = 1 .$$

Informally, (10.1.1) says that as the sample size becomes infinite (and the sample
information becomes better and better), the estimator will be arbitrarily close to the
parameter with high probability, an eminently desirable property. Or, turning things
around, we can say that the probability that a consistent sequence of estimators
misses the true parameter is small. An equivalent statement to (10.1.1) is this: For
every $\epsilon > 0$ and every $\theta \in \Theta ,$ a consistent sequence $W _ { n }$ will satisfy

(10.1.2)
$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta } \left( | W _ { n } - \theta | \geq \epsilon \right) = 0 .$$

Definition 10.1.1 should be compared to Definition 5.5.1, the definition of convergence
in probability. Definition 10.1.1 says that a consistent sequence of estimators converges
in probability to the parameter $\theta$ it is estimating. Whereas Definition 5.5.1 dealt with
one sequence of random variables with one probability structure, Definition 10.1.1
deals with an entire family of probability structures, indexed by $\theta .$ For each different
value of $\theta ,$ the probability structure associated with the sequence $W _ { n }$ is different. And
the definition says that for each value of $\theta ,$ the probability structure is such that the
sequence converges in probability to the true $\theta .$ This is the usual difference between a
probability definition and a statistics definition. The probability definition deals with
one probability structure, but the statistics definition deals with an entire family.

Example 10.1.2 (Consistency of $\bar { X }$ Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \cdots .$ be iid $n \left( \theta , 1 \right) ,$ and consider
☒ ☒ ☒

the sequence

$$\bar { X } _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i }$$

Recall that $\bar { X } _ { n } \sim n \left( \theta , 1 / n \right) ,$ so

$$P _ { \theta } \left( | \bar { X } _ { n } - \theta | < \epsilon \right) = \int _ { \theta - \epsilon } ^ { \theta + \epsilon } \left( \frac { n } { 2 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { - \left( n / 2 \right) \left( \bar { x } _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } d \bar { x } _ { n }$$
$$= \int _ { - \epsilon } ^ { \epsilon } \left( \frac { n } { 2 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { - \left( n / 2 \right) y ^ { 2 } } d y$$
$$= \int _ { - \epsilon \sqrt { n } } ^ { \epsilon \sqrt { n } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { - \left( 1 / 2 \right) t ^ { 2 } } d t$$
$$= P \left( - \epsilon \sqrt { n } < Z < \epsilon \sqrt { n } \right)$$
$$\rightarrow 1 \quad \text { as } n \rightarrow \infty ,$$

(definition)

(substitute $y = \bar { x } _ { n } - \theta$

(substitute $t = y \sqrt { n }$
$\left( Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) \right)$

and, hence, $\bar { X } _ { n }$ is a consistent sequence of estimators of $\theta .$
☒

|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="469" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

In general, a detailed calculation, such as the above, is not necessary to verify
consistency. Recall that, for an estimator $W _ { n } ,$ Chebychev's Inequality states

$$P _ { \theta } \left( | W _ { n } - \theta | \geq \epsilon \right) \leq \frac { \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left[ \left( W _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right] } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } ,$$

so if, for every $\theta \in \Theta ,$

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } E _ { \theta } \left[ \left( W _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 0 ,$$

then the sequence of estimators is consistent. Furthermore, by (7.3.1),

(10.1.3)
$$E _ { \theta } \left[ \left( W _ { n } - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = \text { Var } _ { \theta } W _ { n } + \left[ \text { Bias } _ { \theta } W _ { n } \right] ^ { 2 } .$$

Putting this all together, we can state the following theorem.

Theorem 10.1.3 If $W _ { n }$ is a sequence of estimators of a parameter $\theta$ satisfying

i. $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } W _ { n } = 0 ,$

ii. $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \mathrm { B i a s } _ { \theta } W _ { n } = 0 ,$

for every $\theta \in \Theta ,$ then $W _ { n }$ is a consistent sequence of estimators $\theta f \theta .$

Example 10.1.4 (Continuation of Example 10.1.2) Since

$$E _ { \theta } \bar { X } _ { n } = \theta \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } \bar { X } _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { n } ,$$

the conditions of Theorem 10.1.3 are satisfied and the sequence $\bar { X } _ { n }$ is consistent.
Furthermore, from Theorem 5.2.6, if there is iid sampling from any population with
mean $\theta ,$ then $\bar { X } _ { n }$ is consistent for $\theta$ as long as the population has a finite variance. ||

At the beginning of this section we commented that the worth of an inconsistent
sequence of estimators should be questioned. Part of the basis for this comment is
the fact that there are so many consistent sequences, as the next theorem shows. Its
proof is left to Exercise 10.2.

Theorem 10.1.5 Let $W _ { n }$ be a consistent sequence of estimators of a parameter $\theta .$
Let $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ and $b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ be sequences of constants satisfying

i. $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } a _ { n } = 1 ,$

ii. $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } b _ { n } = 0 .$

Then the sequence $U _ { n } = a _ { n } W _ { n } + b _ { n }$ is a consistent sequence of estimators of $\theta .$

We close this section with the outline of a more general result concerning the consis-
tency of maximum likelihood estimators. This result shows that MLEs are consistent
estimators of their parameters and is the first case we have seen in which a method
of finding an estimator guarantees an optimality property.

To have consistency of the MLE, the underlying density (likelihood function) must
satisfy certain "regularity conditions" that we will not go into here, but see Miscel-
lanea 10.6.2 for details.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="470" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

Theorem 10.1.6 (Consistency of MLEs) Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ be iid $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ and let
$L \left( \theta | x \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right)$ be the likelihood function. $\mathrm { L e t } \widehat { \theta }$ denote the $M L E$ of $\theta .$ Let $\tau \left( \theta \right)$
be a continuous function of $\theta .$ Under the regularity conditions in Miscellanea 10.6.2
on $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ and, hence, $L \left( \theta | x \right) ,$ for every $\epsilon > 0$ and every $\theta \in \Theta ,$

$$\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } P _ { \theta } \left( | \tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) - \tau \left( \theta \right) | \geq \epsilon \right) = 0 .$$

That $i s ,$ $\tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ is a consistent estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Proof: The proof proceeds by showing that $\frac { 1 } { n } \log L \left( \widehat { \theta } | \mathrm { x } \right)$ converges almost surely to
$E _ { \theta } \left( \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right)$ for every $\theta \in \Theta .$ Under some conditions on $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ this implies that
$\widehat { \theta }$ converges to $\theta$ in probability and, hence, $\tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ converges to $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ in probability. For
details see Stuart, Ord, and Arnold (1999, Chapter 18).
☐


### 10.1.2 Efficiency

The property of consistency is concerned with the asymptotic accuracy of an estima-
tor: Does it converge to the parameter that it is estimating? In this section we look
at a related property, efficiency, which is concerned with the asymptotic variance of
an estimator.

In calculating an asymptotic variance, we are, perhaps, tempted to proceed as fol-
lows. Given an estimator $T _ { n }$ based on a sample of size $n ,$ we calculate the finite-sample
variance $\mathrm { V a r } T _ { n } ,$ and then evaluate $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } k _ { n } \text { Var } T _ { n } ,$ where $k _ { n }$ is some normalizing
constant. (Note that, in many cases, $\mathrm { V a r } T _ { n } \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty ,$ so we need a factor $k _ { n }$
to force it to a limit.)

Definition 10.1.7 For an estimator $T _ { n } ,$ if $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } k _ { n } \text { Var } T _ { n } = \tau ^ { 2 } < \infty ,$ where
$\left\{ k _ { n } \right\}$ is a sequence of constants, then $\tau ^ { 2 }$ is called the limiting variance or limit of the
variances.

Example 10.1.8 (Limiting variances) For the mean $\bar { X } _ { n }$ of $n$ iid normal obser-
vations with $\mathrm { E } X = \mu$ and $\mathrm { V a r } X = \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ if we take $T _ { n } = \bar { X } _ { n } ,$ then $\lim n V a r X _ { n } = \sigma ^ { 2 }$ is
the limiting variance of $T _ { n } .$

But a troubling thing happens if, for example, we were instead interested in es-
timating $1 / \mu$ using $1 / \bar { X } _ { n } .$ If we now take $T _ { n } = 1 / \bar { X } _ { n } ,$ we find that the variance is
Var $T _ { n } = \infty ,$ so the limit of the variances is infinity. But recall Example 5.5.23, where
we said that the "approximate" mean and variance of $1 / \bar { X } _ { n }$ are

$$E \left( \frac { 1 } { \bar { X } _ { n } } \right) \approx \frac { 1 } { \mu }$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \frac { 1 } { \bar { X } _ { n } } \right) \approx \left( \frac { 1 } { \mu } \right) ^ { 4 } \mathrm { V a r } \bar { X } _ { n } ,$$

and thus by this second calculation the variance is $\mathrm { V a r } T _ { n } \approx \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n \mu ^ { 4 } } < \infty .$

This example points out the problems of using the limit of the variances as a large
sample measure. Of course the exact finite sample variance of $1 / \bar { X }$ is $\infty .$ However, if

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="471" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

$\mu \neq 0 ,$ the region where $1 / \bar { X }$ gets very large has probability going to 0. So the second
approximation in Example 10.1.8 is more realistic (as well as being much more useful).
It is this second approach to calculating large sample variances that we adopt.

Definition 10.1.9 For an estimator $T _ { n } ,$ suppose that $k _ { n } \left( T _ { n } - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ in
distribution. The parameter $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ is called the asymptotic variance or variance of the
limit distribution of $T _ { n } .$

For calculations of the variances of sample means and other types of averages, the
limit variance and the asymptotic variance typically have the same value. But in more
complicated cases, the limiting variance will sometimes fail us. It is also interesting
to note that it is always the case that the asymptotic variance is smaller than the
limiting variance (Lehmann and Casella 1998, Section 6.1). Here is an illustration.

Example 10.1.10 (Large-sample mixture variances) The hierarchical model

$$Y _ { n } | W _ { n } = w _ { n } \sim n \left( 0 , w _ { n } + \left( 1 - w _ { n } \right) \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$W _ { n } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( p _ { n } \right) ,$$

can exhibit big discrepancies between the asymptotic and limiting variances. (This
is also sometimes described as a mixture model, where we observe $Y _ { n } \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right)$ with
probability $p _ { n }$ and $Y _ { n } \sim n \left( 0 , \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } \right)$ with probability $1 - p _ { n }$

First, using Theorem 4.4.7 we have

$$V a r \left( Y _ { n } \right) = p _ { n } + \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } .$$

It then follows that the limiting variance of $Y _ { n }$ is finite only if $\lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } < \infty .$
On the other hand, the asymptotic distribution of $Y _ { n }$ can be directly calculated
using

$$P \left( Y _ { n } < a \right) = p _ { n } P \left( Z < a \right) + \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) P \left( Z < a / \sigma _ { n } \right) .$$

Suppose now we let $p _ { n } \rightarrow 1$ and $\sigma _ { n } \rightarrow \infty$ in such a way that $\left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \infty .$ It then
follows that $P \left( Y _ { n } < a \right) \rightarrow P \left( Z < a \right) ,$ that is, $Y _ { n } \rightarrow n \left( 0 , 1 \right) ,$ and we have

$$\text { limiting variance } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } p _ { n } + \left( 1 - p _ { n } \right) \sigma _ { n } ^ { 2 } = \infty ,$$
$$a s y m p t o t i c \quad v a r i a n c e = 1 .$$

See Exercise 10.6 for more details.

Il

In the spirit of the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (Theorem 7.3.9), there is an optimal
asymptotic variance.

Definition 10.1.11 $A$ sequence of estimators $W _ { n }$ is asymptotically efficient for a
parameter $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ if $\sqrt { n } \left[ W _ { n } - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , v \left( \theta \right) \right]$ in distribution and

$$v \left( \theta \right) = \frac { \left[ \tau ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { E _ { \theta } \left( \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log f \left( X | \theta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) }$$

that is, the asymptotic variance of $W _ { n }$ achieves the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="472" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

Recall that Theorem 10.1.6 stated that, under general conditions, MLEs are con-
sistent. Under somewhat stronger regularity conditions, the same type of theorem
holds with respect to asymptotic efficiency so, in general, we can consider MLEs to
be consistent and asymptotically efficient. Again, details on the regularity conditions
are in Miscellanea 10.6.2.

Theorem 10.1.12 (Asymptotic efficiency of MLEs) Let $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ be iid
$f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ let $\widehat { \theta }$ denote the $M L E$ of $\theta ,$ and let $\tau \left( \theta \right)$ be a continuous function of $\theta .$ Under
the regularity conditions in Miscellanea 10.6.2 on $f \left( x | \theta \right)$ and, hence, $L \left( \theta | x \right) ,$

$$\sqrt { n } \left[ \tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , v \left( \theta \right) \right] ,$$

where $v \left( \theta \right)$ is the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound. That $i s ,$ $\tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ is a consistent and asymp-
totically efficient estimator of $\tau \left( \theta \right) .$

Proof: The proof of this theorem is interesting for its use of Taylor series and its
exploiting of the fact that the MLE is defined as the zero of the likelihood function.
We will outline the proof showing that $\widehat { \theta }$ is asymptotically efficient; the extension to
$\tau \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ is left to Exercise 10.7.

Recall that $l \left( \theta | x \right) = \sum \log f \left( x _ { i } | \theta \right)$ is the $\log$ likelihood function. Denote derivatives
(with respect to $\theta$ by $l ^ { \prime } , l ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots .$ Now expand the first derivative of the log likelihood
around the true value $\theta _ { 0 } ,$

(10.1.4)
$$l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta | x \right) = l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) + \left( \theta - \theta _ { 0 } \right) l ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) + \cdots ,$$

where we are going to ignore the higher-order terms (a justifiable maneuver under
the regularity conditions).

Now substitute the MLE 0 for $\theta ,$ and realize that the left-hand side of (10.1.4) is 0.
Rearranging and multiplying through by $\sqrt { n }$ gives us

(10.1.5)
$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { 0 } \right) = \sqrt { n } \frac { - l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) } { l ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) } = \frac { - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { n } } l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) } { \frac { 1 } { n } l ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | x \right) }$$

If we let $I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = E \left[ l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | X \right) \right] ^ { 2 } = 1 / v \left( \theta \right)$ denote the information number for one ob-
servation, application of the Central Limit Theorem and the Weak Law of Large
Numbers will show (see Exercise 10.8 for details)

$$- \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { n } } l ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | X \right) \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right] ,$$
(10.1.6)

(in distribution)

$$\frac { 1 } { n } l ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } | X \right) \rightarrow I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) .$$

(in probability)

Thus, if we let $W \sim n \left[ 0 , I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right] ,$ then $\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ converges in distribution to $W / I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \sim$
$n \left[ 0 , 1 / I \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right] ,$ proving the theorem.

Example 10.1.13 (Asymptotic normality and consistency) The above the-
orem shows that it is typically the case that MLEs are efficient and consistent. We

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="473" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

want to note that this phrase is somewhat redundant, as efficiency is defined only
when the estimator is asymptotically normal and, as we will illustrate, asymptotic
normality implies consistency. Suppose that

$$\sqrt { n } \frac { W _ { n } - \mu } { \sigma } \rightarrow Z ,$$

where $Z \sim n \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$ By applying Slutsky's Theorem (Theorem 5.5.17) we conclude

$$W _ { n } - \mu = \left( \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right) \left( \sqrt { n } \frac { W _ { n } - \mu } { \sigma } \right) \rightarrow \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \left( \frac { \sigma } { \sqrt { n } } \right) Z = 0 ,$$

so $W _ { n } - \mu \rightarrow 0$ in distribution. From Theorem 5.5.13 we know that convergence in
distribution to a point is equivalent to convergence in probability, so $W _ { n }$ is a consistent
estimator of $\mu .$

Il


### 10.1.3 Calculations and Comparisons

The asymptotic formulas developed in the previous sections can provide us with
approximate variances for large-sample use. Again, we have to be concerned with reg-
ularity conditions (Miscellanea 10.6.2), but these are quite general and almost always
satisfied in common circumstances. One condition deserves special mention, however,
whose violation can lead to complications, as we have already seen in Example 7.3.13.
For the following approximations to be valid, it must be the case that the support of
the pdf or pmf, hence likelihood function, must be independent of the parameter.

If an MLE is asymptotically efficient, the asymptotic variance in Theorem 10.1.6
is the Delta Method variance of Theorem 5.5.24 (without the $1 / n$ term). Thus, we
can use the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound as an approximation to the true variance of
the MLE. Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ are id $f \left( x | \theta \right) ,$ $\widehat { \theta }$ is the MLE of $\theta ,$ and $I _ { n } \left( \theta \right) =$
$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \theta } \log L \left( \theta | X \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$ is the information number of the sample. From the Delta Method
and asymptotic efficiency of MLEs, the variance of $h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ can be approximated by

$$\left( 1 0 . 1 . 7 \right) \quad \mathrm { V a r } \left( h \left( \theta \right) | \theta \right) \approx \frac { \left[ h ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { I _ { n } \left( \theta \right) }$$
$$= \frac { \left[ h ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { \mathrm { E } _ { \theta } \left( - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta | X \right) \right) }$$
$$\approx \frac { \left[ h ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) \right] ^ { 2 } | _ { \theta = \widehat { \theta } } } { - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta | \mathrm { X } \right) | _ { \theta = \widehat { \theta } } } . \quad \left( \begin{array}{} \text { the denominator is } \widehat { I } _ { n } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) , \mathrm { t h e } \\ \text { observed information number } \end{array} \right)$$
$\left( \begin{array}{} { \text { using the identity } } \\ { \text { of Lemma } 7 . 3 . 1 1 } \end{array} \right)$

Furthermore, it has been shown (Efron and Hinkley 1978) that use of the observed
information number is superior to the expected information number, the information
number as it appears in the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound.

Notice that the variance estimation process is a two-step procedure, a fact that is
somewhat masked by (10.1.7). To estimate $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) ,$ first we approximate $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$
then we estimate the resulting approximation, usually by substituting $\widehat { \theta }$ for 0. The
resulting estimate can be denoted by $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \widehat { \theta } } h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ or $\widetilde { \mathrm { V a r } } _ { \theta }$ $h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="474" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

It follows from Theorem 10.1.6 that $- \frac { 1 } { n } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta | X \right) | _ { \theta = \widehat { \theta } }$ is a consistent estimator
of $I \left( \theta \right) ,$ so it follows that $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \widehat { \theta } } h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ is a consistent estimator of $\mathrm { V a r } _ { \theta } h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) .$

Example 10.1.14 (Approximate binomial variance) In Example 7.2.7 we saw
that $\widehat { p } = \sum X _ { i } / n$ is the $M L E$ of $p ,$ where we have a random sample $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from
a $\mathrm { B e r n o u l l i } \left( p \right)$ population. We also know by direct calculation that

$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { p } \widehat { p } = \frac { p \left( 1 - p \right) } { n }$$

and a reasonable estimate of $\mathrm { V a r } _ { p } \widehat { p }$ is

(10.1.8)
$$\widehat { \text { Var } } _ { p } \widehat { p } = \frac { \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) } { n }$$

If we apply the approximation in $\left( 1 0 . 1 . 7 \right) ,$ with $h \left( p \right) = p ,$ we get as an estimate of
$\mathrm { V a r } _ { p } \widehat { p } ,$

$$\widehat { \mathrm { V a r } } _ { p } \widehat { p } \approx \frac { 1 } { - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial p ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( p | \mathrm { x } \right) | _ { p = \widehat { p } } } .$$

Recall that

$$\log L \left( p | \mathrm { x } \right) = n \widehat { p } \log \left( p \right) + n \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) \log \left( 1 - p \right) ,$$

and so

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial p ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( p | x \right) = - \frac { n \widehat { p } } { p ^ { 2 } } - \frac { n \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) } { \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 2 } }$$

Evaluating the second derivative at $p = \widehat { p }$ yields

$$\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial p ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( p | \mathrm { x } \right) | _ { p = \widehat { p } } = - \frac { n \widehat { p } } { \widehat { p } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { n \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) } { \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { n } { \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) } ,$$

which gives a variance approximation identical to (10.1.8). We now can apply Theorem
10.1.6 to assert the asymptotic efficiency of $\widehat { p }$ and, in particular, that

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { p } - p \right) \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , p \left( 1 - p \right) \right]$$

in distribution. If we also employ Theorem 5.5.17 (Slutsky's Theorem) we can conclude
that

$$\sqrt { n } \frac { \widehat { p } - p } { \sqrt { \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) } } \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , 1 \right] .$$

Estimating the variance of $\widehat { p }$ is not really that difficult, and it is not necessary to
bring in all of the machinery of these approximations. If we move to a slightly more
complicated function, however, things can get a bit tricky. Recall that in Exercise
5.5.22 we used the Delta Method to approximate the variance of $\widehat { p } / \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) ,$ an estimate

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="475" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

of the odds $p / \left( 1 \quad - p \right) .$ Now we see that this estimator is, in fact, the MLE of the odds,
and we can estimate its variance by

$$\frac { \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial p } \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { \text { Var } \left( \frac { \widehat { p } } { 1 - \widehat { p } } \right) = \frac { \left[ \frac { \partial } { \partial p } \left( \frac { p } { 1 - p } \right) \right] } { \partial p ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( p | x \right) | _ { p = \widehat { p } } }$$
$$= \frac { \left[ \frac { \left( 1 - p \right) + p } { \left( 1 - p \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { p = \widehat { p } } ^ { 2 } } { \frac { n } { p \left( 1 - p \right) } | _ { p = \widehat { p } } }$$
$$= \frac { \widehat { p } } { n \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) ^ { 3 } } .$$

Moreover, we also know that the estimator is asymptotically efficient.
Il

The MLE variance approximation works well in many cases, but it is not infallible.
In particular, we must be careful when the function $h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ is not monotone. In such
cases, the derivative $h ^ { \prime }$ will have a sign change, and that may lead to an underesti-
mated variance approximation. Realize that, since the approximation is based on the
Cramér-Rao Lower Bound, it is probably an underestimate. However, nonmonotone
functions can make this problem worse.

Example 10.1.15 (Continuation of Example 10.1.14) Suppose now that we
want to estimate the variance of the Bernoulli distribution, $p \left( 1 \quad - p \right) .$ The MLE of this
variance is given by $\widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) ,$ and an estimate of the variance of this estimator can
be obtained by applying the approximation of (10.1.7). We have

$$\frac { r } { \text { Var } } \left( \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) \right) = \frac { \left[ \frac { \partial } { \partial p } \left( p \left( 1 - p \right) \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } { - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial p ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( p | x \right) | }$$
$$= \frac { \left( 1 - 2 p \right) ^ { 2 } | p = \widehat { p } } { \frac { n } { p \left( 1 - p \right) } | _ { p = \widehat { p } } }$$
$$= \frac { \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) \left( 1 - 2 \widehat { p } \right) ^ { 2 } } { n } ,$$
p=p

which can be 0 if $\widehat { p } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ,$ a clear underestimate of the variance of $\widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) .$ The fact
that the function $p \left( 1 \quad - \quad p \right)$ is not monotone is a cause of this problem.

Using Theorem 10.1.6, we can conclude that our estimator is asymptotically efficient
as long as $p \neq 1 / 2 .$ If $p = 1 / 2$ we need to use a second-order approximation as given
in Theorem 5.5.26 (see Exercise 10.10).
Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="476" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

The property of asymptotic efficiency gives us a benchmark for what we can hope
to attain in asymptotic variance (although see Miscellanea 10.6.1). We also can use
the asymptotic variance as a means of comparing estimators, through the idea of
asymptotic relative efficiency.

Definition 10.1.16 If two estimators $W _ { n }$ and $V _ { n }$ satisfy

$$\sqrt { n } \left[ W _ { n } - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , \sigma _ { W } ^ { 2 } \right]$$
$$\sqrt { n } \left[ V _ { n } - \tau \left( \theta \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , \sigma _ { V } ^ { 2 } \right]$$

in distribution, the asymptotic relative efficiency (ARE) of $V _ { n }$ with respect to $W _ { n }$ is

$$\mathrm { A R E } \left( V _ { n } , W _ { n } \right) = \frac { \sigma _ { W } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { V } ^ { 2 } } .$$

Example 10.1.17 (AREs of Poisson estimators) Suppose that $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ are
iid Poisson(1), and we are interested in estimating the 0 probability. For example,
the number of customers that come into a bank in a given time period is sometimes
modeled as a Poisson random variable, and the $0$ probability is the probability that no
one will enter the bank in one time period. If $X \sim \mathrm { P o i s s o n } \left( \lambda \right) ,$ then $P \left( X = 0 \right) = e ^ { - \lambda } ,$
and a natural (but somewhat naive) estimator comes from defining $Y _ { i } = I \left( X _ { i } = 0 \right)$
and using

$$\widehat { \tau } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } Y _ { i } .$$

The $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are $\mathrm { B e r n o u l l i } \left( e ^ { - \lambda } \right) ,$ and hence it follows that

$$E \left( \widehat { \tau } \right) = e ^ { - \lambda } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \mathrm { V a r } \left( \widehat { \tau } \right) = \frac { e ^ { - \lambda } \left( 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } \right) } { n } .$$

Alternatively, the MLE of $e ^ { - \lambda }$ is $e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } } ,$ where $\widehat { \lambda } = \sum _ { i } X _ { i } / n$ is the MLE of $\lambda .$ Using
Delta Method approximations, we have that

$$E \left( e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } } \right) \approx e ^ { - \lambda } \quad \text { and } \quad \text { Var } \left( e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } } \right) \approx \frac { \lambda e ^ { - 2 \lambda } } { n } .$$

Since

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \tau } - e ^ { - \lambda } \right) \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , e ^ { - \lambda } \left( 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } \right) \right]$$
$$\sqrt { n } \left( e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } } - e ^ { - \lambda } \right) \rightarrow n \left[ 0 , \lambda e ^ { - 2 \lambda } \right]$$

in distribution, the ARE of $\widehat { \tau }$ with respect to the MLE $e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } }$ is

$$A R E \left( \widehat { \tau } , e ^ { - \widehat { \lambda } } \right) = \frac { \lambda e ^ { - 2 \lambda } } { e ^ { - \lambda } \left( 1 - e ^ { - \lambda } \right) } = \frac { \lambda } { e ^ { \lambda } - 1 }$$

Examination of this function shows that it is strictly decreasing with a maximum of
1 (the best that $\widehat { \tau }$ could hope to do) attained at $\lambda = 0$ and tailing off rapidly (being
less than 10% when $\lambda = 4$ to asymptote to 0 as $\lambda \rightarrow \infty .$ (See Exercise 10.9.)
Il

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="477" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

Since the MLE is typically asymptotically efficient, another estimator cannot hope
to beat its asymptotic variance. However, other estimators may have other desir-
able properties (ease of calculation, robustness to underlying assumptions) that make
them desirable. In such situations, the efficiency of the MLE becomes important in
calibrating what we are giving up if we use an alternative estimator.

We will look at one last example, contrasting ease of calculation with optimal
variance. In the next section the robustness issue will be addressed.

Example 10.1.18 (Estimating a gamma mean) Difficult as $\mathrm { i t }$ may seem to
believe, estimation of the mean of a gamma distribution is not an easy task. Recall
that the gamma pdf $f \left( x | \alpha , \beta \right)$ is given by

$$f \left( x | \alpha , \beta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \alpha \right) \beta ^ { \alpha } } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } .$$

The mean of this distribution is $\alpha \beta ,$ and to compute the maximum likelihood estima-
tor we have to deal with the derivative of the gamma function (called the digamma
function), which is never pleasant. In contrast, the method of moments gives us an
easily computable estimate.

To be specific, suppose we have a random sample $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ from the gamma
density above, but reparameterized so the mean, denoted by $\mu = \alpha \beta ,$ is explicit. This
gives

$$f \left( x | \mu , \beta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left( \mu / \beta \right) \beta ^ { \mu / \beta } } x ^ { \mu / \beta - 1 } e ^ { - x / \beta } ,$$

and the method of moments estimator of $\widehat { \mu }$ is $\bar { X } ,$ with variance $\beta \mu / n .$

To calculate the MLE, we use the log likelihood

$$l \left( \mu , \beta | x \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \log f \left( x _ { i } | \mu , \beta \right) .$$

To ease the computations, assume that $\beta$ is known so we solve $\frac { d } { d \mu } l \left( \mu , \beta | x \right) = 0$ to get
the MLE p. There is no explicit solution, so we proceed numerically.

By Theorem 10.1.6 we know that $\widehat { \mu }$ is asymptotically efficient. The question of
interest is how much do we lose by using the easier-to-calculate method of moments
estimator. To compare, we calculate the asymptotic relative efficiency,

$$\mathrm { A R E } \left( \widehat { \mu } , \bar { X } \right) = \left[ \beta \mu \right] \left[ \mathrm { E } \left( - \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d \mu ^ { 2 } } l \left( \mu , \beta | X \right) \right) \right]$$

and display it in Figure 10.1.1 for a selection of values of $\beta .$ Of course, we know that
the ARE must be greater than 1, but we see from the figure that for larger values of
$\beta$ it pays to do the more complex calculation and use the MLE. (See Exercise 10.11
for an extension, and Example A.0.7 for details on the calculations.)

II

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="478" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ASYMPTOTIC EVALUATIONS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

<figcaption>Figure 10.1.1. Asymptotic relative efficiency of the method of moments estimator versus the MLE of a gamma mean. The four curves correspond $t o$ scale parameter values of $\left( 1 , 3 , 5 , 1 0 \right) ,$ with the higher curves corresponding to the higher values of the scale parameter.</figcaption>

### 10.1.4 Bootstrap Standard Errors

The bootstrap, which we first saw in Example 1.2.20, provides an alternative means of
calculating standard errors. (It can also provide much more; see Miscellanea 10.6.3.)

The bootstrap is based on a simple, yet powerful, idea (whose mathematics can get
quite involved).1 In statistics, we learn about the characteristics of the population by
taking samples. As the sample represents the population, analogous characteristics
of the sample should give us information about the population characteristics. The
bootstrap helps us learn about the sample characteristics by taking resamples (that
is, we retake samples from the original sample) and use this information to infer to
the population. The bootstrap was developed by Efron in the late 1970s, with the
original ideas appearing in Efron (1979a, b) and the monograph by Efron (1982). See
also Efron (1998) for more recent thoughts and developments.

Let us first look at a simple example where the bootstrap really is not needed.

Example 10.1.19 (Bootstrapping a variance) In Example 1.2.20 we calculated
all possible averages of four numbers selected from

$$2 , 4 , 9 , 1 2 ,$$

where we drew the numbers with replacement. This is the simplest form of the boot-
strap, sometimes referred to as the nonparametric bootstrap. Figure 1.2.2 displays
these values in a histogram.

What we have created is a resample of possible values of the sample mean. We
saw that there are $\left( \begin{array}{} { 4 + 4 - 1 } \\ { 4 } \end{array} \right) = 3 5$ distinct possible values, but these values are not
equiprobable (and thus cannot be treated like a random sample). The $4 ^ { 4 } = 2 5 6$
(nondistinct) resamples are all equally likely, and they can be treated as a random
sample. For the ith resample, we let $\bar { x } _ { i } ^ { * }$ be the mean of that resample. We can then

1 See Lehmann (1999, Section 6.5) for a most readable introduction.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="479" -->
<!-- PageHeader="POINT ESTIMATION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 10.1" -->

estimate the variance of the sample mean $\bar { X }$ by
☒

(10.1.9)
$$\mathrm { V a r } ^ { * } \left( \bar { X } \right) = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { n } - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n ^ { n } } \left( \bar { x } _ { i } ^ { * } - \bar { \bar { x } } ^ { * } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$ ☒

where $\bar { x ^ { * } } = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n ^ { n } } \bar { x } _ { i } ^ { * } ,$ the mean of the resamples. (It is standard to let the notation
$*$ denote a bootstrapped, or resampled, value.)

For our example we have that the bootstrap mean and variance are $\overrightarrow { x ^ { * } } = 6 . 7 5$ and
$\mathrm { V a r } ^ { * } \left( \bar { X } \right) = 3 . 9 4 .$ It turns out that, as far as means and variances are concerned, the
☒

bootstrap estimates are almost the same as the usual ones (see Exercise 10.13).
II

We have now seen how to calculate a bootstrap standard error, but in a problem
where it is really not needed. However, the real advantage of the bootstrap is that,
like the Delta Method, the variance formula (10.1.9) is applicable to virtually any
estimator. Thus, for any estimator $\widehat { \theta } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \widehat { \theta } ,$ we can write
☒

$$\mathrm { V a r } ^ { * } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { n } - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n ^ { n } } \left( \widehat { \theta } _ { i } ^ { * } - \bar { \theta } ^ { * } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$
(10.1.10)

where $\widehat { \theta } _ { i } ^ { * }$ is the estimator calculated from the ith resample and $\widetilde { \theta } ^ { * } = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n ^ { n } } \widehat { \theta } _ { i } ^ { * } ,$ the
mean of the resampled values.

Example 10.1.20 (Bootstrapping a binomial variance) In Example 10.1.15,
we used the Delta Method to estimate the variance of $\widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) .$ Based on a sample of
size $n$ n, we could alternatively estimate this variance by

$$\mathrm { V a r } ^ { * } \left( \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) \right) = \frac { 1 } { n ^ { n } - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n ^ { n } } \left( \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) _ { i } ^ { * } - \widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right) ^ { * } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

But now a problem pops up. For our Example 10.1.19, with $n = 4 ,$ there were
256 terms in the bootstrap sum. In more typical sample sizes, this number grows so
large as to be uncomputable. (Enumerating all the possible resamples when $n > 1 5$
is virtually impossible, certainly for the authors.) But now we remember that we are
statisticians - we take a sample of the resamples.

Thus, for a sample $x = \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right)$ and an estimate $\widehat { \theta } \left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) = \widehat { \theta } ,$ select
$B$ resamples (or bootstrap samples) and calculate

(10.1.11)
$$\mathrm { V a r } _ { B } ^ { * } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) = \frac { 1 } { B - 1 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { B } \left( \widehat { \theta } _ { i } ^ { * } - \bar { \widehat { \theta } } ^ { * } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

Example 10.1.21 (Conclusion of Example 10.1.20) For a sample of size $n = 2 4 ,$
we compute the Delta Method variance estimate and the bootstrap variance estimate
of $\widehat { p } \left( 1 - \widehat { p } \right)$ using $B = 1 0 0 0 .$ For $\widehat { p } \neq 1 / 2 ,$ we use the first-order Delta Method variance
of Example 10.1.15, while for $\widehat { p } = 1 / 2 ,$ we use the second-order variance estimate
of Theorem 5.5.26 (see Exercise 10.16). We see in Table 10.1.1 that in all cases the

Il
