<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="543" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

For any line $y = c + d x ,$ the residual sum of squares (RSS) is defined to be

$$\mathrm { R S S } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( c + d x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

The RSS measures the vertical distance from each data point to the line $c + d x$ and
then sums the squares of these distances. (Two such distances are shown in Figure
11.3.1.) The least squares estimates of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are defined to be those values $a$ and $b$
such that the line $a + b x$ minimizes RSS. That is, the least squares estimates, $a$ and
$b ,$ satisfy

$$\min _ { c , d } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( c + d x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( a + b x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

This function of two variables, $c$ and $d ,$ can be minimized in the following way. For
any fixed value of $d ,$ the value of $c$ that gives the minimum value can be found by
writing

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( c + d x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( y _ { i } - d x _ { i } \right) - c \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

From Theorem 5.2.4, the minimizing value of $c$ is

(11.3.9)
$$c = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - d x _ { i } \right) = \bar { y } - d \bar { x } .$$

Thus, for a given value of $d ,$ the minimum value of RSS is

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( y _ { i } - d x _ { i } \right) - \left( \bar { y } - d \bar { x } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) - d \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = S _ { y y } - 2 d S _ { x y } + d ^ { 2 } S _ { x x } .$$ ☒

The value of $d$ that gives the overall minimum value of RSS is obtained by setting
the derivative of this quadratic function of $d$ equal to 0. The minimizing value is

(11.3.10)
$$d = \frac { S _ { x y } } { S _ { x x } }$$

This value is, indeed, a minimum since the coefficient of $d ^ { 2 }$ is positive. Thus, by
(11.3.9) and (11.3.10), $a$ and $b$ from (11.3.8) are the values of $c$ and $d$ that minimize
the residual sum of squares.

The RSS is only one of many reasonable ways of measuring the distance from the
line $c + d x$ to the data points. For example, rather than using vertical distances we
could use horizontal distances. This is equivalent to graphing the $y$ variable on the
horizontal axis and the $x$ variable on the vertical axis and using vertical distances as
we did above. Using the above results (interchanging the roles of $x$ and $y$ y), we find
the least squares line is $\widehat { x } = a ^ { \prime } + b ^ { \prime } y ,$ where

$$b ^ { \prime } = \frac { S _ { x y } } { S _ { y y } } \text { and } a ^ { \prime } = \bar { x } - b ^ { \prime } \bar { y } .$$

$y$
$x$
$\widehat { y } = - \left( a ^ { \prime } / b ^ { \prime } \right) + \left( 1 / b ^ { \prime } \right) x$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="544" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

Usually the line obtained by considering horizontal distances is different from the
line obtained by considering vertical distances. From the values in Table 11.3.1, the
regression of $y$ on $x$ (vertical distances) is $\widehat { y } = 1 . 8 6 + . 6 8 x .$ The regression of $x$ on
$y$ (horizontal distances) is $\widehat { y } = - 2 . 3 1 + 2 . 8 2 x .$ In Figure 12.2.2, these two lines are
shown (along with a third line discussed in Section 12.2). If these two lines were the
same, then the slopes would be the same and $b / \left( 1 / b ^ { \prime } \right)$ would equal 1. But, in fact,
$b / \left( 1 / b ^ { \prime } \right) \leq 1$ with equality only in special cases. Note that

$$\frac { b } { 1 / b ^ { \prime } } = b b ^ { \prime } = \frac { \left( S _ { x y } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } S _ { y y } }$$

Using the version of Holder's Inequality in (4.7.9) with $p = q = 2 ,$ $a _ { i } = x _ { i } - \bar { x } ,$ and
$b _ { i } = y _ { i } - \bar { y } ,$ we see that $\left( S _ { x y } \right) ^ { 2 } \leq S _ { x x } S _ { y y }$ and, hence, the ratio is less than 1.

If $x$ is the predictor variable, $y$ is the response variable, and we think of predicting
$y$ from $x ,$ then the vertical distance measured in RSS is reasonable. It measures the
distance from $y _ { i }$ to the predicted value of $y _ { i } ,$ $\widehat { y } _ { i } = c + d x _ { i }$ ;. But if we do not make this
distinction between $x$ and $y ,$ then it is unsettling that another reasonable criterion,
horizontal distance, gives a different line.

The least squares method should be considered only as a method of "fitting a
line" to a set of data, not as a method of statistical inference. We have no basis
for constructing confidence intervals or testing hypotheses because, in this section,
we have not used any statistical model for the data. When we think of $a$ and $b$
in the context of this section, it might be better to call them least squares solutions
rather than least squares estimates because they are the solutions of the mathematical
problem of minimizing the RSS rather than estimates derived from a statistical model.
But, as we shall see, these least squares solutions have optimality properties in certain
statistical models.


### 11.3.2 Best Linear Unbiased Estimators: A Statistical Solution

In this section we show that the estimates $a$ and $b$ from (11.3.8) are optimal in the
class of linear unbiased estimates under a fairly general statistical model. The model
is described as follows. Assume that the values $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are known, fixed values.
(Think of them as values the experimenter has chosen and set in a laboratory exper-
iment.) The values $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ are observed values of uncorrelated random variables
$Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$ The linear relationship assumed between the $x s$ and the $y s$ is

(11.3.11)
$$E Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where we also assume that

(11.3.12)
$$\mathrm { V a r } Y _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$

There is no subscript in $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ because we are assuming that all the $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ have the same
(unknown) variance. These assumptions about the first two moments of the $Y _ { i }$ are the
only assumptions we need to make to proceed with the derivation in this subsection.
For example, we do not need to specify a probability distribution for the $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="545" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

The model in (11.3.11) and (11.3.12) can also be expressed in this way. We assume
that

(11.3.13)
$$Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } + \epsilon _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n }$ are uncorrelated random variables with

(11.3.14)
$$E \epsilon _ { i } = 0 \quad \text { and } \quad \text { Var } \epsilon _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$

The $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n }$ are called the random errors. Since $Y _ { i }$ depends only on $\epsilon _ { i }$ and the $\epsilon _ { i } \mathrm { s }$
are uncorrelated, the $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are uncorrelated. Also, from (11.3.13) and (11.3.14), the
expressions for $\mathrm { E } Y _ { i }$ and $\mathrm { V a r } Y _ { i }$ in (11.3.11) and (11.3.12) are easily verified.

To derive estimators for the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$ we restrict attention to the class
of linear estimators. An estimator is a linear estimator if it is of the form

(11.3.15)
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } ,$$ ☒

where $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ are known, fixed constants. (Exercise 7.39 concerns linear estima-
tors of a population mean.) Among the class of linear estimators, we further restrict
attention to unbiased estimators. This restricts the values of $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that can be
used.

An unbiased estimator of the slope $\beta$ must satisfy

$$E \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } = \beta ,$$ ☒

regardless of the true value of the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ This implies that

$$\beta = E \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } E Y _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right)$$
$$= \alpha \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } \right) + \beta \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } \right)$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒

This equality is true for all $\alpha$ and $\beta$ if and only if

(11.3.16)
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } = 0$$
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } = 1$$
and
☒ ☒

Thus, $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ must satisfy (11.3.16) in order for the estimator to be an unbiased
estimator of $\beta .$

In Chapter 7 we called an unbiased estimator "best" if it had the smallest variance
among all unbiased estimators. Similarly, an estimator is the best linear unbiased
estimator $\left( B L U E \right)$ if it is the linear unbiased estimator with the smallest variance.
We will now show that the choice of $d _ { i } = \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) / S _ { x x }$ that defines the estimator
$b = S _ { x Y } / S _ { x x }$ is the best choice in that it results in the linear unbiased estimator of $\beta$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="546" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

with the smallest variance. (The $d _ { i } s$ must be known, fixed constants but the $x _ { i } s$ are
known, fixed constants, so this choice of $d _ { i } s$ is legitimate.)

A note on notation: The notation $S _ { x Y }$ stresses the fact that $S _ { x Y }$ is a random vari-
able that is a function of the random variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$ $S _ { x Y }$ also depends on the
nonrandom quantities $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$

Because $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are uncorrelated with equal variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ the variance of any
linear estimator is given by

$$\mathrm { V a r } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } \text { Var } Y _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$$

The BLUE of $\beta$ is, therefore, defined by constants $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that satisfy $\left( 1 1 . 3 . 1 6 \right)$
and have the minimum value of $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$ (The presence of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ has no effect on the
minimization over linear estimators since it appears as a multiple of the variance of
every linear estimator.)

The minimizing values of the constants $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ can now be found by using
Lemma 11.2.7. To apply the lemma to our minimization problem, make the following
correspondences, where the left-hand sides are notation from Lemma 11.2.7 and the
right-hand sides are our current notation. Let

$$k = n , \quad v _ { i } = x _ { i } , \quad c _ { i } = 1 , \quad \text { and } \quad a _ { i } = d _ { i } ,$$

which implies $\bar { v } _ { c } = \bar { x } .$ If $d _ { i }$ is of the form

(11.3.17)
$$d _ { i } = K c _ { i } \left( v _ { i } - \bar { v } _ { c } \right) = K \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

then, by Lemma 11.2.7, $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ maximize

(11.3.18)
$$\frac { \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } }$$

among all $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that satisfy $\sum d _ { i } = 0 .$ Furthermore, since

$$\left\{ \left( d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n } \right) : \sum d _ { i } = 0 , \sum d _ { i } x _ { i } = 1 \right\} \subset \left\{ \left( d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n } \right) : \sum d _ { i } = 0 \right\} ,$$

if $d _ { i } s$ of the form (11.3.17) also satisfy (11.3.16), they certainly maximize (11.3.18)
among all $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that satisfy (11.3.16). (Since the set over which the maximum is
taken is smaller, the maximum cannot be larger.) Now, using (11.3.17), we have

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } K \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) x _ { i } = K S _ { x x } .$$

The second constraint in (11.3.16) is satisfied if $K = \frac { 1 } { S _ { x x } } .$ Therefore, with $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$
defined by

(11.3.19)
$$d _ { i } = \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) } { S _ { x x } } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="547" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 11.3.2. Geometric description of the $B L U E$</figcaption>

$\sum d _ { i } x _ { i } = 1$

\-

$\Sigma d _ { i } = 0$

0

$d$

</figure>


both constraints of (11.3.16) are satisfied and this set of $d _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ produces the maximum.
Finally, note that for all $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that satisfy (11.3.16),

$$\frac { \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } }$$

Thus, for $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that satisfy (11.3.16), maximization of (11.3.18) is equivalent
to minimization of $\sum d _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$ Hence, we can conclude that the $d _ { i } s$ defined in (11.3.19)
give the minimum value of $\sum d _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ among all $d _ { i } s$ that satisfy (11.3.16), and the linear
unbiased estimator defined by these $d _ { i } s ,$ namely,

$$b = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) } { S _ { x x } } y _ { i } = \frac { S _ { x y } } { S _ { x x } }$$

is the BLUE of $\beta .$

$A$ geometric description of this construction of the BLUE of $\beta$ is given in Figure
11.3.2, where we take $n = 3 .$ The figure shows three-dimensional space with coor-
dinates $d _ { 1 } ,$ $d _ { 2 } ,$ and $d _ { 3 } .$ The two planes represent the vectors $\left( d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , d _ { 3 } \right)$ that satisfy
the two linear constraints in (11.3.16), and the line where the two planes intersect
consists of the vectors $\left( d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , d _ { 3 } \right)$ that satisfy both equalities. For any point on the
line, $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ is the square of the distance from the point to the origin 0. The vector
$\left( d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , d _ { 3 } \right)$ that defines the BLUE is the point on the line that is closest to 0. The
sphere in the figure is the smallest sphere that intersects the line, and the point of
intersection is the point $\left( d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , d _ { 3 } \right)$ that defines the BLUE of $\beta .$ This, we have shown,
is the point with $d _ { i } = \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) / S _ { x x } .$

The variance of $b$ is

(11.3.20)
$$\mathrm { V a r } b = \sigma ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } .$$

Since $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are values chosen by the experimenter, they can be chosen to make
$S _ { x x }$ large and the variance of the estimator small. That is, the experimenter can design

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="548" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

the experiment to make the estimator more precise. Suppose that all the $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$
must be chosen in an interval $\left[ e , f \right] .$ Then, if $n$ is even, the choice of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ that
makes $S _ { x x }$ as large as possible is to take half of the $x _ { i } s$ equal to $e$ and half equal
to $f$ (see Exercise 11.26). This would be the best design in that it would give the
most precise estimate of the slope $\beta$ if the experimenter were certain that the model
described by (11.3.11) and (11.3.12) was correct. In practice, however, this design is
seldom used because an experimenter is hardly ever certain of the model. This two-
point design gives information about the value of $\mathrm { E } \left( Y | x \right)$ at only two values, $x = e$
and $x = f .$ If the population regression function $\mathrm { E } \left( Y | x \right) ,$ which gives the mean of $Y$
as a function of $x ,$ is nonlinear, it could never be detected from data obtained using
the "optimal" two-point design.

We have shown that $b$ is the BLUE of $\beta .$ A similar analysis will show that $a$ is the
BLUE of the intercept a. The constants $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ that define a linear estimator of
$\alpha$ must satisfy

(11.3.21)
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } = 1$$
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } x _ { i } = 0 .$$ ☒
and
☒

The details of this derivation are left as Exercise 11.27. The fact that least squares es-
timators are BLUEs holds in other linear models also. This general result is called the
Gauss-Markov Theorem (see Christensen 1996; Lehmann and Casella 1998, Section
3.4, or the more general treatment in Harville 1981).


### 11.3.3 Models and Distribution Assumptions

In this section, we will introduce two more models for paired data $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right)$
that are called simple linear regression models.

To obtain the least squares estimates in Section 11.3.1, we used no statistical model.
We simply solved a mathematical minimization problem. Thus, we could not derive
any statistical properties about the estimators obtained by this method because there
were no probability models to work with. There are not really any parameters for
which we could construct hypothesis tests or confidence intervals.

In Section 11.3.2 we made some statistical assumptions about the data. Specifically,
we made assumptions about the first two moments, the mean, variance, and covariance
of the data. These are all statistical assumptions related to probability models for
the data, and we derived statistical properties for the estimators. The properties of
unbiasedness and minimum variance, which we proved for the estimators $a$ and $b$ of
the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$ are statistical properties.

To obtain these properties we did not have to specify a complete probability model
for the data, only assumptions about the first two moments. We were able to obtain a
general optimality property under these minimal assumptions, but the optimality was
only in a restricted class of estimators-linear unbiased estimators. We were not able
to derive exact tests and confidence intervals under this model because the model does
not specify enough about the probability distribution of the data. We now present
two statistical models that completely specify the probabilistic structure of the data.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="549" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->


### Conditional normal model

The conditional normal model is the most common simple linear regression model and
the most straightforward to analyze. The observed data are the $n$ pairs, $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \cdots ,$
$\left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right) .$ The values of the predictor variable, $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ are considered to be known,
fixed constants. As in Section 11.3.2, think of them as being chosen and set by the
experimenter. The values of the response variable, $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } ,$ are observed values of
random variables, $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$ The random variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are assumed to be
independent. Furthermore, the distribution of the $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ is normal, specifically,

(11.3.22)
$$Y _ { i } \sim n \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n .$$

Thus the population regression function is a linear function of x, that is, $E \left( Y | x \right) =$
$\alpha + \beta x ,$ and all the $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ have the same variance, $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ The conditional normal model
can be expressed similar to (11.3.13) and (11.3.14), namely,

(11.3.23)
$$Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } + \epsilon _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$$

where $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n }$ are iid $n \left( 0 , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$ random variables.

The conditional normal model is a special case of the model considered in Sec-
tion 11.3.2. The population regression function, $E \left( Y | x \right) = \alpha + \beta x ,$ and the variance,
Var $Y = \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ are as in that model. The uncorrelatedness of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ (or, equiva-
lently, $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n }$ has been strengthened to independence. $A n d ,$ of course, rather than
just the first two moments of the distribution of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } ,$ the exact form of the
probability distribution is now specified.

The joint pdf of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ is the product of the marginal pdfs because of the
independence. It is given by

$$f \left( y | \alpha , \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) = f \left( y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n } | \alpha , \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } f \left( y _ { i } | \alpha , \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } \sigma } \exp \left[ - \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n / 2 } \sigma ^ { n } } \exp \left[ - \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) \right] .$$

(11.3.24)

It is this joint probability distribution that will be used to develop the statistical
procedures in Sections 11.3.4 and 11.3.5. For example, the expression in (11.3.24) will
be used to find MLEs of $\alpha ,$ $\beta ,$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$


#### Bivariate normal model

In all the previous models we have discussed, the values of the predictor variable,
$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ have been fixed, known constants. But sometimes these values are actually
observed values of random variables, $X _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } .$ In Galton's example in Section 11.3,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="550" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->

$x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ were observed heights of fathers. But the experimenter certainly did not
choose these heights before collecting the data. Thus it is necessary to consider models
in which the predictor variable, as well as the response variable, is random. One such
model that is fairly simple is the bivariate normal model. $A$ more complex model is
discussed in Section 12.2.

In the bivariate normal model the data $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right)$ are observed values
of the bivariate random vectors $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right) .$ The random vectors are in-
☒ ☒
dependent and the joint distribution of $\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right)$ is assumed to be bivariate normal.
Specifically, it is assumed that

$$\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right) \sim \text { bivariate normal } \left( \mu _ { X } , \mu _ { Y } , \sigma _ { X } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } , \rho \right) .$$

The joint pdf and various properties of a bivariate normal distribution are given
in Definition 4.5.10 and the subsequent discussion. The joint pdf of all the data
$\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ is the product of these bivariate pdfs.
☒ ☒

In a simple linear regression analysis, we are $\mathrm { s t i l }$ thinking of $x$ as the predictor
variable and $y$ as the response variable. That is, we are most interested in predicting
the value of $y$ having observed the value of x. This naturally leads to basing inference
on the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x .$ For a bivariate normal model,
☒
the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x$ is normal. The population regression
function is now a true conditional expectation, as the notation suggests, and is

$$\mathrm { E } \left( Y | x \right) = \mu _ { Y } + \rho \frac { \sigma _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { X } } \left( x - \mu _ { X } \right) = \left[ \mu _ { Y } - \rho \frac { \sigma _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { X } } \mu _ { X } \right] + \left[ \rho \frac { \sigma _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { X } } \right] x .$$
(11.3.25)

The bivariate normal model implies that the population regression is a linear function
of $x .$ We need not assume this as in the previous models. Here $E \left( Y | x \right) = \alpha + \beta x ,$
where $\beta = \rho \frac { \sigma _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { X } }$ and $\alpha = \mu _ { Y } - \rho \frac { \sigma _ { Y } } { \sigma _ { X } } \mu _ { X } .$ Also, as in the conditional normal model, the
conditional variance of the response variable $Y$ does not depend on x,

(11.3.26)
$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( Y | x \right) = \sigma _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - \rho ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

For the bivariate normal model, the linear regression analysis is almost always
carried out using the conditional distribution of $\left( Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } \right)$ given $X _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n } =$
☒
$x _ { n } ,$ rather than the unconditional distribution of $\left( X _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( X _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right) .$ But then we
☒ ☒
are in the same situation as the conditional normal model described above. The fact
that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are observed values of random variables is immaterial if we condition
on these values and, in general, in simple linear regression we do not use the fact
of bivariate normality except to define the conditional distribution. (Indeed, for the
most part, the marginal distribution of $X$ is of no consequence whatsoever. In linear
regression it is the conditional distribution that matters.) Inference based on point
estimators, intervals, or tests is the same for the two models. See Brown (1990b) for
an alternative view.


##### 11.3.4 Estimation and Testing with Normal Errors

In this and the next subsections we develop inference procedures under the conditional
normal model, the regression model defined by (11.3.22) or (11.3.23).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="551" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

First, we find the maximum likelihood estimates of the three parameters, $\alpha ,$ $\beta ,$
and $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Using the joint pdf in (11.3.24), we see that the log likelihood function is

$$\log L \left( \alpha , \beta , \sigma ^ { 2 } | \mathrm { x } , \mathrm { y } \right) = - \frac { n } { 2 } \log \left( 2 \pi \right) - \frac { n } { 2 } \log \sigma ^ { 2 } - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } .$$

For any fixed value of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\log L$ is maximized as a function of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ by those values,
$\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta } ,$ that minimize

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

But this function is just the RSS from Section 11.3.1! There we found that the mini-
mizing values are

$$\widehat { \beta } = b = \frac { S _ { x y } } { S _ { x x } } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \widehat { \alpha } = a = \bar { y } - b \bar { x } = \bar { y } - \widehat { \beta } \bar { x } .$$

Thus, the least squares estimators of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are also the MLEs of $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ The
values $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are the maximizing values for any fixed value of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Now, substituting
in the $\log$ likelihood, to find the MLE of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ we need $t o$ maximize

$$- \frac { n } { 2 } \log \left( 2 \pi \right) - \frac { n } { 2 } \log \sigma ^ { 2 } - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \alpha } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$

This maximization is similar to finding the MLE of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$ in ordinary normal sampling
(see Example 7.2.11), and we leave the details to Exercise 11.28. The MLE of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$
under the conditional normal model, is

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \alpha } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

the RSS, evaluated at the least squares line, divided by the sample size. Henceforth,
when we refer to RSS we mean the RSS evaluated at the least squares line.

In Section 11.3.2, we showed that $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ were linear unbiased estimators of $\alpha$ and
$\beta .$ However, $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is not an unbiased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ For the calculation of $E \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ and $i n$
many subsequent calculations, the following lemma will be useful.

Lemma 11.3.2 Let $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ be uncorrelated random variables with $V a r Y _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 }$
for all $i = 1 , \ldots , n .$ Let $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ and $d _ { 1 } , \ldots , d _ { n }$ be two sets of constants. Then

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } Y _ { i } , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } \right) = \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } d _ { i } \right) \sigma ^ { 2 } .$$

Proof: This type of result has been encountered before. It is similar to Lemma 5.3.3
and Exercise 11.11. However, here we do not need either normality or independence
of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$
☐

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="552" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->

We next find the bias in $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ From (11.3.23) we have

$$\epsilon _ { i } = Y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } .$$

We define the residuals from the regression to be

(11.3.27)
$$\widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } = Y _ { i } - \widehat { \alpha } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } ,$$

and thus

$$\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \mathrm { R S S } .$$

It can be calculated (see Exercise 11.29) that

$$E \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } = 0 ,$$

and a lengthy calculation (also in Exercise 11.29) gives

(11.3.28)
$$\mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } = \mathrm { E } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { n - 2 } { n } + \frac { 1 } { S _ { x x } } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } + x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } - 2 x _ { i } \bar { x } \right) \right) \sigma ^ { 2 }$$

Thus,

$$E \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \mathrm { E } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ \frac { n - 2 } { n } + \frac { 1 } { S _ { x x } } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } + x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } - 2 x _ { i } \bar { x } \right) \right] \sigma ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \left[ \frac { n - 2 } { n } + \frac { 1 } { n S _ { x x } } \left\{ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - 2 S _ { x x } - 2 \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right\} \right]$$
$$\left( \sum x _ { i } \bar { x } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sum x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$
$$\left( \sum x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sum x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } = S _ { x x } \right)$$

$$= \left( \frac { n - 2 } { n } + 0 \right) \sigma ^ { 2 }$$
$$= \frac { n - 2 } { n } \sigma ^ { 2 }$$

The MLE $\widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 }$ is a biased estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ The more commonly used estimator of $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$
which is unbiased, is

(11.3.29)
$$S ^ { 2 } = \frac { n } { n - 2 } \widehat { \sigma } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \alpha } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$$

To develop estimation and testing procedures, based on these estimators, we need
to know their sampling distributions. These are summarized in the following theorem.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="553" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

Theorem 11.3.3 Under the conditional normal regression model (11.3.22), the sam-
pling distributions of $t h e$ estimators a, $\widehat { \beta } ,$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are

$$\widehat { \alpha } \sim n \left( \alpha , \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n S _ { x x } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) , \quad \widehat { \beta } \sim n \left( \beta , \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right) ,$$

with

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right) = \frac { - \sigma ^ { 2 } \bar { x } } { S _ { x x } } .$$

Furthermore, $\left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right)$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are independent and

$$\frac { \left( n - 2 \right) S ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } } \sim \chi _ { n - 2 } ^ { 2 } .$$

Proof: We first show that $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ have the indicated normal distributions. The
estimators $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are both linear functions of the independent normal random
variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } .$ Thus, by Corollary 4.6.10, they both have normal distributions.
Specifically, in Section 11.3.2, we showed that $\widehat { \beta } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } Y _ { i } ,$ where the $d _ { i }$ are given
in (11.3.19), and we also showed that

$$E \beta = \beta \text { and } \mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \beta } = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } .$$

The estimator $\widehat { \alpha } = \bar { Y } - \widehat { \beta } \bar { x }$ can be expressed as $\widehat { \alpha } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } Y _ { i } ,$ where

$$c _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { n } - \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \bar { x } } { S _ { x x } }$$

and thus it is straightforward to verify that

$$E \widehat { \alpha } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } E Y _ { i } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } - \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \bar { x } } { S _ { x x } } \right) \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) = \alpha ,$$
$$\mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \alpha } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n S _ { x x } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right] ,$$

showing that $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ have the specified distributions. Also, $\mathrm { C o v } \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right)$ is easily cal-
culated using Lemma 11.3.2. Details are left to Exercise 11.30.

We next show that $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are independent of $S ^ { 2 } ,$ a fact that will follow from
Lemma 11.3.2 and Lemma 5.3.3. From the definition of $\widehat { \epsilon } _ { i }$ in (11.3.27), we can write

(11.3.30)
$$\widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ \delta _ { i j } - \left( c _ { j } + d _ { j } x _ { i } \right) \right] Y _ { j } ,$$

where

$$\delta _ { i j } = \left\{ \begin{array}{} 1 & \mathrm { i f } i = j \\ 0 & \mathrm { i f } i \neq j \end{array} , \quad c _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { n } - \frac { \left( x _ { j } - \bar { x } \right) \bar { x } } { S _ { x x } } , \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad d _ { j } = \frac { \left( x _ { j } - \bar { x } \right) } { S _ { x x } } . \right.$$
$$\begin{array}{} \end{array}$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="554" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

Since $\widehat { \alpha } = \sum c _ { i } Y _ { i }$ and $\widehat { \beta } = \sum d _ { i } Y _ { i } ,$ application of Lemma 11.3.2 together with some
algebra will show that

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } , \widehat { \alpha } \right) = \mathrm { C o v } \left( \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } , \widehat { \beta } \right) = 0 , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n .$$

Details are left to Exercise 11.31. Thus, it follows from Lemma 5.3.3 that, under
normal sampling, $S ^ { 2 } = \sum \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 2 \right)$ is independent of $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta } .$

To prove that $\left( n - 2 \right) S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { n - 2 } ^ { 2 } ,$ we write $\left( n - 2 \right) S ^ { 2 }$ as the sum of $n \quad - \quad 2$
independent random variables, each of which has a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution. That is, we find
constants $a _ { i j } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ and $j = 1 , \ldots , n - 2 ,$ that satisfy

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 2 } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } Y _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 }$$
(11.3.31)

where

\>
i=1
n
a¿j = 0, j = 1, ... , n-2,
and

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } a _ { i j ^ { \prime } } = 0 , \quad j \neq j ^ { \prime } .$$

The details are somewhat involved because of the general nature of the $x _ { i } \mathrm { s } .$ We omit
details.

☐

The RSS from the linear regression contains information about the worth of a
polynomial $\mathrm { f i t }$ of a higher order, over and above a linear fit. Since, in this model, we
assume that the population regression is linear, the variation in this higher-order fit
is just random variation. Robson (1959) gives a general recursion formula for finding
coefficients for such higher-order polynomial fits, a formula that can be adapted to
explicitly find the $a _ { i j } s$ of (11.3.31). Alternatively, Cochran's Theorem (see Miscellanea
11.5.1) can be used to establish that $\sum \epsilon _ { i } ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \sim \chi _ { n - 2 } ^ { 2 } .$

Inferences regarding the two parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are usually based on the following
two Student's t distributions. Their derivations follow immediately from the normal
and $x ^ { 2 }$ distributions and the independence in Theorem 11.3.3. We have

(11.3.32)
$$\frac { \widehat { \alpha } - \alpha } { S \sqrt { \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) / \left( n S _ { x x } \right) } } \sim t _ { n - 2 }$$

and

(11.3.33)
$$\frac { \widehat { \beta } - \beta } { S / \sqrt { S _ { x x } } } \sim t _ { n - 2 } .$$

The joint distribution of these two $t$ statistics is called a bivariate Student's $t$ dis-
tribution. This distribution is derived in a manner analogous to the univariate case.
We use the fact that the joint distribution of $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ is bivariate normal and the
same variance estimate $S$ is used in both univariate $t$ statistics. This joint distribution
would be used if we wanted to do simultaneous inference regarding $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ However,
we shall deal only with the inferences regarding one parameter at a time.

Usually there is more interest in $\beta$ than in $\alpha .$ The parameter $\alpha$ is the expected
value of $Y$ at $x = 0 ,$ $E \left( Y | x = 0 \right) .$ Depending on the problem, this may or may not

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="555" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

be an interesting quantity. In particular, the value $x = 0$ may not be a reasonable
value for the predictor variable. However, $\beta$ is the rate of change of $\mathrm { E } \left( Y | x \right)$ as a
function of x. That is, $\beta$ is the amount that $\mathrm { E } \left( Y | x \right)$ changes if $x$ is changed by one
unit. Thus, this parameter relates to the entire range of $x$ values and contains the
information about whatever linear relationship exists between $Y$ and x. (See Exercise
11.33.) Furthermore, the value $\beta = 0$ is of particular interest.

If $\beta = 0 ,$ then $E \left( Y | x \right) = \alpha + \beta x = \alpha$ and $Y \sim n \left( \alpha , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) ,$ which does not depend on
x. In a well-thought-out experiment leading to a regression analysis we do not expect
this to be the case, but we would be interested in knowing this if it were true.

The test that $\beta = 0$ is quite similar to the ANOVA test that all treatments are
equal. In the ANOVA the null hypothesis states that the treatments are unrelated to
the response in any way, while in linear regression the null hypothesis $\beta = 0$ states
that the treatments $\left( x \right)$ are unrelated to the response in a linear way.

To test

(11.3.34)
$$H _ { 0 } : \quad \beta = 0$$
$$H _ { 1 } : \quad \beta \neq 0$$
versus

using (11.3.33), we reject $H _ { 0 }$ at level $\alpha$ if

$$| \frac { \widehat { \beta } - 0 } { S / \sqrt { S _ { x x } } } | > t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 }$$

or, equivalently, if

(11.3.35)
$$\frac { \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } } > F _ { 1 , n - 2 , \alpha } .$$

Recalling the formula for $\widehat { \beta }$ and that $\mathrm { R S S } = \sum \widehat { \epsilon } _ { i } ^ { 2 } ,$ we have

$$\frac { \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } } = \frac { S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } } { \mathrm { R S S } / \left( n - 2 \right) } = \frac { \text { Regression sum of squares } } { \text { Residual sum of squares/df } } .$$

This last formula is summarized in the regression ANOVA table, which is like the
ANOVA tables encountered in Section 11.2. For simple linear regression, the table,
resulting in the test given in (11.3.35), is given in Table 11.3.2. Note that the table
involves only a hypothesis about $\beta .$ The parameter $\alpha$ and the estimate $\widehat { \alpha }$ play the
same role here as the grand mean did in Section 11.2. They merely serve to locate
the overall level of the data and are "corrected" for in the sums of squares.

Example 11.3.4 (Continuation of Example 11.3.1) The regression ANOVA
for the grape crop yield data follows.

We draw one final parallel with the analysis of variance. It may not be obvious
from Table 11.3.2, but the partitioning of the sum of squares of the ANOVA has an
analogue in regression. We have

Total sum of $s q u a r e s = R e g r e s s i o n$ sum of squares + Residual sum of squares

(11.3.36)
$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \widehat { y } _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { y } _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

where $\widehat { y } _ { i } = \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } .$ Notice the similarity of these sums of squares to those in ANOVA.
The total sum of squares is, of course, the same. The RSS measures deviation of the
fitted line from the observed values, and the regression sum of squares, analogous to
the ANOVA treatment sum of squares, measures the deviation of predicted values
("treatment means") from the grand mean. Also, as in the ANOVA, the sum of
squares identity is valid because of the disappearance of the cross-term (see Exercise
11.34). The total and residual sums of squares in (11.3.36) are clearly the same as
in Table 11.3.2. But the regression sum $\mathrm { o f }$ squares looks different. However, they are
equal (see Exercise 11.34); that is,

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \widehat { y } _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } }$$

The expression $S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x }$ is easier to use for computing and provides the link with the
$t$ test. But $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \widehat { y } _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 }$ is the more easily interpreted expression.

A statistic that is used to quantify how well the fitted line describes the data is the
coefficient of determination. It is defined as the ratio of the regression sum of squares
to the total sum of squares. It is usually referred to as $r ^ { 2 }$ and can be written in the
various forms

$$r ^ { 2 } = \frac { \text { Regression sum of squares } } { \text { Total sum of squares } } = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \widehat { y } _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \bar { y } \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } S _ { y y } } .$$

The coefficient of determination measures the proportion of the total variation in
$y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ (measured by $S _ { y y }$ that is explained by the fitted line (measured by the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="557" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

regression sum of squares). From (11.3.36), $0 \leq r ^ { 2 } \leq 1 .$ If $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ all fall exactly
on the fitted line, then $y _ { i } = \widehat { y _ { i } }$ for all $i$ and $r ^ { 2 } = 1 .$ If $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ are not close to the
fitted line, then the residual sum of squares will be large and $r ^ { 2 }$ will be near 0. The
coefficient of determination can also be (perhaps more straightforwardly) derived as
the square of the sample correlation coefficient of the $n$ pairs $\left( y _ { 1 } , x _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( y _ { n } , x _ { n } \right)$ or
of the $n$ pairs $\left( y _ { 1 } , \widehat { y } _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( y _ { n } , \widehat { y } _ { n } \right) .$

Expression (11.3.33) can be used to construct a $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ confidence interval for
$\beta$ given by

(11.3.37)
$$\widehat { \beta } - t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } \frac { S } { \sqrt { S _ { x x } } } < \beta < \widehat { \beta } + t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } \frac { S } { \sqrt { S _ { x x } } } .$$

Also, a level $\alpha$ test of $H _ { 0 } : \beta = \beta _ { 0 }$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \beta \neq \beta _ { 0 }$ rejects $H _ { 0 }$ if

(11.3.38)
$$| \frac { \widehat { \beta } - \beta _ { 0 } } { S / \sqrt { S _ { x x } } } | > t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } .$$

As mentioned above, it is common to test $H _ { 0 } : \beta = 0$ versus $H _ { 1 } : \beta \neq 0$ to determine
if there is some linear relationship between the predictor and response variables.
However, the above test is more general, since any value of $\beta _ { 0 }$ can be specified. The
regression ANOVA, which is locked into a "recipe," can test only $H _ { 0 } : \beta = 0 .$


### 11.3.5 Estimation and Prediction at a Specified $x = x _ { 0 }$

Associated with a specified value of the predictor variable, say $x = x _ { 0 } ,$ there is a
population of $Y$ values. In fact, according to the conditional normal model, a random
observation from this population is $Y \sim n \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$ After observing the regression
data $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right)$ and estimating the parameters $\alpha ,$ $\beta ,$ and $\sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ perhaps the
experimenter is going to set $x = x _ { 0 }$ and obtain a new observation, call it $Y _ { 0 } .$ There
might be interest in estimating the mean of the population from which this observation
will be drawn, or even predicting what this observation will be. We will now discuss
these types of inferences.

We assume that $\left( x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right)$ satisfy the conditional normal regression
model, and based on these $n$ observations we have the estimates $\widehat { \alpha } ,$ $\widehat { \beta } ,$ and $S ^ { 2 } .$ Let
$x _ { 0 }$ be a specified value of the predictor variable. First, consider estimating the mean
of the $Y$ population associated with $x _ { 0 } ,$ that is, $E \left( Y | x _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } .$ The obvi-
ous choice for our point estimator is $\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } .$ This is an unbiased estimator since
$E \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) = E \widehat { \alpha } + \left( E \widehat { \beta } \right) x _ { 0 } = \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } .$ Using the moments given in Theorem 11.3.3,
we can also calculate

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) = \mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \alpha } + \left( \mathrm { V a r } \widehat { \beta } \right) x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + 2 x _ { 0 } \mathrm { C o v } \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right)$$
$$= \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { n S _ { x x } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } - \frac { 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } x _ { 0 } x } { S _ { x x } }$$
$$= \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - \bar { x } ^ { 2 } + \bar { x } ^ { 2 } - 2 x _ { 0 } \bar { x } + x _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right)$$

$\left( \pm \bar { x } \right)$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="558" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->

$$= \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } \left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] + \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$

recombine
terms

)

$$= \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right)$$

$$\left( \sum x _ { i } ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { n } \left( \sum x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } = S _ { x x } \right)$$

Finally, since $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are both linear functions of $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n } ,$ so is $\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } .$ Thus
$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 }$ has a normal distribution, specifically,

(11.3.39)
$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \sim n \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right) \right) .$$

By Theorem 11.3.3, $\left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right)$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are independent. Thus $S ^ { 2 }$ is also independent $\mathrm { o f }$
$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 }$ (Theorem 4.6.12) and

$$\frac { \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } \right) } { S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } } } \sim t _ { n - 2 } .$$

This pivot can be inverted to give the $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ confidence interval for $\alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } ,$

$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } - t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$
$$\leq \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } \leq \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } + t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } .$$

(11.3.40)

The length of the confidence interval for $\alpha + \beta x _ { 0 }$ depends on the values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$
through the value of $\left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } .$ It is clear that the length of the interval is shorter
if $x _ { 0 }$ is near $\bar { x }$ and minimized at $x _ { 0 } = \bar { x } .$ Thus, in designing the experiment, the
experimenter should choose the values $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ so that the value $x _ { 0 } ,$ at which the
mean is to be estimated, is at or near $\bar { x } .$ It is only reasonable that we can estimate
more precisely near the center of the data we observed.

A type of inference we have not discussed until now is prediction of an, as yet,
unobserved random variable $Y ,$ a type of inference that is of interest in a regression
setting. For example, suppose that $x$ is a college applicant's measure of high school
performance. $A$ college admissions officer might want to use $x$ to predict $Y ,$ the stu-
dent's grade point average after one year of college. Clearly, $Y$ has not been observed
yet since the student has not even been admitted! The college has data on former
students, $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right) ,$ giving their high school performances and one-year
GPAs. These data might be used to predict the new student's GPA.

Definition 11.3.5 A $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ prediction interval for an unobserved random
variable $Y$ based on the observed data $\mathrm { X }$ is a random interval $\left[ L \left( X \right) , U \left( X \right) \right]$ with the
property that

$$P _ { \theta } \left( L \left( X \right) \leq Y \leq U \left( X \right) \right) \geq 1 - \alpha$$

for all values of the parameter $\theta .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="559" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

Note the similarity in the definitions of a prediction interval and a confidence in-
terval. The difference is that a prediction interval is an interval on a random variable,
rather than a parameter. Intuitively, since a random variable is more variable than
a parameter (which is constant), we expect a prediction interval to be wider than a
confidence interval of the same level. In the special case of linear regression, we see
that this is the case.

We assume that the new observation $Y _ { 0 }$ to be taken at $x = x _ { 0 }$ has a $n \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$
distribution, independent of the previous data, $\left( x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , Y _ { n } \right) .$ The estimators
$\widehat { \alpha } ,$ $\widehat { \beta } ,$ and $S ^ { 2 }$ are calculated from the previous data and, thus, $Y _ { 0 }$ is independent of
$\widehat { \alpha } ,$ $\beta ,$ and $S ^ { 2 } .$ Using $\left( 1 1 . 3 . 3 9 \right) ,$ we find that $Y _ { 0 } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right)$ has a normal distribution
with mean $E \left( Y _ { 0 } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) \right) = \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } \right) = 0$ and variance

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( Y _ { 0 } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) \right) = \mathrm { V a r } Y _ { 0 } + \mathrm { V a r } \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) = \sigma ^ { 2 } + \sigma ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right) .$$

Using the independence of $S ^ { 2 }$ and $Y _ { 0 } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) ,$ we see that

$$T = \frac { Y _ { 0 } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } \right) } { S \sqrt { 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } } } \sim t _ { n - 2 }$$

which can be rearranged in the usual $w a y$ to obtain the $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ prediction
interval,

(11.3.41)

$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } - t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$
$$< Y _ { 0 } < \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } + t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } } .$$

Since the endpoints of this interval depend only on the observed data, (11.3.41) defines
a prediction interval for the new observation $Y _ { 0 } .$


### 11.3.6 Simultaneous Estimation and Confidence Bands

In the previous section we looked at prediction at a single value $x _ { 0 } .$ In some circum-
stances, however, there may be interest in prediction at many $x _ { 0 } .$ For example, in the
previously mentioned grade point average prediction problem, an admissions officer
probably has interest in predicting the grade point average of many applicants, which
naturally leads to prediction at many $x _ { 0 } .$

The problem encountered is the (by now) familiar problem of simultaneous infer-
ence. That is, how do we control the overall confidence level for the simultaneous
inference? In the previous section, we saw that a $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for the
mean of the $Y$ population associated with $x _ { 0 } ,$ that is, $E \left( Y | x _ { 0 } \right) = \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } ,$ is given by

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="560" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->

$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } - t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$
$$< \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 } < \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 } + t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } } .$$

Now suppose that we want to make an inference about the $Y$ population mean at a
number of $x _ { 0 }$ values. For example, we might want intervals for $E \left( Y | x _ { 0 i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , m .$
We know that if we set up $m$ intervals as above, each at level $1 - \alpha ,$ the overall
inference will not be at the $1 - \alpha$ level.

A simple and reasonably good solution is to use the Bonferroni Inequality, as used
in Example 11.2.9. Using the inequality, we can state that the probability is at least
$1 - \alpha$ that

$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 i } - t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / \left( 2 m \right) } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$
$$< \alpha + \beta x _ { 0 i } < \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { 0 i } + t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / \left( 2 m \right) } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { 0 i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$

(11.3.42)

simultaneously for $i = 1 , \ldots , m .$ (See Exercise 11.39.)

We can take simultaneous inference in regression one step further. Realize that our
assumption about the population regression line implies that the equation $E \left( Y | x \right) =$
$\alpha + \beta x$ holds for all $x ;$ hence, we should be able to make inferences at all x. Thus, we
want to make a statement like (11.3.42), but we want it to hold for all $x .$ As might be
expected, as he did for the ANOVA, Scheffé derived a solution for this problem. We
summarize the result for the case of simple linear regression in the following theorem.

Theorem 11.3.6 Under the conditional normal regression model (11.3.22), the prob-
ability is at least $1 - \alpha$ that

$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x - M _ { \alpha } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$
$$< \alpha + \beta x < \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x + M _ { \alpha } S \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } }$$

(11.3.43)

simultaneously for all x, where $M _ { \alpha } = \sqrt { 2 F _ { 2 , n - 2 , \alpha } } .$

Proof: If we rearrange terms, it should be clear that the conclusion of the theorem
is true if we can find a constant $M _ { \alpha }$ that satisfies

$$P \left( \frac { \left( \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x \right) - \left( \alpha + \beta x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } \leq M _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } \text { for all } x \right] = 1 - \alpha$$

or, equivalently,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="561" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION" -->

$$P \left( \max _ { x } \frac { \left( \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x \right) - \left( \alpha + \beta x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } \leq M _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \alpha .$$

The parameterization given in Exercise 11.32, which results in independent estimators

for $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$ makes the above maximization easier. Write
$$\widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x = \bar { Y } + \widehat { \beta } \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ,$$
$$\left( \mu _ { \bar { Y } } = \mathrm { E } \bar { Y } = \alpha + \beta \bar { x } \right)$$
$$\alpha + \beta x = \mu _ { Y } + \beta \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ,$$

and, for notational convenience, define $t = x - \bar { x } .$ We then have

$$\frac { \left( \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x \right) - \left( \alpha + \beta x \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } = \frac { \left( \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) + \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) t \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } ,$$

and we want to find $M _ { \alpha }$ to satisfy

$$P \left( \max _ { t } \frac { \left( \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) + \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) t \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } \leq M _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \alpha .$$

Note that $S ^ { 2 }$ plays no role in the maximization, merely being a constant. Applying
the result of Exercise 11.40, a direct application of calculus, we obtain

$$\max _ { t } \frac { \left( \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) + \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) t \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { \bar { S } _ { x x } } \right] } = \frac { n \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) ^ { 2 } + S _ { x x } \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } }$$

$$\left. = \frac { \frac { \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } / n } + \frac { \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } } } { S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } } . \quad \quad \text { \left(multiply by o } ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } \right)$$
(11.3.44)

From Theorem 11.3.3 and Exercise 11.32, we see that this last expression is $\mathrm { t h e }$
quotient of independent chi squared random variables, the denominator being divided
by its degrees of freedom. The numerator is the sum of two independent random
variables, each of which has a $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution. Thus the numerator is distributed as
$\chi _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,$ the distribution of the quotient is

$$\frac { \frac { \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } / n } + \frac { \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) ^ { 2 } } { \sigma ^ { 2 } / S _ { x x } } } { S ^ { 2 } / \sigma ^ { 2 } } \sim 2 F _ { 2 , n - 2 } ,$$

and

$$P \left( \max _ { t } \frac { \left( \left( \bar { Y } - \mu _ { \bar { Y } } \right) + \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) t \right) ^ { 2 } } { S ^ { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { S _ { x x } } \right] } \leq M _ { \alpha } ^ { 2 } \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

if $M _ { \alpha } = \sqrt { 2 F _ { 2 , n - 2 } } ,$ proving the theorem.
☐

<!-- PageNumber="562" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ANALYSIS OF VARIANCE AND REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 11.3" -->
