As might be expected, this line is different from the least squares line. In fact, as
we shall see, this line always lies between the ordinary regression of $y$ on $x$ and the
ordinary regression of $x$ on $y .$ This is illustrated in Figure 12.2.2, where the data in
Table 11.3.1 were used to calculate the orthogonal least squares line $\widehat { y } = - . 4 9 + 1 . 8 8 x .$

In simple linear regression we saw that, under normality, the ordinary least squares
solutions for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ were the same as the MLEs. Here, the orthogonal least squares
solution is the MLE only in a special case, when we make certain assumptions about
the parameters.

The difficulties to be encountered with likelihood estimation once again illustrate
the differences between a mathematical solution and a statistical solution. We ob-
tained a mathematical least squares solution to the line fitting problem without much
difficulty. This will not happen with the likelihood solution.


#### 12.2.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation

We first consider the maximum likelihood solution of the functional linear relationship
model, the situation for the structural relationship model being similar and, in some
respects, easier. With the normality assumption, the functional relationship model
can be expressed as

$$Y _ { i } \sim n \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } \right)$$
and X ;~ n(§2, 0%), i = 1, ... , n,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="584" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

where the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ and $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are independent. Given observations $\left( x , y \right) = \left( \left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \cdots \right) ,$
$\left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right) ,$ the likelihood function is

(12.2.12)
$$L \left( \alpha , \beta , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } | \left( x , y \right) \right) =$$
$$\frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n } } \frac { 1 } { \left( \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { n / 2 } } \exp \left[ - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } \right] \exp \left[ - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } \right] .$$

The problem with this likelihood function is that it does not have a finite maximum.
To see this, take the parameter configuration $\xi _ { i } = x _ { i }$ and then let $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0 .$ The value
of the function goes to infinity, showing that there is no maximum likelihood solution.
In fact, Solari (1969) has shown that if the equations defining the first derivative of
$L$ are set equal to 0 and solved, the result is a saddle point, not a maximum. Notice
that as long as we have total control over the parameters, we can always force the
likelihood function to infinity. In particular, we can always take a variance to 0, while
keeping the exponential term bounded.

We will make the common assumption, which not only is reasonable but also alle-
viates many problems, that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\lambda > 0$ is fixed and known. (See Kendall
and Stuart 1979, Chapter 29, for a discussion of other assumptions on the variances.)
This assumption is one of the least restrictive, saying that we know only the ratio of
the variances, not the individual values. Moreover, the resulting model is relatively
well behaved.

Under this assumption, we can write the likelihood function as

(12.2.13)
$$L \left( \alpha , \beta , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } | \left( \mathrm { x } , \mathrm { y } \right) \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n } } \frac { \lambda ^ { n / 2 } } { \left( \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { n } } \exp \left[ - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } + \lambda \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } \right] ,$$

which we can now maximize. We will perform the maximization in stages, making
sure that, at each $\mathrm { s t e r } ,$ we have a maximum before proceeding to the next step. By
examining the function (12.2.13), we can determine a reasonable order of maximiza-
tion.

First, for each value of a, $\beta ,$ and $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } ,$ to maximize L with respect to $\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n }$ we
minimize $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } + \lambda \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$ . (See Exercise 12.3 for details.) For
each $i ,$ we have a quadratic in $\xi _ { i }$ and the minimum is attained at

$$\xi _ { i } ^ { * } = \frac { x _ { i } + \lambda \beta \left( y _ { i } - \alpha \right) } { 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } }$$

On substituting back we get

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } ^ { * } \right) ^ { 2 } + \lambda \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } ^ { * } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="585" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION WITH ERRORS IN VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

The likelihood function now becomes

$$\max _ { \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } } L \left( \alpha , \beta , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } | \left( \mathrm { x } , \mathrm { y } \right) \right)$$
$$\left. . 1 4 \right) \quad = \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n } } \frac { \lambda ^ { n / 2 } } { \left( \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { n } } \exp \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \right\} .$$

Now, we can maximize with respect to $\alpha$ and $\beta ,$ but a little work will show that we
have already done this in the orthogonal least squares solution! Yes, there is somewhat
of a correspondence between orthogonal least squares and maximum likelihood in the
EIV model and we are about to exploit it. Define

(12.2.15)
$$\alpha ^ { * } = \sqrt { \lambda } \alpha , \quad \beta ^ { * } = \sqrt { \lambda } \beta , \quad y _ { i } ^ { * } = \sqrt { \lambda } y _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n .$$

The exponent of (12.2.14) becomes

$$\frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \beta ^ { * } { } ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } ^ { * } - \left( \alpha ^ { * } + \beta ^ { * } x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

which is identical to the expression in the orthogonal least squares problem. From
(12.2.11) we know the minimizing values of $\alpha ^ { * }$ and $\beta ^ { * } ,$ and using (12.2.15) we obtain
our MLEs for the slope and intercept:

$$\mathrm { a n d } \quad \widehat { \beta } = \frac { - \left( S _ { x x } - \lambda S _ { y y } \right) + \sqrt { \left( S _ { x x } - \lambda S _ { y y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } } } { 2 \lambda S _ { x y } } . ,$$
(12.2.16) â = y - §ã

It is clear from the formula that, at $\lambda = 1 ,$ the MLEs agree with the orthogonal least
squares solutions. This makes sense. The orthogonal least squares solution treated
$x$ and $y$ as having the same magnitude of error and this translates into a variance
ratio of 1. Carrying this argument further, we can relate this solution to ordinary
least squares or maximum likelihood when the $x s$ are assumed to be fixed. If the $x s$
are fixed, their variance is 0 and hence $\lambda = 0 .$ The maximum likelihood solution for
general $\lambda$ does reduce to ordinary least squares in this case. This relationship, among
others, is explored in Exercise 12.4.

Putting (12.2.16) together with (12.2.14), we now have almost completely maxi-
mized the likelihood. We have

(12.2.17)
$$\max _ { \alpha , \beta , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } } L \left( \alpha , \beta , \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } | \left( \mathrm { x } , \mathrm { y } \right) \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) ^ { n } } \frac { \lambda ^ { n / 2 } } { \left( \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { n } } \exp \left[ - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } \frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right] .$$

Now maximizing $L$ with respect to $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is very similar to finding the MLE of $\sigma ^ { 2 }$
in ordinary normal sampling (see Example 7.2.11), the major difference being the
exponent of n, rather than $n / 2 ,$ on $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } .$ The details are left to Exercise 12.5. The

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="586" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

resulting MLE for $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is

(12.2.18)
$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 n } \frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

From the properties of MLEs, it follows that the MLE of $\sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 }$ is given by $\widehat { \sigma } _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } = \widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } / \lambda$
and $\widehat { \xi } _ { i } = \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } .$ Although the $\widehat { \xi } _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are not usually of interest, they can sometimes
be useful if prediction is desired. Also, the $\widehat { \xi } _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are useful in examining the adequacy
of the fit (see Fuller 1987).

$I t$ is interesting to note that although $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are consistent estimators, $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is not.
More precisely, as $n \rightarrow \infty ,$

$$\widehat { \alpha } \rightarrow \alpha \text { in probability } ,$$

$$\widehat { \beta } \rightarrow \beta \text { in probability } ,$$

but

$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \text { in probability. } .$$

General results on consistency in EIV functional relationship models have been ob-
tained by Gleser (1981).

We now turn to the linear structural relationship model. Recall that here we assume
that we observe pairs $\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ according to
☒

$$Y _ { i } \sim n \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$X _ { i } \sim n \left( \xi _ { i } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$\xi _ { i } \sim n \left( \xi , \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

where the $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are independent and, given the $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s } ,$ the $X _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ and $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are independent. As
mentioned before, inference about $\alpha$ and $\beta$ will be made from the marginal distribution
of $X _ { i }$ and $Y _ { i } ,$ that is, the distribution obtained by integrating out $\xi _ { i } .$ If we integrate
out $\xi _ { i }$ , we obtain the marginal distribution of $\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right)$ (see Exercise 12.6):
☒ ☒

$$\left( 1 2 . 2 . 1 9 \right) \left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right) \sim \text { bivariate normal } \left( \xi , \alpha + \beta \xi , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } + \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } + \beta ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } , \beta \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$ ☒

Notice the similarity of the correlation structure to that of the RCB ANOVA (see Mis-
cellanea 11.5.3). There, conditional on blocks, the observations were uncorrelated, but
unconditionally, there was correlation (the intraclass correlation). Here, the functional
relationship model, which is conditional on the $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s } ,$ has uncorrelated observations, but
the structural relationship model, where we infer unconditional on the $\xi _ { i } s ,$ has cor-
related observations. The $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are playing a role similar to blocks and the correlation
that appears here is similar to the intraclass correlation. (In fact, it is identical to the
intraclass correlation if $\beta = 1$ and $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 }$

To proceed with likelihood estimation in this case, given observations $\left( x , y \right) =$
$\left( \left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) , \ldots , \left( x _ { n } , y _ { n } \right) \right) ,$ the likelihood function is that of a bivariate normal, as was
encountered in Exercise 7.18. There, it was seen that the likelihood estimators in
the bivariate normal could be found by equating sample quantities to population
quantities. Hence, to find the MLEs of a, $\beta ,$ $\xi ,$ $\sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } ,$ and $\sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } ,$ we solve

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="587" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION WITH ERRORS IN VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

(12.2.20)

$$\bar { y } = \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } \widehat { \xi } ,$$
$$\bar { x } = \widehat { \xi } ,$$
$$\frac { 1 } { n } S _ { y y } = \widehat { \sigma } _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } + \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } ,$$
$$\frac { 1 } { n } S _ { x x } = \widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } + \widehat { \sigma } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } ,$$
$$\frac { 1 } { n } S _ { x y } = \widehat { \beta } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } .$$

Note that we have five equations, but there are six unknowns, so the system is in-
determinate. That is, the system of equations does not have a unique solution and
there is no unique value of the parameter vector $\left( \alpha , \beta , \xi , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } \right)$ that maximizes
the likelihood.

Before we go on, realize that the variances of $X _ { i }$ and $Y _ { i }$ here are different from the
variances in the functional relationship model. There we were working conditional on
$\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } ,$ and here we are working marginally with respect to the $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s } .$ So, for example,
in the functional relationship model we write Var $X _ { i } = \sigma _ { 8 } ^ { 2 }$ (where it is understood
that this variance is conditional on $\left. \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) ,$ while in the structural model we write
$\mathrm { V a r } X _ { i } = \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } + \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 }$ (where it is understood that this variance is unconditional on
$\left. \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) .$ This should not be a source of confusion.

A solution to the equations in (12.2.20) implies a restriction on $\widehat { \beta } ,$ a restriction that
we have already encountered in the functional relationship case (see Exercise 12.4).
From the above equations involving the variances and covariance, it is straightforward
to deduce that

$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } \geq 0 \quad \text { only if } S _ { x x } \geq \frac { 1 } { \widehat { \beta } } S _ { x y } ,$$
$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } \geq 0 \quad \text { only if } S _ { y y } \geq \widehat { \beta } S _ { x y } ,$$

which together imply that

$$\frac { | S _ { x y } | } { S _ { x x } } \leq | \widehat { \beta } | \leq \frac { S _ { y y } } { | S _ { x y } | } .$$

(The bounds on $\widehat { \beta }$ are established in Exercise 12.9.)

We now address the identifiability problem in the structural relationship case, a
problem that can be expected since, in (12.2.19) we have more parameters than are
needed to specify the distribution. To make the structural linear relationship model
identifiable, we must make an assumption that reduces the number of parameters
to five. It fortunately happens that the assumption about variances made for the
functional relationship solves the identifiability problem here. Thus, we assume that
$\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\lambda$ is known. This reduces the number of unknown parameters to five
and makes the model identifiable. (See Exercise 12.8.) More restrictive assumptions,
such as assuming that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is known, may lead to MLEs of variances that have the
value 0. Kendall and Stuart (1979, Chapter 29) have a full discussion of this.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="588" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

Once we assume that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ the maximum likelihood estimates for $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$
in this model are the same as in the functional relationship model and are given by
(12.2.16). The variance estimates are different, however, and are given by

$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( S _ { x x } - \frac { S _ { x y } } { \widehat { \beta } } \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \widehat { \sigma } _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } { \lambda } = \frac { 1 } { n } \left( S _ { y y } - \widehat { \beta } S _ { x y } \right) ,$$

(12.2.21)

$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n } \frac { S _ { x y } } { \widehat { \beta } } .$$

(Exercise 12.10 shows this and also explores the relationship between variance esti-
mates here and in the functional model.) Note that, in contrast to what happened
in the functional relationship model, these estimators are all consistent in the linear
structural relationship model (when $\left. \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } \right) .$


##### 12.2.4 Confidence Sets

As might be expected, the construction of confidence sets in the EIV model is a
difficult task. A complete treatment of the subject needs machinery that we have
not developed. In particular, we will concentrate here only on confidence sets for the
slope, $\beta .$

As a first attack, we could use the approximate likelihood method of Section 10.4.1
to construct approximate confidence intervals. In practice this is probably what is
most often done and is not totally unreasonable. However, these approximate intervals
cannot maintain a nominal $1 - \alpha$ confidence level. In fact, results of Gleser and Hwang
(1987) yield the rather unsettling result that any interval estimator of the slope whose
length is always finite will have confidence coefficient equal to 0!

For definiteness, in the remainder of this section we will assume that we are in the
structural relationship case of the EIV model. The confidence set results presented
are valid in both the structural and functional cases and, in particular, the formulas
remain the same. We continue to assume that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ where $\lambda$ is known.

Gleser and Hwang (1987) identify the parameter

$$\tau ^ { 2 } = \frac { \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } }$$

as determining the amount of information potentially available in the data to de-
termine the slope $\beta .$ They show that, as $\tau ^ { 2 } \rightarrow 0 ,$ the coverage probability of any
finite-length confidence interval on $\beta$ must also go to 0. To see why this is plausible,
note that $\tau ^ { 2 } = 0$ implies that the $\xi _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ do not vary and it would be impossible to $f i t \quad a$
unique straight line.

An approximate confidence interval for $\beta$ can be constructed by using the fact that
the estimator

$$\widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \left( 1 + \lambda \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( S _ { x x } S _ { y y } - S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } \right) } { \left( S _ { x x } - \lambda S _ { y y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } }$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="589" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION WITH ERRORS IN VARIABLES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

is a consistent estimator of $\sigma _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } ,$ the true variance of $\widehat { \beta } .$ Hence, using the CLT together
with Slutsky's Theorem (see Section 5.5), we can show that the interval

$$\widehat { \beta } - \frac { z _ { \alpha / 2 } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } } { \sqrt { n } } \leq \beta \leq \widehat { \beta } + \frac { z _ { \alpha / 2 } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } } { \sqrt { n } }$$

is an approximate $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\beta .$ However, since it has finite length,
it cannot maintain $1 - \alpha$ coverage for all parameter values.

Gleser (1987) considers a modification of this interval and reports the infimum of
its coverage probabilities as a function of $\tau ^ { 2 } .$ Gleser's modification, $C _ { G } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) ,$ is

(12.2.22)
$$\widehat { \beta } - \frac { t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } } { \sqrt { n - 2 } } \leq \beta \leq \widehat { \beta } + \frac { t _ { n - 2 , \alpha / 2 } \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } } { \sqrt { n - 2 } } .$$

Again using the CLT together with Slutsky's Theorem, we can show that this is an
approximate $1 - \alpha$ confidence interval for $\beta .$ Since this interval also has finite length,
it also cannot maintain $1 - \alpha$ coverage for all parameter values. Gleser does some
finite-sample numerical calculations and gives bounds on the infima of the coverage
probabilities as a function of $\tau ^ { 2 } .$ For reasonable values of $n \left( \geq 1 0 \right) ,$ the coverage
probability of a nominal 90% interval will be at least 80% if $\tau ^ { 2 } \geq . 2 5 .$ As $\tau ^ { 2 }$ or $n$
increases, this performance improves.

In contrast to $C _ { G } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right)$ of (12.2.22), which has finite length but no guaranteed coverage
probability, we now look at an exact confidence set that, as it must, has infinite length.
The set, known as the Creasy-Williams confidence set, is due to Creasy (1956) and
Williams (1959) and is based on the fact (see Exercise 12.11) that if $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$ then

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \beta \lambda Y _ { i } + X _ { i } , Y _ { i } - \beta X _ { i } \right) = 0 .$$

Define $r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right)$ to be the sample correlation coefficient between $\beta \lambda Y _ { i } + X _ { i }$ and $Y _ { i } - \beta X _ { i } ,$
that is,

$$r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right) = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( \beta \lambda y _ { i } + x _ { i } \right) - \left( \beta \lambda \bar { y } + \bar { x } \right) \right) \left( \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) - \left( \bar { y } - \beta \bar { x } \right) \right) } { \sqrt { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( \beta \lambda y _ { i } + x _ { i } \right) - \left( \beta \lambda \bar { y } + \bar { x } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) - \left( \bar { y } - \beta \bar { x } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } }$$

(12.2.23)
$$= \frac { \beta \lambda S _ { y y } + \left( 1 - \beta ^ { 2 } \lambda \right) S _ { x y } - \beta S _ { x x } } { \sqrt { \left( \beta ^ { 2 } \lambda ^ { 2 } S _ { y y } + 2 \beta \lambda S _ { x y } + S _ { x x } \right) \left( S _ { y y } - 2 \beta S _ { x y } + \beta ^ { 2 } S _ { x x } \right) } } .$$

Since $\beta \lambda Y _ { i } + X _ { i }$ and $Y _ { i } - \beta X _ { i }$ are bivariate normal with correlation 0, it follows (see
Exercise 11.33) that

$$\frac { \sqrt { n - 2 } r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right) } { \sqrt { 1 - r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } } \sim t _ { n - 2 }$$

for any value of $\beta .$ Thus, we have identified a pivotal quantity and we conclude that

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="590" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.2" -->

the set

(12.2.24)
$$\left\{ \beta : \frac { \left( n - 2 \right) r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } { 1 - r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } \leq F _ { 1 , n - 2 , \alpha } \right\}$$

is a $1 - \alpha$ confidence set for $\beta$ (see Exercise 12.11).

Although this confidence set is a $1 - \alpha ,$ it suffers from defects similar to those
of Fieller's intervals. The function describing the set (12.2.24) has two minima, where
the function is 0. The confidence set can consist of two finite disjoint intervals, one
finite and two infinite disjoint intervals, or the entire real line. For example, the graph
of the $F$ statistic function for the data in Table 11.3.1 with $\lambda = 1$ is in Figure 12.2.3.
The confidence set is all the $\beta \mathrm { s }$ where the function is less than or equal to $F _ { 1 , 2 2 , \alpha } .$
For $\alpha = . 0 5$ and $F _ { 1 , 2 2 , 0 5 } = 4 . 3 0 ,$ the confidence set is $\left[ - 1 . 1 3 , - . 1 4 \right] \cup \left[ . 8 9 , 7 . 3 8 \right] .$ For
$\alpha = . 0 1$ and $F _ { 1 , 2 2 , 0 1 } = 7 . 9 5 ,$ the confidence set is $\left( - \infty , - 1 8 . 1 8 \right] \cup \left[ - 1 . 6 8 , . 0 6 \right] \cup \left[ . 6 0 , \infty \right) .$

Furthermore, for every value of $\beta , - r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right) = r _ { \lambda } \left( - 1 / \left( \lambda \beta \right) \right)$ (see Exercise $\left. 1 2 . 1 2 \right) s o$
that if $\beta$ is in the confidence set, $\mathrm { S O }$ is $- 1 / \left( \lambda \beta \right) .$ Using this confidence set, we cannot
distinguish $\beta$ from $- 1 / \left( \lambda \beta \right)$ and this confidence set always contains both positive and
negative values. We can never determine the sign of the slope from this confidence
set!

The confidence set given in (12.2.24) is not exactly the one discussed by Creasy
(1956) but rather a modification. She was actually interested in estimating $\phi ,$ the
angle that $\beta$ makes with the x-axis, that is, $\beta = \tan \left( \phi \right) ,$ and confidence sets there
have fewer problems. Estimation of $\phi$ is perhaps more natural in EIV models (see,
for example, Anderson 1976), but we seem to be more inclined to estimate $\alpha$ and $\beta .$

Most of the other standard statistical analyses that can be done in the ordinary
linear regression case have analogues in EIV models. For example, we can test hy-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="591" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOGISTIC REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.3" -->

potheses about $\beta$ or estimate values of $\mathrm { E } Y _ { i } .$ More about these topics can be found in
Fuller (1987) or Kendall and Stuart (1979, Chapter 29).


## 12.3 Logistic Regression

The conditional normal model of Section 11.3.3 is an example of a generalized linear
model (GLM). A GLM describes a relationship between the mean of a response vari-
able $Y$ and an independent variable $x .$ But the relationship may be more complicated
than the $E Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ of (11.3.2). Many different models can be expressed as GLMs.
In this section, we will concentrate on a specific GLM, the logistic regression model.


### 12.3.1 The Model

A GLM consists of three components: the random component, the systematic com-
ponent, and the link function.

(1) The response variables $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are the random component. They are assumed
to be independent random variables, each with a distribution from a specified
exponential family. The $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ are not identically distributed, but they each have a
distribution from the same family: binomial, Poisson, normal, etc.

(2) The systematic component is the model. It is the function of the predictor variable
$x _ { i } ,$ linear in the parameters, that is related to the mean of $Y _ { i } .$ So the systematic
component could be $\alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ or $\alpha + \beta / x _ { i } ,$ for example. We will consider only
$\alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ here.

(3) Finally, the link function $g \left( \mu \right)$ links the two components by asserting that $g \left( \mu _ { i } \right) =$
$\alpha + \beta x _ { i } ,$ where $\mu _ { i } = \mathrm { E } Y _ { i } .$

The conditional normal regression model of Section 11.3.3 is an example of a GLM.
In that model, the responses $Y _ { i } \mathrm { s }$ all have normal distributions. Of course, the normal
family is an exponential family, which is the random component. The form of the
regression function is $\alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ in this model, which is the systematic component.
Finally, the relationship $\mu _ { i } = E Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ is assumed. This means the link function
is $g \left( \mu \right) = \mu .$ This simple link function is called the identity link.

Another very useful GLM is the logistic regression model. In this model, the re-
sponses $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are independent and $Y _ { i } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( \pi _ { i } \right) .$ (The Bernoulli family is
an exponential family.) Recall, $E Y _ { i } = \pi _ { i } = P \left( Y _ { i } = 1 \right) .$ In this model, $\pi _ { i }$ is assumed
to be related to $x _ { i }$ by

(12.3.1)
$$\log \left( \frac { \pi _ { i } } { 1 - \pi _ { i } } \right) = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } .$$

The left-hand side is the log of the odds of success for $Y _ { i } .$ The model assumes this
$\log - 0 d d s$ (or logit) is a linear function of the predictor x. The Bernoulli pmf can be
written in exponential family form as

$$\pi ^ { y } \left( 1 - \pi \right) ^ { 1 - y } = \left( 1 - \pi \right) \exp \left\{ y \log \left( \frac { \pi _ { i } } { 1 - \pi _ { i } } \right) \right\} .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="592" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.3" -->

The term $\log \left( \pi / \left( 1 - \pi \right) \right)$ is the natural parameter of this exponential family, and in
(12.3.1) the link function $g \left( \pi \right) = \log \left( \pi / \left( 1 - \pi \right) \right)$ is used. When the natural parameter
is used in this way, it is called the canonical link.

Equation (12.3.1) can be rewritten as

$$\pi _ { i } = \frac { e ^ { \alpha + \beta x _ { i } } } { 1 + e ^ { \alpha + \beta x _ { i } } }$$

or, more generally,

(12.3.2)
$$\pi \left( x \right) = \frac { e ^ { \alpha + \beta x } } { 1 + e ^ { \alpha + \beta x } }$$

We see that $0 < \pi \left( x \right) < 1 ,$ which seems appropriate because $\pi \left( x \right)$ is a probability.
But, if it is possible that $\pi \left( x \right) = 0$ or 1 for some $x ,$ then this model is not appropriate.
If we examine $\pi \left( x \right)$ more closely, its derivative can be written

(12.3.3)
$$\frac { d \pi \left( x \right) } { d x } = \beta \pi \left( x \right) \left( 1 - \pi \left( x \right) \right) .$$

As the term $\pi \left( x \right) \left( 1 - \pi \left( x \right) \right)$ is always positive, the derivative of $\pi \left( x \right)$ is positive, 0, or
negative according as $\beta$ is positive, 0, or negative. If $\beta$ is positive, $\pi \left( x \right)$ is a strictly
increasing function of $x ;$ if $\beta$ is negative, $\pi \left( x \right)$ is a strictly decreasing function of $x ;$ if
$\beta = 0 ,$ $\pi \left( x \right) = e ^ { \alpha } / \left( 1 + e ^ { \alpha } \right)$ for all x. As in simple linear regression, if $\beta = 0 ,$ there is no
relationship between $\pi$ and x. Also, in a logistic regression model, $\pi \left( - \alpha / \beta \right) = 1 / 2 .$ A
logistic regression function exhibits this kind of symmetry; for any c, $\pi \left( \left( - \alpha / \beta \right) + c \right) =$
$1 - \pi \left( \left( - \alpha / \beta \right) - c \right) .$

The parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have meanings similar to those in simple linear regression.
Setting $x = 0$ in (12.3.1) yields that $\alpha$ is the log-odds of success at $x = 0 .$ Evaluating
(12.3.1) at $x$ and $x + 1$ yields, for any x,

$$\log \left( \frac { \pi \left( x + 1 \right) } { 1 - \pi \left( x + 1 \right) } \right) - \log \left( \frac { \pi \left( x \right) } { 1 - \pi \left( x \right) } \right) = \alpha + \beta \left( x + 1 \right) - \alpha - \beta \left( x \right) = \beta .$$

Thus, $\beta$ is the change in the log-odds of success corresponding to a one-unit increase
in x. In simple linear regression, $\beta$ is the change in the mean of $Y$ corresponding to
a one-unit increase in $x .$ Exponentiating both sides of this equality yields

(12.3.4)
$$e ^ { \beta } = \frac { \pi \left( x + 1 \right) / \left( 1 - \pi \left( x + 1 \right) \right) } { \pi \left( x \right) / \left( 1 - \pi \left( x \right) \right) }$$

The right-hand side is the odds ratio comparing the odds of success at $x + 1$ to the odds
of success at x. (Recall that in Examples 5.5.19 and 5.5.22 we looked at estimating
odds.) In a logistic regression model this ratio is constant as a function of $x .$ Finally,

(12.3.5)
$$\frac { \pi \left( x + 1 \right) } { 1 - \pi \left( x + 1 \right) } = e ^ { \beta } \frac { \pi \left( x \right) } { 1 - \pi \left( x \right) }$$

that is, $e ^ { \beta }$ is the multiplicative change in the odds of success corresponding to a
one-unit increase in $x .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="593" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOGISTIC REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.3" -->

Equation (12.3.2) suggests other ways of modeling the Bernoulli success probability
$\pi \left( x \right)$ as a function of the predictor variable $x .$ Recall that $F \left( w \right) = e ^ { w } / \left( 1 + e ^ { w } \right)$ is the
cdf of a logistic(0, 1) distribution. In (12.3.2) we have assumed $\pi \left( x \right) = F \left( \alpha + \beta x \right) .$
We can define other models for $\pi \left( x \right)$ by using other continuous cdfs. If $F \left( w \right)$ is the
standard normal cdf, the model is called probit regression (see Exercise 12.17). If a
Gumbel cdf is used, the link function is called the log-log link.


### 12.3.2 Estimation

In linear regression, where we use a model such as $Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } + \varepsilon _ { i } ,$ the technique of
least squares was an option for calculating estimates of $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ This is no longer the
case here. In the model (12.3.1) with $Y _ { i } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( \pi _ { i } \right) ,$ we no longer have a direct
connection between $Y _ { i }$ and $\alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ (which is why we need a link function). Thus,
least squares is no longer an option.

The estimation method that is most commonly used is maximum likelihood. In
the general model we have $Y _ { i } \sim \text { Bernoulli } \left( \pi _ { i } \right) ,$ where $\pi \left( x \right) = F \left( \alpha + \beta x \right) .$ If we let
$F _ { i } = F \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) ,$ then the likelihood function is

$$L \left( \alpha , \beta | \mathrm { y } \right) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \pi \left( x _ { i } \right) ^ { y _ { i } } \left( 1 - \pi \left( x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 1 - y _ { i } } = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } F _ { i } { } ^ { y _ { i } } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) ^ { 1 - y _ { i } } .$$

with $\log$ likelihood

$$\log L \left( \alpha , \beta | \mathrm { y } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left\{ \log \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) + y _ { i } \log \left( \frac { F _ { i } } { 1 - F _ { i } } \right) \right\} .$$

We obtain the likelihood equations by differentiating the log likelihood with respect
to $\alpha$ and $\beta .$ Let $d \quad F \left( w \right) / d w = f \left( w \right) ,$ the pdf corresponding to $F \left( w \right) ,$ and let $f _ { i } =$
$f \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) .$ Then

$$\frac { \partial \log \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) } { \partial \alpha } = - \frac { f _ { i } } { 1 - F _ { i } } = - \frac { F _ { i } f _ { i } } { F _ { i } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) }$$

and

(12.3.6)
$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha } \log \left( \frac { F _ { i } } { 1 - F _ { i } } \right) = \frac { f _ { i } } { F _ { i } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) }$$

Hence,

(12.3.7)
$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha } \log L \left( \alpha , \beta | \mathrm { y } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - F _ { i } \right) \frac { f _ { i } } { F _ { i } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) } .$$

A similar calculation yields

(12.3.8)
$$\frac { \partial } { \partial \beta } \log L \left( \alpha , \beta | y \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - F _ { i } \right) \frac { f _ { i } } { F _ { i } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) } x _ { i }$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="594" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.3" -->

For logistic regression, with $F \left( w \right) = e ^ { w } / \left( 1 + e ^ { w } \right) ,$ $f _ { i } / \left[ F _ { i } \left( 1 - F _ { i } \right) \right] = 1 ,$ and (12.3.7)
and (12.3.8) are somewhat simpler.

The MLEs are obtained by setting (12.3.7) and (12.3.8) equal to 0 and solving for
$\alpha$ and $\beta .$ These are nonlinear equations in $\alpha$ and $\beta$ and must be solved numerically.
(This will be discussed later.) For logistic and probit regression, the $\log$ likelihood is
strictly concave. Hence, if the likelihood equations have a solution, it is unique and
it is the MLE. However, for some extreme data the likelihood equations do not have
a solution. The maximum of the likelihood occurs in some limit as the parameters $\mathrm { g c }$
to $\pm \infty .$ See Exercise 12.16 for an example. This is because the logistic model assumes
$0 < \pi \left( x \right) < 1 ,$ but, for certain data sets, the maximum of the logistic likelihood occurs
in a limit with $\pi \left( x \right) = 0$ or 1. The probability of obtaining such data converges to $0$
if the logistic model is true.

Example 12.3.1 (Challenger data) $\mathrm { A }$ by now infamous data set is that of space
shuttle O-ring failures, which have been linked to temperature. The data in Table
12.3.1 give the temperatures at takeoff and whether or not an O-ring failed.

Solving (12.3.6) and (12.3.7) using $F \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) = e ^ { \alpha + \beta x _ { i } } / \left( 1 + e ^ { \alpha + \beta x _ { i } } \right)$ yields MLEs
$\widehat { \alpha } = 1 5 . 0 4 3$ and $\widehat { \beta } = - . 2 3 2 .$ Figure 12.3.1 shows the fitted curve along with the data.

The space shuttle Challenger exploded during takeoff, killing the seven astronauts
aboard. The explosion was the result of an O-ring failure, believed to be caused by
the unusually cold weather $\left( 3 1 ^ { \circ } F \right)$ at the time of launch. The MLE of the probability
of O-ring failure at $3 1 ^ { \circ }$ is .9996. (See Dalal, Fowlkes, and Hoadley 1989 for the full
story.)

II

We have, thus far, assumed that at each value of $x _ { i } ,$ we observe the result of only one
Bernoulli trial. Although this often the case, there are many situations in which there
are multiple Bernoulli observations at each value of $x .$ We now revisit the likelihood
solution in this more general case.

Suppose there are $J$ different values of the predictor $x$ in the data set $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { J } .$
Let $n _ { j }$ denote the number of Bernoulli observations at $x _ { j } ,$ and let $Y _ { j } ^ { * }$ denote the
number of successes in these $n _ { j }$ observations. Thus, $Y _ { j } ^ { * } \sim \text { binomial } \left( n _ { j } , \pi \left( x _ { j } \right) \right) .$ Then
the likelihood is

$$L \left( \alpha , \beta | \mathrm { y } ^ { * } \right) = \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } \pi \left( x _ { j } \right) ^ { y _ { j } ^ { * } } \left( 1 - \pi \left( x _ { j } \right) \right) ^ { n _ { j } - y _ { j } ^ { * } } = \prod _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } F _ { j } { } ^ { y _ { j } ^ { * } } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) ^ { n _ { j } - y _ { j } ^ { * } } ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="595" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.3" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOGISTIC REGRESSION" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 12.3.1. The data of Table 12.3.1 along with the fitted logistic curve</figcaption>

85

</figure>


and the likelihood equations are

$$0 = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } \left( y _ { j } ^ { * } - n _ { j } F _ { j } \right) \frac { f _ { j } } { F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) }$$
$$0 = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } \left( y _ { j } ^ { * } - n _ { j } F _ { j } \right) \frac { f _ { j } } { F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) } x _ { j }$$

As we have estimated the parameters of the logistic regression using maximum
likelihood, we can next get approximate variances using MLE asymptotics. However,
we have to proceed in a more general way. In Section 10.1.3 we saw how to approximate
the variance of the MLE using the information number. We use the same strategy
here, but as there are two parameters, there is an information matrix given by the
$2 \times 2$ matrix

$$\left( 1 2 . 3 . 9 \right) \quad I \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} & - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } | \mathrm { y } \right) & - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } \partial \theta _ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } | \mathrm { y } \right) \\ & - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta _ { 1 } \partial \theta _ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } | \mathrm { y } \right) & - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { \partial \theta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \log L \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \theta _ { 2 } | \mathrm { y } \right) & \end{array} \right)$$

For logistic regression, the information matrix is given by

$$\left( 1 2 . 3 . 1 0 \right) \quad I \left( \alpha , \beta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } n _ { j } F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) & \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } n _ { j } F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) \\ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } n _ { j } F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) & \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } n _ { j } F _ { j } \left( 1 - F _ { j } \right) \end{array} \right) ,$$

and the variances of the MLEs $\widehat { \alpha }$ and $\widehat { \beta }$ are usually approximated using this matrix.
Note that the elements of $I \left( \alpha , \beta \right)$ do not depend on $Y _ { 1 } ^ { * } , \ldots , Y _ { J } ^ { * } .$ Thus, the observed
information is the same as the information in this case.

In Section 10.1.3, we used the approximation (10.1.7), namely, $\mathrm { V a r } \left( h \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) | \theta \right) \approx$
$\left[ h ^ { \prime } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } / I \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) ,$ where $I \left( \cdot \right)$ was the information number. Here, we cannot do exactly
the same with the information matrix, but rather we need to get the inverse of the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="596" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.4" -->

matrix and use the inverse elements to approximate the variance. Recall that the
inverse of a $2 \times 2$ matrix is given by

$$\left( \begin{array}{} { a } & { b } \\ { c } & { d } \end{array} \right) ^ { - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { a d - b c } \left( \begin{array}{} { d } & { - b } \\ { - c } & { a } \end{array} \right) .$$

To obtain the approximate variances, the MLEs are used to estimate the parameters
in the matrix (12.3.10), and the estimates of the variances, $\left[ \sec \left( \widehat { \alpha } \right) \right] ^ { 2 }$ and $\left[ \sin \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } ,$ are
the diagonal elements of the inverse of $I \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right) .$ (The notation $\mathrm { s e } \left( \widehat { \alpha } \right)$ stands for standard
error of $\left( \widehat { \alpha } \right)$

Example 12.3.2 (Challenger data continued) The estimated information ma-
trix of the estimates from the Challenger data is given by

$$I \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } \widehat { F } _ { j } \left( 1 - \widehat { F } _ { j } \right) & \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } \widehat { F } _ { j } \left( 1 - \widehat { F } _ { j } \right) \\ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } \widehat { F } _ { j } \left( 1 - \widehat { F } _ { j } \right) & \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } x _ { j } ^ { 2 } \widehat { F } _ { j } \left( 1 - \widehat { F } _ { j } j \right) \end{array} \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} 3 . 1 5 \\ 2 1 4 . 7 5 \end{array} \right.$$
214.75

where $\widehat { F } _ { j } = e ^ { \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { j } } / \left( 1 + e ^ { \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { j } } \right)$ and has inverse

$$I \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} 5 4 . 4 4 & - . 8 0 \\ - . 8 0 & . 0 1 2 \end{array} \right) .$$

The likelihood asymptotics tell use that, for example, $\widehat { \beta } \pm z _ { \alpha / 2 } \mathrm { s e } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right)$ is, for large
samples, an approximate $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ confidence interval for $\beta .$ So for the Challenger
data we have a 95% confidence interval of

$$\beta \in - . 2 3 2 \pm 1 . 9 6 \times \sqrt { . 0 1 2 } \Rightarrow - . 4 4 7 \leq \beta \leq - . 0 1 7 ,$$

supporting the conclusion that $\beta < 0 .$
II

It is, perhaps, most common in this model to test the hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \beta = 0 ,$
because, as in simple linear regression, this hypothesis states there is no relationship
between the predictor and response variables. The Wald test statistic, $Z = \widehat { \beta } / \sec \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) ,$
has approximately a standard normal distribution if $H _ { 0 }$ is true and the sample size
is large. Thus, $H _ { 0 }$ can be rejected if | $| Z | \geq z _ { \alpha / 2 } .$ Alternatively, $H _ { 0 }$ can be tested with
the log LRT statistic

$$- 2 \log \lambda \left( \mathrm { y } ^ { * } \right) = 2 \left[ \log L \left( \widehat { \alpha } , \widehat { \beta } | \mathrm { y } ^ { * } \right) - L \left( \widehat { \alpha } _ { 0 } , 0 | \mathrm { y } ^ { * } \right) \right] ,$$

where $\widehat { \alpha } _ { 0 }$ is the MLE of $\alpha$ assuming $\beta = 0 .$ With standard binomial arguments
(see Exercise 12.20), it can be shown that $\widehat { \alpha } _ { 0 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { i } / n = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } y _ { j } ^ { * } / \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { J } n _ { j } .$
Therefore, under $H _ { 0 } ,$ $- 2 \log \lambda$ has an approximate $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution, and we can $r e j e c t$
$H _ { 0 }$ at level $\alpha \text { if } - 2 \log \lambda \geq \chi _ { 1 , \alpha } ^ { 2 } .$

We have introduced only the simplest logistic regression and generalized linear
models. Much more can be found in standard texts such as Agresti (1990).

14728.5
).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="597" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ROBUST REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.4" -->


## 12.4 Robust Regression

As in Section 10.2, we now want to take a look at the performance of our procedures
if the underlying model is not the correct one, and we will take a look at some robust
alternatives to least squares estimation, starting with a comparison analogous to the
mean/median comparison.

Recall that, when observing $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ we can define the mean and the median
as minimizers of the following quantities:

$$m : \min _ { m } \left\{ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - m \right) ^ { 2 } \right\} , \quad \mathrm { m e d i a n : } \min _ { m } \left\{ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } | x _ { i } - m | \right\} .$$

For simple linear regression, observing $\left( y _ { 1 } , x _ { 1 } \right) ,$ (y2, 2), ... , (yn, In), we know that
least squares regression estimates satisfy

least squares:
$\sin \left\{ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ y _ { i } - \left( a + b x _ { i } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } \right\}$

$$a n a l o g o u s l y \quad d e f i n e \quad l e a s t \quad a b s o l u t e \quad d e v i a t i o n \left( L A D \right) r e g r e s s i o n$$
and we estimates by

least absolute deviation:
$\min _ { a , b } \left\{ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } | y _ { i } - \left( a + b x _ { i } \right) | \right\}$

(The LAD estimates may not be unique. See Exercise 12.25.)

Thus, we see that the least squares estimators are the regression analogues of the
sample mean. This should make us wonder about their robustness performance (as
listed in items (1)-(3) of Section 10.2).

Example 12.4.1 (Robustness of least squares estimates) If we observe $\left( y _ { 1 } , x _ { 1 } \right) ,$
$\left( y _ { 2 } , x _ { 2 } \right) , \ldots , \left( y _ { n } , x _ { n } \right) ,$ where

$$Y _ { i } = \alpha + \beta x _ { i } + \varepsilon _ { i } ,$$

and the $\varepsilon _ { i }$ are uncorrelated with $E _ { i } = 0$ and Var $\varepsilon _ { i } = \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ we know that the least
squares estimator $b$ with variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } / \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 }$ is the BLUE of $\beta ,$ satisfying (1) of
Section 10.2.

To investigate how $b$ performs for small deviations, we assume that

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( \varepsilon _ { i } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \sigma ^ { 2 } & \text { with probability } 1 - \delta \\ \tau ^ { 2 } & \text { with probability } \delta . \end{array} \right.$$

Writing $b = \sum d _ { i } Y _ { i } ,$ where $d _ { i } = \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) / \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$ we now have

$$\mathrm { V a r } \left( b \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } d _ { i } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { V a r } \left( \varepsilon _ { i } \right) = \frac { \left( 1 - \delta \right) \sigma ^ { 2 } + \delta \tau ^ { 2 } } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } .$$

This shows that, as with the sample mean, for small perturbations $b$ perform pretty
well. (But we could, of course, blow things up by contaminating with a Cauchy pdf,

<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->

<td>2.4</td>
<td>2.6</td>
<td>2.9</td>
</tr>

for example.) The behavior of the least squares intercept $a$ is similar (see Exercise
12.22). See also Exercise 12.23 to see how bias contamination affects things.
11

We next look at the effect of a "catastrophic" observation and compare least squares
to its median-analogous alternative, least absolute deviation regression.

Example 12.4.2 (Catastrophic observations) McPherson (1990) describes an
experiment in which the levels of carbon dioxide $\left( C O _ { 2 } \right)$ and oxygen $\left( O _ { 2 } \right)$ were mea-
sured in the pouches of 24 potoroos (a marsupial). Interest is in the regression of $C O _ { 2 }$
on $O _ { 2 } ,$ where the experimenter expects a slope of -1. The data for 23 animals (one
had missing values) are given in Table 12.4.1. For the original data, the least squares
and LAD lines are quite close:

$$l e a s t \quad s q u a r e s : y = 1 8 . 6 7 \quad - . 8 9 x ,$$

least absolute deviation: $y = 1 8 . 5 9 \quad - . 8 9 x .$

However, an aberrant observation can upset least squares. When entering the data
the $O _ { 2 }$ value of 18 on Animal 15 was incorrectly entered as 10 (we really did this).
For this new (incorrect) data set we have

$$l e a s t \quad s q u a r e s : y = 6 . 4 1 \quad - . 2 3 x ,$$

least absolute deviation: $y = 1 5 . 9 5 \quad - . 7 5 x ,$

showing that the aberrant observation had much less of an effect on LAD. See Figure
12.4.1 for a display of the regression lines.

These calculations illustrate the resistance of LAD, as opposed to least squares.
Since we have the mean/median analogy, we can surmise that this behavior is reflected
in breakdown values, which are 0% for least squares and 50% for LAD.
$I /$

However, the mean/median analogy continues. Although the LAD estimator is
robust to catastrophic observations, it loses much in efficiency with respect to the
least squares estimator (see also Exercise 12.25).

Example 12.4.3 (Asymptotic normality of the LAD estimator) We adapt
the argument leading to (10.2.6) to derive the asymptotic distribution of the LAD
estimator. Also, to simplify things, we consider only the model

$$Y _ { i } = \beta x _ { i } + \varepsilon _ { i } ,$$

that is, we set $\alpha = 0 .$ (This avoids having to deal with a bivariate limiting distribu-
tion.)

In the terminology of M-estimators, the LAD estimator is obtained by minimizing

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \rho \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } | y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } |$$
$$= \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) I \left( y _ { i } > \beta x _ { i } \right) - \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) I \left( y _ { i } < \beta x _ { i } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

(12.4.1)

We then calculate $\psi = \rho ^ { \prime }$ and solve $\sum _ { i } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) = 0$ for $\beta ,$ where

$$\psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) = x _ { i } I \left( y _ { i } > \beta x _ { i } \right) - x _ { i } I \left( y _ { i } < \beta x _ { i } \right) .$$

If $\widehat { \beta } _ { L }$ is the solution, expand $\psi$ in a Taylor series around $\beta :$

$$\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } _ { L } x _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) + \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { L } - \beta \right) \frac { d } { d \widehat { \beta } _ { L } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } _ { L } x _ { i } \right) | _ { \widehat { \beta } _ { L } = \beta } + \cdots .$$

Although the left-hand side of the equation is not equal to 0, we assume that it
approaches 0 as $n \rightarrow \infty$ (see Exercise 12.27). Then rearranging we obtain

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="600" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.4" -->

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { L } - \beta \right) = \frac { \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) } { \frac { 1 } { n } \frac { d } { d \widehat { \beta } _ { L } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } _ { L } x _ { i } \right) | _ { \widehat { \beta } _ { L } = \beta } } .$$
(12.4.2)

First look at the numerator. As $E _ { \beta } \psi \left( Y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } _ { L } x _ { i } \right) = 0$ and $\mathrm { V a r } \psi \left( Y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } _ { L } x _ { i } \right) = x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ,$ it
follows that

(12.4.3)
$$\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { n } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( Y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) = \sqrt { n } \left[ \frac { - 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( Y _ { i } - \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) \right] \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

where $\sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } = \lim _ { n \rightarrow \infty } \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } .$ Turning to the denominator, we must be a bit careful
as $\psi$ has points of nondifferentiability. We therefore first apply the Law of Large
Numbers before differentiating, and use the approximation

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \frac { d } { d \beta _ { 0 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } \right) \approx \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { d } { d \beta _ { 0 } } \mathrm { E } _ { \beta } \left[ \psi \left( Y _ { i } - \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } \right) \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { d } { d \beta _ { 0 } } \left[ x _ { i } P _ { \beta } \left( Y _ { i } > \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } \right) - x _ { i } P _ { \beta } \left( Y _ { i } < \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } \right) \right]$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } f \left( \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) + x _ { i } ^ { 2 } f \left( \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } - \beta x _ { i } \right) .$$
(12.4.4)

If we now evaluate the derivative at $\beta _ { 0 } = \beta ,$ we have

$$\frac { 1 } { n } \frac { d } { d \beta _ { 0 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi \left( y _ { i } - \beta _ { 0 } x _ { i } \right) | _ { \beta _ { 0 } = \beta } \approx 2 f \left( 0 \right) \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ,$$

and putting this together with (12.4.2) and (12.4.3), we have

(12.4.5)
$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { L } - \beta \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { 4 f \left( 0 \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } } \right) . ,$$

Finally, for the case of $\alpha = 0 ,$ the least squares estimator is $\widehat { \beta } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } y _ { i } / \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { i } ^ { 2 }$
and satisfies

$$\sqrt { n } \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) \rightarrow n \left( 0 , \frac { 1 } { \sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } } \right)$$

so that the asymptotic relative efficiency of $\widehat { \beta } _ { L }$ to $\widehat { \beta }$ is

$$A R E \left( \widehat { \beta } _ { L } , \widehat { \beta } \right) = \frac { 1 / \sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } } { 1 / \left( 4 f \left( 0 \right) ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { x } ^ { 2 } \right) } = 4 f \left( 0 \right) ^ { 2 }$$

whose values are the same as those given in Table 10.2.1, comparing the median to
the mean. Thus, for normal errors the ARE of the LAD estimator to least squares
is only 64%, showing that the LAD estimator gives up a good bit of efficiency with
respect to least squares.

|

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="601" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ROBUST REGRESSION" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.5" -->

So we are in the same situation that we encountered in Section 10.2.1. The LAD
alternative to least squares seems to lose too much in efficiency if the errors are truly
normal. The compromise, once again, is an M-estimator. We can construct one by
minimizing a function analogous to (10.2.2), which would be $\sum _ { i } \rho _ { i } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) ,$ where

$$\left( 1 2 . 4 . 6 \right) \quad \rho _ { i } \left( \alpha , \beta \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } & \mathrm { i f } | y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } | \leq k \\ k | y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } | - \frac { 1 } { 2 } k ^ { 2 } & \mathrm { i f } | y _ { i } - \alpha - \beta x _ { i } | > k , \end{array} \right.$$

where $k$ is a tuning parameter.

Example $1 2 . 4 . 4$ (Regression M-estimator) Using the function $\left( 1 2 . 4 . 6 \right)$ with
$k = 1 . 5 0 ,$ we fit the M-estimators of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for the data of Table 12.4.1. The results
were

M-estimate for original data: $y = 1 8 . 5 \quad - . 8 9 x$
M-estimate for mistyped data: $y = 1 4 . 6 7 \quad - . 6 8 x ,$

where we estimated $\sigma$ by .23, the standard deviation of the residuals from the least
squares fit.

Thus we see that the M-estimate is somewhat more resistant than the least squares
line, behaving more like the LAD fit when there are outliers.

II

As in Section 10.2, we expect the ARE of the M-estimator to be better than that of
the $L A D .$ This is the case, however, the calculations become very involved (even more
so than for the LAD) so we will not give the details here. Huber (1981, Chapter $\left. 7 \right)$
gives a detailed treatment of M-estimator asymptotics; see also Portnoy (1987). We
content ourselves with an evaluation of the M-estimator through a small simulation
study, reproducing a table like Table 10.2.3.

Example 12.4.5 (Simulation of regression AREs) For the model $Y _ { i } = \alpha +$
$\beta x _ { i } + \varepsilon _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 5 ,$ we take the $x _ { i }$ to be $\left( - 2 , - 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 \right) ,$ $\alpha = 0 ,$ and $\beta = 1 .$ We
generate $\varepsilon _ { i }$ from normals, double exponentials, and logistic distributions and calculate
the variance of the least squares, $L A D ,$ and M-estimator. These are presented in the
following table.


<table>
<caption>Regression M-estimator AREs, $k = 1 . 5$ (based on 10,000 simulations)</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th>Normal</th>
<th>Logistic</th>
<th>Double exponential</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>vs. least squares</td>
<td>0.98</td>
<td>1.03</td>
<td>1.07</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>vs. LAD</td>
<td>1.39</td>
<td>1.27</td>
<td>1.14</td>
</tr>
</table>


The M-estimator variance is similar to that of least squares for all three distributions
and is a uniform improvement over LAD. The dominance of the M-estimator over
LAD is more striking than that of the Huber estimator over the median (as given in
Table 10.2.3).
11

<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.5" -->


## 12.5 Exercises

12.1 Verify the expressions in (12.2.7). (Hint: Use the Pythagorean Theorem.)

12.2 Show that the extrema of

$$f \left( b \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + b ^ { 2 } } \left[ S _ { y y } - 2 b S _ { x y } + b ^ { 2 } S _ { x x } \right]$$

are given by

$$b = \frac { - \left( S _ { x x } - S _ { y y } \right) \pm \sqrt { \left( S _ { x x } - S _ { y y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } } } { 2 S _ { x y } } .$$

Show that the $+$ solution gives the minimum of $f \left( b \right) .$

12.3 In maximizing the likelihood (12.2.13), we first minimized, for each value of $\alpha ,$ $\beta ,$ and
$\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } ,$ the function

$$f \left( \xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } + \lambda \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } \right)$$

with respect to $\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { n } .$

(a) Prove that this function is minimized at

$$\xi _ { i } ^ { * } = \frac { x _ { i } + \lambda \beta \left( y _ { i } - \alpha \right) } { 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } } .$$

(b) Show that the function

$$D _ { \lambda } \left( \left( x , y \right) , \left( \xi , \alpha + \beta \xi \right) \right) = \left( x - \xi \right) ^ { 2 } + \lambda \left( y - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi \right) \right) ^ { 2 }$$

defines a metric between the points $\left( x , y \right)$ and $\left( \xi , \alpha + \beta \xi \right) .$ A metric is a distance
measure, a function $D$ that measures the distance between two points $A$ and $B .$
A metric satisfies the following four properties:

i. $D \left( A , A \right) = 0 .$

ii. $D \left( A , B \right) > 0$ if $A \neq B .$

iii. $D \left( A , B \right) = D \left( B , A \right)$ (reflexive).

iv. $D \left( A , B \right) \leq D \left( A , C \right) + D \left( C , B \right)$ (triangle inequality).

12.4 Consider the MLE of the slope in the $\mathrm { E I V }$ model

$$\widehat { \beta } \left( \lambda \right) = \frac { - \left( S _ { x x } - \lambda S _ { y y } \right) + \sqrt { \left( S _ { x x } - \lambda S _ { y y } \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } } } { 2 \lambda S _ { x y } } ,$$

where $\lambda = \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 }$ is assumed known.

(a) Show that $\lim _ { \lambda \rightarrow 0 } \widehat { \beta } \left( \lambda \right) = S _ { x y } / S _ { x x } ,$ the slope of the ordinary regression of $y$ on
$x .$

(b) Show that $\lim _ { \lambda \rightarrow \infty } \widehat { \beta } \left( \lambda \right) = S _ { y y } / S _ { x y } ,$ the reciprocal of the slope of the ordinary
regression of $x$ on $y .$

(c) Show that $\widehat { \beta } \left( \lambda \right)$ is, in fact, monotone in $\lambda$ and is increasing if $S _ { x y } > 0$ and
decreasing if $S _ { x y } < 0 .$

(d) Show that the orthogonal least squares line $\left( \lambda = 1 \right)$ is always between the lines
given by the ordinary regressions of $y$ on $x$ and of $x$ on $y .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="603" -->
<!-- PageHeader="EXERCISES" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.5" -->

Calculate the MLE of the slope assuming the EIV model. Also, calculate the
least squares slopes of the regressions of $y$ on $x$ and of $x$ on $y ,$ and show how
these quantities bound the MLE.

12.5 In the EIV functional relationship model, where $\lambda = \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } / \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 }$ is assumed known, show
that the MLE of $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is given by (12.2.18).

12.6 Show that in the linear structural relationship model (12.2.6), $\mathrm { i f }$ we integrate out $\xi _ { i } ,$
the marginal distribution of $\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right)$ is given by (12.2.19).

12.7 Consider a linear structural relationship model where we assume that $\xi _ { i }$ has an
improper distribution, $\xi _ { i } \sim \mathrm { u n i f o r m } \left( - \infty , \infty \right) .$

(a) Show that for each $i ,$

$$\int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { \left( 2 \pi \right) } \frac { 1 } { \sigma _ { \delta } \sigma _ { \epsilon } } \exp \left[ - \left( \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \xi _ { i } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] \exp \left[ - \left( \frac { \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta \xi _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } \right) \right] d \xi$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 \pi } } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { \beta ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } + \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } } \exp \left[ - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \left( y _ { i } - \left( \alpha + \beta x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { \beta ^ { 2 } \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } + \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } } \right] .$$

(Completing the square in the exponential makes the integration easy.)

(b) The result of the integration in part (a) looks like a pdf, and if we consider it a
pdf of $Y$ conditional on $X ,$ then we seem to have a linear relationship between
$X$ and $Y .$ Thus, it is sometimes said that this "limiting case" of the structural
relationship leads to simple linear regression and ordinary least squares. Explain
why this interpretation of the above function is wrong.

12.8 Verify the nonidentifiability problems in the structural relationship model in the
following ways.

(a) Produce two different sets of parameters that give the same marginal distribution
to $\left( X _ { i } , Y _ { i } \right) .$

(b) Show that there are at least two distinct parameter vectors that yield the same
solution to the equations given in (12.2.20).

12.9 In the structural relationship model, the solution to the equations in (12.2.20) implies
a restriction on $\widehat { \beta } ,$ the same restriction seen in the functional relationship case (see
Exercise 12.4).

(a) Show that in $\left( 1 2 . 2 . 2 0 \right) ,$ the MLE of $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 }$ is nonnegative only if $S _ { x x } \geq \left( 1 / \widehat { \beta } \right) S _ { x y } .$
Also, the MLE of $\sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 }$ is nonnegative only if $S _ { y y } \geq \widehat { \beta } S _ { x y } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="604" -->
<!-- PageHeader="REGRESSION MODELS" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Section 12.5" -->

(b) Show that the restrictions in part (a), together with the rest of the equations in
(12.2.20), imply that

$$\frac { | S _ { x y } | } { S _ { x x } } \leq | \widehat { \beta } | \leq \frac { S _ { y y } } { | S _ { x y } | } .$$

12.10 (a) Derive the MLEs for $\left( \alpha , \beta , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } \right)$ in the structural relationship model by
solving the equations $\left( 1 2 . 2 . 2 0 \right) u n d e r$ the assumption that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Calculate the MLEs for $\left( \alpha , \beta , \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } , \sigma _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } \right)$ for the data of Exercise 12.4 by assuming
the structural relationship model holds and that $\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } .$

(c) Verify the relationship between variance estimates in the functional and struc-
tural relationship models. In particular, show that

$$\widehat { \mathrm { V a r } } X _ { i } \left( \mathrm { s t r u c t u r a l } \right) = 2 \widehat { \mathrm { V a r } } X _ { i } \left( \mathrm { f u n c t i o n a l } \right) .$$

That is, verify

$$\left( S _ { x x } - \frac { S _ { x y } } { \widehat { \beta } } \right) = \frac { \lambda } { 1 + \lambda \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( y _ { i } - \left( \widehat { \alpha } + \widehat { \beta } x _ { i } \right) \right) ^ { 2 } .$$

(d) Verify the following equality, which is implicit in the MLE variance estimates
given in (12.2.21). Show that

$$S _ { x x } - \frac { S _ { x y } } { \widehat { \beta } } = \lambda \left( S _ { y y } - \widehat { \beta } S _ { x y } \right) .$$

12.11 (a) Show that for random variables $X$ and $Y$ and constants $a$ a, $b , c , d ,$

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( a Y + b X , c Y + d X \right) = a c \mathrm { V a r } Y + \left( b c + a d \right) \mathrm { C o v } \left( X , Y \right) + b d \mathrm { V a r } X .$$

(b) Use the result in part (a) to verify that in the structural relationship model with
$\sigma _ { \delta } ^ { 2 } = \lambda \sigma _ { \epsilon } ^ { 2 } ,$

$$\mathrm { C o v } \left( \beta \lambda Y _ { i } + X _ { i } , Y _ { i } - \beta X _ { i } \right) = 0 ,$$

the identity on which the Creasy-Williams confidence set is based.

(c) Use the results of part (b) to show that

$$\frac { \sqrt { \left( n - 2 \right) } r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right) } { \sqrt { 1 - r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } } \sim t _ { n - 2 }$$ ☒ ☒

for any value of $\beta ,$ where $r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right)$ is given in (12.2.23). Also, show that the confi-
dence set defined in (12.2.24) has constant coverage probability equal to $1 - \alpha .$

of $\left( 1 2 . 2 . 2 3 \right) ,$ and $C _ { \lambda } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) ,$ the Creasy-Williams confidence set of (12.2.24).

(a) Show that

$$C _ { G } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right) = \left\{ \beta : \frac { \left( \beta - \widehat { \beta } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 2 \right) } \leq F _ { 1 , n - 2 , \alpha } \right\} , :$$

where $\widehat { \beta }$ is the MLE of $\beta$ and $\widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } ^ { 2 }$ is the previously defined consistent estimator
of $\sigma _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } .$

(b) Show that the Creasy-Williams set can be written in the form

$$\left\{ \beta : \frac { \left( \beta - \widehat { \beta } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \widehat { \sigma } _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } / \left( n - 2 \right) } \left[ \frac { \left( 1 + \lambda \beta \widehat { \beta } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] \leq F _ { 1 , n - 2 , \alpha } \right\} .$$

Hence $C _ { G } \left( \widehat { \beta } \right)$ can be derived by replacing the term in square brackets with 1, its
probability limit. (In deriving this representation, the fact that $\widehat { \beta }$ and $- 1 / \left( \lambda \widehat { \beta } \right)$
are roots of the numerator of $r _ { \lambda } \left( \beta \right)$ is of great help. In particular, the fact that

$$\frac { r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } { 1 - r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta \right) } = \frac { \lambda ^ { 2 } S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } \left( \beta - \widehat { \beta } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \beta + \left( 1 / \lambda \beta \right) \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 1 + \lambda \beta ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( S _ { x x } S _ { y y } - S _ { x y } ^ { 2 } \right) }$$

is straightforward to establish.)

12.25 A problem with the LAD regression line is that it is not always uniquely defined.

(a) Show that, for a data set with three observations, $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) ,$ $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } \right) ,$ and $\left( x _ { 3 } , y _ { 3 } \right)$
(note the first two $x s$ are the same), any line that goes through $\left( x _ { 3 } , y _ { 3 } \right)$ and lies
between $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right)$ and $\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } \right)$ is a least absolute deviation line.

(b) For three individuals, measurements are taken on heart rate $\left( x , \right.$ in beats per
minute) and oxygen consumption $\left( y , \right.$ in ml/kg). The $\left( x , y \right)$ pairs are (127, 14.4),
(127, 11.9), and (136, 17.9). Calculate the slope and intercept of the least squares
line and the range of the least absolute deviation lines.

There seems to be some disagreement over the value of the least absolute deviation
line. It is certainly more robust than least squares but can be very difficult to compute
(but see Portnoy and Koenker 1997 for an efficient computing algorithm). It also
seems that Ellis (1998) questions its robustness, and in a discussion Portnoy and
Mizera (1998) question Ellis.
